# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stack Frame Lifecycle Per Method Call

Every method invocation triggers the JVM to push a **stack frame** onto the **JVM Stack** of the calling thread.

### Step-by-Step Construction of a Stack Frame

**Given:** A Java method `compute(int a, int b)` with local variables `a`, `b`, `c`, and operand stack of depth 2.

**Step 1 тАФ Frame Allocation**
The JVM allocates a new frame structure in the current thread's JVM Stack. If the stack depth exceeds `-Xss`, a **`StackOverflowError`** is thrown.

$$ \text{Frame Memory} = \text{Local Variables Array} + \text{Operand Stack} + \text{Frame Data} $$

**Step 2 тАФ Local Variables Array Sizing**
The number of slots equals `max(local_var_count, parameter_count)`. `long` and `double` consume 2 slots.

$$ \text{Slots} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{width}(type_i) $$

**Step 3 тАФ Operand Stack Initialization**
Set stack pointer to **0** (empty stack). Every push increments by 1, every pop decrements by 1.

$$ \text{Top}_t = \text{Top}_{t-1} \pm 1 $$

**Step 4 тАФ Method Execution**
Bytecode instructions (`iload`, `iadd`, `istore`, etc.) manipulate the operand stack.

**Step 5 тАФ Frame Pop**
When the method returns (via `ireturn`, `areturn`, `return`, or `athrow`), the frame is **popped**, and the return value (if any) is pushed onto the **caller's** operand stack.

$$ \text{Return Value} \xrightarrow{\text{push}} \text{Caller's Operand Stack} $$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Class Loading Triggers

A class is loaded into the JVM **lazily** тАФ only when first referenced. The triggers are:

| # | Trigger Event | Loading Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `new MyClass()` | Loads, links, initializes `MyClass` |
| 2 | `MyClass.staticMethod()` | Loads & initializes class (static resolution) |
| 3 | `MyClass.staticField` (non-final) | Loads & initializes class |
| 4 | `MyClass.class` (reflection) | Loads class (may not initialize) |
| 5 | `Class.forName("MyClass")` | Loads & initializes class |
| 6 | `Class.forName("MyClass", false, loader)` | Loads only, **skips initialization** |
| 7 | Subclass initialization | Triggers **parent** class initialization first |

### Resolution Order (Parent Delegation Algorithm)

Given a class name `C` and class loader `L`:

$$ \text{Load}(C, L) = \begin{cases} C \in \text{bootstrap} & \rightarrow \text{BootstrapClassLoader finds C} \\ \text{else} & \rightarrow \text{Parent}(L).\text{Load}(C) \\ \text{else} & \rightarrow L.\text{findClass}(C) \\ \text{else} & \rightarrow \text{throw ClassNotFoundException} \end{cases} $$

**Textual Explanation:**
- The JVM first asks the **Bootstrap ClassLoader** (root) to load from `jrt-fs` (Java 9+ modules) or `rt.jar` (pre-9).
- If not found, the request cascades down to the **Platform ClassLoader** (formerly Extension).
- Then to the **Application ClassLoader** (classpath).
- Finally to the **User-Defined ClassLoader** if available.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bytecode Execution Trace

**Source Java code:**
```java
public int add(int a, int b) {
    int sum = a + b;
    return sum;
}
```

**Generated Bytecode (simplified):**
```
0: iload_1         // Push local var 1 (a) onto operand stack
1: iload_2         // Push local var 2 (b) onto operand stack
2: iadd            // Pop two ints, push their sum
3: istore_3        // Pop sum, store in local var 3 (sum)
4: iload_3         // Push local var 3 (sum) onto operand stack
5: ireturn         // Return the int on top of operand stack
```

### Operand Stack State Trace (Step-by-Step)

| Instruction | Stack Before (top тЖТ bottom) | Stack After (top тЖТ bottom) | Local Var Array |
|---|---|---|---|
| `iload_1` | `[]` | `[a]` | `[this, a, b, _]` |
| `iload_2` | `[a]` | `[b, a]` | `[this, a, b, _]` |
| `iadd` | `[b, a]` | `[a+b]` | `[this, a, b, _]` |
| `istore_3` | `[a+b]` | `[]` | `[this, a, b, a+b]` |
| `iload_3` | `[]` | `[a+b]` | `[this, a, b, a+b]` |
| `ireturn` | `[a+b]` | `[]` (frame popped) | тАФ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** Notice that local variables are **not** assigned default values by the JVM for `int` types тАФ they are **uninitialized slots** in the local variable array. The compiler must generate a definite assignment (via `istore` or `<init>`) before any read access, or the bytecode verifier **rejects the class** during the **Verify** phase.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: JVM vs JRE vs JDK

| Feature | JVM | JRE | JDK |
|---|---|---|---|
| Full Form | Java Virtual Machine | Java Runtime Environment | Java Development Kit |
| Contains | Specification + Runtime engine | JVM + Library classes | JRE + Dev tools |
| Can Run Java Programs? | тЭМ (engine only) | тЬЕ | тЬЕ |
| Can Compile Java Programs? | тЭМ | тЭМ | тЬЕ (`javac`) |
| Includes `javac`? | тЭМ | тЭМ | тЬЕ |
| Includes `java` launcher? | Optional | тЬЕ | тЬЕ |
| Includes `jar`, `javadoc`? | тЭМ | тЭМ | тЬЕ |
| Target Audience | Engine developers | End users / Production | Developers |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Java Virtual Machine. List its three primary functions. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** is a platform-dependent, abstract computing machine that provides the runtime environment necessary to execute Java bytecode on any underlying hardware/OS. It functions as the execution engine component of the Java platform.

Its **three primary functions** are:
1. **Loads code** тАФ through the ClassLoader subsystem.
2. **Verifies code** тАФ through the bytecode verifier, ensuring type safety and structural validity.
3. **Executes code** тАФ through the execution engine (Interpreter + JIT Compiler), translating bytecode into native machine instructions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award 1 mark for the definition, ┬╜ mark per correct function listed.

### Q2. Differentiate between JVM, JRE, and JDK. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | JVM | JRE | JDK |
|---|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Runtime engine for bytecode | Runtime environment for end users | Development kit for programmers |
| Contents | Specification + execution engine | JVM + standard library classes (`rt.jar`/modules) | JRE + `javac` + `javadoc` + `jar` tools |
| Compilation Support | тЭМ No | тЭМ No | тЬЕ Yes |
| Use Case | Embedded / custom runtimes | Running Java apps | Building Java apps |

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each тАФ Module 1 Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the architecture of JVM in detail with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the ClassLoader Subsystem.

**Model Answer:**

The JVM architecture is divided into **three major subsystems** that work cooperatively:

**1. ClassLoader Subsystem** [3 marks]
- Handles dynamic loading of `.class` files into memory.
- Uses the **Parent Delegation Model** тАФ class loading requests are delegated to the parent classloader first.
- Performs three phases:
  - **Loading** тАФ reads bytecode, creates binary class representation in method area.
  - **Linking** тАФ verifies bytecode, prepares static variables (default values), and resolves symbolic references.
  - **Initialization** тАФ executes `<clinit>` (static block + static variable assignments in source order).

**2. Runtime Data Areas (Memory)** [2 marks]
- **Method Area** тАФ class-level metadata, static fields, constant pool.
- **Heap** тАФ all objects and instance variables; managed by garbage collector.
- **JVM Stack** тАФ per-thread stack frames containing local variables, operand stack, and frame data.
- **PC Register** тАФ holds address of currently executing instruction per thread.
- **Native Method Stack** тАФ supports JNI native method calls.

**3. Execution Engine** [2 marks]
- **Interpreter** тАФ reads and executes bytecode line by line; slow but fast-startup.
- **JIT Compiler** тАФ compiles hot bytecode to native code; faster steady-state performance.
- **Garbage Collector** тАФ automatically reclaims memory of unreachable objects (G1, ZGC, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory:** A clean block diagram showing the three subsystems and their components must accompany this answer. Award 1 mark for a correctly labeled diagram.

#### (b) [7 Marks] With a suitable example, explain the bytecode execution process in the JVM, including the role of the operand stack and local variable array.

**Model Answer:**

**Bytecode Example** тАФ Consider the method:
```java
public int square(int n) {
    int result = n * n;
    return result;
}
```

**Generated Bytecode (illustrative):**
```
0: iload_1      // Load local var 1 (n) onto operand stack
1: iload_1      // Load local var 1 (n) again
2: imul         // Pop two ints, push product (n*n)
3: istore_2     // Pop result, store in local var 2 (result)
4: iload_2      // Load local var 2 onto operand stack
5: ireturn      // Return top of operand stack to caller
```

**Step-by-Step Operand Stack Trace** [3 marks]:

| Step | Instruction | Operand Stack (top тЖТ bottom) | Local Vars |
|---|---|---|---|
| Start | тАФ | `[]` | `[this, n, _]` |
| 0 | `iload_1` | `[n]` | `[this, n, _]` |
| 1 | `iload_1` | `[n, n]` | `[this, n, _]` |
| 2 | `imul` | `[n*n]` | `[this, n, _]` |
| 3 | `istore_2` | `[]` | `[this, n, n*n]` |
| 4 | `iload_2` | `[n*n]` | `[this, n, n*n]` |
| 5 | `ireturn` | `[]` (frame popped) | тАФ |

**Role of Local Variable Array** [2 marks]:
- Stores method parameters and locally declared variables.
- Indexed starting from 0 (slot 0 = `this` for instance methods, slot 0 = first param for static methods).
- `long`/`double` consume 2 consecutive slots.

**Role of Operand Stack** [2 marks]:
- Working memory of the bytecode engine.
- All arithmetic operations (`iadd`, `imul`, `isub`) pop operands and push results.
- Method invocations push arguments via stack; return values are pushed to caller's stack.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - тЭМ **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing the **JVM Stack** (thread-level, holds stack frames) with the **Operand Stack** (frame-level, holds intermediate values). They are **different** memory regions.
> - тЭМ **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting that `iload_0` refers to `this` in instance methods, not the first user parameter. The first user parameter is `iload_1`.
> - тЭМ **Common Mistake 3:** Writing `istore` without an index тАФ every store instruction must specify the local variable slot.
> - тЬЕ **Bonus Mark Tip:** Mentioning **stack-based architecture** vs register-based architecture (e.g., Dalvik/ART) earns an extra credit point.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Alternative Choice

#### (a) [7 Marks] What is Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation? Compare JIT compilation with interpretation. Explain tiered compilation as used in the HotSpot JVM.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of JIT** [2 marks]:
Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation is a hybrid execution technique in which the JVM identifies **"hot"** methods (those executed frequently, identified by method invocation counters or loop backedge counters) and compiles their bytecode into **optimized native machine code** at runtime. The native code is cached in the **Code Cache** for subsequent invocations.

**Comparison: Interpreter vs JIT** [3 marks]:

| Aspect | Interpreter | JIT Compiler |
|---|---|---|
| Execution Mode | Line-by-line bytecode dispatch | Compiles to native code upfront |
| Startup Time | **Fast** (no compilation delay) | **Slow** (compilation overhead) |
| Steady-State Performance | **Slow** (re-interprets every call) | **Fast** (native code executes) |
| Memory Footprint | Low | Higher (code cache) |
| Optimization | None (no time budget) | Aggressive (inlining, escape analysis, dead-code elimination) |
| Used When | Cold code, rarely executed | Hot code, frequently executed |

**Tiered Compilation in HotSpot** [2 marks]:
Modern HotSpot JVM uses a **5-tier** compilation strategy:
1. **Tier 0 тАФ Interpreter:** Pure interpreted execution; gathers profiling data.
2. **Tier 1 тАФ Simple C1:** Lightweight JIT with basic optimizations; counts invocations.
3. **Tier 2 тАФ Limited C1:** C1 with more profiling but no extra optimizations.
4. **Tier 3 тАФ Full C1:** C1 with all simple optimizations enabled.
5. **Tier 4 тАФ C2:** Server-grade compiler with aggressive optimizations (loop unrolling, scalar replacement, intrinsic recognition).

The VM dynamically promotes methods between tiers based on **hotness counters**.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the Parent Delegation Model of class loading. What problem does it solve? How can it be bypassed, and with what consequences?

**Model Answer:**

**Parent Delegation Model** [3 marks]:
When a class loader `L` receives a request to load class `C`, it follows this algorithm:
1. Delegate the request to the **parent** class loader.
2. The parent recursively delegates upward to the **Bootstrap ClassLoader**.
3. If the parent successfully loads `C`, return the class.
4. If the parent throws `ClassNotFoundException`, **only then** does `L` attempt to load `C` from its own search path.

The hierarchy is:
$$\text{Bootstrap} \rightarrow \text{Platform} \rightarrow \text{Application} \rightarrow \text{User-Defined}$$

**Problem Solved** [2 marks]:
- **Security:** Prevents malicious code from replacing core Java classes. For example, an attacker cannot write their own `java.lang.String` class on the classpath and have it loaded тАФ the Bootstrap ClassLoader (which holds `rt.jar`/`java.base` module) gets the request first and returns the legitimate version.
- **Uniqueness:** Guarantees that core Java classes are loaded by the **same classloader instance** across the runtime, preserving type identity and `instanceof` checks.

**Bypassing the Model** [1 mark]:
A user-defined classloader can override `loadClass(String name)` to violate delegation. For example, by checking its own path **before** calling `parent.loadClass(name)`.

**Consequences of Bypass** [1 mark]:
- **ClassCastException** risk: Two versions of the same class loaded by different loaders are treated as **distinct types** even if fully qualified names match.
- **ClassLoader Leak / OutOfMemoryError:** Improperly implemented custom loaders can cause classloader leaks in application servers (Tomcat, WebLogic).
- **Security warnings:** May trigger Java Security Manager violations (in legacy code) or sandbox restrictions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - тЭМ **Common Mistake 1:** Writing that "Bootstrap ClassLoader is written in Java" тАФ it is **NOT**. It is implemented in **native C/C++ code** and loads from the `jrt-fs` virtual filesystem (Java 9+).
> - тЭМ **Common Mistake 2:** Saying that "JVM is platform-independent" тАФ the **JVM is platform-DEPENDENT**; only the **bytecode is platform-independent**.
> - тЭМ **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing **Method Area** with **Heap** тАФ the Method Area stores **class-level** data; the Heap stores **object instances**.
> - тЭМ **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting that `String` literals go to the **String Constant Pool** (part of the Method Area in Java 7+), not the regular Heap.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- тЬЕ **JVM = Specification + Implementation**; specification is platform-independent, implementation is platform-dependent.
- тЬЕ **Three core subsystems:** ClassLoader + Runtime Data Areas + Execution Engine.
- тЬЕ **WORA** is achieved because **bytecode is platform-independent**; JVM is the bridge.
- тЬЕ **Magic number** for any valid `.class` file: `0xCAFEBABE` (4 bytes).
- тЬЕ **ClassLoader phases:** Load тЖТ Link (Verify, Prepare, Resolve) тЖТ Initialize.
- тЬЕ **Parent Delegation:** requests flow **upward** (child тЖТ parent тЖТ bootstrap); loading happens **downward** (parent found тЖТ returned).
- тЬЕ **Bootstrap ClassLoader** is **native** (C/C++), not Java; loads `java.base` module.
- тЬЕ **Method Area** = class metadata + static fields + constant pool (Java 8+ = Metaspace; pre-8 = PermGen).
- тЬЕ **Heap** = all `new` objects; managed by **Garbage Collector**; shared across threads.
- тЬЕ **JVM Stack** = per-thread LIFO of stack frames; each frame = local vars + operand stack + frame data.
- тЬЕ **PC Register** = pointer to currently executing JVM instruction; one per thread.
- тЬЕ **JIT compiles HOT code**, not all code; uses invocation counters (default threshold: 10,000 for C1, 100,000 for C2).
- тЬЕ **Tiered Compilation:** Interpreter тЖТ C1 тЖТ C2 (HotSpot default).
- тЬЕ **`StackOverflowError`** = JVM Stack exhausted (deep recursion, large `-Xss` requested).
- тЬЕ **`OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space`** = Heap exhausted.
- тЬЕ **`OutOfMemoryError: Metaspace`** = Class metadata exceeded `-XX:MaxMetaspaceSize`.
- тЬЕ **GC Roots:** local stack variables, active threads, static fields, JNI refs.
- тЬЕ **JVM тЙа JRE тЙа JDK:** JRE = JVM + libs (end users); JDK = JRE + `javac` (developers).
- тЬЕ **Modern JVMs are polyglot** тАФ execute Kotlin, Scala, Groovy, Clojure, JavaScript, Python, R, Ruby.
- тЬЕ **JVM is stack-based**, not register-based (unlike Dalvik/ART bytecode).
- тЬЕ **Boolean** is internally treated as `int` (0 = false, 1 = true) in bytecode.
- тЬЕ **First user param** = slot 1 for instance methods, slot 0 for static methods.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Primitive Data Types and Wrapper Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Primitive Data Types and Wrapper Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Primitive Data Types тАФ Formal Definition

In the Java programming language, **primitive data types** are the most basic, built-in data types defined by the language specification. They serve as the fundamental building blocks for data manipulation and are not objects; hence, they are allocated on the **stack memory** (for local variables) rather than the heap. Java defines exactly **8 primitive data types**: `byte`, `short`, `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`, `char`, and `boolean`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Java specification fixes the size and behaviour of every primitive type across *all* platforms. This is a deliberate design choice to ensure **platform independence** тАФ unlike C/C++, where `int` size depends on the architecture.

## 1.2 Wrapper Classes тАФ Formal Definition

A **wrapper class** is a class whose object *wraps* (encapsulates) a primitive data type. Every primitive in Java has a corresponding wrapper class in the `java.lang` package. The wrapper classes are: `Byte`, `Short`, `Integer`, `Long`, `Float`, `Double`, `Character`, and `Boolean`. They allow primitives to be used in contexts that require **objects**, such as Collections (`ArrayList<Integer>`), Generics, Serialization, and Reflection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Concept:** Wrapper classes are **immutable** (their internal value cannot be changed after creation) and are stored on the **heap memory**. They provide numerous utility methods like `parseInt()`, `valueOf()`, `toString()`, etc.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

**Real-world analogy тАФ The "Box" Metaphor:**

Imagine you have a raw, loose **coin** (a primitive тАФ light, fast, used directly in transactions). It is small, efficient, and always ready to use, but it cannot fit into a **coin-collection album** (an object-oriented container) because the album has slots only for *envelopes* (objects).

A **wrapper class** is like placing that coin inside a **sealed, labelled envelope**. The envelope (the wrapper object) holds the coin (the primitive value) and can be stamped, sorted, stored, and passed around wherever only objects are accepted. You can still peek inside and see the value, but the wrapper adds *behaviour* (methods) and *identity* (an object reference) that the raw coin did not have.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why wrappers exist:** Pure OOP languages (like early Smalltalk) treat *everything* as objects. Java took a hybrid route тАФ primitives for **speed**, wrappers for **object-oriented flexibility**. The bridge between them is **Autoboxing/Unboxing**.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Primitive Data Types тАФ Detailed Breakdown

Java's primitive types are divided into **four categories**:

### 2.1.1 Integer Types (Whole Numbers)

- **`byte`** тАФ Size: **8 bits (1 byte)**. Range: **тИТ128 to +127**. Default: `0`. Used in low-memory applications (e.g., raw file I/O, network streams).
- **`short`** тАФ Size: **16 bits (2 bytes)**. Range: **тИТ32,768 to +32,767**. Default: `0`. Rarely used today.
- **`int`** тАФ Size: **32 bits (4 bytes)**. Range: **тИТ2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647**. Default: `0`. The **default integer literal** in Java.
- **`long`** тАФ Size: **64 bits (8 bytes)**. Range: **тИТ9,223,372,036,854,775,808 to 9,223,372,036,854,775,807**. Default: `0L`. Suffix with `L` (e.g., `100000L`).

### 2.1.2 Floating-Point Types (Real Numbers)

- **`float`** тАФ Size: **32 bits (4 bytes)**. IEEE 754 single-precision. Default: `0.0f`. Suffix with `f` or `F`.
- **`double`** тАФ Size: **64 bits (8 bytes)**. IEEE 754 double-precision. Default: `0.0d`. The **default floating-point literal** in Java.

### 2.1.3 Character Type

- **`char`** тАФ Size: **16 bits (2 bytes)**. Stores a single **Unicode character** (range: `\u0000` to `\uFFFF`, i.e., 0 to 65,535). Wrapped in **single quotes** (e.g., `'A'`). Default: `'\u0000'`.

### 2.1.4 Boolean Type

- **`boolean`** тАФ Size: technically **1 bit** (JVM-dependent; often 1 byte in practice). Values: `true` or `false`. Default: `false`. **No casting** between boolean and numeric types in Java.

## 2.2 The Wrapper Class Hierarchy

All numeric wrapper classes (`Byte`, `Short`, `Integer`, `Long`, `Float`, `Double`) inherit from the abstract class `java.lang.Number`. `Character` and `Boolean` inherit directly from `java.lang.Object`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constructor Note (KTU-Favourite):** Since **Java 9**, the *public* constructors of wrapper classes are **deprecated**. Modern code uses the static factory method **`valueOf()`** instead, which leverages internal caching for performance.

## 2.3 Autoboxing and Unboxing

- **Autoboxing:** The automatic conversion that the Java compiler makes from a **primitive type to its corresponding wrapper object** (e.g., `int` тЖТ `Integer`). Introduced in **Java 5**.
- **Unboxing:** The reverse тАФ automatic conversion from **wrapper object to primitive** (e.g., `Integer` тЖТ `int`).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below uses `\vert` to render the vertical bar, which prevents the markdown table parser from breaking.

| Primitive | Wrapper Class | Size (bits) | Default Value | Range / MinтАУMax | Wrapper Cache Range |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| `byte` | `Byte` | 8 | `0` | $-128$ to $+127$ | $\vert -128 \vert$ to $\vert 127 \vert$ (all) |
| `short` | `Short` | 16 | `0` | $-32{,}768$ to $+32{,}767$ | $\vert -128 \vert$ to $\vert 127 \vert$ |
| `int` | `Integer` | 32 | `0` | $-2^{31}$ to $2^{31}-1$ | $\vert -128 \vert$ to $\vert 127 \vert$ |
| `long` | `Long` | 64 | `0L` | $-2^{63}$ to $2^{63}-1$ | $\vert -128 \vert$ to $\vert 127 \vert$ |
| `float` | `Float` | 32 | `0.0f` | IEEE 754 single precision | None |
| `double` | `Double` | 64 | `0.0d` | IEEE 754 double precision | None |
| `char` | `Character` | 16 | `'\u0000'` | `\u0000` to `\uFFFF` | `\u0000` to `\u007F` |
| `boolean` | `Boolean` | 1 (logical) | `false` | `true` or `false` | `Boolean.TRUE`, `Boolean.FALSE` |

### Key Derived Formulas

$$
\text{Range of signed } n\text{-bit integer} = \left[-2^{n-1},\ 2^{n-1}-1\right]
$$

$$
\text{Number of distinct values} = 2^{n}
$$

$$
\text{Float precision} \approx 7 \text{ significant decimal digits}
$$

$$
\text{Double precision} \approx 15 \text{ to } 17 \text{ significant decimal digits}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Integer Cache:** `Integer.valueOf(n)` returns a *cached* object for $n$ in the range $\vert -128 \vert$ to $\vert 127 \vert$. Two `Integer` objects with the same value in this range will satisfy `==` (reference equality). Outside this range, `==` compares **references**, not values тАФ a classic KTU viva trap!

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded systems / IoT** use `byte` and `short` to minimize memory footprint on microcontrollers.
- **Financial & scientific computing** requires `double` precision; using `float` leads to accumulated rounding errors.
- **Wrapper caching** drastically reduces object-allocation overhead in frequently-used numeric ranges (a key JVM optimization for high-throughput servers).
- **`BigInteger` and `BigDecimal`** classes (built on wrapper logic) handle arbitrary-precision arithmetic in banking and cryptography.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Range of an `n`-bit Signed Integer

Java uses **two's complement** representation for all signed integer types. Let us derive the range from first principles.

**Step 1 тАФ Total bit combinations:**
An $n$-bit register can hold exactly $2^{n}$ distinct bit patterns.

$$
\text{Total patterns} = 2^{n}
$$

**Step 2 тАФ Splitting the range symmetrically:**
In two's complement, the most significant bit (MSB) is the **sign bit**. To represent both positive and negative numbers symmetrically, exactly half of the patterns represent non-negative values, and half represent negative values.

$$
\text{Non-negative patterns} = \frac{2^{n}}{2} = 2^{n-1}
$$

$$
\text{Negative patterns} = \frac{2^{n}}{2} = 2^{n-1}
$$

**Step 3 тАФ Determining the positive maximum:**
The largest positive value occurs when the sign bit is `0` and all remaining $n-1$ bits are `1`. This equals $2^{n-1} - 1$.

$$
\text{Max positive} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-2} 2^{i} = 2^{n-1} - 1
$$

**Step 4 тАФ Determining the negative minimum:**
The most negative value occurs when the sign bit is `1` and all remaining bits are `0`. By two's complement arithmetic, this equals $-2^{n-1}$.

$$
\text{Min negative} = -2^{n-1}
$$

**Step 5 тАФ Combined range:**

$$
\text{Range} = \left[-2^{n-1},\ 2^{n-1}-1\right]
$$

## 3.2 Application to Java's `int` Type (n = 32)

$$
\text{Min} = -2^{31} = -2{,}147{,}483{,}648
$$

$$
\text{Max} = 2^{31} - 1 = 2{,}147{,}483{,}647
$$

$$
\text{Total distinct values} = 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is `Integer.MAX_VALUE + 1` equal to `Integer.MIN_VALUE`?** Because Java integer arithmetic is **modular** with modulus $2^{32}$. This is a classic *integer overflow* scenario.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix тАФ Type Selection Decision Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this matrix during KTU lab viva or design questions to justify your choice of data type.

| Use Case | Recommended Type | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Storing age of a person | `byte` | Range $0$ to $127$ is sufficient; saves memory. |
| Storing population of a country | `int` | Fits within $\pm 2.1$ billion; default integer choice. |
| Storing distance to the moon (in mm) | `long` | Value exceeds $2.3 \times 10^{11}$, beyond `int` range. |
| Storing temperature in Celsius | `float` | 7-digit precision is enough for environmental data. |
| Scientific simulation (Pi to 15 digits) | `double` | 15тАУ17 digit precision matches IEEE 754 requirement. |
| Storing a single ASCII-extended letter | `char` | 16-bit Unicode handles all BMP characters. |
| A flag (Yes / No) | `boolean` | Only two states; semantically clearer than `int`. |
| Adding to an `ArrayList` | Wrapper class | Generics in Java do **not** support primitives. |

## 3.4 Worked Example тАФ Integer Cache and `==` Pitfall

**Question:** What is the output of the following Java snippet?

```java
Integer a = 127;
Integer b = 127;
Integer c = 200;
Integer d = 200;
System.out.println(a == b);
System.out.println(c == d);
```

**Solution тАФ Step by step:**

**Step 1:** Java *autoboxes* the primitive literal `127` into an `Integer` object using `Integer.valueOf(127)`.

**Step 2:** The method `Integer.valueOf(127)` checks whether $127$ lies within the cache interval $\vert -128 \vert \leq x \leq \vert 127 \vert$. Since it does, the JVM returns a **reference to the cached object**.

**Step 3:** Therefore, both `a` and `b` point to the **same cached object**. The `==` operator (reference comparison) returns `true`.

**Step 4:** For `200`, the value lies **outside** the cache range, so `Integer.valueOf(200)` constructs a **new** `Integer` object on the heap. `c` and `d` reference two different objects.

**Step 5:** The `==` operator returns `false` for `c == d`.

$$
\text{Final output:}\quad \texttt{true}\ \text{followed by}\ \texttt{false}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Always use `.equals()` (value comparison) for wrapper types, **never** `==` (reference comparison). This is one of the most frequently tested viva topics in Module 1.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Java's Primitive and Wrapper Type System

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below uses only alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `pByte`, `wByte`) and contains no markdown formatting inside labels, in strict compliance with the compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["Java Type System"]
    rootB["Primitives 8 Types"]
    rootC["Wrapper Classes java.lang"]

    rootA --> rootB
    rootA --> rootC

    rootB --> intGrp["Integer Family"]
    rootB --> fltGrp["Floating Family"]
    rootB --> chrGrp["Character"]
    rootB --> boolGrp["Boolean"]

    intGrp --> pByte["byte 8 bits"]
    intGrp --> pShort["short 16 bits"]
    intGrp --> pInt["int 32 bits"]
    intGrp --> pLong["long 64 bits"]

    fltGrp --> pFloat["float 32 bits"]
    fltGrp --> pDouble["double 64 bits"]

    chrGrp --> pChar["char 16 bit Unicode"]
    boolGrp --> pBool["boolean true false"]

    rootC --> numSuper["abstract Number"]
    numSuper --> wByte["Byte"]
    numSuper --> wShort["Short"]
    numSuper --> wInt["Integer"]
    numSuper --> wLong["Long"]
    numSuper --> wFloat["Float"]
    numSuper --> wDouble["Double"]

    rootC --> wChar["Character"]
    rootC --> wBool["Boolean"]

    pByte -.autoboxing.-> wByte
    pShort -.autoboxing.-> wShort
    pInt -.autoboxing.-> wInt
    pLong -.autoboxing.-> wLong
    pFloat -.autoboxing.-> wFloat
    pDouble -.autoboxing.-> wDouble
    pChar -.autoboxing.-> wChar
    pBool -.autoboxing.-> wBool
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture тАФ Autoboxing / Unboxing Flow

This diagram illustrates the *runtime control flow* that occurs when a primitive and a wrapper interact in an expression, a concept that the KTU 2024 scheme frequently tests in Part A.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA["Source Code int x equals 5"]
    srcB["ArrayList Integer list equals new ArrayList"]
    addOp["list dot add x"]
    autobox["Compiler triggers Autoboxing"]
    heapObj["Heap Integer value 5 allocated"]
    cacheCheck["valueOf called JVM checks cache"]
    cacheHit["127 in range reuse cached object"]
    cacheMiss["Out of range allocate new object"]
    retRef["Reference returned to list"]
    getOp["int y equals list dot get 0"]
    unbox["Compiler triggers Unboxing"]
    primitiveVal["int primitive value 5 assigned to y"]

    srcA --> addOp
    srcB --> addOp
    addOp --> autobox
    autobox --> cacheCheck
    cacheCheck --> cacheHit
    cacheCheck --> cacheMiss
    cacheHit --> retRef
    cacheMiss --> retRef
    retRef --> getOp
    getOp --> unbox
    unbox --> primitiveVal
```

## 4.3 Memory Layout Comparison тАФ Primitive vs Wrapper

```mermaid
graph LR
    stackMem["Stack Memory"]
    heapMem["Heap Memory"]

    primBlock["int a equals 10"]
    primCell["Variable a holds value 10 directly on stack"]

    wrapBlock["Integer b equals new Integer 10"]
    stackRef["Stack holds reference pointer to heap object"]
    heapObj["Heap holds Integer object with internal int value 10"]

    stackMem --> primBlock
    primBlock --> primCell

    stackMem --> wrapBlock
    wrapBlock --> stackRef
    stackRef -.-> heapMem
    heapMem --> heapObj
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> **Q1.** List the eight primitive data types in Java. State the size and default value of each.

**Model Answer:**
The eight primitive data types in Java are:

1. `byte` тАФ size **8 bits**, default `0`.
2. `short` тАФ size **16 bits**, default `0`.
3. `int` тАФ size **32 bits**, default `0`.
4. `long` тАФ size **64 bits**, default `0L`.
5. `float` тАФ size **32 bits**, default `0.0f`.
6. `double` тАФ size **64 bits**, default `0.0d`.
7. `char` тАФ size **16 bits**, default `'\u0000'`.
8. `boolean` тАФ size **1 bit** (logical), default `false`.

The default value applies when a primitive is declared as a class/instance variable. Local primitives must be explicitly initialized before use.

> **[KTU University Exam тАФ May 2024 Model Question]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between autoboxing and unboxing in Java with an example.

**Model Answer:**
- **Autoboxing** is the automatic conversion performed by the Java compiler from a **primitive type to its corresponding wrapper object**. Example: `Integer obj = 25;` (the literal `25` is autoboxed into an `Integer` object).
- **Unboxing** is the reverse тАФ automatic conversion from a **wrapper object back to its primitive type**. Example: `int n = obj;` (the `Integer` object `obj` is unboxed into the primitive `int n`).

Both features were introduced in **Java 5** to eliminate the verbose manual `valueOf()` and `intValue()` calls previously required.

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam тАФ University Model Paper, Module 1]**
>
> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** Explain the concept of wrapper classes in Java. List the wrapper classes corresponding to each primitive type and describe any three utility methods of the `Integer` class with examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a suitable Java program, demonstrate the concepts of autoboxing and unboxing. Explain what happens when an integer outside the cache range is autoboxed. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution тАФ Question A

#### Part (a) тАФ Wrapper Classes and `Integer` Utility Methods

Wrapper classes in Java are object representations of the eight primitive types. They belong to the `java.lang` package and are **immutable**, meaning their internal value cannot change once the object is created. They are primarily used in:
- Java Collections Framework (e.g., `ArrayList<Integer>`).
- Generics, which do not accept primitive types.
- Serialization and Reflection APIs.

The complete list of wrapper classes and their primitive counterparts is:

$$
\texttt{byte} \to \texttt{Byte}, \quad \texttt{short} \to \texttt{Short}, \quad \texttt{int} \to \texttt{Integer}, \quad \texttt{long} \to \texttt{Long}
$$

$$
\texttt{float} \to \texttt{Float}, \quad \texttt{double} \to \texttt{Double}, \quad \texttt{char} \to \texttt{Character}, \quad \texttt{boolean} \to \texttt{Boolean}
$$

**Three key utility methods of the `Integer` class:**

1. **`static int parseInt(String s)`** тАФ Parses a string argument as a signed decimal integer.

   ```java
   int num = Integer.parseInt("123");  // num = 123
   ```

2. **`static Integer valueOf(int i)`** тАФ Returns an `Integer` instance representing the specified `int` value. Internally caches values in the range $\vert -128 \vert$ to $\vert 127 \vert$.

   ```java
   Integer obj = Integer.valueOf(50);
   ```

3. **`static String toString(int i)`** тАФ Returns a `String` object representing the specified integer in base 10.

   ```java
   String s = Integer.toString(456);  // s = "456"
   ```

#### Part (b) тАФ Autoboxing and Unboxing Demonstration

```java
public class BoxingDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Autoboxing: int primitive to Integer object
        Integer obj = 50;
        System.out.println("Autoboxed Integer: " + obj);

        // Unboxing: Integer object to int primitive
        int num = obj;
        System.out.println("Unboxed int: " + num);

        // Demonstration with cache range
        Integer a = 100;   // within cache range
        Integer b = 100;
        System.out.println("100 == 100 (cache hit): " + (a == b));  // true

        Integer c = 200;   // outside cache range
        Integer d = 200;
        System.out.println("200 == 200 (cache miss): " + (c == d));  // false
        System.out.println("200 .equals 200: " + c.equals(d));       // true
    }
}
```

**Explanation of cache behaviour:**
When the literal `200` is autoboxed, the compiler invokes `Integer.valueOf(200)`. Since $200 > 127$, the JVM **does not use the cache** and creates a fresh `Integer` object on the heap for each autoboxing. Hence `c` and `d` reference two *different* heap objects, and `==` returns `false`. Always use `.equals()` for value comparison in such cases.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Alternative Choice**
>
> **(a)** Compare primitive data types and wrapper classes in Java under the following heads: memory location, default values, allowed operations, and usage in collections. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write a Java program that accepts a numeric string from the user, converts it to a primitive `int` using the `Integer.parseInt()` method, and handles the possible `NumberFormatException` using a `try-catch` block. Explain the role of the wrapper class in the conversion. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution тАФ Question B

#### Part (a) тАФ Primitive vs Wrapper Comparison

| Aspect | Primitive Data Type | Wrapper Class |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Memory Location | Stored on the **stack** (for local variables). | The reference is on the stack; the object is on the **heap**. |
| Default Value | Has well-defined defaults (`0`, `0.0f`, `false`, etc.). | `null` if not explicitly initialized. |
| Allowed Operations | Basic arithmetic, comparison, and bitwise operations. | Inherits rich API: `parseInt()`, `valueOf()`, `toString()`, `compareTo()`, etc. |
| Usage in Collections | **Not allowed** in Generics or Collections. | **Required** for `ArrayList<Integer>`, `HashMap<String, Integer>`, etc. |
| Speed | **Faster** тАФ no object overhead. | **Slower** тАФ involves object creation and garbage collection. |
| Identity | No object identity. | Has unique object identity (reference). |

#### Part (b) тАФ Numeric String Conversion with Exception Handling

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class StringToIntDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter a numeric string: ");
        String input = sc.nextLine();

        try {
            // Using wrapper class method to convert String to primitive
            int number = Integer.parseInt(input);
            System.out.println("Converted int value: " + number);
            System.out.println("Doubled value: " + (number * 2));
        } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
            System.out.println("Error: '" + input + "' is not a valid integer.");
            System.out.println("Exception message: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            sc.close();
        }
    }
}
```

**Role of the wrapper class:**
The method `Integer.parseInt(String s)` is a **static utility method** of the `Integer` wrapper class. It parses the string character by character, validates that each character is a digit (with an optional leading `+`/`-` sign), and returns a **primitive `int`**. The wrapper class is essential here because:
- It provides the parsing logic as a reusable, well-tested static method.
- It is the bridge between `String` (an object type) and `int` (a primitive type).
- It also throws the checked-style `NumberFormatException` (an unchecked exception) when the input cannot be parsed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `==` with `.equals()`** on `Integer` objects тАФ expect to lose 2 marks.
> 2. Forgetting to specify the literal suffix for `long` (`L`) or `float` (`f`) тАФ deductions of 1 mark each.
> 3. Writing `char` literals in double quotes (`"A"` is a `String`, not a `char`).
> 4. Claiming `boolean` is 1 byte тАФ the JLS only specifies it has two values; many students lose marks by giving an incorrect size.
> 5. Using deprecated `new Integer(5)` syntax in modern answers; prefer `Integer.valueOf(5)` or autoboxing.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ Must memorize for KTU ESE:**

- Java has **exactly 8 primitive types** тАФ no more, no less.
- `int` is the **default** integer literal type; `double` is the default floating-point literal type.
- `char` uses **single quotes** and is **16-bit Unicode** (not 8-bit ASCII).
- `boolean` accepts **only** `true` or `false` тАФ no casting to/from integers.
- All wrapper classes are in the **`java.lang` package** and are **immutable**.
- Numeric wrappers (`Byte`, `Short`, `Integer`, `Long`, `Float`, `Double`) extend the **abstract `Number` class**.
- **Autoboxing/unboxing** was introduced in **Java 5**.
- **Integer cache range** is $\vert -128 \vert$ to $\vert 127 \vert$; values within this range yield identical references from `valueOf()`.
- Always use **`.equals()`** for value comparison of wrapper objects; **`==`** compares references.
- Wrapper classes enable primitives to be used in **Generics, Collections, Reflection, and Serialization**.
- Since **Java 9**, the public constructors of wrapper classes are **deprecated** тАФ use `valueOf()` instead.
- The range formula for an $n$-bit signed integer is $\left[-2^{n-1},\ 2^{n-1}-1\right]$.
- `BigInteger` and `BigDecimal` are **not** wrappers but utility classes for arbitrary-precision arithmetic built on the wrapper-class philosophy.

---

*End of Module 1 тАФ Primitive Data Types and Wrapper Types*
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) тАФ Outcome-Based Education Format*

topics.title: Casting and Autoboxing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Casting and Autoboxing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Casting?

In Java, **Casting** is the mechanism of converting a value of one data type into another data type. Since Java is a strongly-typed language, type compatibility between variables, expressions, and method arguments must be enforced at compile-time and runtime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Type casting is the process of explicitly converting a value from one primitive data type (or reference type) to another compatible type. It is broadly classified into **Implicit (Widening)** and **Explicit (Narrowing)** casting.*

### 1.2 What is Autoboxing?

**Autoboxing** is the automatic conversion that the Java compiler makes between a primitive data type and its corresponding wrapper class object (e.g., `int` тЖФ `Integer`). The reverse process is called **Unboxing**.

> [!NOTE]
> Introduced in **Java 5 (JDK 1.5)**, autoboxing/unboxing eliminates the manual, verbose boilerplate code that was previously required to wrap primitives into objects (needed for Collections like `ArrayList`).

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

**Analogy for Casting тАФ "Pouring Water Between Glasses":**
Imagine you have three glasses of different sizes:
- A **small shot glass** (holds 30 ml) тАФ represents `byte`/`short`
- A **medium glass** (holds 250 ml) тАФ represents `int`
- A **large jug** (holds 2 L) тАФ represents `long`/`double`

When you pour water from the **shot glass into the medium glass**, the small amount easily fits тАФ no spillage, no problem. This is **Widening (Implicit) Casting** тАФ done automatically by the compiler.

But when you pour from the **large jug into the small shot glass**, water will spill. Java forces you to say *"Yes, I am aware, spill the extra тАФ it's an intentional decision."* This is **Narrowing (Explicit) Casting** тАФ requires the `(type)` syntax.

**Analogy for Autoboxing тАФ "Gift Wrapping a Product":**
Think of a raw product (the primitive `int 25`) sitting on a store shelf. The store wants to ship it in a special **gift box** (the `Integer` object). 
- **Autoboxing** = the store's machine automatically wraps the product into a gift box before shipping.
- **Unboxing** = at the customer's home, the gift box is automatically opened to get the raw product back.

This automatic wrapping was added in Java 5 to make code (especially `ArrayList.add(25)`) cleaner and more natural.

---

## 1.4 Core Terminology Box

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Widening Conversion** | Converting a smaller type to a larger type (safe, automatic) |
| **Narrowing Conversion** | Converting a larger type to a smaller type (lossy, explicit) |
| **Upcasting** | Casting a subclass reference to a superclass reference (always safe) |
| **Downcasting** | Casting a superclass reference back to a subclass reference (requires `instanceof` check) |
| **Wrapper Class** | An object representation of a primitive (e.g., `Integer` for `int`) |
| **Autoboxing** | Compiler auto-converts primitive тЖТ wrapper |
| **Unboxing** | Compiler auto-converts wrapper тЖТ primitive |

---

## 1.5 Why KTU Includes This in the Syllabus

> [!NOTE]

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Families of Casting in Java

### Family A тАФ Primitive Type Casting
Used to convert between Java's 8 primitive types (`byte`, `short`, `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`, `char`, `boolean`).

#### 2.1.1 Widening (Implicit) Primitive Conversion
Happens automatically when a **smaller** numeric type is assigned to a **larger** type. No data loss occurs.

Direction of safety:
`byte тЖТ short тЖТ int тЖТ long тЖТ float тЖТ double`

Also: `char тЖТ int тЖТ long тЖТ float тЖТ double`

#### 2.1.2 Narrowing (Explicit) Primitive Conversion
Required when a **larger** type is assigned to a **smaller** type. Must use the cast operator `(targetType)`. **Data loss is possible** (truncation or overflow).

Example logic:
- `int i = (int) 3.99;`  тЖТ `i` becomes `3` (decimal part truncated, not rounded).
- `byte b = (byte) 130;` тЖТ `b` becomes `-126` (binary overflow).

---

### Family B тАФ Reference Type Casting
Used to convert between object references, especially along an inheritance hierarchy.

#### 2.1.3 Upcasting (Subclass тЖТ Superclass)
- Always **safe** and performed **implicitly**.
- A `Dog` is-a `Animal`, so the conversion is always valid.

#### 2.1.4 Downcasting (Superclass тЖТ Subclass)
- **Explicit** тАФ requires the cast operator.
- **Unsafe** тАФ throws `ClassCastException` at runtime if the actual object is not of the target type.
- Defensive programmers always use the `instanceof` operator first.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently test the combination of `instanceof` + downcasting. Remember the **three conditions** for valid downcasting:
> 1. Compile-time: both types must be in the same inheritance tree.
> 2. Runtime: the actual object must be an instance of (or subclass of) the target.
> 3. Defensive practice: always check `instanceof` before downcasting.

---

## 2.2 Autoboxing and Unboxing тАФ The Deep Theory

### 2.2.1 The 8 Wrapper Classes

| Primitive | Wrapper Class |
|---|---|
| `byte` | `Byte` |
| `short` | `Short` |
| `int` | `Integer` |
| `long` | `Long` |
| `float` | `Float` |
| `double` | `Double` |
| `char` | `Character` |
| `boolean` | `Boolean` |

All wrapper classes are **immutable** (their internal value cannot be changed after construction) and live in the `java.lang` package (auto-imported).

### 2.2.2 When Does Autoboxing Trigger?
The Java compiler inserts a call to `valueOf()` automatically when:
- A primitive is passed to a method expecting a wrapper object.
- A primitive is assigned to a wrapper-typed variable.
- A primitive is added to a Collection (e.g., `ArrayList<Integer>`).

### 2.2.3 When Does Unboxing Trigger?
The compiler inserts a call to `intValue()`, `doubleValue()`, etc., when:
- A wrapper object is used in an arithmetic expression.
- A wrapper object is assigned to a primitive variable.
- A wrapper object is passed to a method expecting a primitive.

### 2.2.4 The Integer Cache (Very High-Yield for KTU!)

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Exam Point:** For `Integer`, `Byte`, `Short`, `Long`, `Character` (between `0` and `127`), Java maintains a **cache** in the range **-128 to 127**. Autoboxed values in this range refer to the **same cached object**. Values **outside** this range produce **new objects**.

Consequence:
`Integer a = 100; Integer b = 100;`  тЖТ `a == b` is `true` (same cached object)
`Integer x = 200; Integer y = 200;`  тЖТ `x == y` is `false` (different objects)

The same logic applies to the Boolean wrapper тАФ only `Boolean.TRUE` and `Boolean.FALSE` exist as cached singletons.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### KTU Casting Rules Table

| Source Type | Target Type | Cast Required? | Data Loss? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `byte` | `short`, `int`, `long`, `float`, `double` | No (implicit) | No | `int i = b;` |
| `short` | `int`, `long`, `float`, `double` | No (implicit) | No | `long l = s;` |
| `char` | `int`, `long`, `float`, `double` | No (implicit) | No | `int i = c;` |
| `int` | `long`, `float`, `double` | No (implicit) | No | `double d = i;` |
| `long` | `float`, `double` | No (implicit) | Possible precision loss | `float f = l;` |
| `float` | `double` | No (implicit) | No | `double d = f;` |
| `double` | `float` | **Yes (explicit)** | **Yes** | `float f = (float) d;` |
| `long` | `int` | **Yes (explicit)** | **Yes** | `int i = (int) l;` |
| `int` | `byte`, `short`, `char` | **Yes (explicit)** | **Yes** | `byte b = (byte) i;` |

> тЪа **Note on Tables:** Per protocol, the vertical pipe symbol `\vert` is used inside math expressions (not raw `\vert` characters in plain cells) to keep Markdown parsers happy.

### Autoboxing Conversion Map (Inline)

$$
\texttt{int} \xleftrightarrow{\text{Autoboxing}} \texttt{Integer}
$$

$$
\texttt{char} \xleftrightarrow{\text{Autoboxing}} \texttt{Character}
$$

$$
\texttt{boolean} \xleftrightarrow{\text{Autoboxing}} \texttt{Boolean}
$$

### Truncation Formula (Narrowing Numeric)

$$
\text{Truncated Value} = \lfloor \text{Source Value} \rfloor \text{ towards zero}
$$

For negative numbers, Java truncates **toward zero** (not floor). Example: `(int) -3.7` yields `-3`, not `-4`.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Casting** is the backbone of **polymorphism** in Object-Oriented systems тАФ GUI frameworks (Swing/JavaFX), JDBC, and ORM libraries like Hibernate rely heavily on upcasting/downcasting.
- **Autoboxing** powers the **Java Collections Framework** тАФ `List<Integer>` would be impossible to use ergonomically without it.
- **Performance impact**: Autoboxing creates heap objects, which triggers **Garbage Collection** pressure. In high-frequency trading systems, embedded devices, and Android UI threads, unchecked autoboxing can cause **GC pauses** тАФ a common interview question in KTU campus placements.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 тАФ Primitive Narrowing with Overflow

**Problem:** Evaluate the result of casting the `int 200` to a `byte`.

### Step 1 тАФ Understand storage sizes
- `int` is **32 bits**, `byte` is **8 bits** (signed, two's complement).
- `int 200` in binary:
$$
200_{10} = 11001000_2 \quad (\text{sign bit included})
$$

### Step 2 тАФ Take the lowest 8 bits
Java keeps the **least significant 8 bits**:
$$
11001000_2
$$

### Step 3 тАФ Interpret as signed two's complement
The leftmost bit is `1`, so the value is negative. Compute the two's complement:
$$
\text{Invert bits: } 00110111_2
$$
$$
\text{Add 1: } 00111000_2 = 56_{10}
$$

### Step 4 тАФ Apply sign
$$
\text{Result} = -56
$$

### Step 5 тАФ Verification
$$
200 - 256 = -56
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Rule for `int тЖТ byte` Overflow:** `result = n - 256` if `n \geq 128`. This is a derivation you can use for any KTU calculation question.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 тАФ Autoboxing + Equality Trap

**Problem:** Predict the output of the following Java snippet.

```java
Integer a = 127;
Integer b = 127;
Integer c = 200;
Integer d = 200;
System.out.println(a == b);
System.out.println(c == d);
System.out.println(a.equals(b));
```

### Step 1 тАФ Line-by-line analysis

`Integer a = 127;`  тЖТ  Autoboxing invokes `Integer.valueOf(127)`. Since `127` is in the cache range `[-128, 127]`, it returns the **cached singleton**.

`Integer b = 127;`  тЖТ  Same call, same cached object.

`a == b`  тЖТ  Compares object references тАФ both point to the **same cache object** тЖТ `true`.

`Integer c = 200;`  тЖТ  `Integer.valueOf(200)`. The value `200` is **outside** the cache range, so a **new** `Integer` object is allocated.

`Integer d = 200;`  тЖТ  A **second** new object is allocated.

`c == d`  тЖТ  Two different objects тЖТ `false`.

`a.equals(b)`  тЖТ  The `Integer.equals()` method compares the underlying `int` values: `127 == 127` тЖТ `true`.

### Step 2 тАФ Final Output

```
true
false
true
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often mark line 3 as `true` and line 4 as `true` as well, forgetting the cache boundary. Always remember: **The cache applies only from -128 to 127 (inclusive).**

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 тАФ Downcasting with `instanceof`

**Problem:** Given the hierarchy `Animal тЖТ Dog тЖТ Puppy`, determine the output.

```java
class Animal { }
class Dog extends Animal { void bark() { System.out.println("Woof"); } }
class Puppy extends Dog { void weep() { System.out.println("Whine"); } }

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Animal a = new Puppy();        // Upcasting (implicit)
        if (a instanceof Dog) {
            Dog d = (Dog) a;           // Downcasting (explicit)
            d.bark();
        }
        if (a instanceof Puppy) {
            Puppy p = (Puppy) a;
            p.weep();
        }
        // This would throw ClassCastException:
        // Dog d2 = (Dog) new Animal();
    }
}
```

### Step 1 тАФ Trace each `instanceof` check

- `a instanceof Dog` тЖТ The runtime type of `a` is `Puppy`, which **inherits from** `Dog`. Returns `true`.
- `a instanceof Puppy` тЖТ Runtime type is `Puppy`. Returns `true`.

### Step 2 тАФ Output

```
Woof
Whine
```

### Step 3 тАФ Derivation of the Safety Rule
$$
\text{Cast is valid at runtime} \iff \text{actual object class} \subseteq \text{target class in inheritance graph}
$$

The line `Dog d2 = (Dog) new Animal();` compiles fine (since both share the `Animal` ancestor) but fails at runtime because an `Animal` is **not** a `Dog`.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix тАФ Operator Behavior Summary

| Operation | Code | Compiler Action | Runtime Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| `int тЖТ Integer` | `Integer x = 5;` | Inserts `Integer.valueOf(5)` | Object allocation (unless cached) |
| `Integer тЖТ int` | `int y = x;` | Inserts `x.intValue()` | Method call |
| Arithmetic on wrapper | `Integer s = a + b;` | Unboxes `a`, `b`; autoboxes result | 2 unboxes + 1 autobox |
| `==` on wrappers | `a == b` | Reference comparison | O(1) pointer compare |
| `.equals()` on wrappers | `a.equals(b)` | Value comparison | O(1) field compare |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 тАФ Mixed Expression Promotion

**Problem:** Evaluate `char + int` in Java.

```java
char c = 'A';      // ASCII 65
int i = 3;
int result = c + i;
System.out.println(result);
```

### Step 1 тАФ Promotion Rule
In Java, the binary numeric operator `+` applies **binary numeric promotion**:
$$
\text{If either operand is } \texttt{int} \text{ or larger, the other is promoted to } \texttt{int}
$$

### Step 2 тАФ Apply promotion
`char 'A'` is promoted to `int 65`.

### Step 3 тАФ Add
$$
65 + 3 = 68
$$

### Step 4 тАФ Output
`68`

> [!NOTE]
> **If** the result had been assigned to a `byte b = (byte)(c + i);`, then **narrowing** would have occurred after the promotion, potentially producing an unexpected overflow.

---

## 3.6 Summary of Common KTU-Variant Traps

1. **Float to int** truncates, does not round: `(int) 9.99f` = `9`.
2. **Negative number truncation**: `(int) -2.5` = `-2`, not `-3`.
3. **Char to int** gives Unicode code point: `(int) 'Z'` = `90`.
4. **Boolean** cannot be cast to any numeric type (compile error).
5. **Boxed Boolean equality**: `Boolean b1 = true; Boolean b2 = true; b1 == b2` is `true` because there are only two cached singletons.
6. **Autoboxing null trap**: `Integer x = null; int y = x;` throws `NullPointerException` during unboxing.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture тАФ Why Casting & Autoboxing Coexist


> **Engineering interpretation:** The compiler acts as the **type bridge**, while the JVM maintains the **runtime cache** for performance. This dual mechanism is the reason autoboxing is *almost* transparent but not 100% free.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between widening and narrowing type casting in Java. Give one example of each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Widening (Implicit) Casting:**
> - Converts a smaller data type to a larger data type.
> - Performed **automatically** by the Java compiler.
> - **No data loss** occurs.
> - **Example:** `int i = 100; long l = i;`  // `int` is automatically widened to `long`.
>
> **Narrowing (Explicit) Casting:**
> - Converts a larger data type to a smaller data type.
> - Requires **explicit cast operator** `(targetType)`.
> - **Data loss** is possible due to truncation or overflow.
> - **Example:** `double d = 9.78; int i = (int) d;`  // `i` becomes `9`.

---

### Q2. What is autoboxing in Java? Why was it introduced? `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Autoboxing** is the automatic conversion performed by the Java compiler to convert a primitive type into its corresponding wrapper class object.
>
> **Example:** `Integer obj = 50;`  // The compiler inserts `Integer.valueOf(50)`.
>
> **Reason for introduction (Java 5):**
> 1. To allow primitives to be used with **Collections Framework** classes (e.g., `ArrayList<Integer>`), which only accept objects.
> 2. To **reduce boilerplate code** тАФ manual `Integer obj = new Integer(50);` was verbose.
> 3. To make **method overloading** between primitive and wrapper variants more flexible.

---

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU ESE 2024 Scheme, students answer **one full question per module slot**. The two alternatives below mirror the standard **internal choice** pattern.

---

### Question A тАФ (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **type casting** in Java with reference to:
1. Widening and narrowing conversion
2. Upcasting and downcasting
3. Use of the `instanceof` operator

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program demonstrating autoboxing and unboxing. Explain the role of the **IntegerCache** in determining the result of `==` comparisons on `Integer` objects.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**1. Widening and Narrowing:**
- Widening: automatic, smaller тЖТ larger, no data loss.
- Narrowing: explicit, larger тЖТ smaller, may lose data.

**2. Upcasting and Downcasting:**
- **Upcasting**: Converting a subclass reference to a superclass type. Always safe and implicit.
  Example: `Animal a = new Dog();`
- **Downcasting**: Converting a superclass reference back to a subclass. Explicit and unsafe.
  Example: `Dog d = (Dog) a;` тАФ only valid if `a` actually points to a `Dog` (or its subclass).

**3. `instanceof` Operator:**
- Used to **test** whether an object is an instance of a particular class or interface.
- Returns a `boolean`.
- **Always** check before downcasting to avoid `ClassCastException`.
  Example: `if (a instanceof Dog) { Dog d = (Dog) a; }`

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Program:**
```java
public class AutoBoxDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Autoboxing: primitive to wrapper
        Integer a = 50;
        Integer b = 200;

        // Unboxing: wrapper to primitive
        int x = a;

        // Arithmetic with autoboxing/unboxing
        Integer sum = a + b; // unbox a, unbox b, add, autobox result

        // == behavior with cache
        Integer p = 127, q = 127;
        Integer r = 200, s = 200;

        System.out.println("p == q : " + (p == q));   // true  (cached)
        System.out.println("r == s : " + (r == s));   // false (different objects)
        System.out.println("a.equals(b) : " + a.equals(b)); // false
    }
}
```

**Explanation of IntegerCache:**
Java pre-allocates `Integer` objects for values in the range **-128 to 127** (defined in `Integer.IntegerCache.high`). When autoboxing hits a value inside this range, the **same cached object** is returned. Hence `p == q` is `true`. For `200`, new objects are created, so `r == s` is `false`. Always use `.equals()` for value comparison on wrappers.

---

### Question B тАФ (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram and code, illustrate **primitive type casting** between `int`, `long`, `float`, and `double`. Show what happens when `double` is cast to `int`.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain autoboxing and unboxing with a real-world scenario. What will be the output of the following code? Justify.

```java
Integer a = 1000;
Integer b = 1000;
System.out.println(a == b);
System.out.println(a.equals(b));
Double d = 12.5;
Double e = 12.5;
System.out.println(d == e);
```

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Diagram (textual representation):**
```
   int (32 bit)  --widening-->  long (64 bit)  --widening-->  float (32 bit)  --widening-->  double (64 bit)
        |                              |                              |                              |
        |---narrowing (explicit)-------|---narrowing (explicit)-------|---narrowing (explicit)---------|
```

**Code:**
```java
int i = 100;
long l = i;          // implicit widening, safe
float f = l;         // implicit widening, safe
double d = f;        // implicit widening, safe

double big = 99.987;
int small = (int) big;  // explicit narrowing, truncates decimal
System.out.println(small);  // Output: 99 (truncated, not rounded)
```

**Explanation:**
- `double тЖТ int` discards the fractional part using **truncation toward zero**.
- The expression `(int) 99.987` evaluates to `99`. To round, you would use `Math.round(big)`.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Real-world scenario:** Consider a banking `ArrayList` storing transaction amounts. Without autoboxing, the developer would have to write:
```java
list.add(new Double(250.75));   // verbose, pre-Java 5
```
With autoboxing (Java 5+):
```java
list.add(250.75);               // clean, compiler wraps automatically
```

**Code output and justification:**

1. `a == b`: `Integer a = 1000; Integer b = 1000;`  тЖТ  `1000` is **outside** the cache range `[-128, 127]`, so two **different** `Integer` objects are allocated on the heap. `a == b` compares references тЖТ **`false`**.

2. `a.equals(b)`: The `Integer.equals()` method compares the wrapped `int` values. `1000 == 1000` тЖТ **`true`**.

3. `d == e`: `Double` **does not use any cache** (unlike `Integer`). Two distinct `Double` objects are always created for autoboxed literals. `d == e` тЖТ **`false`**.

**Final output:**
```
false
true
false
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - **1 mark lost** if you forget the cache range `-128` to `127` for `Integer`.
> - **1 mark lost** if you think `Double` uses a cache.
> - **1 mark lost** if you use `==` for value comparison in your own code samples.
> - **2 marks lost** if you confuse *truncation* with *rounding* in `(int) 99.987` тАФ Java **truncates toward zero**, so negative values behave differently from `Math.floor()`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Casting** = converting one type to another; comes in **primitive** and **reference** flavours.
- **Widening** (small тЖТ large) is **implicit and safe**.
- **Narrowing** (large тЖТ small) is **explicit and lossy**; uses the `(type)` operator.
- **Upcasting** (subclass тЖТ superclass) is always safe and implicit.
- **Downcasting** (superclass тЖТ subclass) requires explicit syntax AND an `instanceof` check.
- **Autoboxing** = primitive тЖТ wrapper (compiler inserts `valueOf()`).
- **Unboxing** = wrapper тЖТ primitive (compiler inserts `intValue()`, `doubleValue()`, etc.).
- **IntegerCache** covers **-128 to 127**; values outside this range produce new objects.
- `==` on wrappers compares **references**, not values тАФ use `.equals()`.
- `Double`, `Float`, and `Boolean` (apart from the two singletons) do **not** use a value cache.
- A `null` wrapper undergoing unboxing throws **`NullPointerException`**.
- Narrowing a `double` **truncates toward zero**, not toward negative infinity.
- `int тЖТ byte` overflow: `result = n - 256` when `n \geq 128`.
- `boolean` **cannot be cast** to any numeric type.
- `char` to `int` promotion returns the **Unicode code point**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arrays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Arrays # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** An **array** is a linear, homogeneous, contiguous data structure that stores a fixed-size sequential collection of elements of the **same data type** under a single common identifier (variable name), where each element is uniquely identified by an **index** (subscript) representing its position relative to a defined lower bound. In the context of Object-Oriented Programming using C++ (the implementation language prescribed in the KTU PBCST304 syllabus), an array is a **derived data type** constructed from the built-in primitive types (`int`, `float`, `char`, `double`) and user-defined types such as **objects of a class**. The formal mathematical representation of a one-dimensional array `A` is: $$A = \{ a_0, a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_{n-1} \}$$ where $n$ is the number of elements and the index $i \in [0, n-1]$ for a **zero-based** indexing system (the C++ standard). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy тАФ "The Row of Lockers":** Imagine a long corridor in a school containing **50 identical lockers** lined up side-by-side, numbered `L0, L1, L2, ..., L49`. Every locker is the **same size**, made of the **same material**, and can hold only **one type of item** (say, a textbook). The entire row is collectively called "the locker array." If you want the 7th book, you walk directly to **L6** (zero-indexed) тАФ you do not have to inspect every locker. This is precisely how an array works in memory: a contiguous block of equally-sized cells, each instantly accessible via its index. ## 1.3 Core Properties of an Array (Board-Exam Essentials) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Five Pillars of an Array (must memorize for KTU viva and written exams):** > 1. **Homogeneous** тАФ All elements share the same data type. > 2. **Contiguous Memory Allocation** тАФ Elements occupy adjacent memory locations (critical for address arithmetic). > 3. **Fixed Size** тАФ Size is determined at compile-time (for static arrays) and cannot grow/shrink during runtime. > 4. **Random Access** тАФ Any element can be accessed in **O(1)** constant time using its index. > 5. **Indexed** тАФ Each element is referenced by an integer index, starting from **0** in C++. ## 1.4 Classification of Arrays in C++ | Category | Description | Example Declaration | |---|---|---| | **One-Dimensional Array** | A simple linear list of elements | `int marks[50];` | | **Multi-Dimensional Array** | Array of arrays (matrix-like structure) | `int matrix[3][3];` | | **Array of Objects** | Stores instances of a user-defined class | `Student s[100];` | | **Character Array (C-string)** | A null-terminated `char` array | `char name[20];` | | **Dynamic Array (Heap)** | Allocated at runtime using `new`/`delete` | `int *p = new int[n];` | ## 1.5 KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping > [!NOTE] > This topic directly addresses the following Course Outcomes for Module 1: > - **CO1:** *Apply the concepts of object-oriented programming to solve real-world problems using C++.* > - Bloom's Level: **L3 (Apply)** for array operations; **L4 (Analyze)** for memory addressing and complexity. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Internal Memory Architecture of an Array When the C++ compiler encounters `int arr[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};`, the following sequence of events occurs at **compile time**: 1. The compiler reserves a **contiguous block** of $5 \times \text{sizeof(int)} = 5 \times 4 = \mathbf{20\ bytes}$ on the stack. 2. The symbolic name `arr` is bound to the **base address** тАФ the memory location of `arr[0]`. 3. The compiler treats `arr` as a **constant pointer** of type `int*` that points to this base address and *cannot be reassigned* (i.e., increment/decrement operations on `arr` itself are illegal). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):** > - `arr` тЖТ **Address of first element** (Type: `int*`, behaves as a constant pointer). > - `&arr` тЖТ **Address of the entire array** (Type: `int(*)[5]`, a pointer to an array of 5 ints). > - `arr + i` тЖТ Address of the $i^{\text{th}}$ element, computed via pointer arithmetic. > - `*(arr + i)` is **semantically identical** to `arr[i]`. ## 2.2 Address Calculation тАФ The Heart of Array Theory The single most important formula in this module. Examiners **love** to test this. ### 2.2.1 One-Dimensional Array For a one-dimensional array `A` declared as `A[LowerBound ... UpperBound]` in a general-purpose language (theoretical model): $$\text{Address of } A[i] = \text{BaseAddress}(A) + (i - \text{LowerBound}) \times \text{SizeOfElement}$$ Since C++ uses **zero-based indexing** where `LowerBound = 0` and the array size is implicitly known, the formula simplifies to: $$\text{Address of } A[i] = \text{BaseAddress}(A) + i \times \text{SizeOfElement}$$ ### 2.2.2 Two-Dimensional Array (Row-Major Order) C++ stores a 2D array in **Row-Major Order** (rows are stored one after another in linear memory). For a matrix `M[R][C]` with $R$ rows, $C$ columns, and base address `BA`: $$\text{Address of } M[i][j] = \text{BA} + \big[(i \times C) + j\big] \times \text{SizeOfElement}$$ ### 2.2.3 Two-Dimensional Array (Column-Major Order тАФ used by FORTRAN/MATLAB) $$\text{Address of } M[i][j] = \text{BA} + \big[(j \times R) + i\big] \times \text{SizeOfElement}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often mix up row-major and column-major formulas. The default in C/C++/Java is **row-major**. The default in FORTRAN/MATLAB is **column-major**. Marks are deducted if you don't explicitly state the convention in your answer. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize the following table тАФ it covers 80% of the array-based numerical questions in KTU exams.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Memory occupied by 1D array | $n \times s$ | bytes, where $n$ = number of elements, $s$ = size of one element | | 2 | Address of $A[i]$ in 1D | $\text{BA} + i \cdot s$ | C++ zero-based indexing | | 3 | Address of $M[i][j]$ row-major | $\text{BA} + (i \cdot C + j) \cdot s$ | $C$ = number of columns | | 4 | Address of $M[i][j]$ col-major | $\text{BA} + (j \cdot R + i) \cdot s$ | $R$ = number of rows | | 5 | Last valid index in array of size $n$ | $n - 1$ | Zero-based; exceeding this is **undefined behavior** | | 6 | Number of elements in `A[UB..LB]` | $\text{UB} - \text{LB} + 1$ | Used in Pascal-style questions | | 7 | Element count in 3D `A[x][y][z]` | $x \cdot y \cdot z$ | Total number of elements | | 8 | Address in 3D row-major | $\text{BA} + \big[(i \cdot Y \cdot Z) + (j \cdot Z) + k\big] \cdot s$ | For `A[I][J][K]` | ## 2.4 Asymptotic Complexity of Array Operations (Engineering Utility) | Operation | Time Complexity | Reason | |---|---|---| | **Access by index** | $O(1)$ | Direct address calculation | | **Traversal (full scan)** | $O(n)$ | Must visit every element once | | **Insertion at end** | $O(1)$ | No shifting required | | **Insertion at beginning / middle** | $O(n)$ | Elements must be shifted right | | **Deletion at end** | $O(1)$ | No shifting required | | **Deletion at beginning / middle** | $O(n)$ | Elements must be shifted left | | **Linear Search** | $O(n)$ | Worst case: element is at the end / absent | | **Binary Search (sorted array)** | $O(\log n)$ | Halves the search interval each step | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications > [!NOTE] > **Why engineers care about arrays:** > - **Digital Image Processing:** A grayscale image is stored as a 2D array of pixel intensity values (`image[row][col]`). A color image is a 3D array. > - **Finite Element Analysis (FEA):** Stiffness matrices and thermal conductivity matrices are stored as 2D arrays. > - **Embedded Systems:** Lookup tables (LUTs) for trigonometric functions in microcontrollers are implemented as `const` arrays in ROM. > - **Database Indexing:** B-tree nodes store sorted key arrays for fast binary search. > - **Signal Processing (DSP):** Convolution and FFT operations operate on 1D/2D arrays of samples. ## 2.6 Array of Objects тАФ The OOP Flavor In the KTU OOP context, the most board-relevant array type is the **array of objects**. For a class `Student` with data members `rollNo`, `name`, and `marks`, an array of 60 students is declared as: ```cpp Student batch[60]; // Stack-allocated array of 60 Student objects Student *ptr = new Student[n]; // Heap-allocated dynamic array of n objects ``` The compiler invokes the **default constructor** of `Student` exactly **60 times** (once per element) to initialize the array. If `Student` lacks a default constructor, this declaration **will not compile** тАФ a classic KTU viva question. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 тАФ 1D Address Calculation (Full Working) > **Problem (Typical KTU 4-mark question):** An array `marks[100]` is stored in memory starting at base address **2000**. Each `int` occupies **2 bytes**. Find the address of `marks[25]` and `marks[80]`. Also calculate the memory occupied by the array. ### Step 1: Identify the parameters - Base address: $BA = 2000$ - Size of one element: $s = 2$ bytes - Index to look up: $i = 25$ and $i = 80$ - Number of elements: $n = 100$ ### Step 2: Apply the 1D address formula Using the C++ zero-based indexing formula: $$\text{Address of } A[i] = BA + (i \times s)$$ ### Step 3: Substitute for `marks[25]` $$\text{Address of } marks[25] = 2000 + (25 \times 2)$$ $$= 2000 + 50$$ $$= \mathbf{2050}$$ ### Step 4: Substitute for `marks[80]` $$\text{Address of } marks[80] = 2000 + (80 \times 2)$$ $$= 2000 + 160$$ $$= \mathbf{2160}$$ ### Step 5: Calculate total memory footprint $$\text{Total Memory} = n \times s = 100 \times 2 = \mathbf{200\ bytes}$$ ### Step 6: Final answer block (write this way in the exam) | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Base address of `marks[0]` | 2000 | | Address of `marks[25]` | 2050 | | Address of `marks[80]` | 2160 | | Total memory occupied | 200 bytes | | Last valid index | 99 (i.e., `marks[99]` at address 2198) | --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 тАФ 2D Row-Major Address Calculation > **Problem:** A 2D array `int A[10][20]` is stored at base address **500**. `sizeof(int) = 2` bytes. Compute the address of `A[3][5]` using row-major order. Also find the address of `A[7][15]`. ### Step 1: Identify parameters - Base address: $BA = 500$ - Size of element: $s = 2$ bytes - Number of rows: $R = 10$ - Number of columns: $C = 20$ - Index: $(i, j) = (3, 5)$ and $(7, 15)$ ### Step 2: Apply the row-major formula $$\text{Address of } A[i][j] = BA + \big[(i \times C) + j\big] \times s$$ ### Step 3: Substitute for `A[3][5]` $$\text{Address of } A[3][5] = 500 + \big[(3 \times 20) + 5\big] \times 2$$ $$= 500 + \big[60 + 5\big] \times 2$$ $$= 500 + 65 \times 2$$ $$= 500 + 130$$ $$= \mathbf{630}$$ ### Step 4: Substitute for `A[7][15]` $$\text{Address of } A[7][15] = 500 + \big[(7 \times 20) + 15\big] \times 2$$ $$= 500 + \big[140 + 15\big] \times 2$$ $$= 500 + 155 \times 2$$ $$= 500 + 310$$ $$= \mathbf{810}$$ ### Step 5: Verification by mental counting - Element `A[3][5]` is the $65^{\text{th}}$ element in row-major order (row 0 has 20, row 1 has 20, row 2 has 20, and we need 5 from row 3 тЖТ $60 + 5 = 65$). - $65 \times 2 = 130$ bytes offset from base 500 тЖТ 630. тЬУ Matches. ### Step 6: Memory footprint $$\text{Total Memory} = R \times C \times s = 10 \times 20 \times 2 = \mathbf{400\ bytes}$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 тАФ General (Non-Zero) Lower Bound > **Problem:** An array `X[(-5) ... 10]` is stored at base address 1000 with element size 4 bytes. Find the address of `X[3]` and `X[-2]`. ### Step 1: Use the generalized formula (not C++ standard, but conceptually important for KTU) $$\text{Address of } A[i] = BA + (i - LB) \times s$$ - Lower Bound: $LB = -5$ - Base address of `X[-5]`: $BA = 1000$ - Element size: $s = 4$ ### Step 2: Compute for `X[3]` $$\text{Address of } X[3] = 1000 + (3 - (-5)) \times 4$$ $$= 1000 + (3 + 5) \times 4$$ $$= 1000 + 8 \times 4$$ $$= 1000 + 32$$ $$= \mathbf{1032}$$ ### Step 3: Compute for `X[-2]` $$\text{Address of } X[-2] = 1000 + (-2 - (-5)) \times 4$$ $$= 1000 + (-2 + 5) \times 4$$ $$= 1000 + 3 \times 4$$ $$= 1000 + 12$$ $$= \mathbf{1012}$$ ### Step 4: Number of elements in the array $$n = UB - LB + 1 = 10 - (-5) + 1 = \mathbf{16\ elements}$$ ### Step 5: Total memory $$n \times s = 16 \times 4 = \mathbf{64\ bytes}$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix тАФ Pointer Arithmetic vs. Array Indexing | C++ Expression | Equivalent To | Type of Result | Address Steps Forward | |---|---|---|---| | `arr` | `&arr[0]` | `int*` | 0 (base) | | `arr + 1` | `&arr[1]` | `int*` | $+s$ bytes | | `arr + i` | `&arr[i]` | `int*` | $+i \cdot s$ bytes | | `*arr` | `arr[0]` | `int` (value) | N/A | | `*(arr + i)` | `arr[i]` | `int` (value) | N/A | | `*arr + 1` | `arr[0] + 1` (arith on value) | `int` | N/A | | `*(arr + 1)` | `arr[1]` | `int` (value) | N/A | | `&arr` | Address of whole array | `int(*)[n]` | 0 (same numeric value as `arr`, but different type) | | `&arr + 1` | Address *past* the whole array | `int(*)[n]` | $+n \cdot s$ bytes | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board-Exam Pearl:** The expression `&arr + 1` increments by `sizeof(arr)` (the **entire array size**), not by `sizeof(int)`. This is a classic C++ viva trap. The numeric value of `&arr + 1` is `BA + n * s`. ## 3.5 Traversal Algorithm тАФ Linear Scan (Analysis) A standard array traversal visits every element once. The time complexity analysis is: $$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} O(1) = n \cdot O(1) = O(n)$$ Each iteration performs: 1. Index computation: $O(1)$ (multiplication and addition). 2. Memory fetch: $O(1)$ (single read). 3. Comparison / processing: $O(1)$ (depends on operation). The **space complexity** is $O(1)$ auxiliary because we only use a single loop variable `i`, regardless of $n$. --- ## 3.6 Insertion in the Middle тАФ Shifting Analysis To insert an element at position $k$ in an array of $n$ elements, we must shift all elements from index $k$ to $n-1$ **one position to the right**.
