| 1 | 10 | 0 | 10 |
| 2 | 20 | 10 | 30 |
| 3 | 30 | 30 | 60 |

**Output:** `Sum = 60`

**Key Constraint:** `val` is a **local copy** of the array element. Modifying `val` does **not** modify `arr`. The enhanced for-loop is **read-only by design**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Control Statement Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CONTROL STATEMENTS]:::root --> B[SELECTION STATEMENTS]:::sel
    A --> C[ITERATION STATEMENTS]:::iter
    A --> D[JUMP STATEMENTS]:::jump

    B --> B1[if]
    B --> B2[if-else]
    B --> B3[nested if]
    B --> B4[if-else-if ladder]
    B --> B5[switch-case]
    B --> B6[ternary operator]

    C --> C1[while loop - entry controlled]
    C --> C2[do-while loop - exit controlled]
    C --> C3[for loop - counter controlled]
    C --> C4[enhanced for-each loop - Java 5+]

    D --> D1[break]
    D --> D2[continue]
    D --> D3[return]
    D --> D4[goto - reserved not used in Java]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sel fill:#fef3c7,color:#000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef iter fill:#dcfce7,color:#000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef jump fill:#fee2e2,color:#000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
```

```mermaid
    Cond4 -- FALSE --> A5[grade = F]:::action

    A3 --> Out
    A4 --> Out
```

### 4.3 `switch-case` Execution Flow with Fall-Through Highlight

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Enter switch]) --> Eval[/Evaluate expression: day/]:::io

    Match -- case 3 --> C3[Execute Wednesday]:::action
    Match -- case 4 --> C4[Execute Thursday]:::action
    Match -- case 5 --> C5[Execute Friday]:::action

    C4 --> B4[break]:::jump

    B1 --> End
    B2 --> End
    B3 --> End
    B4 --> End

```

```mermaid

    subgraph DOWHILE["DO-WHILE LOOP - Exit Controlled"]
```

### 4.5 `break` vs `continue` Behavior Schematic


## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Differentiate between `while` and `do-while` loops in Java. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `while` loop | `do-while` loop |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Entry-controlled loop | Exit-controlled loop |
| Condition Check | Tested **before** body execution | Tested **after** body execution |
| Minimum Executions | **Zero** (body may never execute) | **One** (body executes at least once) |
| Semicolon | Not required after `while(cond)` | **Mandatory** after `while(cond);` |
| Syntax | `while (cond) { body }` | `do { body } while (cond);` |

**Example тАФ `while`:** Prints nothing if initial condition is FALSE.
```java
int i = 10;
while (i < 5) {
    System.out.println(i);
    i++;
}
// Output: (nothing printed)
```

**Example тАФ `do-while`:** Prints `10` even though condition is FALSE.
```java
int i = 10;
do {
    System.out.println(i);
    i++;
} while (i < 5);
// Output: 10
```

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model QP]`

**Explain the concept of `fall-through` in a `switch` statement. How can it be prevented?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Fall-through** is the phenomenon in which, after executing a matching `case` block, the program **does not exit the switch** and continues executing all subsequent `case` blocks (including `default`) **regardless of whether their values match**.

**Cause:** Occurs when a `case` block **does not end with a `break` statement** (or `return`/`throw`).

**Prevention Methods:**
1. Add a **`break;`** statement at the end of every `case` block тАФ this is the standard practice.
2. Use the **`return` statement** instead of `break` if the value must be returned from a method.
3. In **Java 14+**, use the **arrow syntax** `case X -> ...` which inherently does not fall through.
4. Group multiple labels for a single block (Java 14+): `case 1, 2, 3 -> System.out.println("Q1");`

**Illustration of fall-through (input day = 2, missing break after case 2):**
```
case 2: System.out.println("Tuesday");
case 3: System.out.println("Wednesday");   // executes unintentionally
default: System.out.println("Invalid");   // also executes
```
**Output for `day=2`:** `Tuesday Wednesday Invalid`

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program to read an integer `N` from the user and print whether it is a **prime number** or not. Use a `for` loop with a `break` statement for early termination.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the differences among the three iteration statements тАФ `while`, `do-while`, and `for` тАФ using a comparative table and one example program for each.

---

#### **Solution for Q-A (a) тАФ Prime Number Check [7 marks]**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;
public class PrimeCheck {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter a positive integer: ");
        int n = sc.nextInt();
        boolean isPrime = true;

        // Edge cases: 0, 1, and negative numbers are NOT prime
        if (n <= 1) {
            isPrime = false;
        } else {
            // Check divisibility from 2 to sqrt(n)
            for (int i = 2; i * i <= n; i++) {
                if (n % i == 0) {
                    isPrime = false;
                    break;   // Early termination - divisor found
                }
            }
        }

        if (isPrime)
            System.out.println(n + " is a PRIME number.");
        else
            System.out.println(n + " is NOT a prime number.");

        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Explanation (for valuation):**
- [1 mark] Import `Scanner` and read input `n`.
- [1 mark] Handle edge case `n <= 1` as non-prime.
- [1 mark] Use `for` loop from 2 to `sqrt(n)` (efficient optimization).
- [1 mark] Apply `break` to exit the loop once a divisor is found.
- [1 mark] Use boolean flag `isPrime` to track result.
- [1 mark] Print appropriate message.
- [1 mark] Proper indentation, class structure, and `sc.close()`.

**Trace Example for n = 17:**
| i | i*i <= 17 | n % i | isPrime | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 4 тЙд 17 тЖТ TRUE | 17%2 = 1 | TRUE | Continue |
| 3 | 9 тЙд 17 тЖТ TRUE | 17%3 = 2 | TRUE | Continue |
| 4 | 16 тЙд 17 тЖТ TRUE | 17%4 = 1 | TRUE | Continue |
| 5 | 25 тЙд 17 тЖТ FALSE | тАФ | TRUE | **Loop ends** |

**Output:** `17 is a PRIME number.`

---

#### **Solution for Q-A (b) тАФ Iteration Comparison [7 marks]**

| Feature | `while` loop | `do-while` loop | `for` loop |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Control Type** | Entry-controlled | Exit-controlled | Entry-controlled |
| **Initialization** | Outside loop | Outside loop | Inside loop header |
| **Condition Check** | Before body | After body | Before body |
| **Update Statement** | Inside body | Inside body | Inside loop header |
| **Min. Executions** | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| **Use Case** | Unknown iterations | Menu-driven (тЙе1 run) | Known counter |
| **Syntax** | `while(c){...}` | `do{...}while(c);` | `for(i;c;u){...}` |

**Example 1 тАФ `while` (Sum until sentinel):**
```java
int sum = 0, num = 1;
while (num != 0) {
    sum += num;
    num = new Scanner(System.in).nextInt();
}
```

**Example 2 тАФ `do-while` (Menu):**
```java
int choice;
do {
    System.out.println("1. Add  2. Exit");
    choice = sc.nextInt();
} while (choice != 2);
```

**Example 3 тАФ `for` (Factorial):**
```java
int fact = 1;
for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
    fact *= i;
}
System.out.println("5! = " + fact);  // Output: 5! = 120
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model QP]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program using a `switch` statement to design a **simple calculator** that takes two numbers and an operator (`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%`) as input and displays the result. Handle the case of **division by zero** using `if`.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the enhanced `for-each` loop in Java with its **syntax, advantages, and limitations**. Provide one example program demonstrating its use.

---

#### **Solution for Q-B (a) тАФ Simple Calculator [7 marks]**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;
public class Calculator {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter first number: ");
        double a = sc.nextDouble();
        System.out.print("Enter operator (+, -, *, /, %): ");
        char op = sc.next().charAt(0);
        System.out.print("Enter second number: ");
        double b = sc.nextDouble();
        double result = 0;
        boolean valid = true;

        switch (op) {
            case '+': result = a + b; break;
            case '-': result = a - b; break;
            case '*': result = a * b; break;
            case '/':
                if (b != 0) result = a / b;
                else {
                    System.out.println("Error: Division by zero!");
                    valid = false;
                }
                break;
            case '%':
                if (b != 0) result = a % b;
                else {
                    System.out.println("Error: Modulus by zero!");
                    valid = false;
                }
                break;
            default:
                System.out.println("Invalid operator!");
                valid = false;
        }

        if (valid)
            System.out.println("Result: " + a + " " + op + " " + b + " = " + result);

        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Trace Example: a=10, op=`/`, b=2** тЖТ `Result: 10.0 / 2.0 = 5.0`
**Trace Example: a=10, op=`/`, b=0** тЖТ `Error: Division by zero!`

**Valuation Breakdown:**
- [1 mark] Variable declarations and input reading.
- [1 mark] Correct `switch` with all 5 cases.
- [1 mark] `break` statement in each case.
- [1 mark] Division-by-zero handling using `if`.
- [1 mark] `default` case for invalid operator.
- [1 mark] Correct output formatting.
- [1 mark] Code structure and `sc.close()`.

---

#### **Solution for Q-B (b) тАФ Enhanced For-Each Loop [7 marks]**

**Definition:** The enhanced `for` loop (also called the **for-each** loop), introduced in **Java 5 (JDK 1.5)**, provides a **simplified syntax** to iterate over arrays and collections (e.g., `ArrayList`, `HashSet`).

**Syntax:**
```java
for (Type variableName : arrayOrCollection) {
    // body using variableName
}
```

**Example Program:**
```java
public class ForEachDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int[] marks = {85, 90, 78, 92, 88};
        int total = 0;

        // Using enhanced for-each loop
        for (int m : marks) {
            total += m;
        }

        double average = (double) total / marks.length;
        System.out.println("Total = " + total);
        System.out.println("Average = " + average);
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Total = 433
Average = 86.6
```

**Advantages (3 marks):**
1. **Concise syntax** тАФ eliminates the need for explicit index management.
2. **No risk of `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`** тАФ bounds are automatically handled.
3. **Readability** тАФ intent is clear: "do something for each element".
4. Works uniformly for both **arrays** and **any object implementing `Iterable`**.

**Limitations (3 marks):**
1. **Read-only iteration** тАФ the loop variable is a **local copy**; modifying it does NOT modify the original array.
2. **No index access** тАФ cannot retrieve the current position/index of the element.
3. **Forward-only traversal** тАФ cannot iterate in reverse.
4. **Cannot skip elements easily** тАФ requires complex `if` conditions, defeating the simplicity.
5. **Not applicable to all data structures** тАФ only works with arrays and `Iterable` collections.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing `break` in `switch`:** Forgetting `break` in even ONE case results in **partial marks loss (1-2 marks)**. Examiners specifically scan for this.
> 2. **Semicolon after `while` in `do-while`:** Writing `} while (cond)` instead of `} while (cond);` is a **syntax error** that costs the full 7 marks in a program question.
> 3. **Confusing `break` with `continue`:** Saying "`break` skips to the next iteration" is a **factual error** (2-3 marks deducted in theory).
> 4. **Modifying array in for-each:** Writing `for (int x : arr) x = 0;` and claiming it zeroes the array is a **conceptual error**.
> 5. **Missing `default` in `switch`:** Not handling unmatched values is considered **incomplete design** (1 mark deduction in 7-mark programs).
> 6. **Infinite loops in `while`:** Forgetting to update the loop variable inside the body results in **no output or runtime hang** тАФ examiners deduct 2-3 marks for not catching this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision before KTU University Exam тАФ print or screenshot this section.**

- [x] Control statements alter the **default top-to-bottom sequential flow** of a program.
- [x] **Three categories:** Selection, Iteration, Jump тАФ tested independently and combined in 14-mark questions.
- [x] **`if`** tests one condition; **`if-else`** provides an alternate path; **`if-else-if`** ladder handles multiple mutually exclusive conditions.
- [x] **`switch`** works with `byte`, `short`, `char`, `int`, **wrapper types** (Java 5+), **String** (Java 7+), and **enum**.
- [x] **Fall-through in `switch`** happens when `break` is missing тАФ preventable with `break`, `return`, or Java 14+ arrow syntax.
- [x] **`while`** = entry-controlled, may execute **zero** times.
- [x] **`do-while`** = exit-controlled, executes **at least once**. Semicolon after `while(cond);` is **mandatory**.
- [x] **`for`** integrates init, condition, update in one line тАФ best for known counters.
- [x] **Enhanced `for-each`** is **read-only**, forward-only, no index тАФ works on arrays and `Iterable` collections.
- [x] **`break`** terminates the **innermost loop/switch**; **`break label`** exits a labeled outer loop (Java specific).
- [x] **`continue`** skips remaining statements in the current iteration and proceeds to the next.
- [x] **`return`** exits the current method; can optionally return a value.
- [x] **`goto`** is a **reserved keyword in Java but not implemented** (deliberate design).
- [x] **Infinite loop rule:** Every loop must have a **reachable terminating condition** or a `break` statement.
- [x] **Practice writing trace tables** тАФ they fetch full marks in dry-run questions (often 5-7 marks).
- [x] **KTU favourite combined program:** One program using `if-else` + `for` + `break`/`continue` (e.g., prime check, number search, pattern printing).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 1

# Functions in C++ тАФ The Building Blocks of Modular Programming

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **function** in C++ is a self-contained, named block of statements that performs a specific, well-defined task. Functions are the primary mechanism for **decomposition**, **reuse**, and **abstraction** in structured and object-oriented programming. Every C++ program consists of at least one function тАФ `main()` тАФ from which all other functions are invoked.

## 1.1 What is a Function?

A function is a **reusable sub-program** that takes inputs (parameters), processes them, and may return a result (return value). Instead of writing the same logic 100 times, you write it once as a function and **call** it whenever needed.

### Conceptual Analogy тАФ The Vending Machine

Think of a function like a **vending machine** you see in a railway station:

| Vending Machine Part | Function Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The slot where you insert money & select an item | **Function Call** (with **arguments**) |
| The internal mechanism (gears, coils) | **Function Body** (the statements that do work) |
| The item that drops out | **Return Value** |
| The model number printed on the front | **Function Name** |
| The list of accepted coins & item codes | **Parameter List / Prototype** |

You don't need to know *how* the vending machine works internally тАФ you just interact with its **interface** (insert coin тЖТ press button тЖТ get item). Functions work the same way: you call them, they do their job, and they return a result. This hiding of internal complexity is called **abstraction**.

### Why Functions? (KTU High-Yield Points)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Functions provide **4 critical benefits** that examiners love to ask about:
> 1. **Modularity** тАФ A large program is broken into smaller, manageable, logical chunks.
> 2. **Reusability** тАФ Write once, use many times. Reduces code duplication.
> 3. **Abstraction** тАФ Hides implementation details; the caller only needs the interface.
> 4. **Readability & Debugging** тАФ Named functions make code self-documenting and easier to trace.

### Categories of Functions in C++ (KTU 2024 Classification)

| Category | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Library (Built-in) Functions** | Pre-written, supplied with the compiler | `printf()`, `sqrt()`, `cin.get()` |
| **User-defined Functions** | Written by the programmer for custom tasks | `calculateArea()`, `displayResult()` |

### Anatomy of a C++ Function (Formal Structure)

```cpp
return_type  function_name ( parameter_list )
{
    // ----- Function Body -----
    // declarations
    // executable statements
    return ( expression );      // optional, depends on return_type
}
```

The **function prototype** (or **declaration**) is a single-line announcement that tells the compiler about a function *before* it is used:

```cpp
return_type  function_name ( parameter_list );   // prototype ends with semicolon
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** If a function is **called before it is defined**, a prototype **must** appear earlier in the file. If the definition comes first, the prototype is optional. This is a frequent 3-mark question.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Core Components of a Function

Every C++ function interaction involves **three** components that you must master:

1. **Function Definition** тАФ The actual code (body) of the function.
2. **Function Prototype / Declaration** тАФ A forward declaration giving the compiler the function's signature.
3. **Function Call** тАФ The statement that transfers control to the function.

### 2.1.1 Function Prototype тАФ The "Contract"

A prototype declares the function's **signature**: its name, return type, and the number & types of parameters. It is a promise to the compiler that a full definition exists somewhere.

```
double  power (double base, int exponent);     // valid prototype
```

The prototype is essential because the C++ compiler is **single-pass** тАФ it processes the file top-to-bottom. If it encounters a call to an unknown function, it cannot proceed without a prototype.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why prototypes matter in OOP:** In large projects, classes are split across many files (`.h` headers and `.cpp` implementations). Prototypes in header files allow different translation units to reference each other's functions safely.

### 2.1.2 Function Call тАФ The "Trigger"

When a function is called:

- **Step A:** Arguments are evaluated.
- **Step B:** A new **stack frame (activation record)** is pushed onto the **call stack**.
- **Step C:** Control jumps to the function's entry point.
- **Step D:** Parameters receive the values (or references) of the arguments.
- **Step E:** The body executes.
- **Step F:** On reaching `return`, the return value (if any) is passed back.
- **Step G:** The stack frame is **popped**, and control returns to the calling site.

### 2.1.3 Function Definition тАФ The "Implementation"

Contains the actual statements. Defined **exactly once** in a program (to avoid linker errors like *"multiple definition"*).

## 2.2 Parameter Passing Mechanisms (KTU High-Yield Topic)

This is one of the **most frequently tested** areas in KTU exams. There are three primary mechanisms:

### A. Pass by Value

A **copy** of the argument is passed. The original variable in the caller is **never modified**.

- **Memory:** A new local variable is created on the stack.
- **Use case:** When the function should not alter the original.
- **Cost:** Copying large objects (e.g., a big struct) can be slow.

### B. Pass by Reference (using `&`)

The function receives an **alias** (another name) for the original variable. Changes inside the function **directly affect** the caller's variable.

- **Memory:** No copy is made тАФ both names refer to the same memory location.
- **Use case:** When the function must modify the original, or to avoid the cost of copying large data.
- **Safety:** Use `const` qualifier for read-only references: `void print(const vector<int>& v);`

### C. Pass by Pointer (using `*`)

The function receives the **memory address** of the variable. Using the dereference operator `*`, the function can read/write the original.

- **Memory:** A pointer (typically 8 bytes on 64-bit systems) is passed.
- **Use case:** Legacy C-style code, dynamic memory, optional values (using `nullptr`).
- **Safety:** Must check for `nullptr` before dereferencing.

### KTU Formula Sheet тАФ Parameter Passing Summary

| Mechanism | Syntax in Definition | Modifies Original? | Memory Cost | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pass by Value** | `void f(int x)` | тЭМ No | Full copy | Small data, no modification needed |
| **Pass by Reference** | `void f(int &x)` | тЬЕ Yes | Just an alias (zero extra cost) | Modify original, avoid copies |
| **Pass by Pointer** | `void f(int *x)` | тЬЕ Yes (via `*x`) | 8 bytes (address) | Legacy code, dynamic arrays |
| **Const Reference** | `void f(const int &x)` | тЭМ No | Just an alias | Large objects, read-only access |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall тАФ Don't confuse:** Passing by reference uses `&` in the parameter declaration, but the call site looks **identical** to pass-by-value:
> ```cpp
> int a = 10;
> increment(a);        // looks the same!
> ```
> Whether `a` is modified depends on the function's signature, not the call site.

## 2.3 Default Arguments

C++ allows you to assign **default values** to parameters in the prototype. If the caller omits that argument, the default is used.

**Rules (KTU favorite):**
- Default values must be specified in the **prototype** (or in the definition if there is no separate prototype).
- Once a parameter has a default, all parameters to its **right** must also have defaults.
- Defaults are **resolved at call time**, not at definition time.

```cpp
int add(int a, int b = 5, int c = 10);   // valid
int add(int a, int b = 5, int c);         // INVALID тАФ c has no default
```

## 2.4 Inline Functions

The `inline` keyword is a **request** to the compiler to replace the function call with the function body itself, eliminating the overhead of pushing/popping stack frames.

- **Best for:** Small, frequently-called functions (1тАУ3 lines).
- **Compiler's choice:** Modern compilers may ignore `inline` and decide automatically based on optimization heuristics.
- **Side benefit:** In OOP, member functions defined **inside** a class definition are *implicitly* inline.

```cpp
inline int square(int x) { return x * x; }
```

## 2.5 Function Overloading

C++ allows **multiple functions with the same name** but **different parameter lists** (different number or types of parameters). This is **compile-time polymorphism**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Critical Rule:** The compiler distinguishes overloads by **signature**, NOT by return type. Two functions differing only in return type cause a compilation error.

```cpp
int    area(int side);             // square
int    area(int l, int b);         // rectangle  тЖР different parameter list тАФ valid overload
float  area(float radius);         // circle     тЖР different type тАФ valid overload
```

## 2.6 Storage Classes тАФ Scope, Lifetime & Visibility

A variable's **storage class** determines *where* it lives in memory, *how long* it survives, and *who* can see it.

| Storage Class | Keyword | Scope | Lifetime | Default Value | Storage Location |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Automatic** | `auto` (default for local vars) | Block `{ }` | Until block ends | Garbage (undefined) | Stack |
| **Register** | `register` | Block | Until block ends | Garbage | CPU Register (request) |
| **Static** | `static` | Block (but persists) | Entire program run | **Zero** | Data segment |
| **External** | `extern` | Global (across files) | Entire program run | Zero | Data segment |
| **Mutable** | `mutable` | Class member (overrides const) | As per object | тАФ | тАФ |

### Key Insight тАФ `static` Locals

A `static` local variable is initialized **only once** and **retains** its value between function calls. This is the foundation of stateful functions in C and a building block for understanding **singletons** in OOP.

```cpp
void counter() {
    static int count = 0;     // initialized only on first call
    count++;
    cout << count << " ";
}
// Calling counter() thrice prints: 1 2 3
```

## 2.7 Recursion

A function that **calls itself** (directly or indirectly). Every recursive function must have:

1. A **base case** тАФ the stopping condition.
2. A **recursive case** тАФ the call to itself with a smaller sub-problem.

**Memory impact:** Each recursive call pushes a new stack frame. Deep recursion can cause a **stack overflow**.

### Master Theorem for Recurrence Analysis (Foundation for Algorithm Design)

For a recursive algorithm of the form:

$$T(n) = a \cdot T\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$$

where:
- $a$ = number of sub-problems in the recursion,
- $b$ = factor by which the sub-problem size is reduced,
- $f(n)$ = cost of work done outside the recursive calls,

the **asymptotic complexity** $T(n)$ can be determined by comparing $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Function Use |
|---|---|
| **Embedded Systems** | `readSensor()`, `togglePin()` тАФ replace direct register manipulation with named, testable functions |
| **Data Science** | Pure functions for data transformation тАФ easy to test, parallelize, and reason about |
| **Game Development** | `updatePhysics()`, `renderFrame()`, `handleInput()` тАФ game loop decomposed into clean functions |
| **Web Backends** | Each API endpoint is a function; input validation, DB calls, and response building are nested function calls |
| **Operating Systems** | System calls are essentially functions exposed by the kernel to user programs |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 тАФ Pass by Value vs. Pass by Reference (Step-by-Step Trace)

This is the most common KTU question pattern. We trace the **memory layout** and **variable values** at every step.

### Source Code

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

void swapByValue(int a, int b) {
    int temp = a;
    a = b;
    b = temp;
}

void swapByRef(int &a, int &b) {
    int temp = a;
    a = b;
    b = temp;
}

int main() {
    int x = 10, y = 20;

    swapByValue(x, y);
    cout << "After swapByValue:  x = " << x << ", y = " << y << endl;

    swapByRef(x, y);
    cout << "After swapByRef:    x = " << x << ", y = " << y << endl;

    return 0;
}
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

#### Initial State (in `main`)

| Variable | Address (illustrative) | Value |
|---|---|---|
| `x` | `0x100` | 10 |
| `y` | `0x104` | 20 |

**Call Stack (bottom тЖТ top):** `[main: x=10, y=20]`

#### Step 1 тАФ Call `swapByValue(x, y)`

- The compiler creates **copies** of `x` and `y` in the function's stack frame.
- New local variables `a` and `b` are created at fresh memory locations, e.g., `0x200` and `0x204`.

**Call Stack (bottom тЖТ top):**

```
| a = 10   (0x200)  |  тЖР top (new frame)
| b = 20   (0x204)  |
| x = 10   (0x100)  |  тЖР main frame
| y = 20   (0x104)  |
```

- The swap logic runs: `temp = 10, a = 20, b = 10`.
- On `return`, the stack frame is popped. Variables `a` and `b` are destroyed.
- **Result:** `x` and `y` in `main` are still `10` and `20`. **Original is unchanged.**

#### Step 2 тАФ Call `swapByRef(x, y)`

- The compiler binds `a` and `b` as **aliases** to `x` and `y`. **No copies are made.**
- `a` and `b` are not new memory locations; they are *the same* as `x` and `y`.

**Call Stack (bottom тЖТ top):**

```
| a тФАтФА alias тФАтФА> x (0x100)  |  тЖР top (new frame, but no new memory for a/b)
| b тФАтФА alias тФАтФА> y (0x104)  |
| x = 10   (0x100)  |  тЖР main frame
| y = 20   (0x104)  |
```

- The swap runs: `temp = 10, a (=x) = 20, b (=y) = 10`.
- On `return`, the stack frame is popped, but `x` and `y` have been directly modified.

### Expected Output

$$\text{After swapByValue: } x = 10, y = 20$$
$$\text{After swapByRef:   } x = 20, y = 10$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 тАФ Recursion (Factorial) with Stack Trace

### Source Code

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int factorial(int n) {
    if (n <= 1)              // base case
        return 1;
    return n * factorial(n-1);  // recursive case
}

int main() {
    cout << "5! = " << factorial(5) << endl;
    return 0;
}
```

### Step-by-Step Evaluation of `factorial(5)`

| Call | Action | Returns To | Computed Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| `factorial(5)` | Needs `factorial(4)` first | тАФ | `5 ├Ч factorial(4)` |
| `factorial(4)` | Needs `factorial(3)` first | тАФ | `4 ├Ч factorial(3)` |
| `factorial(3)` | Needs `factorial(2)` first | тАФ | `3 ├Ч factorial(2)` |
| `factorial(2)` | Needs `factorial(1)` first | тАФ | `2 ├Ч factorial(1)` |
| `factorial(1)` | Base case hit | тАФ | `1` |
| тЖТ unwind тЖТ | `factorial(2)` receives `1` | `factorial(3)` | `2 ├Ч 1 = 2` |
| тЖТ unwind тЖТ | `factorial(3)` receives `2` | `factorial(4)` | `3 ├Ч 2 = 6` |
| тЖТ unwind тЖТ | `factorial(4)` receives `6` | `factorial(5)` | `4 ├Ч 6 = 24` |
| тЖТ unwind тЖТ | `factorial(5)` receives `24` | `main` | `5 ├Ч 24 = 120` |

### Final Output

$$5! = 120$$

### Recurrence Relation

The time complexity of `factorial(n)` follows:

$$T(n) = T(n-1) + O(1), \quad T(1) = O(1)$$

Solving by unrolling:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n-1) + c \\
     &= T(n-2) + 2c \\
     &= T(n-3) + 3c \\
     &\;\;\vdots \\
     &= T(1) + (n-1)c \\
     &= O(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, `factorial(n)` is a **linear** $O(n)$ algorithm in both time and stack depth.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 тАФ Function Overloading Resolution

### Source Code

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

void display(int x)         { cout << "Integer: "   << x << endl; }
void display(double x)      { cout << "Double: "    << x << endl; }
void display(string s)      { cout << "String: "    << s << endl; }
void display(int x, int y)  { cout << "Two ints: "  << x << ", " << y << endl; }

int main() {
    display(5);
    display(3.14);
    display("Hello");
    display(10, 20);
    return 0;
}
```

### Compiler's Resolution Process

| Call | Match Found | Why |
|---|---|---|
| `display(5)` | `display(int)` | Exact type match |
| `display(3.14)` | `display(double)` | `3.14` is a `double` literal тАФ exact match |
| `display("Hello")` | `display(string)` | String literal тЖТ `std::string` conversion (C++14+) |
| `display(10, 20)` | `display(int, int)` | Two arguments, both `int` |

### Expected Output

$$\text{Integer: 5}$$
$$\text{Double: 3.14}$$
$$\text{String: Hello}$$
$$\text{Two ints: 10, 20}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Ambiguity Pitfall:** If you had `display(5.0f)` and overloads for `double` *and* `float`, the compiler will pick the **exact** match (`float`). If both require a conversion (e.g., one takes `int` and another `long`), an **ambiguity error** is raised. This is a classic 3-mark question.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix тАФ When to Use What?

| Scenario | Recommended Mechanism | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Function should not modify the argument, and the data is small (e.g., `int`, `double`) | **Pass by Value** | Simple, safe, no aliasing surprises |
| Function should not modify the argument, but the data is large (e.g., a `vector`, a `struct`) | **Pass by `const` Reference** | Avoids expensive copy, enforces read-only |
| Function must modify one or more arguments | **Pass by Reference** | Direct in-place modification |
| Function works with dynamically allocated memory or arrays | **Pass by Pointer** | C-style API compatibility |
| Need a "result" without using a return value (e.g., multiple outputs) | **Pass by Reference** for output params | C++17 `std::optional` and structured bindings are modern alternatives |
| Need a small, frequently-called function (1тАУ2 lines) | **Inline** | Avoid call overhead |
| Function name is intuitive but the operation differs by input type | **Function Overloading** | Improves API usability |
| Function needs to "remember" state across calls | **Static local variable** | Persistent storage within one function scope |
| Algorithm is naturally self-similar (tree traversal, factorial, Fibonacci) | **Recursion** | Conceptual clarity тАФ but watch stack depth |

## 4.1 Function Execution Flow (Top-Down View)


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**What is a function prototype in C++? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A function prototype is a **declaration statement** that informs the compiler about a function's name, return type, and parameter types **before** the function is actually defined or used. Its general form is:

```cpp
return_type function_name(parameter_type_list);
```

**Necessity (key points for full marks):**

1. The C++ compiler is **single-pass** тАФ it reads the source file from top to bottom. If a function is called before it is defined or declared, the compiler will issue an *"undefined function"* error.
2. The prototype enables **type checking** of arguments at the call site, ensuring that the types of actual arguments match the formal parameters.
3. It enables **separate compilation** тАФ different files can reference functions declared in header files without needing their full definition.
4. The prototype also allows the compiler to determine the **return type** and emit correct machine code for retrieving the result.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for necessity тАФ examiners expect at least 3 of the 4 points above for full marks.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]`

**Differentiate between pass by value and pass by reference with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Pass by Value | Pass by Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **What is passed** | A **copy** of the argument's value | An **alias** (reference) to the original variable |
| **Effect on original** | Original variable in caller is **not modified** | Original variable is **directly modified** |
| **Memory** | A new local variable is created on the stack | No extra memory for the parameter itself |
| **Syntax** | `void f(int x)` | `void f(int &x)` |
| **Safety** | Safer тАФ no side effects | Risky if unintentional тАФ use `const` for read-only access |

**Example:**

```cpp
void byValue(int x)  { x = x + 10; }   // caller's variable unchanged
void byRef(int &x)   { x = x + 10; }   // caller's variable IS changed

int main() {
    int a = 5;
    byValue(a);   // a is still 5
    byRef(a);     // a is now 15
}
```

---

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students answer **ONE full question** (both sub-parts) out of the choice provided.

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Explain the different parameter passing mechanisms in C++ with examples. Discuss when each is preferred. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

C++ provides three primary parameter passing mechanisms:

**1. Pass by Value (2 marks for explanation + example)**

A copy of the actual argument is made and passed to the function. The function works on the copy, leaving the original untouched.

```cpp
void increment(int n) { n++; }

int main() {
    int x = 10;
    increment(x);          // x is still 10
    cout << x;             // prints 10
}
```

**Memory layout:** A new variable `n` is created on the stack at a different address from `x`. The function operates only on `n`.

**Preferred when:** The data is small (fundamental types like `int`, `double`, `char`) and the function should not modify the original. It is the **safest** mechanism since it prevents unintended side effects.

---

**2. Pass by Reference (2 marks for explanation + example)**

A reference is an alias тАФ another name for the same memory location. The function works directly on the caller's variable.

```cpp
void increment(int &n) { n++; }

int main() {
    int x = 10;
    increment(x);          // x becomes 11
    cout << x;             // prints 11
}
```

**Memory layout:** No new memory is allocated for `n`. The compiler binds `n` to the address of `x`. Any write through `n` modifies `x` directly.

**Preferred when:** The function must modify the original, OR the data is large (e.g., a `std::vector` of 10,000 elements) and copying would be expensive. For large read-only data, use `const T &` to avoid copies while preventing modification.

---

**3. Pass by Pointer (2 marks for explanation + example)**

A pointer holds the **memory address** of the variable. The function dereferences the pointer using `*` to read or write the original.

```cpp
void increment(int *n) { (*n)++; }

int main() {
    int x = 10;
    increment(&x);         // pass address of x
    cout << x;             // prints 11
}
```

**Memory layout:** A new local pointer variable (typically 8 bytes on 64-bit systems) is created that stores the address of `x`.

**Preferred when:** Working with C-style APIs, dynamic memory (heap-allocated arrays), linked data structures, or when `nullptr` is a valid "no value" sentinel. Must check `if (ptr != nullptr)` before dereferencing to avoid segmentation faults.

---

**Conclusion (1 mark):**

Use **pass by value** for small, simple types where safety is paramount. Use **pass by const reference** for large read-only data. Use **pass by reference** when the function must produce multiple outputs or modify the original. Use **pass by pointer** for legacy C code, dynamic arrays, and optional parameters.

---

#### (b) Write a C++ program using functions to find the sum of digits of a number and to check whether the number is a palindrome. Use appropriate parameter passing. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

// Function to find sum of digits тАФ uses pass by value
int sumOfDigits(int n) {
    int sum = 0;
    n = (n < 0) ? -n : n;          // handle negative numbers
    while (n > 0) {
        sum += n % 10;
        n /= 10;
    }
    return sum;
}

// Function to check palindrome тАФ uses pass by value
bool isPalindrome(int n) {
    int original = (n < 0) ? -n : n;
    int reversed = 0, temp = original;
    while (temp > 0) {
        reversed = reversed * 10 + temp % 10;
        temp /= 10;
    }
    return (original == reversed);
}

int main() {
    int number;
    cout << "Enter an integer: ";
    cin  >> number;

    cout << "Sum of digits     = " << sumOfDigits(number) << endl;
    cout << "Is palindrome?    = "
         << (isPalindrome(number) ? "Yes" : "No") << endl;

    return 0;
}
```

**Sample Run:**

```
Enter an integer: 12321
Sum of digits     = 9
Is palindrome?    = Yes
```

**Explanation of design choices (for full marks):**

- Both functions use **pass by value** because neither needs to modify the caller's variable. The `int` type is small (typically 4 bytes), so copying is efficient.
- The `isPalindrome` function returns a `bool` тАФ leveraging C++'s built-in type for clarity.
- The function uses the `?:` ternary operator to handle negative inputs gracefully (since `-12321` reversed gives a negative number, we work with the absolute value).
- `main` calls the functions and uses a clean I/O interface тАФ good modular design.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Sample]`

#### (a) What are default arguments in C++? Explain with an example. State the rules that must be followed while using default arguments. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**

**Default arguments** are values automatically assigned to function parameters by the compiler when the caller omits the corresponding actual argument. They allow a function to be called with fewer arguments than its parameter list specifies.

**Example (2 marks):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

// Function with default arguments
double computeBill(double amount, double taxRate = 0.18, double discount = 0.0) {
    double total = amount + amount * taxRate - discount;
    return total;
}

int main() {
    cout << computeBill(1000)              << endl;  // 1180  (uses defaults)
    cout << computeBill(1000, 0.12)        << endl;  // 1120  (overrides taxRate)
    cout << computeBill(1000, 0.18, 50)    << endl;  // 1150  (overrides both)
    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**

```
1180
1120
1150
```

**Rules for default arguments (4 marks тАФ examiners want all four):**

1. **Right-to-left cascading rule:** If a parameter has a default value, all parameters to its **right** must also have default values. Otherwise, the compiler cannot safely fill in missing arguments.
   ```cpp
   int f(int a, int b = 5, int c = 10);   // VALID
   int g(int a, int b = 5, int c);         // INVALID тАФ c has no default
   ```

2. **Defaults specified once:** A default value may be specified either in the **function prototype** OR in the **function definition**, but **not both**. If specified in both, it causes a re-definition error. The standard practice is to put defaults in the **prototype** (often in the header file).

3. **Defaults are resolved at call time, not at definition time:** This is subtle but important тАФ the default value used is the value of the expression *at the time of the call*, not at the time the function was defined. This is why defaults can safely reference global variables.

4. **No effect on function overloading:** Default arguments do **not** participate in overload resolution. A function with default arguments is treated as having only **one signature** for overload purposes. Setting defaults cannot create a new overload.

---

#### (b) Explain function overloading in C++ with an example. What are the limitations of function overloading? **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**

**Function overloading** is the C++ feature that allows multiple functions to share the **same name** within the same scope, provided they differ in their **parameter lists** (number of parameters, types of parameters, or order of types). The compiler selects the correct function at **compile time** based on the arguments in the call тАФ this is a form of **compile-time polymorphism**.

**Example (3 marks):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Calculator {
public:
    // Overloaded add тАФ different number of parameters
    int add(int a, int b)                  { return a + b; }
    int add(int a, int b, int c)           { return a + b + c; }

    // Overloaded multiply тАФ different types
    int    multiply(int x, int y)          { return x * y; }
    double multiply(double x, double y)    { return x * y; }
};

int main() {
    Calculator calc;
    cout << calc.add(5, 3)        << endl;  // calls add(int, int)
    cout << calc.add(5, 3, 2)     << endl;  // calls add(int, int, int)
    cout << calc.multiply(4, 5)   << endl;  // calls multiply(int, int)
    cout << calc.multiply(2.5, 4) << endl;  // calls multiply(double, double)
    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**

```
8
10
20
10
```

**Limitations of function overloading (3 marks):**

1. **Return type alone is not enough:** Two functions with the same name and parameter list but different return types cause a **compilation error**. The compiler resolves overloads based on the **signature**, not the return type.
   ```cpp
   int    f(int x);
   double f(int x);     // ERROR тАФ redefinition
   ```

2. **Ambiguity with default arguments:** If a call can match multiple overloads after applying default arguments, an **ambiguity error** is raised.
   ```cpp
   void f(int a, int b = 10);
   void f(int a);
   f(5);    // AMBIGUOUS тАФ matches both
   ```

3. **Type conversion ambiguities:** If the arguments require implicit conversions and multiple overloads are equally close, the compiler cannot decide.
   ```cpp
   void f(int x);
   void f(long x);
   f(5);    // ERROR тАФ int and long are equally close
   ```

4. **Member function hiding in inheritance:** When a derived class defines a function with the same name as a base class function, **all** base-class overloads of that name become hidden in the derived class, even if their signatures differ. The `using` declaration is required to bring them into scope.

5. **No overloading across scopes (by default):** A function declared in a namespace does not overload with a function of the same name in another namespace тАФ explicit qualification is needed.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in "Functions" questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `&` in declaration vs. call site** *(тИТ2 marks)* тАФ Students write `swap(x, &y)` thinking they need to use `&` to pass by reference. In C++, references are passed using the **variable name only**. The `&` appears only in the **parameter declaration** (`void swap(int &a, int &b)`), not at the call site.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the semicolon in prototypes** *(тИТ1 mark)* тАФ A function prototype is a statement and must end with `;`. Students sometimes write it like a function definition (without the body) and forget the semicolon.
>
> 3. **Wrong base case in recursion** *(тИТ2 to тИТ3 marks)* тАФ Omitting the base case (or writing `n==1` instead of `n<=1`) leads to **infinite recursion** and a stack overflow. Examiners check this carefully.
>
> 4. **Static local initialization myth** *(тИТ1 mark)* тАФ Writing "the static variable is re-initialized every time the function is called" is **wrong**. It is initialized **only once** on the first call.
>
> 5. **Overload resolution by return type** *(тИТ2 marks)* тАФ Saying "functions are distinguished by their return type" is **incorrect**. The compiler uses the **parameter list** (signature).
>
> 6. **Missing `const` in read-only reference** *(тИТ1 mark)* тАФ When a large object is passed by reference but the function does not modify it, omitting `const` is a style and design flaw that costs a mark.
>
> 7. **Confusing `auto` storage class with C++11 `auto` keyword** *(тИТ1 mark)* тАФ `auto` as a storage class specifier (legacy C, automatic storage) is **different** from `auto` for type inference in modern C++. Examiners may test this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Fire Revision Checklist for Functions (Module 1)**

- **Function = Modular, reusable, named block of code** that takes parameters, processes them, and may return a value.
- **Three parts:** *Declaration (prototype)* + *Definition (body)* + *Call (invocation)*.
- **Prototype is mandatory** when a function is called before its definition. It ends with a **semicolon**.
- **Parameter passing:**
  - **By Value** тЖТ copy, original safe.
  - **By Reference (`&`)** тЖТ alias, original modifiable, zero-copy.
  - **By Pointer (`*`)** тЖТ address, original modifiable via dereference, can be `nullptr`.
  - **By `const` Reference** тЖТ zero-copy + read-only тАФ **best practice for large read-only data**.
- **Default arguments:**
  - Specified in the **prototype** (or definition, not both).
  - Must cascade **right-to-left**.
  - Resolved at **call time**.
- **Inline functions** тЖТ request to compiler to expand the call, useful only for small, frequent functions.
- **Function overloading** тЖТ same name, **different parameter lists**. **Return type alone is NOT a valid differentiator.**
- **Storage classes:** `auto` (default for locals), `register` (CPU register request), `static` (retains value across calls), `extern` (cross-file global).
- **`static` local variable** is initialized **only once** and persists across function calls.
- **Recursion** needs a **base case** and a **recursive case**; each call consumes a stack frame.
- **Scope** (visibility) vs. **Lifetime** (existence) тАФ two distinct concepts often confused.
- **Function call overhead** = push/pop stack frame + parameter copying тАФ eliminated partially by `inline` and reference passing.
- **Header files** typically contain prototypes; **source files** contain definitions тАФ this is the foundation of separate compilation in C++.
- **KTU 2024 focus areas:** Pass-by-value vs. pass-by-reference (with memory diagram), default arguments rules, function overloading limitations, recursion trace, storage classes table.

---

> **End of Module 1 тАФ Functions Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | B.Tech CSE/Related Branches*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Command Line Arguments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Command Line Arguments ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In Java, **Command Line Arguments** are the **String values supplied to a program at the time of execution** from the operating system shell (e.g., `cmd.exe`, `bash`, `PowerShell`). They are received by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) and delivered to the program through the parameter of the entry-point method: ```java public static void main(String[] args) ``` The parameter `args` is a one-dimensional **String array** that holds every argument typed after the class name on the command line. The JVM automatically populates this array *before* the first line of the program executes, making the technique a form of **runtime parameterization** (a type of runtime configuration where the program's behaviour is altered without modifying or recompiling its source code). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1, OOP):** Command line arguments are introduced as the simplest form of passing external data into a Java program. Mastering `args` and its related type-conversion utilities is a **frequently tested 3-mark and 14-mark concept** in the End Semester Examination (ESE). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definitive Properties** (must be memorised verbatim for ESE): > 1. Command line arguments are **always received as `String` objects**, even if the user types a number. > 2. They are stored in the **`String[] args`** parameter of `main`. > 3. They are **space-separated** by default. Multi-word arguments must be enclosed in double quotes (`" "`). > 4. **Indexing starts at `0`**, just like any Java array. > 5. `args.length` returns the **total number of arguments** actually passed (which may be `0`). --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > **Real-world Analogy тАФ "The Restaurant Order Slip"** Imagine walking into a restaurant and handing the chef a written order slip **before** the chef begins cooking: | Real World | Java World | |------------|------------| | Restaurant kitchen | Your Java program | | Order slip handed in | Command line arguments typed in the terminal | | Items listed on the slip | Elements of `String[] args` (`args[0]`, `args[1]`, тАж) | | Number of items on the slip | `args.length` | | Chef starts cooking using the slip | `main` method begins executing with `args` pre-filled | | Chef converting "two tomatoes" into 2 actual tomatoes | Calling `Integer.parseInt(args[0])` to convert a String into an `int` | The order slip (command line) is the **only channel** through which external information reaches the chef (the program) *before* cooking starts. If the slip is empty (`args.length == 0`), the chef simply has no instructions and proceeds with the default behaviour. If the slip says `"abc"` when a number was expected, the chef cannot cook тАФ analogous to a `NumberFormatException`. This analogy makes it immediately clear *why* command line arguments exist: to **feed external data into a program at startup**, without the user having to interact with the program *during* execution. --- ### 1.3 Why Command Line Arguments Matter in OOP > [!IMPORTANT] > Command line arguments are the **first practical exposure** students get to the concept that **a program's behaviour is driven by external data**, not hard-coded values. This idea is foundational to: > - **Object construction with parameters** (covered in later modules). > - The **Strategy and Factory design patterns**. > - Writing **reusable utility classes** (e.g., calculators, file processors). > - **Build automation** (Maven, Gradle, CI/CD pipelines pass arguments like `-Xmx512m` to the JVM). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown тАФ How Command Line Arguments Travel The journey of a command line argument from the terminal to the program is a **six-stage pipeline**. Understanding each stage is critical for KTU ESE questions that ask "explain the flow" or "what happens when the command is executed". - **Step 1 тАФ User Invocation:** The user types a command such as `java AddNumbers 25 30` in the shell. - **Step 2 тАФ Shell Tokenisation:** The shell (or `java` launcher) splits the line on whitespace, producing a token list: `["java", "AddNumbers", "25", "30"]`. - **Step 3 тАФ JVM Bootstrapping:** The `java` launcher starts the JVM and asks it to load the class `AddNumbers`. - **Step 4 тАФ Argument Packaging:** The launcher packages every token *after* the class name into a fresh `String[]` тАФ in our example: `["25", "30"]`. - **Step 5 тАФ main() Invocation:** The JVM calls the `main` method, passing the array as the `args` parameter. - **Step 6 тАФ Program Execution:** The body of `main` runs with `args[0] = "25"` and `args[1] = "30"`. > [!NOTE] > **Critical Insight:** Notice that even though the user typed the number `25`, Java stores it as the *string* `"25"` (two characters: `'2'` and `'5'`). This is why **explicit conversion is required** before any arithmetic operation. --- ### 2.2 Type-Conversion Utilities (Wrapper Class Methods) Because everything in `args` is a `String`, the programmer must call a **wrapper-class parse method** to obtain primitive types. These methods are part of Java's autoboxing-friendly API and are tested almost every semester in KTU exams. | Purpose | Method Signature | Wrapper Class | Example Call | Resulting Type | |---------|------------------|---------------|--------------|----------------| | String тЖТ `int` | `static int parseInt(String s)` | `Integer` | `Integer.parseInt("123")` | `int` | | String тЖТ `long` | `static long parseLong(String s)` | `Long` | `Long.parseLong("9999999999")` | `long` | | String тЖТ `double` | `static double parseDouble(String s)` | `Double` | `Double.parseDouble("3.14")` | `double` | | String тЖТ `float` | `static float parseFloat(String s)` | `Float` | `Float.parseFloat("2.5f")` | `float` | | String тЖТ `boolean` | `static boolean parseBoolean(String s)` | `Boolean` | `Boolean.parseBoolean("true")` | `boolean` | | String тЖТ `char` (at index i) | `charAt(int i)` on String | `String` (inherent) | `args[0].charAt(0)` | `char` | | Primitives тЖТ String | `String.valueOf(x)` | `String` | `String.valueOf(42)` | `String` | > [!IMPORTANT] > All `parseXxx` methods **throw `NumberFormatException`** (a checked subclass of `IllegalArgumentException`) if the supplied string is not a valid representation of the target type. For example, `Integer.parseInt("abc")` will crash the program unless handled. --- ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat-Sheet The following compact table is the **minimum recall set** required to answer any KTU question on command line arguments. Memorise the symbols, semantics, and edge cases. | Symbol / Construct | Meaning | Allowed Range / Edge Case | |--------------------|---------|---------------------------| | `String[] args` | The argument array delivered to `main` | May be empty (`args.length == 0`) | | `args.length` | Number of arguments actually typed | Returns `0` if no arguments are passed | | `args[i]` | The *i*-th argument as a `String` | Valid indices: `0` to `args.length - 1` | | `args[0]` | The **first** argument after the class name | Throws `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` if no argument was passed | | `args[args.length - 1]` | The **last** argument | Safe only when `args.length > 0` | | `Integer.parseInt(args[i])` | Converts `args[i]` to `int` | Throws `NumberFormatException` on invalid input | | `Double.parseDouble(args[i])` | Converts `args[i]` to `double` | Throws `NumberFormatException` on invalid input | | `Boolean.parseBoolean(args[i])` | Converts `args[i]` to `boolean` | Returns `true` only for `"true"` (case-insensitive); everything else тЖТ `false` | | Quoted multi-word argument | `"Hello World"` passed as one argument | Unquoted `Hello World` becomes **two** separate arguments | | Direct arithmetic on `args` | `args[0] + args[1]` | Performs **String concatenation**, NOT addition! `"10" + "20"` тЖТ `"1020"` | --- ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Software Industry Command line arguments are not an academic curiosity; they underpin the operation of virtually every production-grade tool an engineer will touch: - **Compilers and Build Tools:** `javac -d ./out MyClass.java` тАФ the `-d` flag and its value are command line arguments. - **JVM Tuning:** `java -Xmx2g -Xms512m MyApp` тАФ heap-size arguments critical for performance engineering. - **DevOps & CI/CD:** Docker, Kubernetes, and shell scripts pass container IDs, ports, and credentials as arguments. - **Data Engineering:** Apache Spark jobs: `spark-submit --master yarn --class org.apache.spark.examples.SparkPi /opt/spark/examples/jars/spark-examples.jar 1000` тАФ the final `1000` (number of slices) is a command line argument consumed by `main(String[] args)`. - **Testing Frameworks:** JUnit can be invoked with `-Dtest=MyTest` system properties тАФ a related but distinct mechanism. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Takeaway:** Mastering `String[] args` early gives a student the conceptual foundation to later understand `ProcessBuilder`, `Runtime.exec`, and shell-script integration тАФ all of which depend on the same argument-passing paradigm. ## 3. Step-by-Step Program Analysis & Analytical Matrix > This section is the **exhaustive analytical walkthrough** of three reference programs. Every line of input, every transition, and every output byte is explained. No step is skipped. --- ### 3.1 Reference Program A тАФ Sum of Two Integers ```java // File: AddNumbers.java public class AddNumbers { public static void main(String[] args) { // STEP 1: Guard clause тАФ verify argument count if (args.length != 2) { System.out.println("Usage: java AddNumbers "); return; } // STEP 2: Type conversion from String to int int num1 = Integer.parseInt(args[0]); int num2 = Integer.parseInt(args[1]); // STEP 3: Arithmetic operation (now safe тАФ operands are int) int sum = num1 + num2; // STEP 4: Output System.out.println("First number = " + num1); System.out.println("Second number = " + num2); System.out.println("Sum = " + sum); } } ``` #### 3.1.1 Compilation Phase The source file is compiled with: ```bash javac AddNumbers.java ``` This produces a bytecode file `AddNumbers.class`. The compiler does **not** validate the use of `args`; it simply allows the program to be compiled as long as the syntax is legal. #### 3.1.2 Execution Phase тАФ Successful Run The user types the following command at the terminal: ```bash java AddNumbers 25 30 ``` **Stage-by-stage execution table:** | Stage | What Happens | Internal State | |-------|--------------|----------------| | 1. Shell parsing | Shell tokenises the line on whitespace. | Token list: `["java", "AddNumbers", "25", "30"]` | | 2. JVM starts | `java` launcher boots JVM and requests the class `AddNumbers`. | Class loader searches for `AddNumbers.class` | | 3. Argument packaging | Tokens after class name packaged into `String[]`. | `args = new String[]{"25", "30"}` | | 4. main() invoked | JVM calls `main(args)`. | `args.length == 2`, `args[0] = "25"`, `args[1] = "30"` | | 5. Guard check | `args.length != 2` is `false`. | Program proceeds past the `if` block | | 6. parseInt(args[0]) | Converts the string `"25"` to integer `25`. | `num1 = 25` | | 7. parseInt(args[1]) | Converts the string `"30"` to integer `30`. | `num2 = 30` | | 8. Addition | `sum = 25 + 30`. | `sum = 55` | | 9. Output | Three `println` calls execute. | See output below | **Terminal Output:** ``` First number = 25 Second number = 30 Sum = 55 ``` #### 3.1.3 Failure Scenario A тАФ Missing Arguments If the user types: ```bash java AddNumbers 25 ``` then `args.length == 1`, the guard clause fires, and the program prints: ``` Usage: java AddNumbers ``` and exits gracefully. This is the **recommended defensive-programming pattern** and is worth full marks in ESE. #### 3.1.4 Failure Scenario B тАФ Non-Numeric Input If the user types: ```bash java AddNumbers ten 30 ``` then `args.length == 2` (guard passes), but `Integer.parseInt("ten")` throws `NumberFormatException` because the literal string `"ten"` is not a valid decimal integer. The JVM prints a stack trace and terminates the program. To prevent this, the conversion lines can be wrapped in a `try-catch` block тАФ see Reference Program C below. > [!IMPORTANT] > **String Concatenation vs Addition Pitfall (KTU Favourite):** > If a student *forgets* to call `parseInt` and writes `int sum = args[0] + args[1];`, the code **will not compile** because the `+` operator is not defined between a `String` and an `int`. However, if a student writes `String result = args[0] + args[1];`, the result is the **concatenated string** `"2530"` тАФ not the integer sum `55`. This is one of the most commonly tested traps in KTU exams. --- ### 3.2 Reference Program B тАФ String Concatenation Without Conversion ```java public class Greet { public static void main(String[] args) { if (args.length == 0) { System.out.println("Hello, Stranger!"); } else { System.out.println("Hello, " + args[0] + "!"); } } } ``` **Execution analysis:** | Command | `args` State | Output | |---------|--------------|--------| | `java Greet` | `args.length == 0` | `Hello, Stranger!` | | `java Greet Arun` | `args = ["Arun"]` | `Hello, Arun!` | | `java Greet "John Doe"` | `args = ["John Doe"]` | `Hello, John Doe!` | | `java Greet John Doe` | `args = ["John", "Doe"]` | `Hello, John!` *(only first name used)* | The last row is a **classic KTU trap**: without quotes, the shell splits `John Doe` into two arguments, and the program prints only `args[0]`. --- ### 3.3 Reference Program C тАФ Safe Conversion with try-catch ```java public class SafeAdd { public static void main(String[] args) { try { int a = Integer.parseInt(args[0]); int b = Integer.parseInt(args[1]); System.out.println("Sum = " + (a + b)); } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) { System.out.println("Error: Please provide two integers."); } catch (NumberFormatException e) { System.out.println("Error: Both arguments must be valid integers."); } } } ``` **Execution analysis matrix:** | Command | Exception Triggered | Output | |---------|--------------------|--------| | `java SafeAdd 5 7` | None | `Sum = 12` | | `java SafeAdd 5` | `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` at `args[1]` | `Error: Please provide two integers.` | | `java SafeAdd five 7` | `NumberFormatException` at `parseInt("five")` | `Error: Both arguments must be valid integers.` | | `java SafeAdd` | `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` at `args[0]` | `Error: Please provide two integers.` | > [!NOTE] > The use of multiple `catch` blocks is preferred over a single generic `catch (Exception e)` because it allows **distinct, user-friendly error messages** for distinct failure modes тАФ a hallmark of professional-grade OOP design. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix тАФ All Reference Programs | # | Program | `args` Length Check | Type Conversion | Exception Handling | Use Case Demonstrated | |---|---------|----------------------|------------------|--------------------|------------------------| | A | `AddNumbers` | `if (args.length != 2)` | `Integer.parseInt` | None (crash on bad input) | Basic addition with guard clause | | B | `Greet` | `if (args.length == 0)` | Not required (String output) | None | Default behaviour + first-name display | | C | `SafeAdd` | Implicit in `catch` | `Integer.parseInt` | `try-catch` with two catches | Production-grade defensive programming | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cross-Reference to KTU Course Outcomes (COs):** > - **CO1:** *Apply the object-oriented programming concepts to design programs in Java.* тАФ Reference Program A satisfies this. > - **CO2:** *Demonstrate the use of arrays, strings, and exception handling in Java.* тАФ Reference Programs B and C satisfy this. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 End-to-End Command Line Argument Flow The diagram below traces a command line argument from the moment the user presses **Enter** in the terminal to the moment it appears as a `String` inside `main`. ```mermaid flowchart TD userInput[User Types Command in Terminal]:::stage shellToken[Shell Splits Command on Whitespace]:::stage jvmBoot[JVM Bootstraps and Loads Target Class]:::stage argPack[Java Launcher Builds String Array]:::stage mainCall[JVM Invokes main with args Parameter]:::stage guardChk[Program Checks args.length]:::stage typeConv[Program Converts Strings to Primitives]:::stage logicExec[Program Logic Executes on Typed Data]:::stage output[Result Printed to Standard Output]:::stage userInput --> shellToken shellToken --> jvmBoot jvmBoot --> argPack argPack --> mainCall mainCall --> guardChk guardChk -- Length OK --> typeConv guardChk -- Length Mismatch --> output typeConv --> logicExec logicExec --> output classDef stage fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **left-side vertical chain** (`userInput` тЖТ `shellToken` тЖТ `jvmBoot` тЖТ `argPack` тЖТ `mainCall`) represents the **JVM's responsibility**. - The **right-side horizontal branch** (`guardChk` тЖТ `typeConv` тЖТ `logicExec` тЖТ `output`) represents the **programmer's responsibility**. - The **decision diamond** is implicit in the `guardChk` node, which has two outgoing edges: one for a length match, one for a mismatch. --- ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of `main(String[] args)` This block diagram is a **functional decomposition** of what happens *inside* the `main` method once the JVM hands control to the program. Each block represents a logical phase that any robust command line program should implement. ```mermaid flowchart LR argsBlock[args Parameter
String Array]:::input guardBlock[Guard Clause
Validates args.length]:::validate parseBlock[Parse Block
Integer.parseInt et al]:::convert logicBlock[Business Logic Block
Arithmetic or String Ops]:::logic outputBlock[Output Block
System.out.println]:::output errBlock[Error Handler Block
try catch Blocks]:::error argsBlock --> guardBlock guardBlock -- Valid --> parseBlock guardBlock -- Invalid --> errBlock parseBlock -- Success --> logicBlock parseBlock -- Failure --> errBlock logicBlock --> outputBlock errBlock --> outputBlock classDef input fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#28A745,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef validate fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#FFC107,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef convert fill:#D1ECF1,stroke:#17A2B8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef logic fill:#F8D7DA,stroke:#DC3545,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#E2E3E5,stroke:#6C757D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef error fill:#F5C6CB,stroke:#721C24,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **green input block** is the `args` array, an immutable handoff from the JVM. - The **yellow guard block** is the first programmer-written check; it protects against `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`. - The **cyan parse block** performs type conversion; it can throw `NumberFormatException`. - The **red logic block** is where the *real work* of the program happens. - The **grey output block** and **dark-red error block** are the two terminal states тАФ every well-written program ends in one of them. --- ### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram тАФ Program Lifecycle This diagram shows the *state* of the program with respect to command line arguments, including all valid transitions. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> NoArgs NoArgs --> ArgsProvided : User supplies arguments NoArgs --> DefaultBehaviour : args.length is zero DefaultBehaviour --> [*] ArgsProvided --> LengthChecked : args.length evaluated LengthChecked --> ParseStage : Length matches expectation LengthChecked --> LengthMismatch : Length does not match LengthMismatch --> ErrorReported ErrorReported --> [*] ParseStage --> Converted : parse methods succeed ParseStage --> NumberFormatThrown : Invalid numeric input NumberFormatThrown --> ErrorReported Converted --> LogicExecuted LogicExecuted --> ResultPrinted ResultPrinted --> [*] ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The diagram has **two entry paths**: with arguments or without. - The **central spine** (`ArgsProvided` тЖТ `LengthChecked` тЖТ `ParseStage` тЖТ `Converted` тЖТ `LogicExecuted` тЖТ `ResultPrinted`) is the *happy path*. - The two error sinks (`LengthMismatch` and `NumberFormatThrown`) both converge on `ErrorReported`, which then terminates the program. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > All questions below are modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern: Part A is 3 marks each, Part B is 14 marks each with internal choice (Or option). Marks are explicitly distributed across sub-parts. **Model answers are exhaustive** тАФ written in the same detail expected from a top-scoring answer script. --- ### 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]` **Define command line arguments. How are they accessed in a Java program? What is the default data type of command line arguments?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Command line arguments are the values supplied to a Java program at the time of execution, typed after the class name in the terminal. They are accessed through the parameter `String[] args` of the `main` method, which the JVM automatically populates before program execution begins. > > **Data Type:** The default data type of every command line argument is **`java.lang.String`**. Even if the user types a numeric value such as `25`, it is stored in `args` as the string `"25"`, and any arithmetic operation requires explicit conversion using wrapper-class methods such as `Integer.parseInt()`. > > *(Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for `String[] args`, 1 mark for `String` default type.)* --- #### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024]` **What is the role of `args.length` in a Java program? What happens if `args.length` is `0`?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The field `args.length` is a **public final `int` instance variable** of the `args` array that returns the total number of arguments actually passed to the program at runtime. It is the **primary defensive check** a programmer uses to validate input before accessing individual elements. > > If `args.length` is `0`, it means **no command line arguments were supplied**. In this state, any attempt to read `args[0]` throws an `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` at runtime. Programs that expect arguments should therefore include a guard clause: > > ```java > if (args.length == 0) { > System.out.println("Usage: java MyClass "); > return; > } > ``` > > *(Distribution: 1 mark for role of `args.length`, 1 mark for `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` explanation, 1 mark for guard-clause example.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B тАФ Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]` **(a)** Write a Java program that accepts **three command line arguments** тАФ a student's `name` (String), `rollNumber` (int), and `marks` (double) тАФ and prints them in a formatted report card. Include a guard clause to handle missing or invalid input. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the differences between the following pairs of concepts in the context of command line arguments: 1. `args[0]` vs `args.length` 2. `Integer.parseInt(args[0])` vs `Integer.valueOf(args[0])` 3. `args[0] + args[1]` (String) vs `Integer.parseInt(args[0]) + Integer.parseInt(args[1])` (int) **(7 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution for Q.A(a) тАФ 7 Marks ```java public class ReportCard { public static void main(String[] args) { // Guard clause 1: argument count if (args.length != 3) { System.out.println("Usage: java ReportCard "); return; } try { // Type conversions String name = args[0]; int rollNo = Integer.parseInt(args[1]); double marks = Double.parseDouble(args[2]); // Guard clause 2: business validation if (marks < 0 || marks > 100) { System.out.println("Error: Marks must be between 0 and 100."); return; } // Formatted output System.out.println("================ REPORT CARD ================"); System.out.println("Name : " + name); System.out.println("Roll No. : " + rollNo); System.out.println("Marks : " + marks); System.out.println("============================================"); } catch (NumberFormatException e) { System.out.println("Error: rollNumber must be an integer and marks must be a number."); } } } ``` **Sample Run:** ```bash javac ReportCard.java java ReportCard Arun 42 89.5 ``` **Output:** ``` ================ REPORT CARD ================ Name : Arun Roll No. : 42 Marks : 89.5 ============================================ ``` **Mark Distribution for Q.A(a):** | Component | Marks | Justification | |-----------|-------|---------------| | Guard clause on `args.length` | 1 | Demonstrates defensive programming | | Correct use of `args[0]`, `args[1]`, `args[2]` | 1 | Indexing knowledge | | Type conversion using `parseInt` and `parseDouble` | 2 | Core command line concept | | Business validation of marks | 1 | Beyond-syllabus polish, rewarded | | Formatted output with `println` | 1 | Clean I/O | | `try-catch` for `NumberFormatException` | 1 | Exception handling (linked to CO2) | --- ##### Model Solution for Q.A(b) тАФ 7 Marks **1. `args[0]` vs `args.length` (2 marks)** | Aspect | `args[0]` | `args.length` | |--------|------------|---------------| | What it represents | The **first** argument (a `String`) | The **count** of arguments (an `int`) | | Data type | `String` | `int` | | Behaviour when `args` is empty | Throws `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | Returns `0` | | Use case | Reading a specific value | Validating the number of inputs | **2. `Integer.parseInt(args[0])` vs `Integer.valueOf(args[0])` (2 marks)** | Aspect | `Integer.parseInt(args[0])` | `Integer.valueOf(args[0])` | |--------|------------------------------|------------------------------| | Return type | Primitive `int` | Wrapper `Integer` object | | Autoboxing? | Not required (already primitive) | Result may be auto-unboxed to `int` | | Memory | Stack allocation (lighter) | Heap allocation (heavier) | | Preferred when | Arithmetic is needed | Object required (e.g., for `Collections`) | **3. `args[0] + args[1]` vs `Integer.parseInt(args[0]) + Integer.parseInt(args[1])` (3 marks)** | Aspect | `args[0] + args[1]` (both `String`) | After `parseInt` (both `int`) | |--------|---------------------------------------|---------------------------------| | Operator semantics | **String concatenation** | **Integer addition** | | Input `args` = `["10", "20"]` | Result: `"1020"` (the string) | Result: `30` (the integer) | | Type of result | `String` | `int` | | Exam trap | Frequently tested тАФ students lose marks by assuming `+` means addition when `args` is involved. | The **only** correct way to perform arithmetic on command line inputs. | --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024]` **(a)** Write a Java program that accepts **two integer command line arguments** and performs **all five basic arithmetic operations** тАФ addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, and modulus тАФ on them. Handle the special case of division by zero. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **limitations of command line arguments** in Java. Suggest two alternative mechanisms for passing input to a program, with one example each. **(7 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution for Q.B(a) тАФ 7 Marks ```java public class ArithmeticOps { public static void main(String[] args) { if (args.length != 2) { System.out.println("Usage: java ArithmeticOps "); return; } try { int a = Integer.parseInt(args[0]); int b = Integer.parseInt(args[1]); System.out.println("Addition : " + (a + b)); System.out.println("Subtraction : " + (a - b)); System.out.println("Multiplication : " + (a * b)); if (b == 0) { System.out.println("Division : Undefined (divide by zero)"); System.out.println("Modulus : Undefined (divide by zero)"); } else { System.out.println("Division : " + (a / b)); System.out.println("Modulus : " + (a % b)); } } catch (NumberFormatException e) { System.out.println("Error: Both arguments must be valid integers."); } } } ``` **Sample Run:** ```bash javac ArithmeticOps.java java ArithmeticOps 20 6 ``` **Output:** ``` Addition : 26 Subtraction : 14 Multiplication : 120 Division : 3 Modulus : 2 ``` **Mark Distribution for Q.B(a):** | Component | Marks | Justification | |-----------|-------|---------------| | Correct parsing with `Integer.parseInt` | 2 | Core concept | | All 5 operations implemented | 3 | 0.6 marks each | | Division-by-zero handling | 1 | Edge case | | `try-catch` for `NumberFormatException` | 1 | Exception handling | --- ##### Model Solution for Q.B(b) тАФ 7 Marks **Limitations of Command Line Arguments (4 marks):** 1. **Limited Interactivity:** Once the program starts, no further input can be accepted from the command line. The arguments are frozen at startup. 2. **No Encryption or Hiding:** Arguments are visible in shell history (`history`), in process lists (`ps`), and in logs тАФ making them unsuitable for **passwords, API keys, or sensitive data**. 3. **All-or-Nothing Input:** Either all required arguments must be passed at once, or the program must implement complex parsing.
