There is no support for interactive prompts. 4. **String-Only by Default:** Numeric data must be **explicitly converted**; failure to do so leads to silent bugs (e.g., concatenation instead of addition). 5. **Difficult for End-Users:** Casual users are uncomfortable with terminals; command line programs exclude the vast majority of non-technical users. 6. **No Validation in the Shell:** The shell does not check argument types or values; all validation must be coded manually in Java. **Alternative Mechanisms (3 marks):** | Alternative | Description | Example | |-------------|-------------|---------| | **Scanner Class** | Reads interactive input from the keyboard (`System.in`) at runtime. | `Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in); int n = sc.nextInt();` | | **BufferedReader** | Reads input line-by-line from the console; more flexible but slightly verbose. | `BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));` | | **JOptionPane (Swing)** | Pops up a graphical dialog box to collect input. | `String name = JOptionPane.showInputDialog("Enter your name");` | | **GUI Forms (Swing/JavaFX)** | Full graphical user interface with text fields and buttons. | A login form that reads username and password. | | **Command Line Files / Properties** | Read from a configuration file or `.properties` file at startup. | `Properties prop = new Properties(); prop.load(new FileInputStream("config.properties"));` | --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE marks on Command Line Argument questions тАФ and how to AVOID it:** > > 1. **Forgetting the type conversion** тАФ writing `int sum = args[0] + args[1];` will not even compile, but writing `String s = args[0] + args[1];` silently gives `"1020"`. **Always use `Integer.parseInt`.** > 2. **Skipping the `args.length` check** тАФ accessing `args[0]` without verifying `args.length > 0` results in `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`. The examiner will deduct **at least 1 mark** for not handling this. > 3. **Confusing `parseInt` and `valueOf`** тАФ these are not the same. `parseInt` returns `int`, `valueOf` returns `Integer`. Read the question carefully. > 4. **Forgetting that command line arguments are space-separated** тАФ `java MyClass John Doe` is two arguments, not one. Quote multi-word arguments. > 5. **Missing `try-catch` for `NumberFormatException`** тАФ even if the question does not explicitly ask for exception handling, adding `try-catch` blocks earns **bonus marks** and demonstrates mastery of CO2. > 6. **Not including a `Usage` message** тАФ a professional touch that is often expected in 14-mark answers and rewarded accordingly. > 7. **Hard-coding values inside `main`** тАФ defeats the entire purpose of command line arguments. The examiner will look specifically for `args[i]` usage. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this and read it the night before the exam):** - тШР Command line arguments are passed to `main(String[] args)` automatically by the JVM. - тШР They are **always `String`** тАФ never `int`, `double`, etc., by default. - тШР `args.length` tells you **how many** arguments were passed; check it **before** accessing any `args[i]`. - тШР `args[0]` is the **first** argument, not `args[1]` тАФ indexing starts at **`0`**. - тШР Use `Integer.parseInt(args[i])`, `Double.parseDouble(args[i])`, `Boolean.parseBoolean(args[i])` for type conversion. - тШР All parse methods throw **`NumberFormatException`** on invalid input; handle with `try-catch`. - тШР Accessing a missing argument throws **`ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`**; handle with a guard clause. - тШР The `+` operator on two `String` arguments performs **concatenation**, not addition тАФ `"10" + "20" = "1020"`. - тШР Multi-word arguments must be enclosed in **double quotes**: `java MyClass "John Doe"`. - тШР A typical **defensive pattern** is: (1) check `args.length`, (2) parse with `try-catch`, (3) validate business rules, (4) execute logic, (5) print output. - тШР The `String[] args` parameter can be renamed (e.g., `String[] argv`) тАФ the name is conventional, not mandatory. - тШР Command line arguments cannot be modified by the program тАФ they are **read-only** at runtime. - тШР Pass **sensitive data** via environment variables or config files, **never** as command line arguments. - тШР Common exam traps: string concatenation, missing `args.length` check, confusion between `parseInt` and `valueOf`. - тШР Always include a **Usage message** in production-grade answers тАФ it is a free mark. - тШР `main` is the **only** method the JVM calls automatically тАФ `args` is the **only** mechanism the JVM uses to pass data into the program at startup.

topics.title: Variable Length Arguments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Variable Length Arguments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Variable Length Arguments** (commonly called **Varargs**) is a Java language feature introduced in **JDK 5.0** that allows a method to accept **zero, one, or multiple arguments of a specified type** without having to overload the method for each possible argument count. It is implemented using the **ellipsis operator (`...`)** placed between the data type and the parameter name.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition**: *"A method that takes a variable number of arguments is known as a variable-arity method or simply varargs method. Internally, the compiler converts these arguments into a single-dimensional array of the specified type."*

**Formal Syntax:**
```java
returnType methodName(dataType ... parameterName) {
    // method body
}
```

**Generalized Form:**
```java
static void display(String message, int ... numbers) {
    // 'numbers' behaves as int[]
}
```

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a **tea stall in Kerala** and ask the vendor: *"Give me some bananas."* You don't tell him **exactly how many** тАФ you just stretch out your hand. The vendor gives you **one, two, or a bunch** depending on your grip. You never know the count in advance.

> Variable Length Arguments work the **same way**: you tell the method *"give me some values of type int"* by writing `int ... x`, and the Java runtime decides **how many** values were passed at the call site. The method is smart enough to handle **0, 1, 2, or 100** values using the **exact same signature**.

- If you pass **0 arguments** тЖТ the array is created with **length 0** (empty, not null).
- If you pass **N arguments** тЖТ the array is created with **length N**.
- If you pass **one argument** тЖТ a one-element array is created.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Examiners**: A method declared with `...` is **not** a different method. It is internally a **method accepting an array** of that type. This means `method(int... x)` and `method(int[] x)` **cannot be overloaded together** тАФ they have the same erased signature!

### Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

| Metric | Value / Behavior |
|---|---|
| **Maximum varargs parameters per method** | **1 (One only)** |
| **Position of varargs** | **Last parameter (mandatory)** |
| **Minimum arguments to varargs** | **0 (zero arguments allowed)** |
| **Internal JVM representation** | **Single-dimensional array** |
| **Java Version introduced** | **JDK 5.0 (J2SE 5.0 / Tiger Release)** |
| **Autoboxing interaction** | Allowed but separate overloads preferred |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Internal Operational Mechanism тАФ Step-by-Step Breakdown

When the Java compiler encounters a method signature containing `...`, it performs the following transformations:

**Step 1 тАФ Source Level**
The programmer writes:
```java
void printNumbers(int ... nums) { ... }
```

**Step 2 тАФ Compiler Transformation (Bytecode Level)**
The compiler converts the ellipsis `...` into a **single-dimensional array parameter**:
```java
void printNumbers(int[] nums) { ... }
```

**Step 3 тАФ Call Site Transformation**
- If arguments are passed individually тЖТ compiler wraps them in an array.
- `printNumbers(1, 2, 3)` becomes `printNumbers(new int[]{1, 2, 3})`.
- `printNumbers()` becomes `printNumbers(new int[0])`.

**Step 4 тАФ Runtime Behavior**
Inside the method body, the parameter can be used exactly like an array тАФ accessed via index, used in `for-each` loops, passed to other methods, etc.

### KTU High-Yield Rules (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These rules are **favourite KTU 2024 question patterns**. Memorize verbatim.

| Rule # | Rule Statement | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **R1** | Only **ONE** varargs parameter is allowed per method | Two `...` would create ambiguity |
| **R2** | The varargs parameter **MUST be the last parameter** | Otherwise the compiler cannot determine where varargs ends |
| **R3** | You **CAN** mix varargs with normal parameters | `void show(String s, int... x)` is legal |
| **R4** | You **CAN** overload methods using varargs | Resolution depends on argument count and type |
| **R5** | You **CANNOT** overload `m(int... x)` and `m(int[] x)` together | Both erase to the same array signature |
| **R6** | `m(int... x)` and `m(int... x, int y)` are **NOT valid** together | Varargs must always be last |
| **R7** | Calling `m(int... x)` with **zero arguments** is legal | Compiler creates an empty array |

### Method Resolution Priority (Compiler Phase)

When a method call is made and multiple overloaded methods match, the compiler applies this **strict 5-step priority order** (extremely high yield for KTU 2024!):

| Priority | Match Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1 (Highest)** | **Exact match** (primitive/widening) | `m(int)` called with `m(10)` |
| **2** | **Autoboxing / Unboxing** | `m(Integer)` called with `m(10)` |
| **3** | **Varargs match** | `m(int... x)` called with `m(10, 20)` |
| **4** | **Varargs with autoboxing** | `m(Integer... x)` called with `m(10)` |
| **5 (Lowest)** | **Most specific varargs** | Resolved by compiler analysis |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Logging frameworks** (like `Log4j`, `SLF4J`) use varargs to accept any number of message arguments.
- **String formatting** methods (e.g., `String.format`) internally use varargs.
- **Mathematical utilities** like `Math.max` were enhanced in Java to support variable arguments.
- **GUI event handlers** where callback counts cannot be predicted in advance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Worked Example 1: Basic Varargs Declaration and Invocation

**Problem:** Demonstrate a method that accepts a variable number of integer arguments and prints their sum.

**Step 1 тАФ Declaration (source code):**
```java
class VarargsDemo {
    static int sum(int ... numbers) {
        int total = 0;
        for (int n : numbers) {
            total += n;
        }
        return total;
    }
}
```

**Step 2 тАФ Compiler interpretation:**
$$
\text{int ... numbers} \;\xrightarrow{\text{compiler}}\; \text{int[] numbers}
$$

The compiler rewrites the method as:
```java
static int sum(int[] numbers) { ... }
```

**Step 3 тАФ Different call sites resolved by the compiler:**

| Call Statement | Compiler-Generated Internal Form | Result |
|---|---|---|
| `sum()` | `sum(new int[0])` | Returns **0** |
| `sum(10)` | `sum(new int[]{10})` | Returns **10** |
| `sum(10, 20)` | `sum(new int[]{10, 20})` | Returns **30** |
| `sum(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)` | `sum(new int[]{1, 2, 3, 4, 5})` | Returns **15** |

**Step 4 тАФ Inside the method, `numbers` behaves as an array:**
$$
\text{numbers.length} = N, \quad \text{numbers}[0], \text{numbers}[1], \ldots, \text{numbers}[N-1]
$$

### Worked Example 2: Varargs Mixed with Normal Parameters

**Problem:** Write a method that accepts a `String` label followed by any number of `double` values.

**Step 1 тАФ Source Code:**
```java
class Report {
    static void printReport(String title, double ... values) {
        System.out.println("Title: " + title);
        for (double v : values) {
            System.out.println("  Value: " + v);
        }
    }
}
```

**Step 2 тАФ Invocation verification:**
- `printReport("Sales", 100.5)` тЖТ valid: `title="Sales"`, `values=[100.5]`
- `printReport("Sales", 100.5, 200.75, 300.0)` тЖТ valid: array of 3 doubles
- `printReport("Sales")` тЖТ valid: `values` becomes an **empty array** (length 0)
- `printReport(100.5, "Sales")` тЖТ **COMPILE ERROR** (varargs must be last)

**Step 3 тАФ Mathematical proof of compliance with Rule R2:**

$$
\text{Parameter Order} = \underbrace{String \; title}_{\text{fixed}} \; \to \; \underbrace{double \ldots \; values}_{\text{varargs, last}}
$$

The general order must be:
$$
\text{fixed}_1, \; \text{fixed}_2, \; \ldots, \; \text{fixed}_n, \; \text{varargs}
$$

Any deviation:
$$
\text{fixed}_1, \; \text{varargs}, \; \text{fixed}_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{COMPILE-TIME ERROR}
$$

### Worked Example 3: Method Resolution Priority (KTU Favourite)

**Problem:** Predict the output of the following program.

```java
class Test {
    static void show(int a) { System.out.println("int"); }
    static void show(int ... a) { System.out.println("varargs"); }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        show(10);
        show(10, 20);
        show();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step resolution (apply the priority table from Section 2):**

| Call | Available Matches | Resolution Path | Selected Method | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `show(10)` | `show(int)`, `show(int...)` | Priority 1: exact match | `show(int)` | **`int`** |
| `show(10, 20)` | `show(int)` (1 arg), `show(int...)` | Only varargs accepts 2 args | `show(int...)` | **`varargs`** |
| `show()` | `show(int)` (needs 1 arg), `show(int...)` | Only varargs accepts 0 args | `show(int...)` | **`varargs`** |

**Final Output:**
$$
\boxed{\text{int} \quad \text{varargs} \quad \text{varargs}}
$$

### Worked Example 4: Ambiguity Error (Compile-Time Pitfall)

**Problem:** Why does the following program cause a compile error?

```java
class Ambiguous {
    static void test(int ... a) { System.out.println("int varargs"); }
    static void test(long ... a) { System.out.println("long varargs"); }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        test(10);  // Which one is called?
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step analysis:**

**Step 1 тАФ Identify the call:** `test(10)` passes an `int` literal.

**Step 2 тАФ Check `test(int ... a)`:**
- Priority 1: `int` is **already** an `int` тЖТ **exact match**.

**Step 3 тАФ Check `test(long ... a)`:**
- Priority 1: `int` widens to `long` тЖТ **wider primitive match**.

**Step 4 тАФ Apply priority rules:**
- Both reach Priority 1 / 2 (no autoboxing needed since `int`/`long` are primitives).
- No single method is **more specific** than the other.

**Step 5 тАФ Compiler conclusion:**
$$
\text{Both methods equally applicable} \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{Reference to test is ambiguous}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{COMPILE-TIME ERROR: reference to test is ambiguous}}
$$

### Analytical Summary Matrix

| Concept | Behavior | KTU Question Likelihood |
|---|---|---|
| Empty varargs call | Legal, creates array of length 0 | тШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕ |
| Varargs as last parameter | Mandatory rule | тШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕ |
| One varargs per method | Mandatory rule | тШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕ |
| Mixing varargs with normal args | Legal | тШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕ |
| Overloading `m(int...x)` and `m(int[]x)` | **Compile error** (duplicate) | тШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕ |
| Priority over autoboxing | Varargs is **lower** priority | тШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕ |
| Cannot pass `int[]` and primitives mixed | `m(1, new int[]{2,3})` is **illegal** | тШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram A: Compiler Transformation Pipeline (Varargs Internals)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Code with int dot dot dot x] --> B[Java Compiler javac]
    B --> C[Bytecode int array x]
    C --> D[JVM Loads Method]
    D --> E{Call Site Analysis}
    E -->|Zero args| F[Create int array length 0]
    E -->|N args| G[Create int array length N]
    F --> H[Execute Method Body]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Return Result]
```

### Diagram B: Method Resolution Priority Flow (KTU High-Yield)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Method Call Encountered] --> Q1{Is there an exact type match?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| P1[Select Exact Match Method]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is there an autoboxing or unboxing match?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| P2[Select Boxing Match]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is there a varargs match?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| P3[Select Varargs Method]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is there a varargs with boxing match?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| P4[Select Boxing Varargs Method]
    Q4 -->|No| Err[COMPILE TIME ERROR No Method Found]
```

### Diagram C: Parameter Ordering Rules (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Legal["LEGAL METHOD SIGNATURES"]
        L1[m int dot dot dot x]
        L2[m String s int dot dot dot x]
        L3[m String s int n int dot dot dot x]
    end
    subgraph Illegal["ILLEGAL METHOD SIGNATURES"]
        I1[m int dot dot dot x int y]
        I2[m int dot dot dot x String s]
        I3[m int dot dot dot x int dot dot dot y]
    end
```

### Diagram D: Varargs Call Site Wrapping Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Call[User Writes m of 1 comma 2 comma 3] --> Wrap[Compiler Wraps in Array]
    Wrap --> New1[New int array of 1 comma 2 comma 3]
    Call2[User Writes m of empty] --> Wrap2[Compiler Wraps in Empty Array]
    Wrap2 --> New2[New int array of length 0]
    Call3[User Writes m of int array of 1 comma 2] --> Direct[Pass Array Directly]
```

### Diagram E: Overloading with Varargs тАФ Resolution Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Method Call show of args] --> A{Number of Arguments}
    A -->|0 args| V[Select show int dot dot dot]
    A -->|1 arg| T{Type of Argument}
    T -->|int| E[Select show int Exact Match Wins]
    T -->|long| W[Select show long via Widening]
    A -->|2 or more args| V2[Only show int dot dot dot matches]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024, Model]**
*Define Variable Length Arguments in Java. Write its syntax and state any two rules that must be followed while using varargs.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Variable Length Arguments (varargs) is a feature in Java, introduced in JDK 5.0, that allows a method to accept zero or more arguments of the same type. Internally, the compiler treats the varargs parameter as a single-dimensional array.

**Syntax:**
```java
returnType methodName(dataType ... parameterName)
```

**Two Rules:**
1. A method can have **only one** varargs parameter.
2. The varargs parameter must be the **last parameter** in the method signature.
3. (Bonus) Zero or more arguments can be passed to a varargs parameter.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023, Model]**
*What happens internally when a varargs method is called with zero arguments? Explain briefly.*

**Model Answer:**

When a varargs method is called with **zero arguments**, the Java compiler internally creates an **empty array of length 0** (not `null`) and passes it to the method.

**Example:**
```java
void display(int ... x) { System.out.println(x.length); }
display();   // Output: 0
display(5);  // Output: 1
```

The array is initialized with `new int[0]`, so any iteration (`for-each` loop) over `x` simply executes zero times without throwing a `NullPointerException`.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each тАФ Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** тАФ [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2024, Model]

**(a)** Explain the concept of Variable Length Arguments in Java with a suitable example. List any **four rules** that must be followed when declaring a varargs method. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following Java program. Predict the output with proper justification for each `printf` call. **(7 Marks)**

```java
class Demo {
    static void test(int x) {
        System.out.println("test(int): " + x);
    }
    static void test(int ... x) {
        System.out.println("test(int...): length=" + x.length);
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        test();
        test(5);
        test(10, 20);
        test(new int[]{1, 2, 3});
    }
}
```

**Model Solution for (a):**

Variable Length Arguments allow a method to accept a variable number of arguments of the same type. It uses the ellipsis `...` operator.

**Example:**
```java
static int total(int ... marks) {
    int sum = 0;
    for (int m : marks) sum += m;
    return sum;
}
```

**Four Rules:**
1. Only **one** varargs parameter is allowed per method.
2. Varargs must be the **last** parameter in the method signature.
3. Zero or more arguments can be passed.
4. Varargs is internally treated as an **array**; hence `m(int...x)` and `m(int[]x)` cannot be overloaded together.

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Call Statement | Argument Count | Resolution | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| `test()` | 0 | Only `test(int...)` matches | `test(int...): length=0` |
| `test(5)` | 1 | `test(int)` is exact match (Priority 1) | `test(int): 5` |
| `test(10, 20)` | 2 | Only `test(int...)` matches | `test(int...): length=2` |
| `test(new int[]{1,2,3})` | Array passed | Compiler treats as `test(int[])` тЖТ matches `test(int...)` | `test(int...): length=3` |

**Final Output:**
```
test(int...): length=0
test(int): 5
test(int...): length=2
test(int...): length=3
```

**Justification:** The compiler always prefers an **exact match with fixed parameters** over a varargs match. Hence, when a single `int` argument is provided, `test(int)` wins.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** тАФ [KTU University Exam тАУ May 2023, Model]

**(a)** Explain the **method resolution priority** used by the Java compiler when varargs methods are overloaded with other methods. Illustrate with an example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that accepts a course code and a variable number of student marks. Compute and display the **average, highest, and lowest** mark using varargs. Handle the case where **no marks** are passed. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The Java compiler resolves overloaded methods using this strict priority order:

**Priority 1 (Highest):** Exact type match (no widening, no boxing).
**Priority 2:** Widening primitive conversion (`int` тЖТ `long`).
**Priority 3:** Autoboxing / Unboxing (`int` тЖФ `Integer`).
**Priority 4:** Varargs match.
**Priority 5 (Lowest):** Varargs with autoboxing.

**Illustration:**
```java
class Resolve {
    static void show(int x)         { System.out.println("int"); }
    static void show(Integer x)     { System.out.println("Integer"); }
    static void show(int ... x)     { System.out.println("varargs"); }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        show(10);        // Output: int   (Priority 1)
        show(10L);       // Output: varargs (long тЖТ no exact match, varargs widens)
    }
}
```

**Model Solution for (b):**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

class MarksAnalyzer {
    static void analyze(String courseCode, int ... marks) {
        System.out.println("Course: " + courseCode);
        if (marks.length == 0) {
            System.out.println("No marks provided. Exiting.");
            return;
        }
        int sum = 0, highest = marks[0], lowest = marks[0];
        for (int m : marks) {
            sum += m;
            if (m > highest) highest = m;
            if (m < lowest)  lowest  = m;
        }
        double average = (double) sum / marks.length;
        System.out.println("Average: " + average);
        System.out.println("Highest: " + highest);
        System.out.println("Lowest : " + lowest);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        analyze("PBCST304", 85, 90, 78, 92, 88);
        analyze("PBCST304");
    }
}
```

**Sample Output:**
```
Course: PBCST304
Average: 86.6
Highest: 92
Lowest : 78
Course: PBCST304
No marks provided. Exiting.
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "last parameter" rule:** Many students write `void m(int... x, String s)` and lose **2 full marks** in Part B. Always check parameter ordering first.
> 2. **Confusing varargs with method overloading:** Writing both `m(int... x)` and `m(int[] x)` will cause a **duplicate method error**. The compiler treats them as the same method.
> 3. **Assuming `null` for zero arguments:** Zero arguments create an **empty array (`new int[0]`)**, **NOT** `null`. Iterating over it is safe.
> 4. **Incorrect priority reasoning:** Students often claim varargs has the **highest** priority. The correct order is: **exact match тЖТ boxing тЖТ varargs**.
> 5. **Mixing array and individual arguments in the call site:** `m(1, new int[]{2,3})` is **illegal** because the compiler cannot decide where the varargs list ends.
> 6. **Forgetting to import `Scanner` or use proper I/O in Part B code questions** тАФ KTU deducts marks for non-compilable code.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- тЬЕ Varargs was introduced in **Java 5.0** (J2SE 5.0).
- тЬЕ Syntax uses the **ellipsis (`...`)** operator between type and parameter name.
- тЬЕ Internally, the compiler converts varargs to a **single-dimensional array**.
- тЬЕ Only **ONE** varargs parameter is permitted per method.
- тЬЕ Varargs parameter **must be the last** parameter in the signature.
- тЬЕ **Zero arguments are legal** тАФ creates an empty array of length `0`.
- тЬЕ Varargs and autoboxing/unboxing are **independent overload-resolution phases**.
- тЬЕ Priority order: **Exact Match тЖТ Widening тЖТ Boxing/Unboxing тЖТ Varargs тЖТ Varargs with Boxing**.
- тЬЕ Cannot overload `m(int...x)` and `m(int[]x)` together тАФ same erased signature.
- тЬЕ Cannot mix varargs with array arguments in a single call (e.g., `m(1, new int[]{2,3})` is illegal).
- тЬЕ `m(int...x)` and `m(int...x, int y)` together are **invalid** тАФ varargs must be last.
- тЬЕ Varargs is heavily used in **logging frameworks, printf, format strings, and assertion mechanisms**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **CLASS (Object-Oriented Programming Definition):**
> A **class** is a user-defined, composite, reference data type in C++ that serves as a **blueprint**, **template**, or **prototype** for creating objects. It encapsulates **data members** (attributes/state) and **member functions** (methods/behaviour) into a single, cohesive logical unit, supporting the OOP pillars of **Encapsulation** and **Data Abstraction**.

Mathematically, a class $C$ can be represented as a set of attributes and operations:

$$C = \{A, M, V\}$$

Where:
- $A$ = Set of attributes (data members) $\rightarrow A = \{a_1, a_2, a_3, \dots, a_n\}$
- $M$ = Set of member functions (methods) $\rightarrow M = \{m_1, m_2, m_3, \dots, m_n\}$
- $V$ = Set of visibility/access specifiers $\rightarrow V = \{public, private, protected\}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A class is **declaration only** тАФ no memory is allocated for data members until an **object (instance)** of that class is created. This is a frequently asked concept in KTU examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect**:

| Real-World Entity | OOP Counterpart | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Architectural Blueprint | **Class** | Defines structure but is not a building itself |
| Actual Building constructed | **Object** | A real, tangible instance occupying space |
| Rooms, doors, windows | **Data Members** | Properties describing the structure |
| "Open door", "Switch on light" | **Member Functions** | Behaviours/actions the structure can perform |
| Locked vs Open doors | **Access Specifiers** | Control over who/what can access parts |

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Explanation:** A class is like a *recipe for biriyani*. The recipe (class) lists ingredients and steps, but it is not food. Only when you actually cook using that recipe do you get a *dish* (object) that occupies your plate (memory).

## 1.3 Core Characteristics of a Class

1. **User-defined type** тАФ Programmer creates it (unlike `int`, `float`).
2. **Reference type in C++** тАФ Objects are accessed via references/pointers (unlike structures in C, which are value types by default).
3. **Encapsulation unit** тАФ Bundles data + functions together.
4. **Abstraction enabler** тАФ Hides internal complexity using `private`/`protected`.
5. **Access control granularity** тАФ Three visibility levels: `public`, `private`, `protected`.
6. **No memory on declaration** тАФ Memory is allocated only when an object is instantiated.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Boundaries

| Property | Standard Value / Boundary |
|---|---|
| Default access in `class` | **private** |
| Default access in `struct` | **public** |
| Minimum members to form a class | **Zero (empty class is valid, size = 1 byte)** |
| Maximum access specifiers per class | **No hard limit (KTU perspective)** |
| Keyword to define | `class` (C++), `public class` (Java) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomical Structure of a C++ Class тАФ Operational Breakdown

A class declaration in C++ follows a strict, layered structure. Let us dissect each layer:

### Layer 1 тАФ Class Header
```cpp
class ClassName {
```
This opens the class scope. The keyword `class` is mandatory in C++ (unlike `struct`, where it is optional).

### Layer 2 тАФ Access Specifier Block
```cpp
private:    // visible only inside the class
protected:  // visible inside class + derived classes
public:     // visible everywhere
```

### Layer 3 тАФ Data Members (State)
```cpp
int rollNo;       // attribute
float marks;      // attribute
char name[50];    // attribute
```

### Layer 4 тАФ Member Functions (Behaviour)
```cpp
void setData(int r);   // mutator
int getRollNo();       // accessor
```

### Layer 5 тАФ Scope Resolution Closure
```cpp
};
```

## 2.2 The Three Pillars Encoded in a Class

| Pillar | Class Mechanism | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Encapsulation** | Binding data + functions into one unit (`class`) | Reduces system complexity |
| **Abstraction** | `private`/`protected` hiding implementation | Protects internal state |
| **Modularity** | One class = one logical entity | Improves maintainability |

## 2.3 Access Specifier Decision Matrix

| Access Specifier | Accessible Within Class | Accessible in Derived Class | Accessible Outside Class | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `private` | **Yes** | **No** | **No** | Internal data, helper functions |
| `protected` | **Yes** | **Yes** | **No** | Data meant for inheritance |
| `public` | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | Interface (functions exposed to user) |

## 2.4 Defining Member Functions тАФ Two Methods

### Method 1: Inside the Class (Implicitly Inline)
```cpp
class Student {
    public:
        void display() {   // Inline by default
            cout << "Hello";
        }
};
```

### Method 2: Outside the Class (Using Scope Resolution Operator `::`)
```cpp
class Student {
    public:
        void display();    // Declaration only
};

void Student::display() {  // Definition outside
    cout << "Hello";
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The scope resolution operator `::` tells the compiler that the function `display()` belongs to the class `Student`. Without `Student::`, it becomes a regular global function тАФ a very common mistake leading to *compilation errors* in board exams.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Size of empty class | $Size = 1 \text{ byte}$ | To ensure unique address per object |
| 2 | Size with members | $Size = \sum(\text{sizeof each member}) + \text{padding}$ | Memory estimation |
| 3 | Object memory | $Memory(obj) = Size(class)$ | Each object gets independent copy |
| 4 | Static member | Shared across **all** objects (one copy only) | Class-level data |
| 5 | Access default (`class`) | **private** | Without explicit specifier |
| 6 | Access default (`struct`) | **public** | C-compatibility reason |
| 7 | Number of objects possible | $N = \frac{\text{Available Heap Memory}}{Size(Class)}$ | Theoretical limit |
| 8 | Function binding | Early binding (static) by default; `virtual` enables late binding | Polymorphism support |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of Classes

| Engineering Domain | Class Use-Case |
|---|---|
| **Embedded Systems** | Class for `Sensor` with `read()`, `calibrate()` methods |
| **Database Systems** | Class for `Connection`, `Query`, `Transaction` |
| **Game Development** | Classes for `Player`, `Enemy`, `Weapon`, `Level` |
| **Automotive (ECU)** | Classes for `EngineControlUnit`, `BrakeSystem` |
| **Networking** | Classes for `Packet`, `Router`, `Socket` |
| **Compiler Design** | Classes for `Token`, `SymbolTable`, `ASTNode` |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Memory Footprint of a Class Object

Let us rigorously derive how much memory an object occupies given a class definition.

### Step 1 тАФ Class Definition
```cpp
class Box {
    int length;     // 4 bytes
    int width;      // 4 bytes
    int height;     // 4 bytes
};
```

### Step 2 тАФ Sum of Primitive Member Sizes
$$Size_{raw} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{sizeof}(member_i)$$

### Step 3 тАФ Substitute Values
$$\begin{aligned}
Size_{raw} &= \text{sizeof}(length) + \text{sizeof}(width) + \text{sizeof}(height) \\
&= 4 + 4 + 4 \\
&= 12 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 тАФ Apply Alignment Padding Rule
The compiler aligns members to the size of the largest primitive type. Since all are `int` (4 bytes), no extra padding is required.

$$Size_{object} = Size_{raw} + Padding = 12 + 0 = 12 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 5 тАФ Validation with Mixed Types
```cpp
class Mixed {
    char c;      // 1 byte
    int i;       // 4 bytes
    double d;    // 8 bytes
};
```

$$\begin{aligned}
Size_{raw} &= 1 + 4 + 8 = 13 \text{ bytes} \\
Largest &= 8 \text{ bytes (alignment boundary)} \\
Size_{aligned} &= 16 \text{ bytes (after padding)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Logic:** Raw sum gives 13 bytes. Alignment forces the total to be a multiple of 8 тЖТ next multiple тЙе 13 is 16. Hence, 3 bytes of padding are inserted.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix тАФ Class vs Object vs Structure

| Parameter | Class | Object | Structure |
|---|---|---|---|
| What is it? | Blueprint / Template | Instance of class | C-style user-defined type |
| Memory allocated? | **No** (declaration only) | **Yes** (on instantiation) | **Yes** (on declaration) |
| Default access | `private` | N/A | `public` |
| Can have functions? | **Yes** | Inherits from class | Only in C++ (not in C) |
| Reference type? | **Yes** (in C++) | Yes | No (value type in C) |
| Supports inheritance? | **Yes** | Inherits | Limited |
| Existence | Logical | Physical | Physical |

## 3.3 Derivation: Number of Member Functions Accessible

For a class with $p$ private functions, $q$ protected functions, and $r$ public functions:

$$Total_{functions} = p + q + r$$

Accessible from **outside** the class:
$$Accessible_{outside} = r$$

Accessible from a **derived class**:
$$Accessible_{derived} = q + r$$

Accessible from **within** the class:
$$Accessible_{inside} = p + q + r$$

### Worked Example
Suppose a class has $p = 3$ private, $q = 2$ protected, $r = 5$ public functions.

$$\begin{aligned}
Total &= 3 + 2 + 5 = 10 \\
From\ outside &= 5 \\
From\ derived &= 2 + 5 = 7 \\
From\ inside &= 3 + 2 + 5 = 10
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Static vs Non-Static Member Memory

Let $N$ be the number of objects and $S$ be the size of one non-static data member.

**Non-static members** (instance variables):
$$Memory_{non\text{-}static} = N \times S$$

**Static members** (class variables):
$$Memory_{static} = S \quad (\text{shared, only one copy})$$

**Total memory for N objects:**
$$\begin{aligned}
Memory_{total} &= Memory_{non\text{-}static} + Memory_{static} \\
&= N \times S_{non\text{-}static} + S_{static}
\end{aligned}$$

### Example
Class `Counter` has one non-static `int id` (4 bytes) and one static `int count` (4 bytes).

For $N = 100$ objects:
$$Memory_{total} = (100 \times 4) + 4 = 404 \text{ bytes}$$

## 3.5 Complete C++ Class Declaration тАФ Annotated Model

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Student {                  // Class declaration begins
private:                          // Access specifier 1
    int rollNo;                   // Data member 1
    float marks;                  // Data member 2

protected:                        // Access specifier 2
    char grade;                   // Data member 3 (inheritable)

public:                           // Access specifier 3
    Student();                    // Default constructor declaration
    void setData(int r, float m); // Mutator function declaration
    void display();               // Accessor function declaration
};

Student::Student() {              // Constructor definition
    rollNo = 0;
    marks = 0.0;
    grade = 'F';
}

void Student::setData(int r, float m) {   // Mutator definition
    rollNo = r;
    marks = m;
    if (marks >= 90) grade = 'A';
    else grade = 'B';
}

void Student::display() {                 // Accessor definition
    cout << "Roll: " << rollNo;
    cout << ", Marks: " << marks;
    cout << ", Grade: " << grade;
}

int main() {
    Student s1;        // Object instantiation
    s1.setData(101, 95.5);
    s1.display();
    return 0;
}
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Class

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDefClass fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDefData fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#9a3412,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDefFunc fill:#14532d,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDefAccess fill:#581c87,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDefObject fill:#854d0e,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    A["CLASS Student"]:::classDefClass

    A --> B["ACCESS SPECIFIERS"]:::classDefAccess
    B --> B1["private"]:::classDefAccess
    B --> B2["protected"]:::classDefAccess
    B --> B3["public"]:::classDefAccess

    A --> C["DATA MEMBERS STATE"]:::classDefData
    C --> C1["int rollNo"]:::classDefData
    C --> C2["float marks"]:::classDefData
    C --> C3["char grade"]:::classDefData

    A --> D["MEMBER FUNCTIONS BEHAVIOUR"]:::classDefFunc
    D --> D1["Student constructor"]:::classDefFunc
    D --> D2["void setData"]:::classDefFunc
    D --> D3["void display"]:::classDefFunc

    A --> E["INSTANTIATED OBJECTS"]:::classDefObject
    E --> E1["Student s1"]:::classDefObject
    E --> E2["Student s2"]:::classDefObject
    E --> E3["Student s3"]:::classDefObject
```

## 4.2 Access Visibility Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDefScope fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDefDerived fill:#7e22ce,stroke:#9333ea,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDefOutside fill:#991b1b,stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDefMember fill:#166534,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    P["private members"]:::classDefScope
    PR["protected members"]:::classDefDerived
    PU["public members"]:::classDefOutside

    SC["WITHIN CLASS SCOPE"]:::classDefScope
    DC["DERIVED CLASS SCOPE"]:::classDefDerived
    OC["OUTSIDE CLASS MAIN"]:::classDefOutside

    SC --> P
    SC --> PR
    SC --> PU

    DC --> PR
    DC --> PU

    OC --> PU

    P -.denied.- DC
    P -.denied.- OC
    PR -.denied.- OC
```

## 4.3 Memory Layout Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDefObj fill:#9f1239,stroke:#be123c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDefMem fill:#1e40af,stroke:#2563eb,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDefStatic fill:#5b21b6,stroke:#7c3aed,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    OBJ["HEAP OR STACK MEMORY"]:::classDefObj
    OBJ --> M1["OBJECT s1 size 12 bytes"]:::classDefMem
    OBJ --> M2["OBJECT s2 size 12 bytes"]:::classDefMem
    OBJ --> M3["OBJECT s3 size 12 bytes"]:::classDefMem

    M1 --> F1["rollNo 4 bytes"]:::classDefMem
    M1 --> F2["marks 4 bytes"]:::classDefMem
    M1 --> F3["grade 4 bytes"]:::classDefMem

    STAT["STATIC DATA SEGMENT"]:::classDefStatic
    STAT --> S1["objectCount SHARED 4 bytes"]:::classDefStatic
```

## 4.4 Class Definition vs Object Instantiation Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Programmer
    participant C as Compiler
    participant M as Memory

    P->>C: Writes class Student { ... };
    Note over C: CLASS DECLARATION PHASE
    C->>C: No memory allocated for data members
    C-->>P: Compile OK, blueprint ready

    P->>C: Student s1;
    Note over C: OBJECT CREATION PHASE
    C->>M: Allocate sizeof Student bytes for s1
    M-->>C: Address 0x7FFE.. returned
    C-->>P: s1 ready at address 0x7FFE..

    P->>C: Student s2, s3;
    C->>M: Allocate 2 more chunks
    M-->>C: Two distinct addresses returned
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024]
**Define a class in C++. What is the difference between a class and an object?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

A **class** in C++ is a user-defined data type that acts as a blueprint for creating objects. It binds together **data members** (variables) and **member functions** (methods) into a single logical unit, supporting encapsulation.

**Difference Table:**

| Basis | Class | Object |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Logical template | Physical instance |
| Memory | No memory allocated | Memory is allocated |
| Declaration keyword | `class` | Class name as type |
| Existence | At compile time | At runtime |

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for difference)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]
**Explain the three access specifiers in a class with their visibility scope.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

The three access specifiers in a C++ class are:

1. **private:** Members are accessible **only within the same class**. Not accessible from derived classes or outside.
2. **protected:** Members are accessible **within the class and its derived classes**, but not from outside.
3. **public:** Members are accessible **everywhere** тАФ inside the class, derived class, and outside (e.g., from `main()`).

Default access in a `class` is `private`; default in a `struct` is `public`. *(1 mark each)*

---

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A тАФ Choice 1 [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of a class in C++ with a suitable example. Discuss the role of access specifiers. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

A **class** is a blueprint for creating objects, encapsulating data and behaviour.

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class BankAccount {
private:
    int accountNumber;
    double balance;

public:
    BankAccount(int acc, double bal) {
        accountNumber = acc;
        balance = bal;
    }

    void deposit(double amount) {
        if (amount > 0) balance += amount;
    }

    void withdraw(double amount) {
        if (amount <= balance) balance -= amount;
    }

    void display() {
        cout << "Account: " << accountNumber;
        cout << ", Balance: " << balance;
    }
};
```

**Role of Access Specifiers:**

- `private` тЖТ `accountNumber` and `balance` are hidden, ensuring **data security**.
- `public` тЖТ `deposit()`, `withdraw()`, `display()` form the **interface** for outside interaction.

This demonstrates **encapsulation**: internal data is protected, and only controlled access is permitted. *(7 marks: 3 for example, 2 for access specifier role, 2 for encapsulation discussion)*

---

**(b)** Differentiate between defining member functions inside and outside the class. When is each method preferred? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

| Aspect | Inside Class | Outside Class |
|---|---|---|
| Default nature | **Implicitly inline** | Normal function |
| Code structure | Class definition becomes long | Cleaner separation |
| Compilation | Faster (inline expansion) | Requires scope resolution `::` |
| Use case | Small, one-line functions | Large, complex functions |

**Outside definition syntax:**
```cpp
return_type ClassName::functionName(args) {
    // body
}
```

The scope resolution operator `::` is mandatory тАФ it links the function to its parent class.

**Preference rule:**
- *Inside:* Short accessor/mutator functions (e.g., `getX()`).
- *Outside:* Long functions, templates, or when the class definition file is separated from implementation file (header + source pattern). *(7 marks: 3 for difference table, 2 for syntax, 2 for preference)*

---

### Question B тАФ Choice 2 [KTU University Exam тАУ May 2023]

**(a)** What is a class? Write a C++ program to define a class `Rectangle` with data members `length` and `width`, and member functions to calculate area and perimeter. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

A **class** is a user-defined type that combines data and functions into one unit, serving as a template for objects.

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Rectangle {
private:
    float length;
    float width;

public:
    void setDimensions(float l, float w) {
        length = l;
        width = w;
    }

    float calculateArea() {
        return length * width;
    }

    float calculatePerimeter() {
        return 2 * (length + width);
    }
};

int main() {
    Rectangle rect;
    rect.setDimensions(10.5, 5.2);
    cout << "Area: " << rect.calculateArea();
    cout << "\nPerimeter: " << rect.calculatePerimeter();
    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**
```
Area: 54.6
Perimeter: 31.4
```

**Explanation:**
- Data members `length` and `width` are kept `private` to enforce data hiding.
- `setDimensions()` is a mutator; `calculateArea()` and `calculatePerimeter()` are accessors/computational methods.
- Object `rect` is instantiated in `main()`. *(7 marks: 2 for definition, 3 for code, 2 for explanation)*

---

**(b)** Explain the concept of static data members in a class. How do they differ from non-static members? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Static data members** belong to the **class itself**, not to any individual object. They are shared across all objects of the class тАФ only **one copy** exists in memory regardless of the number of objects.

**Non-static members** belong to **each object separately** тАФ every object has its own independent copy.

```cpp
class Employee {
private:
    int id;              // non-static
    static int count;    // static - shared

public:
    Employee() { count++; }
    static int getCount() { return count; }
};

int Employee::count = 0;  // mandatory definition outside class
```

**Key Differences:**

| Feature | Static Member | Non-Static Member |
|---|---|---|
| Ownership | Belongs to class | Belongs to object |
| Number of copies | One (shared) | One per object |
| Memory location | Static data segment | Heap/Stack (with object) |
| Access via | `ClassName::member` or object | Only via object |
| Initialization | Outside class with `::` | Inside constructor |

**Real-world analogy:** A static variable is like a *company-wide notice board* (one for all employees), while a non-static variable is each *employee's personal ID card*. *(7 marks: 2 for concept, 3 for example, 2 for differences)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting `ClassName::` before static member definition** тЖТ Compilation error (loses 2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing `class` (default private) with `struct` (default public)** тЖТ Logic errors in inheritance code.
> 3. **Defining a member function outside the class without the scope resolution operator `::`** тЖТ The function becomes global, leading to *undefined reference* errors.
> 4. **Writing `int Employee::count = 0;` inside the class body** тЖТ Treated as a *re-declaration*, not definition. Marks deducted for missing outside definition.
> 5. **Allocating memory to a class declaration directly** тЖТ Conceptually wrong; only objects get memory.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Class** = Blueprint / template; **Object** = Instance of class.
- Default access in `class` is **private**; in `struct` it is **public**.
- A class **does not occupy memory** at declaration time; memory is allocated **only when an object is created**.
- **Three access specifiers:** `private`, `protected`, `public`.
- **Scope resolution operator `::`** is mandatory when defining member functions outside the class.
- **Inline functions:** those defined *inside* the class body are *implicitly inline*.
- **Static members** are shared across all objects; defined outside the class with `ClassName::`.
- **Empty class size = 1 byte** (to ensure unique address per object).
- **Encapsulation** is achieved by binding data + functions and using access specifiers.
- A class supports **inheritance, polymorphism, and abstraction** (other OOP pillars).
- The keyword `class` is mandatory in C++ to declare a class (unlike `struct`).
- Each object has its **own copy** of non-static data members.
- Member functions can be **overloaded** but cannot be made `virtual` in `struct` (C++ specific).
- Class declaration ends with a **semicolon** (`;`) тАФ common KTU mistake to forget.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Abstract Classes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Abstract Classes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Abstract Class (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> An **abstract class** in Java is a class declared with the `abstract` keyword that is considered **incomplete** by the compiler. It serves as a *blueprint* for other classes, allowing a mixture of **abstract methods** (method signatures without a body) and **concrete methods** (fully implemented methods). An abstract class **cannot be instantiated** directly using the `new` keyword, but it can be subclassed, providing a common base for a group of related, derived classes.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect drafting the master plan for a housing colony**. You define the **mandatory structural rules** тАФ every house *must* have a kitchen, a bedroom, a living room, and a roof. However, you, the architect, never actually *build* a "generic house." You leave the design of the **kitchen's interior** and the **bedroom's furniture** to the individual house owners (the concrete subclasses).

In this analogy:
- The **architect's master plan** = the **Abstract Class**.
- The **mandatory rules (with no fixed design)** = **Abstract Methods** (every subclass *must* implement them).
- The **fixed, pre-designed rules (like a standard window size)** = **Concrete Methods** (already implemented, inherited as-is).
- The **actual houses built by owners** = **Concrete Subclasses** that `extend` the abstract class.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need Abstract Classes?**
> - To enforce a **contract** тАФ guaranteeing that all subclasses follow a common structure.
> - To enable **polymorphism** тАФ you can refer to any subclass object using an abstract class reference.
> - To promote **code reusability** тАФ common logic is written once in the abstract class and inherited.

### Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `abstract` keyword | Used to declare a class or method as abstract |
| Abstract method | A method declared *without* a body (only signature) |
| Concrete method | A method with a complete implementation (with body) |
| Instantiation | The act of creating an object using `new` тАФ *forbidden* for abstract classes |
| Subclass | A class that `extends` an abstract class and provides implementations |
| Partial Abstraction | Abstract classes support **0% to 100% abstraction** |
| Pure Abstract Class | An abstract class with **no concrete methods** at all |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Syntax of Declaring an Abstract Class

The general syntax in Java follows this pattern:

```java
abstract class ClassName {
    // data members (variables)
    // concrete methods (with body)
    // abstract methods (without body)
}
```

The `abstract` keyword can be placed **before** or **after** the access modifier (e.g., `public abstract class` or `abstract public class`). Conventionally, `public` comes first.

### 2.2 Rules Governing Abstract Classes (The "Golden Rules")

1. **Cannot be Instantiated:** You cannot write `AbstractClass obj = new AbstractClass();`. The compiler will throw an error.
2. **May or May Not Contain Abstract Methods:** A class is abstract if declared so, even if it has zero abstract methods.
3. **Abstract Methods Have No Body:** They end with a semicolon, not curly braces.
   - *Correct:* `abstract void draw();`
   - *Wrong:* `abstract void draw() { }` тАФ this is a syntax error.
4. **Any Class with At Least One Abstract Method Must Be Declared Abstract:** Otherwise, the compiler raises an error.
5. **Subclasses Must Override All Abstract Methods:** Unless the subclass is also declared `abstract`.
6. **Abstract Classes Can Have Constructors:** These are invoked when a subclass object is created (via the chain `super()` call).
7. **Abstract Classes Can Have `final` Methods:** Such methods cannot be overridden by subclasses.
8. **Abstract Classes Can Have `static` Methods:** These can be called using the abstract class name.
9. **Abstract Classes Can Have Member Variables:** Both `static` and `non-static`, `final` and `non-final`.
10. **Abstract Classes Can Implement Interfaces:** Without necessarily implementing all the interface methods.
11. **Cannot Be Combined with `final`:** You cannot write `final abstract class` because `final` prevents inheritance, while `abstract` *requires* it тАФ they are contradictory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - A class is **abstract** by **declaration** OR by **inheriting an unimplemented abstract method**.
> - A class is **concrete** only if **every** method has an implementation.

### 2.3 Abstract Methods тАФ The Contract

An abstract method is essentially a *promise* made by the abstract class to its subclasses:

> "I am telling you that this method **must exist**, but I am leaving its **implementation to you**."

```java
abstract class Shape {
    abstract double area();      // contract: every subclass must implement area()
    abstract double perimeter(); // contract: every subclass must implement perimeter()
}
```

If a concrete subclass fails to implement **any** of these abstract methods, the subclass itself must be declared `abstract`.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Rule / Property | Description | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| `abstract class X { }` | Declares X as abstract | **High** |
| `abstract void method();` | Declares a method with no body | **High** |
| Cannot instantiate | `new AbstractClass()` is illegal | **Very High** |
| Subclass must implement | Or be declared abstract itself | **Very High** |
| Can have constructors | Used via `super()` in subclass | **Medium** |
| Can have `static` methods | Called via class name | **Medium** |
| Cannot be `final` | Mutually exclusive modifiers | **High** |
| Supports partial abstraction | Mix of abstract + concrete methods | **High** |
| Single inheritance | A class extends **one** abstract class | **High** |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In real software engineering, abstract classes are foundational in:

- **Framework Design:** Frameworks like Spring and Hibernate define abstract base classes that user code extends.
- **Template Method Design Pattern:** Abstract class defines the skeleton of an algorithm; subclasses override specific steps.
- **API Standardization:** SDKs expose abstract classes to enforce a consistent structure across user implementations.
- **GUI Toolkits:** Java's `AbstractAction` and `AbstractTableModel` are real-world abstract classes in the JDK.
- **Hardware Abstraction:** Embedded systems often use abstract classes to model generic device drivers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Building an Abstract Class Hierarchy

Let's derive a complete, working example step-by-step тАФ a **Vehicle hierarchy** that KTU examiners love to ask about.

#### Step 1: Identify the Common Behavior
All vehicles share two properties: they have a **maximum speed** and they **move**. However, "how" they move differs (Car drives, Ship sails, Plane flies).

#### Step 2: Create the Abstract Base Class
We define `Vehicle` as abstract, declaring the contract:

```java
abstract class Vehicle {
    int maxSpeed;
    
    Vehicle(int maxSpeed) {
        this.maxSpeed = maxSpeed;
    }
    
    // Concrete method тАФ common to all vehicles
    void displayMaxSpeed() {
        System.out.println("Max Speed: " + maxSpeed + " km/h");
    }
    
    // Abstract method тАФ implementation left to subclasses
    abstract void move();
}
```

**Logical reasoning behind each component:**
- `maxSpeed` is a **concrete data member** тАФ every vehicle has one.
- The `Vehicle(int maxSpeed)` **constructor** initializes it (showing abstract classes can have constructors).
- `displayMaxSpeed()` is a **concrete method** тАФ its logic is universal; no subclass needs to change it.
- `move()` is **abstract** тАФ the action differs per vehicle, so we leave it unimplemented.

#### Step 3: Create Concrete Subclass 1 тАФ `Car`

```java
class Car extends Vehicle {
    Car(int maxSpeed) {
        super(maxSpeed); // calls the abstract class constructor
    }
    
    @Override
    void move() {
        System.out.println("Car drives on the road.");
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step analysis:**
1. `class Car extends Vehicle` тАФ Car inherits from the abstract class.
2. `super(maxSpeed)` тАФ invokes the parent constructor to set `maxSpeed`.
3. `@Override void move()` тАФ Car **fulfills the contract** by providing its own `move()` implementation.

#### Step 4: Create Concrete Subclass 2 тАФ `Ship`

```java
class Ship extends Vehicle {
    Ship(int maxSpeed) {
        super(maxSpeed);
    }
    
    @Override
    void move() {
        System.out.println("Ship sails on water.");
    }
}
```

#### Step 5: Create Concrete Subclass 3 тАФ `Plane`

```java
class Plane extends Vehicle {
    Plane(int maxSpeed) {
        super(maxSpeed);
    }
    
    @Override
    void move() {
        System.out.println("Plane flies in the sky.");
    }
}
```

#### Step 6: The Main Method тАФ Demonstrating Polymorphism

```java
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Vehicle v = new Vehicle(100);  // ERROR! Cannot instantiate abstract class
        
        Vehicle[] vehicles = new Vehicle[3];
        vehicles[0] = new Car(200);
        vehicles[1] = new Ship(50);
        vehicles[2] = new Plane(900);
        
        for (Vehicle v : vehicles) {
            v.displayMaxSpeed(); // inherited concrete method
            v.move();            // polymorphic abstract method call
            System.out.println("---");
        }
    }
}
```

#### Step 7: Output Verification

$$ \text{Output} = \begin{cases} \text{Max Speed: 200 km/h} \\ \text{Car drives on the road.} \\ \text{---} \\ \text{Max Speed: 50 km/h} \\ \text{Ship sails on water.} \\ \text{---} \\ \text{Max Speed: 900 km/h} \\ \text{Plane flies in the sky.} \\ \text{---} \end{cases} $$

**Key takeaway:** A single `Vehicle` reference (the abstract type) invokes different `move()` implementations at runtime тАФ this is **runtime polymorphism** powered by the abstract class contract.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Abstract vs. Concrete

| Scenario | Use Abstract Class | Use Concrete Class |
|---|---|---|
| You want to enforce a contract for subclasses | тЬЕ | тЭМ |
| You need shared code (concrete methods) among subclasses | тЬЕ | тЬЕ |
| You want to prevent direct instantiation | тЬЕ | тЭМ |
| All methods will be uniquely implemented in every subclass | тЭМ (use interface) | тЭМ |
| You want to provide a base implementation | тЬЕ | тЬЕ |
| The class is just a data holder (POJO) | тЭМ | тЬЕ |
| You need to declare only method signatures, not state | тЭМ (use interface) | тЭМ |

### 3.3 Common Compilation Errors to Recognize

| Code | Compiler Reaction |
|---|---|
| `new AbstractClass();` | `Error: AbstractClass is abstract; cannot be instantiated` |
| `abstract void show() { }` | `Error: Abstract methods cannot have a body` |
| `class X extends AbstractClass { }` (no override) | `Error: X is not abstract and does not override abstract method...` |
| `final abstract class X { }` | `Error: illegal combination of modifiers: final and abstract` |
| `abstract static void show();` | `Error: illegal combination of modifiers: abstract and static` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Class Hierarchy Diagram

The following diagram illustrates the **Vehicle abstract class hierarchy** with its concrete subclasses:

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Vehicle {
        <<abstract>>
        -int maxSpeed
        +Vehicle(int maxSpeed)
        +displayMaxSpeed() void
        +move()* void
    }
    
    class Car {
        +Car(int maxSpeed)
        +move() void
    }
    
    class Ship {
        +Ship(int maxSpeed)
        +move() void
    }
    
    class Plane {
        +Plane(int maxSpeed)
        +move() void
    }
    
    Vehicle <|-- Car : extends
    Vehicle <|-- Ship : extends
    Vehicle <|-- Plane : extends
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- `<<abstract>>` is the UML stereotype indicating the class is abstract.
- `+` denotes **public** access; `-` denotes **private** access.
- The asterisk `*` after `move()` in the abstract class signals that `move()` is an **abstract method** (italicized in standard UML).
- Arrows point **from subclass to superclass** in UML тАФ hence `Car`, `Ship`, `Plane` all point upward to `Vehicle`.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

This block diagram explains the **execution and method dispatch flow** when working with an abstract class reference:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Code] --> B{Reference Type}
    B -->|Abstract Class| C[Vehicle v]
    B -->|Concrete Subclass| D[Car c]
    
    C --> E{v.move call}
    D --> E
    
    E --> F{Actual Object Type}
    F -->|Car object| G[Car.move executes]
    F -->|Ship object| H[Ship.move executes]
    F -->|Plane object| I[Plane.move executes]
    
    G --> J[Output: Car drives on the road]
    H --> K[Output: Ship sails on water]
    I --> L[Output: Plane flies in the sky]
```

**Flow Interpretation:**
1. The client code holds a reference of type `Vehicle` (abstract).
2. When `.move()` is called, the **JVM performs dynamic method dispatch** at runtime.
3. The actual method executed depends on the **runtime type of the object** (Car, Ship, or Plane), not the reference type.
4. This is the essence of **polymorphism via abstract classes**.

### 4.3 Conceptual Comparison Chart: Abstract Class vs. Interface

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Abstract Class] -->|Supports| B[0 to 100 percent abstraction]
    A -->|Can have| C[Constructors]
    A -->|Can have| D[State: instance variables]
    A -->|Access| E[public, protected, private, default]
    A -->|Inheritance| F[Single extends]
    
    G[Interface] -->|Supports| H[100 percent abstraction]
    G -->|Cannot have| I[Constructors]
    G -->|Can have| J[Only static final constants]
    G -->|Access| K[public only]
    G -->|Inheritance| L[Multiple implements]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A frequently asked question is "Why use an abstract class instead of an interface?" The key answer is: **abstract classes are used when you need to share code (state + behavior) among related classes, while interfaces are used to define a capability contract across unrelated classes.**

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define an abstract class in Java. Can we instantiate an abstract class? Justify your answer.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An abstract class in Java is a class declared with the `abstract` keyword that may contain both **abstract methods** (methods without a body) and **concrete methods** (methods with a body). It represents an **incomplete blueprint** for other classes.

**No, we cannot instantiate an abstract class** in Java. Attempting to write `new AbstractClass()` produces a **compile-time error** because abstract classes are considered *incomplete* тАФ they may have unimplemented methods. The compiler prevents direct object creation to ensure that only fully-defined classes (subclasses that complete the contract) are instantiated. The correct approach is to create a concrete subclass and instantiate it.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*List any four characteristics of abstract methods in Java.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four key characteristics of abstract methods are:

1. **Declared without a body:** Abstract methods end with a semicolon, not curly braces.
   Example: `abstract void calculate();`

2. **Must be inside an abstract class:** A method can only be abstract if its enclosing class is declared abstract.

3. **Must be overridden:** Any concrete subclass must provide an implementation for every inherited abstract method, or the subclass itself must be declared abstract.

4. **Cannot be `static`, `final`, or `private`:** Static methods belong to the class (not overridable), final methods cannot be overridden, and private methods are not visible to subclasses тАФ all three contradict the purpose of abstract methods.

---

### Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Write a Java program to create an abstract class `Account` with the following:
- A data member `balance` of type `double`.
- An abstract method `calculateInterest()`.
- A concrete method `displayBalance()` that prints the current balance.
- A constructor to initialize `balance`.

Create two concrete subclasses `SavingsAccount` and `CurrentAccount` that override `calculateInterest()` with interest rates of **4%** and **2%** respectively. Demonstrate the program with a suitable `main` method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with an example why an abstract class **cannot be declared as `final`**. Also, explain what happens when a concrete subclass fails to implement an abstract method inherited from its superclass. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(a):

**Step 1: Define the Abstract Class**

```java
abstract class Account {
    double balance;
    
    Account(double balance) {
        this.balance = balance;
    }
    
    void displayBalance() {
        System.out.println("Current Balance: Rs. " + balance);
    }
    
    abstract void calculateInterest();
}
```

**Step 2: Define Subclass `SavingsAccount`**

```java
class SavingsAccount extends Account {
    SavingsAccount(double balance) {
        super(balance);
    }
    
    @Override
    void calculateInterest() {
        double interest = balance * 0.04;
        System.out.println("Savings Account Interest (4%): Rs. " + interest);
    }
}
```

**Step 3: Define Subclass `CurrentAccount`**

```java
class CurrentAccount extends Account {
    CurrentAccount(double balance) {
        super(balance);
    }
    
    @Override
    void calculateInterest() {
        double interest = balance * 0.02;
        System.out.println("Current Account Interest (2%): Rs. " + interest);
    }
}
```

**Step 4: Main Method Demonstration**

```java
public class BankDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Account acc1 = new SavingsAccount(100000);
        acc1.displayBalance();
        acc1.calculateInterest();
        
        System.out.println("---");
        
        Account acc2 = new CurrentAccount(100000);
        acc2.displayBalance();
        acc2.calculateInterest();
    }
}
```

**Step 5: Output**

$$ \text{Output} = \begin{cases} \text{Current Balance: Rs. 100000.0} \\ \text{Savings Account Interest (4%): Rs. 4000.0} \\ \text{---} \\ \text{Current Balance: Rs. 100000.0} \\ \text{Current Account Interest (2%): Rs. 2000.0} \end{cases} $$

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Abstract class definition with all members: **2 marks**
- SavingsAccount subclass with correct override: **2 marks**
- CurrentAccount subclass with correct override: **2 marks**
- Main method with output demonstration: **1 mark**

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(b):

**Part 1: Why Abstract and Final Cannot Be Combined (3.5 marks)**

The `final` and `abstract` keywords are **mutually exclusive** because they serve opposite purposes:

- `final` тЖТ **prevents** a class from being subclassed (no inheritance allowed).
- `abstract` тЖТ **requires** a class to be subclassed (it is incomplete and needs a subclass to fulfill its contract).

If we write `final abstract class Account`, the class would be incomplete (no instantiation, abstract methods exist) but also cannot be inherited тАФ meaning no one could ever implement its abstract methods, and no one could ever instantiate it. This creates a **logical contradiction** that makes the class useless. Hence, Java forbids this combination at compile-time.

**Example of the Compile Error:**

```java
final abstract class Account {  // ERROR
    abstract void calculateInterest();
}
```

The compiler throws: `illegal combination of modifiers: final and abstract`.

**Part 2: What Happens When a Subclass Fails to Implement an Abstract Method (3.5 marks)**

When a concrete subclass inherits an abstract method but does **not** provide an implementation, the subclass **becomes abstract by inheritance**. There are two possible outcomes:

**Case 1: The subclass is declared as a regular class**

```java
class SavingsAccount extends Account {  // ERROR
    // forgot to override calculateInterest()
}
```

The compiler throws: `error: SavingsAccount is not abstract and does not override abstract method calculateInterest() in Account`. The class **cannot be compiled** until either the missing method is implemented or the class is marked `abstract`.

**Case 2: The subclass is also declared abstract**

```java
abstract class SavingsAccount extends Account {
    // no implementation provided
}
```

This is **legal**. The class is now abstract and can be inherited further. The obligation to implement `calculateInterest()` is **deferred** to the next level of subclass in the hierarchy.

**Conclusion:** Java's strict type system ensures that **every abstract method is eventually implemented** somewhere down the inheritance chain, guaranteeing that only fully-implemented classes are instantiated.

---

### ALTERNATIVE Choice: Question B (14 Marks)

**Q4. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Design an abstract class `Employee` with data members `name` (String) and `basicSalary` (double), a concrete method `displayDetails()`, and an abstract method `calculateBonus()`. Create two subclasses `Manager` (bonus = 20% of salary) and `Developer` (bonus = 10% of salary). Write a complete Java program and explain the role of polymorphism in this design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between an **abstract class** and an **interface** in Java with at least **five** distinct points. Provide a small code snippet for each to support your answer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q4(a):

**Step 1: Abstract Class `Employee`**

```java
abstract class Employee {
    String name;
    double basicSalary;
    
    Employee(String name, double basicSalary) {
        this.name = name;
        this.basicSalary = basicSalary;
    }
    
    void displayDetails() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name);
        System.out.println("Basic Salary: Rs. " + basicSalary);
    }
    
    abstract double calculateBonus();
}
```

**Step 2: Subclass `Manager`**

```java
class Manager extends Employee {
    Manager(String name, double basicSalary) {
        super(name, basicSalary);
    }
    
    @Override
    double calculateBonus() {
        return basicSalary * 0.20;
    }
}
```

**Step 3: Subclass `Developer`**

```java
class Developer extends Employee {
    Developer(String name, double basicSalary) {
        super(name, basicSalary);
    }
    
    @Override
    double calculateBonus() {
        return basicSalary * 0.10;
    }
}
```

**Step 4: Main Method with Polymorphism**

```java
public class EmployeeSystem {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Employee[] staff = new Employee[2];
        staff[0] = new Manager("Anjali", 80000);
        staff[1] = new Developer("Rahul", 60000);
        
        for (Employee e : staff) {
            e.displayDetails();
            System.out.println("Bonus: Rs. " + e.calculateBonus());
            System.out.println("---");
        }
    }
}
```

**Step 5: Role of Polymorphism Explained (2 marks within the 7):**

The abstract class `Employee` enables **runtime polymorphism** in two ways:

1. **Common Type Reference:** The array `Employee[] staff` can hold *any* subclass object тАФ Manager, Developer, or future additions like `Intern`. The code remains unchanged regardless of the concrete type stored.

2. **Dynamic Method Dispatch:** When `e.calculateBonus()` is called, the **JVM determines at runtime** whether `e` points to a `Manager` or `Developer`, and invokes the correct overridden method. This eliminates the need for `if-else` or `instanceof` checks, leading to **cleaner, extensible code**.

**If we had used concrete classes without abstraction**, we would have needed separate variables and methods for each employee type, violating the **Open/Closed Principle** of software design.

---

#### Model Solution for Q4(b):

**Five Key Differences Between Abstract Class and Interface:**

| S.No. | Abstract Class | Interface |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Declared using `abstract class` keyword | Declared using `interface` keyword |
| 2 | Can have both abstract and concrete methods | All methods are implicitly `abstract` (pre-Java 8) |
| 3 | Can have instance variables (state) | Only `public static final` constants allowed |
| 4 | Can have constructors | Cannot have constructors |
| 5 | A class can extend **only one** abstract class | A class can implement **multiple** interfaces |
| 6 | Can have `private`, `protected` members | All members are implicitly `public` |
| 7 | Used when classes share **code and behavior** | Used to define a **capability contract** |

**Code Snippet тАФ Abstract Class:**

```java
abstract class Animal {
    String species;
    
    Animal(String species) {
        this.species = species;  // constructor allowed
    }
    
    abstract void sound();       // abstract method
    
    void sleep() {               // concrete method
        System.out.println("Sleeping...");
    }
}
```

**Code Snippet тАФ Interface:**

```java
interface Drawable {
    int PEN_WIDTH = 2;  // implicitly public static final
    
    void draw();       // implicitly public abstract
    
    // Drawable() { }  // ERROR: interfaces cannot have constructors
}
```

**Conclusion:** Abstract classes represent **"is-a" inheritance with shared state**, while interfaces represent **"can-do" capability contracts** without state. The choice depends on whether you need to share code (abstract class) or define a uniform capability (interface).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> **Where students commonly lose marks on Abstract Class questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting `@Override` annotation** тАФ Examiners deduct marks for not explicitly showing method overriding. Always use `@Override` before overridden methods.
> 
> 2. **Missing `super()` call in subclass constructors** тАФ When the abstract class has a constructor, the subclass constructor must invoke `super(...)` as its first statement, or Java inserts a default no-arg `super()` which fails if the parent has no no-arg constructor.
> 
> 3. **Writing a body for abstract methods** тАФ `abstract void show() { }` is a compilation error. Students often confuse "no body" with "empty body."
> 
> 4. **Instantiating abstract classes in the main method** тАФ Writing `Vehicle v = new Vehicle();` to "test" the code is a fatal error that loses 2тАУ3 marks instantly.
> 
> 5. **Confusing abstract class with interface** тАФ When asked "abstract class vs interface," students often write only 2тАУ3 points instead of the expected 5+. Aim for at least 5 distinct differences with code support.
> 
> 6. **Not explaining polymorphism** тАФ When a question involves an abstract class hierarchy, the model answer **must** mention *dynamic method dispatch* and *runtime polymorphism* explicitly. Merely writing code without explanation loses conceptual marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Abstract class** = a class declared with the `abstract` keyword; it is **incomplete** and **cannot be instantiated**.
- Abstract classes can contain **abstract methods** (no body) and **concrete methods** (with body).
- Abstract classes **can** have constructors, static methods, final methods, and member variables.
- Any class with **at least one abstract method** must itself be declared abstract.
- A concrete subclass **must implement all** inherited abstract methods, or be declared abstract itself.
- `abstract` and `final` are **mutually exclusive** modifiers тАФ never combine them.
- `abstract` and `static` are also **mutually exclusive** for the same method.
- Abstract classes enable **runtime polymorphism** via dynamic method dispatch.
- Use **abstract classes** when you need to share code (state + behavior) among related classes.
- Use **interfaces** when you need to define a pure capability contract across unrelated classes.
- The `abstract` keyword must be applied at **both** the class and method level when needed.
- A subclass constructor **must** call a superclass constructor (explicitly via `super()` or implicitly).
- Abstract classes support **partial abstraction** (0% to 100%), while interfaces traditionally support **100% abstraction** (pre-Java 8).
- In UML diagrams, abstract classes are shown with `<<abstract>>` stereotype and *italicized* method names.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
