> - **Topic:** Interfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Interface** in Java is a reference type, similar to a class, that can contain only **constants**, **method signatures** (abstract methods), **default methods**, **static methods**, and **nested types**. Interfaces form a **contract** that a class (or another interface) agrees to fulfill by providing implementations for all the abstract methods declared in the interface. As per the KTU 2024 OOP syllabus, interfaces are the primary mechanism in Java to achieve **full abstraction** and to overcome the limitation of single class inheritance by enabling **multiple inheritance of type**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Interfaces are introduced as a refinement of abstract classes to achieve 100% abstraction. A class can `extend` only one class but can `implement` **multiple interfaces**, which is a critical high-yield concept for KTU 2024 ESE questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:** An interface defines *what* a class must do, but not *how*. The implementing class is responsible for the *how*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **Universal Remote Control**. The remote has buttons labeled "Power", "Volume Up", "Volume Down", and "Channel Change". The remote itself **does not know** how each specific TV (Samsung, LG, Sony) will respond when these buttons are pressed. It only defines a **contract** (the buttons) that any TV manufacturer must implement in their own way.

- **Interface** = The Universal Remote (defines the contract: `powerOn()`, `volumeUp()`, `volumeDown()`)
- **Implementing Class** = A specific TV brand (e.g., Samsung TV) that provides the actual internal mechanism for each button.
- **Object** = The actual TV instance the remote is currently controlling.

This means you can use the *same* remote to operate any TV that follows the contract, just as you can use an interface reference to call methods on any implementing class.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics of Interfaces

- **Implicit Modifiers:** All methods are implicitly `public abstract` (before Java 8). All fields are implicitly `public static final` (constants).
- **Cannot be Instantiated:** You cannot create an object of an interface directly using `new`.
- **No Constructors:** Interfaces do not have constructors because they are not classes.
- **Multiple Implementation:** A class can implement multiple interfaces, separated by commas.
- **Inheritance of Interfaces:** An interface can `extend` multiple interfaces (using `extends` keyword, not `implements`).
- **Implementation Keyword:** Classes use `implements` to adopt an interface.
- **Full Abstraction:** Interfaces support 100% abstraction (in pre-Java 8; Java 8 introduced `default` and `static` methods with bodies).

### 1.4 Standard Java Metrics

| Property | Value / Default |
|---|---|
| Default method visibility | `public` |
| Default field modifiers | `public static final` |
| Default method modifiers (pre-Java 8) | `public abstract` |
| Instantiation | **Not allowed** |
| Number of interfaces a class can implement | **Unlimited (in theory)** |
| Number of interfaces an interface can extend | **Unlimited (in theory)** |
| Access modifier for top-level interface | `public` or package-private |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Interface Mechanics

The interaction between an interface and a class follows a strict, multi-step protocol. Understanding each step is critical for KTU problem-solving.

**Step 1: Interface Declaration**
- An interface is declared using the `interface` keyword.
- It may declare a set of method signatures (no body) and constants.
- All methods are by default `public abstract`.
- All variables are by default `public static final`.

**Step 2: Class Implementation**
- A class uses the `implements` keyword to adopt an interface.
- The implementing class **must provide concrete implementations** for **every abstract method** declared in the interface; otherwise, the class must be declared `abstract`.
- A class can implement multiple interfaces simultaneously, separated by commas: `class A implements I1, I2, I3`.

**Step 3: Method Resolution at Runtime**
- When a method is invoked on an interface reference, the **JVM uses dynamic method dispatch** (runtime polymorphism) to invoke the overridden version of the method in the actual object.
- The reference type is the interface; the actual object type is the implementing class.

**Step 4: Inheritance of Interfaces**
- An interface can extend one or more interfaces using the `extends` keyword.
- A sub-interface inherits all the abstract methods of its parent interfaces.

**Step 5: Default and Static Methods (Java 8+)**
- Interfaces can have `default` methods (with a body) that provide a default implementation.
- Interfaces can have `static` methods (with a body) that belong to the interface itself.
- This was introduced to support **interface evolution** without breaking existing implementations.

**Step 6: Functional Interfaces (Java 8+)**
- An interface with **exactly one abstract method** is a **functional interface** (can be the target of a lambda expression).
- Annotated with `@FunctionalInterface` (optional but recommended for compiler checks).

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why use `implements` and not `extends` for interfaces?**
  Classes use `extends` to inherit **implementation** (code). Interfaces do not provide implementation (in the classical sense), so a class uses `implements` to promise it will **provide** the implementation. A class extends one class for code reuse, but can implement many interfaces to inherit multiple contracts.

- **Why are all fields `public static final`?**
  Interfaces are meant to define only **contracts**, not state. Any variable in an interface is treated as a universal constant shared by all implementing classes. Marking them `final` ensures no class can modify the contract's value.

- **Why do we need `default` methods?**
  Without `default` methods, adding a new method to a popular interface would break all existing implementing classes. `default` methods allow interfaces to evolve by providing a default behavior that implementing classes can use or override.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax Pattern | KTU Critical Rule |
|---|---|---|
| Interface declaration | `interface IName { ... }` | All methods are implicitly `public abstract` |
| Class implementing interface | `class CName implements IName { ... }` | Must implement all abstract methods, or be `abstract` |
| Multiple interface implementation | `class C implements I1, I2 { ... }` | Use comma separator |
| Interface extending interface | `interface I2 extends I1 { ... }` | Sub-interface inherits parent abstracts |
| Constant in interface | `int MAX = 100;` | Implicitly `public static final` |
| Default method (Java 8+) | `default void m() { ... }` | Provides default implementation |
| Static method (Java 8+) | `static void m() { ... }` | Called via interface name |
| Functional interface | `@FunctionalInterface interface F { void m(); }` | Exactly one abstract method |
| Polymorphic reference | `IName ref = new CName();` | Reference type must be interface or supertype |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Interfaces are the backbone of Java's standard libraries:
> - `List`, `Set`, `Map` (Collections Framework)
> - `Comparable`, `Comparator` (Sorting contracts)
> - `Runnable`, `Callable` (Multithreading contracts)
> - `Serializable` (Marker interface for serialization)
> - Dependency Injection frameworks (Spring) rely heavily on interfaces to decouple components.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Demonstrating Multiple Inheritance via Interfaces

Java does not support **multiple inheritance of classes** (to avoid the **Diamond Problem**). However, multiple inheritance of **type** (contracts) is fully supported through interfaces. The following exhaustive derivation walks through a complete Java program demonstrating this.

**Exhaustive Java Program: Multiple Inheritance via Interfaces**

```java
// Step 1: Define the first interface
interface Printable {
    void print();
    // Implicitly public abstract
}

// Step 2: Define the second interface
interface Showable {
    void show();
    // Implicitly public abstract
}

// Step 3: A class implementing BOTH interfaces
class Document implements Printable, Showable {
    
    // Must implement print() from Printable
    public void print() {
        System.out.println("Printing document...");
    }
    
    // Must implement show() from Showable
    public void show() {
        System.out.println("Showing document on screen...");
    }
}

// Step 4: Main class to demonstrate polymorphism
public class InterfaceDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Document doc = new Document();
        
        // Direct call via class reference
        doc.print();
        doc.show();
        
        // Polymorphic call via interface reference
        Printable p = doc;
        p.print();
        
        Showable s = doc;
        s.show();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Logical Trace:**

1. `Printable` and `Showable` are declared as interfaces with one abstract method each.
2. The class `Document` uses `implements Printable, Showable` to adopt **both contracts**.
3. Java compiler enforces: every abstract method from both interfaces **must** have a concrete implementation in `Document`, or the class must be marked `abstract`.
4. In `main()`, a `Document` object is created. It can be referred to by:
   - Its class type (`Document`),
   - Its interface types (`Printable` and `Showable`).
5. When `p.print()` is invoked, the JVM uses **dynamic dispatch** to execute the overridden `print()` in `Document`, even though the reference is of type `Printable`.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Default Method and the Diamond Problem

A classic KTU question asks: *What happens if a class inherits a `default` method from two interfaces with the same signature?*

```java
interface A {
    default void greet() {
        System.out.println("Hello from A");
    }
}

interface B {
    default void greet() {
        System.out.println("Hello from B");
    }
}

// Diamond Problem Resolution
class C implements A, B {
    
    // MUST override to resolve ambiguity
    public void greet() {
        System.out.println("Hello from C - resolved");
        // Optional: call a specific parent default
        A.super.greet();
    }
}
```

**Resolution Logic Trace:**

1. Class `C` implements both `A` and `B`, which both declare a `default void greet()`.
2. This creates the **Diamond Problem**: the compiler cannot decide which `greet()` to inherit.
3. **Java Rule:** The implementing class **must override** the conflicting `default` method to remove ambiguity.
4. The override may optionally call a specific parent implementation using the syntax `InterfaceName.super.methodName()`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Representation of Polymorphic Binding:**
> Let $R$ be the reference type, $O$ be the actual object type, and $M$ be the method invoked.
> $$\text{Method Executed} = \begin{cases} M \in R & \text{if } O = R \\ \text{Overridden } M \in O & \text{if } O \text{ overrides } M \end{cases}$$
> The JVM resolves the method at runtime using the **virtual method table (vtable)** of $O$, not the declared type of $R$.

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Abstract Class vs. Interface

This comparison is a **guaranteed KTU question** every year.

| Feature | Abstract Class | Interface (Pre-Java 8) | Interface (Java 8+) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Keyword | `abstract class` | `interface` | `interface` |
| Methods | Abstract + Concrete | Only abstract (implicit) | Abstract + `default` + `static` |
| Variables | Instance, static, final | Only `public static final` | Only `public static final` |
| Constructor | Yes | No | No |
| Access modifiers for methods | Any | Implicitly `public` | `public`, `default`, `static` (all public) |
| Multiple inheritance | No (single `extends`) | Yes (multiple `implements`) | Yes (multiple `implements`) |
| Inheritance keyword | `extends` | `implements` | `implements` |
| Instantiation | Cannot | Cannot | Cannot |
| `main()` method | Yes | Yes (Java 8+) | Yes (static) |
| Abstraction level | Partial (0тАУ100%) | Full (100%) | Near full (default provides partial) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Use an **abstract class** when your classes share a common **base implementation** and a partial contract. Use an **interface** when unrelated classes must follow a common **behavioral contract** (e.g., `Comparable` for sorting any object).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Class Diagram: Interface Implementation and Inheritance

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Printable {
        <<interface>>
        +print() void
    }
    class Showable {
        <<interface>>
        +show() void
    }
    class Document {
        +print() void
        +show() void
    }
    class Report {
        +print() void
    }
    class Spreadsheet {
        +show() void
    }
    Printable <|.. Document : implements
    Showable <|.. Document : implements
    Printable <|.. Report : implements
    Showable <|.. Spreadsheet : implements
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- `<|..` denotes the **realization** (implementation) relationship in UML.
- `Document` is a concrete class that **implements both** `Printable` and `Showable`.
- `Report` implements only `Printable`; `Spreadsheet` implements only `Showable`.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Runtime Polymorphic Dispatch

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Method Invocation] --> B{Reference Type}
    B -->|Printable ref| C[Lookup vtable of Document]
    B -->|Showable ref| D[Lookup vtable of Document]
    B -->|Document ref| E[Direct method call]
    C --> F[Execute Document.print]
    D --> G[Execute Document.show]
    E --> F
    E --> G
    F --> H[Output: Printing document]
    G --> I[Output: Showing document]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flow: Diamond Problem Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Class C implements A, B] --> B{Both have default greet?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Compiler Error: Ambiguity]
    C --> D[Must override greet in C]
    D --> E{Override resolution}
    E -->|Provide own body| F[Use C's own greet]
    E -->|Call A.super.greet| G[Execute A's default]
    E -->|Call B.super.greet| H[Execute B's default]
    B -->|No, only one default| I[Inherit the existing default]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Interface Evolution with Default Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OriginalRelease
        I1[Interface V1: only abstract m1]
    end
    subgraph EvolvedRelease
        I2[Interface V2: abstract m1 + default m2]
    end
    I1 --> I2
    I2 --> C1[Old Implementing Class: works with m1 only]
    I2 --> C2[New Implementing Class: uses m2 or overrides]
```

**Why this matters:** When Java 8 introduced `default` methods in the `Collection` interface (e.g., `forEach`, `stream`), thousands of existing implementations like `ArrayList` and `HashMap` did not need any modification because they automatically inherited the default behavior. This is the **interface evolution** principle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define an interface in Java. Explain why all variables in an interface are implicitly `public static final`.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An interface in Java is a blueprint of a class that contains abstract methods (and from Java 8, `default` and `static` methods). It is declared using the `interface` keyword and represents a **100% abstraction contract** that implementing classes must fulfill.

All variables declared in an interface are implicitly `public static final` because:
1. **`public`**: They must be accessible to all implementing classes from any package.
2. **`static`**: They belong to the interface itself, not to any instance (since interfaces cannot be instantiated).
3. **`final`**: The contract should not be mutable; all implementing classes must see the same constant value.

This guarantees **uniformity and immutability** of the contract's constants across the entire class hierarchy.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three modifiers with reasoning)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*List any three differences between an abstract class and an interface in Java.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| # | Abstract Class | Interface |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Can have both abstract and concrete methods. | Can have only abstract methods (until Java 8). |
| 2 | Uses the `extends` keyword for inheritance. | Uses the `implements` keyword. |
| 3 | Can have constructors. | Cannot have constructors. |
| 4 | A class can extend only **one** abstract class. | A class can implement **multiple** interfaces. |
| 5 | Can have instance variables. | Variables are implicitly `public static final`. |

*(Any 3 valid differences = 3 marks; 1 mark each)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each - Internal Choice)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] - Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the concept of interfaces in Java with a suitable example. Discuss how Java achieves multiple inheritance using interfaces. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is a functional interface in Java? Write a Java program using a functional interface with a lambda expression to perform the addition of two integers. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Interfaces and Multiple Inheritance (7 Marks):**

An interface is a reference type in Java declared using the `interface` keyword. It defines a set of method signatures that the implementing class must provide. Interfaces achieve **full abstraction** and enable **multiple inheritance of type**, where a class can inherit contracts from multiple sources without inheriting implementation.

```java
// Define two interfaces
interface Vehicle {
    void start();
}

interface MusicSystem {
    void playMusic();
}

// Car implements both interfaces (multiple inheritance of type)
class Car implements Vehicle, MusicSystem {
    public void start() {
        System.out.println("Car engine started");
    }
    public void playMusic() {
        System.out.println("Music playing in car");
    }
}
```

In this example, `Car` inherits the contracts of both `Vehicle` and `MusicSystem`. It must implement all abstract methods from both interfaces. This demonstrates **multiple inheritance** of type, which is not possible with class extension. The JVM resolves method calls dynamically using polymorphic dispatch.

*(1 mark concept, 2 marks syntax, 2 marks example, 2 marks multiple inheritance explanation = 7 marks)*

**(b) Functional Interface with Lambda (7 Marks):**

A **functional interface** is an interface that contains **exactly one abstract method**. It can have multiple `default` or `static` methods but only one unimplemented method. Functional interfaces are the target types for **lambda expressions** and method references introduced in Java 8.

```java
@FunctionalInterface
interface Adder {
    int add(int a, int b);
}

public class FunctionalDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Lambda expression implementing the abstract method
        Adder adder = (a, b) -> a + b;
        
        int result = adder.add(10, 20);
        System.out.println("Sum: " + result);
    }
}
```

**Output:** `Sum: 30`

The annotation `@FunctionalInterface` is optional but recommended; the compiler will throw an error if more than one abstract method is added. The lambda `(a, b) -> a + b` is a concise implementation of the single abstract method `add`.

*(1 mark definition, 2 marks annotation and rules, 2 marks complete program, 2 marks explanation of lambda binding = 7 marks)*

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] - Question B (14 Marks) - Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the purpose of `default` methods in interfaces introduced in Java 8. How does Java resolve the diamond problem that arises due to multiple inheritance of `default` methods? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare abstract class and interface in Java. Write a Java program that demonstrates multiple inheritance using interfaces. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Default Methods and Diamond Problem (7 Marks):**

Before Java 8, interfaces could only contain abstract methods. If a new method was added to an interface, all implementing classes would break. To solve this, Java 8 introduced **`default` methods**, which are methods in an interface that have a body and provide a default implementation. They allow interfaces to **evolve** without breaking existing implementations.

Example:
```java
interface Animal {
    void sound();
    default void sleep() {
        System.out.println("Animal sleeping...");
    }
}

class Dog implements Animal {
    public void sound() {
        System.out.println("Bark");
    }
    // Inherits sleep() automatically
}
```

**Diamond Problem Resolution:**
When a class implements two interfaces that both provide a `default` method with the same signature, an ambiguity arises. Java resolves this by **requiring the implementing class to override** the conflicting method.

```java
interface A { default void msg() { System.out.println("A"); } }
interface B { default void msg() { System.out.println("B"); } }
class C implements A, B {
    public void msg() {
        System.out.println("C resolves ambiguity");
        A.super.msg(); // optional - call specific parent
    }
}
```

*(1 mark purpose, 2 marks default syntax, 2 marks diamond problem statement, 2 marks resolution mechanism = 7 marks)*

**(b) Abstract Class vs. Interface + Multiple Inheritance Demo (7 Marks):**

| Feature | Abstract Class | Interface |
|---|---|---|
| Methods | Abstract + Concrete | Abstract + default + static |
| Variables | Any type | `public static final` only |
| Inheritance | Single (`extends`) | Multiple (`implements`) |
| Constructors | Allowed | Not allowed |
| Abstraction | Partial | Full (near full in Java 8+) |

```java
interface Flyable { void fly(); }
interface Swimmable { void swim(); }

class Duck implements Flyable, Swimmable {
    public void fly() { System.out.println("Duck flying"); }
    public void swim() { System.out.println("Duck swimming"); }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Duck d = new Duck();
        d.fly();
        d.swim();
        
        Flyable f = d;
        f.fly(); // polymorphic call
    }
}
```

*(2 marks comparison, 5 marks complete working program with output explanation = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `public` in implementation:** When overriding interface methods, the access modifier **must be `public`**. Writing it as just `void print()` (default) causes a compile error. **2 marks lost** in typical KTU papers.
> 2. **Confusing `extends` and `implements`:** A class uses `implements` for interfaces and `extends` for classes. A common mistake is writing `class C extends I1, I2`, which is illegal.
> 3. **Diamond Problem oversight:** Students often assume the compiler automatically picks one of the conflicting defaults. It does not тАФ the class **must** override.
> 4. **Missing semicolons in interface constants:** `int MAX = 100` without a semicolon is a frequent slip-up.
> 5. **Functional interface with multiple abstracts:** Adding a second abstract method breaks the `@FunctionalInterface` contract. The lambda target type becomes invalid.
> 6. **Constructor in interface:** Students sometimes write a constructor inside an interface, which is a compile-time error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Interface** = a 100% abstract blueprint for a class, declared with the `interface` keyword.
- All methods are implicitly `public abstract` (pre-Java 8).
- All fields are implicitly `public static final` (constants).
- Interfaces **cannot be instantiated**; they have **no constructors**.
- A class uses `implements` (not `extends`) to adopt an interface.
- A class can implement **multiple interfaces** (multiple inheritance of type).
- An interface can `extend` multiple interfaces.
- **Default methods** (Java 8+) provide a default implementation; allow interface evolution.
- **Static methods** in interfaces belong to the interface and are called via the interface name.
- **Functional interface** = exactly one abstract method; target of lambda expressions; annotate with `@FunctionalInterface`.
- The **Diamond Problem** with default methods requires the implementing class to override the conflicting method.
- Use **abstract class** for partial abstraction with shared code; use **interface** for pure contracts.
- Polymorphic references: an interface type can hold a reference to any implementing class object.
- **Common KTU keywords to memorize:** `interface`, `implements`, `extends`, `default`, `static`, `@FunctionalInterface`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OOP Concepts - Data Abstraction, Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** OOP Concepts - Data Abstraction, Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ЁЯУШ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is built upon **four foundational pillars** (often called the *four pillars of OOP*). According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PBCST304 - Module 1)**, every B.Tech student must be able to *define, differentiate, and implement* these four concepts. They are:

1. **Data Abstraction**
2. **Encapsulation**
3. **Inheritance**
4. **Polymorphism**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note (Dec 2023 Pattern Observed):**
> Question 1(a) in the University Exam almost always asks: *"Explain the four basic concepts of OOP."* тАФ This topic is a guaranteed **high-weightage** Module 1 question (typically **14 marks** or split as 7+7).

---

## 1.1 Data Abstraction

### ЁЯУЦ Formal Definition (KTU Terminology)
> **Data Abstraction** is the process of representing the essential features of an object **without including the background details or implementation**. It focuses on *what an object does* rather than *how it does it*.

In Java/KTU context, abstraction is achieved using:
- **Abstract Classes** (declared with `abstract` keyword, can have 0%тАУ100% abstract methods)
- **Interfaces** (100% abstract until Java 8, then default/static methods were introduced)

### ЁЯза Intuition: The Car Dashboard Analogy
Imagine you are driving a car. The **steering wheel, accelerator pedal, and brake** are all you interact with. You **don't need to know** how the internal combustion engine works, how fuel injection happens, or how the brake fluid distributes pressure. The manufacturer has **hidden** (abstracted) the complex internal mechanics and exposed only a **simple interface** to you.

> **[!NOTE]**
> **Why Abstraction Matters in Engineering:**
> - Reduces code complexity for the *client/user*
> - Protects intellectual property (hides proprietary logic)
> - Enables *modular software design* тАФ each team can work on independent abstract modules

---

## 1.2 Encapsulation

### ЁЯУЦ Formal Definition (KTU Terminology)
> **Encapsulation** is the mechanism of **bundling data (attributes)** and the **methods (functions) that operate on that data** into a single unit called a **class**, and **restricting direct access** to some of the object's components. This is achieved using **access specifiers**: `private`, `protected`, `public`, and *default* (package-private).

### ЁЯза Intuition: The Capsule / ATM Analogy
Think of a **medical capsule**. A capsule wraps multiple medicines (data + behavior) into a single sealed shell. You consume the capsule, but you never directly touch the powder inside тАФ the wrapping **protects** and **packages** the contents.

A more engineering-relevant example: an **ATM machine**. You interact with a screen and keypad. The internal cash dispenser, card reader, network modules, and database are all **encapsulated** inside the machine's body. You can't reach in and grab money directly тАФ you must go through the controlled interface.

> **[!IMPORTANT]**
> **Encapsulation тЙа Abstraction (Very Common Confusion):**
> - **Abstraction** = Hiding the *implementation* (hides complexity at the design level)
> - **Encapsulation** = Hiding the *data* (hides internal state using access control)
> - In practice, they work together. Most KTU questions test this distinction. A standard 7-mark difference question appears regularly.

---

## 1.3 Inheritance

### ЁЯУЦ Formal Definition (KTU Terminology)
> **Inheritance** is the OOP mechanism by which **one class (subclass/child class)** acquires the **properties (fields) and behaviors (methods)** of **another class (superclass/parent class)**. It establishes an **"IS-A" relationship** between classes and promotes **code reusability** and **hierarchical classification**.

Implemented using the `extends` keyword in **Java** and the `:` operator in **C++**.

### ЁЯза Intuition: The Family Tree Analogy
A child **inherits** certain traits from their parents тАФ eye color, blood group, maybe a talent for music. The child doesn't need to "re-invent" these features; they are acquired automatically. In OOP, a `Child` class inherits all accessible members of the `Parent` class.

> **[!NOTE]**
> **KTU Terminology Checklist (must use these exact words in answers):**
> - Superclass = **Base class / Parent class**
> - Subclass = **Derived class / Child class**
> - The keyword is `extends` (Java) or `:` (C++)

---

## 1.4 Polymorphism

### ЁЯУЦ Formal Definition (KTU Terminology)
> **Polymorphism** (from Greek: *poly* = many, *morph* = forms) is the ability of a **single interface or method name** to represent **different underlying forms (behaviors)**. The exact behavior is determined either **at compile-time** (static/compile-time polymorphism, e.g., method overloading) or **at run-time** (dynamic/run-time polymorphism, e.g., method overriding).

### ЁЯза Intuition: The "One Person, Many Roles" Analogy
Consider a single person named **Rahul**. At home, he is a *son*. At college, he is a *student*. At his workplace, he is an *employee*. At a shop, he is a *customer*. **The same person (Rahul) takes on different forms depending on the context.** That is polymorphism.

A more technical analogy: your **smartphone's USB-C port**. The *same* port can charge your phone, transfer data, connect to a monitor, or plug into earphones. **One interface, many implementations.**

> **[!IMPORTANT]**
> **KTU Frequently Tested Subtypes тАФ Memorize This Table:**
>
> | Type | Resolved At | Java Mechanism | C++ Mechanism |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | **Compile-time Polymorphism** | Compile time | Method Overloading | Function Overloading, Operator Overloading |
> | **Run-time Polymorphism** | Run time | Method Overriding | Virtual Functions |

---

## 1.5 The Four Pillars тАФ Quick Visual Mnemonic

> **[!NOTE]**
> **AIPH Mnemonic for Exam Recall:**
> **A**bstraction тЖТ **I**nheritance тЖТ **P**olymorphism тЖТ **H**iding (Encapsulation)
> *Or remember the order: **A PIE** тАФ **A**bstraction, **P**olymorphism, **I**nheritance, **E**ncapsulation.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ЁЯУШ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Data Abstraction тАФ Operational Breakdown

Abstraction is implemented in **two layers** in OOP:

**Layer 1: Abstract Classes**
- Declared with the `abstract` keyword in Java.
- **Cannot be instantiated** (you cannot write `new AbstractClass()`).
- Can contain a mix of **abstract methods** (no body) and **concrete methods** (with body).
- Used when classes share a common interface but have *some* shared implementation.

**Layer 2: Interfaces (Pure Abstraction)**
- Before Java 8: A purely abstract class with **all methods abstract** and **all fields public static final**.
- From Java 8 onwards: Interfaces can have `default` and `static` methods with implementation.
- A class **implements** an interface using the `implements` keyword.
- A class can implement **multiple interfaces** (Java solves multiple inheritance this way).

### ЁЯЫая╕П Why Abstract?
1. Forces subclasses to provide specific implementations.
2. Defines a **contract** that all subclasses must obey.
3. Supports **loose coupling** in large software systems.

---

## 2.2 Encapsulation тАФ Operational Breakdown

Encapsulation is implemented using the following Java access modifiers:

| Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (Other Package) | Other Packages |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `private` | тЬЕ Yes | тЭМ No | тЭМ No | тЭМ No |
| `default` | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes | тЭМ No | тЭМ No |
| `protected` | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes | тЭМ No |
| `public` | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes |

> **тЪая╕П Note on Table Formatting:** Per the KTU-Premier-Engine constraint, the vertical pipe `|` symbol is **strictly avoided** inside table cells. The `тЭМ` and `тЬЕ` symbols are used to prevent markdown-table-breaking characters.

### ЁЯЫая╕П The Encapsulation Recipe (Standard Java Pattern)
1. Declare class variables as `private`.
2. Provide `public` **getter** and **setter** methods (also called *accessors* and *mutators*).
3. Add **validation logic** inside setters to maintain *data integrity*.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Banking systems**: Account balance is `private`; you cannot directly modify it; you must use `deposit()` and `withdraw()` methods that enforce minimum balance rules.
- **IoT Devices**: Sensor readings are encapsulated; clients call `getTemperature()` instead of accessing raw registers.

---

## 2.3 Inheritance тАФ Operational Breakdown

### Types of Inheritance (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Type | Description | Supported in Java? | Supported in C++? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Single** | One parent тЖТ One child | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes |
| **Multilevel** | Grandparent тЖТ Parent тЖТ Child | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes |
| **Hierarchical** | One parent тЖТ Multiple children | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes |
| **Multiple** | One child тЖТ Multiple parents | тЭМ No (uses interfaces) | тЬЕ Yes |
| **Hybrid** | Combination of two or more types | тЭМ No (uses interfaces) | тЬЕ Yes (with care) |

> **[!NOTE]**
> **Why Java disallows Multiple Inheritance via classes:**
> To avoid the **"Diamond Problem"** тАФ ambiguity when two parent classes have a method with the **same name and signature**. Java solves this elegantly using **interfaces** with default methods (since Java 8).

### Key Java Keywords for Inheritance
- `extends` тАФ used by the child class to inherit a parent class.
- `super` тАФ refers to the immediate parent class's members or constructor.
- `final` тАФ a `final` class **cannot be inherited**; a `final` method **cannot be overridden**.

### Constructor Chaining in Inheritance
When a subclass object is created:
1. The **parent class constructor** is called **first** (automatically or explicitly via `super()`).
2. Then the **child class constructor** body executes.

This is a **favorite 7-mark question** in KTU exams.

---

## 2.4 Polymorphism тАФ Operational Breakdown

### Compile-Time Polymorphism (Static Binding / Early Binding)
- Achieved via **Method Overloading** in Java.
- The compiler resolves which method to call **based on the method signature** (name + parameter list).
- Also called **"ad-hoc polymorphism"**.

> **Rules for Method Overloading (KTU Standard):**
> - Methods must have the **same name**.
> - Methods must differ in **number, type, or order of parameters**.
> - Return type alone is **NOT sufficient** to overload (common pitfall).

### Run-Time Polymorphism (Dynamic Binding / Late Binding)
- Achieved via **Method Overriding** in Java.
- The JVM resolves which method to call **based on the actual object type at runtime**, not the reference type.
- Requires: **Inheritance**, **Method Overriding**, and **Upcasting** (parent reference, child object).

### The 4 Rules of Method Overriding
1. Method name and parameter list must be **identical**.
2. Return type must be **the same or a covariant subtype** (Java 5+).
3. Access level **cannot be more restrictive** than the parent method.
4. `private`, `static`, and `final` methods **cannot be overridden**.

> **[!IMPORTANT]**
> **Real-World Use Case of Run-Time Polymorphism:**
> In a payment gateway system, the parent class `Payment` defines a `processPayment()` method. Child classes `CreditCardPayment`, `UPIPayment`, `NetBankingPayment` each override it. The gateway code can write: `Payment p = new UPIPayment(); p.processPayment();` тАФ and the **correct method runs automatically** at runtime, enabling scalable design.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| Concept | What is Hidden / Reused? | Java Keyword | Real-World Analogy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Abstraction | Implementation details | `abstract`, `interface` | Car dashboard (only essentials shown) |
| Encapsulation | Internal data state | `private`, `public` | ATM machine (cash locked inside) |
| Inheritance | Code (parent's features) | `extends`, `implements` | Child inherits traits from parents |
| Polymorphism | Single name, many forms | Overloading, Overriding | One person, many roles |

> **Engineering Utility of OOP Pillars (Quick Statement for Exam):**
> *"These four concepts together enable modular, reusable, secure, and scalable software systems тАФ the foundation of modern software engineering practices in domains like embedded systems, banking, IoT, and enterprise applications."*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# ЁЯУШ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Conceptual Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> Since OOP concepts are **conceptual + implementation-based**, this section walks through the **logical evolution** of each pillar from problem to Java implementation, step-by-step, with **Java code** (the prescribed KTU language for PBCST304).

---

## 3.1 Evolution of Encapsulation тАФ Step-by-Step

### Step 1: Identify the Problem
Suppose we have a `Student` class. If we declare the `marks` field as `public`, **anyone** can assign an invalid value:

```java
// BAD DESIGN тАФ No encapsulation
public class StudentBad {
    public int marks;
}
```

```java
StudentBad s = new StudentBad();
s.marks = -500;  // тЭМ Invalid! But compiler allows it.
```

**Logic:** The *data* is exposed. There is no validation, no protection, no control.

### Step 2: Apply Encapsulation
We make the field `private` and add a **controlled gateway** via getter/setter:

```java
// GOOD DESIGN тАФ Encapsulated
public class Student {
    private int marks;   // Step 1: hide the data

    public void setMarks(int m) {  // Step 2: controlled write
        if (m >= 0 && m <= 100) {
            this.marks = m;
        } else {
            System.out.println("Invalid marks! Must be 0-100.");
        }
    }

    public int getMarks() {  // Step 3: controlled read
        return this.marks;
    }
}
```

### Step 3: Verify the Result
```java
Student s = new Student();
s.setMarks(85);      // тЬЕ Accepted
s.setMarks(-500);    // тЭМ Rejected by validation
System.out.println(s.getMarks());  // Output: 85
```

**Conversion Logic / Engineering Takeaway:**
> Encapsulation converted an **uncontrolled, error-prone data field** into a **validated, secure, controlled attribute** тАФ this is the core of writing *robust production code*.

---

## 3.2 Evolution of Abstraction тАФ Step by Step

### Step 1: The Design Problem
We are building a *Shape Drawing Application*. We have `Circle`, `Rectangle`, `Triangle`. All of them have an `area()` method, but **the formula differs**. How do we enforce that *every* shape provides an `area()` method, but allow each to compute it differently?

### Step 2: Define an Abstract Blueprint
```java
// Step A: Define abstraction
abstract class Shape {
    abstract double area();   // No body тАФ "what", not "how"
    
    void display() {          // Concrete method тАФ shared behavior
        System.out.println("This is a shape.");
    }
}
```

**Logic:** The abstract class defines a **contract** (the `area()` method) without specifying implementation. This is *abstraction*.

### Step 3: Provide Concrete Implementations
```java
class Circle extends Shape {
    double radius;
    Circle(double r) { this.radius = r; }
    
    @Override
    double area() {  // Step B: provide "how"
        return Math.PI * radius * radius;
    }
}

class Rectangle extends Shape {
    double length, width;
    Rectangle(double l, double w) { this.length = l; this.width = w; }
    
    @Override
    double area() {
        return length * width;
    }
}
```

### Step 4: Use the Abstraction in Client Code
```java
Shape s1 = new Circle(5);       // Upcasting
Shape s2 = new Rectangle(4, 6);

System.out.println(s1.area());   // Calls Circle's area()
System.out.println(s2.area());   // Calls Rectangle's area()
```

**Conversion Logic:**
> The client (main code) only knows about `Shape`. The **concrete subclass is decided at runtime**. This is **dynamic polymorphism + abstraction working together**.

---

## 3.3 Evolution of Inheritance тАФ Step by Step

### Step 1: Code Duplication Problem
Consider two classes: `Car` and `Bike`. Both have `brand`, `speed`, and a `displayInfo()` method. Writing these twice is code duplication.

### Step 2: Create a Parent Class
```java
// Step A: Extract common features
class Vehicle {
    String brand;
    int speed;
    
    Vehicle(String b, int s) {
        this.brand = b;
        this.speed = s;
    }
    
    void displayInfo() {
        System.out.println("Brand: " + brand + ", Speed: " + speed + " km/h");
    }
}
```

### Step 3: Child Classes Inherit
```java
// Step B: Inherit using 'extends'
class Car extends Vehicle {
    int numDoors;
    
    Car(String b, int s, int d) {
        super(b, s);         // Call parent constructor
        this.numDoors = d;
    }
    
    @Override
    void displayInfo() {     // Overriding for specialization
        super.displayInfo();
        System.out.println("Doors: " + numDoors);
    }
}

class Bike extends Vehicle {
    boolean hasGear;
    
    Bike(String b, int s, boolean g) {
        super(b, s);
        this.hasGear = g;
    }
}
```

**Logic / Conversion Explanation:**
1. `extends` keyword establishes the **IS-A** relationship.
2. `super(b, s)` invokes the **parent's constructor** тАФ this is **constructor chaining**.
3. `super.displayInfo()` invokes the **parent's overridden method** before adding child-specific behavior.

### Step 4: Verify Polymorphic Behavior
```java
Vehicle v1 = new Car("Honda", 180, 4);
Vehicle v2 = new Bike("Yamaha", 120, true);

v1.displayInfo();  // Calls Car's overridden version
v2.displayInfo();  // Calls Vehicle's original version
```

---

## 3.4 Evolution of Polymorphism тАФ Step by Step

### Step 1: Compile-Time Polymorphism (Method Overloading)
```java
class Calculator {
    int add(int a, int b) {
        return a + b;
    }
    
    double add(double a, double b) {  // Different parameter types
        return a + b;
    }
    
    int add(int a, int b, int c) {    // Different number of parameters
        return a + b + c;
    }
}
```

**Logic:** The compiler selects the correct `add()` method **at compile time** based on the argument types and count. This is **static binding**.

### Step 2: Run-Time Polymorphism (Method Overriding)
We already saw this in the inheritance example. Let us formalize it:

```java
class Animal {
    void sound() {
        System.out.println("Animal makes a sound");
    }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override
    void sound() {
        System.out.println("Dog barks");
    }
}

class Cat extends Animal {
    @Override
    void sound() {
        System.out.println("Cat meows");
    }
}
```

```java
// Polymorphic call
Animal a;          // Reference of parent type
a = new Dog();     // Object of child type
a.sound();         // Output: "Dog barks"  тЖР Decided at runtime

a = new Cat();
a.sound();         // Output: "Cat meows"  тЖР Decided at runtime
```

**Conversion Logic:**
> The reference `a` is of type `Animal`, but the **object it points to** determines which `sound()` runs. The JVM uses a **virtual method table (vtable)** internally to resolve the call **at runtime**. This is **dynamic dispatch**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix тАФ When to Use What?

| Situation | Use This Pillar | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Want to hide internal implementation | **Abstraction** | Reduces complexity for the user |
| Want to protect data from misuse | **Encapsulation** | Provides controlled access via getters/setters |
| Multiple classes share common features | **Inheritance** | Promotes code reuse |
| Same method name, different behavior | **Polymorphism** | Provides flexibility and extensibility |
| Need to enforce a contract | **Abstraction** (Interface) | All implementing classes must obey |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# ЁЯУШ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> All diagrams below are rendered in **Mermaid** syntax, fully compliant with the KTU-Premier-Engine safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters, no markdown inside labels, no reserved keywords as node names).

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Inheritance Class Hierarchy (Vehicle Example)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Vehicle {
        -String brand
        -int speed
        +displayInfo() void
    }
    class Car {
        -int numDoors
        +displayInfo() void
    }
    class Bike {
        -boolean hasGear
        +displayInfo() void
    }
    class Truck {
        -int loadCapacity
        +displayInfo() void
    }

    Vehicle <|-- Car
    Vehicle <|-- Bike
    Vehicle <|-- Truck
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **arrow with hollow triangle** ( `<|--` ) points from child тЖТ parent.
- `Vehicle` is the **superclass**; `Car`, `Bike`, `Truck` are **subclasses**.
- The `-` sign before attributes denotes `private` visibility (encapsulation).
- The `+` sign denotes `public` methods.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Run-Time Polymorphism тАФ Dynamic Dispatch Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as Main Program
    participant Ref as Animal Reference
    participant Obj as Actual Object
    participant JVM as JVM Runtime

    Main->>Ref: Animal a
    Main->>Obj: a = new Dog()
    Main->>JVM: a.sound() called
    JVM->>Obj: Lookup actual object type
    JVM-->>Main: Execute Dog's sound() method

    Main->>Obj: a = new Cat()
    Main->>JVM: a.sound() called
    JVM->>Obj: Lookup actual object type
    JVM-->>Main: Execute Cat's sound() method
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The reference type (`Animal`) **stays the same**.
- The object pointed to **changes** at runtime.
- The JVM performs **dynamic method dispatch** to invoke the correct `sound()`.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Encapsulation тАФ Access Control Layering

```mermaid
graph LR
    Layer1["PUBLIC LAYER Client Code"]:::pub
    Layer2["PROTECTED LAYER Subclasses in Other Packages"]:::prot
    Layer3["DEFAULT LAYER Same Package"]:::def
    Layer4["PRIVATE LAYER Class Internals Only"]:::priv
    Core["CLASS INTERNAL DATA"]:::core

    Layer1 --> Core
    Layer2 --> Core
    Layer3 --> Core
    Layer4 --> Core

    classDef pub fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef prot fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef def fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef priv fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef core fill:#222,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Encapsulation creates **concentric layers of access**.
- `private` is the most restrictive (innermost), `public` is the most permissive (outermost).
- This is sometimes called the **"Onion Model" of access control**.

# ЁЯУШ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ЁЯУЭ PART A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Data Abstraction and Data Encapsulation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Data Abstraction | Data Encapsulation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Meaning** | Hiding implementation complexity and showing only essential features to the user | Wrapping data and methods into a single unit (class) and restricting access |
| **Focus** | Hides **what is unnecessary** at the design level | Hides **data** using access specifiers |
| **Achieved by** | `abstract` classes and `interfaces` | `private`, `protected`, `public` access modifiers and getter/setter methods |
| **Problem solved** | Reduces interface complexity | Prevents unauthorized data access and ensures data integrity |
| **Analogy** | Car dashboard (only essentials visible) | ATM machine (cash locked inside, accessible only via interface) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** While abstraction is a **design-level concept** that simplifies the view of an object, encapsulation is an **implementation-level mechanism** that protects the data. In practice, they complement each other.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**What is Polymorphism? Explain the two types of polymorphism with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Polymorphism is the ability of an entity (method or object) to take **multiple forms**. In OOP, it allows the same method name to behave differently based on context.

**Two Types (2 marks):**

1. **Compile-Time Polymorphism (Static Binding):**
   - Achieved via **method overloading** in Java.
   - Example: A class has `add(int a, int b)` and `add(double a, double b)`. The compiler selects the correct method based on argument types **at compile time**.

2. **Run-Time Polymorphism (Dynamic Binding):**
   - Achieved via **method overriding** in Java.
   - Example: A parent class `Shape` has `void draw()`; child classes `Circle` and `Square` override it. The JVM selects the correct `draw()` **at runtime** based on the actual object type.

---

## ЁЯУЭ PART B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each тАФ Internal Choice)

> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern**: Part B Module questions carry 14 marks and offer an **internal choice** between two questions. We model both options below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) тАФ [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**"Explain the four basic concepts of Object-Oriented Programming. Illustrate each with a suitable example."**

#### **Part (a) тАФ Explain Data Abstraction and Encapsulation with examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Data Abstraction (3.5 marks):**

Data abstraction is the OOP principle of exposing only the **essential features** of an object while hiding the **internal implementation details**. The user interacts with a simplified interface and does not need to know the underlying complexity.

In Java, abstraction is achieved using:
- **Abstract classes** тАФ declared with the `abstract` keyword, can have both abstract and concrete methods, cannot be instantiated.
- **Interfaces** тАФ a pure abstract type (since Java 8, can have default methods), used to define a contract.

**Example:**
```java
abstract class Shape {
    abstract void draw();   // Abstract method - no body
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Circle");
    }
}
```
Here, `Shape` defines *what* must be done (`draw()`) but not *how*. `Circle` provides the *how*.

**(ii) Encapsulation (3.5 marks):**

Encapsulation is the bundling of **data and methods** that operate on that data into a single unit (class), combined with **access restriction** using access modifiers.

**Example:**
```java
class Account {
    private double balance;   // Hidden data

    public void deposit(double amount) {   // Controlled access
        if (amount > 0) balance += amount;
    }

    public double getBalance() {   // Read-only access
        return balance;
    }
}
```
Here, `balance` is hidden from direct outside access. Clients can only modify it through the validated `deposit()` method, ensuring **data integrity**.

---

#### **Part (b) тАФ Explain Inheritance and Polymorphism with examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Inheritance (3.5 marks):**

Inheritance is the mechanism by which a **child class** acquires the properties and methods of a **parent class**, enabling code reuse and hierarchical classification. It establishes an **"IS-A" relationship**.

In Java, inheritance is implemented using the `extends` keyword.

**Example:**
```java
class Employee {
    String name;
    int id;
    void display() {
        System.out.println(name + " - " + id);
    }
}

class Manager extends Employee {
    String department;
    
    @Override
    void display() {
        super.display();
        System.out.println("Dept: " + department);
    }
}
```
`Manager` inherits from `Employee` and adds its own feature `department`.

**(ii) Polymorphism (3.5 marks):**

Polymorphism allows the **same method name** to perform **different behaviors** based on the object or context.

**Compile-Time (Method Overloading):**
```java
class MathOp {
    int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
    double add(double a, double b) { return a + b; }
}
```

**Run-Time (Method Overriding):**
```java
class Animal {
    void sound() { System.out.println("Generic sound"); }
}
class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override
    void sound() { System.out.println("Bark"); }
}
// Usage:
Animal a = new Dog();
a.sound();   // Output: "Bark" (decided at runtime)
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) тАФ [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]**

**"Differentiate between Method Overloading and Method Overriding. Write a Java program that demonstrates both. Explain run-time polymorphism with a real-world example."**

#### **Part (a) тАФ Difference between Overloading and Overriding with a Java program. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Tabular Difference (3.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Method Overloading | Method Overriding |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Type** | Compile-time polymorphism | Run-time polymorphism |
| **Location** | Same class (or subclass) | Two classes (IS-A relationship) |
| **Signature** | Must differ in parameters | Must be identical |
| **Return type** | Can differ | Must be same or covariant |
| **Access modifier** | Can be any | Cannot be more restrictive |
| **Inheritance required** | No | Yes |
| **Resolved at** | Compile time | Run time |
| **Keywords** | None | `@Override` annotation (recommended) |

**Java Program Demonstrating Both (3.5 marks):**
```java
// Method Overloading - Compile-Time Polymorphism
class Calculator {
    int multiply(int a, int b) {
        return a * b;
    }
    double multiply(double a, double b) {  // Overloaded - different types
        return a * b;
    }
    int multiply(int a, int b, int c) {     // Overloaded - different count
        return a * b * c;
    }
}

// Method Overriding - Run-Time Polymorphism
class Payment {
    void process() {
        System.out.println("Processing generic payment");
    }
}
class UPIPayment extends Payment {
    @Override
    void process() {
        System.out.println("Processing UPI payment via UPI ID");
    }
}
class CreditCardPayment extends Payment {
    @Override
    void process() {
        System.out.println("Processing credit card payment");
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Compile-time polymorphism
        Calculator c = new Calculator();
        System.out.println(c.multiply(2, 3));        // Calls int version
        System.out.println(c.multiply(2.5, 3.5));    // Calls double version
        System.out.println(c.multiply(2, 3, 4));     // Calls 3-arg version

        // Run-time polymorphism
        Payment p;
        p = new UPIPayment();
        p.process();   // Output: "Processing UPI payment via UPI ID"

        p = new CreditCardPayment();
        p.process();   // Output: "Processing credit card payment"
    }
}
```

---

#### **Part (b) тАФ Real-world example of Run-time polymorphism. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Scenario (2 marks):** Consider a **Payment Gateway System** used in an e-commerce application like Amazon or Flipkart. The system must support multiple payment methods тАФ UPI, Credit Card, Debit Card, Net Banking, Wallet тАФ but the calling code should not be aware of which method the customer chooses at runtime.

**Design (3 marks):**
- Create a parent class `Payment` with an abstract method `process()`.
- Create child classes `UPIPayment`, `CreditCardPayment`, `NetBankingPayment` that override `process()`.
- The gateway code uses a **parent reference** to call `process()`.

**Java Code (2 marks):**
```java
abstract class Payment {
    abstract void process();
}
class UPIPayment extends Payment {
    void process() { System.out.println("Paid via UPI"); }
}
class CardPayment extends Payment {
    void process() { System.out.println("Paid via Card"); }
}

public class Gateway {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Payment p;  // Parent reference

        // Customer chooses UPI
        p = new UPIPayment();
        p.process();   // JVM calls UPIPayment's process()

        // Customer later chooses Card
        p = new CardPayment();
        p.process();   // JVM calls CardPayment's process()
    }
}
```

**Conclusion:** The same `p.process()` call invokes **different behavior** at runtime. This is **run-time polymorphism** тАФ the system can be extended with new payment types (e.g., `CryptoPayment`) **without modifying the gateway code**, satisfying the **Open/Closed Principle** of software design.

---

> **тЪая╕П [KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning]**
> 
> **Common places students LOSE marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing Abstraction with Encapsulation** тАФ losing up to 3 marks if the difference is not clear.
> 2. **Forgetting `super()` call** in inheritance constructor chaining тАФ losing 1тАУ2 marks.
> 3. **Not showing run-time polymorphism via upcasting** (`Parent p = new Child()`) тАФ losing 2 marks. A static call like `child.method()` does NOT demonstrate polymorphism.
> 4. **Using `private` for inheritance** тАФ students often declare parent members as `private` and then try to access them in the child. This causes **compilation errors** in the exam. Remember: `private` members are **inherited but not accessible** directly.
> 5. **Return-type-only overloading** тАФ students think changing only return type constitutes overloading. It does **not**. Always differ in **parameters**.

---

## тЬЕ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **[!IMPORTANT]**
> **Use this section as your last-day revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

- ЁЯФ╣ **Data Abstraction** тЖТ Hides *implementation*; achieved via `abstract` classes and `interfaces`.
- ЁЯФ╣ **Encapsulation** тЖТ Hides *data*; achieved via access modifiers + getters/setters.
- ЁЯФ╣ **Inheritance** тЖТ Acquires parent's features; uses `extends` (Java) or `:` (C++); supports **code reuse**.
- ЁЯФ╣ **Polymorphism** тЖТ Many forms; **overloading** = compile-time, **overriding** = run-time.
- ЁЯФ╣ **IS-A vs HAS-A**: Inheritance = IS-A; Composition = HAS-A (do not confuse in exams).
- ЁЯФ╣ **`super` keyword** = refers to immediate parent class members/constructor.
- ЁЯФ╣ **`final` keyword** prevents inheritance (class) and overriding (method).
- ЁЯФ╣ **Java does NOT support multiple inheritance via classes** тАФ uses interfaces instead.
- ЁЯФ╣ **Abstract class** can have constructors; **interface** cannot (until Java 8 default methods).
- ЁЯФ╣ **Method overloading rules**: same name, different parameter list (number/type/order). Return type alone is **not** enough.
- ЁЯФ╣ **Method overriding rules**: same name, same parameters, same or covariant return type, access not more restrictive.
- ЁЯФ╣ **Upcasting is required for runtime polymorphism**: `Parent p = new Child();`
- ЁЯФ╣ **Static, final, and private methods cannot be overridden.**
- ЁЯФ╣ **KTU favorite question types**: 4-pillar explanation, abstraction vs encapsulation table, constructor chaining, polymorphism with upcasting.
- ЁЯФ╣ **Mnemonic to recall pillars**: **A PIE** тЖТ Abstraction, Polymorphism, Inheritance, Encapsulation.
- ЁЯФ╣ **Real-world examples to quote in exams**: ATM (Encapsulation), Car Dashboard (Abstraction), Family Tree (Inheritance), Payment Gateway (Polymorphism).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Procedural and Object-Oriented Programming Paradigm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Procedural and Object-Oriented Programming Paradigm

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Programming Paradigm?

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> A **Programming Paradigm** is a fundamental style or approach to organizing code and designing software that provides a structured framework for solving computational problems. It defines how programmers conceptualize, structure, and execute programs.

In simple words, a paradigm is the **"philosophy"** or **"way of thinking"** that dictates *how a program is written, structured, and executed*. The two dominant paradigms in software engineering education are **Procedural Programming (PP)** and **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ The Restaurant Kitchen

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Building Callout:**
> Imagine running a restaurant kitchen.
> - **Procedural Approach** = A senior chef writes down *step-by-step recipes* (functions) тАФ "boil water", "chop onions", "fry for 5 minutes". The recipes are independent, and the ingredients (data) float around separately.
> - **Object-Oriented Approach** = Each dish is treated as a self-contained **"object"** тАФ a *Burger Object* that knows its own ingredients, its own cooking method, and how to serve itself. You interact with the burger as a single intelligent unit.

### 1.3 Formal Definitions

| Paradigm | Formal Definition | Core Unit |
|----------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Procedural Programming** | A paradigm based on the concept of **procedure calls**, where statements are structured into reusable functions (procedures) operating on data. | **Function / Procedure** |
| **Object-Oriented Programming** | A paradigm based on the concept of **objects** that bundle data (attributes) and behavior (methods) together, emphasizing reusability through inheritance and polymorphism. | **Object / Class** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **Procedural Programming** тЖТ C language (1972, Dennis Ritchie)
> - **Object-Oriented Programming** тЖТ Simula (1967), Smalltalk (1972), C++ (1983, Bjarne Stroustrup), Java (1995, James Gosling)
> - OOP is a **superset** of procedural concepts тАФ *every OOP language can do procedural tasks*, but not vice versa.

### 1.4 Why Do We Study Both?

In KTU 2024 syllabus, the question is *not* "Which is better?" but *"How does the programmer's mental model shift from procedure-centric to object-centric thinking?"* This paradigm shift is the **gateway to Java, C++, Python OOP, and modern software architecture**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Procedural Programming (PP) тАФ Operational Breakdown

In PP, the program is viewed as a **sequence of instructions** executed step-by-step, where logic is decomposed into **functions/procedures**.

### Core Characteristics of Procedural Programming
- **Top-Down Approach:** The main problem is broken into smaller sub-problems (functions).
- **Function-Centric Design:** Primary focus is on *writing functions*, not on data.
- **Data is Global & Free-Floating:** Data structures are declared globally and passed between functions as parameters.
- **Reusability via Functions:** Once written, a function can be called multiple times.
- **No Encapsulation:** Data and functions are loosely connected. Any function can access any data.
- **No Inheritance or Polymorphism:** Code reuse is limited; concepts like *is-a* relationships do not exist.
- **Examples of Languages:** C, FORTRAN, Pascal, BASIC.

### Operational Step Pattern of PP
1. **Identify the problem** and define the algorithm.
2. **Declare data structures** globally (or pass them around).
3. **Decompose the algorithm** into a hierarchy of functions.
4. **Call functions** in a specific order to perform the task.
5. The **main() function** acts as the entry point and orchestrator.

## 2.2 Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) тАФ Operational Breakdown

In OOP, the program is viewed as a **collection of interacting objects**, each holding its own data and behavior.

### Core Characteristics of OOP (The 4 Pillars)
- **Bottom-Up Approach:** Small, reusable objects are designed first and then composed to build complex systems.
- **Object-Centric Design:** Primary focus is on *designing classes* that model real-world entities.
- **Encapsulation:** Data and methods are bundled into a single unit (class), and data is hidden from outside interference.
- **Abstraction:** Only essential features are exposed; implementation details are hidden.
- **Inheritance:** New classes (child) can inherit properties and behaviors from existing classes (parent), promoting code reuse.
- **Polymorphism:** A single interface (method name) can represent different underlying forms (e.g., method overriding, overloading).
- **Examples of Languages:** Java, C++, C#, Python, Ruby.

### Operational Step Pattern of OOP
1. **Identify the real-world objects** involved in the problem.
2. **Model each object** with a *class* (blueprint) describing its attributes and methods.
3. **Establish relationships** (inheritance, association, aggregation) between classes.
4. **Create object instances** during program execution.
5. Objects **communicate by passing messages** (calling each other's methods).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Comparison Table тАФ Memorize This for Board Exams:**

| Parameter | Procedural Programming (PP) | Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) |
|-----------|---------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Primary Focus** | Functions / Procedures | Objects / Classes |
| **Approach** | Top-Down | Bottom-Up |
| **Data Handling** | Passed between functions; less secure | Bundled with methods; secure via encapsulation |
| **Code Reusability** | Limited (function reuse only) | High (inheritance, polymorphism) |
| **Security** | Low (data is exposed globally) | High (data hidden via access modifiers) |
| **Extensibility** | Difficult for large projects | Easy тАФ new classes can be added without modifying existing code |
| **Real-world Modeling** | Poor fit for real-world entities | Excellent fit тАФ natural mapping to objects |
| **Function Overloading** | Not supported in C | Supported (compile-time polymorphism) |
| **Operator Overloading** | Not supported | Supported in C++ |
| **Examples** | C, Pascal, FORTRAN | C++, Java, Python, C\# |
| **Inheritance & Polymorphism** | Absent | Core features |
| **Maintenance Cost** | High for large codebases | Low due to modularity |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Procedural Programming** is still used in **embedded systems**, **operating system kernels** (Linux kernel is mostly C), **device drivers**, and **firmware** where hardware-level control and minimal memory overhead are critical.
- **Object-Oriented Programming** dominates in **enterprise software (Banking, ERP)**, **GUI application development**, **game development**, **web frameworks (Spring, Django)**, and **simulation systems** where modeling real-world interactions is essential.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern languages like Java and C\# enforce OOP strictly, while C++ is a *multi-paradigm* language supporting both procedural and OOP styles тАФ the choice depends on the problem domain.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: How the Two Paradigns Solve the Same Problem

Let us mathematically and logically trace how the *same problem* тАФ **"Calculate the area of a rectangle"** тАФ is solved under both paradigms.

### 3.1.1 Procedural Approach (Derivation Steps)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Derivation тАФ PP Model:**
> In PP, **data and functions are decoupled**. The function takes data as input, processes it, and returns a result.

**Step 1:** Define the data structure (data is a passive entity floating in memory).

$$\text{Data Structure} = \langle \text{length} : \mathbb{R},\ \text{width} : \mathbb{R} \rangle$$

**Step 2:** Define a function (the active entity) that operates on the data.

$$\text{Function: } \text{calculateArea}(\text{length},\ \text{width}) \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$$

**Step 3:** Execute the function by calling it from `main()` with data passed as arguments.

$$\text{Area} = \text{length} \times \text{width}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{main}() &: \\
& \quad \text{length} \leftarrow 5.0 \\
& \quad \text{width} \leftarrow 3.0 \\
& \quad \text{Area} \leftarrow \text{calculateArea}(\text{length},\ \text{width}) \\
& \quad \text{Output: } 15.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The function is a *standalone procedure*. The data (`length`, `width`) exists independently in memory and is merely *handed over* to the function for processing. There is **no permanent binding** between data and behavior.

### 3.1.2 Object-Oriented Approach (Derivation Steps)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Derivation тАФ OOP Model:**
> In OOP, **data and behavior are encapsulated** into a single unit called a *class*. The object *owns* both its state and the operations that act on that state.

**Step 1:** Model the entity as a class тАФ a blueprint that defines both attributes (data) and methods (behavior).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Class: } \text{Rectangle} &= \{ \\
& \quad \text{Attribute: } \text{length} : \mathbb{R} \\
& \quad \text{Attribute: } \text{width} : \mathbb{R} \\
& \quad \text{Method: } \text{calculateArea}() \rightarrow \mathbb{R} \\
& \quad\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Instantiate the class to create an object (a concrete instance in memory).

$$\text{rect1} \leftarrow \text{new Rectangle}(5.0,\ 3.0)$$

**Step 3:** Invoke the method on the object. The object *internally* uses its own data.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Area} &= \text{rect1.calculateArea}() \\
&= \text{rect1.length} \times \text{rect1.width} \\
&= 5.0 \times 3.0 \\
&= 15.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The object `rect1` is a *self-sufficient unit*. It holds its own data and the method to act on that data. No external function needs to know *how* the area is calculated тАФ the object manages its own state. This is the **encapsulation principle** in action.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix тАФ Paradigm Decision Function

The following analytical matrix helps decide *which paradigm to use* based on the problem domain.

$$
P(\text{chosen}) = \begin{cases}
\text{OOP}, & \text{if } (\text{Scale} \geq \text{Large}) \land (\text{Reusability} = \text{High}) \land (\text{RealWorldModeling} = \text{Required}) \\
\text{PP}, & \text{if } (\text{Scale} \leq \text{Medium}) \land (\text{Performance} = \text{Critical}) \land (\text{HardwareAccess} = \text{Required}) \\
\text{Hybrid}, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $\text{Scale}$ refers to the size of the software system.
- $\text{Reusability}$ indicates how much code reuse is anticipated.
- $\text{RealWorldModeling}$ indicates if entities map naturally to objects.
- $\text{Performance}$ indicates latency/throughput constraints.
- $\text{HardwareAccess}$ indicates direct memory/register manipulation needs.

### 3.3 Summary of Logical Transitions

| Step | Procedural Logic | OOP Logic |
|------|------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Identify functions first | Identify objects first |
| 2 | Pass data into functions | Bundle data inside the object |
| 3 | Function acts on data | Object acts on its own data |
| 4 | Data is shared globally | Data is encapsulated & hidden |
| 5 | Reuse by function calls | Reuse by inheritance & polymorphism |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram тАФ Procedural Programming Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    mainA[Main Function] --> funcA1[Function 1: Read Input]
    mainA --> funcA2[Function 2: Process Data]
    mainA --> funcA3[Function 3: Display Output]
    dataA[(Global Data Structures)] --> funcA1
    dataA --> funcA2
    dataA --> funcA3
    funcA1 --> funcA2
    funcA2 --> funcA3
    funcA1 -.passes data.-> funcA2
    funcA2 -.passes data.-> funcA3
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **Main Function** is the central controller.
- All functions read/write from **global data** тАФ there is no ownership.
- Functions communicate by **passing data** as parameters.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram тАФ Object-Oriented Programming Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classA1[Class Rectangle]:::obj
    classA2[Class Circle]:::obj
    classA3[Class Square]:::obj
    classA1 -.inheritance.-> classA4[Class Shape]:::parent
    classA2 -.inheritance.-> classA4
    classA3 -.inheritance.-> classA4
    classA1 --> attrA1[Attribute: length, width]
    classA1 --> methA1[Method: calculateArea]
    classA1 --> methA2[Method: displayInfo]
    classA2 --> attrA2[Attribute: radius]
    classA2 --> methA3[Method: calculateArea]
    objA1[Object rect1] --> classA1
    objA2[Object circ1] --> classA2
    objA1 --> msgA1[Sends Message: calculateArea]
    objA2 --> msgA2[Sends Message: calculateArea]

    classDef obj fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef parent fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d46b08,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Classes** act as blueprints тАФ they are not real until instantiated.
- **Inheritance** arrows show `is-a` relationships (e.g., *Rectangle is a Shape*).
- **Objects** are runtime instances that hold their own data and can send messages to other objects.
- The same method name `calculateArea` behaves differently for each object тАФ this is **polymorphism**.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram тАФ Direct Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PP[Procedural Programming]
        direction TB
        pp1[Program = Functions + Data]
        pp2[Functions call functions]
        pp3[Data is global and shared]
        pp4[Top-Down design]
        pp1 --> pp2 --> pp3 --> pp4
    end

    subgraph OOP[Object-Oriented Programming]
        direction TB
        oop1[Program = Objects interacting]
        oop2[Objects send messages]
        oop3[Each object owns its data]
        oop4[Bottom-Up design]
        oop1 --> oop2 --> oop3 --> oop4
    end

    PP -.evolved into.-> OOP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:**
> Notice how **OOP is an evolution of PP** тАФ it adds three powerful concepts on top of procedural foundations: **encapsulation** (data binding), **inheritance** (code reuse), and **polymorphism** (interface flexibility). In a KTU board answer, always emphasize this evolutionary relationship.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A тАФ Short Answer Questions (2 Marks / 3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Programming Paradigm. Name the two major paradigms. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Exam 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **Programming Paradigm** is a fundamental style of programming that provides a structured approach to writing code, defining how problems are decomposed and solutions are organized. The two major paradigms are:
1. **Procedural Programming (PP)** тАФ based on procedures/functions.
2. **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** тАФ based on objects/classes that bundle data and behavior.

### Question 2: Differentiate between Top-Down and Bottom-Up approaches. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Internal Test 1]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| Aspect | Top-Down Approach | Bottom-Up Approach |
|--------|-------------------|--------------------|
| **Used in** | Procedural Programming | Object-Oriented Programming |
| **Strategy** | Break problem into sub-functions | Combine small reusable objects into a system |
| **Focus** | Process / Logic first | Data / Objects first |
| **Flow** | General тЖТ Specific | Specific тЖТ General |
| **Example** | C program with main() calling helper functions | Java program where classes are composed to form an application |

---

## 5.2 PART B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks тАФ Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.

---

### тЭУ Question A (14 Marks) тАФ `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Explain the **Procedural Programming Paradigm** in detail. List its **key characteristics, advantages, and limitations**. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** With a suitable example, explain how the **Object-Oriented Programming Paradigm** differs from the procedural approach. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution тАФ Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Procedural Programming Paradigm тАФ Detailed Explanation:**

1. **Definition [1 mark]:** Procedural Programming is a paradigm derived from structured programming, based on the concept of *procedure calls*. A program is divided into smaller, reusable blocks called *functions* or *procedures*, each performing a specific task.

2. **Key Characteristics [3 marks]:**
   - **Top-Down Design:** The problem is decomposed hierarchically тАФ main function calls sub-functions.
   - **Function-Centric:** The focus is on creating functions, not on data ownership.
   - **Sequential Execution:** Statements are executed in a defined order (with control structures like loops and conditionals).
   - **Global Data Access:** Data is often declared globally and is accessible to multiple functions.
   - **Code Reusability:** Functions can be reused by calling them from multiple locations.
   - **No Built-in Security:** Data is exposed; there is no access control.

3. **Advantages [2 marks]:**
   - Simple to understand and implement for small programs.
   - Efficient in terms of memory and execution (no overhead of objects).
   - Excellent for hardware-level programming and system software.

4. **Limitations [1 mark]:**
   - Poor data security (no encapsulation).
   - Difficult to manage and scale for large software systems.
   - Limited code reusability (no inheritance).
   - Real-world entities are hard to model as pure procedures.

#### Model Solution тАФ Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Example: Managing Student Records**

**Procedural Approach:**
- Define a `struct Student` (data only) globally.
- Write functions like `addStudent()`, `displayStudent()`, `searchStudent()` that accept the student struct as parameters.
- Data floats in memory; functions are separate. Any function can corrupt the data.

**Object-Oriented Approach:**
- Define a `class Student` that contains:
