  - **Attributes** (encapsulated data): `name`, `rollNo`, `marks`.
  - **Methods** (behavior): `inputDetails()`, `displayDetails()`, `calculateGrade()`.
- Create objects like `s1`, `s2` тАФ each object is a self-sufficient unit.
- Data is hidden using access modifiers (`private`, `public`); external code cannot directly modify `marks` without going through validation methods.

**Key Differences Highlighted [3 marks]:**
| Aspect | Procedural (Student) | OOP (Student) |
|--------|----------------------|---------------|
| Data-Function Binding | Loose (data is separate) | Tight (data + methods in one class) |
| Security | Low (no access control) | High (encapsulation) |
| Reusability | Function-level only | Class-level via inheritance |
| Modeling | Artificial mapping to functions | Natural mapping to real-world entity |

---

### тЭУ Question B (14 Marks) тАФ `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

> **(a)** List and explain the **four fundamental features (pillars) of Object-Oriented Programming**. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast **Procedural and Object-Oriented Programming** with a neat comparison table and a real-world analogy. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution тАФ Part (a) [7 Marks]

**The Four Pillars of OOP:**

1. **Encapsulation [2 marks]:** Wrapping data (attributes) and code (methods) into a single unit called a *class*, and restricting direct access to some of the object's components using *access specifiers* (`private`, `protected`, `public`). It ensures data hiding and protects object integrity.
   - *Example:* A `BankAccount` class hides its `balance` variable and exposes only validated methods like `deposit()` and `withdraw()`.

2. **Abstraction [1.5 marks]:** Hiding complex implementation details and exposing only the essential features of an object. Achieved via *abstract classes* and *interfaces*.
   - *Example:* A driver uses the steering wheel and pedals (abstract interface) without knowing the internal combustion mechanism.

3. **Inheritance [2 marks]:** The mechanism by which one class (*child/sub class*) acquires the properties and behaviors of another class (*parent/super class*). It promotes *code reuse* and establishes an *is-a* relationship.
   - *Example:* A `SavingsAccount` class inherits from `BankAccount` and adds interest-related features.

4. **Polymorphism [1.5 marks]:** The ability of a message, function, or object to behave differently based on context. Two forms: *compile-time* (overloading) and *run-time* (overriding).
   - *Example:* A `draw()` method behaves differently for `Circle`, `Rectangle`, and `Triangle` objects.

#### Model Solution тАФ Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Real-World Analogy тАФ "Building a House":**

- **Procedural Approach:** You write a *checklist of steps* тАФ lay foundation, build walls, install wiring, paint, furnish. The *materials* (data) are kept in a separate store and anyone can pick them up at any time. No ownership, no security. If two workers use the same cement bag, there is a conflict.
- **OOP Approach:** You design a *blueprint (class)* for each component тАФ *Wall, Door, Window, Room* тАФ each with its own properties (dimensions, material) and behaviors (open, close, paint). When you build the house, you create *instances (objects)* of these blueprints. Each object manages its own state securely. If a *Door* needs to be redesigned, you modify the *Door blueprint* тАФ it doesn't disturb the *Window* or *Wall*.

**Neat Comparison Table [5 marks]:**

| Feature | Procedural Programming | Object-Oriented Programming |
|---------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Basic Unit** | Function | Object / Class |
| **Approach** | Top-Down | Bottom-Up |
| **Data Security** | Low (global data) | High (encapsulation) |
| **Reusability** | Function calls | Inheritance & Polymorphism |
| **Modeling** | Function-oriented | Real-world entity mapping |
| **Maintenance** | Difficult for large projects | Easier due to modularity |
| **Languages** | C, Pascal, FORTRAN | Java, C++, C\#, Python |
| **Performance** | Faster (less overhead) | Slightly slower (object overhead) |
| **Code Organization** | Procedure-based | Class-based |
| **Best Suited For** | System software, embedded | Application software, GUI, games |

---

## 5.3 тЪая╕П KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Paradigms:** Many students write that "OOP replaces PP." This is **factually wrong**. OOP is an *evolution* that **includes** procedural concepts. Always state: *"OOP is a superset of procedural programming."*
>
> 2. **Skipping Real-World Analogy:** Examiners in KTU award **2-3 bonus marks** for diagrams or analogies. Always include a *real-world example* (Car, Bank Account, Restaurant, House) in Part B answers.
>
> 3. **Confusing "Class" and "Object":** A *Class* is a *blueprint/template*, whereas an *Object* is a *runtime instance* of that class. Marks are deducted for mixing these terms.
>
> 4. **Not Memorizing the 4 Pillars:** The four OOP features тАФ **Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, Polymorphism** тАФ must be memorized with *one-line definitions and one example each*. Examiners often directly ask: *"List the features of OOP."* (7 marks)
>
> 5. **Ignoring Procedural Limitations:** A common pitfall is listing only OOP advantages. The examiner expects a **balanced comparison** тАФ also mention *where PP is better* (embedded systems, OS kernels).
>
> 6. **Missing Keyword Usage:** Use the exact KTU terminology: *encapsulation, abstraction, inheritance, polymorphism, message passing, class, object, method, attribute*. Marks are awarded for **keyword density** in board answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ Module 1, Topic 1:**

### ЁЯФС Key Definitions to Memorize
- **Programming Paradigm** = a style/approach to programming (e.g., PP, OOP, Functional, Logic).
- **Procedural Programming** = functions/procedures operating on data; top-down.
- **Object-Oriented Programming** = objects/classes bundling data + behavior; bottom-up.

### ЁЯФС The 4 Pillars of OOP (with one-line recall)
- **Encapsulation** = *Data + methods bundled, data hidden* (`private`, `public`).
- **Abstraction** = *Show essentials, hide details* (abstract classes, interfaces).
- **Inheritance** = *Child class acquires parent class properties* (code reuse, `is-a`).
- **Polymorphism** = *One interface, many forms* (overloading, overriding).

### ЁЯФС Key Distinctions
- **Class** = Blueprint (logical, no memory).
- **Object** = Instance (physical, occupies memory).
- **Method** = Function defined inside a class.
- **Attribute** = Variable defined inside a class.
- **Message Passing** = Objects communicate by calling each other's methods.

### ЁЯФС Paradigm Comparison Quick-Recall
- **PP** тЖТ C, top-down, function-centric, global data, no security.
- **OOP** тЖТ Java/C++, bottom-up, object-centric, encapsulated data, high security.

### ЁЯФС High-Yield Board Exam Keywords (Use Them in Answers)
*Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Class, Object, Method Overloading, Method Overriding, Access Specifiers, Message Passing, Reusability, Modularity, Top-Down, Bottom-Up, Real-world Modeling.*

### ЁЯФС Historical Timeline (Examiner Loves This)
- **1967** тЖТ Simula (first OOP language)
- **1972** тЖТ C (procedural), Smalltalk (pure OOP)
- **1983** тЖТ C++ (hybrid: procedural + OOP)
- **1995** тЖТ Java (pure OOP, platform independent)

### ЁЯФС Engineering Application Snapshot
| Domain | Preferred Paradigm | Why |
|--------|--------------------|-----|
| OS Kernels, Device Drivers | Procedural (C) | Performance, hardware access |
| Banking, ERP, GUI Apps | OOP (Java, C#) | Security, modularity, reuse |
| Game Development | OOP (C++, Unity/C#) | Real-world entity modeling |
| Embedded Systems | Procedural / Mixed | Memory efficiency |

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microservices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Microservices # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Microservices** (also called the **Microservice Architecture**) is an architectural style that structures an application as a collection of small, autonomous, loosely coupled services, where each service is: > - **Self-contained** (owns its own data and business logic) > - **Independently deployable** (can be updated without redeploying the entire system) > - **Organized around a single business capability** > - **Communicates** through well-defined, lightweight mechanisms (typically HTTP/REST APIs, messaging queues, or gRPC) The term was popularized around **2011тАУ2014** during workshops near Venice, where software architects described "small services with bounded contexts" as an evolution of **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)**. Mathematically, an application $\mathcal{A}$ built using microservices can be expressed as: $$\mathcal{A} = \{S_1, S_2, S_3, \dots, S_n\}$$ where each $S_i$ is an **independent service** with its own: - Codebase $\rightarrow$ $C_i$ - Database $\rightarrow$ $DB_i$ - Deployment pipeline $\rightarrow$ $P_i$ such that: $$C_i \cap C_j = \emptyset \quad \text{and} \quad DB_i \cap DB_j = \emptyset \quad \text{for} \quad i \neq j$$ --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Understanding) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Hospital System** Imagine a **large multi-specialty hospital**: | Traditional Approach (Monolith) | Modern Approach (Microservices) | |--------------------------------|--------------------------------| | One giant building where **everything happens** тАФ OPD, ICU, pharmacy, labs, billing, reception тАФ all in one tightly-coupled structure. | A **central reception** that routes patients to **specialized buildings** тАФ Cardiology Wing, Neurology Wing, Lab Block, Pharmacy Block, Billing Counter. Each wing has its own staff, equipment, and record system. | **Key parallels:** - If the **Pharmacy** needs a software upgrade, the **Cardiology Wing** is **not affected** тЖТ *Independent Deployment* - Each wing uses its **own database** of patient records (Cardiology records тЙа Lab records) тЖТ *Decentralized Data* - The wings talk through a **standard protocol** (HTTP request like a referral slip) тЖТ *API-based communication* - One wing can be **rebuilt or replaced** without touching others тЖТ *Polyglot Development* This is exactly how **Netflix, Amazon, Uber, and Flipkart** run their systems тАФ as hundreds of small, focused services. --- ## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary - **Service**: A small, well-defined, independent unit performing one business function. - **Bounded Context**: A boundary within which a particular model is valid (borrowed from **Domain-Driven Design (DDD)**). - **API Gateway**: A single entry point that routes client requests to appropriate microservices. - **Service Registry**: A directory of running service instances (e.g., *Eureka, Consul*). - **Circuit Breaker**: A pattern that prevents cascading failures (e.g., *Hystrix, Resilience4j*). - **Container**: A lightweight, portable execution environment (e.g., *Docker*). - **Orchestrator**: Tool that manages containers (e.g., *Kubernetes*). > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU expects students to clearly differentiate **Monolithic Architecture** vs **Microservice Architecture**, identify the **characteristics** of microservices, and explain **advantages/disadvantages**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why" тАФ Problems With Traditional Monolithic Architecture A **Monolithic Application** is built as a **single, unified unit** where all business logic, UI, and data access code share the same codebase and runtime. ### Logical Structure of a Monolith: $$\text{Monolith} = \underbrace{UI}_{\text{Presentation}} \cup \underbrace{BLL}_{\text{Business Logic}} \cup \underbrace{DAL}_{\text{Data Access}} \cup \underbrace{DB}_{\text{Single Database}}$$ ### Pain Points (Why We Need Microservices): 1. **Tight Coupling** $\Rightarrow$ A change in the *payment module* requires rebuilding and redeploying the *entire* application. 2. **Single Point of Failure** $\Rightarrow$ A memory leak in *one component* crashes the *whole* system. 3. **Technology Lock-in** $\Rightarrow$ Stuck with one language/framework (e.g., entire stack in Java EE). 4. **Scalability Bottleneck** $\Rightarrow$ Must scale the *entire* application even if only *one feature* is overloaded. 5. **Long Release Cycles** $\Rightarrow$ Teams must coordinate, leading to monthly/yearly releases instead of daily/weekly. --- ## 2.2 Core Characteristics of Microservices (KTU High-Yield) The **"9 Characteristics"** as defined by Martin Fowler & James Lewis: | # | Characteristic | Explanation | Why It Matters | |---|---------------|-------------|----------------| | 1 | **Componentization via Services** | Replaces libraries with replaceable services. | Independent replacement. | | 2 | **Organized Around Business Capability** | Cross-functional teams own a service end-to-end. | Conway's Law alignment. | | 3 | **Decentralized Governance / Polyglot** | Each service picks its own tech stack (Java, Python, Go, Node). | Right tool for the job. | | 4 | **Decentralized Data Storage** | Each service owns its database. | No shared schema conflicts. | | 5 | **Infrastructure Automation** | CI/CD pipelines, automated testing. | Faster, reliable deployments. | | 6 | **Smart Endpoints, Dumb Pipes** | Logic stays in services; transport is simple (HTTP/queue). | Avoids heavy ESB. | | 7 | **Design for Failure** | Assume services *will* fail; use circuit breakers. | Resilience. | | 8 | **Evolutionary Design** | Replace services as understanding grows. | Avoids big-bang rewrites. | | 9 | **Independent Deployment** | Each service deploys on its own schedule. | Continuous delivery. | --- ## 2.3 Advantages and Disadvantages (Board-Exam Favorite) ### тЬЕ Advantages: - **Scalability** тАФ Scale services independently. If *Search* gets 80% traffic, scale only *Search*. - **Fault Isolation** тАФ One service failure doesn't crash others. - **Technology Heterogeneity** тАФ Use Python for ML, Go for performance, Java for transactions. - **Faster Time-to-Market** тАФ Small teams work in parallel. - **Easier Maintenance** тАФ Smaller codebases are easier to understand. - **Reusability** тАФ A *Payment Service* can be reused across multiple products. ### тЭМ Disadvantages: - **Distributed System Complexity** тАФ Network latency, partial failures, eventual consistency. - **Data Consistency** тАФ No ACID transactions across services; need **Saga Pattern**. - **Operational Overhead** тАФ Need containerization, monitoring, orchestration (DevOps heavy). - **Network Security** тАФ More inter-service communication = larger attack surface. - **Testing Complexity** тАФ Integration testing across services is harder. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Comparison Table) | Property | Monolithic Architecture | Microservice Architecture | |----------|------------------------|---------------------------| | **Deployment Unit** | Single unit | Multiple independent units | | **Codebase** | One shared repo | One repo per service | | **Database** | Single shared DB | DB-per-service | | **Scaling** | Horizontal scaling of whole app | Per-service scaling | | **Fault Tolerance** | Low (single point of failure) | High (isolated failures) | | **Tech Stack** | Homogeneous | Polyglot (heterogeneous) | | **Communication** | In-process function calls | HTTP/REST, gRPC, Message Queues | | **Team Structure** | Centralized | Cross-functional, decentralized | | **Release Cycle** | Long (weeks/months) | Short (hours/days) | | **Initial Complexity** | Low | High | | **Operational Cost** | Low (early), High (scaling) | High (requires DevOps maturity) | | **Best For** | Small/simple apps, startups MVP | Large/complex, high-traffic systems | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Use Case | |----------|----------| | **E-Commerce** | Order Service, Inventory, Payment, Recommendation as separate services (Amazon). | | **Streaming** | Video encoding, recommendation, user-profile, billing (Netflix has 700+ microservices). | | **Banking** | Account, KYC, Loan, Transaction, Notification services. | | **Ride-Sharing** | Ride matching, pricing, driver-location, payment (Uber). | | **Healthcare** | Patient records, appointment, prescription, billing тАФ each as a service. | > [!NOTE] > **Rule of Thumb:** If your application is small, has a small team (< 5 devs), and doesn't expect massive traffic тАФ **start with a monolith**, then break it into microservices as complexity grows (Martin Fowler's "Monolith First" principle). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Communication Patterns ## 3.1 Inter-Service Communication (Step-by-Step Analysis) In microservices, services **cannot** call each other like normal functions. They must use network-based communication. There are **two major paradigms**: ### 3.1.1 Synchronous Communication (Request-Response) The caller **waits** for the response before proceeding. **Common Protocols:** - **REST over HTTP** тАФ Most common, uses JSON/XML. - **gRPC** тАФ High-performance, uses Protocol Buffers (binary). - **GraphQL** тАФ Client specifies the data shape. **Mathematical Model of Latency:** For a chain of $n$ synchronous calls, total response time is: $$T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( t_{\text{network}_i} + t_{\text{process}_i} \right)$$ where: - $t_{\text{network}_i}$ = network latency of the $i$-th hop - $t_{\text{process}_i}$ = processing time inside the $i$-th service > **Engineering Insight:** As $n$ grows, $T_{\text{total}}$ grows linearly. A long synchronous chain becomes a **performance bottleneck** тАФ this is why **anti-patterns** like *chatty microservices* are avoided. --- ### 3.1.2 Asynchronous Communication (Event-Driven / Message-Based) The caller does **NOT** wait. It publishes an event/message to a **broker**, and other services consume it. **Common Tools:** - **Message Queues** тАФ RabbitMQ, ActiveMQ (point-to-point). - **Event Streams** тАФ Apache Kafka, AWS Kinesis (pub-sub, log-based). - **Event Bus** тАФ lightweight in-process broker. **Flow:** $$\text{Producer Service} \xrightarrow{\text{Publish Event}} \text{Message Broker} \xrightarrow{\text{Deliver}} \text{Consumer Service(s)}$$ **Advantages:** - **Decoupling** тАФ Producer doesn't know consumers. - **Resilience** тАФ If consumer is down, message is queued. - **Scalability** тАФ Multiple consumers can process in parallel. **Mathematical Throughput Model:** For a Kafka-like partition system with $p$ partitions, max parallel consumers: $$\text{Throughput}_{\max} = p \times \text{Throughput}_{\text{per consumer}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Service Decomposition Strategies (Step-by-Step) Decomposition = "How do I split a monolith into microservices?" Two dominant approaches: ### Strategy 1: Decomposition by Business Capability 1. Identify **business capabilities** (e.g., Order Management, Inventory, Shipping). 2. Each capability becomes a service. 3. **Bounded Context** from DDD defines the boundary. $$\text{Application} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \text{Capability}_i$$ ### Strategy 2: Decomposition by Subdomain (DDD-Aligned) 1. Apply **Domain-Driven Design**. 2. Classify subdomains as: - **Core** (competitive advantage) тАФ e.g., Recommendation Engine. - **Supporting** (necessary but not differentiating) тАФ e.g., User Profile. - **Generic** (commodity) тАФ e.g., Email Notification. $$\text{Service Priority: Core} \succ \text{Supporting} \succ \text{Generic}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** When asked "How do you identify microservice boundaries?" тАФ always mention **Bounded Context (DDD)** and **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)** from OOP. This earns extra marks because it directly connects OOP concepts to microservices. --- ## 3.3 Data Management in Microservices A **golden rule** of microservices: > *"No two services share a database."* ### Two patterns for cross-service data needs: **Pattern A: Saga Pattern** (for distributed transactions) - A saga is a sequence of **local transactions** across services. - If one step fails, **compensating transactions** undo the previous steps. $$\text{Saga} = \{T_1, T_2, T_3, \dots, T_n\}$$ If $T_k$ fails: $$\text{Compensate}(T_1), \text{Compensate}(T_2), \dots, \text{Compensate}(T_{k-1})$$ **Pattern B: API Composition** (for queries) - One service calls others via API, aggregates the results. - Suitable when strong consistency is **not** required. --- ## 3.4 Key Design Patterns at a Glance (KTU Hot Topic) | Pattern | Purpose | Example Tool | |---------|---------|--------------| | **API Gateway** | Single client entry point; routes, auth, rate-limiting. | Kong, NGINX, AWS API Gateway | | **Service Registry & Discovery** | Services register themselves; clients discover dynamically. | Eureka, Consul, Zookeeper | | **Circuit Breaker** | Stops cascading failures; opens circuit after threshold. | Hystrix, Resilience4j | | **Saga** | Manages distributed transactions. | Temporal, Camunda | | **Database per Service** | Each service has its own DB. | тАФ | | **Event Sourcing** | Store state as a sequence of events. | Kafka + Event Store | | **CQRS** | Separate read & write models. | Axon Framework | | **Bulkhead** | Isolate resources per service. | Thread pools, connection pools | | **Sidecar** | Deploy helper components alongside service. | Istio, Linkerd | | **Choreography** | Services coordinate via events (no central controller). | Kafka topics | | **Orchestration** | Central coordinator manages the workflow. | Camunda, AWS Step Functions | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Monolithic vs Microservice тАФ High-Level Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MONO["MONOLITHIC ARCHITECTURE"] M_Client["Client Browser / Mobile App"] M_App["Single Application Server\n(UI + Business Logic + Data Access)"] M_DB[("Shared Database")] M_Client --> M_App --> M_DB end classDef monoStyle fill:#FFD6D6,stroke:#B30000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class M_Client,M_App,M_DB monoStyle ``` ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MICRO["MICROSERVICE ARCHITECTURE"] C["Client / Mobile App"] GW["API Gateway\n(Routing, Auth, Rate Limiting)"] SR["Service Registry\n(Eureka / Consul)"] C --> GW GW --> S1["Service 1: User\n(Java + PostgreSQL)"] GW --> S2["Service 2: Order\n(Python + MongoDB)"] GW --> S3["Service 3: Payment\n(Go + MySQL)"] GW --> S4["Service 4: Inventory\n(Node + Redis)"] S1 -.registers.-> SR S2 -.registers.-> SR S3 -.registers.-> SR S4 -.registers.-> SR S2 <-->|Async Events| MQ["Message Broker\n(Kafka / RabbitMQ)"] S3 <--> MQ end classDef gwStyle fill:#D6E4FF,stroke:#0033B3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef svcStyle fill:#D6FFD6,stroke:#1B7A1B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef infraStyle fill:#FFF4D6,stroke:#B38900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class C gwStyle class GW,SR,infraStyle infraStyle class S1,S2,S3,S4 svcStyle ``` --- ## 4.2 Saga Pattern тАФ Distributed Transaction Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant C as Client participant OS as Order Service participant PS as Payment Service participant IS as Inventory Service participant NS as Notification Service C->>OS: 1. Place Order activate OS OS->>PS: 2. Process Payment activate PS PS-->>OS: 3. Payment Success deactivate PS OS->>IS: 4. Reserve Inventory activate IS IS-->>OS: 5. Inventory Reserved deactivate IS OS->>NS: 6. Send Confirmation Email activate NS NS-->>OS: 7. Email Sent deactivate NS OS-->>C: 8. Order Confirmed deactivate OS Note over IS,NS: If Step 4 fails, compensating transaction
refunds payment (undo Step 2) ``` --- ## 4.3 API Gateway & Service Discovery Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Client Request:\nGET /orders/123"] --> B["API Gateway"] B --> C{"Look up in\nService Registry"} C -->|Found| D["Route to\nOrder Service Instance 2"] C -->|Not Found| E["Return 404 /\nLoad Balancer Picks Instance"] D --> F["Order Service\nreturns JSON"] subgraph REG["Service Registry (Eureka)"] R1["Order-Svc: 10.0.0.5:8080"] R2["Order-Svc: 10.0.0.6:8080"] R3["Order-Svc: 10.0.0.7:8080"] end C -.queries.-> REG classDef gwStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#CC6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef regStyle fill:#E0D6FF,stroke:#3D0099,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef svcStyle fill:#D6FFD6,stroke:#1B7A1B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class B,C,E gwStyle class R1,R2,R3,REG regStyle class D,F svcStyle ``` --- ## 4.4 Circuit Breaker Pattern тАФ Fault Tolerance ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Closed Closed --> Open : Failure threshold reached\n(e.g., 5 failures in 10s) Open --> HalfOpen : Timeout elapsed\n(e.g., 30 seconds) HalfOpen --> Closed : Trial request succeeds HalfOpen --> Open : Trial request fails note right of Closed : All requests pass through.\nFailures are counted. note right of Open : Requests fail fast.\nNo call to downstream service. note right of HalfOpen : Limited trial requests\nallowed to test recovery. ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam тАФ June 2024]** Define Microservice Architecture. List any four of its key characteristics. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Microservice Architecture is an architectural style that structures an application as a suite of small, independently deployable services, each organized around a specific business capability, owning its own database, and communicating through lightweight protocols (HTTP/REST, gRPC, or messaging queues). **Four Key Characteristics (1.5 marks тАФ 0.375 each):** 1. **Independent Deployability** тАФ Each service can be deployed without affecting others. 2. **Decentralized Data Storage** тАФ Each service owns its private database. 3. **Polyglot Technology** тАФ Services can use different languages/frameworks. 4. **Design for Failure** тАФ Services are designed assuming other services may fail; patterns like circuit breakers are used. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023]** Differentiate between Monolithic Architecture and Microservice Architecture in terms of deployment, scaling, and fault tolerance. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Monolithic | Microservice | |--------|-----------|--------------| | **Deployment** | Entire app redeployed even for a small change (0.75 m) | Each service deployed independently (0.75 m) | | **Scaling** | Whole app must be scaled together (horizontal duplication) (0.75 m) | Individual services scaled based on load (0.75 m) | | **Fault Tolerance** | Single point of failure тАФ one bug can crash the system (0.75 m) | Fault isolated тАФ failure of one service doesn't crash others (0.75 m) | > **Note:** Award full 3 marks only if all three aspects are clearly compared in a structured way. --- ## 5.2 PART B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts тАФ **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is provided. The model answer below follows the exact KTU valuation key style. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) тАФ [KTU University Exam тАФ June 2024]** **Q. (a)** Explain the **Monolithic Architecture** in detail. List **three major limitations** that motivate the shift to microservices. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition of Monolithic Architecture (2 marks):** A **Monolithic Architecture** is a traditional software design where the entire application тАФ including the user interface, business logic, and data access layer тАФ is built and packaged as a single, indivisible unit. All modules share the same runtime process, the same codebase, and typically connect to a single shared database. Examples include early Java EE applications packaged as a single `WAR` or `EAR` file. **Structure (1.5 marks):** ``` тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР тФВ SINGLE APPLICATION тФВ тФВ тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР тФВ тФВ тФВ UI тФВ тФВ BLL тФВ тФВ DAL тФВ тФВ тФВ тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ тФВ тФВ тЖУ Common Runtime тФВ тФВ тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР тФВ тФВ тФВ Shared DB тФВ тФВ тФВ тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ тФВ тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ ``` **Three Major Limitations (3.5 marks тАФ ~1.2 marks each):** 1. **Tight Coupling & Deployment Bottleneck:** Since all modules are interwoven in one codebase, a small change in one module (e.g., the payment module) requires rebuilding and redeploying the *entire* application. This leads to slow release cycles. 2. **Poor Scalability:** The application must be scaled as a whole. If only the *search* feature is overloaded, we still scale the entire app, wasting resources. 3. **Single Point of Failure:** A memory leak, bug, or crash in *one* module can bring down the *entire* application, leading to complete system downtime. 4. *(Bonus point, optional)*: **Technology Lock-in** тАФ The entire app is tied to one tech stack. --- **Q. (b)** With a **neat diagram**, explain the **Microservice Architecture**. Discuss any **four advantages** and **two challenges** of using microservices. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Microservice Architecture тАФ Explanation (2 marks):** Microservice Architecture structures an application as a collection of **loosely coupled, independently deployable services**, each focused on a single business capability. Services communicate via lightweight protocols such as **HTTP/REST** or **messaging queues**. Each service has its own database, and teams can use heterogeneous technologies. **Diagram (2 marks):** ``` [ Client / Browser ] тФВ тЦ╝ [ API Gateway ] тФВ тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФ╝тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФмтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР тЦ╝ тЦ╝ тЦ╝ тЦ╝ [User] [Order] [Payment] [Inventory] тФВ тФВ тФВ тФВ [DB_U] [DB_O] [DB_P] [DB_I] тЦ▓ тЦ▓ тЦ▓ тЦ▓ тФФтФАтФАтФА Message Broker (Kafka) тФАтФАтФАтФШ ``` *(Use Mermaid version from Section 4.1 in exam sketches)* **Four Advantages (2 marks тАФ 0.5 each):** 1. **Independent Scalability** тАФ Each service is scaled individually based on load. 2. **Fault Isolation** тАФ Failure of one service does not affect others. 3. **Technology Heterogeneity** тАФ Each service can use the best-fit language/framework. 4. **Faster Releases** тАФ Small, autonomous teams deploy independently, enabling CI/CD. **Two Challenges (1 mark тАФ 0.5 each):** 1. **Distributed System Complexity** тАФ Handling network latency, partial failures, and eventual consistency is difficult. 2. **Operational Overhead** тАФ Requires mature DevOps practices: containerization, orchestration (Kubernetes), monitoring, and CI/CD pipelines. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023]** **Q. (a)** Explain **inter-service communication** in microservices. Differentiate between **synchronous (REST/gRPC)** and **asynchronous (Message Queues/Event-driven)** communication with examples. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Introduction (1.5 marks):** In a microservice architecture, services run in **separate processes** (often on different machines/containers). They cannot call each other like in-process functions. They must use **network-based communication**, which falls into two paradigms: **Synchronous** and **Asynchronous**. **Synchronous Communication (2.5 marks):** - The caller **sends a request and waits** for the response. - **REST over HTTP** тАФ Most common. Uses JSON payload. Example: *Order Service* calls *Payment Service* via `POST /payments`. - **gRPC** тАФ High-performance; uses Protocol Buffers (binary). Example: Internal service-to-service calls in Netflix. - **Pros:** Simple, easy to debug, immediate response. - **Cons:** Tight coupling in time; if the called service is slow/down, the caller is blocked. **Asynchronous Communication (2.5 marks):** - The caller **publishes an event/message** to a broker and **does not wait** for a response. - **Message Queues** (RabbitMQ, ActiveMQ) тАФ Point-to-point. - **Event Streams** (Apache Kafka, AWS Kinesis) тАФ Pub-sub. - Example: When an order is placed, *Order Service* publishes `OrderCreated` to Kafka. *Payment*, *Inventory*, and *Notification* services independently consume it. - **Pros:** Loose coupling, resilience (broker holds messages if consumer is down), high scalability. - **Cons:** Harder to debug; eventual consistency; message ordering issues. **Comparison Table (0.5 marks):** | Aspect | Synchronous | Asynchronous | |--------|-------------|--------------| | Wait | Caller waits | Caller doesn't wait | | Coupling | Time-coupled | Time-decoupled | | Tools | REST, gRPC | Kafka, RabbitMQ | | Use Case | Queries, immediate data | Events, workflows | --- **Q. (b)** Explain the following microservice design patterns: **(i) API Gateway**, **(ii) Service Registry & Discovery**, **(iii) Circuit Breaker**. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(i) API Gateway (2.5 marks):** The **API Gateway** acts as a **single entry point** for all client requests in a microservice system. It receives the request from the client, authenticates it, applies rate-limiting, and routes it to the appropriate backend service. It can also aggregate responses from multiple services. - **Example Tools:** Kong, NGINX, AWS API Gateway, Spring Cloud Gateway. - **Benefits:** Simplifies client logic, centralizes cross-cutting concerns (auth, logging, SSL termination), and hides internal service topology. **(ii) Service Registry & Discovery (2.5 marks):** In a dynamic environment (like containers in Kubernetes), service instances **come and go**. A **Service Registry** is a database of running service instances and their network locations (IP + port). When a service starts, it **registers** itself; when it dies, it **deregisters**. - **Example Tools:** Netflix Eureka, HashiCorp Consul, Apache Zookeeper. - **Workflow:** Service A needs to call Service B тЖТ Service A queries the Registry тЖТ gets B's current address тЖТ makes the call. - **Benefit:** Avoids hardcoding URLs; supports dynamic scaling. **(iii) Circuit Breaker (2 marks):** The **Circuit Breaker** pattern prevents **cascading failures**. It wraps a remote call in a monitor. If calls to a downstream service fail repeatedly (e.g., 5 failures in 10 seconds), the circuit **opens** and immediately fails fast (without calling the service) for a cooldown period. After the cooldown, it goes to **half-open**, allowing trial calls. - **States:** `Closed` (normal) тЖТ `Open` (failing fast) тЖТ `Half-Open` (testing). - **Example Tools:** Resilience4j, Hystrix (legacy). - **Benefit:** Protects the system from one slow/failing service dragging down the entire app. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall тАФ Common Mark Loss Areas:** > 1. **Vague Definitions** тАФ Writing "microservices is small services" is worth 0 marks. Use *Fowler's definition* with terms like "loosely coupled", "bounded context", "polyglot". > 2. **Missing the 'Why'** тАФ Don't just list advantages. Always link advantage тЖТ problem it solves (e.g., "Fault isolation solves single point of failure"). > 3. **Confusing SOA with Microservices** тАФ SOA uses an **Enterprise Service Bus (ESB)**, heavy WS-* standards; microservices use **lightweight HTTP/gRPC, smart endpoints, dumb pipes**. > 4. **Forgetting Trade-offs** тАФ Always mention **challenges** alongside advantages. KTU loves balanced answers. > 5. **Skipping the Diagram in 7-mark questions** тАФ A missing diagram in Q.(b) of Part B = **тИТ1.5 to тИТ2 marks** guaranteed loss. > 6. **Not relating to OOP** тАФ Always mention **SRP, encapsulation, loose coupling, high cohesion** as the OOP foundation of microservices. This is *free marks* on KTU OOP papers. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Notes тАФ Read this 30 minutes before the exam.** - тнР **Definition:** Microservices = small, autonomous, business-focused, independently deployable services that communicate via lightweight protocols. - тнР **Origin:** Term coined ~2011; popularized by **Martin Fowler & James Lewis (2014)**. - тнР **Monolith = Single Unit; Microservices = Suite of Units.** - тнР **9 Key Characteristics** (Fowler & Lewis): Componentization, Business Capability, Decentralized Governance, Decentralized Data, Infrastructure Automation, Smart Endpoints & Dumb Pipes, Design for Failure, Evolutionary Design, Independent Deployment. - тнР **Database per Service** is a *golden rule* тАФ no shared schemas. - тнР **Communication:** Synchronous (REST, gRPC) vs Asynchronous (Kafka, RabbitMQ). - тнР **API Gateway** = single entry point; handles routing, auth, rate-limiting. - тнР **Service Registry** = dynamic directory of services (Eureka, Consul). - тнР **Circuit Breaker** = fault isolation pattern; states: Closed тЖТ Open тЖТ Half-Open. - тнР **Saga Pattern** = distributed transactions via local + compensating transactions. - тнР **Bounded Context** (from DDD) = boundary of a microservice. - тнР **Polyglot** = each service uses its own best-fit technology. - тнР **OOP Connection:** Microservices apply **SRP** (one service, one responsibility) and **Loose Coupling / High Cohesion** at the *system* level. - тнР **Big Companies using it:** Netflix (700+ services), Amazon, Uber, Spotify, Flipkart. - тнР **Martin Fowler's Rule:** "Monolith First" тАФ start with a monolith, then decompose as complexity grows. - тнР **Common Anti-patterns to avoid:** *Distributed Monolith* (tightly coupled services), *Chatty Microservices* (too many sync calls), *Shared Database*. **Best One-Line Exam Answer:** > *"Microservice Architecture is a style that structures an application as a collection of small, loosely coupled, independently deployable services, each owning its data and organized around a single business capability, communicating through lightweight protocols like HTTP/REST or message brokers."*

topics.title: Object-Oriented Programming in Java - Declaring Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Object-Oriented Programming in Java - Declaring Objects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Java, an **object** is a runtime instance of a class stored in the **heap memory** that encapsulates both **state** (fields/attributes) and **behaviour** (methods). **Declaring an object** is the two-stage process of (i) declaring a **reference variable** (a handle that lives on the stack) and (ii) **instantiating** the object using the `new` keyword, which allocates memory on the heap and invokes a constructor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** "Object-Oriented Programming in Java тАФ Declaring Objects" is treated as the gateway concept that links *class design* with *memory reality*. The examiner expects the student to distinguish **declaration** (compile-time activity on the stack) from **instantiation** (run-time activity on the heap).

The general syntax used in the KTU board is:

```java
className  referenceVariable;          // Step 1 тАФ Declaration
referenceVariable = new className();    // Step 2 тАФ Instantiation
```

or the combined single-line form:

```java
className referenceVariable = new className();
```

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **TV Remote** (the *class*) and you buy a *new physical remote control* (the *object*) for your TV. The moment you sign the purchase receipt, you get a **warranty card with a serial number** (the *reference variable*). The serial number on the card is **not** the remote itself тАФ it merely *points* to the physical remote lying in your hand.

| Real-world entity | Java equivalent |
|---|---|
| Blueprint of a remote | `class Remote { тАж }` |
| The actual physical remote you hold | The **object** in heap memory |
| Serial number on the warranty card | The **reference variable** on the stack |
| Writing the serial number on the card | The **declaration** step |
| Manufacturing and handing over the remote | The **instantiation** step (`new`) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mental Model to Lock-In:** A reference variable in Java is **never** the object тАФ it is a *remote control for the remote control*. Lose the reference, and the object becomes unreachable (eligible for **garbage collection**).

### 1.3 Key Terminology Box

> [!NOTE]
> - **Class** тАФ A blueprint/template describing what an object will look like.
> - **Object** тАФ A concrete runtime entity occupying heap memory.
> - **Reference Variable** тАФ A stack-resident pointer that stores the memory address of a heap object.
> - **Instantiation** тАФ The act of bringing a class's blueprint to life by allocating memory via `new`.
> - **Constructor** тАФ A special method implicitly invoked by `new` to initialise the freshly allocated object.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Logical Steps Behind `new`

When the JVM executes the statement `Student s1 = new Student();`, it performs three internal operations. These are **high-yield** for KTU theory questions.

1. **Declaration Phase (Compile-time, Stack Memory)**
   - The compiler allocates a 64-bit (on modern JVMs) slot on the *stack frame* of the current method, named `s1`.
   - The slot is initially filled with the default reference value `null`. **No object exists yet.**

2. **Instantiation Phase (Run-time, Heap Memory)**
   - The `new` operator calculates the memory required by adding the sizes of all **instance fields** (including inherited fields), aligned to an 8-byte boundary.
   - The JVM allocates that many contiguous bytes on the **heap** and zero-initialises every byte (numeric fields тЖТ `0`, boolean тЖТ `false`, references тЖТ `null`).
   - The address of the allocated block is returned as a `reference` value.

3. **Initialisation Phase (Run-time, Constructor Invocation)**
   - The constructor `Student()` is invoked on the newly allocated heap object.
   - Any explicit initialisers and the constructor body run **top-to-bottom**.
   - After the constructor returns, the reference (address) produced in step 2 is stored into the stack slot `s1`.

### 2.2 Why Two Steps? Why Not One?

Java separates *declaration* from *instantiation* so that:

- A reference can be created **before** the actual object exists (useful for null-checking, lazy creation, dependency injection).
- Multiple references can point to the **same** object (aliasing).
- An object can exist **without any reference** for a brief window during construction.

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat-Sheet Table

| Concept | Stack (Reference) | Heap (Object) |
|---|---|---|
| When does it exist? | When the enclosing method is on the call-stack | When `new` executes |
| Size | Fixed (4 bytes on 32-bit JVM, **8 bytes** on 64-bit with compressed oops disabled) | Sum of all instance fields + 16-byte object header |
| Default value | `null` | Fields zero-initialised (`0`, `false`, `null`) |
| Lifetime | Until method returns (LIFO unwind) | Until **Garbage Collector** reclaims it |
| Access | Direct via local variable name | Only through a reference |

| Operation | Syntax | Memory Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Declare reference only | `Student s;` | Stack slot created, value `null` |
| Declare + instantiate | `Student s = new Student();` | Stack slot + heap block + constructor call |
| Assign existing reference | `Student t = s;` | New stack slot pointing to **same** heap object |
| Lose reference | `s = null;` | Original heap object becomes GC-eligible if no other refs exist |
| Compare references | `s == t` | Compares **addresses**, not content |
| Compare content | `s.equals(t)` | Logical / semantic comparison (requires override) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** `==` compares **memory addresses** (reference equality), whereas `.equals()` compares **logical content**. KTU examiners *love* asking the difference.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

The split-stack/heap model enables:

- **Memory safety** тАФ local references vanish automatically when the method exits; no dangling pointers.
- **Garbage collection** тАФ the JVM can prove an object is unreachable by scanning the stack and live references.
- **Polymorphic dispatch** тАФ the JVM uses the actual class stored in the heap object's header (the *vtable pointer*) to resolve overridden methods at runtime.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example тАФ Tracking Memory Through Code

Consider the following canonical KTU textbook snippet:

```java
class Box {
    double width, height, depth;

    Box(double w, double h, double d) {
        width  = w;
        height = h;
        depth  = d;
    }

    double volume() {
        return width * height * depth;
    }
}

class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Box b1;                                 // line 1
        b1 = new Box(2.0, 3.0, 4.0);            // line 2
        Box b2 = b1;                            // line 3
        b1 = null;                              // line 4
    }
}
```

Let us **derive** the state of memory after every line.

---

#### After Line 1: `Box b1;`

- The compiler has reserved 8 bytes (on a typical 64-bit JVM with compressed oops) on `main`'s stack frame, labelled `b1`.
- The slot holds the literal value `null` (i.e., `0x0000000000000000`).

$$
\text{Stack}_{main} = \{\, b1 \mapsto \texttt{null} \,\}
$$

- **Heap is untouched.**

---

#### After Line 2: `b1 = new Box(2.0, 3.0, 4.0);`

Step-by-step internal execution:

1. The JVM computes the size required:

$$
\text{size} = \underbrace{16}_{\text{object header}} + \underbrace{8}_{width} + \underbrace{8}_{height} + \underbrace{8}_{depth} = 40 \text{ bytes}
$$

2. 40 contiguous bytes are allocated on the **Young Generation** of the heap (Eden space).
3. All instance fields are zero-initialised: `width = 0.0`, `height = 0.0`, `depth = 0.0`.
4. The constructor `Box(double, double, double)` is invoked. Inside the constructor, the implicit parameter `this` points to the new heap object.
5. Assignments execute:

$$
\text{width} \leftarrow 2.0 \quad\quad \text{height} \leftarrow 3.0 \quad\quad \text{depth} \leftarrow 4.0
$$

6. The constructor returns the heap address, say `0x7F3A`. This address is written into stack slot `b1`.

$$
\text{Stack}_{main} = \{\, b1 \mapsto \texttt{0x7F3A} \,\}
$$

$$
\text{Heap}_{0x7F3A} = \{\, \text{width}=2.0,\ \text{height}=3.0,\ \text{depth}=4.0 \,\}
$$

---

#### After Line 3: `Box b2 = b1;`

- A new 8-byte slot `b2` is created on the stack.
- The current value of `b1` (i.e., `0x7F3A`) is **copied** into `b2`.

$$
\text{Stack}_{main} = \{\, b1 \mapsto \texttt{0x7F3A},\ b2 \mapsto \texttt{0x7F3A} \,\}
$$

- **No new object is created.** Both `b1` and `b2` now *alias* the same heap entity. Mutating fields via `b1` is observable through `b2` and vice-versa.

---

#### After Line 4: `b1 = null;`

- Stack slot `b1` is overwritten with `null`.

$$
\text{Stack}_{main} = \{\, b1 \mapsto \texttt{null},\ b2 \mapsto \texttt{0x7F3A} \,\}
$$

- The heap object at `0x7F3A` is **still reachable** via `b2`, so the **Garbage Collector will NOT reclaim it**.
- The object becomes GC-eligible only when `b2` also goes out of scope (i.e., when `main` returns).

---

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix тАФ When to Use What?

| Scenario | Recommended Declaration | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Object needed immediately with valid state | `Box b = new Box(2,3,4);` | Eager initialisation тАФ fails fast |
| Object creation may be skipped (e.g., conditional) | `Box b = null;` then `b = new Box(...)` later | Avoids unnecessary heap pressure |
| Two handles to one shared object | `Box a = new Box(); Box b = a;` | Reference aliasing тАФ cheaper than cloning |
| Object created inside an `if` branch | `Box b; if(cond){ b = new Box(); }` | Keeps `b` accessible after the block |

### 3.3 Numeric Summary Table (Memory Footprint)

| Declaration style | Stack bytes used | Heap bytes used | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Box b;` | 8 | 0 | Reference only, safe default |
| `Box b = null;` | 8 | 0 | Identical effect as above |
| `Box b = new Box();` | 8 | **48** (16 header + 3├Ч8 fields + padding to 8) | Full object + reference |
| `Box a = new Box(); Box b = a;` | 16 | 48 | One object, two references |
| `Box a = new Box(); Box b = new Box();` | 16 | 96 | Two independent objects |

> [!NOTE]
> The exact header size is JVM-implementation dependent (HotSpot uses 12 bytes + 4 bytes padding for 64-bit with compressed oops, or 16 bytes without). The **qualitative reasoning** is what examiners reward.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Memory-Model Schematic (Stack vs Heap)

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the state of memory at the end of the `Demo.main` execution described in ┬з3.1.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph STK["STACK FRAME of main"]
        b1["b1 = null"]
        b2["b2 = 0x7F3A"]
    end

    subgraph HEAP["HEAP MEMORY"]
        objA["OBJECT Box @ 0x7F3A"]
        hdr["Object Header + Vtable Pointer"]
        w["width  = 2.0"]
        h["height = 3.0"]
        d["depth  = 4.0"]
    end

    GC["Garbage Collector Watches Live References"]

    objA --- hdr
    objA --- w
    objA --- h
    objA --- d

    b2 -. points to .-> objA
    STK -. scanned by .-> GC
    HEAP -. scanned by .-> GC

    style STK fill:#e8f4ff,stroke:#1f6feb,stroke-width:2px
    style HEAP fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#f76707,stroke-width:2px
    style objA fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44,stroke-width:2px
    style b1 fill:#ffe3e3,stroke:#c92a2a
    style b2 fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44
    style GC fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#495057,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

### 4.2 Flow-Chart тАФ The Lifecycle of an Object Reference

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Class definition loaded by JVM"] --> B["Source code: Box b;"]
    B --> C{"Compile-time check"}
    C -->|Type exists| D["Stack slot 'b' reserved, value = null"]
    C -->|Type missing| E["COMPILE ERROR: cannot find symbol"]

    D --> F["Source code: b = new Box 2.0, 3.0, 4.0"]
    F --> G["new operator computes size: 16 + 3*8 = 40 bytes"]
    G --> H["Allocate 40 bytes on heap Eden"]
    H --> I["Zero-initialise all instance fields"]
    I --> J["Invoke constructor Box w, h, d"]
    J --> K["Constructor assigns width, height, depth"]
    K --> L["Constructor returns heap address 0x7F3A"]
    L --> M["Store 0x7F3A into stack slot 'b'"]
    M --> N["Object is now LIVE and reachable"]

    N --> O["Use object: b.volume, b.width etc."]
    O --> P{"Reference reassigned or method ends?"}
    P -->|"b = null"| Q["Original heap object becomes GC-eligible"]
    P -->|"Reference goes out of scope"| R["Stack slot popped, object GC-eligible"]
    P -->|"b = new Box..."| S["New heap block allocated, b points to new object"]
    Q --> T["Garbage Collector reclaims memory"]
    R --> T
    S --> N

    style A fill:#e7f5ff,stroke:#1971c2
    style E fill:#ffe3e3,stroke:#c92a2a
    style N fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44
    style T fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#f08c00
```

### 4.3 Reference-Aliasing Schematic

This diagram clarifies the **crucial difference** between copying a reference and copying an object тАФ a frequent KTU short-answer topic.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE: Box b2 = b1"]
        s1A["STACK: b1"] -.->|"0x7F3A"| oA["HEAP: Box"]
        s2A["STACK: b2"] -.->|"uninitialised"| xA[" "]
    end

    subgraph AFTER["AFTER: Box b2 = b1"]
        s1B["STACK: b1"] ==>|"0x7F3A"| oB["HEAP: Box width 2.0 height 3.0 depth 4.0"]
        s2B["STACK: b2"] ==>|"0x7F3A"| oB
    end

    BEFORE --> AFTER

    style BEFORE fill:#fff5f5,stroke:#c92a2a
    style AFTER fill:#ebfbee,stroke:#2b8a3e
    style oB fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A тАФ 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam тАФ May 2024]**

> Distinguish between *declaring a reference* and *instantiating an object* in Java. What happens in memory during each step? **(3 marks)**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Declaration** of a reference is a *compile-time* operation in which the JVM reserves a slot (typically **8 bytes** on a 64-bit JVM) on the current method's **stack frame**. The slot is initialised to `null`. No heap memory is touched. *(1 mark)*
2. **Instantiation** is a *run-time* operation triggered by the `new` keyword. The JVM allocates a contiguous block on the **heap**, zero-initialises all instance fields, invokes the matching constructor, and finally stores the resulting heap address into the stack-resident reference variable. *(1.5 marks)*
3. Syntax-wise, the two can be combined (`Box b = new Box();`) but logically they remain two distinct phases. *(0.5 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023]**

> What is the difference between `s1 == s2` and `s1.equals(s2)` when `s1` and `s2` are references to objects? **(3 marks)**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- `s1 == s2` performs a **reference (address) comparison**. It returns `true` only if both references point to the *exact same heap object*. *(1 mark)*
- `s1.equals(s2)` performs a **logical / content comparison**. By default (from `Object`) it behaves like `==`, but classes such as `String`, `Integer`, and user-defined classes can (and should) **override** it to compare internal state. *(1.5 marks)*
- Practical implication: two `Student` objects with identical `name` and `rollNo` fields will yield `==` as `false` (different addresses) but may yield `.equals()` as `true` if properly overridden. *(0.5 marks)*

---

### Part B тАФ 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A тАФ 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam тАФ Jul 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram how an object is created in Java using the `new` keyword. Differentiate between *stack* and *heap* memory during object creation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate **reference aliasing** тАФ i.e., that two references can point to the same object, and changes made through one reference are visible through the other. Show the memory layout. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Steps in Object Creation (7 marks)**

The execution of `Box b = new Box(2.0, 3.0, 4.0);` proceeds in **three logical stages**:

1. **Declaration** тАФ The compiler reserves a 64-bit slot on the stack named `b`, initialised to `null`. *(1 mark)*
2. **Memory Allocation & Instantiation** тАФ The `new` operator computes the heap size:

$$
\text{size} = \text{header} + \sum \text{instance fields} = 16 + 3 \times 8 = 40\ \text{bytes}
$$

and allocates them on the heap (Eden space). *(2 marks)*

3. **Initialisation via Constructor** тАФ All fields are zero-initialised first; then the constructor runs, assigning `width=2.0`, `height=3.0`, `depth=4.0`. The address is returned and stored in `b`. *(2 marks)*

| Stack | Heap |
|---|---|
| Fast, LIFO, automatic cleanup | Slower, GC-managed, shared pool |
| Holds **references** & primitives | Holds **objects** |
| 8-byte slot for `b` | 40-byte `Box` instance |

*(2 marks for the table + diagram drawn on answer sheet тАФ must label stack, heap, reference arrow, and field values.)*

---

**(b) Reference Aliasing Demo (7 marks)**

```java
class Student {
    String name;
    int    marks;

    Student(String n, int m) {
        name  = n;
        marks = m;
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println(name + " : " + marks);
    }
}

class AliasDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student("Anu", 85);
        Student s2 = s1;                 // <-- aliasing: no new object created

        System.out.println("Before mutation:");
        s1.display();
        s2.display();

        s1.marks = 95;                   // mutate via s1

        System.out.println("After mutating s1.marks = 95:");
        s1.display();
        s2.display();                    // change visible through s2 also

        System.out.println("s1 == s2 ? " + (s1 == s2));   // true
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**

```
Before mutation:
Anu : 85
Anu : 85
After mutating s1.marks = 95:
Anu : 95
Anu : 95
s1 == s2 ? true
```

**Memory Layout (to be drawn on answer sheet тАФ 2 marks):**

- A single `Student` object on the heap with `name="Anu"`, `marks=95`.
- Two stack references `s1` and `s2`, both pointing to the **same** heap address.

**Explanation of marks (3 marks for explanation):**
- `Student s2 = s1;` does **not** create a new object; it merely copies the reference. *(1 mark)*
- Therefore `s1 == s2` evaluates to `true`. *(1 mark)*
- Mutating fields via `s1` is observable through `s2` because both references address the same memory. *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B тАФ 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam тАФ Jul 2024]**

**(a)** What is the role of a **constructor** during object declaration and instantiation? Discuss the difference between *default* and *parameterised* constructors with an example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a code snippet the difference between *primitive* variables and *reference* variables in Java. What are the default values assigned to each? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Role of Constructor + Default vs Parameterised (7 marks)**

A **constructor** is a special member function whose name is identical to the class name and which has **no return type** (not even `void`). It is automatically invoked by the `new` operator immediately after heap allocation. Its primary roles are: *(2 marks)*

1. **Initialising** instance fields to meaningful values (instead of zero-defaults).
2. **Enforcing** invariants тАФ e.g., ensuring `marks` is never negative.
3. **Acquiring resources** тАФ e.g., opening a file or socket.

**Default vs Parameterised Constructor:**

```java
class Account {
    int    accNo;
    String holder;
    double balance;

    // Default (no-arg) constructor
    Account() {
        accNo   = 0;
        holder  = "Unknown";
        balance = 0.0;
    }

    // Parameterised constructor
    Account(int a, String h, double b) {
        accNo   = a;
        holder  = h;
        balance = b;
    }
}
```

| Aspect | Default Constructor | Parameterised Constructor |
|---|---|---|
| Arguments | None | One or more |
| Invocation | `new Account()` | `new Account(101, "Ria", 5000.0)` |
| Flexibility | Always produces same shape | Each object can be unique |
| Compiler behaviour | Auto-supplied **only if** no constructor is defined | Never auto-supplied |
| Marks distribution | (2 marks) | (2 marks) |
| Real-world analogy | Blank form | Pre-filled form |

*(1 mark for the example compilation & output prediction)*

---

**(b) Primitive vs Reference Variables (7 marks)**

```java
class PrimitiveVsRef {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int    a = 10,    b = a;     // primitive copy
        Box    x = new Box(),  y = x; // reference copy

        a = 20;
        x.width = 50;

        System.out.println("a = " + a + ", b = " + b);   // 20, 10
        System.out.println("x.width = " + x.width + ", y.width = " + y.width); // 50, 50
    }
}
```

**Default Values Table (3 marks):**

| Data Type | Default Value |
|---|---|
| `byte`, `short`, `int`, `long` | `0` |
| `float`, `double` | `0.0` |
| `char` | `'\u0000'` |
| `boolean` | `false` |
| Any reference type (`String`, custom class, array) | `null` |

**Differences (3 marks):**

| Property | Primitive Variable | Reference Variable |
|---|---|---|
| Stores | Actual value | Memory address of object |
| Memory location | Stack | Reference on stack, object on heap |
| Default value | Type-specific (`0`, `false`, тАж) | `null` |
| Can be `null`? | **No** | Yes |
| Copy semantics | **Value copy** (independent) | **Address copy** (aliased) |
| Size | Fixed (e.g., `int` = 4 bytes) | Typically 8 bytes (compressed: 4) |

**Conclusion:** When `b = a;` is executed for primitives, the value `10` is duplicated. When `y = x;` is executed for references, only the *address* is duplicated тАФ both `x` and `y` still point to the **same** heap object, hence the field mutation is visible through both. *(1 mark)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps тАФ Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Confusing `new` with declaration:** Students often write "`new Box()` creates the variable `b`." This is **wrong**. `new` allocates the *object*; `Box b` creates the *reference*. The KTU key awards 1 mark specifically for this distinction.
> 2. **Confusing stack with heap:** A frequent answer says "the object is created on the stack." Partial credit only тАФ the *reference* lives on the stack; the *object* lives on the heap.
> 3. **Comparing strings with `==`:** In code snippets, students use `s1 == s2` to compare two `String` contents. The examiner deducts marks unless they justify it (e.g., interned literals). Always prefer `.equals()`.
> 4. **Forgetting the no-return-type rule for constructors:** Writing `void Box()` instead of `Box()` costs the constructor-definition mark.
> 5. **Ignoring default initialisation:** Many students believe fields are left "uninitialised" after `new`. They are, in fact, **zero-initialised** by the JVM *before* the constructor body runs.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- An **object declaration** in Java is a **two-step** process: declare a reference, then instantiate with `new`. *(High-yield!)*
- The reference lives on the **stack**, the object on the **heap**.
- `new` performs three jobs: allocate, zero-initialise, invoke constructor.
- Default field values: numeric тЖТ `0`, boolean тЖТ `false`, references тЖТ `null`.
- **Reference aliasing** (`Box b2 = b1;`) does **not** clone the object тАФ both references point to the **same** heap entity.
- `==` compares addresses; `.equals()` compares content (if overridden).
- An object becomes GC-eligible only when **no live reference** points to it.
- Constructors have **no return type** and bear the **same name as the class**.
- A **default constructor** is auto-supplied *only* if the programmer defines no constructor at all.
- Memory sizes: object header тЙИ **16 bytes**; each `double` field = **8 bytes**; each `int` field = **4 bytes**; reference slot тЙИ **8 bytes** (4 with compressed oops).
- Difference between *primitive* and *reference* variable copying is **value-copy vs address-copy** тАФ a classic KTU 3-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object Reference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Object Reference ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) In Object Oriented Programming (OOP), an **Object Reference** is a typed memory handle (a variable) that stores the **heap-memory address** of an object rather than the object's data itself. In Java (the reference language for PBCST304), reference variables do not contain the object тАФ they contain a **remote pointer** to the object living in the **heap area** of the JVM-managed runtime data area. The formal KTU definition is: > An **Object Reference** is a variable of a class type that holds the memory address of an instance (object) of that class, enabling indirect access to the object's instance variables and instance methods through the dot (`.`) operator. A `null` reference is a special reference variable that points to **no object** in memory. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In Java, primitive types (`int`, `float`, `char`, `boolean`, etc.) are **not references** тАФ they store actual values directly on the **stack**. Only class-type variables (objects, arrays, interfaces) are references stored as addresses pointing to heap-allocated entities. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have a **TV remote control** in your hand. - The **TV** = the actual object (with all its internal circuitry, screen, speakers тАФ i.e., its state and behavior). - The **Remote** = the reference variable in your hand. The remote is **not** the TV. - The **Infrared signal / Channel** = the memory address linking the remote to a *specific* TV. - **Pressing a button** (e.g., volume up) = invoking a method using the dot operator `tvRemote.volumeUp()`. - A **remote with no TV paired** = a `null` reference. Pressing a button on it throws a `NullPointerException`. > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** Just as one remote can be **re-paired** to a different TV, a reference variable can be **reassigned** to point to a different object of a compatible type. The original TV is unaffected unless garbage-collected. ### 1.3 Core Properties of an Object Reference | Property | Description | | :--- | :--- | | **Type Safety** | A reference is bound to a specific class/interface type at compile time. | | **Indirect Access** | All operations on the object go through the reference using `.` | | **Reassignable** | A reference can be redirected to another object (subject to inheritance rules). | | **Nullable** | A reference can explicitly hold the value `null` (no object). | | **Pass-by-Value (of the address)** | When passed to a method, the reference itself is copied, but both copies point to the same object. | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Concept тАФ How an Object Reference Works (Step-by-Step) 1. **Class Declaration** тАФ The compiler reads the class blueprint. No memory is allocated for objects yet. 2. **Reference Variable Declaration** тАФ `Student s1;` reserves a slot on the **stack** named `s1`, currently `null`. 3. **Object Instantiation** тАФ `new Student();` triggers the JVM to allocate memory on the **heap** for the object's instance variables and initialize it via the constructor. 4. **Binding** тАФ The `=` operator stores the **heap address** of the newly created object into the reference variable `s1`. 5. **Access** тАФ Statements like `s1.name = "Anu";` use the dot operator to dereference the address and modify the actual heap object. 6. **Reassignment** тАФ `s1 = new Student();` creates a *new* object; `s1` now points to it. The old object becomes eligible for **Garbage Collection** if no other reference points to it. ### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step - **Why a separate stack + heap?** Stack frames are short-lived and LIFO-managed; objects often outlive the method that created them, so they live in the heap. - **Why pass-by-value of the address?** Java's language specification mandates *value semantics for everything*, including references. This avoids C++-style pointer arithmetic and dangling-pointer bugs. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following symbolic relationships are exam-ready. The symbol $\rightarrow$ denotes "points to / references." | Concept | Symbolic Form | Explanation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Reference Assignment | $r_1 \rightarrow O_1$ | Reference $r_1$ now references object $O_1$ | | Aliasing | $r_1 \rightarrow O_1 \;\wedge\; r_2 \rightarrow O_1$ | Two references, one object (shared state) | | Null Reference | $r_1 \rightarrow \emptyset$ | $r_1$ references nothing (null) | | Reference Comparison | $r_1 \;==\; r_2 \iff r_1 \rightarrow O_i \;\wedge\; r_2 \rightarrow O_i$ | `==` checks address equality, not content | | Object Count for GC | $N_{alive} = \sum \mathbb{1}(r_i \rightarrow O)$ | Object becomes GC-eligible when $N_{alive} = 0$ | | Reference Size (typical JVM) | $S_{ref} = 4 \;\text{or}\; 8 \;\text{bytes}$ | Compressed OOPs: 4 bytes; standard: 8 bytes (64-bit JVM) | > [!NOTE] > In the comparison formula above, the symbol $\emptyset$ denotes the **empty set** (used here as "no object") тАФ equivalent to Java's `null`. We use $\mid$ within formulas only via $\mid$ written as a math operator, never breaking the markdown table. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **GUI Frameworks (Swing/JavaFX):** Every `JButton`, `JFrame` is accessed through a reference passed to event listeners. - **Database Connectivity (JDBC):** `Connection`, `Statement`, `ResultSet` are all reference types тАФ crucial for resource management. - **Spring / Dependency Injection:** Beans are wired through object references at runtime; understanding references is foundational to inversion-of-control. - **Android Development:** `Activity`, `View`, and `Context` are all references тАФ leaks occur when references outlive their intended lifecycle. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Trace тАФ Full Lifecycle of a Reference Consider the following KTU-standard Java snippet: ```java class Student { String name; int rollNo; } public class DemoRef { public static void main(String[] args) { Student s1 = new Student(); // Step 1 s1.name = "Anu"; // Step 2 s1.rollNo = 101; // Step 3 Student s2 = s1; // Step 4 тАФ aliasing s2.name = "Manu"; // Step 5 тАФ modifies shared object s1 = new Student(); // Step 6 тАФ s1 redirected s1.name = "Vinu"; // Step 7 Student s3 = null; // Step 8 тАФ null reference // s3.name = "X"; // Would throw NullPointerException } } ``` #### Step-by-Step Logical Walkthrough **Step 1 тАФ Declaration + Instantiation** A new `Student` object is allocated on the **heap**. Let us denote the heap address as $\text{addr} = 0x2001$. The reference `s1` is stored on the **stack**. $$ s_1 \rightarrow \text{Object}_A \quad \text{at address } 0x2001 $$ **Step 2 тАФ State mutation via reference** $$ s_1.\text{name} = \text{"Anu"} \implies \text{Object}_A.\text{name} = \text{"Anu"} $$ **Step 3 тАФ State mutation via reference** $$ s_1.\text{rollNo} = 101 \implies \text{Object}_A.\text{rollNo} = 101 $$ **Step 4 тАФ Aliasing** The value stored in `s1` (the address `0x2001`) is **copied** into `s2`. Both references now point to the **same** object. $$ s_1 \rightarrow \text{Object}_A \quad \wedge \quad s_2 \rightarrow \text{Object}_A $$ $$ \text{Heap address of Object}_A = 0x2001 $$ **Step 5 тАФ Mutating through the alias** Any mutation via `s2` is reflected when accessed via `s1`, because they reference the *same* memory. $$ s_2.\text{name} = \text{"Manu"} \implies \text{Object}_A.\text{name} = \text{"Manu"} $$ Verification: $$ s_1.\text{name} == \text{"Manu"} \;\; \text{is true (same memory cell)} $$ **Step 6 тАФ Reassignment of `s1`** A brand-new `Student` object is created at heap address $0x3002$. The reference `s1` is overwritten with this new address. $$ s_1 \rightarrow \text{Object}_B \quad \text{at address } 0x3002 $$ $$ s_2 \rightarrow \text{Object}_A \quad \text{(unchanged, still at } 0x2001\text{)} $$ **Step 7 тАФ Populating the new object** $$ s_1.\text{name} = \text{"Vinu"} \implies \text{Object}_B.\text{name} = \text{"Vinu"} $$ **Step 8 тАФ Null reference** `s3` is declared but not assigned an object. Its stack value is the special sentinel `null` (typically `0x0`). $$ s_3 \rightarrow \emptyset $$ Any dereference (e.g., `s3.name`) throws `java.lang.NullPointerException`. ### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix тАФ Primitive vs Reference | Aspect | Primitive Variable | Reference Variable | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Stored on | Stack | Stack (the address); object on Heap | | Holds | Actual value (e.g., $42$) | Heap address of an object | | Default value | $0$, $0.0$, `false`, `\u0000` | `null` | | Can be `null`? | No | Yes | | `==` semantics | Compares values | Compares addresses (reference equality) | | Size | Fixed (e.g., $4$ bytes for `int`) | $4$ or $8$ bytes (address) | | Example | `int x = 10;` | `Student s = new Student();` | ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix тАФ `==` vs `.equals()` | Operation | On References | Result | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `s1 == s2` | $s_1 \rightarrow O_i \;\wedge\; s_2 \rightarrow O_i$ | `true` iff **same address** | | `s1.equals(s2)` | Calls `Object.equals()` (or overridden) | `true` iff **logically equal** (content-based) | | `s1 == null` | $s_1 \rightarrow \emptyset$ | `true` iff uninitialized/null | > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners frequently test this distinction. By default, `==` performs **shallow reference comparison** unless the class overrides `equals()` (and `hashCode()`) тАФ for example, the `String` class overrides `equals()` to compare character sequences. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Memory Architecture тАФ Stack vs Heap with References ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph STK["STACK FRAME - main method"] s1Var["s1 : Student
addr = 0x3002"] s2Var["s2 : Student
addr = 0x2001"] s3Var["s3 : Student
value = null"] end subgraph HEAP["HEAP MEMORY - JVM"] objA["Object A : Student
name = Manu
rollNo = 101
at 0x2001"] objB["Object B : Student
name = Vinu
rollNo = 0
at 0x3002"] end s2Var -.-> objA s1Var -.-> objB classDef stack fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000 classDef heap fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 classDef nullStyle fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 class s1Var,s2Var stack class objA,objB heap class s3Var nullStyle ``` > **Figure Caption (for answer scripts):** `s1` and `s2` are stack-resident reference variables. `s1` points to Object B; `s2` points to Object A (aliasing occurred earlier and then `s1` was reassigned). `s3` holds the `null` sentinel, denoting absence of any heap object. ### 4.2 Reference Reassignment & Garbage Collection Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Step 1: Student s1 = new Student()
s1 points to ObjectA"] B["Step 2: s1.name = 'Anu'
ObjectA.name = Anu"] C["Step 3: Student s2 = s1
Both point to ObjectA"] D["Step 4: s1 = new Student()
s1 now points to ObjectB"] E["Step 5: ObjectA has zero references
Eligible for Garbage Collection"] F["Step 6: s3 = null
s3 points to no object"] A --> B --> C --> D --> E D --> F classDef state fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 classDef gc fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 class A,B,C,D,F state class E gc ``` ### 4.3 Reference Type Hierarchy (for KTU exam diagrams) ```mermaid flowchart TB javaRef["java.lang.Object"] classRef["Class Type Reference
e.g. Student s"] ifaceRef["Interface Type Reference
e.g. Comparable c"] arrRef["Array Type Reference
e.g. int arr"] enumRef["Enum Type Reference
e.g. Color c"] javaRef --> classRef javaRef --> ifaceRef javaRef --> arrRef javaRef --> enumRef classDef root fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 classDef leaf fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 class javaRef root class classRef,ifaceRef,arrRef,enumRef leaf ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. Define an object reference. How is it different from a primitive variable?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** An **object reference** is a variable of a class (or interface) type that stores the **memory address** of an object allocated on the heap. It acts as a handle to access the object's instance variables and methods using the dot (`.`) operator. **Differences:** | Aspect | Primitive | Object Reference | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Holds | Actual value | Heap address of object | | Memory | Stack only | Stack (address) + Heap (object) | | Default | $0$ / `false` | `null` | | Nullable | No | Yes | **(3 marks)** тЬУ --- #### **Q2. What is a null reference? What exception occurs when it is dereferenced?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** A **null reference** is a reference variable that does not point to any object in memory. Its value is the literal `null` (conceptually address `0x0`). When a null reference is dereferenced (e.g., `nullRef.name`), the JVM throws a **`java.lang.NullPointerException`** at runtime, terminating the program abnormally unless caught by a `try-catch` block. **(3 marks)** тЬУ --- ### 5.2 Part B тАФ Full-Length Questions (14 Marks) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module Internal Choice тАФ Answer **ONE** of the two. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper]` **(a)** Explain the concept of an object reference in Java with a suitable example. Differentiate between **pass-by-value** for primitives and **pass-by-value of reference** for objects. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate **aliasing** and **reference reassignment**. Show the output and explain the memory state at each step. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** An **object reference** is a typed handle that stores the heap address of an object. Example: ```java class Box { int length; Box(int l) { length = l; } } public class RefDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { Box b = new Box(10); System.out.println(b.length); } } ``` Here, `b` is a reference on the stack pointing to a `Box` object on the heap. **Pass-by-value of reference:** - For primitives, the *value itself* is copied. Modifying the copy does not affect the original. - For objects, the *address* is copied. Both original and parameter point to the **same** heap object, so internal state changes via the parameter are visible to the caller. However, **reassigning** the parameter to a new object does **not** affect the caller's reference. ```java static void modify(Box x) { x.length = 99; } // Affects caller static void reassign(Box x) { x = new Box(50); } // Does NOT affect caller ``` **(7 marks)** тЬУ **Model Solution (b):** ```java class Student { String name; Student(String n) { name = n; } } public class AliasDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { Student s1 = new Student("Anu"); Student s2 = s1; // Aliasing s2.name = "Manu"; System.out.println(s1.name); // Output: Manu (shared object) s1 = new Student("Vinu"); // Reassignment System.out.println(s2.name); // Output: Manu (s2 unaffected) System.out.println(s1.name); // Output: Vinu } } ``` **Memory trace:** 1. `s1` тЖТ `Student("Anu")` at `0xAA01` 2. `s2 = s1` тЖТ `s2` тЖТ `0xAA01` (same object тАФ aliasing) 3. `s2.name = "Manu"` тЖТ modifies object at `0xAA01` 4. `s1 = new Student("Vinu")` тЖТ `s1` тЖТ `0xBB02` (new object); `s2` still тЖТ `0xAA01` **(7 marks)** тЬУ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** What is reference aliasing? Write a program to show how it can lead to unintended modifications, and how reassignment breaks the alias. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the difference between `==` and `.equals()` when comparing object references. Provide an example using the `String` class. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** **Reference aliasing** occurs when two or more reference variables hold the same heap address, thereby pointing to the *same* object.
