Mutating the object through any of the references is visible through all of them. ```java class Account { double balance; Account(double b) { balance = b; } } public class AliasRisk { public static void main(String[] args) { Account a1 = new Account(1000); Account a2 = a1; // ALIAS a2.balance = 5000; // unintended side effect System.out.println(a1.balance); // 5000.0 тАФ affected! a1 = new Account(9999); // BREAK alias System.out.println(a2.balance); // 5000.0 тАФ a1 redirected } } ``` When `a1 = new Account(9999);` is executed, `a1` points to a *new* object. The alias is broken, and `a2` continues to point to the original. **(7 marks)** тЬУ **Model Solution (b):** - `==` compares the **memory addresses** of two references (reference equality). - `.equals()` compares the **logical content** of two objects, provided the class overrides `Object.equals()`. By default, `Object.equals()` behaves like `==`. ```java public class StringEq { public static void main(String[] args) { String s1 = new String("KTU"); String s2 = new String("KTU"); System.out.println(s1 == s2); // false (different addresses) System.out.println(s1.equals(s2)); // true (same content) } } ``` The `String` class overrides `equals()` to perform character-by-character comparison, returning `true` when the two strings have identical content. **(7 marks)** тЬУ --- ### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (Lose 2тАУ3 marks per answer):** > 1. **Confusing `==` with `.equals()`** тАФ `==` compares *addresses*, not content. Writing "`s1 == s2` checks if contents are equal" is **wrong** unless the class is a wrapper like `String` where interning makes them coincidentally equal. > 2. **Claiming Java is "pass-by-reference"** тАФ Java is **always pass-by-value**. For objects, the *reference value (address)* is copied. Reassignment inside the method does **not** affect the caller's variable. > 3. **Forgetting that primitives are not references** тАФ `int x = 5;` is not a reference. It cannot be `null`. > 4. **Skipping the memory diagram** тАФ KTU examiners award 2тАУ3 marks for a clear stack/heap sketch. Always include one. > 5. **Treating `null` as a valid object** тАФ `null` is a *literal* indicating the absence of an object. Dereferencing it causes `NullPointerException`. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ЁЯУМ **Object Reference** = a typed variable holding a heap address; **not** the object itself. - ЁЯУМ Primitives store **values** on the stack; references store **addresses** on the stack pointing to the **heap**. - ЁЯУМ A reference can be **`null`** тАФ dereferencing it throws `NullPointerException`. - ЁЯУМ **Aliasing** = two references, one object. Modifications via one are visible via the other. - ЁЯУМ **Reassignment** redirects a reference to a new object; the old object becomes **garbage-collectable** if no other reference points to it. - ЁЯУМ `==` тЖТ address equality; `.equals()` тЖТ content equality (if overridden). - ЁЯУМ Java is **pass-by-value** (of the reference's address for objects), **never** pass-by-reference. - ЁЯУМ Reference size: typically **4 bytes** (compressed oops) or **8 bytes** (64-bit JVM). - ЁЯУМ Always include a **stack/heap diagram** in 7+ mark answers тАФ it's a guaranteed scoring component. - ЁЯУМ Default value of any reference variable (instance field) is `null`; local references must be explicitly initialized. ---

topics.title: Introduction to Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** using Java, a **method** is a named, reusable block of code encapsulated inside a class that performs a specific, well-defined task. A method is the procedural workhorse of a class тАФ it is the only mechanism through which an object exhibits behavior, manipulates its own state (instance variables), or returns computed results to a calling context.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, a method is formally characterized by its **method signature**, which is the combination of:

$$\text{Method Signature} = \text{Method Name} + \text{Parameter List (types and order)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):**
> A method is the fundamental unit of behavior in Java. Every executable Java statement must reside inside a method. The `main` method is the entry point, but all real logic must be decomposed into well-named, single-responsibility methods.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ The "Vending Machine" View

Imagine a **vending machine** placed in your college canteen:

- The **machine itself** = a `class` (the blueprint)
- The **buttons and coin slot** = the parameters (inputs you supply)
- The **snack that drops out** = the return value (output you receive)
- The **internal mechanism** (coils, motors, dispensers) = the method body (hidden logic)
- **Pressing a button** = calling/invoking the method

You do not need to know *how* the machine works internally (abstraction). You just provide inputs (coins + button code) and receive an output (Coke, KitKat, etc.). That is exactly how a Java method behaves: **input тЖТ processing тЖТ output**.

| Analogy Element | Java Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Vending machine blueprint | `class VendingMachine` |
| A specific machine | `object` (instance) |
| Button "B1" labeled "Coke" | `method dispenseCoke(int money)` |
| Coins inserted | `arguments` |
| Drink received | `return` value |
| "Out of stock" message | `return null` or thrown exception |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of a Method:**
> 1. **Declaration (Header)** тАФ tells the compiler the method's *name, return type, and parameters*.
> 2. **Definition (Body)** тАФ contains the actual statements to execute.
> 3. **Invocation (Call)** тАФ triggers execution from another method or statement.

## 1.3 Why Methods? тАФ The "Why" Before the "How"

A beginner often asks: *"Why not write everything inside `main`?"* The answer lies in three engineering principles:

1. **Reusability (DRY Principle)** тАФ *Don't Repeat Yourself*. Write once, invoke a thousand times.
2. **Modularity** тАФ break a monolithic program into small, manageable, testable units.
3. **Abstraction** тАФ hide complex logic behind a simple, descriptive name.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Standard Naming:** Method names in Java should be **verbs in lowercase**, using **camelCase** for multi-word names. Example: `calculateSalary()`, `findMaximum()`, `printReport()`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Java Method тАФ The Five Mandatory Parts

A method declaration in Java follows a strict syntactic structure. Each part has a distinct role:

```java
accessModifier static returnType methodName(parameterList) {
    // method body
    // local variable declarations
    // executable statements
    return value; // mandatory if returnType is not void
}
```

### 2.1.1 Component-by-Component Breakdown

**(a) Access Modifier**
Defines the **visibility** of the method to other classes.

| Modifier | Visibility Scope | KTU Exam Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `public` | Accessible from **any class** in any package | Very High |
| `protected` | Accessible within the **same package** and **subclasses** | High |
| `private` | Accessible **only within the same class** | Very High |
| *default* (no keyword) | Accessible only within the **same package** | Medium |

**(b) Static Keyword (Optional)**
- `static` methods belong to the **class itself**, not to any specific object.
- They can be called using the **class name** directly: `ClassName.methodName()`.
- Non-static (instance) methods require an **object reference** to be invoked.

**(c) Return Type**
- The **data type** of the value the method sends back to the caller.
- If no value is returned, use the keyword `void`.
- A method can return **at most one value** (a single primitive or a single object reference).

**(d) Method Name**
- Must be a **valid Java identifier**.
- Must follow **camelCase** convention.
- Cannot be a Java reserved keyword (`class`, `int`, `if`, etc.).

**(e) Parameter List (Formal Parameters)**
- A comma-separated list of `type variableName` pairs enclosed in parentheses.
- Can be **empty** (`()`) if the method takes no inputs.
- Each parameter is a **local variable** of the method, initialized by the arguments passed during the call.

## 2.2 Method Invocation тАФ The Calling Mechanism

A method is executed only when it is **called (invoked)**. The syntax for invocation is:

```java
// For static methods
ClassName.methodName(argument1, argument2, ...);

// For instance (non-static) methods
objectReference.methodName(argument1, argument2, ...);
```

### 2.2.1 Formal vs. Actual Parameters тАФ A Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Parameters** = the variables declared in the method's *header* (e.g., `int a, int b` in `int add(int a, int b)`).
> **Actual Parameters / Arguments** = the *values/expressions* passed during the *call* (e.g., `5, 10` in `add(5, 10)`).

The JVM performs a process called **argument passing**: it copies the actual argument value into the formal parameter's memory slot. This is the foundation of Java's **pass-by-value** semantics, discussed in Section 2.4.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet тАФ The Method Specification Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the complete specification of a Java method that you must memorize for the KTU board exam:

| Component | Syntax Token | Mandatory? | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Access Modifier | `public` / `private` / `protected` | No (defaults to package-private) | `public` |
| Static Modifier | `static` | No | `static` |
| Abstract Modifier | `abstract` | No (used in abstract classes) | `abstract` |
| Final Modifier | `final` | No (prevents overriding) | `final` |
| Return Type | Any primitive / object type / `void` | **YES** | `int`, `double`, `String`, `void` |
| Method Name | Valid identifier, camelCase | **YES** | `calculateArea` |
| Parameter List | `(type1 var1, type2 var2, ...)` | **YES** (can be empty) | `(double radius)` |
| Method Body | `{ ... statements ... }` | **YES** | `{ return 3.14 * r * r; }` |
| `return` Statement | `return value;` | **YES** (unless `void`) | `return area;` |

### 2.3.1 The Method Signature Equation

In Java, two methods can have the **same name** only if their **signatures differ**. The signature is governed by:

$$\text{Signature}(m) = \text{name}(m) \oplus \text{orderedTypeList}(\text{params}(m))$$

> The return type is **NOT** part of the method signature in Java. This is a frequent KTU exam trap.

## 2.4 Pass-by-Value in Java тАФ The Golden Rule

Java is **strictly pass-by-value**. There are no pass-by-reference semantics in Java, even for object references.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** "Java passes objects by reference." This is **FALSE**. Java passes a *copy of the reference variable* (the memory address), not the original reference itself. As a result, the method can mutate the *object's internal state* but cannot make the caller's variable point to a different object.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Methods are not merely an academic construct тАФ they underpin every software engineering practice:

- **API Design**: Each public method is a contract between the library and its users.
- **Unit Testing (JUnit)**: Every well-designed method corresponds to one or more test cases (`@Test`).
- **Code Maintainability**: Methods enforce the *Single Responsibility Principle* (SRP) of SOLID design.
- **Parallel Computing**: Methods marked `synchronized` or annotated with concurrency APIs become the unit of thread execution.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Method Execution Flow

## 3.1 Method Declaration тАФ Line-by-Line Analysis

Consider the following KTU-standard method declaration:

```java
public static int findSquare(int number) {
    int result = number * number;
    return result;
}
```

Let us dissect this declaration component by component:

| Token | Classification | Role in Compilation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `public` | Access Modifier | Grants universal visibility to the method. |
| `static` | Non-Access Modifier | Binds the method to the class, not an instance. |
| `int` | Return Type | Promises the caller that an integer value will be returned. |
| `findSquare` | Method Name | The symbolic handle used to invoke the method. |
| `(int number)` | Parameter List | Declares one formal parameter of type `int`. |
| `int result = ...` | Local Variable | A method-scoped variable, destroyed upon method exit. |
| `return result;` | Return Statement | Sends the value back to the caller and exits the method. |

## 3.2 Method Invocation тАФ Stack Frame Analysis

When a method is called, the JVM creates a new **stack frame (activation record)** on the **call stack**. This frame stores:
- The method's local variables
- The return address (where to resume after the method finishes)
- The operand stack for intermediate calculations

Let us trace a complete execution sequence step-by-step.

### Worked Example: Tracing Method Calls

**Source Code:**

```java
public class Calculator {
    public static int add(int a, int b) {
        int sum = a + b;
        return sum;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int x = 10;
        int y = 20;
        int result = add(x, y);
        System.out.println("Sum = " + result);
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

**Step 1 тАФ JVM Entry:**
The JVM invokes `main` as the program entry point. A stack frame for `main` is pushed onto the call stack.

**Step 2 тАФ Local Variable Initialization in `main`:**
The variables `x` and `y` are allocated inside `main`'s stack frame.

$$x \leftarrow 10, \quad y \leftarrow 20$$

**Step 3 тАФ Method Call `add(x, y)`:**
The call `add(x, y)` triggers the following sequence:
1. The current execution point in `main` is saved (return address = line of `add(x, y)`).
2. A new stack frame for `add` is pushed onto the stack.
3. The **actual arguments** `x = 10` and `y = 20` are **copied** into the **formal parameters** `a` and `b` (pass-by-value).

$$a \leftarrow 10, \quad b \leftarrow 20$$

**Step 4 тАФ Local Variable in `add`:**
The local variable `sum` is declared and computed:

$$\text{sum} = a + b = 10 + 20 = 30$$

**Step 5 тАФ Return Statement:**
The statement `return sum;` does two things atomically:
1. The value `30` is returned to the caller (`main`).
2. The stack frame of `add` is **popped** and destroyed (all locals `a`, `b`, `sum` cease to exist).

**Step 6 тАФ Resumption in `main`:**
Control returns to `main`. The returned value `30` is assigned to `result`:

$$\text{result} \leftarrow 30$$

**Step 7 тАФ Output:**
The `println` statement executes, producing:

$$\text{Output} = \texttt{"Sum = 30"}$$

**Step 8 тАФ Program Termination:**
`main` ends. Its stack frame is popped. The call stack becomes empty, and the JVM terminates the program.

### 3.2.1 Stack Memory Visualization (Logical)

```
+-------------------------------+
| main() frame                  |
|   x = 10                      |
|   y = 20                      |
|   result = 30                 |
+-------------------------------+
        тЖС   тЖР return address
+-------------------------------+
| add() frame (DESTROYED)       |
|   a = 10                      |
|   b = 20                      |
|   sum = 30                    |
+-------------------------------+
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `add` frame is destroyed the moment `return sum;` executes. The `main` frame outlives it because execution resumes in `main`.

## 3.3 Pass-by-Value тАФ Proof by Contradiction

To cement the pass-by-value concept, let us examine what happens with **primitive** versus **object reference** parameters.

### Case 1: Primitive Parameter (e.g., `int`)

```java
public static void modifyValue(int num) {
    num = 100; // local change only
}

public static void main(String[] args) {
    int x = 5;
    modifyValue(x);
    System.out.println(x); // prints 5, NOT 100
}
```

**Analysis:**
- The value `5` is copied into the formal parameter `num`.
- The assignment `num = 100` modifies only the *local* copy inside `modifyValue`.
- The caller's variable `x` remains `5`.

### Case 2: Object Reference Parameter (e.g., `StringBuilder`)

```java
public static void modifyObject(StringBuilder sb) {
    sb.append(" World"); // mutates the object
    sb = new StringBuilder("New"); // changes local reference only
}

public static void main(String[] args) {
    StringBuilder greeting = new StringBuilder("Hello");
    modifyObject(greeting);
    System.out.println(greeting); // prints "Hello World"
}
```

**Analysis:**
- The *reference value* (memory address) of `greeting` is copied into the formal parameter `sb`.
- `sb.append(" World")` mutates the **same object** that `greeting` points to.
- `sb = new StringBuilder("New")` reassigns only the **local copy** of the reference; the caller's `greeting` still points to the original "Hello" object.
- The final output is "Hello World", proving the object's *contents* changed but the caller's *reference* did not.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Verdict on Java Parameter Passing:**
> Java is **100% pass-by-value**. For primitives, the value itself is copied. For objects, the reference (address) is copied тАФ but never the original reference variable itself. This is one of the most heavily tested KTU concepts.

## 3.4 The `void` Return Type тАФ Special Case

A method declared with `void` performs an action but does not return a value. The `return` statement is **optional**, and if used, must not be followed by a value.

```java
public static void greet(String name) {
    System.out.println("Hello, " + name + "!");
    return; // optional; exits the method early
}
```

If a `void` method attempts `return value;`, the compiler will reject it with:

$$\text{Error: } \texttt{"unexpected type: required: void, found: int"}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Method Invocation Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the complete lifecycle of a method call тАФ from invocation to stack frame creation to return.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Caller executes methodName args] --> B{Is method static?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Resolve method via ClassName]
    B -- No --> D[Resolve method via objectReference]
    C --> E[Push new Stack Frame onto Call Stack]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Copy actual arguments to formal parameters]
    F --> G[Execute method body statements in order]
    G --> H{Is return type void?}
    H -- No --> I[Evaluate return expression]
    I --> J[Pop Stack Frame from Call Stack]
    J --> K[Pass returned value to caller]
    H -- Yes --> L[Pop Stack Frame from Call Stack]
    L --> M[Control returns to caller]
    K --> M
    M --> N[Caller resumes execution at next statement]
```

## 4.2 Method Component Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following block diagram decomposes a Java method declaration into its constituent syntactic components, showing how they nest within one another.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Method Declaration] --> AM[Access Modifier]
    M --> SM[Static or other Modifiers]
    M --> RT[Return Type]
    M --> MN[Method Name]
    M --> PL[Parameter List]
    M --> MB[Method Body]
    AM --> AM1[public]
    AM --> AM2[private]
    AM --> AM3[protected]
    AM --> AM4[default - package private]
    SM --> SM1[static]
    SM --> SM2[final]
    SM --> SM3[abstract]
    RT --> RT1[Primitive: int, double, char, boolean]
    RT --> RT2[Object: String, Scanner, etc]
    RT --> RT3[void]
    PL --> PL1[Formal Parameters: type variableName]
    PL --> PL2[Empty: parenthesis empty]
    MB --> MB1[Local Variable Declarations]
    MB --> MB2[Executable Statements]
    MB --> MB3[Return Statement - if not void]
```

## 4.3 Stack Frame Lifecycle (Block-Level Architecture)

The following diagram depicts the runtime memory interactions when a method is invoked, showing how the call stack evolves.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CS[Call Stack - grows downward]
        direction TB
        SF1[main frame - x=10, y=20, result=?]
        SF2[add frame - a=10, b=20, sum=30]
    end
    INV[Invocation: add x y] --> SF1
    SF1 --> PUSH[Push add frame]
    PUSH --> SF2
    SF2 --> EXEC[Execute body: sum = a + b]
    EXEC --> RET[return sum value 30]
    RET --> POP[Pop add frame - destroy locals]
    POP --> SF1
    SF1 --> ASSIGN[result = 30]
    ASSIGN --> OUT[System.out.println Sum equals 30]
```

## 4.4 Pass-by-Value Memory Model (Block Diagram)

The following block diagram visually demonstrates the difference between primitive and object reference passing in Java.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CALLER[Caller's Memory Space]
        VAR1[x = 5]
        REF1[greeting - address 0xABC]
    end
    subgraph CALLEE[Callee's Memory Space - copy]
        COPY1[num = 5]
        COPY2[sb - address 0xABC - same object]
    end
    VAR1 -- pass by value: copy of 5 --> COPY1
    REF1 -- pass by value: copy of address --> COPY2
    COPY1 -- reassign num = 100 --> REJECT1[Caller's x remains 5]
    COPY2 -- sb append World --> MUTATE[Object at 0xABC mutated]
    COPY2 -- sb = new object --> REJECT2[Caller still points to 0xABC]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization Tip for Exams:** When asked to explain pass-by-value, draw two boxes тАФ one for the *caller's* variable and one for the *callee's* parameter. Draw an arrow showing the *value* being copied. This single diagram earns full marks on any KTU theory question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Define a method in Java. List any four components of a method declaration with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

A **method** in Java is a named block of code declared within a class that encapsulates a specific behavior or computation. It is the fundamental unit of behavior in object-oriented programming and is invoked (called) to perform its task.

The four major components of a method declaration are:

1. **Access Modifier** тАФ Controls visibility. Example: `public` allows access from any class.
2. **Return Type** тАФ Specifies the data type of the value returned. Example: `int` in `int calculate()`.
3. **Method Name** тАФ A valid Java identifier following camelCase. Example: `calculateSum`.
4. **Parameter List** тАФ The comma-separated list of inputs. Example: `(int a, int b)`.

**Example Signature:**
```java
public int calculateSum(int a, int b)
```

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**What is the difference between formal parameters and actual parameters? Give one example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Formal Parameters | Actual Parameters |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Variables declared in the method's *header/definition* | Values/expressions passed during the *method call* |
| **Scope** | Exist only inside the method body | Exist in the caller's scope |
| **Also Called** | Parameters | Arguments |

**Example:**

```java
// Method definition тАФ 'a' and 'b' are FORMAL parameters
public static int add(int a, int b) {
    return a + b;
}

// Method call тАФ '5' and '10' are ACTUAL parameters / arguments
int result = add(5, 10);
```

In the call `add(5, 10)`, the values `5` and `10` are copied into the formal parameters `a` and `b` respectively via Java's pass-by-value mechanism.

---

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `return` statement** in non-`void` methods тАФ *2 marks deducted*.
> 2. **Confusing method overloading with overriding** тАФ they are different concepts (overloading = same class, overriding = parent-child).
> 3. **Writing `return` with a value inside a `void` method** тАФ *compilation error, full marks lost*.
> 4. **Claiming Java is "pass-by-reference"** тАФ *this is factually wrong; Java is strictly pass-by-value*.
> 5. **Omitting the access modifier in exam answers** тАФ write `public` explicitly to be safe.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the anatomy of a Java method with a suitable example. Discuss the role of access specifiers and return types in method design. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable Java program, demonstrate the concept of **pass-by-value** for both primitive and object type parameters. Explain why Java is strictly pass-by-value even when passing objects. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Anatomy of a Java Method [7 Marks]:**

A Java method declaration consists of six syntactical components: **access modifier**, **non-access modifier** (e.g., `static`), **return type**, **method name**, **parameter list**, and **method body**.

```java
public static double calculateArea(double radius) {
    final double PI = 3.14159;
    double area = PI * radius * radius;
    return area;
}
```

- **Access Specifier (`public`)** [1 Mark]: Makes the method callable from any class. Other options are `private` (class-only), `protected` (package + subclasses), and default (package-only).
- **Non-Access Modifier (`static`)** [1 Mark]: Binds the method to the class itself, allowing invocation without creating an object.
- **Return Type (`double`)** [2 Marks]: Declares that the method will return a `double` precision floating-point value. If no value is returned, `void` is used.
- **Method Name (`calculateArea`)** [1 Mark]: A camelCase verb phrase describing the action.
- **Parameter List (`double radius`)** [1 Mark]: Declares one formal parameter that receives the input value.
- **Method Body** [1 Mark]: The block containing local variable declarations, computation, and the mandatory `return` statement.

**(b) Pass-by-Value Demonstration [7 Marks]:**

```java
public class PassByValueDemo {

    // Primitive parameter тАФ value is copied
    public static void modifyPrimitive(int num) {
        num = num + 50;
        System.out.println("Inside modifyPrimitive, num = " + num);
    }

    // Object reference parameter тАФ reference is copied
    public static void modifyObject(StringBuilder builder) {
        builder.append(" World");        // mutates original object
        builder = new StringBuilder("New"); // only changes local reference
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int x = 10;
        modifyPrimitive(x);
        System.out.println("After modifyPrimitive, x = " + x);

        StringBuilder greeting = new StringBuilder("Hello");
        modifyObject(greeting);
        System.out.println("After modifyObject, greeting = " + greeting);
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Inside modifyPrimitive, num = 60
After modifyPrimitive, x = 10
After modifyObject, greeting = Hello World
```

**Explanation [3 Marks]:**

1. In `modifyPrimitive`, the value `10` of `x` is copied into the local `num`. The reassignment `num = 50` does not affect `x` outside the method. Hence, `x` remains `10`.
2. In `modifyObject`, the *reference* (memory address) of `greeting` is copied into `builder`. The call `builder.append(" World")` mutates the *same object* that `greeting` points to, so the original object now contains "Hello World".
3. The line `builder = new StringBuilder("New")` creates a brand-new object and assigns the *local* `builder` reference to it. This does **not** affect the caller's `greeting` variable, which still points to the original "Hello" object.
4. **Conclusion:** Java is **strictly pass-by-value** тАФ for primitives, the literal value is copied; for objects, the reference (address) is copied. The original reference variable in the caller's scope can never be reassigned by the callee.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Define a method signature. Explain with examples how the Java compiler distinguishes between two methods having the same name. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that contains a `main` method which calls a user-defined method `findMax(int a, int b, int c)` that returns the largest of three integers. Show the complete execution with a sample input. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Method Signature & Name Differentiation [7 Marks]:**

A **method signature** in Java is the combination of the method's name and the ordered list of parameter types. It is the unique identifier that the compiler uses to bind a method call to the correct method definition.

$$\text{Signature} = \text{Method Name} + \text{Parameter Types (order matters)}$$

> **Important:** The return type is **NOT** part of the method signature in Java.

**Example тАФ Same Name, Different Signatures:**

```java
public class MathUtils {
    // Signature 1: add(int, int)
    public static int add(int a, int b) {
        return a + b;
    }

    // Signature 2: add(double, double)
    public static double add(double a, double b) {
        return a + b;
    }

    // Signature 3: add(int, int, int)
    public static int add(int a, int b, int c) {
        return a + b + c;
    }
}
```

When the compiler encounters `add(5, 10)`, it matches the parameter types `(int, int)` to the first method. For `add(2.5, 3.7)`, it matches `(double, double)` to the second. This automatic resolution is called **method overloading**, and it is enabled *solely* by signature differences. [3 Marks for explanation + 2 Marks for example + 2 Marks for example demonstration]

**(b) Program to Find the Largest of Three Integers [7 Marks]:**

```java
public class MaxFinder {

    // User-defined method to find the maximum of three integers
    public static int findMax(int a, int b, int c) {
        int max = a;                       // assume a is the largest initially
        if (b > max) {
            max = b;                       // update if b is larger
        }
        if (c > max) {
            max = c;                       // update if c is larger
        }
        return max;                        // return the largest value
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int num1 = 45, num2 = 78, num3 = 23;
        int largest = findMax(num1, num2, num3);
        System.out.println("The largest number is: " + largest);
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Execution Trace [3 Marks]:**

| Step | Action | State of `max` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `max = a` (initialize with `num1 = 45`) | `max = 45` |
| 2 | Check `b > max` тЖТ `78 > 45` is **true** тЖТ `max = b` | `max = 78` |
| 3 | Check `c > max` тЖТ `23 > 78` is **false** тЖТ no update | `max = 78` |
| 4 | `return max;` returns `78` to `main` | тАФ |
| 5 | `largest` in `main` is assigned `78` | `largest = 78` |

**Output:**
```
The largest number is: 78
```

**Alternate One-Liner Logic (Bonus Knowledge):**
$$\text{findMax}(a,b,c) = \max(a, \max(b,c))$$

This can be coded as:
```java
return Math.max(a, Math.max(b, c));
```

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist тАФ Must Memorize for KTU 2024**

- тЬЕ A **method** is a named, reusable block of code inside a class that performs a specific task.
- тЬЕ The **method signature** = `methodName + parameterTypeList`. Return type is **NOT** part of the signature.
- тЬЕ Method components in order: **access modifier тЖТ static/final тЖТ return type тЖТ method name тЖТ (parameters) тЖТ { body }**.
- тЬЕ `void` return type means the method returns **no value**; a non-`void` method **must** have a `return value;` statement.
- тЬЕ **Formal parameters** are declared in the method header; **actual parameters (arguments)** are passed during the call.
- тЬЕ Java is **strictly pass-by-value**: primitives copy the value; objects copy the reference (address).
- тЬЕ A method can change the *internal state* of an object passed to it, but it **cannot reassign the caller's reference** to a different object.
- тЬЕ Method names must be **camelCase verbs** (e.g., `computeTotal`, not `Compute_Total`).
- тЬЕ `static` methods belong to the **class**; non-static methods belong to an **object (instance)**.
- тЬЕ A stack frame is created on the **call stack** when a method is invoked and destroyed when it returns.
- тЬЕ Two methods in the same class can have the same name **only if their parameter lists differ** (method overloading).
- тЬЕ Common KTU traps: confusing overloading with overriding; claiming Java is pass-by-reference; omitting `return` in non-`void` methods.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constructors
Access Modifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Access Modifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Access Modifiers** (also called **Visibility Modifiers** or **Access Specifiers**) in Object-Oriented Programming are reserved keywords that control the **visibility**, **accessibility**, and **scope** of classes, methods, constructors, and data members across different packages, classes, and inheritance hierarchies. They are the cornerstone of the OOP principle of **Encapsulation** and **Data Hiding**.

In Java (the prescribed language for KTU PBCST304), there are exactly **four** access modifiers:

1. `private`
2. `default` (also called *package-private* тАФ no keyword is written)
3. `protected`
4. `public`

There is also a non-access modifier `static`, `final`, `abstract`, etc., but those are **not** access modifiers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ The "House Security" Model

Imagine your house has multiple zones:

| Zone | Real-world analogy | Java access modifier |
|---|---|---|
| Your personal diary | Only you can read it | `private` |
| Your bedroom | Family members + you | `protected` |
| Your living room | Family + close friends (same package/family) | `default` |
| Your front lawn | Anyone in society | `public` |

> [!NOTE]
> Think of access modifiers as **locks on doors**. Each modifier decides *who holds the key*: only the owner, the family, the neighborhood, or the entire city.

### 1.3 The Four Pillars of Access Control тАФ At a Glance

```
private   тЖТ  strictest, only SAME class
default   тЖТ  SAME package (folder)
protected тЖТ  SAME package + subclasses (even in other packages)
public    тЖТ  accessible EVERYWHERE
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** If a question says *"no keyword is used"*, the answer is **default access**, NOT public. This is one of the most common mistakes in board exams.

### 1.4 Why Access Modifiers Exist тАФ Engineering Motivation

- **Security:** Prevent unauthorized manipulation of sensitive data.
- **Maintainability:** Allow internal restructuring without breaking external code.
- **Encapsulation support:** Enforce the OOP rule *"internal details should be hidden from outside world"*.
- **Controlled inheritance:** Let parent classes expose *only* the parts that child classes should inherit.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Access Modifier Hierarchy

```
MOST RESTRICTIVE  тЖРтАФтАФтАФтАФтАФтАФтАФтАФтАФтАФтАФтАФтАФтАФтАФтАФтАФтЖТ  LEAST RESTRICTIVE
   private   тЖТ   default   тЖТ   protected   тЖТ   public
```

This is a **strict ordering**. Whatever is accessible to `private` is automatically accessible to `default`, `protected`, and `public`. Conversely, `public` gives the maximum freedom.

### 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of Each Modifier

#### (a) `private` Access Modifier
- **Scope:** Only within the **same class**.
- **NOT accessible** in: subclasses (even same package), other classes in same package, or any class in another package.
- **Use case:** Data members of a class that hold sensitive state (e.g., bank balance, password).

#### (b) `default` (Package-Private) Access Modifier
- **Keyword:** *None* тАФ if you write no access modifier, this is what you get.
- **Scope:** Within the **same package**.
- **NOT accessible** in: subclasses in other packages, classes in other packages.
- **Use case:** Helper classes/methods that are meant to be internal to a package.

#### (c) `protected` Access Modifier
- **Scope:** Within the **same package** **PLUS** subclasses in **any package** (via inheritance).
- **NOT accessible** in: non-subclass classes in other packages.
- **Use case:** Methods meant to be overridden by child classes, but still hidden from unrelated classes.

#### (d) `public` Access Modifier
- **Scope:** **Everywhere** тАФ any class in any package.
- **Use case:** API methods, utility functions, constants meant for global use.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet тАФ Access Visibility Matrix

The table below is the **single most important table** for KTU board exams. Memorize it.

| Modifier | Same Class | Same Package (non-subclass) | Subclass (same package) | Subclass (different package) | Other Package (non-subclass) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `private`   | тЬЕ Yes | тЭМ No  | тЭМ No  | тЭМ No  | тЭМ No  |
| `default`   | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes | тЭМ No  | тЭМ No  |
| `protected` | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes | тЭМ No  |
| `public`    | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes | тЬЕ Yes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the matrix тАФ "PDPP":** *Private тЖТ Default тЖТ Protected тЖТ Public* тАФ each step *adds* more accessibility. This is the exact order KTU examiners expect students to write.

### 2.4 Access Modifiers Applicable To What?

| Element | `private` | `default` | `protected` | `public` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Class       | тЭМ | тЬЕ | тЭМ | тЬЕ |
| Variable    | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | тЬЕ |
| Method      | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | тЬЕ |
| Constructor | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | тЬЕ |
| Interface   | тЭМ | тЭМ | тЭМ | тЬЕ (implicit) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top-level classes** in Java can ONLY be `public` or `default`. You cannot declare a top-level class as `private` or `protected`. This is a guaranteed 1-mark question in KTU Part A.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking software:** Account balance declared `private`, exposed via `public` getter/setter with validation logic.
- **Android development:** UI components use `public` access for framework callbacks.
- **Library design (e.g., Java's `Collections` framework):** Internal helper classes are `default` (package-private) to keep APIs clean.
- **Microservices & APIs:** Only `public` methods form the API contract; everything else is hidden via `private`/`protected`.

### 2.6 Key Logical Rules (Why & How)

- **Why `protected` allows different-package access only via inheritance?**
  Because the JVM checks accessibility through the **inheritance chain**, not through object reference. A subclass in another package must *extend* the parent to access protected members.

- **How does `default` differ from `protected` in the same package?**
  They behave **identically** inside the same package. The difference appears only when subclasses cross package boundaries.

- **Why is `public` not always best?**
  Exposing too much increases **coupling**, makes refactoring harder, and violates the *Principle of Least Privilege* тАФ a cornerstone of secure software engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Comprehensive Java Program Demonstrating All Four Modifiers

Below is a **single executable program** that shows every access modifier in action, with comments explaining *why* each line compiles or fails.

```java
package university;            // Package "university"

public class Student {        // TOP-LEVEL class тАФ can only be public or default

    private   int rollNo      = 101;        // STEP 1: only Student class can access
                String name   = "Anu";      // STEP 2: default access тАФ university package only
    protected double cgpa    = 8.7;        // STEP 3: university package + all subclasses
    public    String college  = "KTU";      // STEP 4: accessible everywhere

    private void privateMethod() {           // STEP 5
        System.out.println("Private method called");
    }

    void defaultMethod() {                   // STEP 6
        System.out.println("Default method called");
    }

    protected void protectedMethod() {       // STEP 7
        System.out.println("Protected method called");
    }

    public void publicMethod() {             // STEP 8
        System.out.println("Public method called");
    }

    public void displayAll() {
        // INSIDE THE SAME CLASS тАФ all four are accessible.
        System.out.println(rollNo);          // тЬЕ private OK
        System.out.println(name);            // тЬЕ default OK
        System.out.println(cgpa);            // тЬЕ protected OK
        System.out.println(college);         // тЬЕ public OK
    }
}
```

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Logical Walkthrough

**Step 1 тАФ `private int rollNo`**
   - Logic: Restrict direct read/write to the Student class.
   - Consequence: Even subclasses like `EngineeringStudent` cannot access `rollNo` directly.

**Step 2 тАФ `String name` (default)**
   - Logic: Visible inside the `university` package only.
   - Consequence: A non-student class in the same package (e.g., `UniversityAdmin`) can read it, but classes outside `university` cannot.

**Step 3 тАФ `protected double cgpa`**
   - Logic: Visible inside `university` package + any subclass anywhere.
   - Consequence: An `EngineeringStudent` in another package can read `cgpa`, but a non-related class in another package cannot.

**Step 4 тАФ `public String college`**
   - Logic: Global visibility.
   - Consequence: Any class, anywhere, can read `college`.

**Step 5 to 8 тАФ Method Visibility**
   - Same rules apply to methods. Inside `displayAll()`, all four methods are callable because we are within the same class.

### 3.3 Testing the Access from Different Contexts

#### Context A тАФ Same Class (always тЬЕ)

```java
package university;
public class DemoSameClass {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s = new Student();
        System.out.println(s.rollNo);    // тЬЕ PRIVATE тАФ accessible because same class context
        System.out.println(s.name);      // тЬЕ DEFAULT
        System.out.println(s.cgpa);      // тЬЕ PROTECTED
        System.out.println(s.college);   // тЬЕ PUBLIC
    }
}
```
> **Logic:** `DemoSameClass` is INSIDE `Student.java`'s perspective тАФ when you access `private` from within the same `.java` file's class body, the JVM allows it. Wait тАФ actually, `DemoSameClass` is a *different* class, so accessing `s.rollNo` would be a **compile-time error**.

#### Context B тАФ Same Package, Different Class (тЬЕ default, protected, public; тЭМ private)

```java
package university;
public class SamePackageTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s = new Student();
        // System.out.println(s.rollNo);   // тЭМ COMPILE ERROR тАФ private
        System.out.println(s.name);        // тЬЕ default OK
        System.out.println(s.cgpa);        // тЬЕ protected OK
        System.out.println(s.college);     // тЬЕ public OK
    }
}
```

#### Context C тАФ Different Package, Subclass (тЬЕ protected, public; тЭМ private, default)

```java
package college;                                // DIFFERENT package
import university.Student;
public class EngineeringStudent extends Student {
    void show() {
        // System.out.println(rollNo);         // тЭМ private тАФ not accessible
        // System.out.println(name);           // тЭМ default тАФ different package
        System.out.println(cgpa);              // тЬЕ protected тАФ accessible via inheritance
        System.out.println(college);           // тЬЕ public
    }
}
```

#### Context D тАФ Different Package, Non-Subclass (тЬЕ public only)

```java
package external;
import university.Student;
public class Outsider {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s = new Student();
        // System.out.println(s.rollNo);        // тЭМ private
        // System.out.println(s.name);          // тЭМ default
        // System.out.println(s.cgpa);          // тЭМ protected
        System.out.println(s.college);          // тЬЕ public
    }
}
```

### 3.4 Analytical Equation тАФ Calculating Effective Accessibility

We can model accessibility as a **logical OR** over allowed contexts:

$$
A_{\text{effective}}(x) \;=\; \bigvee_{i=1}^{n} \text{context}_i \;\land\; \text{modifier permits } \text{context}_i
$$

Where each $\text{context}_i$ is one of the **5 columns** in the visibility matrix (Section 2.3). A member $x$ is accessible in context $C$ **iff** the modifier explicitly permits $C$.

For example, for a `protected` member:

$$
A_{\text{protected}} \;=\; \text{(same class)} \lor \text{(same pkg)} \lor \text{(subclass any pkg)}
$$

Explicitly excluded contexts:

$$
\neg A_{\text{protected}} \;=\; \text{(other-package, non-subclass)}
$$

This equation is the **formal specification** of the matrix. KTU examiners may ask you to *"justify"* the matrix in Part B тАФ use this reasoning.

### 3.5 Comparative Logic Tree

```
                    Is it the SAME CLASS?
                            тФВ
                  тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФ┤тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
                 YES                 NO
                  тФВ                   тФВ
              ALL OK тЬЕ        Is it SAME PACKAGE?
                                    тФВ
                          тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФ┤тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
                         YES                 NO
                          тФВ                   тФВ
              default, protected,    Is it a SUBCLASS?
                public OK тЬЕ             тФВ
                                  тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФ┤тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
                                 YES           NO
                                  тФВ             тФВ
                          protected,        only
                          public OK тЬЕ    public OK тЬЕ
```

This decision tree mirrors the visibility matrix **exactly** and is the fastest way to answer any KTU short question.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture тАФ Access Modifier Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["MEMBER DECLARED IN CLASS X"]
    nodeB["CHECK ACCESS MODIFIER KEYWORD"]
    nodeC{"IS IT PRIVATE?"}
    nodeD["SCOPE: ONLY CLASS X"]
    nodeE{"IS IT DEFAULT?"}
    nodeF["SCOPE: CLASS X + SAME PACKAGE CLASSES"]
    nodeG{"IS IT PROTECTED?"}
    nodeH["SCOPE: CLASS X + SAME PACKAGE + SUBCLASSES ANYWHERE"]
    nodeI{"IS IT PUBLIC?"}
    nodeJ["SCOPE: ALL CLASSES EVERYWHERE"]
    nodeK["END OF ACCESS CHECK"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC -- YES --> nodeD --> nodeK
    nodeC -- NO --> nodeE
    nodeE -- YES --> nodeF --> nodeK
    nodeE -- NO --> nodeG
    nodeG -- YES --> nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeG -- NO --> nodeI
    nodeI -- YES --> nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeI -- NO --> nodeK
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram тАФ Visibility Matrix as a Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pkgU["Package: UNIVERSITY"]
    classStu["Class Student"]
    classAdmin["Class UniversityAdmin"]
    pkgC["Package: COLLEGE"]
    classEng["Class EngineeringStudent EXTENDS Student"]
    pkgE["Package: EXTERNAL"]
    classOut["Class Outsider"]

    pkgU --- classStu
    pkgU --- classAdmin
    pkgC --- classEng
    pkgE --- classOut

    classStu -. "private rollNo" .- classStu
    classStu -. "default name" .- classAdmin
    classStu -. "protected cgpa" .- classEng
    classStu -. "public college" .- classOut
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram тАФ Layered Visibility Zones


<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### ЁЯУШ PART A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

> Explain the four access modifiers in Java with their scope of accessibility.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Java provides **four** access modifiers that control the visibility of classes, methods, and data members:

1. **`private`** тАФ Members are accessible **only within the same class** in which they are declared. It is the most restrictive modifier and is used to enforce data hiding.

2. **`default`** тАФ When **no access modifier** is specified, the member has *package-level* access. It is accessible to all classes within the **same package** but not to classes in other packages.

3. **`protected`** тАФ Members are accessible within the **same package** and also to **subclasses in any package** (even outside the original package, through inheritance).

4. **`public`** тАФ Members are accessible from **any class in any package**. It is the least restrictive modifier.

> The visibility order from most to least restrictive is: `private тЖТ default тЖТ protected тЖТ public`.

*(Examiner's note: Full marks are awarded only if the student mentions that `default` has no keyword.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024, Supplementary]`

> Can a top-level class in Java be declared as `private` or `protected`? Justify your answer.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**No**, a top-level class in Java **cannot** be declared as `private` or `protected`. A top-level class can only have one of two access levels:

- `public` тАФ accessible from any package.
- *default* (no modifier) тАФ accessible only within the same package.

**Justification:** A `private` class would be inaccessible to any other class, making it impossible to instantiate or use. A `protected` class would require inheritance for access, but top-level classes cannot be inherited in a way that requires this access level. Hence the Java language designers restricted top-level class modifiers to `public` or default only.

> However, **inner classes** (nested classes) can be declared as `private`, `protected`, or `default`.

---

### ЁЯУХ PART B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]`

> (a) **Discuss in detail the four access modifiers in Java with a suitable example program. Show the visibility of members from different contexts.** **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) **Explain with examples why `protected` access is allowed in subclasses even when they are in different packages. How does this differ from default access?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Detailed explanation with example program (7 marks):**

The four access modifiers in Java, in increasing order of visibility, are: `private`, `default`, `protected`, `public`. Each controls where a class member (variable, method, or constructor) can be accessed from.

Consider the following program that demonstrates all four:

```java
package ktu.demo;

public class Account {
    private   int accNo = 12345;
              String holder = "Rahul";        // default
    protected double balance = 50000.75;
    public    String bank = "SBI";

    public void show() {
        System.out.println(accNo + " " + holder + " " + balance + " " + bank);
    }
}
```

| Access from | `accNo` (private) | `holder` (default) | `balance` (protected) | `bank` (public) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Inside `Account`        | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | тЬЕ |
| Same package, other class | тЭМ | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | тЬЕ |
| Subclass, different pkg   | тЭМ | тЭМ | тЬЕ | тЬЕ |
| Non-subclass, other pkg   | тЭМ | тЭМ | тЭМ | тЬЕ |

The visibility matrix summarizes the access rules clearly.

**(b) Why `protected` works across packages via inheritance (7 marks):**

The `protected` modifier is designed to support the OOP concept of inheritance while still preserving encapsulation. The Java compiler allows a subclass тАФ even one declared in a *different package* тАФ to access `protected` members of its parent. This is because the subclass **IS-A** parent (semantic relationship), so it logically needs access to inherited state and behavior.

**Example:**

```java
package external.pkg;
import ktu.demo.Account;

public class SavingsAccount extends Account {
    void display() {
        // System.out.println(holder);    // тЭМ default NOT accessible
        System.out.println(balance);       // тЬЕ protected accessible
        System.out.println(bank);          // тЬЕ public accessible
    }
}
```

**Difference from default access:**

- `default` members are accessible only within the *same package*. If a subclass lives in another package, it **cannot** access default members, even though it inherits the parent's *type*. The compiler enforces this strictly.
- `protected` extends the visibility *specifically* for subclasses across packages, while still preventing access from non-related classes in other packages.

> Thus, `protected` = `default` + "subclasses anywhere in the world". This distinction is fundamental to designing extensible OOP class hierarchies.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks тАФ Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024, Expected Pattern]`

> (a) **Explain the concept of encapsulation. How do access modifiers help achieve encapsulation in Java? Illustrate with an example.** **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) **Write a Java program that demonstrates the use of `private` data members and `public` getter/setter methods. Explain how access modifiers protect data integrity.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Encapsulation and access modifiers (7 marks):**

**Encapsulation** is the OOP principle of *bundling data and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit (class)* and *restricting direct access to some of the object's components*. It is the practice of **data hiding**.

Access modifiers are the **mechanism** by which encapsulation is implemented in Java. By declaring data members as `private`, we prevent external code from modifying them directly. Public getter and setter methods then provide *controlled* access тАФ allowing validation, logging, or computation before the data is exposed or changed.

**Example illustrating encapsulation:**

```java
public class Employee {
    private String name;          // hidden data
    private double salary;        // hidden data

    public void setSalary(double s) {
        if (s >= 0) {             // validation logic
            salary = s;
        }
    }

    public double getSalary() {
        return salary;
    }
}
```

Here, `salary` cannot be set to a negative value externally because the `setSalary` method enforces the rule. Without `private` access, any external code could assign `emp.salary = -1000`, violating data integrity.

**(b) Program with private members and public getters/setters (7 marks):**

```java
package ktu.bank;

public class BankAccount {
    private int accountNumber;
    private String accountHolder;
    private double balance;

    public BankAccount(int accNo, String name, double initialBalance) {
        this.accountNumber = accNo;
        this.accountHolder = name;
        if (initialBalance >= 0) {
            this.balance = initialBalance;
        } else {
            this.balance = 0;
        }
    }

    public int getAccountNumber() {
        return accountNumber;
    }

    public String getAccountHolder() {
        return accountHolder;
    }

    public double getBalance() {
        return balance;
    }

    public void deposit(double amount) {
        if (amount > 0) {
            balance += amount;
            System.out.println("Deposited: " + amount);
        } else {
            System.out.println("Invalid deposit amount");
        }
    }

    public void withdraw(double amount) {
        if (amount > 0 && amount <= balance) {
            balance -= amount;
            System.out.println("Withdrawn: " + amount);
        } else {
            System.out.println("Insufficient balance or invalid amount");
        }
    }
}
```

**Explanation of how access modifiers protect data integrity:**

- The fields `accountNumber`, `accountHolder`, and `balance` are declared `private`. This means **no external class** can directly modify them.
- The only way to change the balance is via `deposit()` or `withdraw()`, both of which include **validation logic** to reject negative or invalid amounts.
- `accountNumber` is read-only because only a `getter` is provided, not a `setter`. This prevents anyone from changing the account number after creation.
- If these fields were declared `public`, external code could do `acc.balance = -999999`, bypassing all checks and corrupting data.

> This is a textbook case of how **access modifiers + validation methods** form the practical implementation of **encapsulation** тАФ one of the four pillars of OOP.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall тАФ Common Mistakes That Cost Marks**
>
> 1. **Writing "default = public"** тАФ this is WRONG. `default` access is restricted to the same package. Many students confuse the two.
> 2. **Forgetting that `default` has NO keyword** тАФ if a question shows `int x = 5;` without any modifier, it is `default`, NOT public.
> 3. **Saying "top-level classes can be `protected`"** тАФ they CANNOT. Only inner/nested classes can.
> 4. **Confusing `protected` with `default`** in cross-package scenarios тАФ `default` does NOT cross package boundaries, even for subclasses.
> 5. **Not mentioning that `protected` allows access only through inheritance**, not via object reference of the parent type from outside.
> 6. **Writing `friendly` instead of `default`** тАФ Java does not have a `friendly` keyword; this was an old C++ terminology that is technically incorrect for Java.
> 7. **Omitting the visibility matrix in long answers** тАФ examiners give extra credit (often 1тАУ2 marks) when the matrix is clearly drawn.

---

### тЬЕ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ Read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- **Four access modifiers in Java:** `private`, `default` (no keyword), `protected`, `public`.
- **Order of restrictiveness:** `private тЖТ default тЖТ protected тЖТ public`.
- **`private`** тЖТ only the same class.
- **`default`** тЖТ same package (no keyword used).
- **`protected`** тЖТ same package + subclasses in any package.
- **`public`** тЖТ everywhere, no restrictions.
- **Top-level classes** can only be `public` or `default`. NOT `private`/`protected`.
- **All four** modifiers can be applied to **variables, methods, and constructors**.
- **Encapsulation** is implemented primarily via `private` data + `public` getter/setter methods.
- The **visibility matrix** (5 contexts ├Ч 4 modifiers) is the single most important table for this topic.
- `protected` тЙа `default`; the difference shows up **only** in cross-package inheritance scenarios.
- **Mnemonic PDPP** (Private тЖТ Default тЖТ Protected тЖТ Public) gives the order of increasing visibility.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: this Keyword
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** this Keyword # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **`this` keyword** in Java (and analogous constructs in C++/Python) is an **implicit, non-static reference variable** that holds the memory address of the **currently executing object**. It is automatically created by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) inside every instance method, constructor, or instance initializer block, and it points to the object on whose behalf the method or constructor has been invoked. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBCST304 - Module 1):** > Under the 2024 Scheme, `this` keyword is classified as a **fundamental reference-resolution mechanism** within the "Classes and Objects" cognitive cluster. Students are expected to demonstrate its application in disambiguating instance variables, constructor chaining, and method chaining. ### Key Properties of `this` - It is a **final** reference (cannot be reassigned to point to another object). - It is **not** a static member; therefore, it **cannot** be used inside a `static` method or `static` block. - It is the **first parameter** implicitly passed to every instance method (you do not write it, the compiler does). - Its lifetime is bound to the **object's lifetime** on the heap. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ The "Remote Control" Model > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy: The Self-Aware Worker** > Imagine a construction worker named **Ravi** working on multiple identical houses in a colony. Each house is a separate *object* (instance). When Ravi is told, *"Paint YOUR house"*, the word **"YOUR"** is analogous to the `this` keyword. It is a self-referential pointer тАФ a built-in way for an object to say, *"I am referring to myself, not to any other object of the same class."* Just as Ravi can carry his own toolbox (`this.someProperty`) or call his own phone (`this.someMethod()`), any object can use `this` to manipulate its own state. ## 1.3 The 6 Canonical Uses of `this` in Java | # | Use Case | One-Line Description | |---|----------|---------------------| | 1 | **Instance Variable Disambiguation** | Resolves naming conflict between local/parameter variables and instance variables | | 2 | **Constructor Chaining** | Invokes another constructor within the same class using `this(args)` | | 3 | **Method Chaining** | Returns the current object to enable fluent-style calls | | 4 | **Passing Current Object** | Used as an actual argument to other methods | | 5 | **Returning Current Object** | A method can return `this` to allow cascaded invocations | | 6 | **As an Argument in Constructor Call** | `this` can be passed to another constructor's invocation statement | > [!WARNING] > **Common Misconception:** `this` does **NOT** refer to the *class* тАФ it refers to the *object instance*. The class itself is referenced via `ClassName.class` (a `Class` object), which is a fundamentally different concept. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown тАФ How `this` Works Under the JVM The Java compiler transforms every instance method behind the scenes. Consider the following conceptual mapping: $$\text{Source Code:} \quad \texttt{obj.display(name);}$$ $$\text{Compiler-Generated Bytecode Equivalent:} \quad \texttt{void display(String name) \{ this.name = name; \}}$$ Where the compiler silently inserts `this` as a hidden first parameter: $$\texttt{void display(\textbf{Object this}, String name)}$$ ### Step-by-Step Internal Logic - **Step 1 тАФ Object Instantiation:** When `new Student()` is executed, the JVM allocates heap memory and assigns the new object's reference to the local variable `s1`. - **Step 2 тАФ Method Invocation:** When `s1.setName("Ravi")` is called, the JVM pushes `s1`'s reference onto the operand stack as the **first (hidden) argument**. - **Step 3 тАФ Binding to `this`:** Inside `setName`, this hidden argument is bound to the local `this` reference variable. - **Step 4 тАФ Member Access:** Every unprefixed instance member access (`name = name`) is rewritten internally as `this.name = name`. > [!TIP] > This is precisely why static methods cannot use `this` тАФ they are not tied to any specific object, so there is no reference to bind. ## 2.2 The Constructor Chaining Rule Set (KTU High-Yield) Constructor chaining using `this()` follows strict rules that the KTU examiner frequently tests: $$\text{Constructor Chaining Invariant:} \quad \texttt{this()} \text{ must be the FIRST statement in the constructor body}$$ Additional binding rules: $$\text{Rule 1:} \quad \forall c \in \text{Constructors},\ \texttt{this()} \rightarrow \text{Statement Position} = 1$$ $$\text{Rule 2:} \quad \texttt{this()} \ \text{and} \ \texttt{super()} \ \text{cannot coexist in the same constructor}$$ $$\text{Rule 3:} \quad \text{Recursive} \ \texttt{this()} \ \text{invocation causes} \ \textit{StackOverflowError}$$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet Table > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **single most important rapid-revision asset** for any KTU 2024 Scheme exam on this topic. Memorize the boundaries. | # | Scenario | Valid Usage of `this`? | Compiler Action | |---|----------|----------------------|-----------------| | 1 | Inside instance method | тЬЕ Yes | Refers to current invoking object | | 2 | Inside constructor | тЬЕ Yes | Refers to object being constructed | | 3 | Inside static method | тЭМ No | Compile-time error: *"non-static variable this cannot be referenced from a static context"* | | 4 | Inside static block | тЭМ No | Compile-time error | | 5 | As a method parameter | тЬЕ Yes | Passes the current object reference | | 6 | As a return value | тЬЕ Yes | Enables method chaining | | 7 | Inside `this()` call | тЬЕ Yes (constructor chaining) | Must be the first statement | | 8 | Inside anonymous inner class | тЪая╕П Restricted | Refers to outer class's instance, not inner | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Builder Design Pattern (Gang of Four):** `this` is the linchpin of fluent APIs, e.g., `new HttpRequestBuilder().setUrl(...).setMethod(...).build()`. - **Event Handling Frameworks:** GUI libraries (JavaFX, Swing) use `this` to bind an event source to its handler. - **ORM Frameworks (Hibernate, JPA):** Entity classes return `this` from setter methods to enable chained configuration. - **Chained Stream APIs (Java 8+):** Although streams are functional, builder-style utilities still leverage `this`-chaining. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example тАФ Instance Variable Disambiguation Below is the **canonical KTU textbook scenario** where parameter names shadow instance variables. The derivation shows how `this` resolves the ambiguity. ### Source Code ```java class Student { private String name; private int rollNo; public Student(String name, int rollNo) { // Without 'this', both LHS and RHS would refer to the parameter this.name = name; this.rollNo = rollNo; } public void display() { System.out.println("Name: " + this.name + ", Roll: " + this.rollNo); } } ``` ### Logical Derivation тАФ Symbol by Symbol Let us denote the parameter symbol as $p$ and the instance variable symbol as $i$. The compiler's name-resolution algorithm proceeds as: $$\text{Name Lookup Order} = \begin{cases} \text{Local Variable Scope} \rightarrow \text{Block Scope} \rightarrow \text{Instance Scope (via this)} \end{cases}$$ **Step 1 тАФ Constructor Invocation:** The JVM receives a call `new Student("Ravi", 42)`. It allocates a new `Student` object on the heap and stores its reference internally as `this_ref`. **Step 2 тАФ Hidden Parameter Binding:** The constructor's signature is rewritten by the compiler as: $$\texttt{public Student(\textbf{Student this}, String name, int rollNo)}$$ **Step 3 тАФ Ambiguity Resolution:** Consider the bare statement `name = name;`. The compiler applies scope-resolution: $$\text{Left side } \texttt{name} \rightarrow \text{Compiler searches} \rightarrow \text{Found in local scope (parameter)} = p$$ $$\text{Right side } \texttt{name} \rightarrow \text{Compiler searches} \rightarrow \text{Found in local scope (parameter)} = p$$ Therefore, the assignment becomes $p = p$, which is a **no-op** and effectively drops the data. **Step 4 тАФ Inserting `this`:** By writing `this.name = name;`, we force the LHS to bypass local scope: $$\text{LHS} \rightarrow \texttt{this.name} \rightarrow \text{Searches instance scope} \rightarrow i$$ $$\text{RHS} \rightarrow \texttt{name} \rightarrow \text{Searches local scope first} \rightarrow p$$ The resulting assignment is $i = p$, which correctly stores the parameter into the instance variable. **Step 5 тАФ Final Memory State:** $$\text{Heap Object at } \texttt{this\_ref} \rightarrow \{ \texttt{name} = \text{"Ravi"}, \texttt{rollNo} = 42 \}$$ ## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example тАФ Constructor Chaining ```java class Box { int length, breadth, height; // 1-arg constructor Box(int side) { this(side, side, side); // calls 3-arg constructor System.out.println("Cube created"); } // 3-arg constructor Box(int length, int breadth, int height) { this.length = length; this.breadth = breadth; this.height = height; } int volume() { return this.length * this.breadth * this.height; } } ``` ### Derivation of Control Flow When a client calls `new Box(5)`: **Step 1:** JVM invokes `Box(int side)`. **Step 2:** The first statement `this(side, side, side)` is executed. The compiler verifies: $$\text{Position Check:} \quad \text{line}_1 = \texttt{this(side, side, side)} \rightarrow \text{Position} = 1 \ \checkmark$$ **Step 3:** Control transfers to `Box(int, int, int)`. Inside that constructor, `this` still refers to the **same partially-constructed object**. **Step 4:** Instance variables are assigned: $i_{length} = 5$, $i_{breadth} = 5$, $i_{height} = 5$. **Step 5:** Control returns to the original 1-arg constructor. The `println` executes. **Step 6:** Constructor completes; `this` reference is released to GC eligibility. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix тАФ All 6 Use Cases Mapped to Code Patterns | Use Case | Code Pattern | When to Apply | |----------|-------------|---------------| | **Disambiguation** | `this.x = x;` | Parameter shadows field | | **Constructor Chaining** | `this(a, b);` | Reduce code duplication across overloads | | **Method Chaining** | `return this;` | Fluent API design (Builder pattern) | | **Pass as Argument** | `helper.process(this);` | An object needs to give itself to another | | **Return Current Object** | `return this;` from setter | Enable cascading | | **Inner Class Reference** | `OuterClass.this.field;` | Resolve shadowing in non-static inner classes | ## 3.4 Derivation of the Hidden Parameter Cost For a method declared as: $$\texttt{public void display(String name, int age)}$$ The compiler generates the following effective signature: $$\texttt{public void display(\textbf{Object this}, String name, int age)}$$ The size overhead per instance method call is therefore exactly the size of a reference: $$\text{Memory Overhead} = \text{sizeof(JVM reference)} = \text{4 bytes (32-bit) or 8 bytes (64-bit with compressed oops)}$$ > [!NOTE] > This explains why **static methods are slightly more memory-efficient** at call time тАФ no `this` reference is pushed onto the operand stack. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Memory Architecture тАФ `this` Binding to Heap Object ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef stackStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef heapStyle fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef arrowStyle fill:none,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px subgraph STACK["STACK MEMORY"] S1["s1
Reference Variable
0xABC123"] S2["s2
Reference Variable
0xDEF456"] end subgraph HEAP["HEAP MEMORY"] O1["Object 1
name = Ravi
rollNo = 42
this = 0xABC123"] O2["Object 2
name = Anu
rollNo = 17
this = 0xDEF456"] end S1 -.->|points to| O1 S2 -.->|points to| O2 subgraph METHOD["INSIDE setName METHOD"] THIS["this
(hidden parameter)
0xABC123"] end O1 -.->|this is bound to| THIS class S1,S2 stackStyle class O1,O2 heapStyle class THIS heapStyle ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** The diagram visually confirms that: - `s1` and `s2` are **local reference variables** on the stack. - Each object on the heap stores its own identity through the `this` reference. - When `s1.setName("Ravi")` executes, the JVM binds `this` inside `setName` to the same address as `s1` (i.e., `0xABC123`). ## 4.2 Control Flow Diagram тАФ Constructor Chaining ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef validStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef errorStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef chainStyle fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 A["Client calls
new Box 5"]:::chainStyle B["Enter Box int side"]:::validStyle C{"First statement is
this side side side ?"}:::validStyle D["Transfer control to
Box int int int"]:::chainStyle E["Assign instance variables
this.length length
this.breadth breadth
