# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]`
**Define recursion. What are the two essential components that every recursive solution must have?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Recursion is a programming technique in which a method calls itself to solve a problem by breaking it into smaller sub-problems of the same form. (1 mark)

The two essential components are:
1. **Base Case** тАФ A terminating condition that returns a known value without making any further recursive call. It prevents infinite recursion. (1 mark)
2. **Recursive Case** тАФ The branch where the method invokes itself with a **reduced/closer** argument, ensuring progress towards the base case. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024]`
**Differentiate between direct and indirect recursion with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Direct Recursion | Indirect Recursion |
|--------|------------------|--------------------|
| **Definition** | A method calls **itself** directly. | Method A calls B, and B (or some chain) calls A back. |
| **Chain length** | Single method involved. | Cycle of two or more methods. |
| **Example** | `int f(int n) { return f(n-1); }` | `A() { B(); }  B() { A(); }` |
| **Termination** | Base case in the same method. | Base case in any method of the cycle. |

(1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for example/comparison)

---

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2024 Model]`

**Write a Java program to compute the factorial of a given non-negative integer using recursion. Explain the base case, recursive case, and trace the execution for `factorial(5)`. Also, state the time and space complexity.**

#### Part (a) тАФ Java Program with Explanation (7 Marks)

**Java Code:**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class FactorialRecursion {
    static int factorial(int n) {
        if (n == 0)                              // (i) Base case
            return 1;
        return n * factorial(n - 1);             // (ii) Recursive case
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter a non-negative integer: ");
        int n = sc.nextInt();
        System.out.println("Factorial = " + factorial(n));
    }
}
```

**Explanation (Component-wise):**
- **Base case** (`n == 0 тЖТ return 1`): Without this, recursion would never terminate.
- **Recursive case** (`n * factorial(n-1)`): Reduces the problem size by 1 at each step.
- **Recursion terminates** when the argument reaches 0.

(2 marks тАФ code, 2 marks тАФ base case explanation, 2 marks тАФ recursive case explanation, 1 mark тАФ overall flow)

#### Part (b) тАФ Execution Trace & Complexity (7 Marks)

**Stack-Based Execution Trace for `factorial(5)`:**

| Call Number | Invocation | Pending Computation | Returned Value |
|-------------|-----------|---------------------|----------------|
| 1 | `factorial(5)` | `5 * factorial(4)` | тАФ |
| 2 | `factorial(4)` | `4 * factorial(3)` | тАФ |
| 3 | `factorial(3)` | `3 * factorial(2)` | тАФ |
| 4 | `factorial(2)` | `2 * factorial(1)` | тАФ |
| 5 | `factorial(1)` | `1 * factorial(0)` | тАФ |
| 6 | `factorial(0)` | Base case hit | `1` |
| 5 returns | тАФ | `1 * 1` | `1` |
| 4 returns | тАФ | `2 * 1` | `2` |
| 3 returns | тАФ | `3 * 2` | `6` |
| 2 returns | тАФ | `4 * 6` | `24` |
| 1 returns | тАФ | `5 * 24` | `120` |

**Final Output:** `Factorial = 120`

**Complexity Analysis (3 marks):**
- **Time Complexity:** Each call performs $\mathcal{O}(1)$ work, and there are $n+1$ calls $\Rightarrow \mathcal{O}(n)$.
- **Space Complexity:** The call stack holds $n+1$ frames simultaneously at the deepest point $\Rightarrow \mathcal{O}(n)$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024 Model]`

**Explain the Tower of Hanoi problem. Write its recursive algorithm in Java. Derive the recurrence relation for the number of moves and solve it to obtain the closed-form expression $T(n) = 2^{n} - 1$.**

#### Part (a) тАФ Problem Definition and Recurrence Derivation (7 Marks)

**Problem Statement (1 mark):**
The Tower of Hanoi puzzle consists of $n$ disks of distinct sizes placed on a source peg, and two empty auxiliary and destination pegs. The goal is to move all disks from the source to the destination following the rules:
- Only one disk may be moved at a time.
- A larger disk may never rest on a smaller disk.

**Recursive Decomposition (3 marks):**
To move $n$ disks from Source `S` to Destination `D` using Auxiliary `A`:
1. Move the top $n-1$ disks from `S` тЖТ `A` (using `D` as auxiliary).
2. Move the largest disk (disk $n$) from `S` тЖТ `D` directly тАФ **1 move**.
3. Move the $n-1$ disks from `A` тЖТ `D` (using `S` as auxiliary).

**Recurrence Relation (3 marks):**

$$
T(n) \;=\; 2\,T(n-1) \;+\; 1, \qquad T(1) \;=\; 1
$$

#### Part (b) тАФ Recurrence Solution and Java Algorithm (7 Marks)

**Solving the Recurrence (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2\,T(n-1) + 1 \\
     &= 2\bigl(2\,T(n-2) + 1\bigr) + 1 = 2^{2}\,T(n-2) + 2 + 1 \\
     &= 2^{2}\bigl(2\,T(n-3) + 1\bigr) + 2 + 1 = 2^{3}\,T(n-3) + 2^{2} + 2 + 1 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
     &= 2^{k}\,T(n-k) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} 2^{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting $k = n - 1$ and applying $T(1) = 1$:

$$
T(n) \;=\; 2^{n-1}\cdot 1 \;+\; (2^{n-1} - 1) \;=\; 2^{n} - 1
$$

**Java Algorithm (3 marks):**

```java
public class TowerOfHanoi {
    static void hanoi(int n, char src, char aux, char dst) {
        if (n == 1) {
            System.out.println("Move disk 1 from " + src + " to " + dst);
            return;
        }
        hanoi(n - 1, src, dst, aux);
        System.out.println("Move disk " + n + " from " + src + " to " + dst);
        hanoi(n - 1, aux, src, dst);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        hanoi(3, 'A', 'B', 'C');
    }
}
```

**Sample Output for $n = 3$ (verifies $T(3) = 7$ moves):**

```
Move disk 1 from A to C
Move disk 2 from A to B
Move disk 1 from C to B
Move disk 3 from A to C
Move disk 1 from B to A
Move disk 2 from B to C
Move disk 1 from A to C
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Missing Base Case (тАУ2 to тАУ3 marks):** Many students write only the recursive case and forget `if (n == 0) return 1;` or equivalent. The examiner will deduct heavily.
> 2. **No Execution Trace (тАУ2 marks):** For 14-mark questions, the **dry-run / stack-trace table** is mandatory. Writing code without trace usually yields тЙд 8/14.
> 3. **Confusing Tail and Non-Tail Recursion (тАУ1 to тАУ2 marks):** A common KTU trap question: *"Is `factorial` tail recursive?"* Answer: **No**, because the multiplication `n * ...` happens **after** the recursive call returns.
> 4. **Skipping Complexity Analysis (тАУ1 to тАУ2 marks):** Always state both **Time** $\mathcal{O}(n)$ and **Space** $\mathcal{O}(n)$ explicitly.
> 5. **Forgetting the Two Base Cases in Fibonacci (тАУ2 marks):** Fibonacci needs **both** $F(0)=0$ and $F(1)=1$. Missing one is a frequent costly error.
> 6. **Variable Shadowing in Recursion:** Don't reuse the loop variable name as the parameter inside the recursive case.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ Recursion (Module 2, PBCST304)**

- **Definition:** A method that calls **itself** to solve smaller instances of the same problem.
- **Two Mandatory Parts:** (1) **Base Case** тАФ termination condition, (2) **Recursive Case** тАФ self-call with reduced argument.
- **Direct Recursion:** Method calls itself. **Indirect Recursion:** Cycle of two or more methods.
- **Tail Recursion:** Recursive call is the **last** statement. **Non-Tail Recursion:** Work remains after the recursive call returns.
- **Factorial:** $n! = n \cdot (n-1)!$, base $0! = 1$, time $\mathcal{O}(n)$, space $\mathcal{O}(n)$.
- **Fibonacci:** $F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2)$, two bases $F(0)=0, F(1)=1$, naive time $\mathcal{O}(\varphi^{n})$.
- **Tower of Hanoi:** $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1$, closed form $T(n) = 2^{n} - 1$, time $\mathcal{O}(2^{n})$.
- **GCD (Euclidean):** $\gcd(a,b) = \gcd(b,\, a \bmod b)$, base case $b = 0$.
- **Call Stack:** Each recursive call pushes a new **stack frame**; maximum depth equals the recursion depth.
- **StackOverflowError:** Occurs when base case is unreachable or recursion depth is too large.
- **Memoization:** Caches previously computed sub-problem results to avoid exponential blow-up (essential for Fibonacci).
- **Recursion vs Iteration:** Recursion = concise but memory-heavy; Iteration = verbose but memory-efficient.
- **When to Use Recursion:** Tree/graph traversals, divide-and-conquer, backtracking, mathematical self-similar definitions.
- **When to Avoid Recursion:** Simple linear loops, performance-critical code, deep recursions exceeding stack size.
- **Java Specific:** No built-in tail-call optimization in standard JVM. Use `Math.BigInteger` for very large factorials to avoid overflow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Object-Oriented Programming (specifically **Java**, as prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus for PBCST304), a **Static Member** is a class-level member (variable, method, block, or nested class) that is **declared with the `static` keyword** and belongs to the **class itself** rather than to any individual instance (object) of that class.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus terminology:

> A static variable is shared by **all instances of the class** тАФ there exists exactly **one copy** of the static variable regardless of how many objects of the class are created. A static method belongs to the class and can be invoked **without creating an object**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PBCST304, Module 2):** Static members are a core OOP concept that bridges **procedural and object-oriented paradigms**. They are essential for implementing **utility/helper classes**, **constants**, **factory methods**, and the `main()` method itself. Examiners frequently test the distinction between *class-level* (static) and *instance-level* (non-static) lifetime, scope, and memory.

The four major types of static members in Java are:

1. **Static Variables** (also called *class variables*)
2. **Static Methods** (also called *class methods*)
3. **Static Initialization Blocks** (also called *static blocks*)
4. **Static Nested Classes** (also called *static inner classes*)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ "The Common Notice Board"

Imagine a **college classroom**:

- Every **student** is an *object* (instance) of the class `Student`. Each student has their own **personal belongings** тАФ their own bag, their own notebook. These are **instance variables**.
- But there is **one common notice board** at the front of the class. It is **shared by every student**. If the principal posts an announcement on it, *every* student sees the same content. If the principal erases a notice, *all* students lose access to it simultaneously.

That **common notice board** is the **static member**.

- It is **created once** тАФ when the class itself is loaded into memory by the JVM.
- It is **shared by all objects** of that class.
- It can be **accessed even before any object is created**, simply by using the class name.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line takeaway for exams:** "Static = belongs to the *class*, not the *object*. One copy, shared by all."

## 1.3 Where Static Members Reside in Memory

| Memory Area | Stores | Lifetime |
|-------------|--------|----------|
| **Method Area** (part of JVM heap in modern JVMs) | Class metadata, **static variables**, static methods bytecode, constant pool | From class loading until class unloading (typically JVM shutdown) |
| **Heap** | Instance variables (per-object state) | From `new` execution until object becomes unreachable and is GC'd |
| **Stack** | Local variables, method call frames | Per-method-invocation |

**Bold constants/metrics to remember:**
- **One copy per class** (not per object).
- **Loaded at class-loading time** тАФ *before* any object is instantiated.
- **Destroyed at class-unloading time** тАФ typically when the JVM shuts down.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Static Variables (Class Variables)

A **static variable** is declared inside a class with the `static` modifier but **outside any method, constructor, or block**.

### Operational Logic тАФ Step by Step

1. **Declaration Phase:** A static variable is declared with the `static` keyword.
2. **Class Loading Phase:** When the class is first referenced (e.g., `ClassName.staticVar` or `new ClassName()`), the **ClassLoader** loads the `.class` file into the Method Area.
3. **Default Initialization:** The static variable receives its **default value** (numeric тЖТ 0, boolean тЖТ false, object reference тЖТ null) **at the time of class loading**, *before* any object exists.
4. **Explicit Initialization:** The variable is then assigned its declared value (either inline at declaration or inside a `static` block).
5. **Sharing Phase:** All objects of the class **read from and write to the same single memory location**. There is no per-object copy.
6. **Access Phase:** It can be accessed using the class name (`ClassName.variable`) or through any object reference (`obj.variable`) тАФ though the **class-name access is the recommended KTU-exam-friendly style**.

### Why Use Static Variables?

- To store **constants** that are common to all objects (e.g., `static final double PI = 3.14159;`).
- To **count the number of objects** created (`static int count;`).
- To share a **common resource or configuration** across all instances (e.g., a database connection pool reference).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why** do we need static variables? Imagine you want to assign a unique roll number to every student. Each student object needs a *unique* number. If you store `nextRollNo` as an *instance* variable, every newly created object starts from 1. If you store it as a *static* variable, **it is shared**, and the counter increments globally тАФ exactly what you need for auto-numbering.

## 2.2 Static Methods (Class Methods)

A **static method** is declared with the `static` keyword. It belongs to the class and can be called without creating an object.

### Operational Rules (The "Big Four" Restrictions)

A static method **cannot** directly:

1. Use `this` keyword тАФ because `this` refers to the current *object*, and static context has no object.
2. Use `super` keyword тАФ same reason; `super` refers to the parent class object.
3. Call a non-static (instance) method *directly* тАФ instance methods require an object.
4. Access a non-static (instance) variable *directly* тАФ instance variables are stored per-object.

> [!NOTE]
> **The reverse is allowed:** Instance methods *can* access static variables and call static methods directly. This is a **favourite KTU question**.

### Why Use Static Methods?

- **Utility/Helper methods** that do not depend on object state (e.g., `Math.sqrt()`, `Integer.parseInt()`).
- **Factory methods** that produce instances (e.g., `LocalDate.now()`).
- **The `main()` method** itself, which is `public static void main(String[] args)` тАФ must be static so the JVM can invoke it **before any object is created**.

## 2.3 Static Initialization Blocks (Static Blocks)

A **static block** is a block of code enclosed in braces `{}` and preceded by the `static` keyword, used to initialize static variables.

```java
static {
    // initialization logic
}
```

### Operational Rules

- Executed **exactly once**, at the time of class loading.
- Multiple static blocks (if present) execute **in the order they appear** in the source file.
- Executes **before** the `main()` method and **before** any constructor.

## 2.4 Static Nested Classes

A **static nested class** is a class defined inside another class with the `static` modifier. It behaves like a top-level class for access purposes and **does not require an instance of the outer class**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet (Rules + Formulas Table)

| # | Rule / Concept | Formula or Statement | Engineering Utility |
|---|----------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Number of static variable copies per class | **N = 1** (regardless of object count `O`) | Shared configuration across all components |
| 2 | Memory location of static variables | **Method Area (JVM)** | Persists for JVM lifetime |
| 3 | Static method access syntax | `ClassName.methodName()` | Recommended over object access |
| 4 | `this` and `super` in static context | **Not allowed** | Static has no object context |
| 5 | Object can access static member? | **Yes** (legal but discouraged) | Legacy/backward-compat in Java |
| 6 | Static block execution count | **1** per class load | One-time expensive initialization |
| 7 | Execution order on class load | Static vars тЖТ Static blocks тЖТ `main()` тЖТ Constructors | Critical for understanding output-tracing questions |
| 8 | Static method overriding | **Not possible** (method hiding only) | Compile-time polymorphism differs |
| 9 | `static final` variable | Compile-time **constant** | Inlined at compile time for performance |
| 10 | Static nested class needs outer object? | **No** | Improves modularity without coupling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The execution order `Static variable default init тЖТ Static block тЖТ main() тЖТ Constructor` is the **single most tested concept** in KTU Module 2 output-tracing questions. Memorize it cold.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **`System.out`**: A static reference in Java тАФ shared across the entire application.
- **`Math` class**: All methods are static тАФ you never create a `Math` object.
- **Singleton Design Pattern**: Uses a `private static` instance to ensure only one object of a class exists.
- **Logging Frameworks (SLF4J, Log4j)**: Use static `Logger` instances for global logging access.
- **JDBC `DriverManager`**: Static methods register and retrieve database drivers globally.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs

Since OOP is rule-based rather than formula-based, this section provides **exhaustive step-by-step execution analysis** of code involving static members тАФ the most common KTU 14-mark question type.

## 3.1 Walkthrough A тАФ Static Variable Object Counting

```java
class Student {
    static int count = 0;          // class variable
    int rollNo;                    // instance variable
    Student() {
        count++;
        rollNo = count;
    }
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Roll: " + rollNo + ", Total Students: " + count);
    }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student();
        s1.display();
        Student s2 = new Student();
        s2.display();
        Student s3 = new Student();
        s3.display();
        System.out.println("Final count via class: " + Student.count);
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

**Step 1 тАФ Class Loading (`Student` class referenced for the first time):**
The JVM ClassLoader loads `Student.class` into the Method Area. Static variable `count` is allocated and initialized to its default value `0`, then to its declared value `0`.

**Step 2 тАФ First Object `s1` Creation (`new Student()`):**
- Memory for `s1` is allocated on the Heap. Instance variable `rollNo` gets default `0`.
- Constructor executes: `count` increments to `1`. `rollNo` becomes `1`.
- `s1.display()` prints: `Roll: 1, Total Students: 1`.

**Step 3 тАФ Second Object `s2` Creation:**
- New Heap allocation for `s2`. `rollNo` defaults to `0`.
- Constructor: `count` increments to `2` (same shared memory location as before). `rollNo` becomes `2`.
- `s2.display()` prints: `Roll: 2, Total Students: 2`.

**Step 4 тАФ Third Object `s3` Creation:**
- New Heap allocation for `s3`. `rollNo` defaults to `0`.
- Constructor: `count` becomes `3`. `rollNo` becomes `3`.
- `s3.display()` prints: `Roll: 3, Total Students: 3`.

**Step 5 тАФ Class-name Access `Student.count`:**
Static variable is read directly without any object. Output: `Final count via class: 3`.

### Final Output

```
Roll: 1, Total Students: 1
Roll: 2, Total Students: 2
Roll: 3, Total Students: 3
Final count via class: 3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' behind the result:** Even though `count` is incremented by each *constructor* (an instance method), the underlying *variable* is static. All three constructors read and write to the **same single memory cell** in the Method Area. If `count` were non-static, each object would have its own copy and the final value would still be `1` after each constructor.

## 3.2 Walkthrough B тАФ Static Block + Static Variable + Constructor Execution Order

```java
class Demo {
    static int x = initializeX();
    static int y;
    static {
        System.out.println("Static block executed. y = 10");
        y = 10;
    }
    static int initializeX() {
        System.out.println("Static method initializeX() called. x = 5");
        return 5;
    }
    Demo() {
        System.out.println("Constructor called.");
    }
    void show() {
        System.out.println("x = " + x + ", y = " + y);
    }
}

public class TestOrder {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("--- Inside main, before object ---");
        Demo d1 = new Demo();
        Demo d2 = new Demo();
        d1.show();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

**Step 1 тАФ JVM invokes `main()` method of `TestOrder`.**
The statement `System.out.println("--- Inside main, before object ---");` runs first. Output line 1 appears.

**Step 2 тАФ First reference to class `Demo` triggers class loading.**
The class is loaded. Static initialization begins **in the textual order of declaration in the source code**.

**Step 3 тАФ First static declaration: `static int x = initializeX();`**
The method `initializeX()` is called. It prints `Static method initializeX() called. x = 5` (Output line 2) and returns `5`. Now `x = 5`.

**Step 4 тАФ Second static declaration: `static int y;`**
`y` gets default value `0`.

**Step 5 тАФ Static block executes.**
Prints `Static block executed. y = 10` (Output line 3). Then assigns `y = 10`.

**Step 6 тАФ `new Demo()` for `d1`:**
Constructor runs. Output line 4: `Constructor called.`

**Step 7 тАФ `new Demo()` for `d2`:**
Constructor runs again. Output line 5: `Constructor called.`
*(Note: static block did NOT run again тАФ it runs once per class load.)*

**Step 8 тАФ `d1.show()`:**
Output line 6: `x = 5, y = 10`.

### Final Output

```
--- Inside main, before object ---
Static method initializeX() called. x = 5
Static block executed. y = 10
Constructor called.
Constructor called.
x = 5, y = 10
```

### Mathematical Representation of Execution Order

We can model the execution as a sequence. Let `L(c)` denote the time at which class `c` is loaded. Let `S_i` be the i-th static initializer in source order, and `C_j` be the j-th constructor call.

$$
\text{Order} = \underbrace{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_n}_{\text{all static initializers, once}} \;\rightarrow\; \underbrace{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k}_{\text{constructor, per object}}
$$

For our example: $\text{Order} = (\text{initializeX() call},\; \text{static block},\; C_{d1},\; C_{d2})$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why** does the static block not run for `d2`? Because the class is already loaded into the JVM Method Area after the first object. Class loading (and hence static initialization) is a **one-time event per ClassLoader** during the JVM's lifetime.

## 3.3 Walkthrough C тАФ Static Method Restrictions (Compile-Time Error Analysis)

```java
class Checker {
    int instanceVar = 100;
    static int staticVar = 200;
    void instanceMethod() {
        System.out.println("Instance method can access staticVar: " + staticVar);
        System.out.println("Instance method can access instanceVar: " + instanceVar);
    }
    static void staticMethod() {
        System.out.println("Static method can access staticVar: " + staticVar);
        // System.out.println(instanceVar);   // LINE A: COMPILE ERROR
        // instanceMethod();                    // LINE B: COMPILE ERROR
        // System.out.println(this);            // LINE C: COMPILE ERROR
    }
}
```

### Logical Analysis (Line by Line)

| Line | Statement | Verdict | Why (in plain English) |
|------|-----------|---------|------------------------|
| Inside `instanceMethod()` | Access `staticVar` | **Allowed** | An object can see class-level data. |
| Inside `instanceMethod()` | Access `instanceVar` | **Allowed** | The method belongs to the same object. |
| Inside `staticMethod()` | Access `staticVar` | **Allowed** | Static method belongs to the class itself. |
| Line A | Access `instanceVar` | **Compile Error** | `staticMethod` has no `this` reference; it does not know *which* object's `instanceVar` to read. |
| Line B | Call `instanceMethod()` | **Compile Error** | Calling an instance method requires an object. A static method has no associated object. |
| Line C | Use `this` | **Compile Error** | `this` is an implicit reference to the current object. Static methods have no current object. |

### Algebraic Summary

Let `C` be a class. Define:

$$
\text{Accessible}_{\text{static}}(x) = 
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if } x \text{ is static} \\
0, & \text{if } x \text{ is non-static (instance member)}
\end{cases}
$$

Then in a static context, only members where $\text{Accessible}_{\text{static}}(x) = 1$ can be referenced directly.

For instance context:

$$
\text{Accessible}_{\text{instance}}(x) = 1 \quad \forall x \in \text{class members}
$$

(Instance context can access both static and non-static members directly.)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' in one sentence:** Static context represents the *class* acting on its own behalf before any object exists. Instance context represents an *object* acting on its own behalf тАФ and the object carries both its own data and the class's shared data with it.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class Loading and Static Memory Architecture (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[JVM Starts] --> B[main method invoked]
    B --> C{Class Reference Detected}
    C -->|First time| D[ClassLoader loads .class file]
    C -->|Already loaded| H[Skip to execution]
    D --> E[Method Area: Class Metadata]
    E --> F[Static Variables allocated]
    F --> G[Static initializers run in order]
    G --> I[main body executes]
    I --> J{new Object requested?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Heap: Allocate instance]
    K --> L[Instance variables get default values]
    L --> M[Constructor runs]
    M --> N[Object reference returned]
    J -->|No| O[Continue main flow]
    N --> O
    O --> P{More objects?}
    P -->|Yes| J
    P -->|No| Q[main returns]
    Q --> R[JVM exits / class unloaded]
```

## 4.2 Static vs Instance Memory Layout (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MA[Method Area - One per Class]
        SV[static int count = 0]
        SM[static method displayInfo]
        SC[static constant PI = 3.14]
    end
    subgraph H1[Heap - Object s1]
        IV1[int rollNo = 1]
    end
    subgraph H2[Heap - Object s2]
        IV2[int rollNo = 2]
    end
    subgraph H3[Heap - Object s3]
        IV3[int rollNo = 3]
    end
    SV -.shared reference.-> IV1
    SV -.shared reference.-> IV2
    SV -.shared reference.-> IV3
    SM -.invoked via.-> H1
    SM -.invoked via.-> H2
    SM -.invoked via.-> H3
```

## 4.3 Static Block Execution Order Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Class Loading Triggered] --> S1[Step 1: Default-initialize all static vars]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: Execute static initializers and static blocks in textual order]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: main method begins]
    S3 --> S4{new keyword encountered?}
    S4 -->|Yes| S5[Step 4a: Allocate Heap memory for object]
    S4 -->|No| S7[Continue main]
    S5 --> S6[Step 4b: Execute instance initializers and constructor]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8{main ends?}
    S8 -->|No| S4
    S8 -->|Yes| S9[JVM may unload class]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Notice that the **Method Area** block contains *one* copy of `count`, while the **Heap** contains *three separate* copies of `rollNo` (one per object). The dotted arrows from `count` to each object's instance method indicate that instance methods can *read* the shared static variable, but they are not "owners" of it.

## 4.4 Access-Permission Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Member access attempt] --> Q{Context is static or instance?}
    Q -->|Static context| R{Target member is static?}
    Q -->|Instance context| X[ALLOWED - access any member]
    R -->|Yes| Y[ALLOWED - direct access]
    R -->|No| Z[DENIED - needs explicit object reference]
    Y --> END1[Compile OK]
    X --> END1
    Z --> END2[COMPILE ERROR]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** This decision flow is the **mental model** examiners expect you to apply during output-tracing problems. Whenever you see a static method in code, mentally run it through the "Static context тЖТ only static targets allowed" rule.
