### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Write a Java program to demonstrate how the `super` keyword can be used to invoke the **parent class constructor** and access a **shadowed instance variable** of the parent class. Explain the output with a step-by-step trace. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **rules and restrictions** associated with the use of the `super` keyword in Java. What happens if the parent class does not have a no-argument constructor and the subclass does not explicitly invoke `super()`? Illustrate with a code example. **(7 marks)**

---

#### тЬЕ Model Solution тАФ Part (a) (7 marks)

**Java Program:**

```java
class Animal {
    String name = "Generic Animal";   // Parent variable

    Animal() {
        System.out.println("Animal() constructor invoked");
    }

    Animal(String name) {
        this.name = name;
        System.out.println("Animal(String) constructor invoked with name = " + name);
    }

    void speak() {
        System.out.println("Animal speaks generically.");
    }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
    String name = "Buddy";   // Shadows parent variable

    Dog() {
        super("Bruno");      // Calls Animal(String)
        System.out.println("Dog() constructor invoked");
    }

    void printNames() {
        System.out.println("Child name   = " + name);
        System.out.println("Parent name  = " + super.name);
    }
}

public class SuperDemoA {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Dog d = new Dog();
        d.printNames();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Execution Trace:**

| Step | Action | Resulting State |
|------|--------|-----------------|
| 1 | `new Dog()` triggers `Dog()` | Constructor begins |
| 2 | `super("Bruno")` (first line) calls `Animal(String)` | `Animal.name = "Bruno"` |
| 3 | Control returns to `Dog()` body | `Dog.name = "Buddy"` (from initializer) |
| 4 | `d.printNames()` called | Prints both names |
| 5 | `name` resolves to child's `"Buddy"` | Output: `Child name = Buddy` |
| 6 | `super.name` skips child, resolves to parent's `"Bruno"` | Output: `Parent name = Bruno` |

**Predicted Output:**

```
Animal(String) constructor invoked with name = Bruno
Dog() constructor invoked
Child name   = Buddy
Parent name  = Bruno
```

**Mark Distribution:**
- 2 marks: Correct class hierarchy and syntax
- 2 marks: Use of `super("Bruno")` to invoke parent constructor
- 2 marks: Use of `super.name` to access shadowed variable
- 1 mark: Correct output trace

---

#### тЬЕ Model Solution тАФ Part (b) (7 marks)

**Rules and Restrictions of the `super` Keyword:**

1. **`super` cannot be used in a `static` method or static block**, because `super` refers to an instance and static context has no enclosing instance. *(1 mark)*

2. **`super()` or `super(args)` must be the first statement** in a subclass constructor if it is used. *(1 mark)*

3. **`this()` and `super()` cannot appear in the same constructor**, since both demand the first-statement position. *(1 mark)*

4. **Implicit `super()` insertion:** If a subclass constructor does not explicitly call `super(...)`, the compiler automatically inserts a no-argument `super();` call. *(1 mark)*

5. **`super.super` is illegal** тАФ Java does not support skipping intermediate levels in the inheritance chain. *(1 mark)*

**Illustrative Code тАФ Failure Scenario:**

```java
class Parent {
    Parent(int x) {                         // Only parameterized constructor
        System.out.println("Parent(int) called with " + x);
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    Child() {
        // No explicit super() call here
        // Compiler auto-inserts: super();
        // But Parent has NO no-arg constructor!
        // => COMPILE-TIME ERROR
        System.out.println("Child() called");
    }
}

public class SuperErrorDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new Child();
    }
}
```

**Compiler Error Message:**

```
error: no suitable constructor found for Parent()
    Child() {
         ^
  constructor Parent.Parent(int) cannot be applied to given types
```

**Resolution:** The child must explicitly call `super(10);` as the first line.

**Mark Distribution:**
- 3 marks: Enumeration of rules
- 2 marks: Code example showing the error
- 2 marks: Correct resolution explanation

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 2]`

**(a)** With a suitable Java program, demonstrate how the `super` keyword is used to call an **overridden method** of the parent class. Show the difference between calling the method normally and calling it with the `super` prefix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **order of execution** of constructors in a multi-level inheritance hierarchy in Java. How does the `super()` call ensure that parent class members are initialized before the subclass members? Provide a three-level inheritance example. **(7 marks)**

---

#### тЬЕ Model Solution тАФ Part (a) (7 marks)

**Java Program:**

```java
class Shape {
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a generic Shape.");
    }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Circle.");
        super.draw();    // Explicitly call parent version
        System.out.println("Circle drawing complete.");
    }
}

public class SuperMethodDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Circle c = new Circle();
        c.draw();
    }
}
```

**Execution Analysis:**

| Call Site | Resolved To | Reason |
|-----------|-------------|--------|
| `c.draw()` (external) | `Circle.draw()` | Polymorphic dispatch via v-table |
| `this.draw()` (inside Circle) | `Circle.draw()` | Normal binding, would cause infinite recursion |
| `super.draw()` (inside Circle) | `Shape.draw()` | Bypasses virtual dispatch, binds to parent |

**Predicted Output:**

```
Drawing a Circle.
Drawing a generic Shape.
Circle drawing complete.
```

**Significance:** This pattern is the essence of the **Template Method** design тАФ the child extends behaviour by invoking the parent's logic at a controlled point rather than duplicating it.

**Mark Distribution:**
- 2 marks: Correct method overriding
- 2 marks: Correct use of `super.draw()`
- 2 marks: Output explanation
- 1 mark: Mention of Template Method / extension pattern

---

#### тЬЕ Model Solution тАФ Part (b) (7 marks)

**Three-Level Hierarchy Example:**

```java
class Grandparent {
    Grandparent() {
        System.out.println("1. Grandparent() constructor");
    }
}

class Parent2 extends Grandparent {
    Parent2() {
        System.out.println("2. Parent2() constructor");
    }
}

class Child2 extends Parent2 {
    Child2() {
        System.out.println("3. Child2() constructor");
    }
}

public class ConstructorChainDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Child2 obj = new Child2();
    }
}
```

**Predicted Output (Top-Down Order):**

```
1. Grandparent() constructor
2. Parent2() constructor
3. Child2() constructor
```

**Conceptual Explanation:**

1. The JVM begins executing `Child2()`.
2. Its first statement is the implicit `super();` тЖТ calls `Parent2()`.
3. `Parent2()`'s first statement is the implicit `super();` тЖТ calls `Grandparent()`.
4. `Grandparent()`'s first statement is the implicit `super();` тЖТ calls `Object()`.
5. `Object()` runs to completion; control unwinds downward.
6. Only after `Grandparent()` finishes does `Parent2()`'s body run.
7. Only after `Parent2()` finishes does `Child2()`'s body run.

This guarantees that **all inherited members are fully initialized** before the child class uses them тАФ preventing the use of uninitialized state.

**Mark Distribution:**
- 3 marks: Correct hierarchical code
- 2 marks: Accurate execution order explanation
- 2 marks: Justification of why this order is enforced

---

## ЁЯЪи KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting that `super()` is implicit** тАФ If the question shows a constructor with no explicit `super()` call, students often assume no parent constructor runs. **Marks lost: 2тАУ3** for the missing trace step.
>
> 2. **Confusing `super.x` with `super()`** тАФ The dot-suffix `super.x` is a *member access*; the parenthesis `super()` is a *constructor invocation*. Mixing them in code yields compile errors.
>
> 3. **Claiming `super` can be used in `static` methods** тАФ This is a **hard compile error**. Examiners specifically test this misconception.
>
> 4. **Believing `super.super` is valid** тАФ Java does not allow direct grandparent access. Students must traverse one level at a time.
>
> 5. **Writing `super()` after some other statement** тАФ Java mandates that `super()` be the **first statement**. Any preceding print or assignment causes a compile error.
>
> **Pro Tip:** When asked to "explain the output," always trace **every constructor call** including the implicit `super()` insertions. The KTU answer key explicitly rewards step-by-step traces.

---

## ЁЯОп Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Pin This!)**

- тЬЕ `super` is a **reference variable** to the immediate parent class object.
- тЬЕ Three uses: **variable access**, **method invocation**, **constructor chaining**.
- тЬЕ `super` **resolves shadowing** of same-named instance variables.
- тЬЕ `super.method()` calls the **overridden parent version**, bypassing virtual dispatch.
- тЬЕ `super(args)` calls a **parent constructor** and must be the **first statement**.
- тЬЕ If no `super()` is written, the compiler **auto-inserts** a no-argument `super();`.
- тЬЕ Auto-insertion **fails** if the parent lacks a no-arg constructor тЖТ compile error.
- тЬЕ `super` **cannot be used in `static` methods** or `static` blocks.
- тЬЕ `super.super.x` is **illegal** in Java.
- тЬЕ `this()` and `super()` **cannot coexist** in the same constructor.
- тЬЕ Constructor chain **always terminates at `Object()`**, regardless of hierarchy depth.
- тЬЕ Execution order is **top-down**: ancestors initialize before descendants.
- тЬЕ Memory-wise, `super` is an **offset pointer** to the embedded parent sub-object, not a separate allocation.
- тЬЕ Common in production: **Template Method pattern**, **framework extension** (Spring, JUnit, JavaFX), **lifecycle hooks** (Android `onCreate`).
- тЬЕ Frequently asked in KTU as 3-mark (definition) and 7-mark (code + trace) questions.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: protected Members
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** protected Members # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition In Object-Oriented Programming, an **access specifier** (or access modifier) controls the visibility and accessibility of class members (data members and member functions) from other parts of the program. The three fundamental access specifiers in C++ (and conceptually in Java/Python) are: - **private** - **protected** - **public** A **`protected`** member is a hybrid access level that strikes a balance between encapsulation and inheritance. Formally, in the C++ Standard (ISO/IEC 14882), a protected member is accessible: 1. **Within its own class** where it is declared. 2. **Within friends** of that class. 3. **Within any class derived (inherited) from that class** тАФ but only through an object whose type is that of the derived class (or a class further derived from it), not through an object of the base class type. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Interpretation):** > *"Protected members are accessible to the class itself, to its friends, and to any class derived from it. They are not accessible to non-member functions or to unrelated classes."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ The "Family Vault" Intuition Imagine a **Family Vault** kept inside a house: - The **private members** are like the **owner's personal diary** тАФ locked away; only the owner can read it. Not even the children (derived class) can read it directly. - The **public members** are like the **front lawn** тАФ open to absolutely everyone: neighbors, strangers, the postman. - The **protected members** are like the **family heirloom locked in the vault** тАФ accessible to the **owner (base class)**, the **children and grandchildren (derived classes)**, and **trusted family friends (`friend` keyword)**. But the **stranger on the street (an unrelated class or main function)** still cannot access it. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The keyword `protected` is the *bridge* between strict encapsulation (`private`) and open access (`public`). It exists *primarily* to support **inheritance**, allowing derived classes to manipulate inherited state directly without exposing that state to the rest of the program. ## 1.3 Why Does This Access Level Exist? | Encapsulation Need | Solution Provided by `protected` | |--------------------|----------------------------------| | Derived class needs base class data to function properly. | `protected` lets derived class use it directly. | | Unrelated code should NOT touch base class internals. | `protected` denies access to outside classes. | | Friend functions need special access. | `protected` permits friend access. | > [!TIP] > A common KTU exam phrasing: *"Why can't derived classes access `private` members directly?"* The answer: because `private` enforces *strict* encapsulation. `protected` relaxes this **only** for trusted family (inherited classes). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three-Tier Access Model In C++ (the primary language for KTU OOP theory), every class member is bound to one of three access regions declared inside the class body. The default access for a `class` is `private`; the default access for a `struct` is `public`. ### 2.1.1 Access Resolution Rules (C++) A protected member `m` of class `Base` is accessible at a point `P` in the program if **any one** of the following holds: - `P` lies inside a member function, or a friend function/class, of `Base`. - `P` lies inside a member function, or a friend function/class, of a class `Derived` that publicly/protectedly inherits from `Base`, **AND** the access is through a pointer/reference/object of type `Derived` (or a class derived from `Derived`). > [!WARNING] > **Critical KTU Pitfall:** A derived class **cannot** access a protected base member through a pointer of the **base class type**. It must use a pointer/reference of its **own type** or a further-derived type. This is a favourite 7-mark theory question. ## 2.2 Inheritance Type ├Ч Member Access Interaction Matrix When a base class is inherited, the access level of inherited members transforms based on the inheritance mode. The table below is the **single most-tested KTU matrix** for this module. | Base Class Member | `public` Inheritance | `protected` Inheritance | `private` Inheritance | |-------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|------------------------| | `public` `m` | remains `public` | becomes `protected` | becomes `private` | | `protected` `m` | remains `protected` | remains `protected` | becomes `private` | | `private` `m` | **inaccessible** | **inaccessible** | **inaccessible** | > [!NOTE] > The accessibility of `private` members is *never* changed by inheritance тАФ they simply **cannot be accessed directly** by the derived class regardless of inheritance mode. They are inherited but hidden. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Rule / Formula | Engineering Use | |---------|----------------|-----------------| | Protected member access scope | $\text{Scope} = \text{Base} \cup \text{Friends} \cup \text{Derived}_{\text{self-type}}$ | Designing extensible class hierarchies. | | Visibility after public inheritance | $\text{Access}_{\text{after}} = \text{Access}_{\text{before}}$ | Preserving interface contract in IS-A relationships. | | Visibility after protected inheritance | $\text{Access}_{\text{after}} = \min(\text{Access}_{\text{before}}, \text{protected})$ | Controlled derived-chain exposure. | | Visibility after private inheritance | $\text{Access}_{\text{after}} = \text{private}$ for all non-private members | Implementation inheritance (HAS-A via inheritance). | | Rule of thumb for `protected` | Use only when derived class **must** manipulate base internals AND outside world must NOT see them. | GUI frameworks, plugin architectures, base device drivers. | | Friend + protected overlap | $\text{Access} = \text{true}$ if entity is in friend list. | Operator overloading, factory patterns. | > [!IMPORTANT] > $\text{Access}_{\text{after}} = \min(\text{Access}_{\text{before}}, \text{Mode})$ where the ordering is: $\text{public} > \text{protected} > \text{private}$. The "minimum" (most restrictive) wins. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility `protected` members are heavily used in: - **Framework Design (e.g., Qt, MFC, .NET):** Base framework classes expose `protected` hooks that application developers override in derived classes without exposing implementation to end users. - **Embedded Systems:** Base driver class keeps hardware register addresses `protected`; derived chip-specific drivers use them directly. - **GUI Toolkits:** `QWidget` exposes `protected` event handlers like `paintEvent()` тАФ meant for derived widgets (e.g., `MyButton`), hidden from `main()`. - **Game Engines:** Base `Entity` class has `protected` velocity, position; derived `Player` and `Enemy` use them; main loop cannot tamper. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivations: From First Principles to Inheritance Matrix The inheritance access matrix is not arbitrary тАФ it follows a deterministic rule derivable from the C++ standard. Let us **prove** the transformation rules. ### 3.1.1 Derivation of the "Min-Rule" for Inheritance Let: - $M$ = inherited member's access in the base class. - $I$ = inheritance mode (public, protected, or private). - $M'$ = effective access in the derived class. The C++ standard states: the access in a derived class is the **intersection** of the member's original access and the inheritance mode's max-visibility. Mathematically: $$ M' = \min_{\text{restrictive}}(M, I) $$ where the restrictiveness order is: $$ \text{public} < \text{protected} < \text{private} $$ ("less restrictive" = higher visibility) **Step 1:** When $M = \text{public}$ and $I = \text{public}$: $$ M' = \min(\text{public}, \text{public}) = \text{public} $$ **Step 2:** When $M = \text{public}$ and $I = \text{protected}$: $$ M' = \min(\text{public}, \text{protected}) = \text{protected} $$ **Step 3:** When $M = \text{public}$ and $I = \text{private}$: $$ M' = \min(\text{public}, \text{private}) = \text{private} $$ **Step 4:** When $M = \text{protected}$ and $I = \text{public}$: $$ M' = \min(\text{protected}, \text{public}) = \text{protected} $$ **Step 5:** When $M = \text{protected}$ and $I = \text{protected}$: $$ M' = \min(\text{protected}, \text{protected}) = \text{protected} $$ **Step 6:** When $M = \text{protected}$ and $I = \text{private}$: $$ M' = \min(\text{protected}, \text{private}) = \text{private} $$ **Step 7:** When $M = \text{private}$ (any $I$): $$ M' = \text{inaccessible} $$ because `private` members of a base class are never accessible to a derived class directly, irrespective of inheritance mode. > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic:** Each step applies the C++ rule that the *more restrictive* of (member's original access, inheritance mode) wins. The base's `private` is a special case тАФ it is *inherited* as a memory layout but not *accessible* by name. ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 тАФ Direct Access in Derived Class ```cpp #include using namespace std; class Base { protected: int protectedValue; public: Base() : protectedValue(42) {} }; class Derived : public Base { public: void show() { cout << "protectedValue = " << protectedValue; // OK } }; int main() { Derived d; d.show(); // cout << d.protectedValue; // ERROR: not accessible from main return 0; } ``` **Output** $$ \text{protectedValue} = 42 $$ **Step-by-step explanation:** 1. `Base` declares `protectedValue` as `protected`. 2. `Derived` inherits `Base` publicly. 3. Inside `Derived::show()`, the access to `protectedValue` is **legal** because `show()` is a member of `Derived` (i.e., a derived class). 4. Inside `main()`, `d.protectedValue` is **illegal** because `main()` is neither a member, friend, nor derived class of `Base`. ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 тАФ The "Self-Type" Pitfall ```cpp class Base { protected: int x; }; class Derived : public Base { public: void f(Base& b, Derived& d) { x = 10; // OK: accessing own inherited x d.x = 20; // OK: d is of type Derived (self-type) // b.x = 30; // ERROR: b is of type Base, not self-type } }; ``` **Conversion Logic:** 1. `d.x = 20` works because `d` has type `Derived`, which is the "self-type" of the function `f`. 2. `b.x = 30` fails because `b` has type `Base&`. Even though `Base` is the class where `x` is declared, the C++ standard forbids access through a base-type reference inside the derived class member function. > [!WARNING] > **This is a classic KTU 14-mark question.** The rule is often stated as: *"A protected member is accessible in a derived class only when accessed through an object whose type is the derived class or a class derived from it."* ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix тАФ When to Use `protected`? | Scenario | Recommended Access | Reasoning | |----------|---------------------|-----------| | Data only the base class itself uses. | `private` | Maximum encapsulation. | | Data that derived classes must read/write directly. | `protected` | Enables inheritance without breaking encapsulation. | | Data forming the public interface. | `public` | Part of the class's contract. | | Helper functions for derived classes only. | `protected` | E.g., `paintEvent()` in Qt. | | Constructors and destructors. | `public` (usually) | Must be callable from outside. | | Friend class / function needs access. | Use `friend` keyword, not `protected`. | `friend` is orthogonal. | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Access Hierarchy тАФ Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef baseClass fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef derivedClass fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef unrelatedClass fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef friendEntity fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mainFunc fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 Base["Base Class"]:::baseClass Derived["Derived Class"]:::derivedClass Friend["Friend Class or Function"]:::friendEntity Outside["Unrelated Class or main()"]:::unrelatedClass PrivateZone["private members: x, secret"]:::baseClass ProtectedZone["protected members: y, familyData"]:::baseClass PublicZone["public members: z, display()"]:::baseClass Base --> PrivateZone Base --> ProtectedZone Base --> PublicZone AccessA["Allowed Access"]:::derivedClass AccessB["Blocked Access"]:::unrelatedClass PrivateZone -.->|"NO access"| AccessB ProtectedZone -.->|"YES access"| Derived ProtectedZone -.->|"NO access"| Outside PublicZone -.->|"YES access"| Outside PrivateZone -.->|"NO direct access"| Derived PublicZone -.->|"YES access"| Derived ProtectedZone -.->|"YES access"| Friend PrivateZone -.->|"YES access"| Friend PublicZone -.->|"YES access"| Friend Derived --> AccessA ``` ## 4.2 Inheritance Mode ├Ч Member Access тАФ Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef inputBox fill:#e7f1ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef publicBox fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef protectedBox fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef privateBox fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef denyBox fill:#212529,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff Start["Base Member Access
and Inheritance Mode"]:::inputBox Decision{"What was the
base member access?"} Pub["public member"]:::inputBox Prot["protected member"]:::inputBox Priv["private member"]:::inputBox Mode{"Inheritance
Mode?"} P1["remains public"]:::publicBox P2["becomes protected"]:::protectedBox P3["becomes private"]:::privateBox P4["remains protected"]:::protectedBox P5["remains protected"]:::protectedBox P6["becomes private"]:::privateBox P7["inaccessible
to derived class"]:::denyBox Start --> Decision Decision -->|"public"| Pub --> Mode Decision -->|"protected"| Prot --> Mode Decision -->|"private"| Priv --> P7 Mode -->|"public"| P1 Mode -->|"protected"| P2 Mode -->|"private"| P3 Prot -->|"public"| P4 Prot -->|"protected"| P5 Prot -->|"private"| P6 ``` ## 4.3 Multi-Level Inheritance Chain тАФ Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef lvl1 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef lvl2 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef lvl3 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mainBox fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 GrandParent["Class GrandParent
protected: gData"]:::lvl1 Parent["Class Parent : public GrandParent
inherits gData as protected"]:::lvl2 Child["Class Child : public Parent
inherits gData as protected"]:::lvl3 Main["main() function"]:::mainBox GrandParent -->|"public inheritance"| Parent Parent -->|"public inheritance"| Child Access1["Child can access gData
via Child or Parent object"]:::lvl3 Access2["main cannot access gData"]:::mainBox Child --> Access1 Main -.->|"BLOCKED"| Access1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** In a multi-level chain, `protected` data is **cumulatively accessible** to all descendants (as long as every link uses `public` inheritance), but **blocked at any point where a `private` inheritance** is introduced. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]** **What are protected members in C++? Explain their accessibility.** **Model Answer:** Protected members in C++ are class members declared with the keyword `protected`. They can be accessed: 1. **Within the class itself** where they are declared. 2. **Within friend functions and friend classes** of that class. 3. **Within any derived (inherited) class** тАФ but only through an object of the derived class type (or its further derivatives), not through a base-class object pointer/reference. They are **not accessible** from non-member functions (like `main()`) or from unrelated classes. Protected members exist to support inheritance while preserving encapsulation. (3 marks) --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024]** **Differentiate between private and protected access specifiers.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | `private` | `protected` | |--------|-----------|-------------| | Accessible in own class | Yes | Yes | | Accessible in derived class | **No** | Yes | | Accessible via friend | Yes | Yes | | Accessible from `main()`/outside | No | No | | Purpose | Strict encapsulation | Inheritance-friendly encapsulation | The key difference: **`private` blocks derived classes; `protected` permits them.** (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice (either **A** or **B**). Each part is split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** тАФ `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2024]` **Explain the different access specifiers in C++ with a suitable example. Also discuss how the access level of inherited members changes with the type of inheritance.** #### **Part (a) тАФ 7 Marks: Access Specifiers Explained** The three access specifiers in C++ are `private`, `protected`, and `public`. - **`private`:** Members are accessible only inside the class in which they are declared and by its friends. They are *not* inherited in the accessible sense by derived classes. - **`protected`:** Members are accessible inside the class, by friends, **and** by derived classes (through self-type objects). - **`public`:** Members are accessible from anywhere the object is visible. **Code Example (7 marks full):** ```cpp #include using namespace std; class Account { private: double balance; // strictly hidden protected: int accountID; // family-visible public: string holderName; // world-visible Account() : balance(0.0), accountID(0) {} void setBalance(double b) { balance = b; } double getBalance() { return balance; } }; class SavingsAccount : public Account { public: void setID(int id) { accountID = id; } // OK: protected access void display() { cout << "ID: " << accountID << ", Holder: " << holderName << endl; } // void setBalDirect(double b) { balance = b; } // ERROR: private }; int main() { SavingsAccount sa; sa.holderName = "Alice"; // OK: public sa.setID(101); // OK: public method touches protected sa.setBalance(5000.0); // OK: public method touches private sa.display(); // sa.accountID = 200; // ERROR: protected, not accessible here // sa.balance; // ERROR: private return 0; } ``` **Key Point:** `balance` is `private`; not even the derived class touches it directly. `accountID` is `protected`; the derived class can read/write it. `holderName` is `public`; freely usable. #### **Part (b) тАФ 7 Marks: Effect of Inheritance Type on Member Access** The effective access of an inherited member depends on the inheritance mode. The rule is the **"minimum of member access and inheritance mode wins"**: - **Public inheritance:** The IS-A relationship is preserved. Public stays public, protected stays protected. - **Protected inheritance:** Public members become protected; protected members stay protected. Useful when further deriving restricted chains. - **Private inheritance:** All accessible members become private in the derived class. Used for "implemented-in-terms-of" (HAS-A via inheritance). The full table: | Base Member | Public Inherit | Protected Inherit | Private Inherit | |-------------|----------------|-------------------|------------------| | `public` | public | protected | private | | `protected` | protected | protected | private | | `private` | inaccessible | inaccessible | inaccessible | > **Conclusion:** The `protected` access specifier is the natural partner of inheritance тАФ it lets derived classes participate in the base class's internal state, while still hiding it from the outside world. (7 marks) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** тАФ `[KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024]` **What is the significance of `protected` access specifier? Illustrate with a C++ program demonstrating the rules of protected member access in single and multilevel inheritance.** #### **Part (a) тАФ 7 Marks: Significance + Single Inheritance** The `protected` access specifier is significant because it allows a derived class to access base class members that should remain hidden from non-derived code. This is essential in framework and library design, where the base class exposes certain "extension points" to its derived classes but not to application code that uses the framework. **Code (7 marks):** ```cpp #include using namespace std; class Shape { protected: double area; public: Shape() : area(0) {} virtual void computeArea() = 0; // pure virtual double getArea() { return area; } }; class Rectangle : public Shape { protected: double length, breadth; public: Rectangle(double l, double b) : length(l), breadth(b) { computeArea(); } void computeArea() { area = length * breadth; // accessing protected 'area' } }; int main() { Rectangle r(5.0, 3.0); cout << "Area = " << r.getArea() << endl; // cout << r.area; // ERROR: protected, not accessible from main return 0; } ``` Here, `area` is `protected` so `Rectangle::computeArea()` can update it directly, but `main()` cannot. #### **Part (b) тАФ 7 Marks: Multilevel Inheritance** In multilevel inheritance, the protected accessibility propagates down the chain *as long as every link uses public or protected inheritance*. ```cpp #include using namespace std; class A { protected: int a; public: A() : a(100) {} }; class B : public A { protected: int b; public: B() : b(200) {} void showA() { cout << "A.a = " << a << endl; } // protected access OK }; class C : public B { public: void showAll() { cout << "A.a = " << a << endl; // inherited protected, still accessible cout << "B.b = " << b << endl; // own protected, accessible } }; int main() { C obj; obj.showAll(); // obj.a; // ERROR // obj.b; // ERROR return 0; } ``` **Output** $$ A.a = 100 \\ B.b = 200 $$ **Rule:** In multilevel inheritance, a `protected` member of the topmost base class remains accessible to all descendants as long as every inheritance link is `public` (or `protected`). If any link uses `private` inheritance, the chain breaks at that point. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the "self-type" rule:** Students often write `Base b; b.protectedMember;` inside a derived function and lose 2 marks. Always access protected members through a `Derived*` or `Derived&`. > 2. **Confusing `private` with `protected`:** Saying "protected members are NOT inherited" is **wrong** тАФ they are inherited but with restricted visibility. Saying "private members are inherited" тАФ also wrong to phrase it that way. Be precise. > 3. **Java vs. C++ confusion:** In Java, `protected` means **package + subclass** access. In C++, it means **class + friend + subclass** (no package concept). Don't mix them in C++ answers. > 4. **Forgetting to mention `friend`:** When listing where `protected` members are accessible, always include **"and friends"** тАФ it is a board favourite. > 5. **Default access confusion:** Forgetting that a `class` defaults to `private` and a `struct` defaults to `public`. Marks deducted if you say "default is public". --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ Print This Before Exam** - **`protected`** = accessible inside the class, its friends, and its derived classes (via self-type). - **NOT accessible** from `main()` or unrelated classes. - The **"self-type" rule**: derived class can access protected base members **only** through a derived-type object/reference/pointer тАФ never through a base-type one. - **Public inheritance:** `public` тЖТ `public`, `protected` тЖТ `protected`, `private` тЖТ inaccessible. - **Protected inheritance:** `public` тЖТ `protected`, `protected` тЖТ `protected`, `private` тЖТ inaccessible. - **Private inheritance:** `public` тЖТ `private`, `protected` тЖТ `private`, `private` тЖТ inaccessible. - **Default access in `class`** is `private`; in `struct` is `public`. - **Private members are NEVER directly accessible to derived classes**, regardless of inheritance mode. - **`friend` is orthogonal** to access specifiers тАФ a `friend` of the base can access base's `private`, `protected`, and `public` members. - In **multilevel inheritance**, `protected` accessibility propagates if all links are public/protected; breaks at any private link. - **Java's `protected`** is different from C++'s тАФ includes package-level access. KTU OOP module is C++-centric; answer accordingly. - **Use `protected`** when derived classes *must* manipulate base internals AND outside code must not see them. - **Avoid overusing `protected`** тАФ it leaks encapsulation. Prefer `private` with public/protected accessors.

topics.title: Calling Order of Constructors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Calling Order of Constructors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ЁЯУШ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calling Order of Constructors** refers to the **deterministic, well-defined sequence** in which constructors of a class and its parent (super) classes are invoked during the instantiation of a derived class object. In object-oriented programming languages such as **Java** and **C++**, this order follows a strict **top-down (root-to-leaf) traversal** of the class hierarchy tree, ensuring that every inherited data member is properly initialized **before** the derived class constructor executes its own initialization logic.

In simple academic terms for KTU 2024 Scheme: When an object of a derived class is created, the **JVM (Java Virtual Machine)** or **C++ compiler runtime** automatically invokes the constructors starting from the **top-most superclass (Object / root class)** and proceeds **downward** to the most derived class whose object is being instantiated.

### 1.2 The Golden Rule of Constructor Calling

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule:** *"Constructors are executed from the root of the inheritance hierarchy towards the leaf (the instantiated class), but **destruction** happens in the reverse order (leaf тЖТ root)."*

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy тАФ The Family Tree Inheritance

Imagine a multi-generational family where a newborn child (derived class object) is being welcomed into the world. Before the child can be officially named and registered:

1. ЁЯС┤ **The Grandfather (Top-most Superclass)** is consulted first тАФ he is the eldest and his blessings/initials are recorded first.
2. ЁЯСи **The Father (Intermediate Superclass)** is consulted next тАФ his details are recorded before the child.
3. ЁЯС╢ **The Child (Derived Class)** is finally registered with his own unique identity.

This **elder-to-younger** sequence mirrors exactly how constructors are called in OOP. The "elders" (parent classes) must be acknowledged before the "younger" (child class) can exist independently.

### 1.4 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Default Constructor** | A no-argument constructor automatically provided by the compiler if no constructor is explicitly defined. |
| **Parameterized Constructor** | A constructor that accepts one or more arguments for custom initialization. |
| **`super()` call** | An explicit or implicit call from a derived class constructor to a superclass constructor. |
| **`this()` call** | An explicit call from one constructor of a class to another overloaded constructor of the **same** class. |
| **Constructor Chaining** | The sequential invocation of constructors across the inheritance hierarchy. |
| **Object Class** | The **implicit root superclass** of every Java class (directly or transitively). |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ЁЯФм SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Order of Constructor Invocation тАФ Step-by-Step Logic

When `new DerivedClass()` is executed, the following sequence unfolds **deterministically**:

> **Step 1:** Memory is allocated for the **entire object** (including inherited members) on the heap.
>
> **Step 2:** The compiler inserts an **implicit `super()` call** as the **first statement** of every constructor (if not explicitly written).
>
> **Step 3:** The call propagates **upward** to the immediate parent class constructor.
>
> **Step 4:** That parent constructor's first statement (implicit or explicit `super()`) propagates further up the chain.
>
> **Step 5:** The chain terminates at the **`java.lang.Object` class** (or the topmost superclass in C++).
>
> **Step 6:** Execution then **unwinds downward**, executing the body of each constructor from **root тЖТ leaf**.
>
> **Step 7:** Finally, the body of the **derived class constructor** (the one explicitly invoked by `new`) completes.

## 2.2 Critical Rules of Constructor Calling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 1:** Every constructor in a derived class **must** call a superclass constructor тАФ either implicitly (default `super()`) or explicitly (`super(args)`).
>
> **Rule 2:** The call to `super()` or `this()` **must be the first statement** in a constructor body. This is a compile-time error otherwise.
>
> **Rule 3:** A constructor body can use **either** `super()` **or** `this()`, but **never both** in the same constructor.
>
> **Rule 4:** If the superclass has **no default (no-argument) constructor**, the derived class **must explicitly** invoke a parameterized superclass constructor using `super(arguments)`.
>
> **Rule 5:** In **multiple inheritance** (C++), the order follows the **order of declaration** of base classes in the derived class declaration (left to right).
>
> **Rule 6:** In **multilevel inheritance** (A тЖТ B тЖТ C), the order is: **A's constructor тЖТ B's constructor тЖТ C's constructor**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Scenario | Constructor Execution Order | KTU Frequency |
|---|----------|----------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Single-level inheritance (A тЖТ B) | A тЖТ B | тнРтнРтнРтнРтнР |
| 2 | Multilevel inheritance (A тЖТ B тЖТ C) | A тЖТ B тЖТ C | тнРтнРтнРтнРтнР |
| 3 | Hierarchical inheritance (A тЖТ B, A тЖТ C) | A тЖТ B (or A тЖТ C) | тнРтнРтнРтнР |
| 4 | Multiple inheritance in C++ (A, B тЖТ C) | A тЖТ B тЖТ C (declaration order) | тнРтнРтнР |
| 5 | `this()` chaining in same class | Resolved at compile time, executed first | тнРтнРтнРтнР |
| 6 | Explicit `super(args)` call | Resolves to matching parent constructor | тнРтнРтнРтнРтнР |
| 7 | Static initialization in inheritance | Static blocks execute **before** any constructor | тнРтнРтнР |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The constructor calling order is **not merely an academic curiosity** тАФ it has profound engineering implications:
> - **Framework Design:** Frameworks like **Spring (Java)** and **Qt (C++)** rely on predictable constructor chaining to perform dependency injection.
> - **GUI Development:** In **JavaFX** and **Swing**, the parent `JFrame` or `Application` class is initialized before child components, ensuring UI hierarchy is valid.
> - **Embedded Systems:** In **Arduino C++** libraries, base-class hardware initialization must complete before derived sensor classes can use the I/O pins.
> - **Database ORMs:** **Hibernate** uses constructor chaining to populate entity objects with parent table data before child tables.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ЁЯзо SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Single-Level Inheritance

Consider the following Java program тАФ a classic KTU-style question on constructor order.

```java
class A {
    A() {
        System.out.println("Constructor of A");
    }
}

class B extends A {
    B() {
        System.out.println("Constructor of B");
    }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        B obj = new B();
    }
}
```

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Trace

> **Step 1:** The `main` method begins execution. The statement `B obj = new B();` is encountered.
>
> **Step 2:** Memory is allocated on the heap for an object of type `B`. This object contains both the fields of `A` (inherited) and `B` (own). All fields are initialized to default values (`int тЖТ 0`, `Object тЖТ null`).
>
> **Step 3:** The constructor `B()` is invoked. The Java compiler has implicitly inserted `super();` as the first statement of `B()`.
>
> **Step 4:** Control transfers to the **default constructor of A**: `A()`.
>
> **Step 5:** `A()` does not have an explicit `super()` call, so the compiler implicitly calls `super();` which corresponds to `Object()`.
>
> **Step 6:** `Object()` executes (its body is empty), completing the chain at the root.
>
> **Step 7:** Control returns to `A()`. The line `System.out.println("Constructor of A");` executes, printing the first line.
>
> **Step 8:** `A()` completes. Control returns to `B()`. The line `System.out.println("Constructor of B");` executes, printing the second line.
>
> **Step 9:** `B()` completes. The object reference `obj` is assigned the heap address.

### Final Output

```
Constructor of A
Constructor of B
```

### Mathematical Representation of the Call Stack

$$
\text{Call Stack (Execution):} \quad \text{main} \rightarrow B() \rightarrow A() \rightarrow Object()
$$

$$
\text{Output Order (Return):} \quad Object() \rightarrow A() \rightarrow B() \rightarrow \text{main}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Multilevel Inheritance with Parameterized Constructors

```java
class Vehicle {
    Vehicle(int x) {
        System.out.println("Vehicle constructor: " + x);
    }
}

class Car extends Vehicle {
    Car(int y) {
        super(y);  // Explicit call to parameterized parent constructor
        System.out.println("Car constructor: " + y);
    }
}

class SportsCar extends Car {
    SportsCar(int z) {
        super(z);  // Explicit call
        System.out.println("SportsCar constructor: " + z);
    }
}

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SportsCar sc = new SportsCar(100);
    }
}
```

### Exhaustive Trace

> **Step 1:** `new SportsCar(100)` is called. Stack frame for `SportsCar(int z)` is created.
>
> **Step 2:** `z = 100`. The first statement `super(z)` invokes `Car(int y)` with `y = 100`.
>
> **Step 3:** In `Car(int y)`, the first statement `super(y)` invokes `Vehicle(int x)` with `x = 100`.
>
> **Step 4:** `Vehicle(int x)` executes: prints `Vehicle constructor: 100`.
>
> **Step 5:** Control returns to `Car(int y)`, prints `Car constructor: 100`.
>
> **Step 6:** Control returns to `SportsCar(int z)`, prints `SportsCar constructor: 100`.

### Final Output

```
Vehicle constructor: 100
Car constructor: 100
SportsCar constructor: 100
```

### LaTeX-Aligned Logical Flow

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Invocation Path:} \quad & \text{main} \rightarrow \text{SportsCar}(100) \rightarrow \text{Car}(100) \rightarrow \text{Vehicle}(100) \\
\text{Execution Order:} \quad & \text{Vehicle} \rightarrow \text{Car} \rightarrow \text{SportsCar} \\
\text{Output Sequence:} \quad & \text{Vehicle constructor: 100} \\
                              & \text{Car constructor: 100} \\
                              & \text{SportsCar constructor: 100}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Scenario Behavior Analysis

| Scenario | Code Pattern | Output Order | Common Mistake |
|----------|--------------|--------------|----------------|
| Implicit `super()` | `B() {}` in subclass | A тЖТ B | Forgetting to compile-check no-arg parent |
| Explicit `super(args)` | `B() { super(5); }` | A(param) тЖТ B | Wrong argument type passed |
| `this()` chaining | `B() { this(5); } B(int x) {}` | B(5) тЖТ B() | Putting `this()` not as first statement |
| No default in parent | Parent has only `A(int x)` | Compile Error if no `super(int)` | Forgetting mandatory explicit call |
| Static + instance | Static block + constructor | Static тЖТ A тЖТ B | Thinking static runs after constructor |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# ЁЯЧ║я╕П SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Constructor Calling Order in Multilevel Inheritance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Class A : Root Superclass"]:::root
    B1["Class B : Intermediate"]:::mid
    C1["Class C : Derived Instantiated"]:::leaf
    OBJ["Object Class : Implicit Root"]:::object

    OBJ -->|"implicit super()"| A1
    A1 -->|"implicit super() auto-inserted"| B1
    B1 -->|"super call propagates upward"| C1
    C1 -->|"new C triggers chain"| B1

    A1:::root
    B1:::mid
    C1:::leaf
    OBJ:::object

    classDef root fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef mid fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef object fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: Constructor Resolution Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    START["User Code: new DerivedClass()"]:::start
    ALLOC["Step 1: Heap Memory Allocation for Full Object"]:::step
    IMPLICIT["Step 2: Compiler Inserts Implicit super() as First Statement"]:::step
    RESOLVE["Step 3: Resolve Matching Superclass Constructor via Arguments"]:::step
    CHECK{"Step 4: Does super() match an existing parent constructor?"}:::decision
    COMPILE_ERR["COMPILE ERROR: No Suitable Parent Constructor"]:::error
    EXECUTE["Step 5: Execute Parent Constructor Body"]:::step
    PROPAGATE{"Step 6: Does parent have its own super() call?"}:::decision
    OBJECT_END["Step 7: java.lang.Object constructor executes (Chain Terminates)"]:::end
    UNWIND["Step 8: Unwind Stack - Execute Bodies in Reverse-Propagation Order"]:::step
    FINAL["Step 9: Derived Class Constructor Body Executes Last"]:::final
    COMPLETE["Step 10: Object Fully Initialized - Return Reference"]:::done

    START --> ALLOC --> IMPLICIT --> RESOLVE --> CHECK
    CHECK -->|"No"| COMPILE_ERR
    CHECK -->|"Yes"| EXECUTE --> PROPAGATE
    PROPAGATE -->|"Yes (recurses upward)"| EXECUTE
    PROPAGATE -->|"No (reached Object)"| OBJECT_END --> UNWIND --> FINAL --> COMPLETE

    classDef start fill:#A8DADC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#F1FAEE,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#E63946,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef error fill:#B71C1C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef end fill:#457B9D,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef final fill:#2A9D8F,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef done fill:#264653,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Tree for `super()` vs `this()`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is this() used in derived class constructor?"}:::q1
    Q2{"Is super() explicitly written?"}:::q2
    Q3{"Does parent have a matching no-arg constructor?"}:::q3
    P1["Compiler inserts implicit super() automatically"]:::path
    P2["Calls the specified superclass constructor"]:::path
    P3["COMPILE ERROR - Must add explicit super args"]:::err
    P4["this chains to another constructor in SAME class"]:::path
    P5["That other constructor will then call super()"]:::path

    Q1 -->|"No"| Q2
    Q1 -->|"Yes"| P4
    Q2 -->|"No - implicit"| P1
    Q2 -->|"Yes - explicit"| P2
    P1 --> Q3
    Q3 -->|"Yes"| P2
    Q3 -->|"No"| P3
    P4 --> P5

    classDef q1 fill:#FFD60A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef q2 fill:#FFD60A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```
