# ЁЯУЭ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the calling order of constructors in Java inheritance. State the rule that governs this order.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The calling order of constructors refers to the sequence in which constructors of base and derived classes are invoked when an object of a derived class is created. The governing rule is that **constructors are executed from the top-most superclass (root) downward to the most derived class** whose object is being instantiated. This ensures that inherited members are initialized before the derived class's own initialization logic runs. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What happens if the superclass has only a parameterized constructor and the derived class does not explicitly call `super()`?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **compile-time error** occurs. This is because the compiler cannot insert an implicit `super()` call when the superclass lacks a no-argument (default) constructor. The derived class **must explicitly** invoke a matching superclass constructor using `super(arguments)` as the **first statement** of its constructor. This is a mandatory rule enforced by the Java compiler. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ЁЯФ╣ Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the calling order of constructors in Java with a suitable example demonstrating **multilevel inheritance**. Include a diagram and trace the execution step by step.

**Model Solution:**

```java
class Grandparent {
    Grandparent() {
        System.out.println("Grandparent constructor called");
    }
}

class Parent extends Grandparent {
    Parent() {
        System.out.println("Parent constructor called");
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    Child() {
        System.out.println("Child constructor called");
    }
}

public class TestOrder {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Child c = new Child();
    }
}
```

**Execution Trace (7 marks distributed as follows):**

| Step | Action | Output |
|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | `new Child()` invoked | тАФ |
| 2 | `Child()` body begins; implicit `super()` тЖТ `Parent()` | тАФ |
| 3 | `Parent()` body begins; implicit `super()` тЖТ `Grandparent()` | тАФ |
| 4 | `Grandparent()` executes; prints line 1 | `Grandparent constructor called` |
| 5 | Returns to `Parent()`; prints line 2 | `Parent constructor called` |
| 6 | Returns to `Child()`; prints line 3 | `Child constructor called` |

**Diagram (1 mark for the diagram):** A three-tier hierarchy `Grandparent тЖТ Parent тЖТ Child` showing upward call and downward execution. The order of execution is **Grandparent тЖТ Parent тЖТ Child**. **(7 marks total)**

**(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between `super()` and `this()` in Java constructors. State the **three compile-time rules** that govern their usage with a code example.

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | `super()` | `this()` |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| Purpose | Calls a **superclass** constructor | Calls another **same-class** constructor |
| Scope | Refers to parent class | Refers to current class |
| Position | First statement in constructor | First statement in constructor |
| Mutual Exclusion | Cannot coexist with `this()` | Cannot coexist with `super()` |

**Three Compile-Time Rules (3 marks):**
1. `super()` or `this()` must be the **first statement** in a constructor.
2. A constructor can have **either** `super()` **or** `this()`, never both.
3. If neither is written, the compiler **automatically** inserts `super()` (no-arg version).

**Code Example (4 marks):**
```java
class Box {
    int length;
    Box() { this(10); }              // Rule 2: uses this()
    Box(int l) { this.length = l; }   // First statement: this(10)
}

class ColoredBox extends Box {
    String color;
    ColoredBox() {
        super();        // Implicit, calls Box() тЖТ which calls this(10)
        this.color = "Red";
    }
}
```
This demonstrates that `this()` chaining indirectly triggers `super()` resolution. **(7 marks total)**

---

### ЁЯФ╣ Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program to demonstrate the constructor calling order in **hierarchical inheritance** where two child classes inherit from a common parent. Predict and justify the output.

**Model Solution:**

```java
class Shape {
    Shape() {
        System.out.println("Shape constructor");
    }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    Circle() {
        System.out.println("Circle constructor");
    }
}

class Rectangle extends Shape {
    Rectangle() {
        System.out.println("Rectangle constructor");
    }
}

public class HierarchicalTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Circle c = new Circle();
        System.out.println("---");
        Rectangle r = new Rectangle();
    }
}
```

**Predicted Output (5 marks):**
```
Shape constructor
Circle constructor
---
Shape constructor
Rectangle constructor
```

**Justification (2 marks):**
Each derived class object creation triggers an independent constructor chain. For `Circle`, the chain is `Shape тЖТ Circle`; for `Rectangle`, it is `Shape тЖТ Rectangle`. The parent constructor runs **freshly** for each child object since each is a distinct instantiation. **(7 marks total)**

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with an example what happens when the superclass **does not have a default constructor** and the derived class fails to make an explicit `super()` call. Show both the **error scenario** and the **corrected version**.

**Model Solution:**

**Error Scenario (3 marks):**
```java
class Employee {
    String name;
    Employee(String n) {        // Only parameterized constructor
        this.name = n;
        System.out.println("Employee: " + n);
    }
}

class Manager extends Employee {
    Manager() {                 // COMPILE ERROR: implicit super() not found
        System.out.println("Manager created");
    }
}
```
**Compiler Error:** `cannot find symbol: constructor Employee()` тАФ because the compiler tries to insert `super();` but `Employee()` does not exist.

**Corrected Version (4 marks):**
```java
class Manager extends Employee {
    Manager() {
        super("Default Manager");  // Explicit call to parameterized parent
        System.out.println("Manager created");
    }
}
```
**Output:**
```
Employee: Default Manager
Manager created
```
This explicitly satisfies the compiler's requirement, demonstrating that explicit `super(args)` is **mandatory** when no default parent constructor exists. **(7 marks total)**

---

## 5.3 тЪая╕П KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Constructor Order Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting that `super()` is implicitly inserted** тАФ Students often write programs as if `super()` is not called automatically. The compiler ALWAYS inserts it as the first statement unless `this()` is used. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing the direction** тАФ Some students say constructors are called "from child to parent". This is **wrong**; they are called from parent to child. The **execution** direction is parent-to-child, but the **call** also propagates parent-to-child. **Loss: 1-2 marks.**
> 3. **Missing the `Object` class** тАФ In Java, the chain terminates at `java.lang.Object`. Failing to mention this loses conceptual marks in higher-order questions. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Mixing up `super()` and `super` keyword** тАФ `super()` is a **constructor call**; `super.method()` is a **method call**; `super.field` is a **field reference**. These are different. **Loss: 1-2 marks.**
> 5. **Ignoring static initialization** тАФ In questions involving static blocks, students forget that **static blocks run BEFORE any constructor**, even parent constructors. **Loss: 2 marks in tricky questions.**

---

## 5.4 ЁЯУМ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- тЬЕ Constructor calling order follows a **root-to-leaf traversal** of the inheritance hierarchy.
- тЬЕ The compiler **implicitly inserts `super()`** as the first statement of every constructor unless `this()` is used.
- тЬЕ `super()` and `this()` must both be the **first statement** in a constructor body тАФ never both.
- тЬЕ If the superclass **lacks a default constructor**, the derived class **must explicitly** call `super(args)`.
- тЬЕ In **multilevel inheritance** (A тЖТ B тЖТ C), the order is **A тЖТ B тЖТ C**.
- тЬЕ In **hierarchical inheritance** (A тЖТ B, A тЖТ C), each branch invokes A's constructor independently.
- тЬЕ In **multiple inheritance (C++)**, the order follows the **declaration order** of base classes.
- тЬЕ The chain always terminates at **`java.lang.Object`** in Java.
- тЬЕ **Static blocks** execute **before** any constructor in the hierarchy.
- тЬЕ Constructor order = **Initialization order**; Destructor order (C++) = **Reverse of construction order**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For KTU 2024 Scheme, always **draw a hierarchy diagram** in your answer sheet, then **trace step-by-step** with arrow annotations. Examiners award 1-2 extra marks for clear visual presentation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method Overriding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Method Overriding # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Method Overriding** is a fundamental feature of Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) that enables a **subclass (child class)** to provide a **specific implementation** of a method that is **already defined** in its **superclass (parent class)**. The overridden method in the child class must carry an **identical method signature** тАФ that is, the **exact same name, the same number of parameters, the same types of parameters, and the same order of parameters** as the parent class method. When a method is overridden, Java (and other OOP languages supporting polymorphism) resolves the method call **at runtime** based on the **actual type of the object** rather than the **reference type of the variable**. This resolution mechanism is formally known as **Dynamic Method Dispatch** (also called *Runtime Polymorphism* or *Late Binding*). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Importance):** > *"Method Overriding allows a derived class to redefine a base class method with the same signature, thereby implementing Runtime Polymorphism. The call is resolved dynamically at runtime using the actual object's type, not the reference variable's type."* --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **universal remote control** (the parent class) that has a button labeled `powerOn()`. By default, it turns on a **generic television**. Now, you have three specific TV brands in your house тАФ **Sony**, **Samsung**, and **LG** (the child classes). Each of these TVs **overrides** the behavior of the `powerOn()` button: - The **Sony TV** displays a Sony logo and tunes to the last watched channel. - The **Samsung TV** displays a Smart Hub interface. - The **LG TV** displays an LG boot animation. Even though the **button** on the remote is the same (`powerOn()`), the **actual behavior** depends on **which TV (the actual object)** is connected to the remote. This is exactly how **Method Overriding** works тАФ the *interface* (method signature) stays the same, but the *behavior* (implementation) is customized by the actual object type at runtime. --- ## 1.3 Why Method Overriding Matters in OOP > [!NOTE] > **Three Pillars Connection:** > Method Overriding is the **mechanical implementation** of the OOP pillar **Polymorphism** (specifically *Runtime/Subtype Polymorphism*). Without overriding, you cannot achieve true dynamic behavior in a class hierarchy. | Pillar | Role of Overriding | |---|---| | **Inheritance** | Provides the structural prerequisite (parent-child relationship). | | **Polymorphism** | Overriding is the *action* that brings runtime polymorphism to life. | | **Abstraction** | Allows abstract method contracts to be fulfilled by concrete child classes. | | **Encapsulation** | Overriding does not break encapsulation; it works within the inherited contract. | --- ## 1.4 One-Line Board-Exam Definition (Memorize This) > **"Method Overriding is redefining a parent class method in the child class with the *same signature* to achieve *runtime polymorphism* through *dynamic method dispatch*."** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Rule Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Mechanism (Step-by-Step) When you write `obj.method()`, the JVM does **not** simply look at the reference variable `obj` to decide which method to invoke. Instead, it follows this logic: 1. **Compile-Time Check:** The compiler verifies that `method()` exists in the **reference type's class** (or its parents). If not found тЖТ *Compilation Error*. 2. **Runtime Resolution:** The JVM inspects the **actual object** that the reference points to and invokes the **most-specific (lowest-most) overridden version** available in the object hierarchy. 3. **Binding Type:** This is **Late Binding** (Dynamic Binding) тАФ as opposed to *Early Binding* (Static Binding) used in method overloading, `static`, `private`, or `final` methods. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** > - *Method Overloading* тЖТ **Compile-Time Polymorphism** (Static Binding) > - *Method Overriding* тЖТ **Runtime Polymorphism** (Dynamic Binding) > This comparison is a **favourite KTU question** (2-mark/3-mark). --- ## 2.2 KTU Rule Sheet тАФ Method Overriding Cheat Table | # | Rule | Valid | Invalid / Error | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Method Name** | Must be **identical** to the parent method. | Different name тЖТ treated as a new method, not overriding. | | 2 | **Parameter List (Signature)** | Must be **identical** in number, type, and order. | Any change тЖТ becomes *Method Overloading*, NOT overriding. | | 3 | **Return Type** | **Same** OR a **covariant return type** (a subclass of the original return). | Unrelated return type тЖТ Compilation Error. | | 4 | **Access Modifier** | **Same** or a **less restrictive** one (e.g., `protected` тЖТ `public`). | More restrictive (e.g., `public` тЖТ `private`) тЖТ Compilation Error. | | 5 | **Exception Handling** | Can throw the **same, fewer, or narrower** checked exceptions. | Can throw **new or broader** checked exceptions тЖТ Compilation Error. | | 6 | **`static` Methods** | **Cannot be overridden** (they are *hidden*, not overridden). | Overriding a static method triggers a *method hiding* warning, not polymorphism. | | 7 | **`final` Methods** | **Cannot be overridden**. | Attempting to override тЖТ Compilation Error. | | 8 | **`private` Methods** | **Cannot be overridden** (not visible to subclass). | Child defines its own independent method. | | 9 | **`@Override` Annotation** | **Strongly recommended** (compiler-enforced check). | Without it, a typo silently creates a new method. | | 10 | **`abstract` Methods** | **Must be overridden** in the first concrete subclass. | Not overriding тЖТ Compilation Error. | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Pitfall:** If the parameter list is even **slightly different** (e.g., `int x` vs `long x`), Java treats it as **overloading**, NOT overriding. Many students lose marks by confusing the two. --- ## 2.3 The `super` Keyword in Overriding A child class, after overriding a method, can still invoke the parent class's original version using the `super.methodName()` call. This is essential when you want to **extend** the parent's behavior rather than completely **replace** it. ```java class Animal { void speak() { System.out.println("Animal makes a sound"); } } class Dog extends Animal { @Override void speak() { super.speak(); // Calls parent version System.out.println("Dog barks"); // Adds new behavior } } ``` **Execution Trace of `new Dog().speak();`** 1. `Dog.speak()` is called. 2. First line: `super.speak()` тЖТ executes `Animal.speak()` тЖТ prints **"Animal makes a sound"**. 3. Second line: prints **"Dog barks"**. 4. **Final Output:** ``` Animal makes a sound Dog barks ``` --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application of Method Overriding | |---|---| | **GUI Frameworks (JavaFX, Swing, Android)** | Overriding `onClick()`, `onTouch()`, `paint()` for custom UI behavior. | | **Plugin Architectures** | Third-party plugins override framework hooks (e.g., Eclipse extension points). | | **Template Method Design Pattern** | Parent class defines skeleton; child classes override specific steps. | | **Frameworks like Spring, Hibernate** | Beans override lifecycle callbacks (`init()`, `destroy()`). | | **Game Development** | `Entity.update()` overridden differently for Player, Enemy, NPC. | | **JDBC / Servlet API** | Applications override `service()`, `doGet()`, `doPost()` from the Servlet API. | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Insight:** In the **Template Method Design Pattern** (Gang of Four), method overriding is the *exact mechanism* by which the "holes" in a parent template are "filled" by subclasses. This is a frequently asked KTU question in Module 2 / Module 3. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Execution Trace ## 3.1 Exhaustive Analysis: Upcasting + Dynamic Dispatch The classic KTU board question is: *"What is the output of the following program? Explain dynamic method dispatch."* Let us solve it **step-by-step** as a KTU examiner would expect. ### 3.1.1 The Program ```java class Shape { void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing a generic Shape"); } } class Circle extends Shape { @Override void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing a Circle"); } } class Square extends Shape { @Override void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing a Square"); } } class TestOverride { public static void main(String[] args) { Shape s; // Reference of type Shape s = new Circle(); // Upcasting: Circle тЖТ Shape s.draw(); // Call 1 s = new Square(); // Upcasting: Square тЖТ Shape s.draw(); // Call 2 s = new Shape(); // Plain Shape object s.draw(); // Call 3 } } ``` ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Execution Trace | Step | Statement | Reference `s` points to | Actual Object Type | Method Invoked | Output | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `s = new Circle();` | Shape | **Circle** | (Object created) | тАФ | | 2 | `s.draw();` | Shape | **Circle** | `Circle.draw()` | `Drawing a Circle` | | 3 | `s = new Square();` | Shape | **Square** | (Object re-assigned) | тАФ | | 4 | `s.draw();` | Shape | **Square** | `Square.draw()` | `Drawing a Square` | | 5 | `s = new Shape();` | Shape | **Shape** | (Object created) | тАФ | | 6 | `s.draw();` | Shape | **Shape** | `Shape.draw()` | `Drawing a generic Shape` | ### 3.1.3 Final Output ``` Drawing a Circle Drawing a Square Drawing a generic Shape ``` ### 3.1.4 The "Why" Behind This Behavior - At **compile time**, the compiler checks if `draw()` exists in `Shape`. тЬЕ It does. Compilation succeeds. - At **runtime**, the JVM uses the **actual object type** (in the heap) to decide which `draw()` to execute. - This decision-making process is **Dynamic Method Dispatch**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Phrasing (Likely 7-Mark Question):** > *"Explain Dynamic Method Dispatch with a suitable Java program."* тАФ The above table is the **perfect model answer**. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: What Happens When Rules Are Violated? | Scenario | Code Snippet | Compiler Behavior | Runtime Behavior | |---|---|---|---| | Different parameter (overload, not override) | `void draw(int r)` in child | тЬЕ Compiles (treated as overload). | Both methods exist independently. | | More restrictive access | `private void draw()` in child | тЭМ Compile Error: *cannot reduce visibility*. | тАФ | | Covariant return type | Parent returns `Object`, child returns `String` | тЬЕ Compiles. | Polymorphism preserved. | | New checked exception | Parent throws none, child throws `IOException` | тЭМ Compile Error. | тАФ | | Overriding `final` method | `final void draw()` in parent | тЭМ Compile Error: *cannot override final*. | тАФ | | Overriding `static` method | `static void draw()` in both | тЬЕ Compiles (with warning). | **Method Hiding** тАФ static binding applies (NO polymorphism). | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board-Exam Trap:** The compiler error for a more restrictive access modifier reads: > *"attempting to assign weaker access privileges; was `public`"*. > Memorize this exact phrasing for KTU viva/ESE. --- ## 3.3 Case Study: Abstract Method Overriding (KTU Favourite) ```java abstract class Vehicle { abstract void start(); // No body тАФ contract } class Car extends Vehicle { @Override void start() { System.out.println("Car starts with a key"); } } class ElectricCar extends Vehicle { @Override void start() { System.out.println("ElectricCar starts with a button"); } } ``` **Analysis:** - `Vehicle` is **abstract** тЖТ cannot be instantiated (`new Vehicle()` is illegal). - `start()` has **no body** тЖТ subclasses **must override** it, or they too become abstract. - `Car` and `ElectricCar` are **concrete** classes providing the implementation. - This satisfies the **"open-closed principle"**: open for extension, closed for modification. --- ## 3.4 Mathematical Representation of Polymorphic Decision The method resolution at runtime can be expressed symbolically. Given a class hierarchy where a reference $R$ of type $C_{ref}$ points to an object $O$ of type $C_{actual}$: $$ C_{actual} \sqsubseteq C_{ref} $$ This denotes that $C_{actual}$ is a **subtype** of $C_{ref}$ (i.e., $C_{actual}$ is $C_{ref}$ or a descendant). The JVM searches the class hierarchy of $C_{actual}$ **bottom-up** until it finds the most specific override. $$ \text{Method Invoked} = \text{Lookup}(C_{actual}, \text{methodName}) = \arg\min_{C \in \text{Ancestors}(C_{actual})} \{\text{depth}(C)\} $$ In plain English: the JVM picks the **lowest (most-derived) class** in the actual object's hierarchy that defines the method. This is precisely why `s.draw()` prints *"Drawing a Circle"* when `s` holds a `Circle` object. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Class Hierarchy & Dynamic Dispatch Flow (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef parent fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef child fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef call fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 A["Class Shape
+ draw() : void"]:::parent B["Class Circle extends Shape
+ draw() : void OVERRIDDEN"]:::child C["Class Square extends Shape
+ draw() : void OVERRIDDEN"]:::child R["Reference Variable s
Compile-Time Type : Shape"]:::call O1["Heap Object : Circle"]:::call O2["Heap Object : Square"]:::call P1["Prints : Drawing a Circle"]:::output P2["Prints : Drawing a Square"]:::output A --> B A --> C R -.compile-time lookup.-> A R --> O1 R --> O2 O1 -- runtime dispatch --> P1 O2 -- runtime dispatch --> P2 ``` ### 4.1.1 Diagram Explanation - **Yellow Node (Shape):** The parent class containing the original `draw()` method. - **Blue Nodes (Circle, Square):** Child classes that **override** the parent's `draw()`. - **Dotted Line (compile-time lookup):** The compiler only sees that `s` is of type `Shape`, so it confirms `draw()` exists in `Shape`. No further decision at compile time. - **Solid Lines (runtime dispatch):** At runtime, the JVM follows the solid line from the reference to the **actual heap object** (Circle or Square) and invokes the **most specific overridden method**. - **Pink Nodes (Outputs):** The actual printed result based on the object's true type. --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Dynamic Method Dispatch ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef stage fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef result fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 S1["Stage 1 : Source Code
Shape s = new Circle();
s.draw();"]:::stage S2["Stage 2 : Compilation
Compiler verifies draw exists in Shape"]:::stage S3["Stage 3 : Bytecode Generation
invokevirtual Shape.draw"]:::stage D1{"Decision Point
Actual object type?"}:::decision S4["Stage 4 : Class Loader
Loads Circle.class into JVM"]:::stage S5["Stage 5 : Method Table Lookup
Search Circle's vtable for draw"]:::stage R1["Result : Circle.draw() executed"]:::result R2["Result : Square.draw() executed"]:::result R3["Result : Shape.draw() executed"]:::result S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> D1 D1 -- Circle --> S4 --> S5 --> R1 D1 -- Square --> S5 --> R2 D1 -- Shape --> R3 ``` ### 4.2.1 Architecture Explanation - **Stage 1тАУ3 (Compile Time):** The compiler generates an `invokevirtual` bytecode instruction. The compiler does **not** decide which version of `draw()` to call. - **Stage 4тАУ5 (Runtime):** The JVM loads the actual class, consults the **virtual method table (vtable)**, and dispatches to the correct override. - **Decision Point:** The branch is taken at runtime based on the heap object's class. > [!NOTE] > **Advanced Insight (For Curious Students):** > The JVM uses a per-class data structure called the **vtable (Virtual Method Table)**. Each entry maps a method signature to the actual code address. When a method is called virtually, the JVM indexes the vtable of the object's runtime class. This is why overriding has a tiny runtime cost (one extra pointer dereference) compared to static binding. --- ## 4.3 Summary Visual: Overloading vs. Overriding ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef ol fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef or fill:#20B2AA,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 OL["METHOD OVERLOADING
Same Class
Different Parameters
Compile-Time Polymorphism"]:::ol OR["METHOD OVERRIDING
Parent-Child Classes
Same Parameters
Runtime Polymorphism"]:::or OL -- Distinction --> OR ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024]** **Q: Define Method Overriding. List any four rules that must be followed while overriding a method in Java.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Method Overriding is a feature in Java where a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method that is already defined in its superclass. The method in the subclass must have the **same name, same return type (or covariant), and same parameter list** as the method in the parent class. It enables **runtime polymorphism** through **dynamic method dispatch**. **Four Rules (2 Marks тАФ 0.5 each):** 1. The method name and parameter list must be **identical** to the parent class method. 2. The return type must be the **same** or a **covariant subtype** of the parent's return type. 3. The access modifier in the child class must be **same or less restrictive** (e.g., `protected` тЖТ `public` is allowed, but `public` тЖТ `private` is not). 4. The overriding method must **not throw new or broader checked exceptions** than the parent method. > [!TIP] > **Allocation Tip:** 1 mark for definition, 0.5 ├Ч 4 = 2 marks for rules. Total = 3. --- ### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]** **Q: What is Dynamic Method Dispatch? How is it related to method overriding?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition of Dynamic Method Dispatch (2 Marks):** Dynamic Method Dispatch is the mechanism by which a call to an overridden method is **resolved at runtime** based on the **actual type of the object** being referred to, rather than the **type of the reference variable**. In Java, this is achieved using the `invokevirtual` bytecode instruction, which uses the object's runtime class to look up the most-specific implementation in the class's **virtual method table (vtable)**. **Relationship with Overriding (1 Mark):** Dynamic Method Dispatch is the **runtime mechanism** that makes method overriding meaningful. Without method overriding, there would be no "most-specific" method to dispatch to. Thus, overriding provides the *structure*, and dynamic dispatch provides the *behavior* of runtime polymorphism. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) тАФ KTU ESE Internal Choice Format > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module 2 Part B carries **14 marks**, with internal choice between two questions. Each question typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Solutions below follow the official valuation key style. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2024 тАУ Expected Pattern]** **Q: (a)** Explain the concept of method overriding in Java with a suitable example. Discuss dynamic method dispatch with reference to your example. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Concept of Method Overriding (3 Marks):** Method Overriding is an OOP feature that allows a **subclass** to provide a **specialized implementation** of a method inherited from its **superclass**. For overriding to occur: - The method must have the **same name**. - The method must have the **same parameter list** (same number, type, and order). - There must be an **IS-A relationship** (inheritance) between the classes. ```java class Animal { void sound() { System.out.println("Animal makes a sound"); } } class Dog extends Animal { @Override void sound() { // Overriding System.out.println("Dog barks"); } } class Cat extends Animal { @Override void sound() { // Overriding System.out.println("Cat meows"); } } ``` **Dynamic Method Dispatch Explanation (4 Marks):** Dynamic Method Dispatch is the runtime process of selecting which overridden method to invoke based on the actual object type. ```java public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { Animal a; // Reference of type Animal a = new Dog(); // Upcasting a.sound(); // Calls Dog.sound() at runtime a = new Cat(); // Upcasting a.sound(); // Calls Cat.sound() at runtime } } ``` **Output:** ``` Dog barks Cat meows ``` At **compile time**, the compiler only checks that `sound()` exists in `Animal`. At **runtime**, the JVM inspects the actual object in the heap and invokes the **most specific** `sound()` method available in the object's class hierarchy. This is **Dynamic Method Dispatch**, the engine behind **runtime polymorphism**. --- **Q: (b)** Differentiate between method overloading and method overriding. Explain why method overloading is an example of compile-time polymorphism while method overriding is an example of runtime polymorphism. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** | # | Feature | Method Overloading | Method Overriding | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Class Relationship** | Within the **same class** (or subclass). | Between **parent and child** classes. | | 2 | **Method Signature** | **Different** parameter list (number/type/order). | **Identical** parameter list. | | 3 | **Return Type** | Can be **different**. | Must be **same or covariant**. | | 4 | **Access Modifier** | No restriction. | Cannot be **more restrictive**. | | 5 | **Polymorphism Type** | **Compile-time** (Static). | **Runtime** (Dynamic). | | 6 | **Binding** | **Early Binding** by compiler. | **Late Binding** by JVM. | | 7 | **Keywords** | Not required. | `@Override` annotation recommended. | | 8 | **Static Methods** | Can be overloaded. | Can be hidden, **not overridden**. | **Why Overloading is Compile-Time Polymorphism (3 Marks):** In method overloading, the compiler knows **at compile time** which method to call because it can determine the method's identity from the **argument types** passed at the call site. For example, in `obj.add(5)` vs `obj.add(5, 10)`, the compiler resolves to the correct method before the program runs. This is **early/early binding**. **Why Overriding is Runtime Polymorphism (4 Marks):** In method overriding, the compiler **cannot** decide at compile time which version to invoke because the reference variable may be upcast to a parent type while the actual object may be of any subclass. The decision is deferred to **runtime**, where the JVM uses **Dynamic Method Dispatch** to consult the **virtual method table (vtable)** of the actual object's class and invoke the most specific implementation. This is **late binding** and is essential for designing **extensible, framework-based** systems. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice тАУ KTU Pattern]** **Q: (a)** What are the rules to be followed while overriding a method? Explain with examples what happens when these rules are violated. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Rule 1: Same Method Signature (1 Mark)** The overriding method must have the **same name, same number, type, and order of parameters** as the parent method. ```java class Parent { void show(int x) { System.out.println("Parent"); } } class Child extends Parent { @Override void show(int x) { System.out.println("Child"); } // Valid overriding } ``` Violation: `void show(double x)` in child тЖТ **method overloading**, not overriding. **Rule 2: Covariant Return Type Allowed (1 Mark)** The return type can be the same or a subtype of the parent's return type. ```java class Parent { Object get() { return null; } } class Child extends Parent { @Override String get() { return "Hello"; } // Valid тАФ String is a subtype of Object } ``` **Rule 3: Access Modifier Cannot Be Reduced (1 Mark)** The child method's access must be **same or more permissive**. ```java class Parent { public void display() { } } class Child extends Parent { @Override private void display() { } // тЭМ Compile Error: "attempting to assign weaker access privileges" } ``` **Rule 4: Exception Restriction (1 Mark)** The overriding method can throw the **same, fewer, or narrower** checked exceptions тАФ but **not new or broader** ones. ```java class Parent { void read() throws IOException { } } class Child extends Parent { @Override void read() throws Exception { } // тЭМ Compile Error: Exception is broader than IOException } ``` **Rule 5: `static`, `final`, and `private` Methods Cannot Be Overridden (2 Marks):** - `final` methods тЖТ Compile Error. - `private` methods тЖТ Not inherited; the child defines an independent method. - `static` methods тЖТ **Hidden**, not overridden. Static binding is used. No polymorphism. **Rule 6: `abstract` Methods Must Be Overridden (1 Mark):** If a parent class is abstract and defines an abstract method, the **first concrete child class must override** it. Failing to do so makes the child also abstract. --- **Q: (b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate method overriding using a superclass `Employee` and subclasses `Manager` and `Developer`. Each class should override a method `calculateBonus()` to compute bonus based on role-specific logic. Show the use of the `super` keyword to call the parent class version. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** ```java class Employee { String name; double salary; Employee(String name, double salary) { this.name = name; this.salary = salary; } double calculateBonus() { return salary * 0.10; // Generic 10% bonus } void displayBonus() { System.out.println(name + " Bonus: " + calculateBonus()); } } class Manager extends Employee { Manager(String name, double salary) { super(name, salary); } @Override double calculateBonus() { return super.calculateBonus() + 5000; // 10% + 5000 fixed } } class Developer extends Employee { Developer(String name, double salary) { super(name, salary); } @Override double calculateBonus() { return super.calculateBonus() + 2000; // 10% + 2000 fixed } } public class CompanyApp { public static void main(String[] args) { Employee e; e = new Manager("Alice", 80000); e.displayBonus(); // Dynamic dispatch тЖТ Manager.calculateBonus() e = new Developer("Bob", 60000); e.displayBonus(); // Dynamic dispatch тЖТ Developer.calculateBonus() e = new Employee("Charlie", 50000); e.displayBonus(); // No override тЖТ Employee.calculateBonus() } } ``` **Output:** ``` Alice Bonus: 13000.0 Bob Bonus: 8000.0 Charlie Bonus: 5000.0 ``` **Explanation of Marks Distribution (7 Marks):** - Class hierarchy and inheritance structure: **2 marks**. - Correct overriding with `@Override` annotation: **2 marks**. - Use of `super` keyword to invoke parent version: **1.5 marks**. - Main method demonstrating dynamic dispatch: **1.5 marks**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Points in KTU Board Valuation:** > 1. **Confusing Overloading with Overriding** тАФ A single parameter change turns overriding into overloading. Always re-check the **parameter list** before claiming overriding. > 2. **Forgetting `@Override` Annotation** тАФ While not a syntax error, examiners **deduct 0.5 marks** for not using it in board practicals and theory answers. > 3. **Writing `void` in the Return Type When Parent Returns a Value** тАФ This is a *compile error*, not overriding. The return type must match or be covariant. > 4. **Thinking `static` Methods Are Overridden** тАФ They are *hidden*. A question asking "What is the output?" with a `static` method is testing whether you know this. Static methods use **compile-time binding**, so the **reference type** decides, not the object type. > 5. **Forgetting to Use `super` to Call Parent** тАФ When a question says *"extend the parent's behavior"*, examiners expect `super.methodName()`. Not using it loses 1тАУ1.5 marks. > 6. **Missing Constructor in Child** тАФ When the parent has a parameterized constructor, the child must explicitly call `super(args)`. Omitting this is a compile error. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Print this section and revise 5 minutes before the exam.** - тЬЕ **Definition:** Redefining a parent class method in the child class with the **same signature** to achieve **runtime polymorphism**. - тЬЕ **Dynamic Method Dispatch:** Runtime resolution of which overridden method to call, based on the **actual object's type**. - тЬЕ **Signature Rule:** Same **name + same parameter list**. Different params = **overloading**, not overriding. - тЬЕ **Return Type:** Same or **covariant subtype**. - тЬЕ **Access Modifier:** Same or **less restrictive** (more permissive). - тЬЕ **Exceptions:** Can throw **same, fewer, or narrower** checked exceptions тАФ **not broader or new** ones. - тЬЕ **`@Override` Annotation:** Always use it тАФ compiler-verified and examiner-approved. - тЬЕ **Cannot Override:** `static` (hidden), `final` (compile error), `private` (not inherited). - тЬЕ **Must Override:** Any `abstract` method from an abstract parent. - тЬЕ **`super` Keyword:** Used inside overridden method to call parent's original version тАФ useful for **extending**, not just replacing. - тЬЕ **Upcasting:** `Parent p = new Child();` is legal because of IS-A. Enables polymorphism. - тЬЕ **Binding Type:** Overriding = **Late Binding** (Runtime). Overloading = **Early Binding** (Compile-time). - тЬЕ **Real-World Pattern:** **Template Method Design Pattern** relies entirely on method overriding. - тЬЕ **Viva One-Liner:** *"Overriding = same signature, different implementation, different class тАФ resolved at runtime."*

topics.title: Dynamic Method Dispatch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Method Dispatch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dynamic Method Dispatch (DMD)** is a core runtime polymorphism mechanism in Java wherein a call to an **overridden instance method** is resolved at **runtime** (not at compile time) based on the **actual type of the object** the reference variable points to, rather than the declared type of the reference. The Java Virtual Machine (JVM) uses the **method table (vtable)** of the receiving object to determine which implementation of the method to invoke.

In the KTU 2024 OOP syllabus, this concept falls under **Module 2: Inheritance & Polymorphism** and is the central technique through which Java achieves **subtype polymorphism** (also called *inclusion polymorphism*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Phrasing expected in answers):**
> *"Dynamic Method Dispatch is the mechanism by which a call to an overridden method is resolved at run time, based on the type of the object being referred to by the superclass reference variable."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ "The Hospital Reception"

Imagine you arrive at a hospital reception with a reference number. The slip simply says **"Doctor on duty"** (this is your *superclass reference*). You do not know whether the doctor is a **Cardiologist**, a **Neurologist**, or a **Dermatologist** тАФ that decision is made only when you enter the consultation room and the *actual doctor present* examines you. The treatment you receive is determined by **who is physically in the room** (*the actual object type*), not by what your slip says (*the reference type*).

Similarly, in Dynamic Method Dispatch:
- The **reference variable** (slip) is of the **parent type**.
- The **actual object** (doctor) is of the **child type**.
- The **method called** (treatment) is determined by the **object's runtime type**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - **Static Binding (Early Binding):** Happens at compile time тАФ applies to `static`, `private`, `final`, and overloaded methods.
> - **Dynamic Binding (Late Binding):** Happens at runtime тАФ applies to overridden instance methods via DMD.

## 1.3 Conditions Necessary for Dynamic Method Dispatch

For DMD to work, the following **three mandatory conditions** must be satisfied in Java:

1. **Inheritance must exist** between a superclass and a subclass.
2. The subclass must **override** a method inherited from the superclass (method signature must be identical).
3. The method call must be made through a **superclass reference variable** that holds a **subclass object** (upcasting).

> [!WARNING]
> If any of these three conditions fails, Java will either call the superclass method (no overriding) or refuse to compile (no inheritance relationship).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics тАФ How DMD Works Inside the JVM

The following bullet-points describe the **runtime resolution pipeline** the JVM follows when a dynamic dispatch is triggered:

- **Step 1 тАФ Bytecode Verification:** The Java compiler (`javac`) checks only that the method *exists in the declared type* of the reference. It does **not** verify which subclass version will be called.
- **Step 2 тАФ Constant Pool Resolution:** The compiler emits a symbolic reference (e.g., `Animal.speak()`) in the `.class` file's constant pool. This is unresolved at compile time.
- **Step 3 тАФ Object Header Inspection:** At runtime, the JVM inspects the **object header** of the target object to discover its **actual runtime class**.
- **Step 4 тАФ vtable Lookup:** The JVM consults the **virtual method table (vtable)** of the actual object's class. Each class has exactly one vtable, mapping method signatures to the implementation addresses.
- **Step 5 тАФ Address Invocation:** The implementation address found in the vtable is invoked. This is the **late binding** step.
- **Step 6 тАФ Return & Stack Cleanup:** The return value is pushed onto the operand stack, and the method's stack frame is popped.

> [!NOTE]
> The vtable is a **per-class** data structure, populated at class-loading time. It enables a **constant-time O(1)** method dispatch, which is why DMD has negligible performance overhead in modern JVMs (HotSpot, OpenJ9).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Rule / Property | Behavior in DMD | KTU Frequency |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--:|
| 1 | Instance methods (overridden) | Bound at **runtime** using vtable | тШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕ |
| 2 | `static` methods | Bound at **compile time** (no DMD) | тШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕ |
| 3 | `private` methods | Bound at **compile time** (no override possible) | тШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕ |
| 4 | `final` methods | Bound at **compile time** (cannot be overridden) | тШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕ |
| 5 | `final` *class* (no subclass exists) | No DMD possible тАФ no polymorphic object | тШЕтШЕтШЕ |
| 6 | Instance variables / fields | Bound at **compile time** (NO DMD for data) | тШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕ |
| 7 | Constructors | Bound at **compile time** (no virtual constructors) | тШЕтШЕтШЕ |
| 8 | Upcasting (Sub тЖТ Super) | Implicit, always safe | тШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕ |
| 9 | Downcasting (Super тЖТ Sub) | Explicit, requires `instanceof` check | тШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕ |
| 10 | Method hiding (static override) | Compile-time binding тАФ **not** DMD | тШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Answers:** *"Methods are virtual, fields are not."* тАФ Always state this in exams whenever DMD is discussed with data members.

## 2.3 Why Dynamic Method Dispatch Matters in Real Engineering

- **Framework Design:** Spring, Hibernate, JavaFX, and Android all rely on DMD to allow user-defined classes to inject custom behavior into framework callbacks.
- **Plugin Architectures:** DMD enables the JVM to invoke user code that did not exist when the framework was compiled.
- **Design Pattern Backbone:** Patterns like **Strategy**, **Template Method**, **State**, and **Observer** are physically implemented using DMD in Java.
- **Open/Closed Principle (SOLID):** DMD allows software modules to be *open for extension* (new subclasses) but *closed for modification* (existing code untouched).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Java Demonstration тАФ Complete Code Trace

Below is the **canonical textbook demonstration** of Dynamic Method Dispatch that KTU examiners frequently reproduce in question papers. We will trace execution step-by-step.

```java
class Animal {
    void speak() {
        System.out.println("Animal speaks");
    }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override
    void speak() {
        System.out.println("Dog barks");
    }
}

class Cat extends Animal {
    @Override
    void speak() {
        System.out.println("Cat meows");
    }
}

public class DispatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Animal ref;          // superclass reference variable

        ref = new Dog();     // upcast: Dog object stored in Animal reference
        ref.speak();         // OUTPUT: Dog barks

        ref = new Cat();     // upcast: Cat object stored in Animal reference
        ref.speak();         // OUTPUT: Cat meows
    }
}
```

## 3.2 Exhaustive Execution Trace (Step-by-Step)

The table below exhaustively maps every line of execution, the memory state, and the JVM's dispatch decision:

| Line | Code Executed | ref (type) | Object (actual) | Method Bound To | Output |
|:--:|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--:|
| 11 | `Animal ref;` | `Animal` | `null` | тАФ | (none) |
| 13 | `ref = new Dog();` | `Animal` | `Dog` instance | vtable of `Dog` | (none) |
| 14 | `ref.speak();` | `Animal` | `Dog` instance | `Dog.speak()` resolved at runtime | `Dog barks` |
| 16 | `ref = new Cat();` | `Animal` | `Cat` instance | тАФ | (none) |
| 17 | `ref.speak();` | `Animal` | `Cat` instance | `Cat.speak()` resolved at runtime | `Cat meows` |

**Final Console Output (must be reproduced verbatim in KTU answers):**

```text
Dog barks
Cat meows
```

## 3.3 The Decisive Mathematical Model

Let us formalize DMD using set-theoretic notation that KTU evaluators appreciate for clarity:

$$
\forall \, ref \in T_{super}, \; obj \in T_{sub} \text{ where } T_{sub} \subseteq T_{super} \text{ (inheritance)}
$$

The dispatch function $\Delta$ that maps a method invocation to a concrete method body is defined as:

$$
\Delta(ref, m) = \begin{cases}
m_{T_{obj}} & \text{if } m \text{ is overridden in } T_{obj} \\
m_{T_{super}} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $\Delta$ is the dispatch operator.
- $ref$ is the reference variable of declared type $T_{super}$.
- $m$ is the method name (signature).
- $T_{obj}$ is the **runtime (actual) class** of the object.
- $T_{super}$ is the **compile-time (declared) class** of the reference.

The **decision time** $t_d$ for the dispatch is given by:

$$
t_d = t_{load} + t_{vtable\_lookup} + t_{invocation}
$$

Where $t_{vtable\_lookup}$ is $O(1)$ for virtual methods in the HotSpot JVM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Insight for Toppers:** The vtable lookup can be viewed as a *function composition* $f \circ g$, where $f$ maps the object to its class metadata, and $g$ maps the method signature to the implementation address. Both $f$ and $g$ are $O(1)$ hash lookups.

## 3.4 Counter-Example: When DMD Does NOT Apply

Consider the following case which KTU frequently tests as a *trick question*:

```java
class Super {
    static void show() { System.out.println("Super show"); }
}
class Sub extends Super {
    static void show() { System.out.println("Sub show"); }
}
public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Super obj = new Sub();
        obj.show();     // OUTPUT: Super show
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step analysis:**

| Step | Action | Result |
|:--:|:--|:--:|
| 1 | `static` method `show()` in `Super` | Compile-time binding candidate |
| 2 | `static` method `show()` in `Sub` (not an override тАФ it is **method hiding**) | Compile-time binding candidate |
| 3 | Compiler sees reference type `Super` | Binds to `Super.show()` |
| 4 | Output | `Super show` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently lose marks by stating "Sub show" as the output. Remember: **`static` methods are not polymorphic**. They follow the reference type, not the object type.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inheritance Hierarchy Diagram

The following Mermaid block illustrates the class hierarchy used in our canonical example.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Animal {
        +speak() void
    }
    class Dog {
        +speak() void
    }
    class Cat {
        +speak() void
    }
    Animal <|-- Dog
    Animal <|-- Cat
```

## 4.2 Runtime Dispatch Flow тАФ Architectural Block Diagram

This diagram maps the **block-level functional architecture** of how a single `ref.speak()` call is routed through the JVM.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Code Line ref.speak] --> B[Java Compiler javac]
    B --> C{Reference Type Exists in Symbol Table?}
    C -- No --> D[Compilation Error]
    C -- Yes --> E[Emit Symbolic Reference in Constant Pool]
    E --> F[Bytecode Generated]
    F --> G[JVM Loads Class at Runtime]
    G --> H[Object Header Reads Actual Class]
    H --> I[Fetch vtable of Actual Class]
    I --> J{Method Overridden in vtable?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Invoke Subclass Method]
    J -- No --> L[Invoke Superclass Method]
    K --> M[Push Return Value on Stack]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Pop Stack Frame Continue Execution]
```

## 4.3 Memory Layout тАФ Reference vs Object

The diagram below depicts the **heap and stack memory state** at the moment of dispatch, which is a commonly drawn figure in KTU answer sheets.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STK[Stack Frame of main]
        S1[ref variable declared as Animal]
    end
    subgraph HEP[Heap Memory]
        O1[Dog Object]
        O2[Cat Object]
    end
    subgraph VTBL1[Dog vtable]
        V1[speak -> Dog.speak]
    end
    subgraph VTBL2[Cat vtable]
        V2[speak -> Cat.speak]
    end
    S1 -.first assignment.-> O1
    S1 -.second assignment.-> O2
    O1 --- VTBL1
    O2 --- VTBL2
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam тАФ May 2024]`
**Define Dynamic Method Dispatch. State the two essential conditions required for Dynamic Method Dispatch in Java.**

**Model Answer:**

> *Dynamic Method Dispatch is the mechanism by which a call to an overridden method is resolved at run time, based on the actual type of the object being referenced by a superclass variable, rather than the type of the reference variable itself.*

**Two essential conditions:**

1. There must be an **inheritance relationship** between two classes (superclass and subclass).
2. The method in the subclass must **override** a method declared in the superclass with an identical signature, and the call must be made through a **superclass reference holding a subclass object**.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark per condition.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023]`
**Why do `static` methods not participate in Dynamic Method Dispatch?**

**Model Answer:**

> *Static methods belong to the class, not to any individual object. They are resolved at compile time based on the declared type of the reference variable (static binding). Since they cannot be overridden (only hidden) and have no runtime association with the object's actual class, they are not eligible for dynamic method dispatch.*

*(Examiner's Note: Mention the term "method hiding" for full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A тАФ `[KTU University Exam тАФ Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Dynamic Method Dispatch** with a suitable Java program. Show how the output differs from that of a **static binding** scenario. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the role of the **virtual method table (vtable)** in implementing Dynamic Method Dispatch? Explain with a diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (a):

```java
class Shape {
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a generic shape");
    }
    static void label() {
        System.out.println("Generic Shape Label");
    }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a circle");
    }
    static void label() {
        System.out.println("Circle Label");
    }
}

class Square extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a square");
    }
    static void label() {
        System.out.println("Square Label");
    }
}

public class DMDDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s;

        s = new Circle();
        s.draw();          // DMD: resolves to Circle.draw()
        s.label();         // Static binding: resolves to Shape.label()

        s = new Square();
        s.draw();          // DMD: resolves to Square.draw()
        s.label();         // Static binding: resolves to Shape.label()
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```text
Drawing a circle
Generic Shape Label
Drawing a square
Generic Shape Label
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**

- `s.draw()` тЖТ **Dynamic dispatch.** JVM sees `s` is an `Animal`-typed reference pointing to a `Circle` object; consults the vtable; invokes `Circle.draw()`. Then for `Square`, the vtable entry for `draw()` points to `Square.draw()`.
- `s.label()` тЖТ **Static dispatch.** The compiler sees `s` declared as `Shape` and binds the call to `Shape.label()`. The runtime object type is irrelevant.

This proves that **only overridden instance methods** participate in DMD, while `static` methods are statically bound.

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks for code, 2 marks for output, 3 marks for explanation of static vs dynamic binding.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (b):

The **virtual method table (vtable)** is a per-class lookup table maintained by the JVM inside the **method area** of native memory. Each entry in the vtable maps a method signature to a **direct pointer** to the bytecode implementation of that method for the class.

**Structure of a vtable for our example:**

| Class | draw() entry | label() entry |
|:--|:--|:--|
| `Shape` | тЖТ `Shape.draw()` | тЖТ `Shape.label()` |
| `Circle` | тЖТ `Circle.draw()` | inherited тЖТ `Shape.label()` |
| `Square` | тЖТ `Square.draw()` | inherited тЖТ `Shape.label()` |

**Diagram of the vtable lookup process:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Call s.draw where s points to Circle object] --> B[JVM reads object header]
    B --> C[Identify actual class: Circle]
    C --> D[Access Circle vtable]
    D --> E{Entry for draw exists?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Invoke Circle.draw]
    E -- No --> G[Walk up inheritance chain to Shape]
    G --> H[Invoke Shape.draw]
```

**Key role of the vtable:**

- Enables **O(1) method dispatch** at runtime.
- Each subclass inherits the parent's vtable and **overwrites** only the entries for methods it overrides.
- This is the **fundamental data structure** that makes polymorphism in Java efficient.

*(Marks distribution: 3 marks for vtable concept, 2 marks for table, 2 marks for diagram.)*

---

### Question B тАФ Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam тАФ Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat Java program, demonstrate how **Dynamic Method Dispatch** works in a **multilevel inheritance** chain (Class A тЖТ B тЖТ C), and explain how the JVM decides which method to invoke at each level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **static binding** and **dynamic binding**. Give two examples each where Java uses each type. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (a):

```java
class A {
    void show() {
        System.out.println("Class A show");
    }
}
class B extends A {
    @Override
    void show() {
        System.out.println("Class B show");
    }
}
class C extends B {
    @Override
    void show() {
        System.out.println("Class C show");
    }
}

public class MultilevelDispatch {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        A ref;

        ref = new B();
        ref.show();      // Calls B.show()

        ref = new C();
        ref.show();      // Calls C.show()

        ref = new A();
        ref.show();      // Calls A.show()
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```text
Class B show
Class C show
Class A show
```

**Explanation of JVM dispatch at each level:**

| Call | Reference Type | Object Type | JVM vtable consulted | Method Invoked |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| `ref = new B(); ref.show();` | `A` | `B` | `B` vtable | `B.show()` |
| `ref = new C(); ref.show();` | `A` | `C` | `C` vtable | `C.show()` |
| `ref = new A(); ref.show();` | `A` | `A` | `A` vtable | `A.show()` |

The JVM always looks at the **actual object's vtable**, not the reference's. In the third case, the object *is* of type `A`, so the JVM invokes `A.show()`. This demonstrates that even in a **multilevel chain** (A тЖТ B тЖТ C), DMD correctly propagates overrides.

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks for code, 1 mark for output, 4 marks for dispatch table + explanation.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (b):

| Aspect | Static Binding (Early Binding) | Dynamic Binding (Late Binding) |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **When resolved** | At **compile time** | At **runtime** |
| **Mechanism** | Compiler matches the call to a method by reference type | JVM matches the call to a method by object's runtime class |
| **Performance** | Faster (no vtable lookup) | Slightly slower (vtable indirection) |
| **Flexibility** | Less flexible | More flexible (enables polymorphism) |
| **Methods involved** | `static`, `private`, `final`, constructors | Overridden instance methods |

**Two examples of static binding:**

1. `Math.max(int, int)` тАФ called as a static method resolved at compile time.
2. A `final` method in a class тАФ cannot be overridden, hence bound at compile time.

**Two examples of dynamic binding:**

1. Overridden `equals()` method called via an `Object` reference.
2. The `paintComponent(Graphics g)` method in Java Swing when overridden in a custom `JPanel` subclass.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Do **not** confuse **method overloading** (compile-time polymorphism, static binding) with **method overriding** (runtime polymorphism, dynamic binding). Marks are deducted when students interchange the two.
> - Do **not** claim that **data members (fields)** participate in DMD. Fields are always statically bound.
> - Always mention the terms **"upcasting"** and **"vtable"** in long answers тАФ these are high-value keywords that examiners scan for.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Dynamic Method Dispatch = **runtime resolution** of overridden instance methods using the **actual object type**, not the reference type.
- **Three Required Conditions:** Inheritance + Overriding + Upcasting (super-reference holding sub-object).
- **vtable:** The internal per-class data structure that makes DMD an **O(1)** operation in the JVM.
- **Golden Rule:** *"Methods are virtual, fields are not."* тАФ Fields are statically bound, methods can be dynamically dispatched.
- **Static methods are NOT polymorphic** тАФ they are hidden, not overridden, and follow the reference type.
- **Final, private, static** methods are always **statically bound** тАФ they cannot be overridden.
- **Constructors** are never dispatched dynamically тАФ Java has no virtual constructors.
- **Upcasting** is implicit and always safe; **downcasting** is explicit and must be guarded by `instanceof`.
- **Real-world usage:** DMD powers the **Strategy**, **Template**, **Observer**, and **State** design patterns, and is the foundation of frameworks like **Spring** and **JavaFX**.
- **Performance:** DMD overhead is negligible due to **vtable caching** in modern JVMs (HotSpot, OpenJ9).
- **KTU Keyword Checklist** to include in every answer: *runtime polymorphism, vtable, upcasting, overridden method, object header, late binding, JVM.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using final with Inheritance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Using final with Inheritance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Java Programming Language**, the keyword `final` is a **non-access modifier** that is used to **restrict the user** by preventing the modification of variables, methods, and classes. When applied in the context of **Inheritance** (a Module 2 pillar of OOP), the `final` keyword enforces **definitive binding** between superclass and subclass components.

There are **three primary applications** of `final` that interact directly with inheritance:

| Application | Target | Effect on Inheritance |
|-------------|--------|----------------------|
| `final` Class | Entire class | Inheritance is **BLOCKED** тАФ no subclass can be created |
| `final` Method | Method declaration | Method **OVERRIDING is BLOCKED** in subclasses |
| `final` Variable | Field / Local variable | The variable becomes a **CONSTANT** тАФ value is locked after initialization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** As per the PBCST304 Module 2 syllabus, students must be able to *explain and apply* the `final` keyword in conjunction with class inheritance, method overriding, and member variables. This topic is a **frequent 3-mark and 7-mark question** in university examinations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** The `final` keyword is **not** the same as the `finally` block (exception handling) or the `finalize()` method (Garbage Collection). KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

To make the concept immediately clear, consider the following analogy:

> ЁЯПЫя╕П **"The Sealed Monument" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a famous **monument** (the parent class). The government declares it a **"Protected National Heritage"** (the `final` modifier).
>
> - **Final Class** = The monument itself is **sealed**. Nobody is allowed to construct a replica, extension, or modified copy of it. Inheritance is **forbidden**. Example: `String`, `Math`, `System` classes in Java are all `final`.
>
> - **Final Method** = The monument has a **fixed, sacred ritual** (a method). Children, followers, and future generations (subclasses) **cannot modify** how the ritual is performed. They can inherit the behavior, but **override is not allowed**.
>
> - **Final Variable** = A **graven inscription** on the monument тАФ the value is **carved in stone forever**. Once set, it can never be rewritten. This is how we create true **constants** in Java.

This mental model makes the three behaviors of `final` crystal clear: **lock the class, lock the method, lock the value**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Conventions (Bolded Highlights)

- **Naming Convention for `final` variables:** `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE` (e.g., `MAX_VALUE`, `PI`, `DEFAULT_SIZE`).
- **Default Initialization:** A `final` instance variable must be initialized in **every constructor** OR at the point of declaration.
- **Blank `final` variable:** A `final` variable declared but not initialized at declaration is called a *blank final variable* тАФ it must be initialized in the constructor.
- **Final + Static combo:** `public static final` is the canonical Java constant pattern (e.g., `Math.PI`).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown тАФ The Three Pillars of `final`

### ЁЯФТ Pillar 1: `final` Classes (Inheritance Blockade)

When a class is declared `final`, **no other class can extend it**. This terminates the inheritance chain at that node.

```java
final class Vehicle {
    void start() {
        System.out.println("Vehicle starts");
    }
}

class Car extends Vehicle {  // тЭМ COMPILE-TIME ERROR
    // Cannot inherit from final class
}
```

**Compiler Error Produced:**
```
error: cannot inherit from final class Vehicle
class Car extends Vehicle
```

**Real-world Java examples:** `java.lang.String`, `java.lang.Math`, `java.lang.System` тАФ all are final to guarantee **immutability** and **security**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Justification:** Making a class `final` is an intentional design decision to **prevent subclass-based corruption** of critical system components.

---

### ЁЯФТ Pillar 2: `final` Methods (Overriding Blockade)

When a method is declared `final`, subclasses **may inherit the method** but **cannot override (redefine) it**. This locks the behavior of that specific method in the inheritance hierarchy.

```java
class BankAccount {
    final double calculateInterest(double principal) {
        return principal * 0.05;
    }
}

class SavingsAccount extends BankAccount {
    @Override
    double calculateInterest(double principal) {
        return principal * 0.07;  // тЭМ COMPILE-TIME ERROR
    }
}
```

**Compiler Error:**
```
error: calculateInterest(double) in SavingsAccount cannot override
calculateInterest(double) in BankAccount
overridden method is final
```

**Important sub-rules:**
- тЬЕ **Overloading** of a `final` method is **allowed** (different parameters).
- тЬЕ **Hiding** a `final static` method in a subclass is **allowed** (no overriding for static methods anyway).

---

### ЁЯФТ Pillar 3: `final` Variables (Constant Creation)

A `final` variable can be assigned **only once**. After assignment, it behaves as a **read-only constant**.

```java
class Constants {
    final int MAX = 100;            // Final instance variable
    final int MIN;                   // Blank final variable
    static final double PI = 3.14159; // Final static constant
    
    Constants() {
        MIN = 1;  // Blank final initialized in constructor
    }
}
```

**Sub-rules for `final` variables:**
1. A `final` variable **must be definitely assigned** before use.
2. For **reference variables**, `final` means the **reference cannot change**, but the **internal state of the object can** (immutability of reference, not object).
3. `final` parameters cannot be reassigned inside the method.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Rule Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Rule | Allowed? | KTU Weightage |
|---|------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Extending a `final` class | тЭМ Compile Error | тнРтнРтнР |
| 2 | Overriding a `final` method | тЭМ Compile Error | тнРтнРтнРтнР |
| 3 | Overloading a `final` method | тЬЕ Allowed | тнРтнР |
| 4 | Hiding a `final static` method | тЬЕ Allowed | тнРтнР |
| 5 | Reassigning a `final` variable | тЭМ Compile Error | тнРтнРтнР |
| 6 | Modifying state of a `final` referenced object | тЬЕ Allowed | тнРтнРтнР |
| 7 | Initializing a blank `final` in constructor | тЬЕ Allowed | тнРтнР |
| 8 | Declaring `abstract final` method together | тЭМ Logical Conflict | тнРтнРтнР |
| 9 | Making a constructor `final` | тЭМ Not Allowed (constructors can't be inherited) | тнР |
| 10 | Using `final` with an interface variable | тЬЕ Implicitly `public static final` | тнРтнР |

---

## 2.3 The Logical Impossibility: `abstract` + `final`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core logical contradiction:** An `abstract` method **must** be overridden in a subclass to provide a body. A `final` method **cannot** be overridden. Therefore, declaring a method as both `abstract` and `final` produces a **compile-time error** тАФ the two modifiers are mutually exclusive.

**Mathematical/Logical Expression of the contradiction:**

$$
\text{Valid} = \neg(\text{abstract} \land \text{final})
$$

In plain logic: **A method can never be simultaneously abstract and final.**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of `final` |
|--------|------------------------|
| **Banking Software** | `final` interest rate calculation methods to prevent tampering |
| **Cryptography** | `final` key-handling classes to prevent subclassing attacks |
| **API Design** | `final` public methods to lock down published contract behavior |
| **Embedded Systems** | `final` variables for hardware register mappings (true constants) |
| **Game Development** | `final` entity classes (e.g., `Player`) to prevent gameplay cheats via custom subclasses |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Trace Analysis & Logical Proofs

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a programming concept, "derivations" here translate to **compile-time logical analyses** and **step-by-step program traces** that justify whether a given `final` usage is **valid** or **erroneous**.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Logical Proof of Why `final` Classes Cannot Be Inherited

**Premise (P1):** Inheritance in Java follows the keyword `extends`, creating an **"is-a"** relationship.

**Premise (P2):** A subclass inherits all non-private members and may add new ones, thereby **extending** the parent's behavior.

**Premise (P3):** The `final` modifier, by language specification, **prohibits modification**.

**Logical Chain:**

$$
\text{Step 1: Inherit} \Rightarrow \text{Subclass acquires parent's structure}
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: Subclass} \Rightarrow \text{Can add new methods/fields (modification by extension)}
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: final} \Rightarrow \text{Modification is FORBIDDEN}
$$

$$
\text{Step 4: Contradiction} \Rightarrow \text{Therefore, extends} \rightarrow \text{final class is INVALID}
$$

**Conclusion:** Allowing inheritance of a `final` class would violate the very meaning of `final` тАФ hence the compiler rejects it.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Program Trace: Final Method Behavior

```java
class Parent {
    final void display() {
        System.out.println("Parent's final display()");
    }
    void greet() {
        System.out.println("Parent's greet()");
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    // Attempting override of final method
    // void display() { }  // тЭМ COMPILE ERROR
    
    // Overriding non-final method - allowed
    @Override
    void greet() {
        System.out.println("Child's overridden greet()");
    }
}

public class TestFinal {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Child c = new Child();
        c.display();  // Calls Parent's final version (inherited, not overridden)
        c.greet();    // Calls Child's overridden version
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step execution trace:**

| Line | Action | Output/State |
|------|--------|--------------|
| 1 | `Child c = new Child();` | Object `c` of type `Child` created |
| 2 | `c.display();` | Method resolution: `display` is `final` in `Parent`, inherited as-is тЖТ calls Parent version |
| 3 | `c.greet();` | Method resolution: `greet` is non-final тЖТ dynamic dispatch to `Child`'s version |
| тАФ | **Final Output** | `Parent's final display()` \n `Child's overridden greet()` |

**Compilation Output (if `display` override is uncommented):**
```
error: display() in Child cannot override display() in Parent
overridden method is final
```

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Program Trace: Blank `final` Variable

```java
class Student {
    final int rollNumber;  // Blank final - must be assigned in constructor
    final String name;
    
    Student(int r, String n) {
        rollNumber = r;   // тЬЕ Assigned once
        name = n;         // тЬЕ Assigned once
    }
    
    void changeRoll(int r) {
        // rollNumber = r;  // тЭМ COMPILE ERROR - already assigned
    }
}
```

**Logical verification:**

$$
\text{State of } rollNumber: \text{UNDEFINED} \xrightarrow{\text{Constructor}} \text{DEFINED} \xrightarrow{\text{Any further attempt}} \text{COMPILE ERROR}
$$

This proves the **single-assignment property** of `final` variables.

---

## 3.4 Derivative Analysis: `final` Reference vs Immutable Object

A common KTU pitfall: students think `final` makes the object immutable. **It does not.**

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;

class ReferenceDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        final ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>();
        list.add("Hello");   // тЬЕ Allowed - object state changed
        list.add("World");   // тЬЕ Allowed
        System.out.println(list);  // [Hello, World]
        
        // list = new ArrayList<>();  // тЭМ COMPILE ERROR - reference reassignment
    }
}
```

**Logical expression:**

$$
\text{final Reference} \Rightarrow \text{Reference is locked, NOT the heap object}
$$

$$
\text{Object State} = \text{Mutable as usual}
$$

---

## 3.5 Final Method Overloading тАФ Allowed Scenario

```java
class Calculator {
    final int add(int a, int b) {
        return a + b;
    }
    
    // тЬЕ Overloading - different signature
    final double add(double a, double b) {
        return a + b;
    }
    
    // тЬЕ Overloading - different number of parameters
    final int add(int a, int b, int c) {
        return a + b + c;
    }
}
```

**Rule Justification:** Overloading is resolved at **compile time** based on signature (parameter types/count), not at runtime. Since `final` only prevents **overriding** (runtime polymorphism), overloading is unaffected.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Final Keyword Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([final keyword applied to]) --> A{Target Type?}
    A -->|Class| B[final class]
    A -->|Method| C[final method]
    A -->|Variable| D[final variable]
    
    B --> B1[Inheritance: NOT allowed]
    B --> B2[Object creation: Allowed]
    B1 --> B1A[Compile error on extends]
    B2 --> B2A[Acts as leaf in hierarchy]
    
    C --> C1[Overriding: NOT allowed]
    C --> C2[Overloading: Allowed]
    C --> C3[Hiding static: Allowed]
    C1 --> C1A[Compile error on override]
    C2 --> C2A[Different signatures compile]
    
    D --> D1[Reassignment: NOT allowed]
    D --> D2[Object mutation: Allowed]
    D --> D3[Single assignment only]
    D1 --> D1A[Compile error on second write]
    D2 --> D2A[Internal state can change]

    style B fill:#E94B3C,stroke:#000,color:#fff

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Final Class Hierarchy Blockade

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class Vehicle {
        <<final>>
        +void start()
        +void stop()
    }

        +void honk()
    }

    }

        +void bark()

    Vehicle --> Car : тЭМ NOT ALLOWED
    Car --> SportsCar : тЬЕ Allowed
    Animal --> Dog : тЬЕ Allowed
    
    note for Vehicle "Sealed class\nInheritance BLOCKED"
    note for Animal "Normal class\nInheritance ALLOWED"
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Final Method тАФ Override Blockade Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as main method
    participant Parent as Parent Class
    participant Child as Child Class
    
    Note over Parent: final void show()
    Note over Child: Attempts to override show()
    
    Main->>Child: new Child()
    Child->>Parent: inherits show() as-is
    Main->>Child: child.show()
    Child->>Parent: delegates to Parent.show()
    Parent-->>Main: Output from Parent version

    rect rgb(255, 200, 200)
    Note right of Child: Compile-time error if override attempted
    end
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: `final` Variable Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Declared: final int X
    Declared --> Blank: No value assigned yet
    Declared --> Initialized: value assigned at declaration
    
    Blank --> ConstructorAssigned: initialized in constructor
    Blank --> CompileError: used before initialization
    
    ConstructorAssigned --> Locked: Object construction complete
    Initialized --> Locked: Immediate lock
    
    Locked --> CompileError: any reassignment attempt
    Locked --> [*]: program ends
    
    note right of Locked: Constant value\nRead-only forever
```
