# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is the difference between `final`, `finally`, and `finalize()` in Java?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Keyword | Type | Purpose | Context |
|---------|------|---------|---------|
| `final` | Modifier | Restricts modification of classes, methods, variables | Inheritance & constants |
| `finally` | Block | Code that always executes after try-catch | Exception handling |
| `finalize()` | Method | Called by Garbage Collector before object destruction | Memory cleanup |

`final` is a **modifier**, `finally` is an **exception-handling block**, and `finalize()` is an **Object class method** invoked by the JVM garbage collector. They are completely unrelated concepts. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Can a `final` method be overloaded? Justify with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Yes**, a `final` method **can be overloaded** in Java. The `final` modifier only prevents **overriding** (runtime polymorphism), not **overloading** (compile-time polymorphism based on parameter signature).

```java
class Demo {
    final void show(int x) { System.out.println("int: " + x); }
    final void show(double x) { System.out.println("double: " + x); }
}
```

Here both `show` methods are `final` but coexist due to different parameter types тАФ this is **overloading, not overriding**. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) тАФ Module Internal Choice

---

### ЁЯУШ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the three uses of the `final` keyword in Java with suitable examples. How does it affect inheritance? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The `final` keyword in Java is a non-access modifier that imposes restrictions to **enforce design contracts and ensure security**. It has three primary applications:

**(1) Final Class тАФ Blocks Inheritance (3 marks)**

When a class is declared `final`, it cannot be subclassed. This terminates the inheritance chain.

```java
final class ImmutablePoint {
    private final int x, y;
    ImmutablePoint(int x, int y) { this.x = x; this.y = y; }
    int getX() { return x; }
    int getY() { return y; }
}

// class ExtendedPoint extends ImmutablePoint { } 
// тЭМ Compile error: cannot inherit from final class
```

Java's built-in `String` class is `final` to prevent malicious subclassing that could break string immutability.

**(2) Final Method тАФ Blocks Overriding (2 marks)**

```java
class Account {
    final void auditLog(String msg) {
        System.out.println("AUDIT: " + msg);
    }
}
class SavingsAccount extends Account {
    // void auditLog(String msg) { } тЭМ Compile error
}
```

The subclass inherits the method but **cannot redefine it**, preserving parent behavior.

**(3) Final Variable тАФ Creates Constants (2 marks)**

```java
class Config {
    public static final int MAX_USERS = 100;
    final String DB_NAME;
    Config() { DB_NAME = "ProductionDB"; }
}
```

`final` variables act as **read-only constants** that can be assigned only once. The `public static final` combination is the standard Java idiom for true constants.

**Effect on Inheritance:** `final` ensures that **modification through inheritance is prohibited**, guaranteeing that the parent class's contract remains intact for all descendants. This is critical for **security, design integrity, and predictable behavior**.

---

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate that a `final` reference variable cannot be reassigned, but the object it points to can be modified. Explain the output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;
class FinalReferenceDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        final ArrayList<String> colors = new ArrayList<>();
        
        // Modifying object state - ALLOWED
        colors.add("Red");
        colors.add("Green");
        colors.add("Blue");
        
        System.out.println("After additions: " + colors);
        
        // Removing element - ALLOWED
        colors.remove("Green");
        System.out.println("After removal: " + colors);
        
        // Reassigning reference - NOT ALLOWED
        // colors = new ArrayList<>();  
        // Compile error: cannot assign a value to final variable colors
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
After additions: [Red, Green, Blue]
After removal: [Red, Blue]
```

**Explanation (7 marks breakdown):**

- **(2 marks)** The `final` keyword on a reference variable makes the **reference (memory address) constant**, not the object itself. The variable `colors` permanently points to the same `ArrayList` object on the heap.
- **(2 marks)** Methods like `add()` and `remove()` mutate the **internal state** of the object тАФ this does not change the reference, so it is permitted.
- **(2 marks)** Attempting `colors = new ArrayList<>()` would reassign the reference to a new object, which violates the `final` contract тЖТ compile-time error.
- **(1 mark)** This distinction is fundamental in Java: **`final` provides reference immutability, not object immutability**. For true object immutability, all fields must be `final` and `private` with no mutator methods (like the `String` class).

---

### ЁЯУЧ **Question B (14 Marks)** тАФ Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss why a method cannot be declared both `abstract` and `final` simultaneously. Provide the logical justification and a code example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Logical Justification (5 marks):**

The combination of `abstract` and `final` produces a **logical contradiction** in Java's type system:

- An **abstract method** is declared **without a body** and **must be overridden** by the first concrete (non-abstract) subclass to provide an implementation. Without overriding, the program cannot compile.

- A **final method** is declared with a body and **cannot be overridden** by any subclass. The implementation is permanently fixed in the declaring class.

These two requirements are **mutually exclusive**: one demands overriding, the other forbids it. Allowing both would create an **unresolvable logical paradox** тАФ the method can neither exist without being overridden nor be overridden. The Java compiler therefore rejects this combination.

Formally:
$$
\text{abstract} \Rightarrow \text{override required}, \quad \text{final} \Rightarrow \text{override forbidden}
$$

$$
\therefore \; \text{abstract} \land \text{final} \Rightarrow \bot \text{ (contradiction)}
$$

**Code Example (2 marks):**

```java
abstract class Shape {
    // abstract final void draw(); 
    // тЭМ Compile error: illegal combination of modifiers: abstract and final
    abstract void draw();
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing Circle");
    }
}
```

Here, `draw()` is correctly declared as `abstract` (allowing override) but **not** `final`. The `Circle` subclass successfully overrides it.

---

**(b)** Explain with a Java program how `final` classes prevent inheritance, using a real-world scenario. Why does Java make `String` class `final`? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Program Demonstrating `final` Class (4 marks):**

```java
final class License {
    private final String licenseNumber;
    private final String holderName;
    
    License(String num, String name) {
        this.licenseNumber = num;
        this.holderName = name;
    }
    
    void display() {
        System.out.println("License: " + licenseNumber + ", Holder: " + holderName);
    }
}

// Attempting inheritance - NOT ALLOWED
// class FakeLicense extends License { }
// Compile error: cannot inherit from final class License

public class LicenseTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        License l = new License("KL-07-2024-001", "Rahul Krishnan");
        l.display();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
License: KL-07-2024-001, Holder: Rahul Krishnan
```

**Why `String` is `final` (3 marks):**

Java's `String` class is declared `final` for **three critical engineering reasons**:

1. **Security:** Strings are used for sensitive operations like class loading, network connections, database queries, and file paths. If `String` could be subclassed, a malicious subclass could corrupt the behavior of the entire JVM. Making it `final` prevents such attacks.

2. **Immutability Guarantee:** Strings are immutable in Java. If `String` were inheritable, subclasses could introduce mutability, breaking the immutability contract that the entire Java ecosystem relies upon (e.g., `String` as keys in `HashMap`).

3. **Performance (String Pool):** The JVM uses a **String Constant Pool** for memory optimization. Allowing subclasses would break the pool's invariants and cause unpredictable behavior.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `final` with `finally` or `finalize()`** тАФ Examiners specifically test this distinction. Writing "finally" in a "final" question will cost **at least 1 mark**.
>
> 2. **Assuming `final` makes objects immutable** тАФ Many students write "`final` makes the object immutable" in viva/exams. This is **WRONG**. `final` on a reference locks the **reference**, not the **object's internal state**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting blank `final` must be initialized in EVERY constructor** тАФ A blank `final` instance variable must be assigned in **all** constructors of the class; otherwise, compile error.
>
> 4. **Writing `final abstract` together** тАФ A frequent MCQ trap. Always remember: `abstract` and `final` are **mutually exclusive**.
>
> 5. **Saying constructors can be `final`** тАФ Constructors are never inherited, so they cannot be `final`. This is a common conceptual error.
>
> 6. **Forgetting that `final` methods CAN be overloaded** тАФ Overloading тЙа Overriding. This is a frequently tested nuance.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ЁЯУЛ **Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ Final Keyword with Inheritance**

- тЬЕ `final` is a **non-access modifier** used to impose restrictions.

- тЬЕ `final` can be applied to **three things**: classes, methods, and variables.

- тЬЕ **`final` class** тЖТ Inheritance is **blocked** (cannot use `extends`). Examples: `String`, `Math`, `System`, `Wrapper` classes.

- тЬЕ **`final` method** тЖТ Overriding is **blocked** in subclasses, but **overloading is allowed**.

- тЬЕ **`final` variable** тЖТ Acts as a **constant**, assigned only once. Use `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE` naming.

- тЬЕ **Blank `final` variables** тЖТ Must be initialized in the **constructor** (or in an instance initializer block).

- тЬЕ **`final` reference** тЖТ Reference is locked, but **object state remains mutable** (reference immutability тЙа object immutability).

- тЬЕ **`abstract` + `final`** тЖТ **Illegal combination** due to logical contradiction (override required vs forbidden).

- тЬЕ **Constructors cannot be `final`** (constructors are never inherited, so the concept doesn't apply).

- тЬЕ **Interfaces** тЖТ All variables in an interface are implicitly `public static final`.

- тЬЕ **Performance benefit** тЖТ `final` methods can be inlined by the JIT compiler (minor optimization).

- тЬЕ **Design intent** тЖТ Use `final` to communicate that a class/method/field is **complete and not meant for extension**.

- тЬЕ **Difference summary**:
  - `final` = modifier (this module)
  - `finally` = exception block (Module 4/5)
  - `finalize()` = GC method (Module 4/5)

---

**End of Notes тАФ Module 2: Using `final` with Inheritance** ЁЯОп
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

Swings Fundamentals - Overview of AWT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swings Fundamentals - Overview of AWT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of AWT

The **Abstract Window Toolkit (AWT)** is Java's original, platform-dependent GUI (Graphical User Interface) framework, packaged under `java.awt`. It provides a foundational set of classes and interfaces for building window-based desktop applications, abstracting OS-level windowing operations into a unified Java API.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> AWT is a collection of GUI components (buttons, text fields, labels, etc.) and infrastructure (containers, layout managers, event handlers) that the JVM maps to the **native peer components** of the host operating system. This makes AWT components **heavyweight** and visually platform-bound.

### Key Terminology (High-Yield for KTU)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Heavyweight Component** | A component that has a native OS counterpart (peer). Uses OS resources directly. |
| **Peer-Based Architecture** | Each AWT component delegates rendering to a native OS widget. |
| **Platform-Dependent Look-and-Feel** | A button on Windows looks like a Windows button; on Linux, like a Linux button. |
| **Lightweight Component** | A component with **no native peer**; drawn entirely by Java (e.g., all Swing components). |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ The "Standardized Shipping Container" Model

Imagine you are an international architect who needs to build a small house in **three different countries** (Windows, macOS, Linux):

- You are given a **single, standardized blueprint** (the Java AWT API).
- The construction company in each country uses its **own local bricks, wood, and paint** (native OS widgets), but follows your blueprint.
- The end result: the house **functions identically**, but the *appearance* depends on local materials.

> **AWT behaves exactly this way.** Your Java code is the same, but the rendered output adapts to the host OS. This is why AWT components are sometimes called **"thin Java wrappers over thick native code."**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why learn AWT if Swing exists?** Swing is built **on top of** AWT. You cannot use Swing without understanding AWT's `Component`, `Container`, and event model. KTU Module 4 begins with AWT to build this foundational literacy before transitioning to Swing's lightweight architecture.

---

## 1.3 Why AWT Matters in the Swing Fundamentals Module

The KTU 2024 Scheme introduces **Swing Fundamentals** through this layered progression:

$$ \text{AWT (Foundation)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{AWT Event Model} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Swing (Lightweight Extension)} $$

Mastering AWT is the **prerequisite** for understanding *why* Swing was created (to solve AWT's platform-dependency and "look-and-feel" inconsistency).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 AWT Class Hierarchy тАФ The Architectural Blueprint

The `java.awt` package organizes all GUI elements in a strict inheritance tree, rooted at the abstract class **`java.awt.Component`**.

| Level | Class | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Root (abstract) | `Component` | Base class for *everything* that can be displayed on screen. |
| Concrete extension | `Container` | A `Component` that can *hold* other components. |
| Special container | `Window` | A top-level window with no borders or menu bar. |
| Special container | `Frame` | A top-level window **with** title bar, borders, and menu bar. |
| Special container | `Dialog` | A pop-up window (e.g., for messages). |
| Leaf components | `Button`, `Label`, `TextField`, `Choice`, `List`, `Checkbox` | Non-container interactive/display widgets. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Hot Spot:** Students often confuse `Window` and `Frame`. Remember: **`Frame` is a `Window` + title bar**. You will rarely instantiate `Window` directly in exam code.

---

## 2.2 Components vs Containers тАФ The "Boxes and Items" Model

The AWT divides all visual elements into two categories:

1. **Component** (the "item"): Anything visible тАФ a button, a label, a checkbox.
2. **Container** (the "box"): A special component that *holds* other components. It defines the layout rules.

> **Analogy:** A `Container` is a *room*. A `Button` is a *chair inside that room*. The `LayoutManager` is the *interior designer* deciding where the chair goes.

$$ \text{Container} \subseteq \text{Component} \quad \text{(every container IS-A component)} $$

---

## 2.3 Layout Managers тАФ The Positioning Engine

AWT does **not** allow absolute pixel-based positioning by default. Instead, it delegates placement to a **`LayoutManager`** object. This makes GUI code **resolution-independent** and **locale-resilient**.

### KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet тАФ AWT Layout Managers

| Layout Manager | Behavior | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|
| **`FlowLayout`** | Adds components left-to-right, wrapping to next line when full. | Toolbars, button rows, status bars. |
| **`BorderLayout`** | Divides container into **5 zones**: North, South, East, West, Center. | Main app window (title, menu, content, status). |
| **`GridLayout`** | Equally-sized cells in a grid (rows x cols). | Calculator buttons, keypads. |
| **`GridBagLayout`** | Irregular, customizable grid with cells spanning multiple rows/cols. | Complex forms (most powerful, most complex). |
| **`CardLayout`** | Stacks components like cards; only one visible at a time. | Wizards, tabbed views. |
| **`null` (no manager)** | Manual `setBounds(x,y,w,h)` тАФ absolute positioning. | Quick prototypes only (not recommended). |

---

## 2.4 Event Delegation Model тАФ The "Reporting Chain"

AWT components are **passive**. They don't process their own clicks or key presses. Instead, they **delegate** events to registered **listeners**. This is formally called the **Event Delegation Model** (introduced in JDK 1.1, replacing the older JDK 1.0 inheritance-based model).

$$ \text{Source} \xrightarrow{\text{generates}} \text{Event Object} \xrightarrow{\text{passed to}} \text{Listener} \xrightarrow{\text{calls}} \text{Handler Method} $$

### The Four Pillars of the Event Model

1. **Event Source**: The component that fires the event (e.g., a `Button`).
2. **Event Object**: A subclass of `java.awt.AWTEvent` (e.g., `ActionEvent`).
3. **Event Listener Interface**: A contract the handler must implement (e.g., `ActionListener`).
4. **Event Handler Method**: The method called when the event occurs (e.g., `actionPerformed`).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | AWT/Swing Usage |
|---|---|
| Enterprise desktop tools | IDEs (Eclipse/Swing), NetBeans |
| Educational software | Applets (historical), simulation GUIs |
| Embedded HMI panels | Kiosk apps, industrial control panels |
| Banking/CRM legacy systems | Maintained AWT/Swing front-ends |

> [!NOTE]
> While modern Java favors **JavaFX** and **web-based front-ends**, KTU evaluates AWT/Swing because they teach the **foundational OOP GUI patterns** that transfer to *any* UI framework (event listeners, MVC separation, layout management).

---

## 2.6 AWT vs Swing тАФ The Comparison Table KTU Loves to Ask

| Feature | AWT | Swing |
|---|---|---|
| Package | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |
| Component Weight | **Heavyweight** (native peers) | **Lightweight** (pure Java drawing) |
| Look-and-Feel | Platform-dependent | Pluggable (Metal, Nimbus, System) |
| Component Class Root | `java.awt.Component` | `javax.swing.JComponent` (extends `Container` which extends `Component`) |
| Speed | Faster for simple UIs | Slightly slower (Java-rendered) |
| MVC Support | Minimal | Strong (e.g., `JTable`, `JTree`) |
| Top-level Container | `Frame` | `JFrame` |
| MDI Support | No | Yes (`JDesktopPane`, `JInternalFrame`) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix тАФ Building a Standard AWT Window

The construction of any AWT GUI follows a **five-step derivation**. Memorize this sequence тАФ it appears in **every** KTU practical and theory question.

| Step | Action | Code |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Import** | Bring AWT classes into scope. | `import java.awt.*;` |
| **2. Extend** | Choose a top-level container (usually `Frame`). | `class MyApp extends Frame` |
| **3. Components** | Instantiate and configure widgets. | `Button b = new Button("OK");` |
| **4. Layout** | Attach a `LayoutManager` and add components. | `setLayout(new FlowLayout()); add(b);` |
| **5. Display** | Set size and visibility. | `setSize(400, 300); setVisible(true);` |

---

## 3.2 Worked Code Example #1 тАФ Minimal AWT Frame

```java
import java.awt.*;

public class AwtDemo1 extends Frame {
    public AwtDemo1() {
        // Step 3: Create components
        Label lbl = new Label("Welcome to AWT");
        Button btn = new Button("Click Me");

        // Step 4: Set layout and add components
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        add(lbl);
        add(btn);

        // Step 5: Display the frame
        setTitle("KTU AWT Demo");
        setSize(300, 150);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new AwtDemo1();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Reasoning

- **Why `extends Frame`?** Inheriting from `Frame` lets us call its methods (`setSize`, `setLayout`, `add`) directly without an intermediate object reference.
- **Why `FlowLayout`?** It is the **default** layout of `Applet` and `Panel`. For a top-level `Frame`, the default is `BorderLayout` тАФ but we override it here for simplicity.
- **Why `setVisible(true)` at the end?** AWT does not render until the component tree is fully assembled. Calling this *before* `add()` can cause display glitches.

---

## 3.3 Worked Code Example #2 тАФ Event Handling with ActionListener

This derivation adds the **Event Delegation Model** to the previous example.

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class AwtEventDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    Label status;

    public AwtEventDemo() {
        status = new Label("Status: Waiting...");
        Button btn = new Button("Submit");

        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        add(btn);
        add(status);

        // Step: Register the listener (this class) with the source (btn)
        btn.addActionListener(this);

        setTitle("AWT Event Demo");
        setSize(300, 150);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    // Event Handler Method (contract from ActionListener)
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        status.setText("Status: Button Clicked at " + e.getWhen());
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new AwtEventDemo();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Reasoning

| Line | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| `implements ActionListener` | Our class promises to provide the `actionPerformed` method. |
| `btn.addActionListener(this);` | **Registration** тАФ the button now knows *who* to call. |
| `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` | The **callback** тАФ automatically invoked by AWT's event dispatch thread. |
| `e.getWhen()` | Returns event timestamp in milliseconds (inherited from `AWTEvent`). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Students often write `addActionListener(new ActionListener() { ... })` (anonymous inner class) in theory papers. Both forms are correct, but the **named class implementation** is preferred for clarity in 14-mark answers.

---

## 3.4 Worked Code Example #3 тАФ BorderLayout Demonstration

```java
import java.awt.*;

public class BorderLayoutDemo extends Frame {
    public BorderLayoutDemo() {
        setLayout(new BorderLayout());

        add(new Button("North"),  BorderLayout.NORTH);
        add(new Button("South"),  BorderLayout.SOUTH);
        add(new Button("East"),   BorderLayout.EAST);
        add(new Button("West"),   BorderLayout.WEST);
        add(new Button("Center"), BorderLayout.CENTER);

        setTitle("BorderLayout Zones");
        setSize(400, 250);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new BorderLayoutDemo();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Reasoning

- **North/South** regions take their **preferred height** and full width.
- **East/West** regions take their **preferred width** and remaining height.
- **Center** region takes **all remaining space** тАФ it is the only region that auto-stretches.
- If a region is empty, its space is absorbed by `CENTER`.

---

## 3.5 Mathematical Representation of Event Probability (Optional Theoretical Insight)

In a multi-component AWT form with $n$ independent event sources, the probability that *no* event fires during a user interaction is:

$$ P(\text{no event}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(1 - p_i\right) $$

where $p_i$ is the probability that source $i$ triggers an event. This is rarely tested directly but reinforces that event handling is a **probabilistic, asynchronous** process тАФ your listeners must be robust to order-independent firing.

---

## 3.6 Key API Methods тАФ Rapid Reference

| Method | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `setSize(int w, int h)` | Sets the component's dimensions in pixels. |
| `setLayout(LayoutManager m)` | Attaches a layout manager. |
| `setTitle(String s)` | Sets the `Frame`'s title bar text. |
| `add(Component c)` | Adds a component to the container. |
| `addActionListener(ActionListener l)` | Registers an event handler. |
| `setBackground(Color c)` | Sets background color (e.g., `Color.RED`). |
| `setVisible(boolean b)` | Shows or hides the component. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AWT Class Hierarchy тАФ Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    objRoot["Object"]
    compRoot["Component"]
    ctnrRoot["Container"]
    wndRoot["Window"]
    frmRoot["Frame"]
    dlgRoot["Dialog"]
    panRoot["Panel"]
    aplRoot["Applet"]
    btnLeaf["Button"]
    lblLeaf["Label"]
    txtLeaf["TextField"]
    chkLeaf["Checkbox"]
    lstLeaf["List"]
    choLeaf["Choice"]

    objRoot --> compRoot
    compRoot --> ctnrRoot
    ctnrRoot --> wndRoot
    ctnrRoot --> panRoot
    wndRoot --> frmRoot
    wndRoot --> dlgRoot
    panRoot --> aplRoot
    compRoot --> btnLeaf
    compRoot --> lblLeaf
    compRoot --> txtLeaf
    compRoot --> chkLeaf
    compRoot --> lstLeaf
    compRoot --> choLeaf
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `Component` is the **abstract root** of all AWT visual classes.
- `Container` is a *subtype* of `Component` (it can be displayed *and* hold others).
- `Frame`, `Dialog`, `Panel`, `Applet` are the **four primary concrete containers** KTU tests.

---

## 4.2 Event Delegation Model тАФ Flow Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    userA[User Action]
    srcA[Event Source: Button]
    evtA[Event Object: ActionEvent]
    regA[Registered Listener]
    hndA[Handler Method: actionPerformed]
    resA[Result: Label Updated]

    userA -->|Click| srcA
    srcA -->|Fires| evtA
    evtA -->|Passed to| regA
    regA -->|Invokes| hndA
    hndA -->|Updates UI| resA
```

**Flow Explanation (for exam writing):**
1. **User** performs an action (mouse click, key press).
2. The **Event Source** detects the action and constructs an **Event Object**.
3. The source iterates through its **registered listeners** and dispatches the event.
4. The **Listener's handler method** executes the response logic.
5. The **UI is updated** (label text changes, dialog appears, etc.).

---

## 4.3 Layout Manager Decision Tree тАФ When to Use What

```mermaid
graph TD
    startLayout[Need a Layout?]
    qSimple[Simple Row of Buttons?]
    qZones[Five Functional Zones?]
    qGrid[Equal-Sized Grid?]
    qWizards[Step-by-Step Wizard?]
    qComplex[Complex Asymmetric Form?]

    flowPick[Use FlowLayout]
    borderPick[Use BorderLayout]
    gridPick[Use GridLayout]
    cardPick[Use CardLayout]
    bagPick[Use GridBagLayout]

    startLayout --> qSimple
    qSimple -->|Yes| flowPick
    qSimple -->|No| qZones
    qZones -->|Yes| borderPick
    qZones -->|No| qGrid
    qGrid -->|Yes| gridPick
    qGrid -->|No| qWizards
    qWizards -->|Yes| cardPick
    qWizards -->|No| qComplex
    qComplex -->|Yes| bagPick
```

**Exam Tip:** If a question says *"design a calculator UI"* тЖТ answer is **`GridLayout`**. If it says *"design a main window with a menu, toolbar, content, and status bar"* тЖТ answer is **`BorderLayout`**.

---

## 4.4 AWT-to-Swing Evolution тАФ Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    osLayer[Host Operating System]
    peerLayer[Native Peer Components]
    awtLayer[java.awt: Heavyweight Foundation]
    swingLayer[javax.swing: Lightweight Extension]
    appLayer[Your Java Application]

    osLayer --> peerLayer
    peerLayer --> awtLayer
    awtLayer -->|inherits from| swingLayer
    swingLayer -->|built on| appLayer
```

**Key Insight for KTU Essays:** Swing's `JComponent` extends `java.awt.Container` (which extends `Component`). So **Swing is a strict superset** of AWT's container model тАФ it adds lightweight painting on top of AWT's existing event and layout infrastructure.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define AWT. Why is it called heavyweight? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit)** is Java's original GUI framework provided in the `java.awt` package for building platform-dependent window-based applications.
>
> AWT components are called **heavyweight** because every AWT component has a corresponding **native peer component** in the host operating system. The JVM allocates OS resources for each peer, making AWT components consume significant memory and follow the host OS's look-and-feel.
>
> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for heavyweight reasoning, 1 mark for peer concept)*

---

### Question 2: List any four AWT layout managers with their default alignment behavior. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Layout Manager | Default Behavior |
|---|---|
| `FlowLayout` | Left-to-right, top-to-bottom, centered horizontally; default 5px gaps. |
| `BorderLayout` | Five zones (North, South, East, West, Center); center takes remaining space. |
| `GridLayout` | Equal-sized rectangular grid; components fill cells in row-major order. |
| `GridBagLayout` | Customizable grid with constraints (anchor, fill, weight, insets). |
| `CardLayout` | Stacks components; only the top "card" is visible at a time. |

*(1 mark per correct pair, up to 3 marks)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each тАФ Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): AWT Component Hierarchy and Event Handling** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **AWT component class hierarchy**. Discuss the role of `Container` and `Component` classes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The AWT class hierarchy is rooted at `java.lang.Object` and branches into `java.awt.Component` (abstract base for all displayable elements). The hierarchy is:

$$\text{Object} \to \text{Component} \to \begin{cases} \text{Container} \to \begin{cases} \text{Window} \to \text{Frame / Dialog} \\ \text{Panel} \to \text{Applet} \end{cases} \\ \text{Button, Label, TextField, Checkbox, List, Choice} \end{cases}$$

- **`Component` class**: Abstract superclass of all AWT visual elements. Provides methods like `setSize`, `setLocation`, `setBackground`, `setForeground`, `repaint`, and `add`. Every class that appears on screen inherits from it.
- **`Container` class**: A special subclass of `Component` that can *contain* other components. Provides `add()`, `remove()`, `removeAll()`, and `getComponents()`. Containers define the visual grouping and delegate positioning to a `LayoutManager`.
- **Leaf components** (e.g., `Button`, `Label`): Extend `Component` directly but not `Container`. They cannot hold children.

*(3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for Component role, 2 marks for Container role)*

**(b)** Explain the **Event Delegation Model** in AWT with a suitable Java code example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Event Delegation Model** is a publish-subscribe pattern where the event source delegates event processing to registered listener objects. It has four participants:

1. **Event Source**: The component generating the event (e.g., `Button`).
2. **Event Object**: Encapsulates event data (e.g., `ActionEvent` extends `AWTEvent`).
3. **Listener Interface**: Contract the handler must implement (e.g., `ActionListener`).
4. **Event Handler**: The method that responds (e.g., `actionPerformed`).

**Code Example:**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class EventDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    TextField tf;

    public EventDemo() {
        tf = new TextField(20);
        Button b = new Button("Greet");
        b.addActionListener(this);            // registration
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        add(tf);
        add(b);
        setSize(300, 150);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {  // handler
        tf.setText("Hello from AWT!");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new EventDemo();
    }
}
```

**Flow:**
- User clicks `Button` тЖТ JVM creates `ActionEvent` тЖТ JVM passes it to all registered `ActionListener`s тЖТ `actionPerformed` is invoked тЖТ `TextField` text changes.

*(2 marks for four participants, 3 marks for code, 2 marks for flow explanation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Layout Managers тАФ A Comparative Analysis** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare **`FlowLayout`**, **`BorderLayout`**, and **`GridLayout`** with diagrams showing component placement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | FlowLayout | BorderLayout | GridLayout |
|---|---|---|---|
| Arrangement | Sequential, wraps on overflow | Five named zones | Equal-sized cells in grid |
| Default Container | `Panel`, `Applet` | `Window`, `Frame`, `Dialog` | None (must set explicitly) |
| Region Count | Dynamic | Fixed (5) | Defined by rows ├Ч cols |
| Resize Behavior | Components stay at preferred size | North/South stretch horizontally; East/West vertically; Center fills all remaining space | All cells resize uniformly |
| When to Use | Toolbars, button strips | Main app windows | Calculators, keypads |

**Diagram тАФ BorderLayout zones (most tested):**

```
+-------------------------------+
|           NORTH               |
+----------+----------+----------+
|          |          |          |
|   WEST   |  CENTER  |   EAST   |
|          |          |          |
+----------+----------+----------+
|           SOUTH               |
+-------------------------------+
```

**Diagram тАФ GridLayout 3├Ч2:**

```
+--------+--------+
|  (0,0) |  (0,1) |
+--------+--------+
|  (1,0) |  (1,1) |
+--------+--------+
|  (2,0) |  (2,1) |
+--------+--------+
```

*(2 marks per layout for explanation + diagram тАФ partial credit if diagram is missing)*

**(b)** Write a Java program using AWT that creates a `Frame` with `BorderLayout` containing a label in the **North**, a `TextArea` in the **Center**, and three buttons in the **South**. Implement clicking the "Clear" button to empty the text area. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class ClearDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    TextArea ta;
    Button clearBtn, saveBtn, exitBtn;

    public ClearDemo() {
        setLayout(new BorderLayout());

        // North: label
        add(new Label("Feedback Form", Label.CENTER), BorderLayout.NORTH);

        // Center: text area
        ta = new TextArea(5, 30);
        add(ta, BorderLayout.CENTER);

        // South: panel with 3 buttons
        Panel southPanel = new Panel();
        southPanel.setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        clearBtn = new Button("Clear");
        saveBtn  = new Button("Save");
        exitBtn  = new Button("Exit");
        southPanel.add(clearBtn);
        southPanel.add(saveBtn);
        southPanel.add(exitBtn);
        add(southPanel, BorderLayout.SOUTH);

        // Register listeners
        clearBtn.addActionListener(this);
        saveBtn.addActionListener(this);
        exitBtn.addActionListener(this);

        setTitle("AWT Layout Demo");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String cmd = e.getActionCommand();
        if (cmd.equals("Clear")) {
            ta.setText("");                // clear logic
        } else if (cmd.equals("Exit")) {
            dispose();                      // close window
            System.exit(0);
        } else if (cmd.equals("Save")) {
            // (save logic omitted for brevity)
            ta.setText("Saved successfully!");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new ClearDemo();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Key Steps:**
- Nested `Panel` is used in the South region because `BorderLayout` allows only **one component per zone**. To hold three buttons, we embed a `Panel` with its own `FlowLayout`.
- `e.getActionCommand()` returns the button's label тАФ a clean way to identify which button fired.
- `dispose()` releases the AWT resources of the frame; `System.exit(0)` terminates the JVM.

*(2 marks for layout structure, 2 marks for component placement, 2 marks for event logic, 1 mark for explanation)*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in AWT/Swing Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting `setVisible(true)`** тАФ the GUI will not appear. (Loss: up to 1 mark per question.)
> 2. **Confusing `Window` and `Frame`** тАФ `Window` has no title bar; `Frame` does. For top-level apps, always use `Frame` or `JFrame`.
> 3. **Not implementing the full listener interface** тАФ declaring `implements ActionListener` but missing `actionPerformed` causes a compilation error. (Loss: 2 marks.)
> 4. **Adding multiple components to a `BorderLayout` zone** тАФ only the **last added** is visible. Use a `Panel` to group components within a zone. (Loss: 1тАУ2 marks.)
> 5. **Mixing AWT and Swing carelessly** тАФ adding an AWT `Button` and a Swing `JButton` in the same container causes "heavyweight/lightweight mixing" runtime warnings. KTU may deduct 1 mark for unsafe mixing.
> 6. **Calling `setSize` on a `Frame` after `pack()`** тАФ `pack()` computes size from layout; subsequent `setSize` is ignored. Know the order of operations.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **AWT** = Abstract Window Toolkit, in `java.awt`, **heavyweight**, platform-dependent.
- [x] **Component** = root abstract class for all visual AWT elements.
- [x] **Container** = a `Component` that can hold other components.
- [x] **Top-level containers** = `Frame`, `Dialog`, `Window`, `Applet`.
- [x] **Default layout of `Frame`** = `BorderLayout`. Default of `Panel` = `FlowLayout`.
- [x] **`BorderLayout` has 5 zones**: North, South, East, West, **Center** (absorbs leftover space).
- [x] **`GridLayout`** = equal-sized cells; **`FlowLayout`** = sequential wrap; **`CardLayout`** = stacked visibility.
- [x] **Event Delegation Model** = Source тЖТ Event тЖТ Listener тЖТ Handler.
- [x] **Mandatory pattern**: `implements ActionListener` + `addActionListener(this)` + override `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`.
- [x] **`Frame` methods** you'll use daily: `setTitle`, `setSize`, `setLayout`, `setVisible`, `add`, `setBackground`.
- [x] **Swing vs AWT**: Swing is in `javax.swing`, **lightweight**, pluggable look-and-feel, prefixed with `J` (e.g., `JFrame`, `JButton`).
- [x] **AWT peer's existence** is what makes components "heavyweight" тАФ every AWT widget has a native OS counterpart consuming OS resources.
- [x] **Window closing event** in AWT is **not** automatic тАФ you must add a `WindowListener` with `windowClosing` calling `System.exit(0)`. *(In Swing, `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` handles it.)*
- [x] **Color constants**: `Color.RED`, `Color.BLUE`, `Color.GREEN`, `Color.WHITE`, `Color.BLACK` тАФ note the **U.S. spelling** (`Color`, not `Colour`).
- [x] **Layout manager rule**: If you set `setLayout(null)`, you **must** call `setBounds(x, y, w, h)` on every child. This is a common KTU bonus-mark technique for absolute positioning questions.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes тАФ Swings Fundamentals: Overview of AWT**
> *Prepared in alignment with the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education framework.*

topics.title: Swing vs AWT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swing vs AWT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Java GUI (Graphical User Interface) programming under KTU's **PBCST304 тАУ Object Oriented Programming** syllabus, the two foundational widget toolkit frameworks are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit):** The original, platform-dependent GUI component library of Java, introduced in **JDK 1.0 (1996)**. It resides in the `java.awt` package and uses **heavyweight components** that rely on the underlying operating system's native peer components for rendering and event handling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Swing:** A more advanced, **lightweight**, and **platform-independent** GUI component library, introduced as part of the **Java Foundation Classes (JFC)** in **JDK 1.2 (1998)**. It resides in the `javax.swing` package and provides a **pluggable look-and-feel (PLAF)** architecture that allows components to render consistently across all operating systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are ordering furniture for a new house:

- **AWT** is like buying **directly from a local carpenter** in your town. The furniture (button, text field) is built using the local wood and tools available in your OS. It looks and behaves differently depending on which country you are in (Windows button looks like a Windows button, Mac button looks like a Mac button). It is "heavy" because it carries the weight of the native OS underneath.

- **Swing** is like buying **flat-pack furniture from a global brand like IKEA**. The furniture is pre-designed and self-contained, made of the same material regardless of where you assemble it. It comes with a uniform appearance across all platforms but also allows you to choose a "theme" (pluggable look-and-feel). It is "light" because it draws itself using Java's own paint system, not relying on the host OS.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Exam:** Swing was built *on top of* AWT. AWT provides the underlying windowing infrastructure (top-level containers like `Frame`, `Window`, `Dialog`), while Swing provides the rich set of UI components that sit inside these AWT containers.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Classification Summary

| Parameter | AWT | Swing |
|---|---|---|
| **Package** | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |
| **Component Weight** | **Heavyweight** | **Lightweight** |
| **Platform Dependency** | **High** (OS-dependent peer) | **Low** (PLAF-based) |
| **Component Prefix** | `Button`, `TextField` | `JButton`, `JTextField` |
| **MVC Support** | No native MVC | Yes тАФ **Model-View-Controller** based |
| **JDK Introduction** | **JDK 1.0** | **JDK 1.2 (JFC)** |
| **Pluggable Look & Feel** | Not Supported | **Fully Supported** |
| **Default Layout Manager** | `BorderLayout` (for `Frame`) | Various (commonly `BorderLayout`) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Analysis тАФ Why and How

#### **Why AWT is Heavyweight**
- Every AWT component (e.g., `Button`, `Checkbox`, `TextField`) has a corresponding **native peer object** created by the JVM using OS-native code (via JNI тАФ Java Native Interface).
- The peer handles rendering and event dispatching at the OS level.
- **Consequence:** The component's appearance, size, and behavior are dictated by the host platform, leading to the "write once, debug everywhere" problem.

#### **Why Swing is Lightweight**
- Swing components **inherit from `java.awt.Container`** but do not have native peers.
- They render themselves by overriding the `paintComponent(Graphics g)` method, calling Java 2D API drawing primitives.
- **Consequence:** Pure Java rendering guarantees uniform appearance, and the developer can override painting for custom UI behavior.

#### **Pluggable Look-and-Feel (PLAF)**
- Swing's most celebrated feature. Implemented via the `LookAndFeel` class hierarchy rooted at `javax.swing.LookAndFeel`.
- Three standard PLAFs:
  1. **Metal** (default cross-platform L&F, also called *Ocean*)
  2. **Windows** L&F (mimics Windows native appearance)
  3. **Motif** L&F (mimics Unix/Linux)
- Set programmatically via:
```java
UIManager.setLookAndFeel("javax.swing.plaf.metal.MetalLookAndFeel");
```

#### **MVC Architecture in Swing**
- Each Swing component (e.g., `JButton`, `JSlider`) separates:
  - **Model** тАФ stores data/state (e.g., `ButtonModel`)
  - **View** тАФ renders the component (`BasicButtonUI`)
  - **Controller** тАФ handles user input (`ButtonUI` listeners)
- This separation allows developers to plug in custom models or renderers without altering the component's behavior.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Comparison Parameter | AWT | Swing |
|---|---|---|
| **Inheritance Root** | `java.awt.Component` | `javax.swing.JComponent` (extends `java.awt.Container`) |
| **Peer Component** | Yes (Native OS Peer) | No (Pure Java Rendering) |
| **Top-Level Containers** | `Frame`, `Dialog`, `Window` | `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow` (inherit AWT counterparts) |
| **Intermediate Containers** | `Panel`, `ScrollPane` | `JPanel`, `JScrollPane` |
| **Basic Controls** | `Button`, `Label`, `TextField`, `Checkbox` | `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, `JCheckBox` |
| **Advanced Components** | Limited | `JTable`, `JTree`, `JTabbedPane`, `JSlider`, `JMenuBar` |
| **Event Handling** | Event Delegation Model (since JDK 1.1) | Inherits AWT's Event Delegation Model |
| **Layout Managers** | `FlowLayout`, `BorderLayout`, `GridLayout`, `CardLayout`, `GridBagLayout` | All AWT layouts + `BoxLayout`, `GroupLayout`, `SpringLayout` |
| **Double Buffering** | Manual (developer must implement) | **Built-in** (default for all Swing components) |
| **Painting API** | `update()` and `paint()` | `paintComponent()`, `paintBorder()`, `paintChildren()` |
| **Thread Safety** | Not thread-safe; uses `Event Dispatch Thread` only | Not thread-safe; mandates `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` for thread safety |
| **Icon Support** | Limited | Full `ImageIcon` and `Icon` interface support |
| **Tooltips** | Not native | Native `setToolTipText(String)` |
| **Keyboard Mnemonics** | Limited | Full support (e.g., `setMnemonic(KeyEvent.VK_S)`) |
| **Applet Support** | `java.applet.Applet` | `javax.swing.JApplet` (deprecated since JDK 9) |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AWT** is rarely used in modern enterprise applications. However, understanding AWT is **mandatory for KTU exams** because Swing's top-level containers and the event-dispatch mechanism are inherited directly from AWT.
- **Swing** powers numerous legacy desktop applications, IDEs (e.g., older versions of IntelliJ IDEA, NetBeans), and is the foundation upon which **JavaFX** (the modern replacement) was conceptualized.
- In real-world **B.Tech project work** (KTU main project), Swing remains a viable choice for quick GUI prototypes when the constraint is to remain within pure Java without external dependencies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix тАФ AWT vs Swing Decision Workflow

Since this is a comparison topic rather than a mathematical derivation, the following **analytical matrix** replaces the standard algebraic derivation. Each step represents a **decision-layer** in choosing or analyzing the correct GUI framework.

---

### **Step 1 тАФ Identify the Platform Dependency Requirement**

- If the application **must mimic the host OS's native look exactly**, AWT is the closer match.
- If the application **must look identical across platforms**, Swing is the correct choice.

**Inference:** Cross-platform consistency тЖТ **Swing wins**.

---

### **Step 2 тАФ Evaluate the Component Sophistication Need**

- For **basic UI** (single button + text field + label), AWT is sufficient.
- For **rich UI** (tables, trees, tabbed panes, sliders, menus with icons), Swing is mandatory тАФ AWT has **no equivalent** for these advanced widgets.

**Inference:** Advanced component requirement тЖТ **Swing wins**.

---

### **Step 3 тАФ Assess the Painting Customization Need**

- AWT: The developer must override `paint(Graphics g)` and handle the full painting cycle, including clearing the background.
- Swing: The developer overrides **only** `paintComponent(Graphics g)`, and Swing automatically handles border painting, child painting, and double-buffering.

**Inference:** Custom rendering with cleaner code тЖТ **Swing wins**.

---

### **Step 4 тАФ Determine the Pluggable Look-and-Feel Requirement**

- AWT: L&F is fixed by the OS тАФ no developer control.
- Swing: L&F is fully swappable at runtime via `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(...)`.

**Inference:** Runtime theme switching тЖТ **Swing wins**.

---

### **Step 5 тАФ Analyze Memory & Performance Footprint**

- AWT components carry the overhead of native peer objects, which consume both Java heap memory and native OS memory.
- Swing components are pure Java objects тАФ smaller memory footprint, but slightly more CPU due to Java-based rendering.

**Inference:** For low-memory embedded systems тЖТ **AWT has marginally lower native overhead**, but Swing's pure-Java footprint is more predictable.

---

### **3.1 Consolidated Analytical Decision Matrix**

$$
\text{Decision Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot S_i
$$

Where:
- $w_i$ = weight assigned to each evaluation criterion (sum of weights = 1)
- $S_i$ = score for AWT or Swing on criterion $i$ (0 = unsuitable, 1 = suitable)

| Criterion $i$ | Weight $w_i$ | AWT Score $S_i$ | Swing Score $S_i$ | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Platform Independence | 0.30 | 0 | 1 | **Swing** |
| Advanced Components | 0.25 | 0 | 1 | **Swing** |
| Custom Painting | 0.15 | 0.3 | 1 | **Swing** |
| Pluggable L&F | 0.15 | 0 | 1 | **Swing** |
| Native Performance | 0.15 | 0.7 | 0.5 | **AWT** |
| **Total** | **1.00** | **0.105** | **0.925** | **Swing (0.925)** |

**Conclusion:** For 92.5% of modern Java GUI requirements under KTU's B.Tech curriculum scope, **Swing is the correct choice**.

---

### **3.2 Sample Java Code Demonstrating the Difference**

**AWT Example:**
```java
import java.awt.*;

public class AWTExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Frame f = new Frame("AWT Window");
        Button b = new Button("Click Me");
        b.setBounds(50, 80, 80, 30);
        f.add(b);
        f.setSize(300, 200);
        f.setLayout(null);
        f.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

**Swing Equivalent:**
```java
import javax.swing.*;

public class SwingExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        JFrame f = new JFrame("Swing Window");
        JButton b = new JButton("Click Me");
        b.setBounds(50, 80, 100, 30);
        f.add(b);
        f.setSize(300, 200);
        f.setLayout(null);
        f.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        f.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

**Critical Observation:** The Swing version adds `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` because Swing does not terminate the JVM when the window closes by default тАФ this is a frequent **valuation pitfall** in KTU lab exams.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]**
*Define AWT. Why are AWT components called "heavyweight"?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit)** is the original Java GUI framework provided in the `java.awt` package, introduced in **JDK 1.0 (1 Mark)**.
- AWT components are called **"heavyweight"** because each AWT component has a corresponding **native peer object** created by the JVM in the underlying operating system **(1 Mark)**.
- This peer object handles rendering and event processing, making the component depend on the host platform's native GUI resources, hence "heavy" **(1 Mark)**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024]**
*What is Pluggable Look-and-Feel (PLAF) in Swing? Name any two PLAFs.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **PLAF** is a Swing feature that allows the developer to change the appearance of all Swing components at runtime without modifying the application code **(2 Marks)**.
- Two standard PLAFs: **Metal** (default cross-platform) and **Windows L&F** (mimics Windows native appearance) **(1 Mark)**.

---

### **Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) тАФ Module Internal Choice Pattern**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) тАФ [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) Explain the key differences between AWT and Swing in detail. Compare their features with respect to platform dependency, component weight, and MVC support. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The key differences between AWT and Swing can be enumerated as follows:

1. **Platform Dependency:**
   - AWT components are **platform-dependent** because they rely on native OS peer components for rendering. A `Button` in AWT looks different on Windows, Mac, and Linux.
   - Swing components are **platform-independent**. They render themselves using Java 2D API, ensuring uniform appearance across all operating systems. *(2 Marks)*

2. **Component Weight:**
   - AWT components are **heavyweight** тАФ each has a native peer consuming both Java heap and native OS memory.
   - Swing components are **lightweight** тАФ they have no peer and are pure Java objects, inheriting from `javax.swing.JComponent`. *(2 Marks)*

3. **MVC Support:**
   - AWT does not follow the **Model-View-Controller** pattern natively.
   - Swing components are designed using **MVC architecture** тАФ e.g., `JButton` uses `ButtonModel` (Model), `BasicButtonUI` (View), and event listeners (Controller). This separation enables flexible customization. *(2 Marks)*

4. **Pluggable Look-and-Feel:**
   - AWT does not support PLAF.
   - Swing supports PLAF via `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(...)`, with options like Metal, Windows, Motif, and Nimbus. *(1 Mark)*

**(b) Describe the Swing component hierarchy with a neat diagram. List any six advanced Swing components and their use. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Swing Hierarchy (ASCII Diagram for Exam):**
```
java.lang.Object
   тФФтФАтФА java.awt.Component
         тФФтФАтФА java.awt.Container
               тФЬтФАтФА java.awt.Window
               тФВ     тФФтФАтФА java.awt.Frame
               тФВ           тФФтФАтФА javax.swing.JFrame
               тФЬтФАтФА javax.swing.JComponent  тЖР Root of all Swing components
               тФВ     тФЬтФАтФА JButton
               тФВ     тФЬтФАтФА JLabel
               тФВ     тФЬтФАтФА JTextField
               тФВ     тФЬтФАтФА JTable
               тФВ     тФЬтФАтФА JTree
               тФВ     тФФтФАтФА ... (other Swing components)
               тФФтФАтФА java.awt.Panel
                     тФФтФАтФА javax.swing.JPanel
```
*(3 Marks)*

**Six Advanced Swing Components:**

| Component | Use |
|---|---|
| `JTable` | Displays data in a tabular (rows ├Ч columns) format |
| `JTree` | Displays hierarchical data (e.g., file directory) |
| `JTabbedPane` | Provides tabbed interface within a single window |
| `JSlider` | Allows user to select a value from a bounded range |
| `JMenuBar` | Implements pull-down menu bar at the top of a window |
| `JOptionPane` | Displays standard dialog boxes (information, warning, input) |

*(4 Marks тАФ 0.5 for each component + 1 for the table presentation)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024 Model]**

**(a) With suitable examples, explain the concept of Heavyweight vs Lightweight components in Java. Why does Swing prefer lightweight components? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Heavyweight Components (AWT):**
- A heavyweight component is one that is **associated with a native OS peer** (a counterpart in the host operating system).
- **Example:** `java.awt.Button` тАФ when created, the JVM allocates a native button in the host OS via JNI.
- The peer handles rendering, event dispatch, and layout, making the component's behavior OS-dependent. *(2 Marks)*

**Lightweight Components (Swing):**
- A lightweight component has **no native peer** тАФ it borrows screen space and event handling from its nearest heavyweight ancestor (typically the `JFrame`).
- **Example:** `javax.swing.JButton` тАФ paints itself using the `paintComponent(Graphics g)` method and Java 2D API.
- It shares the native window of its parent, consuming less memory and providing platform independence. *(2 Marks)*

**Why Swing Prefers Lightweight Components:**
1. **Platform Independence:** Pure Java rendering ensures consistent look across all OS. *(1 Mark)*
2. **Pluggable Look-and-Feel:** Lightweight components can adopt any PLAF at runtime. *(0.5 Mark)*
3. **Customization:** Developers can override `paintComponent()` to render components in any custom way. *(0.5 Mark)*
4. **Memory Efficiency:** No dual memory allocation (Java + native peer). *(0.5 Mark)*
5. **Rich Component Set:** Enables advanced widgets like `JTable`, `JTree`, `JTabbedPane` that AWT cannot provide. *(0.5 Mark)*

**(b) Write a Java program using Swing to create a window with a JLabel, a JTextField, and a JButton. When the button is clicked, display the text from the JTextField in the JLabel. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class SwingDemo extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JLabel label;
    JTextField textField;
    JButton button;

    public SwingDemo() {
        setTitle("Swing Demo");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        label = new JLabel("Enter text and click button:");
        textField = new JTextField(20);
        button = new JButton("Show Text");

        add(label);
        add(textField);
        add(button);

        button.addActionListener(this);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String text = textField.getText();
        label.setText("You entered: " + text);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new SwingDemo();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Key Elements (2 Marks):**
- `JFrame` is the top-level Swing container inheriting from AWT's `Frame`.
- `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` ensures the JVM terminates when the window closes тАФ **a mandatory line often forgotten by students**.
- The `ActionListener` interface (from `java.awt.event`) is used to handle button clicks, demonstrating that Swing uses AWT's event delegation model.
- `FlowLayout` arranges components left-to-right.

**Expected Output:** Initially the label reads "Enter text and click button:". After typing text and clicking the button, the label updates to "You entered: <text>".

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting `setDefaultCloseOperation`:** Swing's `JFrame` does **not** terminate the JVM on close by default. If a student writes only `JFrame` code without this line, they lose **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Confusing `paint()` with `paintComponent()`:** For custom Swing rendering, students must override `paintComponent(Graphics g)` and call `super.paintComponent(g)` as the first line. Overriding `paint()` directly is a Swing anti-pattern and loses marks.
> 3. **Mixing heavyweight and lightweight components:** Placing an AWT `Button` inside a Swing `JFrame` causes z-order and clipping bugs. This is a frequently tested conceptual trap тАФ **never mix AWT and Swing components in the same container hierarchy**.
> 4. **Missing `J` prefix:** AWT `Button` тЙа Swing `JButton`. Using `Button` instead of `JButton` inside a `JFrame` is an automatic deduction.
> 5. **Not invoking the Event Dispatch Thread:** All Swing UI updates should occur on the EDT. Wrapping `new SwingDemo()` inside `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new SwingDemo())` is the KTU-recommended best practice.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **AWT** = `java.awt`, **JDK 1.0**, **heavyweight**, **platform-dependent**, **no MVC**, **no PLAF**.
- **Swing** = `javax.swing`, **JDK 1.2 (JFC)**, **lightweight**, **platform-independent**, **MVC-based**, **PLAF-supported**.
- Swing is **built on top of AWT** тАФ Swing's top-level containers (`JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet`) inherit from AWT's `Frame`, `Dialog`, and `Applet`.
- **All Swing components** are prefixed with **`J`** (e.g., `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JPanel`).
- The **MVC pattern** in Swing separates data (Model), rendering (View), and input handling (Controller).
- **PLAF** in Swing allows runtime switching of component appearance using `UIManager.setLookAndFeel()`.
- **Never mix AWT and Swing components** in the same container hierarchy тАФ it causes z-order and event-dispatch bugs.
- Swing provides **advanced components** unavailable in AWT: `JTable`, `JTree`, `JTabbedPane`, `JSlider`, `JMenuBar`, `JOptionPane`, `JProgressBar`.
- Swing supports **built-in double buffering** for flicker-free rendering, whereas AWT requires manual implementation.
- Swing provides **tooltips** (`setToolTipText`) and **keyboard mnemonics** (`setMnemonic`) as first-class features.
- For custom painting in Swing, override `paintComponent(Graphics g)` тАФ **never** override `paint()` directly.
- `JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE` must be explicitly set to terminate the JVM on window close.
- Use `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` to ensure thread-safe UI construction on the Event Dispatch Thread.
- AWT's **Event Delegation Model** is the foundation of Swing's event handling тАФ both use `java.awt.event.*` listener interfaces.
- AWT uses `Component.update()` and `paint()`; Swing uses `JComponent.paintComponent()`, `paintBorder()`, and `paintChildren()` for fine-grained painting control.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swing Key Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swing Key Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Java Swing** is a lightweight, **Model-View-Controller (MVC) based** GUI (Graphical User Interface) toolkit that is part of the **Java Foundation Classes (JFC)**. It is a successor to the Abstract Window Toolkit (AWT) and provides a rich set of **pluggable look-and-feel** components written entirely in Java (peerless architecture), residing in the `javax.swing` package hierarchy.

In the context of the KTU 2024 OOP syllabus (Module 4 тАФ GUI Programming), **Swing Key Features** refers to the architectural and functional enhancements that Swing provides over legacy AWT. These features are the **distinguishing properties** of Swing components that a student must enumerate and justify during theory examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ The "Lego Furniture" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Think of AWT as a **rental wooden chair** тАФ its appearance is decided by the host operating system (Windows looks like Windows, macOS looks like macOS). You cannot repaint it.
>
> Think of **Swing** as a **DIY Lego furniture set** тАФ every piece is manufactured in Java itself, so the furniture looks **identical regardless of which room (OS) you assemble it in**. You can swap the seat, change the color, or apply a "Plastic Theme" (Metal, Nimbus, Motif) on the fly. This is the essence of **Pluggable Look And Feel (PLAF)**.

## 1.3 Why Swing? тАФ The Motivation

Before Swing (pre-JDK 1.2), Java GUIs used AWT, which had severe limitations:
- **Heavyweight components** (relied on OS peers) тЖТ look varied by OS.
- **Limited component set** (no tree, no table, no tabbed pane).
- **"Deadlock-prone"** event delegation model.
- **No MVC separation** тЖТ code mixing logic and display.

Swing was introduced in **JDK 1.2 (1998)** as part of JFC to overcome every one of these flaws.

## 1.4 The Seven Defining "Key Features" of Swing (Syllabus Anchor)

The KTU 2024 Module 4 explicitly lists the following **Key Features of Swing**:

| # | Key Feature | One-Line Essence |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lightweight Components** | Drawn entirely by Java (no OS peer). |
| 2 | **Pluggable Look And Feel (PLAF)** | Runtime swappable UI skins. |
| 3 | **MVC Architecture** | Separates data, display, and control. |
| 4 | **Double Buffering** | Eliminates flicker during repaint. |
| 5 | **Rich, Extensible Component Set** | Trees, tables, tabbed panes, color choosers, etc. |
| 6 | **Support for Borders, ToolTips & Keyboard Mnemonics** | Enhanced UX primitives. |
| 7 | **Accessibility (JAAPI) & Java 2D Integration** | Screen readers + vector graphics support. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Hierarchy Root Class** for all Swing components is `java.awt.Container` тЖТ `javax.swing.JComponent` тЖТ concrete classes like `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, `JFrame`, `JPanel`, etc. Understanding this inheritance is **mandatory** for KTU descriptive questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Feature 1 тАФ Lightweight Components

### 2.1.1 Concept
**Heavyweight components** (AWT: `Button`, `TextField`) rely on a **peer object** in the underlying OS тАФ e.g., a Win32 button on Windows, a Cocoa button on macOS. This causes the "My Button looks different on Linux" problem.

**Lightweight components** (Swing: `JButton`, `JTextField`) **have no native peer**. They:
- Inherit from `JComponent`.
- Borrow the screen real estate of a **nearest heavyweight ancestor** (typically a `JFrame` or `JApplet`).
- Are drawn pixel-by-pixel by Java's own rendering pipeline (`javax.swing.RepaintManager`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** A heavyweight component is a **hotel room booked at Marriott** тАФ its furniture is fixed by the chain. A lightweight component is a **foldable camping tent pitched inside that hotel room** тАФ fully under your control.

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic
- Mixed AWT + Swing painting is called **"Z-order issues"** or **"Heavyweight/Lightweight mixing problems"**.
- Fix: Call `JPopupMenu.setLightWeightPopupEnabled(false)` or avoid AWT containers in Swing hierarchies.

## 2.2 Feature 2 тАФ Pluggable Look And Feel (PLAF)

### 2.2.1 Concept
The **Look And Feel (L&F)** is a class that dictates *how* a component is drawn. Swing ships with multiple L&F packages:
- `javax.swing.plaf.metal` тАФ **Metal** (cross-platform default, "Ocean" theme in JDK 1.5+).
- `com.sun.java.swing.plaf.windows` тАФ **Windows** L&F.
- `com.sun.java.swing.plaf.motif` тАФ **CDE/Motif** L&F (Unix).
- `javax.swing.plaf.nimbus` тАФ **Nimbus** (since JDK 1.6u10, vector-based, modern).

### 2.2.2 How It Works (Step Logic)
1. The application boots.
2. Programmer calls: `UIManager.setLookAndFeel("javax.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel");`
3. The **`UIManager`** loads the corresponding `LookAndFeel` subclass.
4. `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(frame)` is called to **re-render** every component.
5. End result: Every `JButton`/`JTable` is repainted using Nimbus rules тАФ *without* changing the source code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU loves this one-liner. Memorize:
> **`UIManager.setLookAndFeel(className);`**
> **`SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(rootContainer);`**

## 2.3 Feature 3 тАФ MVC Architecture

Swing was **the first production-grade Java library** to enforce MVC. In Swing, the correspondence is:

| MVC Layer | Swing Implementation |
|---|---|
| **Model** | Interfaces like `ButtonModel`, `ListModel`, `TableModel`, `Document`, `TreeModel`. |
| **View** | The `UI` delegate (e.g., `ButtonUI`, `LabelUI`) тАФ protected method `updateUI()`. |
| **Controller** | Listeners (`ActionListener`, `MouseListener`, `KeyListener`, `ChangeListener`). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** MVC allows the *same* `JTable` to display data from a database, an XML file, or a custom `Vector` тАФ without changing the view or controller code. This is the foundation of modern frameworks like Spring MVC.

## 2.4 Feature 4 тАФ Double Buffering

When a component is repainted, AWT may show **flicker** because it erases, then redraws, then displays. Swing solves this with **double buffering**:
1. Draw the new frame into an **off-screen `BufferedImage`** (the back buffer).
2. Once complete, blit the entire image to the screen in one atomic operation.
3. The user sees only the final, completed image тАФ no flicker.

The system is orchestrated by `javax.swing.RepaintManager` and is **enabled by default** on all `JComponent` instances. Programmers can manually invoke:
- `setDoubleBuffered(true)` тАФ default.
- Override `paintComponent(Graphics g)` (NEVER `paint(Graphics g)`).

## 2.5 Feature 5 тАФ Rich, Extensible Component Set

Swing provides **~40 top-level components** (`JButton`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JTextField`, `JPasswordField`, `JTextArea`, `JScrollPane`, `JList`, `JTable`, `JTree`, `JTabbedPane`, `JSlider`, `JProgressBar`, `JMenuBar`, `JMenu`, `JMenuItem`, `JToolBar`, `JOptionPane`, `JColorChooser`, `JFileChooser`, etc.) тАФ far more than AWT's 7.

## 2.6 Feature 6 тАФ Borders, ToolTips, Keyboard Mnemonics & Accelerators

| UX Primitive | Method | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Border** | `component.setBorder(BorderFactory.createLineBorder(Color.RED));` | Visual grouping/title around a component. |
| **ToolTip** | `button.setToolTipText("Click to save");` | Hover-text help. |
| **Mnemonic** | `menuItem.setMnemonic('S');` | Underlined hot-key (e.g., `Alt+S`). |
| **Accelerator** | `menuItem.setAccelerator(KeyStroke.getKeyStroke("ctrl S"));` | Global hot-key (e.g., `Ctrl+S`). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`KeyStroke`** is the Swing class that represents a keyboard combination. It is decoupled from the OS, unlike AWT.

## 2.7 Feature 7 тАФ Accessibility (Java Accessibility API) & Java 2D

- **JAAPI (Java Accessibility API):** Classes in `javax.accessibility` allow screen readers (JAWS, NVDA) to introspect Swing components and announce their state.
- **Java 2D Integration:** All Swing painting goes through `Graphics2D`, which supports anti-aliasing, gradients, transforms, and complex shapes.
- **Unicode & i18n:** Native support for international text via `java.awt.Font` and `Locale`.

| Feature | Class / API Anchor | Package | One-Liner Exam Answer |

## 2.9 Engineering Utility of Swing Features

- **IDE Development:** IntelliJ IDEA & NetBeans use Swing's L&F and KeyStroke APIs.
- **Data Dashboards:** `JTable` + `JFreeChart` (Java 2D) for stock trading terminals.
- **Kiosk Software:** Custom PLAF + double-buffering for animation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix тАФ Code & Conceptual Derivations

## 3.1 Demonstration: All 7 Features in One Runnable Java Program

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Java listing below demonstrates **every** Swing key feature in a single compilable file. KTU often asks for short snippets proving PLAF, MVC, or Double Buffering тАФ this program covers them.

```java
// File: SwingKeyFeaturesDemo.java
// Purpose: Demonstrate all 7 Swing Key Features
import javax.swing.*;
import javax.swing.border.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class SwingKeyFeaturesDemo extends JFrame {
    private JTextField nameField;
    private JLabel    displayLabel;
    private int       clickCount = 0;   // Controller's state (MVC)

    public SwingKeyFeaturesDemo() {
        // === FEATURE 2: Pluggable Look and Feel ============================
        try {
            UIManager.setLookAndFeel("javax.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel");
            SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(this);
        } catch (Exception e) { /* fall back silently */ }

        // === FEATURE 6: Border (titled line border) ========================
        setTitle("Swing Key Features Demo");
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 20, 20));
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        JPanel panel = new JPanel();
        panel.setLayout(new BoxLayout(panel, BoxLayout.Y_AXIS));
        panel.setBorder(BorderFactory.createTitledBorder(
                BorderFactory.createLineBorder(Color.BLUE),
                "Login Panel", TitledBorder.LEFT, TitledBorder.TOP));

        // === FEATURE 5: Rich Components ====================================
        nameField = new JTextField(15);

        JButton submitBtn = new JButton("Submit");
        submitBtn.setToolTipText("Click to login");                 // FEATURE 6
        submitBtn.setMnemonic('S');                                 // FEATURE 6
        submitBtn.setAccelerator(
                KeyStroke.getKeyStroke(KeyEvent.VK_ENTER, 0));      // FEATURE 6

        displayLabel = new JLabel("Hello, Guest");
        displayLabel.setFont(new Font("Serif", Font.BOLD, 18));

        // === FEATURE 3: MVC - Controller = ActionListener =================
        submitBtn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                clickCount++;                                        // Model
                displayLabel.setText("Hello, " + nameField.getText() // View
                        + "  |  Clicks=" + clickCount);
            }
        });

        // === FEATURE 4: Double Buffering - enabled by default ============
        // Implicitly handled by JComponent.repaint() тЖТ RepaintManager
        setDoubleBuffered(true);

        // === FEATURE 1: Lightweight Component assembly ====================
        panel.add(new JLabel("Name:"));
        panel.add(nameField);
        panel.add(submitBtn);
        panel.add(displayLabel);
        add(panel);

        // === FEATURE 7: Java 2D & Accessibility (auto via Swing) ==========
        setSize(420, 280);
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // AWT vs Swing - Swing starts on EDT (Event Dispatch Thread)
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new SwingKeyFeaturesDemo());
    }
}
```

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Walkthrough

**Step 1 тАФ Package Imports.**
`javax.swing.*` brings in `JFrame`, `JButton`, `JTextField`, `JLabel`, `JPanel`, `UIManager`, `SwingUtilities`, `KeyStroke`. `javax.swing.border.*` brings in `BorderFactory` and `TitledBorder`. `java.awt.*` provides layout managers and `Color`, `Font`.

**Step 2 тАФ Constructor Invocation on the EDT.**
`SwingUtilities.invokeLater(...)` ensures the GUI is constructed on the **Event Dispatch Thread**, preventing thread-safety bugs (a critical best-practice the KTU examiner values).

**Step 3 тАФ PLAF Switch.**
The static method `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(String)` accepts the **fully qualified class name** of a `LookAndFeel` subclass. The call `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(this)` then walks the entire component tree and re-asks each `JComponent` to install the new `ComponentUI` delegate.

**Step 4 тАФ Titled Border Construction.**
`BorderFactory.createTitledBorder(outerBorder, title, justification, position)` is a **decorator pattern** тАФ it wraps a `LineBorder` (the "outerBorder") and adds a text title. The result is a single `Border` object assigned via `setBorder()`.

**Step 5 тАФ Mnemonic & Accelerator Pairing.**
- `setMnemonic('S')` тЗТ on `Alt+S`, focus jumps to the button (mnemonic).
- `setAccelerator(KeyStroke...)` тЗТ on the chosen keystroke, the `ActionEvent` is fired **without** focus needing to be on the button (accelerator).

**Step 6 тАФ MVC Triangulation.**
- The **Model** is the `clickCount` integer and the text inside `nameField.getText()`.
- The **View** is the rendering of `displayLabel` (delegated to `LabelUI`).
- The **Controller** is the anonymous inner `ActionListener` whose `actionPerformed` reads the model and mutates the view.

**Step 7 тАФ Double Buffering.**
Calling `setDoubleBuffered(true)` on the `JFrame` propagates the policy to all child `JComponent`s. The `RepaintManager` intercepts `repaint()` calls and consolidates them into a single off-screen pass per frame.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix тАФ AWT vs Swing

| Property | AWT | Swing |
|---|---|---|
| **Component weight** | Heavyweight (OS peer) | Lightweight (Java-drawn) |
| **Component count** | ~7 (Button, Label, etc.) | ~40+ (Trees, Tables, etc.) |
| **Architecture** | None / Ad-hoc | **MVC** |
| **Look & Feel** | OS-fixed (only 1 L&F) | **Pluggable** (Metal, Nimbus, etc.) |
| **Painting** | Direct тЖТ **flickers** | **Double-buffered** |
| **Event Handling** | Inheritance-based | **Delegation-based** |
| **Borders** | Not supported | `BorderFactory` + 8 types |
| **ToolTip** | Not supported | `setToolTipText()` |
| **Mnemonics** | Limited | Full `setMnemonic()` |
| **Key Strokes** | OS-coupled | `KeyStroke` class (decoupled) |
| **Accessibility** | None | `javax.accessibility` |
| **2D Graphics** | Limited | Full `Graphics2D` integration |
| **Base class** | `java.awt.Component` | `javax.swing.JComponent` |

## 3.3 Inheritance Derivation тАФ The Swing Class Hierarchy

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{java.lang.Object} \\
&\;\;\downarrow \\
&\text{java.awt.Component} \\
&\;\;\downarrow \\
&\text{java.awt.Container} \\
&\;\;\downarrow \\
&\text{javax.swing.JComponent} \quad \text{(abstract base for all Swing widgets)} \\
&\;\;\downarrow \\
&\begin{cases}
\text{javax.swing.AbstractButton} \rightarrow \text{JButton, JToggleButton, JCheckBox, JRadioButton} \\
\text{javax.swing.text.JTextComponent} \rightarrow \text{JTextField, JTextArea, JPasswordField} \\
\text{javax.swing.JLabel, JList, JTable, JTree, JSlider, JProgressBar, JMenuBar} \\
\end{cases} \\
&\;\;\downarrow \\
&\text{javax.swing.JFrame} \;\; \text{(top-level window, extends java.awt.Frame)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> `JComponent` is the **mandatory superclass** for all lightweight widgets. It provides `setBorder`, `setToolTipText`, `setDoubleBuffered`, `setOpaque`, and the `paintComponent(Graphics)` template method.
