# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram тАФ MVC Inside a `JButton`

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs are alphanumeric to satisfy the Mermaid compiler safeguards. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone IDs.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    user[End User]
    btn[UI Delegate ButtonUI View]
    model[ButtonModel Model]
    ctrl[ActionListener Controller]
    src[Application Source Data]

    user -->|click event| ctrl
    ctrl -->|reads state| model
    ctrl -->|fires ActionEvent| src
    src -->|updateModel| model
    model -->|state change| btn
    btn -->|repaint request| user

    classDef modelClass fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef viewClass fill:#8AC3FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrlClass fill:#8AFFA0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef userClass fill:#FF8AC3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class model modelClass
    class btn viewClass
    class ctrl ctrlClass
    class user,src userClass
```

**How to read this diagram:** The `JButton` is split into three collaborating objects. The **Controller** (`ActionListener`) is the only object the user ever touches directly. It queries the **Model** (`ButtonModel`) for pressed/armed/selected flags and then dispatches an `ActionEvent` to the **application source**, which in turn mutates the model. The **View** (`ButtonUI`) listens to the model and triggers a re-paint on the screen.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram тАФ Pluggable Look And Feel Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    app[Java Application]
    umgr[UIManager]
    laf1[MetalLookAndFeel]
    laf2[NimbusLookAndFeel]
    laf3[WindowsLookAndFeel]
    uidlg[ComponentUI Delegate]
    comp[JButton Instance]

    app -->|setLookAndFeel| umgr
    umgr --> laf1
    umgr --> laf2
    umgr --> laf3
    laf1 -->|installUI| uidlg
    laf2 -->|installUI| uidlg
    laf3 -->|installUI| uidlg
    uidlg --> comp
    comp -->|paintComponent output| scr[Screen Pixels]
```

**Engineering reading:** At runtime, only **one** `LookAndFeel` subclass is "installed" into the `UIManager`. When a component such as `JButton` is created, it calls `UIManager.getUI(this)` to retrieve the appropriate `ComponentUI` delegate. Calling `updateComponentTreeUI(rootContainer)` re-runs this `getUI` resolution for every descendant, allowing live L&F switching.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram тАФ Double Buffering Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    code[Developer Code Calls repaint]
    rm[RepaintManager]
    bb[Off-Screen BufferedImage]
    ddraw[Draw into Buffer]
    blit[Atomic Blit to Screen]
    scr[Display]

    code --> rm
    rm --> ddraw
    ddraw --> bb
    rm -->|flush completed frame| blit
    blit --> scr
```

**Engineering reading:** `RepaintManager` coalesces multiple `repaint()` calls within a single frame interval into one batch draw, then performs a single `Graphics2D.drawImage(backBuffer, ...)` to the screen тАФ eliminating the erase-repaint gap that causes flicker.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ 3 Mark Conceptual Questions

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions test the **Remember / Understand** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers must be crisp (3тАУ4 lines max) but technically accurate.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024]** Define *Pluggable Look and Feel* in Java Swing. List any two L&F classes available in the JDK.

**Model Answer:**
Pluggable Look and Feel (PLAF) is a Swing architecture that allows the appearance of all GUI components to be **changed at runtime** without modifying the application source code. It is achieved through the `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(String className)` static call followed by `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(rootContainer)`.

Two L&F classes shipped with the JDK are:
1. `javax.swing.plaf.metal.MetalLookAndFeel` (cross-platform default).
2. `javax.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel` (vector-based, modern, since JDK 1.6u10).

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]** What is meant by a *lightweight component*? How is it different from an AWT heavyweight component?

**Model Answer:**
A **lightweight component** is one that has **no native OS peer**; it is drawn entirely in Java using `Graphics2D` and inherits from `javax.swing.JComponent`. A **heavyweight component** (AWT) relies on a peer object in the host operating system (e.g., a Win32 button). Lightweight components therefore offer a **consistent cross-platform appearance** and can have **arbitrary shapes and transparency**, while heavyweights are confined to the rectangular bounding box of their OS peer.

## 5.2 Part B тАФ 14 Mark Descriptive Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern requires **internal choice** in Part B. Either Question A or Question B is presented. Both must be attempted at full 14-mark depth.

---

### тнР QUESTION A тАФ 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024 Model]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **MVC architecture** implemented by Java Swing using a `JButton` as an example. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Write a Java program that creates a `JFrame` containing a `JTextField`, a `JButton`, and a `JLabel`. Demonstrate **(i)** Pluggable Look and Feel switch to Nimbus, **(ii)** Titled Border, **(iii)** ToolTip, and **(iv)** Mnemonic on the button. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution тАФ Part (a)

1. The **MVC pattern** divides an interactive component into three roles: **Model** (data and state), **View** (visual rendering), and **Controller** (input handling).
2. In Swing, a `JButton` delegates:
   - **Model** тЖТ an implementation of the `ButtonModel` interface (typically `DefaultButtonModel`) that holds fields such as `armed`, `pressed`, `selected`, `rollover`, and the `Action`.
   - **View** тЖТ a `ButtonUI` delegate (e.g., `MetalButtonUI`, `WindowsButtonUI`) that decides how the button is drawn. The actual class is fetched dynamically by `UIManager.getUI(this)` and installed via `updateUI()`.
   - **Controller** тЖТ listeners (notably `ActionListener` + the internal `Controller` of the UI delegate that handles `MouseListener` and `KeyListener` events) that translate raw input events into model mutations.
3. **Data flow**: A user click on the button тЖТ `MouseListener` (Controller) тЖТ mutates `ButtonModel` тЖТ fires `ChangeEvent` тЖТ `ButtonUI` (View) repaints the button тЖТ controller also fires `ActionEvent` to registered `ActionListener`s.
4. **Why MVC?** It allows the **same component** to be plugged into a different data source (e.g., a `JTable`'s `TableModel`) without changing either view or controller тАФ a textbook case of *separation of concerns*.

**Block Diagram (textual):**
- `JButton тФАтФАhas-aтФАтФАтЦ╢ ButtonModel (state)`
- `JButton тФАтФАhas-aтФАтФАтЦ╢ ButtonUI (view)`
- `ActionListener тФАтФАlistensтФАтФАтЦ╢ JButton`
- `JButton тФАтФАnotifiesтФАтФАтЦ╢ ButtonUI тЖТ repaint()`

#### Model Solution тАФ Part (b)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import javax.swing.border.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class SwingDemo extends JFrame {
    SwingDemo() {
        // (i) PLAF - Pluggable Look and Feel
        try {
            UIManager.setLookAndFeel("javax.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel");
            SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(this);
        } catch (Exception e) { System.out.println(e); }

        // (ii) Titled Border
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        JPanel p = new JPanel();
        p.setLayout(new BoxLayout(p, BoxLayout.Y_AXIS));
        p.setBorder(BorderFactory.createTitledBorder(
                BorderFactory.createLineBorder(Color.RED), "Login"));

        JTextField tf = new JTextField(15);
        JLabel lbl = new JLabel("Result will appear here");
        JButton btn = new JButton("Submit");
        // (iii) ToolTip
        btn.setToolTipText("Click to greet");
        // (iv) Mnemonic
        btn.setMnemonic('S');

        btn.addActionListener(e ->
            lbl.setText("Hello, " + tf.getText() + "!")
        );

        p.add(new JLabel("Name:"));
        p.add(tf);
        p.add(btn);
        p.add(lbl);
        add(p);

        setTitle("Swing Key Features");
        setSize(400, 250);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new SwingDemo());
    }
}
```

**Mark split suggestion for KTU evaluator:**
- 2 marks: correct `UIManager` + `updateComponentTreeUI` calls.
- 2 marks: `TitledBorder` construction syntax.
- 1 mark: `setToolTipText` call.
- 1 mark: `setMnemonic` call.
- 1 mark: correct GUI assembly and `main` method.

---

### тнР QUESTION B тАФ 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q. (a)** Explain **double buffering** in Swing. Why is it required? How does `RepaintManager` coordinate it? **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Differentiate between AWT and Swing with respect to **at least 8 architectural features**. State the base class for Swing components. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution тАФ Part (a)

1. **Flicker problem in AWT:** When a container is repainted, AWT's default `update(Graphics g)` first clears the background (fills with the background color) and then calls `paint(g)`. The user momentarily sees a blank rectangle, producing flicker тАФ especially noticeable in animations.

2. **Double buffering solution:** Swing draws the entire scene into an **off-screen `BufferedImage`** (the "back buffer"). Only when the complete frame is ready does the `RepaintManager` blit the image to the screen in a single atomic `drawImage()` call. The user never sees the intermediate blank state.

3. **Role of `RepaintManager`:**
   - It is a per-`JFrame`/`JApplet` singleton (retrievable via `RepaintManager.currentManager(component)`).
   - When `component.repaint()` is called, the dirty region is enqueued rather than processed immediately.
   - At the end of the EDT cycle, the manager **coalesces** overlapping dirty regions, then renders the union to the back buffer.
   - The back buffer is finally drawn to the screen using `Graphics2D.drawImage(buffer, x, y, null)`.

4. **Manual control:** `JComponent.setDoubleBuffered(boolean)` toggles the feature (default `true`). Programmers may override `paintComponent(Graphics g)` to do custom painting тАФ this method is called *after* the back buffer has been set up, so any drawing done inside it is automatically double-buffered.

#### Model Solution тАФ Part (b)

| # | Feature | AWT | Swing |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Component weight | Heavyweight (OS peer) | Lightweight (Java-drawn) |
| 2 | Architecture | Ad-hoc | MVC |
| 3 | Look & Feel | Fixed by OS | Pluggable via `UIManager` |
| 4 | Painting | Direct тЖТ flicker | Double-buffered |
| 5 | Component count | ~7 (peer-based) | ~40+ |
| 6 | Event model | Inheritance | Delegation |
| 7 | Borders | Not supported | `BorderFactory` |
| 8 | ToolTips / Mnemonics | Absent | Full support |
| 9 | Key handling | OS-coupled | `KeyStroke` class |
| 10 | Accessibility | None | `javax.accessibility` |
| 11 | 2D Graphics | Limited `Graphics` | Full `Graphics2D` |
| 12 | Base class | `java.awt.Component` | **`javax.swing.JComponent`** |

**Base class statement (mandatory one-liner):** The base class for all Swing components is `javax.swing.JComponent`, which itself extends `java.awt.Container` and overrides `paint(Graphics)` to implement double buffering.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes reported by KTU examiners:**
> 1. **Confusing `paint()` vs `paintComponent()`** тАФ Overriding `paint()` breaks the double-buffering chain and loses up to **2 marks** in practicals. Always override `paintComponent(Graphics g)`.
> 2. **Forgetting `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()`** in the `main` method. Modern Swing mandates EDT initialization; omitting it loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing AWT and Swing top-level containers** тАФ A `Frame` cannot directly contain a `JButton` cleanly. Use `JFrame` everywhere.
> 4. **Writing the L&F class name in the wrong case** тАФ `MetalLookAndFeel` is correct; `metallookandfeel` is not. This is a one-shot error.
> 5. **Missing `updateComponentTreeUI()`** after `setLookAndFeel` тАФ the L&F will not visually change, and the examiner will mark "implementation not working" (-3 marks).
> 6. **Forgetting to call `setVisible(true)`** at the end of the constructor тАФ window won't appear; examiner assumes crash and deducts 2 marks.
> 7. **Confusing "MVC" with "MVP"** тАФ Swing uses **MVC**, not the modern MVP/MVVM. Stick with MVC in answers.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (last-night-before-exam):**
> - тШС Swing's package = `javax.swing`; AWT's package = `java.awt`.
> - тШС Base class of **all** Swing widgets = `javax.swing.JComponent`.
> - тШС `JFrame` is the top-level window (extends `java.awt.Frame`).
> - тШС Swing components are **lightweight** (no OS peer).
> - тШС PLAF is enabled by `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(String)` + `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(Component)`.
> - тШС Swing implements **MVC**: Model = `*Model` interface, View = `*UI` delegate, Controller = listeners.
> - тШС **Double buffering** = off-screen `BufferedImage` painted first, then blitted. Coordinator = `RepaintManager`.
> - тШС Always override `paintComponent(Graphics g)` for custom drawing тАФ never `paint(Graphics g)`.
> - тШС Borders тЖТ `BorderFactory.createTitledBorder(...)`, `createLineBorder(...)`, `createEtchedBorder(...)`, `createEmptyBorder(...)`, etc.
> - тШС Tooltip тЖТ `setToolTipText(String)`.
> - тШС Mnemonic тЖТ `setMnemonic(char)` (Alt + char).
> - тШС Accelerator тЖТ `setAccelerator(KeyStroke.getKeyStroke(...))`.
> - тШС `KeyStroke` decouples keyboard binding from the underlying OS.
> - тШС `javax.accessibility` provides screen-reader integration.
> - тШС Java 2D = `Graphics2D` тЖТ anti-aliasing, gradients, transforms, vector shapes.
> - тШС Initialize the GUI on the **Event Dispatch Thread** via `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(Runnable)`.
> - тШС AWT vs Swing mnemonic table: heavyweight vs lightweight, fixed vs pluggable L&F, direct vs double-buffered painting, ad-hoc vs MVC, ~7 vs ~40+ components.
> - тШС Top Swing components to remember: `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, `JTextArea`, `JPasswordField`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JComboBox`, `JList`, `JTable`, `JTree`, `JTabbedPane`, `JMenuBar`, `JMenu`, `JMenuItem`, `JOptionPane`, `JFileChooser`, `JColorChooser`, `JSlider`, `JProgressBar`, `JScrollPane`, `JToolBar`.
> - тШС Exam-tactic: in 14-mark questions, **always draw the MVC block diagram** тАФ it virtually guarantees the design-mark component of the rubric.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model View Controller (MVC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Model View Controller (MVC) # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** is a **software architectural design pattern** that separates an application into three interconnected logical components: the **Model** (data and business logic), the **View** (user interface and presentation layer), and the **Controller** (input handler and intermediary that processes user actions). The central motivation behind MVC is **Separation of Concerns (SoC)**, which isolates business rules from user-interface considerations, thereby enabling independent development, testing, and maintenance of each layer. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme тАУ PBCST304 (Object Oriented Programming)**, MVC is studied as a classical application of **Object-Oriented Design Principles** such as **encapsulation**, **modularity**, **loose coupling**, and **high cohesion**. It is a direct manifestation of the **GRASP (General Responsibility Assignment Software Patterns)** principles discussed in Module 4 of the syllabus. ## 1.2 The Three Pillars of MVC | Component | Primary Responsibility | Knows About | |-----------|----------------------|-------------| | **Model** | Business logic, data, rules, state | Database, internal data structures | | **View** | UI rendering, output display | Model (read-only access) | | **Controller** | User input handling, request routing | Model + View (orchestrator) | ## 1.3 Real-World Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy тАУ Think of a Restaurant:** > > Imagine a fine-dining restaurant where you (the customer) place an order with the **waiter**: > - **You (User)** тЖТ give input / place order > - **Waiter (Controller)** тЖТ takes your order, communicates to the kitchen, and brings the food back > - **Kitchen (Model)** тЖТ prepares the food using ingredients (data) and recipes (business rules) > - **Plate Presentation (View)** тЖТ how the food is arranged and presented to you > > The waiter doesn't cook (Controller тЙа Model). The kitchen doesn't arrange the plate (Model тЙа View). The plate doesn't take orders (View тЙа Controller). **Each has one job, and they communicate through well-defined channels.** This is MVC in essence. ## 1.4 Why MVC Matters in OOP - **Encapsulation**: Each component hides its internal implementation. - **Reusability**: A single Model can drive multiple Views (e.g., web + mobile UI from the same data). - **Maintainability**: Changes in UI do not affect business logic and vice versa. - **Testability**: Each layer can be unit-tested independently (TDD-friendly). - **Parallel Development**: Front-end and back-end teams can work simultaneously. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Components тАФ Detailed Role Analysis ### 2.1.1 The Model - Represents the **application's data and the rules that govern access/update** to that data. - **Does NOT know** about the View or Controller. - Notifies the View when its state changes (typically via the **Observer Pattern** in classical MVC). - Examples: `Student`, `Account`, `Order` classes; database access objects (DAOs); business logic services. ### 2.1.2 The View - **Passive representation** of the Model. - Renders the Model's data into a format suitable for the user (HTML, GUI, JSON, etc.). - Forwards user gestures (clicks, key-presses) to the Controller. - Should contain **minimal to zero logic** (no business rules). ### 2.1.3 The Controller - Acts as the **mediator** between Model and View. - Receives user input тЖТ translates it into operations on the Model тЖТ selects an appropriate View to render the result. - Holds **application flow logic** (e.g., validation, navigation decisions). ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Request Flow (MVC Interaction Cycle) 1. **User Action**: User interacts with the View (clicks a button, submits a form). 2. **Event Forwarding**: View forwards the input event to the Controller. 3. **Input Translation**: Controller interprets the event and invokes the appropriate Model method. 4. **Model Update**: Model updates its internal state (e.g., saves to database, performs computation). 5. **Notification**: Model notifies the View (or Controller) that its state has changed. 6. **View Refresh**: View re-queries the Model and re-renders the updated state to the user. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet | Aspect | Without MVC (Monolithic) | With MVC | |--------|--------------------------|----------| | **Coupling** | Tight (UI + Logic mixed) | Loose (clearly separated) | | **Cohesion** | Low | High | | **Code Reuse** | Minimal | Models reused across Views | | **Testing** | Hard (UI-bound) | Easy (unit-testable layers) | | **Team Workflow** | Sequential | Parallel | | **Maintenance Cost** | **High** | **Low** | ## 2.4 Architectural Style Classification $$ \text{MVC} \subset \text{Presentation Tier Patterns} \subset \text{Architectural Patterns} $$ MVC belongs to the family of **Presentation Layer Architectural Patterns**. Its siblings include: - **MVP (Model-View-Presenter)** тАФ Presenter handles more UI logic. - **MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel)** тАФ Two-way data binding between View and ViewModel. - **PAC (Presentation-Abstraction-Control)** тАФ Hierarchical MVC variant. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility MVC is the foundational pattern behind: - **Java Spring MVC** (server-side web framework) - **Ruby on Rails**, **Django (MTV variant)**, **Laravel** - **iOS UIKit**, **Android (Activity/Fragment-based)** - **Desktop**: JavaFX, .NET WPF (MVVM) > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** In modern microservice architectures, the **Model** is often the microservice exposing a REST/GraphQL API, the **View** is a SPA (React/Angular), and the **Controller** is the API gateway or BFF (Backend-For-Frontend). The pattern scales from desktop apps to distributed systems. ## 2.6 KTU Pitfall to Avoid > [!WARNING] > A common KTU exam mistake is confusing **MVC with three-tier architecture** (Presentation / Business / Data). MVC is a **pattern within the Presentation Tier**; 3-tier is a **deployment architecture**. MVC тЙа 3-tier. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 A Complete Java Implementation Walkthrough (Step-by-Step) Below is the **canonical KTU-style Java implementation** of MVC for a `Student` record system. Each sub-part is shown with its specific role in the MVC triad. ### 3.1.1 The Model Class тАФ `Student.java` ```java // MODEL: holds data and business logic public class Student { private String name; private int id; private double cgpa; public Student(String name, int id, double cgpa) { this.name = name; this.id = id; this.cgpa = cgpa; } // Business rule public boolean isEligibleForPlacements() { return cgpa >= 6.0; } // Getters and Setters (Encapsulation) public String getName() { return name; } public int getId() { return id; } public double getCgpa() { return cgpa; } public void setCgpa(double cgpa) { this.cgpa = cgpa; } } ``` ### 3.1.2 The View Class тАФ `StudentView.java` ```java // VIEW: pure presentation, no business logic public class StudentView { public void printStudentDetails(String name, int id, double cgpa) { System.out.println("----- Student Record -----"); System.out.println("Name : " + name); System.out.println("ID : " + id); System.out.println("CGPA : " + cgpa); System.out.println("--------------------------"); } } ``` ### 3.1.3 The Controller Class тАФ `StudentController.java` ```java // CONTROLLER: orchestrates Model and View public class StudentController { private Student model; private StudentView view; public StudentController(Student model, StudentView view) { this.model = model; this.view = view; } public void setStudentName(String name) { model.setName(name); } public String getStudentName() { return model.getName(); } public void setStudentCgpa(double cgpa) { model.setCgpa(cgpa); } public double getStudentCgpa() { return model.getCgpa(); } public void updateView() { view.printStudentDetails( model.getName(), model.getId(), model.getCgpa() ); } } ``` > **Note on encapsulation of name:** In the above Model, `name` has only a getter. For a setter to exist, `setName(String name)` would be added. The setter was omitted above for brevity but is implied. ### 3.1.4 The Driver / Main Class тАФ `MVCDemo.java` ```java public class MVCDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { // Step 1: Create Model Student model = new Student("Ananya", 101, 8.7); // Step 2: Create View StudentView view = new StudentView(); // Step 3: Wire them through Controller StudentController controller = new StudentController(model, view); // Step 4: Initial display controller.updateView(); // Step 5: User "action" тАФ update CGPA via Controller controller.setStudentCgpa(9.1); // Step 6: Refresh View controller.updateView(); } } ``` ### 3.1.5 Output Trace ``` ----- Student Record ----- Name : Ananya ID : 101 CGPA : 8.7 -------------------------- ----- Student Record ----- Name : Ananya ID : 101 CGPA : 9.1 -------------------------- ``` ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix тАФ Mapping Code Elements to MVC Roles | Code Element | Class Type | MVC Role | Knows About | Forbidden To | |--------------|------------|----------|-------------|--------------| | `Student` | POJO / Bean | **Model** | Itself, DB (if DAO used) | View, Controller | | `StudentView` | Renderer | **View** | Model (read-only) | Business rules, DB | | `StudentController` | Orchestrator | **Controller** | Model + View | Direct UI rendering | | `MVCDemo` | Client / Main | **Application root** | All three (wiring) | тАФ | ## 3.3 Derivation of the Update Flow Let the user action be $A$, model state be $S$, and displayed view be $V$. The MVC update flow can be expressed as the sequence: $$ A \xrightarrow{\text{View forwards}} C \xrightarrow{\text{Calls}} M(S) \xrightarrow{\text{State change}} S' \xrightarrow{\text{Notification}} V(S') $$ In the Java code above: - $A$ = `controller.setStudentCgpa(9.1)` call from `main`. - $C$ = `StudentController.setStudentCgpa()`. - $M(S) \rightarrow S'$ = `model.setCgpa(9.1)` mutates internal state. - $V(S')$ = `controller.updateView()` calls `view.printStudentDetails()` with new CGPA. ## 3.4 Variants of MVC (Frequently Asked in KTU) | Variant | Flow Direction | Notification | Used In | |---------|---------------|--------------|---------| | **Classical MVC** (Trygve Reenskaug, 1979) | Bi-directional | Observer pattern | SmallTalk | | **MVC Model 2** (Web) | Unidirectional (front-controller) | Pull-based | Java Spring, JSP | | **HMVC** | Hierarchical | Tree of MVC triads | Modular web apps | | **MVVM** | Bi-directional (data binding) | Reactive streams | WPF, Angular, Vue | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level MVC Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR user1[User] view1[View Layer] ctrl1[Controller Layer] model1[Model Layer] db1[(Database)] user1 -- Input Action --> view1 view1 -- Forwards Event --> ctrl1 ctrl1 -- Updates State --> model1 model1 -- Reads/Writes --> db1 model1 -- State Change --> ctrl1 ctrl1 -- Selects View --> view1 view1 -- Renders Output --> user1 ``` ## 4.2 Sequence Diagram тАФ MVC Request Lifecycle ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant V as View participant C as Controller participant M as Model U->>V: 1. Performs Action (click, input) V->>C: 2. Forwards user event C->>M: 3. Invokes business method M->>M: 4. Updates internal state M-->>C: 5. Returns result / notifies C->>V: 6. Selects updated view V-->>U: 7. Renders refreshed display ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (MVC + Persistence) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph presentationTier["PRESENTATION TIER"] v1[VIEW
StudentView.java] c1[CONTROLLER
StudentController.java] end subgraph logicTier["LOGIC TIER"] m1[MODEL
Student.java] b1[BUSINESS RULES
isEligibleForPlacements] end subgraph dataTier["DATA TIER"] d1[(Database)] dao1[StudentDAO] end v1 -->|forwards input| c1 c1 -->|invokes| m1 m1 -->|delegates rules| b1 m1 -->|persist/fetch| dao1 dao1 -->|SQL| d1 c1 -->|refresh| v1 ``` ## 4.4 Component Coupling Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB modelA[Model] viewA[View] ctrlA[Controller] ctrlA -. invokes .-> modelA ctrlA -. updates .-> viewA viewA -. forwards events .-> ctrlA modelA -. notifies change .-> ctrlA viewA -. reads .-> modelA classDef modelStyle fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px classDef viewStyle fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px classDef ctrlStyle fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px class modelA modelStyle class viewA viewStyle class ctrlA ctrlStyle ``` ## 4.5 Variant Comparison Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR mvc1[MVC] mvp1[MVP] mvvm1[MVVM] mvc1 -->|Evolution| mvp1 mvp1 -->|Evolution| mvvm1 mvc1 --- mvc1desc[Controller as Mediator
Push-based updates] mvp1 --- mvp1desc[Presenter handles UI logic
View is passive] mvvm1 --- mvvm1desc[Two-way data binding
Reactive] ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]` **Define the Model-View-Controller (MVC) architectural pattern. List its three components and state one responsibility of each.** **Model Answer:** Model-View-Controller (MVC) is a software design pattern that separates an application into three interconnected components тАФ **Model**, **View**, and **Controller** тАФ to enforce *separation of concerns*. - **Model**: Encapsulates the application's data and business logic (e.g., database access, validation rules). It is independent of the UI. - **View**: Handles the user interface and presentation of data retrieved from the Model. It contains no business logic. - **Controller**: Acts as an intermediary that receives user input from the View, processes it (often by updating the Model), and returns the appropriate View to display. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for listing components + 1 for one responsibility each OR 1 for definition + 2 for components with explanation.)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024]` **Differentiate between Model and View in MVC. Why is the View kept "passive"?** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Model | View | |--------|-------|------| | Purpose | Manages data and business logic | Renders the UI | | Dependency | Independent of View/Controller | Reads from Model | | Logic | Contains business rules | Should contain no business logic | | Example | `Student` class with `cgpa` field | HTML page displaying student info | The View is kept **passive** (logic-free) so that: 1. The same View can be replaced without changing business logic. 2. Multiple Views (web, mobile, CLI) can display the same Model. 3. UI designers and developers can work in parallel. 4. Testing the UI becomes easier. --- ## 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Rule:** In the End-Semester Exam, Module 4 questions offer a choice between **Q11 (A or B)**. Solve any one. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the MVC design pattern with a neat diagram. Describe the role of each component and how they interact. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a Java program to implement MVC for a `Book` library system. The Model should hold title, author, and ISBN. The View should print book details, and the Controller should allow updating the author name and refreshing the view. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to A(a) **MVC Pattern Overview:** MVC separates concerns into three components: - **Model**: Core data and rules (`Book` data + business logic). - **View**: Output rendering (`BookView` printing method). - **Controller**: Mediator (`BookController`). **Interaction Flow:** 1. User triggers an event in the View. 2. View delegates the event to the Controller. 3. Controller calls the appropriate Model method. 4. Model updates its state and notifies back. 5. Controller selects the appropriate View to display the updated data. **Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR u1[User] -->|input| v1[View] v1 -->|event| c1[Controller] c1 -->|update| m1[Model] m1 -->|notify| c1 c1 -->|refresh| v1 v1 -->|display| u1 ``` **Benefits:** - Loose coupling, high cohesion. - Parallel development. - Easy maintenance and unit testing. --- #### Solution to A(b) тАФ Java Code **Model тАФ `Book.java`** ```java public class Book { private String title; private String author; private String isbn; public Book(String title, String author, String isbn) { this.title = title; this.author = author; this.isbn = isbn; } public String getTitle() { return title; } public String getAuthor() { return author; } public String getIsbn() { return isbn; } public void setAuthor(String author) { this.author = author; } } ``` **View тАФ `BookView.java`** ```java public class BookView { public void printBookDetails(String title, String author, String isbn) { System.out.println("Title : " + title); System.out.println("Author : " + author); System.out.println("ISBN : " + isbn); } } ``` **Controller тАФ `BookController.java`** ```java public class BookController { private Book model; private BookView view; public BookController(Book model, BookView view) { this.model = model; this.view = view; } public void setAuthor(String author) { model.setAuthor(author); } public String getAuthor() { return model.getAuthor(); } public void updateView() { view.printBookDetails(model.getTitle(), model.getAuthor(), model.getIsbn()); } } ``` **Driver тАФ `LibraryApp.java`** ```java public class LibraryApp { public static void main(String[] args) { Book book = new Book("Clean Code", "Robert C. Martin", "978-0132350884"); BookView view = new BookView(); BookController controller = new BookController(book, view); controller.updateView(); controller.setAuthor("Uncle Bob"); controller.updateView(); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Title : Clean Code Author : Robert C. Martin ISBN : 978-0132350884 Title : Clean Code Author : Uncle Bob ISBN : 978-0132350884 ``` **Mark Distribution (Typical KTU Key):** - Correct Model class with encapsulation тАФ **2 marks** - Correct View class with print logic тАФ **2 marks** - Correct Controller with setter + updateView тАФ **2 marks** - Working driver / main method тАФ **1 mark** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024]` **(a)** Compare and contrast the **MVC, MVP, and MVVM** architectural patterns. State one real-world framework that uses each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a suitable Java example, demonstrate how MVC achieves **loose coupling** and **high cohesion**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to B(a) | Aspect | MVC | MVP | MVVM | |--------|-----|-----|------| | **Full Form** | Model-View-Controller | Model-View-Presenter | Model-View-ViewModel | | **Mediator** | Controller | Presenter | ViewModel | | **View Type** | Active (sends events) | Passive (Presenter drives it) | Passive (auto-bound) | | **Data Binding** | Manual (Observer) | Manual | Two-way automatic | | **Testability** | Moderate | High | High | | **Real-world use** | Java Spring MVC, Ruby on Rails | GWT, ASP.NET Web Forms | WPF, Angular, Vue.js | **Key Differences:** - In **MVC**, the Controller decides which View to render. In **MVP**, the Presenter holds all UI logic and pushes data to a passive View. - In **MVVM**, the ViewModel exposes data via observables; the View auto-updates via two-way binding, reducing boilerplate. - All three share the Model, but the *intermediary* role becomes progressively more decoupled and testable from MVC тЖТ MVVM. --- #### Solution to B(b) тАФ Loose Coupling & High Cohesion via MVC **Loose Coupling** means components depend on **abstractions**, not concrete implementations. **High Cohesion** means a class has **one well-defined responsibility**. **Example тАФ A `Product` E-Commerce App:** ```java // MODEL тАФ one job: represent product data class Product { private String name; private double price; public Product(String n, double p) { name = n; price = p; } public double getPrice() { return price; } } // VIEW тАФ one job: print class ProductView { public void show(String name, double price) { System.out.println(name + " costs " + price); } } // CONTROLLER тАФ one job: mediate class ProductController { private Product model; private ProductView view; public ProductController(Product m, ProductView v) { model = m; view = v; } public void refresh() { view.show(model.getName(), model.getPrice()); } } ``` **Why this shows Loose Coupling:** - `ProductView` does not know *how* `Product` stores data тАФ it only receives primitive arguments. - `ProductController` is the only component aware of both; either side can be replaced (e.g., a new `JSONProductView` or `SqlProduct`). **Why this shows High Cohesion:** - `Product` has data-handling methods only. - `ProductView` has display methods only. - `ProductController` has orchestration methods only. - **No class has a "mixed" responsibility** тАФ each has exactly one reason to change. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. Many students write the **business logic inside the View** (e.g., price calculation in `printDetails()`). This **breaks MVC** and loses 2тАУ3 marks. > 2. Direct ModelтАУView calls (View calls Model directly) are **forbidden** in classical MVC; the Controller must mediate. > 3. Forgetting `private` fields and using `public` variables in the Model тАФ violates encapsulation (lose 1 mark). > 4. Confusing **MVC** with **3-tier architecture** тАФ they are not the same. MVC is a *pattern*; 3-tier is a *deployment topology*. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **MVC = Model + View + Controller** тАФ the **3 components** must always be named in order. - **Model** = data + business rules (no UI awareness). - **View** = passive UI renderer (no business logic). - **Controller** = mediator between Model and View. - **Goal of MVC** = **Separation of Concerns** тЖТ loose coupling, high cohesion. - **Inventor** = Trygve Reenskaug (SmallTalk, Xerox PARC, 1979). - **Classical MVC** uses the **Observer Pattern** for Model-to-View notification. - **MVC Model 2** is the web adaptation (Front Controller + Dispatcher). - **MVC тЙа 3-tier** (Presentation / Business / Data) тАФ MVC is a *pattern*, 3-tier is a *deployment architecture*. - **Variants** = MVP, MVVM, HMVC тАФ all evolve from MVC. - **Real-world frameworks** = Java Spring MVC, Rails, Django (MTV), Angular, iOS UIKit. - **In KTU 2024 OOP syllabus**, MVC falls under **Module 4: Design Patterns / Advanced OOP Concepts**. - **Key OOP principles applied** = Encapsulation, Abstraction, Modularity, Delegation. - **Common mark-losers**: - Business logic in the View. - Direct ViewтЖФModel calls bypassing the Controller. - Confusing MVC with 3-tier. - Writing public fields in the Model. - **Always include a diagram** in 7-mark questions тАФ KTU examiners award 1тАУ2 marks for a correct block/flow diagram. - **Mention "loose coupling" and "high cohesion"** in any MVC definition тАФ these are **buzzwords** that score full marks. - **Java code must show all three classes + main** in implementation questions.

topics.title: Swing Controls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swing Controls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Swing** is a GUI (Graphical User Interface) toolkit in Java provided as part of the **Java Foundation Classes (JFC)**. It is housed under the `javax.swing` package and represents a set of **lightweight, platform-independent** components that are entirely written in Java, building upon the older, heavyweight AWT (`java.awt`) framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The `javax.swing` package provides classes such as `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, `JTextArea`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JComboBox`, `JList`, `JTable`, `JMenu`, and `JMenuBar`. Every Swing component inherits from `java.awt.Container` (via `JComponent`), and unlike AWT, they do not depend on the underlying operating system's peer components.

**Swing Controls** are the interactive widgets (buttons, text fields, tables, menus, etc.) that the user manipulates to interact with a Java application. They are the **visual vocabulary** of any desktop application built using Java.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **building a physical dashboard for a car**:
- The **steering wheel, accelerator, and gear stick** are the *controls* тАФ they let the driver give input.
- The **speedometer and fuel gauge** are the *display* тАФ they show output.
- The **dashboard layout** is the *Layout Manager* тАФ it arranges everything neatly.
- The **wiring that makes the speedometer needle move when you press the accelerator** is the *Event Handling* mechanism.

In Java Swing:
- `JButton`, `JTextField`, `JCheckBox` = the **steering wheel** (input controls)
- `JLabel` = the **speedometer** (output display)
- `BorderLayout`, `FlowLayout` = the **dashboard layout**
- `ActionListener`, `ItemListener` = the **wiring**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology for Exams:**
> - **Lightweight Component**: A component written entirely in Java that does *not* rely on a native OS peer. (e.g., `JButton`)
> - **Heavyweight Component**: A component that uses a native OS peer (e.g., `java.awt.Button`). Swing is mostly lightweight, with `JFrame`, `JDialog`, and `JApplet` being the exceptions.
> - **Pluggable Look and Feel (PLAF)**: The ability of Swing components to change appearance dynamically without changing code.
> - **MVC Architecture**: Swing components follow the Model-View-Controller design pattern.

### The Class Hierarchy of Swing Controls

The most critical knowledge to memorize is the inheritance chain. KTU frequently asks diagrams of this hierarchy.

```
java.lang.Object
    тФФтФАтФА java.awt.Component
            тФФтФАтФА java.awt.Container
                    тФФтФАтФА javax.swing.JComponent  тЖР ROOT OF ALL SWING CONTROLS
                            тФЬтФАтФА JLabel
                            тФЬтФАтФА JButton (extends AbstractButton)
                            тФВ       тФФтФАтФА JToggleButton
                            тФВ               тФЬтФАтФА JCheckBox
                            тФВ               тФФтФАтФА JRadioButton
                            тФЬтФАтФА JTextField (extends JTextComponent)
                            тФЬтФАтФА JTextArea  (extends JTextComponent)
                            тФЬтФАтФА JComboBox
                            тФЬтФАтФА JList
                            тФЬтФАтФА JTable
                            тФЬтФАтФА JTree
                            тФЬтФАтФА JMenuBar
                            тФЬтФАтФА JMenu
                            тФЬтФАтФА JMenuItem
                            тФФтФАтФА JPanel
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every Swing control ultimately extends `JComponent`**, which itself extends `java.awt.Container`. This means every Swing control can itself act as a container and hold other components.

### Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for Visibility)

- **Package**: `javax.swing`
- **Root Class**: `javax.swing.JComponent`
- **Top-Level Containers**: `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet`
- **Default Look & Feel**: Cross-platform (Metal) тАФ platform-independent rendering
- **Thread Safety Rule**: All Swing components must be accessed only from the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)**, except a few specifically marked thread-safe methods like `repaint()`, `revalidate()`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & Swing Controls Master Reference

## 2.1 Classification of Swing Components

Swing components are broadly classified into **three architectural tiers**:

| Tier | Description | Examples | Exam Weight |
|------|-------------|----------|-------------|
| **Top-Level Containers** | Can be displayed standalone; have a `JRootPane` | `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet` | High |
| **Intermediate Containers** | Used to group and lay out components | `JPanel`, `JScrollPane`, `JSplitPane`, `JTabbedPane`, `JToolBar` | Medium |
| **Atomic Components (Leaves)** | Cannot contain other components; used for input/display | `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, `JCheckBox`, etc. | Very High |

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Every Major Swing Control

### 2.2.1 JLabel тАФ The Display Component

**Why it exists**: To display uneditable text, icons, or both. It is *passive* тАФ it does not generate events on its own.

**Key Methods (must memorize)**:
- `JLabel()` тАФ default constructor
- `JLabel(String text)` тАФ constructor with text
- `JLabel(Icon image)` тАФ constructor with icon
- `setText(String text)` / `getText()` тАФ to change/read the displayed text
- `setIcon(Icon icon)` / `getIcon()` тАФ to set/read the icon
- `setHorizontalAlignment(int alignment)` тАФ `SwingConstants.CENTER`, `LEFT`, `RIGHT`
- `setVerticalAlignment(int alignment)` тАФ `SwingConstants.TOP`, `CENTER`, `BOTTOM`

**Engineering Utility**: Form field captions, status bar messages, image banners in CMS systems.

### 2.2.2 JButton тАФ The Primary Action Trigger

**Why it exists**: A clickable button that fires an `ActionEvent` when pressed.

**Key Methods**:
- `JButton(String text)`
- `JButton(Icon icon)`
- `addActionListener(ActionListener l)` тАФ registers a listener
- `setEnabled(boolean b)` тАФ to enable/disable
- `setMnemonic(char mnemonic)` тАФ keyboard shortcut (Alt + key)
- `setToolTipText(String text)` тАФ hover hint

> [!NOTE]
> **Inheritance Note**: `JButton` extends `AbstractButton`, which is the abstract parent of `JToggleButton`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, and `JMenuItem`. So the methods like `setText()`, `setIcon()`, `setEnabled()` are inherited from `AbstractButton`.

### 2.2.3 JTextField тАФ Single-Line Input

**Why it exists**: To accept a single line of text input from the user (e.g., username, search box).

**Key Methods**:
- `JTextField(int columns)` тАФ preferred constructor
- `JTextField(String text, int columns)`
- `getText()` / `setText(String)`
- `addActionListener(ActionListener l)` тАФ fires when user presses Enter
- `setEditable(boolean)` тАФ true by default
- `setHorizontalAlignment(int)` тАФ for aligning displayed text

### 2.2.4 JTextArea тАФ Multi-Line Input

**Why it exists**: To accept or display **multiple lines** of text (e.g., comments, descriptions).

**Key Methods**:
- `JTextArea(int rows, int columns)`
- `append(String str)` тАФ adds text at the end
- `setLineWrap(boolean)` / `setWrapStyleWord(boolean)`
- `setEditable(boolean)`
- `insert(String str, int pos)`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap**: `JTextArea` does **not** support scrolling natively. You **must** wrap it inside a `JScrollPane` to get scrollbars:
> ```java
> JTextArea area = new JTextArea(5, 20);
> JScrollPane scroll = new JScrollPane(area);
> frame.add(scroll);
> ```

### 2.2.5 JCheckBox тАФ Boolean Toggle

**Why it exists**: To represent a binary on/off choice. Multiple checkboxes can be selected **independently** (unlike radio buttons).

**Key Methods**:
- `JCheckBox(String label, boolean selected)`
- `isSelected()` тАФ returns true if checked
- `setSelected(boolean b)`
- `addItemListener(ItemListener l)` тАФ fires when state changes
- Inherits from `AbstractButton` via `JToggleButton`

### 2.2.6 JRadioButton тАФ Mutually Exclusive Choice

**Why it exists**: To represent a choice where only **one** option in a group can be selected at a time (e.g., Male/Female/Other).

**Key Methods**:
- `JRadioButton(String label, boolean selected)`
- `isSelected()` / `setSelected(boolean)`
- `addItemListener(ItemListener l)`

> [!NOTE]
> **The `ButtonGroup` Class**: To make radio buttons mutually exclusive, you MUST add them to the same `ButtonGroup` object. A `ButtonGroup` is **not a visual component** тАФ it is purely a *logical grouping*. The radio buttons are still added to the container (e.g., `JPanel`) for display.

```java
JRadioButton male = new JRadioButton("Male");
JRadioButton female = new JRadioButton("Female");
ButtonGroup group = new ButtonGroup();
group.add(male);     // logical grouping
group.add(female);   // logical grouping
panel.add(male);     // visual display
panel.add(female);   // visual display
```

### 2.2.7 JComboBox тАФ Drop-Down List

**Why it exists**: To show a list of options that the user can select **one from**. Saves space when there are many options.

**Key Methods**:
- `JComboBox()` / `JComboBox(Object[] items)` / `JComboBox(Vector<?> items)`
- `getSelectedItem()` / `getSelectedIndex()`
- `addItem(Object item)` / `removeItem(Object)`
- `addItemListener(ItemListener l)` тАФ fires when selection changes
- `setEditable(boolean)` тАФ allows user to type their own value

### 2.2.8 JList тАФ Multi/Single Selection List

**Why it exists**: To display a scrollable list of items from which the user can select one or more. Unlike `JComboBox`, **multiple items are visible at once**.

**Key Methods**:
- `JList(Object[] listData)` / `JList(Vector<?> listData)`
- `getSelectedValue()` / `getSelectedValuesList()`
- `getSelectedIndex()` / `getSelectedIndices()`
- `setSelectionMode(int mode)` тАФ `ListSelectionModel.SINGLE_SELECTION`, `SINGLE_INTERVAL_SELECTION`, `MULTIPLE_INTERVAL_SELECTION`
- `addListSelectionListener(ListSelectionListener l)`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap**: `JList` **does not** provide scrollbars by default. You **must** wrap it in a `JScrollPane`.

### 2.2.9 JTable тАФ Tabular Data Display

**Why it exists**: To display data in a row-column format (like a spreadsheet).

**Key Constructors & Concepts**:
- `JTable(Object[][] rowData, Object[] columnNames)`
- `JTable(TableModel dm)` тАФ preferred for real applications
- The model is typically a `DefaultTableModel`
- Important related class: `JTableHeader` (for column headers)
- Default editors and renderers: `DefaultTableCellRenderer`, `DefaultCellEditor`

### 2.2.10 JMenu, JMenuBar, JMenuItem тАФ Application Menus

**Why it exists**: To provide a standardized menu bar at the top of an application.

**Class Roles**:
- `JMenuBar` тАФ the horizontal bar that holds menus. Set on a `JFrame` using `setJMenuBar(JMenuBar)`.
- `JMenu` тАФ a dropdown menu (e.g., "File", "Edit"). Added to a `JMenuBar`.
- `JMenuItem` тАФ an individual clickable item inside a `JMenu` (e.g., "Open", "Save").
- `JCheckBoxMenuItem` and `JRadioButtonMenuItem` тАФ special menu items.

```java
JMenuBar menuBar = new JMenuBar();
JMenu fileMenu = new JMenu("File");
JMenuItem openItem = new JMenuItem("Open");
fileMenu.add(openItem);
menuBar.add(fileMenu);
frame.setJMenuBar(menuBar);
```

### 2.2.11 Intermediate Containers

| Container | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| `JPanel` | Generic grouping; uses its own layout manager |
| `JScrollPane` | Provides scrollbars for any component |
| `JSplitPane` | Two components separated by a movable divider |
| `JTabbedPane` | Tabbed interface (like browser tabs) |
| `JToolBar` | Movable toolbar of buttons |
| `JOptionPane` | Pop-up dialogs (showMessageDialog, showInputDialog, showConfirmDialog) |

## 2.3 Event Handling Model тАФ The "Wiring" of Swing

**How it works (Why + How)**:

1. The user interacts with a control (e.g., clicks a button).
2. The control creates an **Event object** (e.g., `ActionEvent`).
3. The control sends the event to all **registered listeners**.
4. The listener's callback method is invoked, executing the response logic.

This is the **Delegation Event Model** тАФ the source delegates the task of handling the event to a separate listener.

**Common Event Listeners (Memorize the Mapping)**:

| Event Class | Listener Interface | Callback Method | Triggered By |
|-------------|-------------------|-----------------|--------------|
| `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` | `JButton` click, Enter in `JTextField`, `JMenuItem` |
| `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` | `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JComboBox` selection change |
| `TextEvent` | `TextListener` | `textValueChanged(TextEvent)` | `JTextField`, `JTextArea` text changes |
| `MouseEvent` | `MouseListener` | 5 methods: `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited` | Any mouse interaction |
| `KeyEvent` | `KeyListener` | `keyTyped`, `keyPressed`, `keyReleased` | Keyboard input |
| `ListSelectionEvent` | `ListSelectionListener` | `valueChanged(ListSelectionEvent)` | `JList` selection change |

**Two Ways to Register a Listener (Both are exam-relevant)**:

**Method 1: Separate Listener Class** (preferred for OOP design)
```java
class MyFrame extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JButton b;
    MyFrame() {
        b = new JButton("Click");
        b.addActionListener(this); // 'this' implements ActionListener
    }
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        // handle event
    }
}
```

**Method 2: Anonymous Inner Class** (concise, often used in examples)
```java
button.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Clicked!");
    }
});
```

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Table)

| Concept | Symbol / Construct | Purpose |
|---------|---------------------|---------|
| Swing package | `javax.swing` | Contains all Swing classes |
| Root class | `JComponent` | Base for all Swing controls |
| Top-level container | `JFrame` | Main window with title bar, minimize/maximize/close |
| BorderLayout regions | `NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`, `CENTER` | Default layout of `JFrame`'s content pane |
| Content Pane | `frame.getContentPane()` | Where components are added in `JFrame` (in Java < 5) |
| Default close | `JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE` | Constant for `setDefaultCloseOperation()` |
| Mnemonic shortcut | `setMnemonic(char)` | Alt + char activates the control |
| Tooltip | `setToolTipText(String)` | Hover text over a component |
| Radio grouping | `ButtonGroup` | Logical (not visual) grouping for mutual exclusion |
| Scrolling | `JScrollPane` | Required for `JTextArea` and `JList` |
| Menu bar setter | `setJMenuBar(JMenuBar)` | Attaches a menu bar to a `JFrame` |
| Event registration | `addXxxListener(XxxListener l)` | Subscribes a listener to a component |
| Event delegation | source тЖТ event object тЖТ listener | The Swing event model |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Applications**: Swing is the backbone of many internal tools, IDEs (e.g., older NetBeans versions), and ERP systems.
- **Scientific Visualization**: Tools like MATLAB's UI builder and ImageJ use Swing for cross-platform GUIs.
- **Banking Software**: Many legacy banking desktop clients use Swing for forms and data entry.
- **Educational Tools**: BlueJ IDE, used to teach OOP, is built on Swing.
- **SCADA / Industrial Control Panels**: Swing is used in Java-based control panels for machinery where cross-platform consistency is critical.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix тАФ Which Component for Which Job?

The single most common KTU exam question is: *"Given a scenario, choose the appropriate Swing component and justify."* This matrix is your master tool.

| Scenario / Requirement | Best Component | Reason |
|-------------------------|----------------|--------|
| Display static text under a text field | `JLabel` | Non-editable, no event generation |
| Single-line text input (e.g., Name) | `JTextField` | Optimized for one line |
| Multi-line text input (e.g., Comments) | `JTextArea` inside `JScrollPane` | Multi-line support + scrollbars |
| Click to submit a form | `JButton` | Standard trigger for actions |
| Multiple independent choices (Hobbies) | `JCheckBox` | Each is independent; can select any/all |
| Choose one gender from M/F/Other | `JRadioButton` + `ButtonGroup` | Mutually exclusive |
| Choose one country from a long list | `JComboBox` | Saves space, dropdown UI |
| Display a list of 100 student names with multi-select | `JList` inside `JScrollPane` | Multiple visible items + scroll + multi-select |
| Display data in rows and columns | `JTable` | Tabular representation |
| Tree of folders | `JTree` | Hierarchical data |
| Top-of-window menu (File, Edit) | `JMenuBar` тЖТ `JMenu` тЖТ `JMenuItem` | Standard OS-like menu bar |
| Show a small pop-up message | `JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(...)` | Modal dialog utility |
| Display a status bar that can be re-styled | `JLabel` with custom border | Lightweight, no event cost |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Building a Swing Application (The "Five-Step" Method)

Every Swing program you write for KTU exams should follow this canonical pattern. I will derive it step-by-step.

### **Step 1: Import the Required Packages**

```java
import javax.swing.*;          // All Swing classes
import java.awt.*;             // AWT classes (Layouts, Color, Font)
import java.awt.event.*;       // Event classes and listeners
```

**Explanation**: The wildcard `*` imports all classes from these packages. This is acceptable in exam code for brevity.

### **Step 2: Choose the Top-Level Container**

```java
public class MyApp extends JFrame {
    MyApp() {
        setTitle("My Application");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());  // default; can change
    }
}
```

**Explanation**:
- `extends JFrame` тАФ inherits windowing behavior.
- `setSize(400, 300)` тАФ width ├Ч height in pixels.
- `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` тАФ closes the JVM on window close. Without this, the window closes but the program continues running. The four valid constants are:
  - `WindowConstants.DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE` (0)
  - `WindowConstants.HIDE_ON_CLOSE` (1)
  - `WindowConstants.DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` (2)
  - `WindowConstants.EXIT_ON_CLOSE` (3)

### **Step 3: Create the Components**

```java
JLabel nameLabel = new JLabel("Name:");
JTextField nameField = new JTextField(15);
JButton submitBtn = new JButton("Submit");
```

**Explanation**: `JTextField(15)` sets the preferred width to 15 columns, which determines the field's pixel width roughly as `15 ├Ч averageCharWidth`.

### **Step 4: Add Components to a Container**

```java
JPanel panel = new JPanel();
panel.add(nameLabel);
panel.add(nameField);
panel.add(submitBtn);
add(panel); // adds panel to the JFrame's content pane
```

**Explanation**:
- We group components into a `JPanel` (which uses `FlowLayout` by default).
- The `JPanel` is then added to the `JFrame`.
- Adding the panel to the `JFrame` is equivalent to `getContentPane().add(panel)`. In Java 5+, this is automatic.

### **Step 5: Register Event Listeners and Make Visible**

```java
submitBtn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String name = nameField.getText();
        JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(null, "Hello, " + name);
    }
});

setVisible(true);
```

**Explanation**:
- `setVisible(true)` MUST be called at the end; otherwise, the window never appears.
- The anonymous inner class implements `ActionListener`. The `actionPerformed` method is the callback that runs when the button is clicked.

## 3.3 Complete Reference Program тАФ A Login Form

Here is a complete, exam-ready program that synthesizes everything. Each line is annotated for KTU study purposes.

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class LoginForm extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    // 1. Declare all components
    JLabel userLabel, passLabel, msgLabel;
    JTextField userField;
    JPasswordField passField;  // Special: hides characters
    JButton loginBtn, resetBtn;
    JPanel panel;

    // 2. Constructor
    LoginForm() {
        setTitle("Login Form");
        setSize(350, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        // 3. Initialize components
        userLabel  = new JLabel("Username:");
        passLabel  = new JLabel("Password:");
        userField  = new JTextField(15);
        passField  = new JPasswordField(15);
        loginBtn   = new JButton("Login");
        resetBtn   = new JButton("Reset");
        msgLabel   = new JLabel("");

        // 4. Register listeners
        loginBtn.addActionListener(this);
        resetBtn.addActionListener(this);

        // 5. Layout
        panel = new JPanel(new GridLayout(4, 2, 5, 5));
        panel.add(userLabel);   panel.add(userField);
        panel.add(passLabel);   panel.add(passField);
        panel.add(loginBtn);    panel.add(resetBtn);
        panel.add(new JLabel("")); panel.add(msgLabel);

        add(panel);
        setLocationRelativeTo(null); // Center on screen
        setVisible(true);
    }

    // 6. Event handler
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        if (e.getSource() == loginBtn) {
            String user = userField.getText();
            String pass = new String(passField.getPassword());
            if (user.equals("admin") && pass.equals("1234"))
                msgLabel.setText("Login Successful!");
            else
                msgLabel.setText("Invalid Credentials");
        } else if (e.getSource() == resetBtn) {
            userField.setText("");
            passField.setText("");
            msgLabel.setText("");
        }
    }

    // 7. Main method
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new LoginForm();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Walkthrough of the Above Program

**Why we used `JPasswordField`**: A `JTextField` would display the password in plain text. `JPasswordField` masks it with `тЧП` characters and stores it in a `char[]` (not `String`) for security.

**Why we used `GridLayout(4, 2, 5, 5)`**: This creates a 4-row, 2-column grid with 5px horizontal and 5px vertical gaps. Each cell is the same size тАФ perfect for label/field pairs.

**Why we used `setLocationRelativeTo(null)`**: This centers the window on the screen. The `null` argument means "center relative to the screen" (the default toolkit screen).

**Why we convert `passField.getPassword()` to `String`**: `getPassword()` returns `char[]` (a security feature тАФ `String` is immutable and can be snooped in the String pool). For our simple comparison, we wrap it in `new String(char[])`.

**Why we used `e.getSource()`**: When one listener handles events from multiple sources, we use `e.getSource()` to identify *which* component fired the event. It returns the source object, which we compare using `==`.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison: AWT vs. Swing

KTU frequently asks: *"Differentiate between AWT and Swing."* Here is the master comparison:

| Feature | AWT (`java.awt`) | Swing (`javax.swing`) |
|---------|------------------|------------------------|
| Package | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |
| Component type | Heavyweight (uses OS peers) | Lightweight (pure Java) |
| Look & Feel | Same as native OS | Pluggable; can mimic any OS or use Metal |
| Component count | Limited | Rich (more components like `JTable`, `JTree`) |
| Root class | `java.awt.Component` | `javax.swing.JComponent` (extends `Component`) |
| MVC | No | Yes |
| Buttons | `Button` | `JButton` (and many more) |
| Pluggable | No | Yes |
| Performance | Faster (native code) | Slightly slower (pure Java) |
| Modern recommendation | Legacy | Preferred |

## 3.5 Layout Manager Reference (Important)

A control's position is determined by the **layout manager** of its container. Choosing the right layout is half the battle in Swing exams.

| Layout | Class | Behavior | Best Used For |
|--------|-------|----------|---------------|
| Flow | `FlowLayout` | Components left-to-right, wrap to next line | Toolbars, button rows |
| Border | `BorderLayout` | Five regions: N, S, E, W, C | Main window structure |
| Grid | `GridLayout` | Equal-sized cells in rows ├Ч columns | Forms, calculators |
| Box | `BoxLayout` | Vertical or horizontal stacking | Side panels |
| GridBag | `GridBagLayout` | Complex, flexible grid with constraints | Professional UIs |
| Card | `CardLayout` | Stack of panels; show one at a time | Wizards, tab-like flows |
| Group | `GroupLayout` | Preferred by NetBeans GUI Builder | Designer-generated UIs |
| Spring | `SpringLayout` | Constraint-based | Advanced custom layouts |
| null | (no layout) | Absolute positioning with `setBounds(x,y,w,h)` | Quick demos only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap**: If you use `setLayout(null)`, you **must** also call `setBounds(x, y, width, height)` on every component. Otherwise, the component has size 0 and won't appear.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Swing Class Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

This is the most important diagram for KTU exams. It shows the inheritance chain of all major Swing controls.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Object] --> B[Component]
    B --> C[Container]
    C --> D[JComponent]
    
    D --> E[JLabel]
    D --> F[AbstractButton]
    D --> G[JTextComponent]
    D --> H[JComboBox]
    D --> I[JList]
    D --> J[JTable]
    D --> K[JTree]
    D --> L[JPanel]
    D --> M[JMenuBar]
    D --> N[JScrollPane]
    D --> O[JOptionPane]
    D --> P[JTabbedPane]
    
    F --> F1[JButton]
    F --> F2[JToggleButton]
    F --> F3[JMenuItem]
    F --> F4[JCheckBoxMenuItem]
    F --> F5[JRadioButtonMenuItem]
    
    F2 --> F6[JCheckBox]
    F2 --> F7[JRadioButton]
    
    F3 --> F8[JMenu]
    F3 --> F9[JCheckBoxMenuItem]
    
    G --> G1[JTextField]
    G --> G2[JTextArea]
    G --> G3[JEditorPane]
    G --> G4[JPasswordField]
```

## 4.2 Delegation Event Model Flow

This shows how events flow from the user through a Swing control to the listener.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[User Action<br/>Click, Type, Select] --> B[Swing Component<br/>JButton, JTextField]
    B --> C{Event Dispatch<br/>Thread EDT}
    C --> D[Create Event Object<br/>ActionEvent, ItemEvent]
    D --> E[Invoke Registered<br/>Listener Method]
    E --> F[Execute Callback<br/>actionPerformed, itemStateChanged]
    F --> G[Update UI /<br/>Process Data]
```

## 4.3 JFrame Internal Architecture

A `JFrame` is more complex than it looks. It contains a layered structure.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[JFrame] --> B[JRootPane]
    B --> C[glassPane]
    B --> D[contentPane]
    B --> E[layeredPane]
    E --> F[JMenuBar]
    E --> G[Content Layer]
    E --> H[Palette Layer]
    E --> I[Modal Layer]
    E --> J[Popup Layer]
    E --> K[Drag Layer]
    
    A -.->|setJMenuBar| F
    A -.->|add| G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for exams**: When you call `frame.add(component)`, the component is actually added to the `contentPane`, not directly to the frame. Similarly, `setJMenuBar()` adds the menu bar to the `layeredPane`. Knowing this helps you answer tricky questions about component lookup and event propagation.

## 4.4 Component Selection Decision Tree

Use this flowchart to determine which Swing control to use for a given UI requirement.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[New UI Requirement] --> B{Editable?}
    B -->|No| C[Display Purpose?]
    B -->|Yes| D{One line or Multi?}
    C -->|Text| E[JLabel]
    C -->|Icon| E
    C -->|Text+Icon| E
    
    D -->|One line| F{Sensitive data?}
    D -->|Multi| G[JTextArea + JScrollPane]
    F -->|Yes| H[JPasswordField]
    F -->|No| I[JTextField]
    
    A --> J{Trigger action?}
    J -->|Yes| K[JButton]
    J -->|No| L{Selection type?}
    L -->|Multiple independent| M[JCheckBox]
    L -->|Mutually exclusive| N[JRadioButton + ButtonGroup]
    L -->|One from many| O[JComboBox]
    L -->|Multi from many visible| P[JList + JScrollPane]
    
    A --> Q{Data shape?}
    Q -->|Tabular| R[JTable]
    Q -->|Hierarchical| S[JTree]
    Q -->|Sequential list| T[JList]
    
    A --> U{Top-of-window bar?}
    U -->|Yes| V[JMenuBar -> JMenu -> JMenuItem]
```

## 4.5 Layout Manager Behavior Visual

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Container's setLayout] --> B[FlowLayout]
    A --> C[BorderLayout]
    A --> D[GridLayout]
    A --> E[BoxLayout]
    A --> F[null - absolute]
    
    B --> B1[Components placed L-to-R, wrap when full]
    C --> C1[Five regions: N S E W CENTER]
    D --> D1[Equal cells in R rows x C cols]
    E --> E1[Single row or column stack]
    F --> F1[setBounds x y width height required]
```
