# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
*What is the difference between `java.awt` and `javax.swing` packages in Java?*

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Aspect | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |
|--------|-----------|---------------|
| Component weight | Heavyweight (uses native OS peer) | Lightweight (pure Java) |
| Base class | `java.awt.Component` | `javax.swing.JComponent` |
| Look & Feel | Tied to OS | Pluggable (PLAF) |
| MVC | Not followed | Follows MVC |
| Component set | Limited | Rich and extended |

A `JButton` (Swing) is preferred over a `Button` (AWT) because it is platform-independent, supports icons and HTML labels, and can change its appearance dynamically.

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
*Explain the role of `JScrollPane` with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

`JScrollPane` is an intermediate Swing container that provides **horizontal and/or vertical scrollbars** to any component that is too large to fit in its allocated area. It is essential for components that display variable-length content, such as `JTextArea`, `JList`, `JTable`, and `JTree`.

**Example**:
```java
JTextArea area = new JTextArea(10, 30);
JScrollPane scroll = new JScrollPane(area);  // wrap area inside scroll
frame.add(scroll);
```
Without `JScrollPane`, the `JTextArea` would be fixed in size and overflow text would be invisible. With it, scrollbars appear automatically when content exceeds the visible area.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each тАФ Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain the Delegation Event Model in Java Swing with a suitable diagram. List any four event listener interfaces from `java.awt.event`.

**(b) (7 marks)** Write a Java Swing program to create a form that accepts a student's name, age, and gender (using radio buttons). On clicking a "Display" button, show all entered details in a `JOptionPane` message dialog.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Delegation Event Model** is the event-handling mechanism used by Swing (and modern AWT). It works on the principle that **the source of an event delegates the responsibility of handling it to a separate listener object**.

**Step-by-step flow**:
1. The user interacts with a Swing component (e.g., clicks a `JButton`).
2. The component (the **source**) constructs an `Event` object (e.g., `ActionEvent`) containing details such as the source, the command, and any modifiers.
3. The component fires the event by passing it to all **registered listeners**.
4. The listener's corresponding callback method (e.g., `actionPerformed`) is invoked, executing the response logic.

**Diagram**:
```
   User Action
       тЖУ
   Component (Source)  --fires-->  Event Object
       тЖУ
   Registered Listeners
       тЖУ
   Callback Method Executes
       тЖУ
   Response Logic
```

**Four Event Listener Interfaces from `java.awt.event`**:
1. **`ActionListener`** тАФ interface with `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` method. Used by `JButton`, `JMenuItem`, `JTextField` (on Enter).
2. **`ItemListener`** тАФ interface with `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` method. Used by `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JComboBox`.
3. **`MouseListener`** тАФ interface with 5 methods: `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited`. Used by any component.
4. **`KeyListener`** тАФ interface with 3 methods: `keyTyped`, `keyPressed`, `keyReleased`. Used for keyboard handling.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class StudentForm extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JLabel nameLbl, ageLbl, genderLbl;
    JTextField nameField, ageField;
    JRadioButton male, female, other;
    JButton displayBtn;
    JPanel panel;

    StudentForm() {
        setTitle("Student Form");
        setSize(350, 250);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        nameLbl  = new JLabel("Name:");
        nameField = new JTextField(15);
        ageLbl   = new JLabel("Age:");
        ageField = new JTextField(15);
        genderLbl = new JLabel("Gender:");

        male   = new JRadioButton("Male");
        female = new JRadioButton("Female");
        other  = new JRadioButton("Other");

        ButtonGroup bg = new ButtonGroup();
        bg.add(male); bg.add(female); bg.add(other);

        displayBtn = new JButton("Display");
        displayBtn.addActionListener(this);

        add(nameLbl);  add(nameField);
        add(ageLbl);   add(ageField);
        add(genderLbl);
        add(male);     add(female);  add(other);
        add(displayBtn);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String name = nameField.getText();
        String age  = ageField.getText();
        String gender = male.isSelected() ? "Male" :
                        female.isSelected() ? "Female" :
                        other.isSelected() ? "Other" : "Not specified";
        JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(this,
            "Name: " + name + "\nAge: " + age + "\nGender: " + gender);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new StudentForm();
    }
}
```

**Key Points (worth 7 marks)**:
- `JFrame`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, `JRadioButton`, `JButton`, `JOptionPane` correctly used.
- `ButtonGroup` correctly created to make radio buttons mutually exclusive.
- `addActionListener(this)` registers the form (which implements `ActionListener`) as the listener.
- The ternary operator correctly identifies which radio is selected.
- `JOptionPane.showMessageDialog` displays a modal dialog.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 тАФ Alternative Choice]

**(a) (7 marks)** Differentiate between `JTextField` and `JTextArea`. Explain the role of `JScrollPane` with examples.

**(b) (7 marks)** Write a Java Swing program that uses a `JComboBox` containing a list of 5 programming languages. When the user selects an item, the program should display a brief description of the selected language in a `JLabel`.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

| Feature | `JTextField` | `JTextArea` |
|---------|--------------|-------------|
| Lines accepted | Single line only | Multiple lines |
| Typical use | Name, email, search box | Comments, description, log |
| Scrolling | Not needed | Requires `JScrollPane` |
| `setLineWrap` | Not applicable | Available |
| Inheritance | `JTextComponent` | `JTextComponent` |
| Default rows/cols | Specified by columns | Specified by rows ├Ч columns |

**Role of `JScrollPane`**:
`JScrollPane` is an intermediate container that provides **automatic scrollbars** for components whose content exceeds the visible area. It is **mandatory** for `JTextArea` (multi-line) and `JList` (multi-item).

**Example with `JTextArea`**:
```java
JTextArea area = new JTextArea(5, 20);
area.setLineWrap(true);
JScrollPane sp = new JScrollPane(area);  // wrap to get scrollbars
frame.add(sp);
```

**Example with `JList`**:
```java
String[] data = {"Apple", "Banana", "Cherry", "Date", "Elderberry"};
JList<String> list = new JList<>(data);
JScrollPane sp = new JScrollPane(list);  // mandatory
frame.add(sp);
```

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class LanguageInfo extends JFrame implements ItemListener {
    JComboBox<String> langBox;
    JLabel descLabel;

    LanguageInfo() {
        setTitle("Programming Language Info");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        String[] langs = {"Java", "Python", "C++", "JavaScript", "Ruby"};
        langBox = new JComboBox<>(langs);
        langBox.addItemListener(this);

        descLabel = new JLabel("Select a language to see its description.");
        descLabel.setPreferredSize(new Dimension(380, 60));
        descLabel.setVerticalAlignment(SwingConstants.TOP);

        add(new JLabel("Choose Language:"));
        add(langBox);
        add(descLabel);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e) {
        if (e.getStateChange() == ItemEvent.SELECTED) {
            String lang = (String) langBox.getSelectedItem();
            String desc;
            switch (lang) {
                case "Java":     desc = "Java: Object-oriented, platform-independent, used in enterprise."; break;
                case "Python":   desc = "Python: High-level, dynamic, used in AI/ML and scripting."; break;
                case "C++":      desc = "C++: High-performance, systems programming, OOP."; break;
                case "JavaScript": desc = "JavaScript: Web scripting, frontend and Node.js backend."; break;
                case "Ruby":     desc = "Ruby: Dynamic, elegant syntax, used in Rails web framework."; break;
                default:         desc = "No description available.";
            }
            descLabel.setText(desc);
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new LanguageInfo();
    }
}
```

**Key Points (worth 7 marks)**:
- `JComboBox<String>` uses generics to specify the type of items.
- `ItemListener` is the correct listener for `JComboBox` selection change.
- The check `e.getStateChange() == ItemEvent.SELECTED` filters out `DESELECTED` events (which fire twice for each change in some swing versions).
- `getSelectedItem()` returns `Object` (cast to `String` here).
- The `switch` statement is Java 7+ syntax, fully supported in KTU 2024.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting `setVisible(true)`**: If a student writes a complete program but forgets `setVisible(true)`, the window never shows. **Lose 1 mark**.
> 
> 2. **Not wrapping `JTextArea` in `JScrollPane`**: When asked for a multi-line input, students often add the `JTextArea` directly to the frame. The examiner expects scrollbars. **Lose 1 mark**.
> 
> 3. **Confusing `WindowListener` with `ActionListener`**: For a button click, the answer is `ActionListener` + `actionPerformed`, NOT `WindowListener` (which is for window-level events like open/close).
> 
> 4. **Confusing `JFrame`'s `add()` and `setJMenuBar()`**: Adding a `JMenu` to a `JFrame` using `add()` will not display the menu at the top тАФ only `setJMenuBar(JMenuBar)` does. **Lose 2 marks** on menu questions.
> 
> 5. **Missing `ButtonGroup` for RadioButtons**: If you show radio buttons but no `ButtonGroup`, they will behave like checkboxes (multiple can be selected). **Lose 1.5 marks**.
> 
> 6. **Using `==` to compare strings**: A common bug. Use `.equals()` for `String` comparison. **Lose 1 mark**.
> 
> 7. **Confusing `getText()` from `JTextField` vs. `JPasswordField`**: For `JPasswordField`, you MUST use `getPassword()` (returns `char[]`), not `getText()`. The `getText()` method is deprecated for `JPasswordField` for security reasons.
> 
> 8. **Forgetting `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`**: Without this, clicking the close button hides the window but the JVM keeps running, and the program appears to hang.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### **Quick-Recall Bullet Checklist**

- **Package**: `javax.swing` тАФ the package to import for all Swing components.
- **Root Class**: `JComponent` тАФ all Swing controls (except `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet`) extend this.
- **Top-Level Containers**: `JFrame` (main window), `JDialog` (pop-up), `JApplet` (browser). Only these are heavyweight.
- **Lightweight vs. Heavyweight**: Swing is lightweight (pure Java); AWT is heavyweight (OS peers).
- **Pluggable Look and Feel (PLAF)**: A key advantage of Swing тАФ change appearance without changing code.
- **JLabel**: For displaying text/icons; not editable; no events generated on its own.
- **JButton**: Clickable button; fires `ActionEvent`; `addActionListener()` to handle.
- **JTextField**: Single-line text input; `addActionListener` fires on Enter key.
- **JTextArea**: Multi-line text input; **MUST** be wrapped in `JScrollPane` for scrollbars.
- **JPasswordField**: Special text field that masks input; use `getPassword()` (returns `char[]`), not `getText()`.
- **JCheckBox**: Independent toggle; use `isSelected()` to check state; fires `ItemEvent`.
- **JRadioButton**: Mutually exclusive; **MUST** be added to a `ButtonGroup` for mutual exclusion.
- **ButtonGroup**: A *logical* (not visual) group; does not display; holds radio buttons for grouping.
- **JComboBox**: Drop-down list; saves space; fires `ItemEvent` on selection change.
- **JList**: Visible scrollable list; **MUST** be in `JScrollPane`; supports multi-select via `setSelectionMode()`.
- **JTable**: Tabular data display; use `DefaultTableModel` for real applications.
- **JMenuBar**: The top horizontal bar; set on a frame with `setJMenuBar(JMenuBar)`.
- **JMenu**: A dropdown menu inside a `JMenuBar` (e.g., "File", "Edit").
- **JMenuItem**: An individual clickable item inside a `JMenu` (e.g., "Open", "Save").
- **Delegation Event Model**: The source creates an event and delegates handling to a registered listener.
- **ActionListener**: `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` тАФ for buttons, menu items, Enter in text fields.
- **ItemListener**: `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` тАФ for checkboxes, radio buttons, combo boxes.
- **Layout Managers**: `FlowLayout` (default for `JPanel`), `BorderLayout` (default for `JFrame`'s content pane), `GridLayout`, `BoxLayout`, `GridBagLayout`.
- **setLayout(null)**: Disables the layout manager; you must call `setBounds(x, y, width, height)` on every component.
- **setVisible(true)**: Must be called last in the constructor for the window to appear.
- **setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)**: Closes the JVM on window close. Without it, the program hangs.
- **EDT (Event Dispatch Thread)**: All Swing GUI operations should occur on this thread for thread safety.
- **MVC**: Swing components follow Model-View-Controller; model holds data, view displays, controller handles input.
- **getContentPane()**: In Java < 5, you had to call this to add components to a `JFrame`. From Java 5+, `add()` on the frame is automatic.
- **addXxxListener(XxxListener l)**: The general pattern for registering any event listener in Swing.
- **Anonymous Inner Class**: A common, concise way to register a listener inline, e.g., `button.addActionListener(new ActionListener() { ... })`.
- **Lambda Expressions**: In Java 8+, `button.addActionListener(e -> { ... })` is the modern way; allowed in KTU 2024 scheme.
- **JOptionPane**: Static methods `showMessageDialog`, `showInputDialog`, `showConfirmDialog` for pop-up dialogs.
- **setLocationRelativeTo(null)**: Centers the window on the screen.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components and Containers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Components and Containers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ЁЯУШ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the Java Abstract Window Toolkit (**AWT**) and **Swing** frameworks, every visual element that the user can see and interact with on a Graphical User Interface (GUI) is fundamentally built upon two cornerstone abstract classes: **`Component`** and **`Container`**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official KTU Definition:**
> - **Component:** An abstract class (`java.awt.Component`) that serves as the *superclass* for all GUI-related classes which can be displayed on the screen and can interact with the user. Examples include `Button`, `Label`, `TextField`, `Checkbox`, `List`, etc.
> - **Container:** A specialized subclass of `Component` that has the unique ability to *hold and organize other components* (including other containers). A container provides a spatial layout mechanism for the components added to it. Examples include `Frame`, `Dialog`, `Panel`, and `Applet`.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition ЁЯОи

Think of building a GUI like assembling a **physical office desk**:

| Real-World Object | Java GUI Equivalent |
|------------------|---------------------|
| The desk itself | The **Frame** (top-level window) |
| Drawers, trays, folders on the desk | **Components** (Button, Label, TextField) |
| The desktop organizer holding pens, papers, etc. | **Container** (Panel) |
| The "how things are arranged" rule (rows, columns, grid) | **Layout Manager** (FlowLayout, GridLayout) |

Just as a desk has multiple compartments that hold objects, a **Container holds Components**. You cannot place a button directly onto the screen тАФ you must place it inside a container (like a `Frame` or `Panel`), which itself is placed on the screen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Hierarchy Insight:** A `Container` **IS-A** `Component` (it inherits from it). This means a container can be placed *inside* another container! This enables nested, hierarchical UI construction.

## 1.3 Key Physical/Conceptual Constants

- The base class is **`java.awt.Component`** in AWT (and `javax.swing.JComponent` in Swing).
- Every Java GUI program must inherit from either **`java.applet.Applet`** (deprecated) or top-level container classes like **`java.awt.Frame`** / **`javax.swing.JFrame`**.
- The `main()` method of a standalone GUI application typically ends with the standard exit code value of **0**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ЁЯУШ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Component Class Hierarchy (AWT)

The `Component` class is the **root of all visual AWT classes**. Below is the structural breakdown:

### ЁЯУВ Class Hierarchy Breakdown

- **`java.awt.Component`** (Abstract Class)
  - **Container** (Abstract Subclass тАФ can hold other components)
    - `Panel` (Generic container, no title bar)
      - `Applet` (Embedded in web/browser тАФ deprecated)
    - `Window` (Top-level window, no title bar/borders)
      - `Frame` (Top-level window WITH title bar & borders)
        - `Dialog` (Pop-up window for messages)
    - `ScrollPane` (Container with scrollable region)
  - **Non-Container Components** (Visual elements, cannot hold others)
    - `Button`, `Label`, `TextField`, `TextArea`
    - `Checkbox`, `Choice`, `List`
    - `Canvas` (Custom drawing surface)

## 2.2 Container тАФ The "Holder" of Components

A `Container` object has the exclusive ability to invoke the **`add()`** method to insert other components. The signature is:

$$ \text{Component add(\text{Component comp})} $$

The container is responsible for **layout management** тАФ deciding the position and size of every added component based on the **LayoutManager** it is assigned.

## 2.3 Layout Managers тАФ The Arrangement Rules

A Layout Manager is an object that implements the `java.awt.LayoutManager` interface and governs component placement.

| Layout Manager | Arrangement Strategy | Best Used For |
|---------------|---------------------|---------------|
| `FlowLayout` | Left-to-right, wraps to next line | Toolbars, button rows |
| `BorderLayout` | 5 regions: North, South, East, West, Center | App frames, dialogs |
| `GridLayout` | Equal-sized cells in a grid | Calculators, keypads |
| `GridBagLayout` | Flexible grid with varying cell sizes | Complex forms |
| `CardLayout` | Stacked cards, one visible at a time | Tabbed wizards, slideshows |
| `null` (Absolute) | Manual `setBounds(x, y, w, h)` | Pixel-perfect custom UIs |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following **must-know** methods for KTU exams:

| Method | Purpose | Container/Component |
|--------|---------|---------------------|
| `add(Component c)` | Adds component to container | Container |
| `setLayout(LayoutManager m)` | Sets layout strategy | Container |
| `setSize(int w, int h)` | Sets dimensions in pixels | Component |
| `setBounds(int x, int y, int w, int h)` | Sets position & size | Component |
| `setTitle(String s)` | Sets window title | Frame |
| `setVisible(boolean b)` | Shows/hides component | Component |
| `setBackground(Color c)` | Sets background color | Component |
| `setForeground(Color c)` | Sets foreground (text) color | Component |
| `addWindowListener(WindowListener l)` | Handles window events | Window |
| `validate()` | Re-lays out container | Container |
| `getContentPane().add(...)` | Adds to frame's content area (Swing) | JFrame |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

GUI components and containers are the **backbone of every desktop, web, and mobile application**. From the ATM machine you use, to the cockpit of an aircraft, to the SCADA dashboards in power plants тАФ every interactive system is built upon this exact **ComponentтАУContainer paradigm**. Mastering it equips you to design any visual interface.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ЁЯУШ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The "Why" Behind Container Composition тАФ Analytical Breakdown

### Step 1: Why does `Container` extend `Component`?

**Reasoning:** Because a container itself *must be displayable* on the screen. Since a Frame is a Container and we want to *see* the Frame, the Frame must inherit all the display-related behavior (`setSize`, `setVisible`, `setBackground`) from `Component`. This enables **nested composition**: a `Panel` (a Container) can be added *into* a `Frame` (also a Container), forming a tree structure.

### Step 2: The "add() Method" Decision Logic

When you call `container.add(button)`, the following sequence executes internally:

1. The container **stores a reference** to the `button` in its internal data structure (a list/array of components).
2. The container's **current `LayoutManager`** is consulted.
3. The layout manager **calculates** the position and size of the button based on the container's current dimensions and the layout's rules.
4. The button is **rendered** on screen at the calculated coordinates.

### Step 3: The Layout Manager Equation (Generalized)

For a container of width $W$ and height $H$, with $n$ components, the position of the $i$-th component under different layouts is given by:

$$ \text{Position}_i = f(W, H, n, \text{layoutRule}) $$

#### Worked Example: FlowLayout Positioning

For a `FlowLayout` with horizontal gap $g_h$ and vertical gap $g_v$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_i &= x_0 + (i \cdot (w_i + g_h)) \mod W_{\text{row}} \\
y_i &= y_0 + (\text{rowIndex}_i \cdot (h_{\max} + g_v))
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $x_0, y_0$ = origin offset (usually 5 pixels)
- $w_i$ = width of component $i$
- $h_{\max}$ = maximum height in the current row
- $\text{rowIndex}_i$ = row number of component $i$

#### Worked Example: GridLayout Cell Calculation

For a `GridLayout` with $R$ rows and $C$ columns inside a container of size $(W, H)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{cellWidth} &= \frac{W - (C-1) \cdot g_h}{C} \\
\text{cellHeight} &= \frac{H - (R-1) \cdot g_v}{R} \\
\text{cellRow}_i &= \left\lfloor \frac{i}{C} \right\rfloor \\
\text{cellCol}_i &= i \mod C
\end{aligned}
$$

This means for cell index $i = 0, 1, 2, \dots, n-1$, you can compute the exact pixel position of any component on the grid.

## 3.2 The Complete GUI Construction тАФ Analytical Matrix

| Step | Action | Code | Why |
|------|--------|------|-----|
| 1 | Create the top-level container | `Frame f = new Frame("MyApp");` | Frame is a visible top-level Container |
| 2 | Set the layout manager | `f.setLayout(new FlowLayout());` | Defines how children will be arranged |
| 3 | Create child components | `Button b = new Button("OK");` | Components are the interactive elements |
| 4 | Add children to container | `f.add(b);` | Container holds and displays them |
| 5 | Set container size | `f.setSize(400, 300);` | Defines window dimensions in pixels |
| 6 | Make it visible | `f.setVisible(true);` | Renders it on screen |

## 3.3 The Swing Equivalent тАФ Content Pane Concept

In **Swing**, a `JFrame` does not directly accept `add()`. Instead, you must use:

$$ \text{frame.getContentPane().add(\text{component})} $$

**Why?** A `JFrame` is composed of multiple layers (root pane, layered pane, glass pane, content pane). The **content pane** is the actual `Container` that holds your visual components. The reasoning: this layered architecture enables advanced features like menu bars, tool tips, and pop-up dialogs to be rendered on separate layers without interfering with your main UI.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# ЁЯУШ SECTION_4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Component-Class Hierarchy (AWT) тАФ Visual Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Object] --> B[Component]
    B --> C[Container]
    B --> D[Button]
    B --> E[Label]
    B --> F[TextField]
    B --> G[Checkbox]
    B --> H[List]
    B --> I[Choice]
    B --> J[Canvas]
    C --> K[Panel]
    C --> L[Window]
    C --> M[ScrollPane]
    K --> N[Applet]
    L --> O[Frame]
    L --> P[Dialog]
    O --> Q[MyApp]
    K --> R[MyPanel]
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Btn1]
    R --> T[Btn2]
    R --> U[Btn3]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: GUI Composition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Screen] --> B[Frame Top-Level Container]
    B --> C{LayoutManager}
    C -->|FlowLayout| D[Left to Right Row]
    C -->|BorderLayout| E[5 Region Map]
    C -->|GridLayout| F[Equal Cell Grid]
    C -->|CardLayout| G[Stacked Cards]
    D --> H[Component 1]
    D --> I[Component 2]
    D --> J[Component 3]
    E --> K[North]
    E --> L[South]
    E --> M[East]
    E --> N[West]
    E --> O[Center]
    F --> P[Cell 1]
    F --> Q[Cell 2]
    F --> R[Cell 3]
    F --> S[Cell 4]
    G --> T[Card A]
    G --> U[Card B]
```

## 4.3 Container-Component Containment Model

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Frame] --> B[Panel North]
    A --> C[Panel Center]
    A --> D[Panel South]
    B --> E[Label Title]
    B --> F[TextField Input]
    C --> G[TextArea Output]
    C --> H[Button Submit]
    D --> I[Button Cancel]
    D --> J[Button Reset]
```

## 4.4 The `add()` Method Execution Pipeline

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dev as Developer
    participant Cont as Container
    participant LM as LayoutManager
    participant OS as Operating System
    Dev->>Cont: add(button)
    Cont->>Cont: store reference internally
    Cont->>LM: request layout calculation
    LM->>LM: compute x, y, width, height
    LM-->>Cont: return position data
    Cont->>OS: request redraw
    OS-->>Dev: visible on screen
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# ЁЯУШ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## ЁЯУЭ Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]

**Define the terms Component and Container in Java AWT. How are they related?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Component** is an abstract class in `java.awt` that is the superclass of all graphical user interface elements which can be displayed on the screen and can interact with the user. Examples include `Button`, `Label`, `TextField`, and `Checkbox`. **(1 mark)**
>
> A **Container** is a subclass of `Component` that has the unique capability of holding and organizing other components (including other containers) by providing a spatial layout. Examples include `Frame`, `Dialog`, and `Panel`. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Relationship:** A Container **IS-A** Component (it inherits from it), which means containers themselves can be displayed on the screen and can also contain other containers, enabling a nested, hierarchical UI tree. **(1 mark)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024]

**List any three LayoutManager classes in Java AWT and state the arrangement strategy of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Layout Manager | Strategy (1 mark each) |
|---------------|------------------------|
| `FlowLayout` | Arranges components left-to-right in rows, wrapping to the next row when the container width is exceeded. |
| `BorderLayout` | Divides the container into 5 regions: North, South, East, West, and Center. |
| `GridLayout` | Arranges components in a grid of equal-sized cells defined by rows and columns. |

---

## ЁЯУЭ Part B Questions (14 Marks Each тАФ Internal Choice)

### ЁЯФ╣ Question A (14 Marks)

#### Q3a. [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023] (7 marks)

**Explain the AWT Component class hierarchy with a neat diagram. Differentiate between Panel and Frame.**

**Model Answer:**

The AWT Component class hierarchy is rooted at `java.awt.Component` (abstract class). The key branches are:

1. **`Container` (abstract)** тАУ A subclass of Component that can hold other components.
2. **Non-container visual components** тАУ `Button`, `Label`, `TextField`, etc.

The `Container` branch further splits into:
- `Panel` (a generic container without a title bar or border)
- `Window` (top-level window)
  - `Frame` (window with title bar and borders)
  - `Dialog` (pop-up window)
- `ScrollPane` (container with scrollable region)

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Component] --> B[Container]
    A --> C[Button]
    A --> D[Label]
    B --> E[Panel]
    B --> F[Window]
    B --> G[ScrollPane]
    F --> H[Frame]
    F --> I[Dialog]
```

**Difference between Panel and Frame: (3 marks)**

| Feature | Panel | Frame |
|---------|-------|-------|
| Title bar | Does not have | Has a title bar |
| Borders/Menu | No native borders or menu support | Supports borders and menu bar |
| Top-level | Cannot be displayed standalone | Can be top-level window |
| Use case | Embedded inside Frame/Applet | Main application window |
| Inheritance | Extends Container directly | Extends Window |

#### Q3b. (7 marks)

**Write a Java AWT program to create a Frame with three Buttons (OK, Cancel, Submit) arranged using FlowLayout. Set the frame size to 400├Ч300 and make it visible.**

**Model Answer:**

```java
import java.awt.*;

class AWTButtonDemo extends Frame {
    AWTButtonDemo() {
        // Step 1: Set the layout manager (1 mark)
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        
        // Step 2: Create components (1 mark)
        Button b1 = new Button("OK");
        Button b2 = new Button("Cancel");
        Button b3 = new Button("Submit");
        
        // Step 3: Add components to container (1 mark)
        add(b1);
        add(b2);
        add(b3);
        
        // Step 4: Set size and visibility (1 mark)
        setSize(400, 300);
        setTitle("Button Demo");
        setVisible(true);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        AWTButtonDemo f = new AWTButtonDemo();  // (3 marks)
    }
}
```

**Explanation:** The class `AWTButtonDemo` extends `Frame` (a top-level container). The constructor sets a `FlowLayout`, creates three `Button` components, adds them via `add()`, and configures the frame's size and visibility.

---

### ЁЯФ╣ Question B (Alternative 14 Marks)

#### Q4a. [KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024] (7 marks)

**Explain the BorderLayout with a diagram. How is it different from GridLayout?**

**Model Answer:**

**BorderLayout (4 marks):** It divides the container into **5 distinct regions**: `North` (top), `South` (bottom), `East` (right), `West` (left), and `Center` (middle). Each region can hold at most **one component** (or a container of components). The North and South regions span the full width horizontally; East and West occupy the remaining vertical space; Center fills the leftover space.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[BorderLayout Container] --> B[North]
    A --> C[South]
    A --> D[East]
    A --> E[West]
    A --> F[Center]
```

**Difference from GridLayout (3 marks):**

| Feature | BorderLayout | GridLayout |
|---------|--------------|------------|
| Regions | 5 named regions | Equal grid of rows ├Ч columns |
| Component size | Variable per region | All cells are equal size |
| Components per area | 1 per region | 1 per cell |
| Default for Frame | Yes | No |

#### Q4b. (7 marks)

**Write a Java AWT program that uses a `Panel` inside a `Frame` to display a `Label` and a `TextField` using BorderLayout.**

**Model Answer:**

```java
import java.awt.*;

class PanelDemo extends Frame {
    PanelDemo() {
        // Frame uses BorderLayout by default (1 mark)
        setLayout(new BorderLayout());
        
        // Create a Panel (2 marks)
        Panel p = new Panel();
        p.setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        
        // Create components
        Label l = new Label("Name:");
        TextField tf = new TextField(20);
        
        // Add components to Panel
        p.add(l);
        p.add(tf);
        
        // Add Panel to Frame's center (2 marks)
        add(p, BorderLayout.CENTER);
        
        // Frame configuration
        setSize(400, 150);
        setTitle("Panel Demo");
        setVisible(true);  // (2 marks)
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new PanelDemo();
    }
}
```

**Explanation:** A `Panel` (a generic Container) is created and populated with a `Label` and `TextField` using `FlowLayout`. This panel is then added to the center region of a `Frame` that uses `BorderLayout`. This demonstrates **nested container composition** тАФ a container inside another container.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `setVisible(true)`:** The most common mistake тАФ your program compiles and runs but nothing appears. Always remember to call `setVisible(true)` as the LAST step.
> 2. **Confusing AWT vs Swing imports:** AWT uses `java.awt.*` (e.g., `Frame`, `Button`). Swing uses `javax.swing.*` (e.g., `JFrame`, `JButton`). Mixing them causes compilation errors.
> 3. **Swing content pane oversight:** In Swing, `frame.add(button)` will compile but **not display** the button. You must use `frame.getContentPane().add(button)`.
> 4. **Wrong BorderLayout region names:** Use **exact strings** `"North"`, `"South"`, `"East"`, `"West"`, `"Center"` (capitalized). Lowercase will throw a runtime error.
> 5. **Not extending `Frame`:** In a standalone Java GUI, the main class must extend `Frame` (AWT) or `JFrame` (Swing) for the window to be a valid top-level container.
> 6. **Missing `main()` method signature:** KTU strictly requires `public static void main(String[] args)`.

---

## ЁЯза Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Exam Day:**

- тЬЕ **`Component`** is the abstract superclass of all AWT visual elements; it defines display-related methods like `setSize`, `setVisible`, `setBackground`.
- тЬЕ **`Container`** is a subclass of `Component` that can hold other components via the `add()` method.
- тЬЕ A **Container IS-A Component** тАФ this is what enables nested UI (containers inside containers).
- тЬЕ **`Frame`** is the most commonly used top-level container in standalone AWT applications; it has a title bar and border.
- тЬЕ **`Panel`** is a generic container without a title bar тАФ used to group components inside a Frame.
- тЬЕ **Default Layouts:** `Frame` тЖТ `BorderLayout`; `Panel` тЖТ `FlowLayout`.
- тЬЕ **5 BorderLayout regions:** `North`, `South`, `East`, `West`, `Center` тАФ exactly one component per region.
- тЬЕ **GridLayout** divides the container into equal-sized cells in a rows ├Ч columns grid.
- тЬЕ **FlowLayout** arranges components left-to-right, wrapping to the next line.
- тЬЕ **For Swing:** Use `JFrame`, `JButton`, etc. and always use `getContentPane().add()`.
- тЬЕ The execution order in any AWT program: **Create Container тЖТ Set Layout тЖТ Create Components тЖТ Add Components тЖТ setSize тЖТ setVisible(true)**.
- тЬЕ **The `add()` method signature:** `Component add(Component comp)` тАФ returns the added component.
- тЬЕ `Applet` is **deprecated** since Java 9; KTU may still ask conceptually, but avoid in practical code.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swing Packages
Event Handling in Swings
Swing Layout Managers
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swing Layout Managers

# Swing Layout Managers тАФ Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Layout Manager (Java AWT/Swing):** A `java.awt.LayoutManager` (or its sub-interface `LayoutManager2`) is a **strategy object** that governs the **size, position, and arrangement** of `Component` objects (widgets) inside a `Container`. It implements the **Strategy Design Pattern** from the Gang-of-Four catalog, decoupling *what* is placed in a container from *how* it is visually arranged.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Course Outcome: **CO4** тАФ *Apply event-driven programming concepts to develop GUI-based applications using Java Swing*), Layout Managers form the **non-visual backbone** of any desktop application. Without a layout manager, components either overlap, get clipped, or fail to resize correctly when the user stretches the window.

**Hierarchy in the Java Class Library:**

$$ \text{LayoutManager (interface)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{LayoutManager2 (sub-interface)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Concrete classes} $$

All concrete layout managers (`BorderLayout`, `FlowLayout`, `GridLayout`, `BoxLayout`, `GridBagLayout`, `CardLayout`, `GroupLayout`, `SpringLayout`) implement one of these two interfaces and are part of the **`java.awt`** package, even when used inside Swing (`javax.swing`) components.

## 1.2 The Intuition тАФ A Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy тАФ The Interior Designer of Your Window**
>
> Imagine you are moving into a **new apartment room** (your `JFrame` / `JPanel`). You have furniture (your buttons, labels, text fields тАФ the `Component` objects). You could drop the furniture randomly on the floor, but the room will look chaotic, and if you re-shape the room, furniture will sit on top of the carpet edges.
>
> Hiring a **Layout Manager** is like hiring an **interior designer** who follows specific rules:
> - A **FlowLayout** designer lines furniture up in a row, wrapping to the next row when the line is full.
> - A **GridLayout** designer places furniture in a strict N ├Ч M matrix, like egg cartons.
> - A **BorderLayout** designer divides the room into five zones тАФ North wall, South wall, East wall, West wall, and the Center тАФ placing the most important piece in the Center.
> - A **BoxLayout** designer stacks furniture either vertically (bookshelf) or horizontally (shelf).
> - A **GridBagLayout** designer is the most flexible тАФ a perfectionist who uses a spreadsheet of constraints to place every piece exactly where you want it, even if pieces are different sizes.

**Why is this important to KTU examiners?** Because the examiner is not testing whether you can *memorize* a constructor тАФ they are testing whether you can **choose the correct layout** for a problem, **justify** the choice, and **predict** what happens when the window is resized.

## 1.3 Key Constants & Metrics

- **Pixel (px)** тАФ the standard unit of measurement; default font in `MetalLookAndFeel` is typically the **Dialog 12 pt** font (~ **12 pixels** tall).
- **Container insets** тАФ measured in **pixels**, default top/left/bottom/right = **11, 11, 11, 11** for `JFrame` content pane, **0** for `JPanel`.
- **`setPreferredSize(Dimension)`** тАФ the **layout-hint size** in **pixels**; the layout manager respects this *only* when there is room to honour it.
- **Reasonable upper bound for component count in a single panel** тАФ typically **тЙд 30** before usability degrades (usability guideline, not a Java constant).

---

## 1.4 Why Layout Managers Exist тАФ The Problem They Solve

If you set absolute coordinates via `setBounds(x, y, width, height)`, three problems appear:

1. **Platform dependency** тАФ fonts and widget sizes differ across Windows, macOS, Linux.
2. **Resizing failure** тАФ the layout freezes when the user resizes the window.
3. **Locale dependency** тАФ translated text (e.g., German "Abbrechen" vs English "Cancel") overflows fixed-width components.

Layout Managers solve all three by treating the container as a **rectangular contract** and re-computing child geometry every time the container is invalidated (`validate()` / `revalidate()`).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The LayoutManager Contract тАФ Operational Logic

Every `LayoutManager` must implement **five** methods. A `LayoutManager2` extends this with two more:

**Core contract (LayoutManager):**

1. `addLayoutComponent(String name, Component comp)` тАФ called when a component is added with a *string constraint* (used by `BorderLayout` to assign "North", "South", etc.).
2. `removeLayoutComponent(Component comp)` тАФ called when a component is removed.
3. `preferredLayoutSize(Container parent)` тАФ returns the **ideal** size of the container that will display all children comfortably.
4. `minimumLayoutSize(Container parent)` тАФ returns the **smallest** acceptable size (usually smaller than preferred).
5. `layoutContainer(Container parent)` тАФ the **drawing call**: actually positions and sizes every child. Called automatically by the AWT/Swing rendering thread.

**Extended contract (LayoutManager2):**

6. `addLayoutComponent(Component comp, Object constraints)` тАФ the **modern, type-safe** way to attach a constraint object (e.g., a `GridBagConstraints` instance).
7. `maximumLayoutSize(Container parent)` тАФ the **largest** acceptable size, used by scrolling containers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question Pattern:** *"Explain the role of the `layoutContainer()` method. When is it invoked?"* тАФ The answer must mention that the AWT event-dispatch thread calls it **after** `validate()` (or `revalidate()`) and that the layout manager is responsible for calling `component.setBounds(...)` on every child.

---

## 2.2 The "Big Five" Layout Managers тАФ KTU Reference Sheet

| # | Manager | Default for | Arrangement Rule | Constructor Variants | Key Methods |
|---|---------|-------------|------------------|----------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **FlowLayout** | `JPanel` | Left-to-right, top-to-bottom wrapping | `()`, `(int align)`, `(int align, int hgap, int vgap)` | align тИИ {LEFT, CENTER, RIGHT} |
| 2 | **BorderLayout** | `JFrame` content pane, `JDialog` | 5 zones: NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST, CENTER | `()`, `(int hgap, int vgap)` | `add(comp, BorderLayout.NORTH)` etc. |
| 3 | **GridLayout** | none (opt-in) | Rigid N ├Ч M grid, equal cells | `(rows, cols)`, `(rows, cols, hgap, vgap)` | cells expand to fill container |
| 4 | **BoxLayout** | Boxes (`Box.createXxxBox()`) | Single row OR single column | `new BoxLayout(target, axis)` | axis тИИ {X\_AXIS, Y\_AXIS, LINE\_AXIS, PAGE\_AXIS} |
| 5 | **GridBagLayout** | none (opt-in) | Flexible grid with per-cell constraints | `new GridBagLayout()` | paired with `GridBagConstraints` |

### Supplementary Layout Managers (often asked as 3-mark questions)

| Manager | Use Case |
|---------|----------|
| **CardLayout** | Tab-like UIs, wizards (step 1 тЖТ step 2 тЖТ step 3) тАФ `next()`, `previous()`, `show(cardName)` |
| **GroupLayout** | Auto-generated by NetBeans GUI Builder; horizontal + vertical parallel/sequential groups |
| **SpringLayout** | Precise constraint-based positioning via `Spring` objects |
| **GridLayout(rows, cols, hgap, vgap)** | When *hgap* or *vgap* is 0, cells touch тАФ useful for calculator-style button panels |

---

## 2.3 The Chain of Method Calls тАФ How Layout Works at Runtime

When a `JFrame` is first shown, the AWT rendering pipeline executes the following sequence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1. } & \texttt{setVisible(true)} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Peer created, native window opens} \\
\text{2. } & \texttt{addNotify()} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Component attached to native resource} \\
\text{3. } & \texttt{validate()} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Tree validated top-down} \\
\text{4. } & \texttt{layoutContainer(parent)} \;\text{ called on each container's layout manager} \\
\text{5. } & \text{Each child receives } \texttt{setBounds(x, y, w, h)} \\
\text{6. } & \texttt{paint()} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Components actually drawn on screen}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The same contract is used in **embedded systems GUI toolkits** (e.g., Qt's QLayout, Android's ConstraintLayout, JavaFX's Pane) тАФ the Strategy Pattern transcends languages.

---

## 2.4 Decision Matrix тАФ "Which Layout Should I Use?"

| Scenario | Recommended Layout | Reasoning |
|----------|-------------------|-----------|
| A login form (2 labels + 2 fields + 1 button) | `GridBagLayout` or nested `JPanel`s with `BoxLayout` | Need aligned columns, mixed sizes |
| A toolbar with 4 buttons in a row | `FlowLayout(LEFT)` or `BoxLayout(X\_AXIS)` | Single horizontal row, natural wrap if window shrinks |
| A main app window with menu, status bar, central canvas | `BorderLayout` | The textbook 5-zone pattern |
| A calculator button pad (4├Ч4 = 16 equal buttons) | `GridLayout(4, 4, 2, 2)` | Equal cells, 2px gaps |
| A multi-step form (Page 1 тЖТ Page 2 тЖТ Page 3) | `CardLayout` | Show one "card" at a time |
| A complex form with labels, fields, checkboxes, radio buttons | **Nested panels**, each with its own layout | Most real-world GUIs use this pattern |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *Programming language note:* This module is part of **PBCST304 тАФ Object Oriented Programming**, so Java source code is the expected medium of demonstration (the "no Python" rule applies to non-CS subjects only).

## 3.1 Example 1 тАФ `BorderLayout` (Default of `JFrame`)

**Step 1 тАФ Conceptual goal:** Place a header at the top, a status bar at the bottom, a navigation panel on the left, a settings panel on the right, and a main canvas in the center.

**Step 2 тАФ Java code with exhaustive annotation:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class BorderLayoutDemo extends JFrame {
    public BorderLayoutDemo() {
        setTitle("BorderLayout Demo");
        setSize(500, 400);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        // Step 3: Get the content pane. In Swing, you do NOT add
        // directly to the JFrame; you add to its content pane.
        Container cp = getContentPane();

        // Step 4: Set the layout manager explicitly.
        // (It is ALREADY BorderLayout by default тАФ shown for clarity.)
        cp.setLayout(new BorderLayout(5, 5));   // 5px gaps

        // Step 5: Add components with region constraints.
        cp.add(new JButton("North Menu"),  BorderLayout.NORTH);
        cp.add(new JButton("South Status"), BorderLayout.SOUTH);
        cp.add(new JButton("West Nav"),     BorderLayout.WEST);
        cp.add(new JButton("East Settings"),BorderLayout.EAST);
        cp.add(new JTextArea("Center Work Area"), BorderLayout.CENTER);

        // Step 6: Make the window visible тАФ triggers validate() internally.
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(BorderLayoutDemo::new);
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Line } \texttt{cp.setLayout(new BorderLayout(5, 5))} \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{hgap = vgap = 5 pixels} \\
\text{Line } \texttt{cp.add(..., BorderLayout.NORTH)} \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{LayoutManager2.addLayoutComponent stores (button, "North")} \\
\text{Line } \texttt{setVisible(true)} \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{Invokes} \; \texttt{validate()} \;\Rightarrow\; \texttt{layoutContainer()} \\
\text{Inside } \texttt{layoutContainer()} \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{NORTH and SOUTH take preferred height, full width} \\
                                  \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{WEST and EAST take preferred width, remaining height} \\
                                  \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{CENTER takes ALL remaining space}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU pitfall:** If you call `frame.add(button)` **without** a region, the button is silently assigned to `CENTER`. If you then add a second component without a region, it **replaces** the first one. Many students lose marks for not knowing this.

---

## 3.2 Example 2 тАФ `GridLayout` (Calculator Pad)

**Step 1 тАФ Conceptual goal:** Build a 4 ├Ч 4 calculator button pad with 16 equal-sized buttons and 3-pixel gaps.

**Step 2 тАФ Java code with annotation:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class GridLayoutDemo extends JFrame {
    public GridLayoutDemo() {
        setTitle("GridLayout Demo тАФ Calculator Pad");
        setSize(300, 300);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        Container cp = getContentPane();

        // Step 3: rows = 4, cols = 4, hgap = 3, vgap = 3
        cp.setLayout(new GridLayout(4, 4, 3, 3));

        // Step 4: The order of add() = left-to-right, top-to-bottom.
        String[] labels = {
            "7", "8", "9", "/",
            "4", "5", "6", "*",
            "1", "2", "3", "-",
            "0", ".", "=", "+"
        };
        for (String s : labels) {
            cp.add(new JButton(s));
        }

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(GridLayoutDemo::new);
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Grid cell width} \;&=\; \frac{\texttt{containerWidth} - 4 \times \texttt{hgap}}{4} \\
\text{Grid cell height} \;&=\; \frac{\texttt{containerHeight} - 4 \times \texttt{vgap}}{4} \\
\text{All cells} \;&=\; \text{exactly that size тАФ the layout IGNORES component preferred size} \\
\text{Component count} \;&>\; \texttt{rows} \times \texttt{cols} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{extra cells ignored} \\
\text{Component count} \;&<\; \texttt{rows} \times \texttt{cols} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{rows is forced to fit}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Many students write `new GridLayout(0, 4)` thinking *"any number of rows"*. Java's behavior is the opposite: setting `rows = 0` means **any number of rows**, but the **columns are fixed at 4**. Confusing the two will cost marks.

---

## 3.3 Example 3 тАФ `BoxLayout` (Vertical Form)

**Step 1 тАФ Conceptual goal:** Stack 4 form rows vertically, each row a label + text field.

**Step 2 тАФ Java code with annotation:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class BoxLayoutDemo extends JFrame {
    public BoxLayoutDemo() {
        setTitle("BoxLayout Demo тАФ Vertical Form");
        setSize(350, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        JPanel form = new JPanel();
        // Step 3: BoxLayout is constructor-injected with the target
        // container as the FIRST argument and axis as the second.
        form.setLayout(new BoxLayout(form, BoxLayout.Y_AXIS));

        // Step 4: Add label-field rows.
        for (int i = 1; i <= 4; i++) {
            form.add(new JLabel("Field " + i + ":"));
            form.add(new JTextField(15));
        }

        add(form);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(BoxLayoutDemo::new);
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BoxLayout} \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{components arranged along ONE axis only} \\
\text{Y\_AXIS} \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{top-to-bottom stack} \\
\text{X\_AXIS} \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{left-to-right row} \\
\text{Alignment} \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{default is CENTER; can override per-component with } \texttt{setAlignmentX(float)} \\
\text{Fillers} \;&\Rightarrow\; \texttt{Box.createVerticalGlue()} \text{ pushes components apart}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Example 4 тАФ `GridBagLayout` (The Power Tool)

**Step 1 тАФ Conceptual goal:** A login form with two labels, two fields, a "Remember me" checkbox, and a "Login" button тАФ all aligned in two columns.

**Step 2 тАФ Java code with annotation:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class GridBagLayoutDemo extends JFrame {

    private void addComponent(Container cp, Component c,
                              int row, int col, int width, int height,
                              int anchor, int fill) {
        GridBagConstraints g = new GridBagConstraints();
        g.gridx = col;        g.gridy = row;
        g.gridwidth = width;  g.gridheight = height;
        g.anchor = anchor;    g.fill = fill;
        g.insets = new Insets(4, 4, 4, 4);  // 4px padding around each cell
        g.weightx = (width > 1) ? 1.0 : 0.5;
        g.weighty = (height > 1) ? 1.0 : 0.0;
        cp.add(c, g);
    }

    public GridBagLayoutDemo() {
        setTitle("GridBagLayout Demo тАФ Login");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        Container cp = getContentPane();
        cp.setLayout(new GridBagLayout());

        addComponent(cp, new JLabel("Username:"), 0, 0, 1, 1,
                     GridBagConstraints.WEST, GridBagConstraints.NONE);
        addComponent(cp, new JTextField(15),       0, 1, 1, 1,
                     GridBagConstraints.WEST, GridBagConstraints.HORIZONTAL);
        addComponent(cp, new JLabel("Password:"),  1, 0, 1, 1,
                     GridBagConstraints.WEST, GridBagConstraints.NONE);
        addComponent(cp, new JPasswordField(15),   1, 1, 1, 1,
                     GridBagConstraints.WEST, GridBagConstraints.HORIZONTAL);
        addComponent(cp, new JCheckBox("Remember me"), 2, 0, 2, 1,
                     GridBagConstraints.WEST, GridBagConstraints.NONE);
        addComponent(cp, new JButton("Login"),     3, 1, 1, 1,
                     GridBagConstraints.EAST, GridBagConstraints.NONE);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(GridBagLayoutDemo::new);
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{gridx}, \texttt{gridy} \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{cell coordinates (top-left = 0,0)} \\
\texttt{gridwidth}, \texttt{gridheight} \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{how many cells this component spans} \\
\texttt{fill} \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{NONE, HORIZONTAL, VERTICAL, BOTH} \\
\texttt{anchor} \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{CENTER, NORTH, NORTHWEST, etc.} \\
\texttt{weightx}, \texttt{weighty} \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{how extra space is distributed (0 = don't grow)} \\
\texttt{insets} \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{per-component padding in pixels}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **High-loss-of-marks area:** Students often set `fill = BOTH` on a `JLabel` тАФ the label then stretches to fill the entire cell, and text appears in the top-left corner with vast empty space. The KTU correct answer: use `fill = NONE` with an appropriate `anchor`.

---

## 3.5 Example 5 тАФ `CardLayout` (Tab-like Wizard)

**Step 1 тАФ Conceptual goal:** A 3-step registration wizard with "Next" and "Previous" buttons.

**Step 2 тАФ Java code with annotation:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class CardLayoutDemo extends JFrame {
    private CardLayout cards = new CardLayout();
    private JPanel cardPanel = new JPanel(cards);

    public CardLayoutDemo() {
        setTitle("CardLayout Demo тАФ Wizard");
        setSize(400, 250);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        // Step 3: Build the three "cards"
        JPanel p1 = new JPanel(); p1.add(new JLabel("Step 1: Name"));   p1.add(new JTextField(12));
        JPanel p2 = new JPanel(); p2.add(new JLabel("Step 2: Email"));  p2.add(new JTextField(12));
        JPanel p3 = new JPanel(); p3.add(new JLabel("Step 3: Submit"));

        // Step 4: Register each card with a string name
        cardPanel.add(p1, "step1");
        cardPanel.add(p2, "step2");
        cardPanel.add(p3, "step3");

        // Step 5: Navigation buttons
        JButton prev = new JButton("< Prev");
        JButton next = new JButton("Next >");
        prev.addActionListener(e -> cards.previous(cardPanel));
        next.addActionListener(e -> cards.next(cardPanel));

        JPanel nav = new JPanel();
        nav.add(prev);  nav.add(next);

        // Step 6: Outer layout тАФ BorderLayout so cards fill the center
        setLayout(new BorderLayout());
        add(cardPanel, BorderLayout.CENTER);
        add(nav,      BorderLayout.SOUTH);

        cards.show(cardPanel, "step1");
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(CardLayoutDemo::new);
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{All "cards" are added to the SAME container} \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{but only one is visible at a time} \\
\texttt{cards.next(container)} \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{advance to the next registered card} \\
\texttt{cards.previous(container)} \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{go back one} \\
\texttt{cards.show(container, "name")} \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{jump directly to a named card} \\
\text{Use case} \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{wizards, tabbed views without a } \texttt{JTabbedPane}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix тАФ "What happens when I resize the window?"

| Layout Manager | Resize Behaviour | Honours Preferred Size? | Best For |
|----------------|------------------|-------------------------|----------|
| `FlowLayout` | Components wrap to next row/column | тЬЕ Yes | Toolbars, button bars |
| `BorderLayout` | NORTH/SOUTH stay at preferred height; CENTER stretches | тЪая╕П Partially | Main app windows |
| `GridLayout` | All cells resize equally; preferred size ignored | тЭМ No | Calculator pads |
| `BoxLayout` | Components keep preferred size; glue/filler absorbs extra | тЬЕ Yes | Vertical/horizontal stacks |
| `GridBagLayout` | Components grow proportionally based on `weightx`/`weighty` | тЪая╕П Configurable | Complex forms |
| `CardLayout` | All cards fill the entire container | тЭМ No | Wizards |
| `null` (no manager) | **Nothing** тАФ components freeze at `setBounds()` | N/A | Never (anti-pattern) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Layout Manager Class Hierarchy

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **`java.awt`** layout hierarchy. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[LayoutManager Interface] --> A2[LayoutManager2 Interface]
    A1 --> B1[FlowLayout]
    A1 --> B2[BorderLayout]
    A1 --> B3[GridLayout]
    A1 --> B4[CardLayout]
    A1 --> B5[BoxLayout]
    A1 --> B6[GridBagLayout]
    A2 --> B2
    A2 --> B5
    A2 --> B6
    A1 --> B7[GroupLayout]
    A1 --> B8[SpringLayout]
    C1[Container] --> D1[setLayout Manager]
    D1 --> A1
    D1 --> A2
    C1 --> E1[JFrame]
    C1 --> E2[JPanel]
    C1 --> E3[JDialog]
    E1 --> F1[Default BorderLayout]
    E2 --> F2[Default FlowLayout]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- `LayoutManager2` extends `LayoutManager` and adds constraint-based adding.
- `BorderLayout`, `BoxLayout`, `GridBagLayout` implement **both** interfaces.
- `FlowLayout`, `GridLayout`, `CardLayout`, `GroupLayout`, `SpringLayout` implement only `LayoutManager`.
- `JFrame` content pane uses `BorderLayout` by default; raw `JPanel` uses `FlowLayout` by default.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture тАФ How a Resize Event Triggers Re-Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    evtStart([User Resizes the Window]) --> evtStepA[Native OS sends resize event to AWT]
    evtStepA --> evtStepB[Component Resize event dispatched on EDT]
    evtStepB --> evtStepC[Container.invalidate called on JFrame content pane]
    evtStepC --> evtStepD[Container.validate traverses component tree]
    evtStepD --> evtStepE[For each Container, invoke layoutContainer on its LayoutManager]
    evtStepE --> evtStepF[LayoutManager computes preferred minimum maximum sizes]
    evtStepF --> evtStepG[LayoutManager calls setBounds on every child Component]
    evtStepG --> evtStepH[Component.validate propagates downward]
    evtStepH --> evtStepI[repaint schedules paint on EDT]
    evtStepI --> evtStepJ[Components redrawn with new geometry]
    evtStepJ --> evtEnd([Window reflects new size])
```

**Engineering interpretation:** This is the AWT/Swing "re-layout pipeline." Every layout manager must be **stateless across calls** (or at least idempotent) because the same `layoutContainer` method can be invoked **thousands of times** during the lifetime of a window.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram тАФ BorderLayout Five-Zone Geometry

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph OuterFrame["JFrame content pane (500 wide ├Ч 400 tall)"]
        zoneN[ZONE NORTH: full width ├Ч preferred height 60px]
        zoneW[ZONE WEST: preferred width 100px ├Ч remaining height]
        zoneC[ZONE CENTER: occupies all remaining space]
        zoneE[ZONE EAST: preferred width 100px ├Ч remaining height]
        zoneS[ZONE SOUTH: full width ├Ч preferred height 40px]
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:** The contract is:
- **NORTH** and **SOUTH** span the **full container width**.
- **EAST** and **WEST** occupy **preferred width** between NORTH and SOUTH.
- **CENTER** swallows **whatever space remains**.

---

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart тАФ "Which Layout Manager Should I Choose?"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Need a layout]) --> q1{Is it a single row or column?}
    q1 -- Yes, row --> flowA[FlowLayout LEFT or BoxLayout X AXIS]
    q1 -- Yes, column --> boxA[BoxLayout Y AXIS]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Is it a wizard with multiple pages?}
    q2 -- Yes --> cardA[CardLayout]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Is it a rigid grid like a calculator?}
    q3 -- Yes --> gridA[GridLayout rows cols hgap vgap]
    q3 -- No --> q4{Does it have a menu top, status bottom, main center?}
    q4 -- Yes --> borderA[BorderLayout]
    q4 -- No --> q5{Need fine control over cell sizes and alignment?}
    q5 -- Yes --> gbagA[GridBagLayout with GridBagConstraints]
    q5 -- No --> nestedA[Nest multiple JPanels each with its own layout]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
