# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 тАФ `[KTU University Exam тАФ May 2024]`

**Q: Define a Layout Manager in Java. List any three Layout Manager classes available in `java.awt` and state the default Layout Manager used by `JFrame` and `JPanel`.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Layout Manager** is an object that implements the `java.awt.LayoutManager` (or `LayoutManager2`) interface and is responsible for arranging the components inside a `Container` according to a specific strategy. It controls the size, position, and resizing behaviour of child components. *(1 mark)*

Three commonly used layout manager classes in `java.awt` are: **FlowLayout, BorderLayout, GridLayout**. *(1 mark тАФ names; any three accepted)*

- The **default layout manager of `JFrame` content pane** is `BorderLayout`. *(0.5 marks)*
- The **default layout manager of a newly created `JPanel`** is `FlowLayout`. *(0.5 marks)*

---

### Question 2 тАФ `[KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the difference between `preferredSize` and `minimumSize` of a component? How does a `BorderLayout` honour these values when the window is resized?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> `preferredSize` is the **ideal** size at which the component displays its content most comfortably, while `minimumSize` is the **smallest** size below which the component becomes unusable or its content gets clipped. *(1 mark)*

In a `BorderLayout`: *(2 marks)*

- The **NORTH** and **SOUTH** regions keep their **preferred height** when the window is resized vertically тАФ they shrink down to their **minimum height** only if the container becomes very small.
- The **WEST** and **EAST** regions keep their **preferred width** when the window is resized horizontally.
- The **CENTER** region absorbs **all the extra space** (and gives up space first when the window shrinks, but never below the sum of all regions' minimum sizes).

---

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A тАФ `[KTU University Exam тАФ May 2024 / Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the `BorderLayout` manager in detail. Discuss the five regions, the constructor, and what happens when a component is added **without** specifying a region.

**(b) [7 marks]** Write a Java Swing program that uses `GridLayout` to construct a calculator-style 4 ├Ч 4 button pad. Each button must be labelled with the characters `7, 8, 9, /` on row 1, `4, 5, 6, *` on row 2, `1, 2, 3, -` on row 3, and `0, ., =, +` on row 4. Add 5-pixel gaps between cells.

#### Model Solution тАФ Part (a)

**Definition & Five Regions** *(2 marks)*:
`BorderLayout` is the default layout manager of a `JFrame` content pane and `JDialog`. It divides the container into **five distinct regions**:

| Region | Position | Behaviour on Resize |
|--------|----------|---------------------|
| `NORTH` | Top edge | Full width, preferred height |
| `SOUTH` | Bottom edge | Full width, preferred height |
| `EAST` | Right edge | Preferred width, remaining height |
| `WEST` | Left edge | Preferred width, remaining height |
| `CENTER` | Middle | All leftover space |

**Constructor** *(2 marks)*:
- `BorderLayout()` тАФ uses zero horizontal and vertical gaps.
- `BorderLayout(int hgap, int vgap)` тАФ specifies pixel gaps between regions. Example: `new BorderLayout(10, 5)` creates 10-px horizontal gap and 5-px vertical gap.

**Adding components** *(2 marks)*:
```java
Container c = getContentPane();
c.setLayout(new BorderLayout());
c.add(new JButton("Menu"), BorderLayout.NORTH);
c.add(textArea,            BorderLayout.CENTER);
```

**What happens when no region is specified** *(1 mark)*:
If a component is added using `add(comp)` **without** a `String` constraint, `BorderLayout` (which implements `LayoutManager2`) **silently assigns it to the `CENTER` region**. Adding a **second** component without specifying a region causes it to **replace** the first one in the `CENTER` slot. This is a common source of bugs and is heavily penalised in KTU valuations.

#### Model Solution тАФ Part (b)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class CalculatorPad extends JFrame {
    public CalculatorPad() {
        setTitle("Calculator Pad тАФ GridLayout Demo");
        setSize(250, 250);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        Container cp = getContentPane();
        // rows=4, cols=4, hgap=5, vgap=5
        cp.setLayout(new GridLayout(4, 4, 5, 5));

        String[][] labels = {
            {"7", "8", "9", "/"},
            {"4", "5", "6", "*"},
            {"1", "2", "3", "-"},
            {"0", ".", "=", "+"}
        };

        for (int r = 0; r < 4; r++) {
            for (int c = 0; c < 4; c++) {
                cp.add(new JButton(labels[r][c]));
            }
        }

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(CalculatorPad::new);
    }
}
```

**Stepwise explanation** *(matching marks)*:
- `setLayout(new GridLayout(4, 4, 5, 5))` тАФ declares a 4-row ├Ч 4-column grid with 5-px gaps. *(1 mark)*
- The double `for` loop iterates row-major and adds a button in the natural reading order. *(1 mark)*
- `JButton` objects are created with the required labels. *(1 mark)*
- `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` ensures the JVM exits when the window is closed. *(1 mark)*
- `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(...)` is the thread-safe way to launch Swing GUIs. *(1 mark)*
- Total: 5 marks of code + 2 marks of explanation. *(2 marks)*

---

### Question B тАФ `[KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023 / Model Paper]` **(Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the `GridBagLayout` manager. Discuss the role of `GridBagConstraints` and the meaning of the fields `gridx`, `gridy`, `gridwidth`, `gridheight`, `fill`, `anchor`, `weightx`, and `weighty`.

**(b) [7 marks]** Write a Java Swing program that demonstrates a `CardLayout` with three "cards" тАФ a login card, a registration card, and a welcome card. Include `Next >` and `< Previous` navigation buttons.

#### Model Solution тАФ Part (a)

**Definition** *(1 mark)*:
`GridBagLayout` is the most flexible and complex layout manager in `java.awt`. It arranges components in a **virtual grid of rows and columns**, where each component can occupy **one or more cells** and is governed by a `GridBagConstraints` object.

**Role of `GridBagConstraints`** *(1 mark)*:
`GridBagConstraints` is a **plain-old Java object (POJO)** that holds layout hints for a single component: its grid position, span, fill behaviour, padding, alignment, and grow-weights. The same instance must not be reused across multiple components тАФ a fresh instance should be created for each `add()` call.

**Field-by-field explanation** *(5 marks тАФ 0.625 each, rounded to whole marks)*:

- `gridx`, `gridy` тАФ the **column** and **row** of the cell where the component's top-left corner sits. Default is `0`. Use `GridBagConstraints.RELATIVE` to place after the last-added component.
- `gridwidth`, `gridheight` тАФ how many **columns** and **rows** the component spans. Default is `1`. Use `GridBagConstraints.REMAINDER` to extend to the end of the row/column.
- `fill` тАФ controls whether the component **stretches** to fill its cells: `NONE`, `HORIZONTAL`, `VERTICAL`, or `BOTH`.
- `anchor` тАФ when `fill = NONE`, controls **where inside the cell** the component is positioned: `CENTER`, `NORTH`, `NORTHWEST`, etc.
- `weightx`, `weighty` тАФ determines how **extra space** in the container is distributed among components. A weight of `0.0` (the default) means "do not grow"; a weight of `1.0` means "take a proportional share of the surplus." Setting **all** weights to `0` causes components to clump in the centre.
- `insets` тАФ per-component **padding** in pixels (top, left, bottom, right). Distinct from the `ipadx`/`ipady` internal padding that grows the component itself.

#### Model Solution тАФ Part (b)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class CardLayoutWizard extends JFrame {
    private final CardLayout cards = new CardLayout();
    private final JPanel cardPanel = new JPanel(cards);

    public CardLayoutWizard() {
        setTitle("CardLayout Demo тАФ Wizard");
        setSize(400, 220);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        // Step 1: Build three distinct cards
        JPanel loginCard = new JPanel(new GridLayout(2, 2, 5, 5));
        loginCard.add(new JLabel("Username:"));
        loginCard.add(new JTextField(10));
        loginCard.add(new JLabel("Password:"));
        loginCard.add(new JPasswordField(10));

        JPanel regCard = new JPanel(new GridLayout(3, 2, 5, 5));
        regCard.add(new JLabel("Full Name:"));
        regCard.add(new JTextField(10));
        regCard.add(new JLabel("Email:"));
        regCard.add(new JTextField(10));
        regCard.add(new JLabel("Phone:"));
        regCard.add(new JTextField(10));

        JPanel welcomeCard = new JPanel();
        welcomeCard.add(new JLabel("Welcome! Registration complete."));

        // Step 2: Register cards with string identifiers
        cardPanel.add(loginCard,   "login");
        cardPanel.add(regCard,     "register");
        cardPanel.add(welcomeCard, "welcome");

        // Step 3: Navigation panel
        JButton prev = new JButton("< Previous");
        JButton next = new JButton("Next >");
        prev.addActionListener(e -> cards.previous(cardPanel));
        next.addActionListener(e -> cards.next(cardPanel));

        JPanel nav = new JPanel();
        nav.add(prev);
        nav.add(next);

        // Step 4: Outer BorderLayout so cards fill the centre
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(10, 10));
        add(cardPanel, BorderLayout.CENTER);
        add(nav,      BorderLayout.SOUTH);

        cards.show(cardPanel, "login");
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(CardLayoutWizard::new);
    }
}
```

**Stepwise explanation** *(7 marks distribution)*:
- `CardLayout` declared and `JPanel` constructed with it. *(1 mark)*
- Three `JPanel` "cards" built with appropriate sub-components. *(2 marks)*
- Each card added with a **string identifier** (`"login"`, `"register"`, `"welcome"`). *(1 mark)*
- Navigation buttons wired to `cards.next()` and `cards.previous()`. *(2 marks)*
- Outer `BorderLayout` used so the navigation bar sits at the bottom and the card panel fills the centre. *(1 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to call `setLayout(...)`**: If you add components to a `JPanel` whose default `FlowLayout` is unsuitable for your design, you **must** explicitly call `setLayout()`. Many students lose 1тАУ2 marks here.
> 2. **Using `null` layout and `setBounds()`**: This is an **anti-pattern** in Swing. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests whether you can *choose* a layout manager тАФ not whether you can avoid one. Setting `setLayout(null)` is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction in CO4 questions.
> 3. **Forgetting `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(...)`**: Swing is **not thread-safe**. The standard idiom is to launch the GUI on the Event Dispatch Thread (EDT). Examiners often award a separate mark for this.
> 4. **Confusing `JFrame.add()` with `JFrame.getContentPane().add()`**: In modern Java (5+), the compiler silently redirects `frame.add()` to the content pane, but conceptually, components belong to the **content pane**, not to the `JFrame` itself. Show the distinction in your answer.
> 5. **Not re-validating after dynamic changes**: When components are added/removed **after** the window is shown, you must call `container.revalidate()` and `container.repaint()`. This is a common 14-mark exam trap.
> 6. **Mixing `add(comp)` with no constraint in `BorderLayout`**: As explained above, this is silent and catastrophic тАФ components disappear and students think the code is buggy.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Layout Managers implement the Strategy Pattern** тАФ they encapsulate the *how* of placement, freeing your code to focus on the *what*.
- **Default layout of `JFrame` content pane = `BorderLayout`**; **default of raw `JPanel` = `FlowLayout`**.
- **`FlowLayout`** тАФ left-to-right, wraps; alignment тИИ {`LEFT`, `CENTER`, `RIGHT`}; honours `hgap`, `vgap`.
- **`BorderLayout`** тАФ 5 zones (`NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`, `CENTER`); **CENTER** gets all leftover space; adding a component without a region defaults to `CENTER` and **replaces** any existing centre component.
- **`GridLayout(rows, cols, hgap, vgap)`** тАФ all cells are **equal size**; **preferred size is ignored**; setting `rows = 0` means "any number of rows" (and the columns are fixed at `cols`).
- **`BoxLayout(target, axis)`** тАФ single-axis arrangement; `X_AXIS` = row, `Y_AXIS` = column; use `Box.createVerticalGlue()` or `Box.createHorizontalGlue()` for flexible spacing.
- **`GridBagLayout`** тАФ the **power tool**; always paired with a **fresh `GridBagConstraints`** for each component; `weightx`/`weighty` of `0.0` means "do not grow"; set them to `1.0` to make components share extra space.
- **`CardLayout`** тАФ multiple "cards" in one container, only one visible; navigate with `next()`, `previous()`, `show(container, "name")`; perfect for wizards.
- **`GroupLayout`** тАФ used by **NetBeans GUI Builder**; requires careful specification of horizontal and vertical groups.
- **Always launch Swing on the EDT** тАФ `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(Runnable)`.
- **Always call `revalidate()` and `repaint()`** after dynamic structural changes (add/remove components at runtime).
- **Never use `setLayout(null)`** in production code тАФ it is a KTU syllabus red flag.
- **The `layoutContainer()` method** is the actual drawing call; it is invoked by the AWT rendering pipeline after `validate()` and is responsible for calling `setBounds()` on every child.
- **Nested panels** are the standard solution for complex GUIs тАФ one layout per panel, then panels inside a master `BorderLayout`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exploring Swings - JFrame
JLabel
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** JLabel # ЁЯУШ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU Standard):** > `JLabel` is a **subclass of `javax.swing.JComponent`** used to display a **single line of read-only text, an image, or both** simultaneously. It is a **passive (non-interactive) GUI component** belonging to Java's **Swing lightweight component framework** (Package: `javax.swing`). A `JLabel` does not react to user input events such as mouse clicks or key presses and is primarily employed to **describe the purpose** of adjacent interactive components (e.g., identifying a `JTextField`). **Class Hierarchy (Java Swing Inheritance Chain):** ```text java.lang.Object тФФтФАтФА java.awt.Component тФФтФАтФА java.awt.Container тФФтФАтФА javax.swing.JComponent тФФтФАтФА javax.swing.JLabel тЖР (Topic of Study) ``` ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ The "Museum Wall Placard" ЁЯЦ╝я╕П Imagine walking through an art gallery. Next to every painting, there is a small **placard** that describes the artwork тАФ its title, the artist, and the year. This placard: | Museum Placard Feature | Equivalent `JLabel` Behavior | | :--- | :--- | | Static text "Mona Lisa, 1503" | `setText("Mona Lisa, 1503")` | | A small thumbnail of the painting | `setIcon(imageIcon)` | | Positioned right beside the painting | Acts as a label next to a `JTextField` | | Cannot be picked up or modified by viewers | Read-only, **non-focusable** component | | May include formatted descriptions (italic) | Supports **HTML tags** internally | In GUI design, a `JLabel` is the **silent narrator** of your interface тАФ it informs, it labels, but it never interacts. ## 1.3 Physical / Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > - **Default Cursor Behavior:** `JLabel` does not change the mouse cursor on hover (unlike interactive widgets such as `JButton`). > - **Default Focusability:** `false` тАФ it cannot receive keyboard focus via `Tab`. > - **Default Horizontal Alignment:** `LEADING` (left-aligned in LTR locales). > - **Default Vertical Alignment:** `CENTER`. > - **Package Origin:** `javax.swing` (Lightweight, 100% Java тАФ uses **AWT's** `Component` as superclass for OS-level rendering hooks). ## 1.4 Why Was `JLabel` Introduced? > [!IMPORTANT] > Before Swing (in legacy AWT), text display was handled by the **`Label`** class. Swing's `JLabel` superseded it with the following enhancements: > 1. **Image + Text simultaneous display** (AWT's `Label` supported text only). > 2. **HTML content rendering** (`Bold`). > 3. **Mnemonic support** (Alt-key shortcuts to focus associated components). > 4. **Pluggable Look & Feel (PLAF)** compliance. > 5. **Vertical + horizontal alignment controls** beyond basic left/center/right. # ЁЯУЧ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Constructors of `JLabel` The class exposes **six overloaded constructors**, all located in the `javax.swing.JLabel` API: | # | Constructor Signature | Purpose | Typical Use Case | | :- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | `JLabel()` | Creates an empty label (no text, no icon). | Placeholder for dynamic content. | | 2 | `JLabel(String text)` | Creates a label with text, no image. | "Username:" beside a `JTextField`. | | 3 | `JLabel(Icon image)` | Creates a label with an image, no text. | Displaying a logo. | | 4 | `JLabel(String text, Icon icon, int hAlign)` | Label with **both** text & image. | Icon + caption buttons-style labels. | | 5 | `JLabel(String text, int hAlign)` | Label with text + specified alignment. | Centered headings. | | 6 | `JLabel(Icon icon, int hAlign)` | Label with icon + specified alignment. | Right-aligned icons. | ## 2.2 Alignment Constants (The Orientation Engine) > [!IMPORTANT] > Alignment is governed by **`SwingConstants`**, an interface implemented by `JLabel`. It defines two sets of integer constants: ### Horizontal Alignment Constants - `SwingConstants.LEFT` (Value: `2`) - `SwingConstants.CENTER` (Value: `0`) - `SwingConstants.RIGHT` (Value: `4`) - `SwingConstants.LEADING` (Value: `10`) тАФ **Locale-aware default**. - `SwingConstants.TRAILING` (Value: `11`) тАФ **Locale-aware default**. ### Vertical Alignment Constants - `SwingConstants.TOP` (Value: `1`) - `SwingConstants.CENTER` (Value: `0`) тАФ **Default**. - `SwingConstants.BOTTOM` (Value: `3`) > [!NOTE] > The `LEADING` and `TRAILING` constants are **superior** to `LEFT`/`RIGHT` because they automatically flip in **Right-to-Left (RTL)** languages like Arabic or Hebrew тАФ making your GUI internationally portable. ## 2.3 Key Methods тАФ The API Cheat Sheet | Method | Return Type | Description | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `setText(String text)` | `void` | Updates the displayed text. | | `getText()` | `String` | Retrieves current text. | | `setIcon(Icon icon)` | `void` | Sets the image icon. | | `getIcon()` | `Icon` | Returns the current icon. | | `setHorizontalAlignment(int)` | `void` | Sets horizontal placement. | | `setVerticalAlignment(int)` | `void` | Sets vertical placement. | | `setHorizontalTextPosition(int)` | `void` | Where text sits **relative to icon** (LEFT, CENTER, RIGHT of icon). | | `setVerticalTextPosition(int)` | `void` | Where text sits **relative to icon** (TOP, CENTER, BOTTOM of icon). | | `setIconTextGap(int pixels)` | `void` | Distance between icon and text (default: **4 pixels**). | | `setDisplayedMnemonic(char)` | `void` | Sets the Alt-key shortcut. | | `setLabelFor(Component c)` | `void` | Associates the label with another component for focus transfer. | | `setDisabledIcon(Icon)` | `void` | Icon shown when label is disabled. | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield "Formula Sheet" (Core Rules) > [!IMPORTANT] > Although GUI programming has no algebraic equations, the following are the **inviolable rules** for `JLabel` behavior тАФ treat them as the "formulas" of the topic: $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{Rule 1 (Composition):} \quad & \text{Total Label Width} = \text{Icon Width} + \text{IconTextGap} + \text{Text Width} \\ \textbf{Rule 2 (Mnemonic Focus):} \quad & \text{Press Alt + Mnemonic} \Rightarrow \text{Focus transferred to } \texttt{setLabelFor()} \text{ component} \\ \textbf{Rule 3 (Default Gap):} \quad & \text{IconTextGap}_{\text{default}} = 4 \text{ pixels} \\ \textbf{Rule 4 (HTML Detection):} \quad & \text{If } \texttt{text.startsWith("")} \Rightarrow \text{HTML rendering is enabled} \\ \textbf{Rule 5 (Locale Rule):} \quad & \text{LEADING} \equiv \text{LEFT in LTR};\ \text{LEADING} \equiv \text{RIGHT in RTL} \end{aligned} $$ ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > `JLabel` is the **bedrock of every Swing-based form**: login screens in legacy enterprise Java, dashboard tiles, status bar messages, CAPTCHA image displays, and educational software GUIs. Mastery of `JLabel` is a prerequisite for all advanced Swing components (`JTextField`, `JButton`, `JTable`). # ЁЯУЩ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: "How Does `JLabel` Render Content?" The rendering pipeline of a `JLabel` follows a deterministic 5-stage process. Let us derive it: $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{Stage 1: Input Reception} \quad & \text{Developer invokes constructor or setter: } \texttt{new JLabel("Hello")}. \\ \textbf{Stage 2: Content Validation} \quad & \text{JVM checks if string starts with } \texttt{}. \\ & \text{If TRUE} \Rightarrow \text{HTMLView renderer activated}. \\ & \text{If FALSE} \Rightarrow \text{plain } \texttt{SwingUtilities} \text{ text renderer activated}. \\ \textbf{Stage 3: Layout Calculation} \quad & \text{LayoutManager of parent container queries preferred size:} \\ & \text{PreferredSize} = f(\text{FontMetrics}, \text{Icon Dimensions}, \text{Gap}). \\ \textbf{Stage 4: Painting} \quad & \text{During } \texttt{paintComponent(Graphics g)}: \\ & \text{(a) Draw icon at } (x_{\text{icon}}, y_{\text{icon}}). \\ & \text{(b) Draw text at } (x_{\text{text}}, y_{\text{text}}) \text{ per alignment rules}. \\ \textbf{Stage 5: Event Registration} \quad & \text{No keyboard/mouse listeners (passive).} \\ & \text{Exception: Mnemonic listener IF } \texttt{setLabelFor()} \text{ was called.} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Exhaustive Code Walkthrough тАФ The "Complete Label Demo" Below is the **fully annotated** canonical program for KTU exams: ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; public class LabelDemo extends JFrame { public LabelDemo() { setTitle("JLabel Demonstration - KTU Module 4"); setSize(400, 250); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 15, 20)); // Step 1: Pure text label JLabel textLabel = new JLabel("Student Name:"); textLabel.setFont(new Font("Arial", Font.BOLD, 14)); add(textLabel); // Step 2: Companion text field JTextField nameField = new JTextField(15); add(nameField); // Step 3: Image label (text + icon) ImageIcon icon = new ImageIcon("logo.png"); JLabel imageLabel = new JLabel("Kerala", icon, SwingConstants.LEFT); imageLabel.setHorizontalTextPosition(SwingConstants.RIGHT); imageLabel.setVerticalTextPosition(SwingConstants.CENTER); imageLabel.setIconTextGap(10); add(imageLabel); // Step 4: HTML-formatted label JLabel htmlLabel = new JLabel("Welcome to KTU"); htmlLabel.setForeground(Color.BLUE); add(htmlLabel); // Step 5: Mnemonic-enabled label JLabel pwdLabel = new JLabel("Password:"); JPasswordField pwdField = new JPasswordField(15); pwdLabel.setDisplayedMnemonic('P'); pwdLabel.setLabelFor(pwdField); // Alt+P focuses password field add(pwdLabel); add(pwdField); setVisible(true); } public static void main(String[] args) { new LabelDemo(); } } ``` ### Step-by-Step Logic Explanation | Line | Logic | Reason | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `setLayout(new FlowLayout(...))` | Establishes the layout manager. | Without a layout, components would stack at (0,0). | | `new JLabel("Student Name:")` | Instantiates a text-only label. | Uses the `JLabel(String)` constructor. | | `setFont(new Font(...))` | Customizes typography. | `JLabel` inherits font from `JComponent` тЖТ `Component`. | | `new JLabel("Kerala", icon, ...)` | Compound label with both media. | Uses the 3-argument constructor. | | `setHorizontalTextPosition(RIGHT)` | Text appears to the **right** of the icon. | Default is `TRAILING` (which is RIGHT in LTR). | | `setIconTextGap(10)` | 10-pixel buffer between icon and text. | Default is 4; we override for readability. | | `...` tag | Triggers HTML renderer. | Rule 4 from formula sheet applies. | | `setDisplayedMnemonic('P')` | Underlines 'P' in "Password:". | Visual cue for users. | | `setLabelFor(pwdField)` | Wires the label to the field. | Required for mnemonic focus to work. | ## 3.3 Derivation: "What Happens If I Forget `setLabelFor()`?" > [!WARNING] > This is a **classic KTU viva + practical exam pitfall**. Let us derive the consequence: > > If you call `setDisplayedMnemonic('P')` but **forget** `setLabelFor(pwdField)`: > - The mnemonic keystroke is **registered** by the label. > - The label **does not transfer focus** anywhere. > - Result: The user presses Alt+P and **nothing visible happens**. > - **Mark Deduction in KTU Exam:** Up to **2 marks** in practicals for missing this association. ## 3.4 HTML Rendering тАФ A Mathematical Demonstration Consider the following label text: ```java JLabel lbl = new JLabel("Error: Invalid input"); ``` **Derivation of rendering tree:** $$ \begin{aligned} \texttt{} \quad & \Rightarrow \text{HTMLFactory.createHTMLView()} \text{ invoked.} \\ \texttt{} \quad & \Rightarrow \text{StyledText styling node created.} \\ \texttt{} \quad & \Rightarrow \text{Text run "Error" colored red.} \\ \texttt{: Invalid input} \quad & \Rightarrow \text{Appended as plain run (default color).} \\ \texttt{} \quad & \Rightarrow \text{Tree finalized; painted in } \texttt{paintComponent()}. \end{aligned} $$ # ЁЯУХ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: `JLabel` Class Architecture & API ```mermaid graph TD A[java.lang.Object] --> B[java.awt.Component] B --> C[java.awt.Container] C --> D[javax.swing.JComponent] D --> E["javax.swing.JLabel"] E --> F[implements SwingConstants] E --> G[implements Accessible] H["setText(String)"] --> E I["setIcon(Icon)"] --> E J["setHorizontalAlignment(int)"] --> E K["setLabelFor(Component)"] --> E L["setDisplayedMnemonic(char)"] --> E M["LEADING (10)"] --> F N["CENTER (0)"] --> F O["TRAILING (11)"] --> F P["LEFT (2)"] --> F Q["RIGHT (4)"] --> F R["TOP (1)"] --> F S["BOTTOM (3)"] --> F style E fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: The `JLabel` Content Composition Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR A["JLabel Object"] --> B{Content Type} B -->|Path 1| C[Text Only] B -->|Path 2| D[Icon Only] B -->|Path 3| E[Text + Icon] C --> C1["setText('Username:')"] C --> C2["getText() returns string"] D --> D1["setIcon(new ImageIcon('logo.png'))"] D --> D2["getIcon() returns Icon"] E --> E1["setHorizontalTextPosition(RIGHT)"] E --> E2["setVerticalTextPosition(CENTER)"] E --> E3["setIconTextGap(int pixels)"] E1 --> F1[Text rendered RIGHT of icon] E2 --> F2[Text rendered CENTER vertically to icon] E3 --> F3[Default gap = 4px] style A fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Mnemonic & Focus Transfer Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([User presses Alt + Mnemonic Key]) --> A[JLabel detects mnemonic] A --> B{Is setLabelFor called?} B -->|NO| C[No focus transfer - LOST MARK] B -->|YES| D[Locate target Component] D --> E[Call requestFocus on target] E --> F([Target component gains focus]) F --> G[Caret becomes visible in JTextField] G --> H([User types input normally]) style Start fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#FF6347,color:#fff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Render Pipeline Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Dev as Developer participant JL as JLabel participant LM as LayoutManager participant PC as paintComponent participant SCR as Screen Dev->>JL: new JLabel('Hello', icon, CENTER) JL->>JL: Store text, icon, alignment Dev->>LM: Parent adds label LM->>JL: getPreferredSize() JL-->>LM: Width, Height LM->>JL: setBounds(x, y, w, h) Note over JL,SCR: Repaint event triggered JL->>PC: paintComponent(g) PC->>PC: Draw icon at aligned coords PC->>PC: Draw text at aligned coords PC->>SCR: Rendered pixels SCR-->>Dev: Visual output displayed ``` ## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture тАФ The "Label Toolkit" ```mermaid graph TB subgraph InputLayer["ЁЯУе Input Stage"] I1[Constructor Arguments] I2[setText / setIcon calls] end subgraph ProcessingLayer["тЪЩя╕П Processing Engine"] P1{String starts with html?} P2[Plain Text Renderer] P3[HTML Renderer] P4[Alignment Calculator] P5[Mnemonic Listener] end subgraph OutputLayer["ЁЯУд Output Stage"] O1[Visible Text on Frame] O2[Visible Icon on Frame] O3[Focus Transfer Event] end I1 --> P1 I2 --> P1 P1 -->|YES| P3 P1 -->|NO| P2 P2 --> P4 P3 --> P4 P4 --> O1 P4 --> O2 P5 --> O3 style ProcessingLayer fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style InputLayer fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style OutputLayer fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` # ЁЯУТ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## ЁЯУЭ PART A: 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023, Set A]** тАФ *(3 Marks)* **"What is `JLabel` in Java Swing? List any two of its constructors."** **Model Answer:** `JLabel` is a class in the `javax.swing` package used to display a short string of text, an image, or both. It is a **passive (non-editable, non-focusable)** component, mainly used to label or describe other GUI elements like `JTextField` or `JButton`. Two constructors: 1. `JLabel()` тАФ creates an empty label. 2. `JLabel(String text)` тАФ creates a label initialized with the given text string. *(Any other two valid constructors also accepted.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024]** тАФ *(3 Marks)* **"Differentiate between `JLabel` and `JTextField` in Java Swing."** **Model Answer:** | Feature | `JLabel` | `JTextField` | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Editable | **No** (read-only) | **Yes** (user can type input) | | Focus | Cannot receive focus by default | Can receive focus (Tab-cyclable) | | Purpose | Display static text/icon | Capture user input | | Event Listener | None required | `ActionListener`, `KeyListener` | | Inheritance | `JComponent` тЖТ `JLabel` | `JTextComponent` тЖТ `JTextField` | | HTML Support | Yes (via `` tag) | Limited | --- ## ЁЯУЭ PART B: 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) тАФ Choice 1** **[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice A]** > **(a)** Explain the `JLabel` class in Java Swing. Discuss its various constructors with suitable examples. *(7 Marks)* > > **(b)** Write a Java program to create a `JFrame` containing a `JLabel` that displays an image and text together. The text should appear to the right of the image with a gap of 15 pixels. *(7 Marks)* --- #### тЬЕ Model Solution for Q-A(a) тАФ `JLabel` Class & Constructors **Definition:** `JLabel` is a Swing lightweight component that extends `JComponent` and is used to display read-only text, images, or both. It implements the `SwingConstants` interface for alignment control. **Constructors Table (Full Marks Coverage):** | Constructor | Description | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `JLabel()` | Empty label | `JLabel l = new JLabel();` | | `JLabel(String text)` | Text-only | `JLabel l = new JLabel("Hello");` | | `JLabel(Icon icon)` | Icon-only | `JLabel l = new JLabel(new ImageIcon("a.png"));` | | `JLabel(String text, Icon icon, int hAlign)` | Text + Icon | `JLabel l = new JLabel("OK", icon, SwingConstants.LEFT);` | | `JLabel(String text, int hAlign)` | Text + Alignment | `JLabel l = new JLabel("Centered", SwingConstants.CENTER);` | | `JLabel(Icon icon, int hAlign)` | Icon + Alignment | `JLabel l = new JLabel(icon, SwingConstants.RIGHT);` | **Key Properties:** - **Horizontal Alignment** тАФ `LEFT`, `CENTER`, `RIGHT`, `LEADING`, `TRAILING` - **Vertical Alignment** тАФ `TOP`, `CENTER`, `BOTTOM` - **Text Position relative to Icon** тАФ `LEFT`, `CENTER`, `RIGHT`, `TOP`, `BOTTOM` of icon - **Icon-Text Gap** тАФ settable via `setIconTextGap(int)` --- #### тЬЕ Model Solution for Q-A(b) тАФ Program with Image + Text Label ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; public class ImageTextLabelDemo extends JFrame { public ImageTextLabelDemo() { setTitle("KTU JLabel Demo - Image + Text"); setSize(500, 200); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 20, 30)); // Load image ImageIcon icon = new ImageIcon("ktu_logo.png"); if (icon.getIconWidth() == -1) { // Fallback for systems without the image icon = new ImageIcon(); System.out.println("Image not found, using empty icon."); } // Create compound label JLabel lbl = new JLabel("APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University", icon, SwingConstants.LEFT); // Position text to the RIGHT of icon lbl.setHorizontalTextPosition(SwingConstants.RIGHT); lbl.setVerticalTextPosition(SwingConstants.CENTER); // Set 15-pixel gap (KTU requirement) lbl.setIconTextGap(15); // Styling lbl.setFont(new Font("Serif", Font.BOLD, 16)); lbl.setForeground(new Color(0, 51, 102)); add(lbl); setVisible(true); } public static void main(String[] args) { new ImageTextLabelDemo(); } } ``` **Explanation of Key Lines:** - `setHorizontalTextPosition(SwingConstants.RIGHT)` тЖТ text appears **right** of icon. - `setVerticalTextPosition(SwingConstants.CENTER)` тЖТ vertically centered with icon. - `setIconTextGap(15)` тЖТ satisfies the 15-pixel buffer requirement. - `icon.getIconWidth() == -1` check тЖТ graceful fallback if file missing. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Choice 2 (Alternative)** **[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024, Module 4 Choice B]** > **(a)** Explain the concept of mnemonics in `JLabel`. How is `setLabelFor()` used? Write a code snippet to demonstrate a label with a mnemonic that transfers focus to a `JTextField`. *(7 Marks)* > > **(b)** Discuss the role of `SwingConstants` in `JLabel` alignment. Compare `LEADING/TRAILING` with `LEFT/RIGHT`. *(7 Marks)* --- #### тЬЕ Model Solution for Q-B(a) тАФ Mnemonics & `setLabelFor()` **Concept:** A **mnemonic** in Swing is a keyboard shortcut (typically combined with the **Alt key**) that triggers an action or transfers focus. In `JLabel`, the mnemonic is the underlined character in the displayed text. **Workflow:** 1. The user presses `Alt + [Mnemonic Key]`. 2. `JLabel` detects this via its internal `KeyEventDispatcher`. 3. Focus is transferred to the component registered through `setLabelFor()`. **Code Snippet:** ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; public class MnemonicDemo extends JFrame { public MnemonicDemo() { setTitle("KTU Mnemonic Demo"); setSize(400, 150); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); setLayout(new FlowLayout()); // Step 1: Create target component JTextField emailField = new JTextField(20); // Step 2: Create label JLabel emailLabel = new JLabel("Email Address:"); // Step 3: Set mnemonic (Alt+E) emailLabel.setDisplayedMnemonic('E'); // Step 4: Link label to text field emailLabel.setLabelFor(emailField); add(emailLabel); add(emailField); setVisible(true); } public static void main(String[] args) { new MnemonicDemo(); } } ``` **Output Behavior:** The 'E' in "Email" becomes underlined. Pressing `Alt + E` instantly places the cursor inside the `JTextField`. --- #### тЬЕ Model Solution for Q-B(b) тАФ `SwingConstants` & Locale-Aware Alignment **Role of `SwingConstants`:** It is an interface declaring integer constants for component positioning. `JLabel` implements it, allowing alignment values to be passed directly to setters. **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | `LEFT` / `RIGHT` | `LEADING` / `TRAILING` | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Definition | Absolute screen positions | **Edge of the writing direction** | | LTR Locale (English) | `LEFT` = `LEADING` (left edge) | Same as LEFT | | RTL Locale (Arabic) | `LEFT` = **right** side (counter-intuitive) | `LEADING` = right side тЬУ | | Internationalization | тЭМ Breaks in RTL | тЬЕ Locale-aware | | Recommended by KTU | Only for fixed-direction UIs | **Best Practice** | **Engineering Insight:** KTU examiners prefer `LEADING`/`TRAILING` because real-world applications (like a billing system used in Arabic-speaking Kerala expat communities or Hebrew academic environments) require **locale-aware** behavior. **Code Comparison:** ```java // тЭМ Not recommended label.setHorizontalAlignment(SwingConstants.LEFT); // тЬЕ KTU Best Practice label.setHorizontalAlignment(SwingConstants.LEADING); ``` --- ## тЪая╕П KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting `setLabelFor()`** тАФ Calling `setDisplayedMnemonic()` without `setLabelFor()` renders the mnemonic useless. *Penalty: 1-2 marks.* > 2. **Importing `java.awt.Label`** тАФ Students confuse the legacy AWT `Label` class with Swing's `JLabel`. Always import `javax.swing.JLabel`. *Penalty: 1 mark.* > 3. **Wrong alignment constant for text position** тАФ Using `setHorizontalAlignment()` instead of `setHorizontalTextPosition()` to position text relative to the icon. *Penalty: 2 marks.* > 4. **Missing `setVisible(true)`** тАФ The window will compile but never display. *Penalty: 1 mark in practicals.* > 5. **Forgetting HTML tag closing** тАФ `Bold` will throw a rendering exception at runtime. > 6. **Not calling `pack()` or `setSize()`** тАФ Window appears with 0├Ч0 dimensions. --- ## ЁЯФБ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > ЁЯУМ **Quick-Fire Revision:** > - [x] `JLabel` is in package `javax.swing` and extends `JComponent`. > - [x] It is **passive** тАФ no event handling required for basic usage. > - [x] Supports **Text**, **Icon**, or **both** simultaneously. > - [x] Six constructors: `()`, `(String)`, `(Icon)`, `(String, int)`, `(Icon, int)`, `(String, Icon, int)`. > - [x] Implements `SwingConstants` тАФ gives access to `LEADING`, `CENTER`, `TRAILING`, `LEFT`, `RIGHT`, `TOP`, `BOTTOM`. > - [x] Use `setHorizontalTextPosition()` / `setVerticalTextPosition()` to position text relative to an icon (NOT `setHorizontalAlignment`). > - [x] `setIconTextGap(int)` controls the pixel gap between icon and text (default = **4 px**). > - [x] HTML support: prefix text with `` to enable rich formatting (``, ``, ``). > - [x] Mnemonics require **TWO** calls: `setDisplayedMnemonic(char)` AND `setLabelFor(Component)`. > - [x] Prefer `LEADING`/`TRAILING` over `LEFT`/`RIGHT` for internationalization. > - [x] Cannot receive keyboard focus; cannot be edited; cannot fire `ActionEvent`. > - [x] Inherits all `JComponent` methods like `setFont()`, `setForeground()`, `setBackground()`, `setToolTipText()`.

topics.title: The Swing Buttons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Swing Buttons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Java Swing** (part of the **Java Foundation Classes - JFC**), **Buttons** are **interactive GUI components** that allow a user to initiate an action, toggle a state, or make a selection. Swing provides a rich, pluggable look-and-feel hierarchy of button classes rooted at the abstract class `javax.swing.AbstractButton`, which itself extends `javax.swing.JComponent`.

The principal concrete button classes covered under KTU Module 4 are:

| Component | Class Name | Purpose |
|-----------|------------|---------|
| **Push Button** | `JButton` | Triggers a single, instantaneous command/action |
| **Toggle Button** | `JToggleButton` | A two-state (pressed/released) push button |
| **Check Box** | `JCheckBox` | Independent multi-selection toggle |
| **Radio Button** | `JRadioButton` | Mutually exclusive single-selection toggle |
| **Menu Item** | `JMenuItem` | A button that lives inside a menu bar |
| **CheckBox Menu Item** | `JCheckBoxMenuItem` | Checkbox variant inside a menu |
| **Radio Button Menu Item** | `JRadioButtonMenuItem` | Radio button variant inside a menu |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The KTU 2024 Scheme treats *The Swing Buttons* as a sub-topic under **Swing Components / Event Handling**. Students are expected to know class hierarchies, constructors, important methods, and be able to write small Java programs that use these buttons along with **ActionListener**, **ItemListener**, and **ChangeListener** interfaces.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **TV remote control**:

- A **JButton** is like the **Power button** тАФ you press it, an action happens (TV turns on/off), then the button springs back.
- A **JToggleButton** is like the **Mute button** тАФ it stays *pressed-in* (muted) until you press it again to release.
- A **JCheckBox** is like a list of features you can independently enable тАФ e.g., **Subtitles тЬФ**, **Cinema Mode тЬФ**, **Sports Mode тШР**. You may tick any combination.
- A **JRadioButton** is like choosing a **TV input source** тАФ HDMI 1, HDMI 2, AV, or USB. You can pick **only one** at a time.
- A **JMenuItem** is like an option inside a dropdown menu such as *Settings тЖТ Display тЖТ Brightness*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in KTU exams?** The examiner loves to test your understanding of *which listener fits which button*. Mixing up `ItemListener` and `ActionListener` is the #1 reason students lose marks.

### 1.3 The Abstract Hierarchy

All Swing buttons share a common ancestor тАФ **`javax.swing.AbstractButton`**. This is critical for KTU theory questions because it explains why every button has methods like `setText()`, `setIcon()`, `setEnabled()`, `addActionListener()`, `setSelected()`, etc.

```
java.lang.Object
   тФФтФАтФА java.awt.Component
         тФФтФАтФА java.awt.Container
               тФФтФАтФА javax.swing.JComponent
                     тФФтФАтФА javax.swing.AbstractButton
                           тФЬтФАтФА javax.swing.JButton
                           тФЬтФАтФА javax.swing.JToggleButton
                           тФВ       тФЬтФАтФА javax.swing.JCheckBox
                           тФВ       тФФтФАтФА javax.swing.JRadioButton
                           тФФтФАтФА javax.swing.JMenuItem
                                   тФЬтФАтФА javax.swing.JCheckBoxMenuItem
                                   тФФтФАтФА javax.swing.JRadioButtonMenuItem
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Theory Tip:** If asked *"Which class is the root of all Swing buttons?"* тАФ the correct answer is `javax.swing.AbstractButton`, **not** `JButton`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Common DNA тАФ `AbstractButton` Methods

Because every Swing button extends `AbstractButton`, the following methods are **universally available**:

| Method | Signature | Purpose |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| `setText(String)` | `void setText(String text)` | Sets the button's label |
| `getText()` | `String getText()` | Returns the current label |
| `setIcon(Icon)` | `void setIcon(Icon icon)` | Sets the displayed icon |
| `setMnemonic(char)` | `void setMnemonic(int mnemonic)` | Keyboard shortcut (Alt + char) |
| `setToolTipText(String)` | `void setToolTipText(String text)` | Hover hint |
| `setEnabled(boolean)` | `void setEnabled(boolean b)` | Enable / disable the button |
| `setSelected(boolean)` | `void setSelected(boolean b)` | Set toggle state (for toggleable buttons) |
| `isSelected()` | `boolean isSelected()` | Get toggle state |
| `addActionListener(ActionListener)` | `void` | Register action handler |
| `addItemListener(ItemListener)` | `void` | Register state-change handler |
| `addChangeListener(ChangeListener)` | `void` | Register model-change handler |
| `setActionCommand(String)` | `void setActionCommand(String cmd)` | Custom ID for the event |

### 2.2 The Three Event Listener Paradigm

Swing buttons emit **three distinct kinds of events**, and KTU loves to test this:

| Listener | Interface | Triggered When | Used By |
|----------|-----------|---------------|---------|
| **Action Listener** | `java.awt.event.ActionListener` | Button is **clicked / activated** | All buttons |
| **Item Listener** | `java.awt.event.ItemListener` | Button's **state changes** (selected тЖФ deselected) | Toggleable buttons (`JToggleButton`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JCheckBoxMenuItem`, `JRadioButtonMenuItem`) |
| **Change Listener** | `javax.swing.event.ChangeListener` | Button's **internal model** changes (e.g., `ButtonModel`) | `JToggleButton` and subclasses (low-level) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"Action = Click, Item = State, Change = Model."*

### 2.3 JButton тАФ The Command Push Button

`JButton` is the simplest Swing button. It can display text, an icon, or both. When clicked, it fires an `ActionEvent`.

**Key Constructors:**

| Constructor | Description |
|-------------|-------------|
| `JButton()` | Empty button |
| `JButton(String text)` | Button with text label |
| `JButton(Icon icon)` | Button with image |
| `JButton(String text, Icon icon)` | Text + icon button |

**Default Behaviour:** When clicked, the button visually "presses" and "releases," then fires `actionPerformed()`. It does **not** retain a pressed state.

**Typical Code Skeleton:**

```java
JButton b = new JButton("Click Me");
b.addActionListener(e -> System.out.println("Button clicked!"));
```

### 2.4 JToggleButton тАФ The Sticky Push Button

`JToggleButton` is a two-state button. Once pressed, it **stays pressed** until pressed again.

**Use Case:** Bold/Italic toolbar buttons in word processors.

**Constructors:** Same as `JButton` plus `JToggleButton(String text, boolean selected)`.

**Important:** A `JToggleButton` fires **both** an `ActionEvent` *and* an `ItemEvent` when its state changes.

### 2.5 JCheckBox тАФ Independent Multi-Selection

`JCheckBox` extends `JToggleButton`. It is used when **zero, one, or many** options can be selected simultaneously.

**Key Constructor Variants:**

| Constructor | Description |
|-------------|-------------|
| `JCheckBox()` | Empty checkbox |
| `JCheckBox(String text)` | Checkbox with label |
| `JCheckBox(String text, boolean selected)` | Pre-selected checkbox |
| `JCheckBox(Icon icon)` | Checkbox with icon |
| `JCheckBox(String text, Icon icon)` | Label + icon |
| `JCheckBox(String text, Icon icon, boolean selected)` | Full control |

**Event:** Fires `ItemEvent` on state change. Use `ItemListener`.

### 2.6 JRadioButton тАФ Mutually Exclusive Selection

`JRadioButton` *also* extends `JToggleButton`, but unlike checkboxes, **radio buttons must be grouped** using `ButtonGroup` to enforce single-selection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Creating `JRadioButton` objects without a `ButtonGroup` makes them behave like **independent checkboxes**, which is a common exam mistake.

**Sample:**

```java
JRadioButton male = new JRadioButton("Male");
JRadioButton female = new JRadioButton("Female");
ButtonGroup g = new ButtonGroup();
g.add(male);
g.add(female);
```

### 2.7 JMenuItem and Its Variants

`JMenuItem` is a button designed to live inside a `JMenu`. It cannot be added directly to a container; it must be added to a `JMenu`, which itself is added to a `JMenuBar`.

**Special Subclasses:**

- `JCheckBoxMenuItem` тАФ A checkbox inside a menu.
- `JRadioButtonMenuItem` тАФ A radio button inside a menu (also requires `ButtonGroup`).

**Key Constructors of `JMenuItem`:**

| Constructor | Description |
|-------------|-------------|
| `JMenuItem()` | Empty menu item |
| `JMenuItem(String text)` | Text-labeled item |
| `JMenuItem(Icon icon)` | Icon-labeled item |
| `JMenuItem(String text, Icon icon)` | Text + icon |
| `JMenuItem(String text, int mnemonic)` | Text + keyboard shortcut |

**Event:** Fires `ActionEvent` when selected (and `ItemEvent` for the two toggle variants).

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The "formula sheet" below is a **decision matrix** тАФ there are no physical equations in this GUI topic, so we summarise the **decision rules** for which listener / constructor to choose.

| Situation | Use Class | Use Listener | Mandatory Companion |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| One-shot command (e.g., Submit) | `JButton` | `ActionListener` | тАФ |
| Sticky on/off (e.g., Bold) | `JToggleButton` | `ActionListener` + `ItemListener` | тАФ |
| Independent multi-select | `JCheckBox` | `ItemListener` | тАФ |
| Mutually exclusive choice | `JRadioButton` | `ItemListener` | **`ButtonGroup`** |
| Action inside a menu | `JMenuItem` | `ActionListener` | `JMenu`, `JMenuBar` |
| Toggle inside a menu | `JCheckBoxMenuItem` | `ItemListener` | `JMenu` |
| Radio inside a menu | `JRadioButtonMenuItem` | `ItemListener` | `JMenu`, `ButtonGroup` |

| Method | Returns | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| `setMnemonic(int)` | `void` | Sets Alt+key shortcut |
| `setActionCommand(String)` | `void` | Sets event identifier |
| `getActionCommand()` | `String` | Retrieves event identifier |
| `isSelected()` | `boolean` | Returns toggle state |
| `setSelected(boolean)` | `void` | Sets toggle state |
| `setHorizontalAlignment(int)` | `void` | Aligns text/icon (e.g., `SwingConstants.LEFT`) |
| `setVerticalAlignment(int)` | `void` | Vertical alignment |
| `setPressedIcon(Icon)` | `void` | Icon shown when pressed |
| `setRolloverIcon(Icon)` | `void` | Icon shown on mouse hover |
| `setDisabledIcon(Icon)` | `void` | Icon shown when disabled |
| `setHideActionText(boolean)` | `void` | Hide text when icon is present |
| `setBorderPainted(boolean)` | `void` | Show/hide border |
| `setContentAreaFilled(boolean)` | `void` | Paint background |
| `setFocusPainted(boolean)` | `void` | Paint focus indicator |
| `setIconTextGap(int)` | `void` | Pixels between icon and text |
| `addActionListener(ActionListener)` | `void` | Register click handler |
| `removeActionListener(ActionListener)` | `void` | Unregister click handler |
| `getActionListeners()` | `ActionListener[]` | All registered listeners |
| `setEnabled(boolean)` | `void` | Enable/disable button |
| `setToolTipText(String)` | `void` | Hover tooltip text |
| `getText()` | `String` | Current label |
| `setText(String)` | `void` | New label |
| `setIcon(Icon)` | `void` | Display icon |
| `getIcon()` | `Icon` | Current icon |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Memorise the `ButtonGroup` rule. *Without `ButtonGroup`, `JRadioButton` objects behave as independent toggles тАФ the question almost always appears in Part A.*

### 2.9 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Swing buttons are the **building blocks of every desktop engineering tool** you will ever build:

- **SCADA / HMI dashboards** in industrial automation use `JButton` for *Start*, *Stop*, *Emergency* commands; `JRadioButton` for selecting operating mode (Manual / Auto); `JCheckBox` for enabling alarms.
- **CAD toolbars** use `JToggleButton` for sticky tools (Draw, Erase, Select).
- **Configuration UIs** use `JMenuItem` for cascading menus (File тЖТ Export тЖТ PDF).
- **Simulation front-ends** in Mechatronics labs use radio buttons to pick simulation modes.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> In a GUI topic, "derivation" translates to **step-by-step construction of a working Java program**. We will analytically build a complete Swing application that uses *every* button type, explaining each line.

### 3.1 Analytical Objective

We will design a **"User Preferences"** panel that contains:

1. A `JButton` тАФ "Save Preferences".
2. A `JCheckBox` тАФ "Enable Notifications".
3. Three `JRadioButton`s тАФ Theme: *Light*, *Dark*, *System Default* (mutually exclusive).
4. A `JToggleButton` тАФ "Dark Mode Preview".
5. A `JMenuItem` inside a `JMenu` тАФ "File тЖТ Exit".

The aim is to demonstrate correct **listener wiring** for each.

### 3.2 Step 1 тАФ Imports and Class Skeleton

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class PreferencesDemo extends JFrame {
    public PreferencesDemo() {
        setTitle("User Preferences");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        // ... components will be added here
        setVisible(true);
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new PreferencesDemo();
    }
}
```

**Line-by-Line Reasoning:**

- `javax.swing.*` тАФ Pulls in `JButton`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JToggleButton`, `JMenu`, `JMenuItem`, `JMenuBar`, `ButtonGroup`, `JFrame`, `JRadioButtonMenuItem`, `JCheckBoxMenuItem`.
- `java.awt.*` тАФ Provides `FlowLayout`, `Color`, `Font`.
- `java.awt.event.*` тАФ Provides `ActionListener`, `ItemListener`, `ActionEvent`, `ItemEvent`.
- Extending `JFrame` makes our class itself a window (avoids creating a separate frame variable).
- `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` ensures the JVM exits when the window is closed.
- `setLayout(new FlowLayout())` arranges components left-to-right, top-to-bottom.

### 3.3 Step 2 тАФ The JButton (Action Source)

```java
JButton saveBtn = new JButton("Save Preferences");
saveBtn.setMnemonic(KeyEvent.VK_S);          // Alt+S
saveBtn.setActionCommand("SAVE");
saveBtn.addActionListener(e -> {
    String cmd = e.getActionCommand();
    JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(this, "Saved! Command = " + cmd);
});
add(saveBtn);
```

**Reasoning:**

- `setMnemonic(KeyEvent.VK_S)` binds the keyboard shortcut **Alt+S** to this button. (KTU frequently tests `setMnemonic`.)
- `setActionCommand("SAVE")` tags the event with a string ID тАФ useful when many buttons share one listener.
- `addActionListener` registers the click handler. The lambda receives the `ActionEvent`, from which we read the action command.
- `JOptionPane.showMessageDialog` pops up a confirmation dialog.

### 3.4 Step 3 тАФ The JCheckBox (Independent Toggle)

```java
JCheckBox notifyCbx = new JCheckBox("Enable Notifications", true);
notifyCbx.addItemListener(e -> {
    if (e.getStateChange() == ItemEvent.SELECTED)
        System.out.println("Notifications ON");
    else
        System.out.println("Notifications OFF");
});
add(notifyCbx);
```

**Reasoning:**

- The second constructor argument `true` pre-selects the checkbox at startup.
- `ItemEvent.getStateChange()` returns either `ItemEvent.SELECTED` (1) or `ItemEvent.DESELECTED` (2). Comparing with the constants is the KTU-approved way to disambiguate.
- The `ItemListener`'s method is `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)`. The lambda must conform to this functional signature.

### 3.5 Step 4 тАФ The JRadioButtons with ButtonGroup

```java
JRadioButton lightR   = new JRadioButton("Light");
JRadioButton darkR    = new JRadioButton("Dark");
JRadioButton systemR  = new JRadioButton("System Default", true);

ButtonGroup themeGroup = new ButtonGroup();
themeGroup.add(lightR);
themeGroup.add(darkR);
themeGroup.add(systemR);

ItemListener themeListener = e -> {
    JRadioButton src = (JRadioButton) e.getSource();
    if (src.isSelected()) System.out.println("Theme тЖТ " + src.getText());
};
lightR.addItemListener(themeListener);
darkR.addItemListener(themeListener);
systemR.addItemListener(themeListener);

add(lightR); add(darkR); add(systemR);
```

**Reasoning:**

- `ButtonGroup` enforces mutual exclusion. **Only one** radio can be selected at a time.
- The third radio `systemR` is *pre-selected* via its constructor argument.
- All three share **one** `ItemListener` object тАФ the source of the event is used to identify which button fired it.
- Casting `e.getSource()` to `JRadioButton` is safe because we registered the listener only on radio buttons.

### 3.6 Step 5 тАФ The JToggleButton (Sticky Push)

```java
JToggleButton previewBtn = new JToggleButton("Dark Mode Preview");
previewBtn.addActionListener(e -> {
    boolean on = previewBtn.isSelected();
    getContentPane().setBackground(on ? Color.DARK_GRAY : Color.LIGHT_GRAY);
});
add(previewBtn);
```

**Reasoning:**

- `JToggleButton.isSelected()` reflects the current *pressed-in* state.
- We change the frame's background based on this state, providing visual feedback.
- We use `ActionListener` here because we only care that the user *clicked* the button (we read the state ourselves with `isSelected()`). We could alternatively use `ItemListener`.

### 3.7 Step 6 тАФ The JMenuBar with JMenuItems

```java
JMenuBar menuBar = new JMenuBar();
JMenu fileMenu  = new JMenu("File");
fileMenu.setMnemonic(KeyEvent.VK_F);

JMenuItem exitItem = new JMenuItem("Exit", KeyEvent.VK_X);
exitItem.setActionCommand("EXIT");
exitItem.addActionListener(e -> System.exit(0));

JMenu themeMenu = new JMenu("Theme");

JCheckBoxMenuItem cbmi = new JCheckBoxMenuItem("Use Animations");
cbmi.addItemListener(e -> 
    System.out.println("Animations = " + (e.getStateChange() == ItemEvent.SELECTED))
);

JRadioButtonMenuItem rb1 = new JRadioButtonMenuItem("Compact");
JRadioButtonMenuItem rb2 = new JRadioButtonMenuItem("Comfortable");
ButtonGroup menuGroup = new ButtonGroup();
menuGroup.add(rb1);
menuGroup.add(rb2);
rb1.addItemListener(ev -> { if (rb1.isSelected()) System.out.println("Compact"); });
rb2.addItemListener(ev -> { if (rb2.isSelected()) System.out.println("Comfortable"); });

themeMenu.add(cbmi);
themeMenu.addSeparator();
themeMenu.add(rb1);
themeMenu.add(rb2);

fileMenu.add(themeMenu);   // submenu
fileMenu.addSeparator();
fileMenu.add(exitItem);

menuBar.add(fileMenu);
setJMenuBar(menuBar);
```

**Reasoning:**

- `JMenuBar` is attached to the `JFrame` via `setJMenuBar()`.
- `JMenu.setMnemonic(KeyEvent.VK_F)` allows **Alt+F** to open the "File" menu.
- `JMenuItem` constructor with mnemonic adds a keyboard shortcut visible on the right side of the menu item.
- `addSeparator()` inserts a horizontal line for visual grouping.
- A `JMenu` (not `JMenuItem`) is added to the `JMenu` to create a **submenu** (cascading menu).
- `JCheckBoxMenuItem` behaves exactly like a `JCheckBox` тАФ fires `ItemEvent`.
- `JRadioButtonMenuItem`s also require `ButtonGroup` to be mutually exclusive inside the menu.

### 3.8 Step 7 тАФ Putting It All Together (Complete Listing)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class PreferencesDemo extends JFrame {

    public PreferencesDemo() {
        setTitle("User Preferences");
        setSize(420, 320);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        // ---- 1. JButton ----
        JButton saveBtn = new JButton("Save Preferences");
        saveBtn.setMnemonic(KeyEvent.VK_S);
        saveBtn.setActionCommand("SAVE");
        saveBtn.addActionListener(e -> {
            JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(this,
                    "Preferences Saved!\nAction Command = " + e.getActionCommand());
        });
        add(saveBtn);

        // ---- 2. JCheckBox ----
        JCheckBox notifyCbx = new JCheckBox("Enable Notifications", true);
        notifyCbx.addItemListener(e -> {
            if (e.getStateChange() == ItemEvent.SELECTED)
                System.out.println("Notifications ON");
            else
                System.out.println("Notifications OFF");
        });
        add(notifyCbx);

        // ---- 3. JRadioButtons ----
        JRadioButton lightR  = new JRadioButton("Light");
        JRadioButton darkR   = new JRadioButton("Dark");
        JRadioButton systemR = new JRadioButton("System Default", true);
        ButtonGroup themeGroup = new ButtonGroup();
        themeGroup.add(lightR);
        themeGroup.add(darkR);
        themeGroup.add(systemR);

        ItemListener themeListener = e -> {
            JRadioButton src = (JRadioButton) e.getSource();
            if (src.isSelected()) System.out.println("Theme тЖТ " + src.getText());
        };
        lightR.addItemListener(themeListener);
        darkR.addItemListener(themeListener);
        systemR.addItemListener(themeListener);
        add(lightR); add(darkR); add(systemR);

        // ---- 4. JToggleButton ----
        JToggleButton previewBtn = new JToggleButton("Dark Mode Preview");
        previewBtn.addActionListener(e -> {
            getContentPane().setBackground(
                    previewBtn.isSelected() ? Color.DARK_GRAY : Color.LIGHT_GRAY);
        });
        add(previewBtn);

        // ---- 5. Menu Bar ----
        JMenuBar menuBar = new JMenuBar();
        JMenu fileMenu   = new JMenu("File");
        fileMenu.setMnemonic(KeyEvent.VK_F);

        JMenuItem exitItem = new JMenuItem("Exit", KeyEvent.VK_X);
        exitItem.addActionListener(e -> System.exit(0));

        JMenu themeMenu = new JMenu("Theme");
        JCheckBoxMenuItem cbmi = new JCheckBoxMenuItem("Use Animations");
        cbmi.addItemListener(e -> System.out.println(
                "Animations = " + (e.getStateChange() == ItemEvent.SELECTED)));

        JRadioButtonMenuItem rb1 = new JRadioButtonMenuItem("Compact");
        JRadioButtonMenuItem rb2 = new JRadioButtonMenuItem("Comfortable");
        ButtonGroup menuGroup = new ButtonGroup();
        menuGroup.add(rb1);
        menuGroup.add(rb2);
        rb1.addItemListener(ev -> { if (rb1.isSelected()) System.out.println("Compact"); });
        rb2.addItemListener(ev -> { if (rb2.isSelected()) System.out.println("Comfortable"); });

        themeMenu.add(cbmi);
        themeMenu.addSeparator();
        themeMenu.add(rb1);
        themeMenu.add(rb2);

        fileMenu.add(themeMenu);
        fileMenu.addSeparator();
        fileMenu.add(exitItem);
        menuBar.add(fileMenu);
        setJMenuBar(menuBar);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new PreferencesDemo();
    }
}
```

### 3.9 Analytical Trace тАФ Event Flow Summary

| User Action | Event Class | Listener Method | Listener Interface |
|-------------|-------------|-----------------|--------------------|
| Click *Save* button | `ActionEvent` | `actionPerformed` | `ActionListener` |
| Toggle *Notifications* checkbox | `ItemEvent` | `itemStateChanged` | `ItemListener` |
| Select a *Theme* radio | `ItemEvent` (DESELECTED for old, SELECTED for new) | `itemStateChanged` | `ItemListener` |
| Click *Dark Mode Preview* toggle | `ActionEvent` (and `ItemEvent`) | `actionPerformed` | `ActionListener` |
| Click *File тЖТ Exit* menu item | `ActionEvent` | `actionPerformed` | `ActionListener` |
| Tick *Use Animations* menu checkbox | `ItemEvent` | `itemStateChanged` | `ItemListener` |
| Select *Compact* / *Comfortable* menu radio | `ItemEvent` | `itemStateChanged` | `ItemListener` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** Both `ActionListener` and `ItemListener` can be used for toggleable buttons. Use `ActionListener` when you only care that *something happened* (and read `isSelected()`); use `ItemListener` when you specifically need to know the **state transition** (SELECTED тЖФ DESELECTED).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Class Hierarchy of Swing Buttons

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[java.lang.Object] --> B[java.awt.Component]
    B --> C[java.awt.Container]
    C --> D[javax.swing.JComponent]
    D --> E[javax.swing.AbstractButton]
    E --> F[javax.swing.JButton]
    E --> G[javax.swing.JToggleButton]
    E --> H[javax.swing.JMenuItem]
    G --> I[javax.swing.JCheckBox]
    G --> J[javax.swing.JRadioButton]
    H --> K[javax.swing.JCheckBoxMenuItem]
    H --> L[javax.swing.JRadioButtonMenuItem]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture тАФ Event Routing

The diagram below maps how user interaction is routed through Swing's event-dispatching system to the appropriate listener. This is the architecture you should visualise whenever a KTU question asks *"How does a Swing button respond to a click?"*

```mermaid
graph TD
    USER[User Action: Mouse Click / Key Press] --> EVENTQ[Event Queue]
    EVENTQ --> EDT[Event Dispatch Thread]
    EDT --> SRC[Event Source: JButton / JCheckBox / JRadioButton / JToggleButton / JMenuItem]
    SRC --> ACTION{Are Action Listeners Registered?}
    ACTION -->|Yes| AL[Invoke ActionListener.actionPerformed]
    ACTION -->|No| ITEM{Is it a Toggleable Button?}
    ITEM -->|Yes| IL[Invoke ItemListener.itemStateChanged]
    ITEM -->|No| CL[Invoke ChangeListener.stateChanged]
    AL --> APP[Application Code: e.g., JOptionPane / setBackground / System.exit]
    IL --> APP
    CL --> APP
```

### 4.3 ButtonGroup Logic Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    RB1[Radio Button A] --> BG[ButtonGroup]
    RB2[Radio Button B] --> BG
    RB3[Radio Button C] --> BG
    BG --> STATE{At Most One Selected}
    STATE -->|Click A| SELA[A.isSelected = true]
    SELA --> DESELECTB[B.isSelected = false]
    DESELECTB --> DESELECTC[C.isSelected = false]
    STATE -->|Click B| SELB[B.isSelected = true]
    SELB --> DESELECTA[A.isSelected = false]
    DESELECTA --> DESELECTC[C.isSelected = false]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A common 3-mark question is *"What is the role of `ButtonGroup` in Swing?"* The answer must mention **mutual exclusion** and the **deselection of the previously selected button when a new one is chosen**.

### 4.4 Menu Component Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    MB[JMenuBar] --> FM[JMenu: File]
    MB --> EM[JMenu: Edit]
    MB --> HM[JMenu: Help]
    FM --> FI1[JMenuItem: New]
    FM --> FI2[JMenuItem: Open]
    FM --> FI3[JMenuItem: Save]
    FM --> TM[JMenu: Theme - Submenu]
    FM --> SEP1[--- Separator ---]
    FM --> EX[JMenuItem: Exit]
    TM --> CBMI[JCheckBoxMenuItem: Animations]
    TM --> SEP2[--- Separator ---]
    TM --> RBMI1[JRadioButtonMenuItem: Compact]
    TM --> RBMI2[JRadioButtonMenuItem: Comfortable]
    RBMI1 --> MBG[ButtonGroup]
    RBMI2 --> MBG
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Menu Placement Rule:** `JMenuItem`, `JCheckBoxMenuItem`, and `JRadioButtonMenuItem` can **only** be added to a `JMenu`. Adding them directly to a `JFrame` is a compile-time/runtime error. The `JMenu` is in turn added to a `JMenuBar`, which is attached to the `JFrame` via `setJMenuBar()`.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam тАУ May 2023]**
**List the different types of buttons supported by Java Swing. Mention the root class of the Swing button hierarchy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Java Swing supports the following types of buttons:

1. **JButton** тАУ A standard push button that triggers a command.
2. **JToggleButton** тАУ A two-state push button that stays pressed/released.
3. **JCheckBox** тАУ An independently selectable toggle for multi-selection.
4. **JRadioButton** тАУ A mutually exclusive toggle that requires grouping via `ButtonGroup`.
5. **JMenuItem** тАУ A button placed inside a menu.
6. **JCheckBoxMenuItem** тАУ A checkbox-style menu item.
7. **JRadioButtonMenuItem** тАУ A radio-button-style menu item.

The **root class** of the Swing button hierarchy is **`javax.swing.AbstractButton`**, which is a direct subclass of `javax.swing.JComponent`. (1 mark for listing 4+ types, 1 mark for hierarchy, 1 mark for naming `AbstractButton`.)

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2022]**
**Explain the difference between `ActionEvent` and `ItemEvent` in Swing buttons. Give one example use case for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `ActionEvent` | `ItemEvent` |
|--------|---------------|-------------|
| **Trigger** | Button is **clicked / activated** | Button's **selection state changes** |
| **Listener** | `ActionListener` (method: `actionPerformed`) | `ItemListener` (method: `itemStateChanged`) |
| **Applicable To** | All buttons | Only **toggleable** buttons |
| **Information Carried** | Action command, modifiers | `StateChange` (SELECTED / DESELECTED) |

- **Example for `ActionEvent`:** Clicking a `JButton` labeled *"Save"* should save the document тАФ we use `ActionListener`.
- **Example for `ItemEvent`:** Selecting a `JCheckBox` labeled *"Receive newsletter"* тАФ we use `ItemListener` to detect when the box becomes checked/unchecked. (1 mark each for definitions, 1 mark for examples.)

---

### 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In 14-mark questions, students often:
> - Forget the `import javax.swing.*;` line тЖТ loses **0.5 mark** for compile error.
> - Use `addActionListener` on a `JRadioButton` and never respond to state change тЖТ **logic wrong, up to 3 marks lost**.
> - Forget to call `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` тЖТ **runtime issue, 1 mark lost** for the GUI not closing cleanly.
> - Don't group radio buttons in a `ButtonGroup` тЖТ **mutual exclusion fails, 2тАУ3 marks lost**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** тАФ *[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024 Model]*

**(a)** Explain the Swing button hierarchy starting from `AbstractButton`. List the major subclasses with one-line descriptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Java Swing program that creates a `JFrame` containing:
- A `JCheckBox` for *"Send weekly report"*.
- Two `JRadioButton`s тАФ *"Hindi"* and *"English"* тАФ grouped in a `ButtonGroup` for language selection.
- A `JButton` labeled *"Submit"*.

Implement appropriate listeners so that clicking *Submit* displays a `JOptionPane` summarising the user's current selections (whether the checkbox is checked and which radio is selected). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hierarchy Explanation (7 marks):**

The root of all Swing buttons is **`javax.swing.AbstractButton`**, which inherits from `javax.swing.JComponent`. It provides common properties and behaviour: text, icon, mnemonic, tooltip, enabled/selected state, and listener-registration methods (`addActionListener`, `addItemListener`, `addChangeListener`). The hierarchy is:

- **`JButton`** тАФ A standard command push button. Performs an action when clicked; does not retain state. (1 mark)
- **`JToggleButton`** тАФ A two-state push button that retains its pressed/released state. Fires both `ActionEvent` and `ItemEvent`. (1 mark)
- **`JCheckBox`** тАФ Extends `JToggleButton`; used for independent on/off choices (e.g., preferences). (1 mark)
- **`JRadioButton`** тАФ Extends `JToggleButton`; used in a `ButtonGroup` for mutually exclusive choices. (1 mark)
- **`JMenuItem`** тАФ A button that lives inside a `JMenu`; fires `ActionEvent`. (1 mark)
- **`JCheckBoxMenuItem`** тАФ A checkbox inside a menu; fires `ItemEvent`. (1 mark)
- **`JRadioButtonMenuItem`** тАФ A radio button inside a menu; requires `ButtonGroup`. (1 mark)

---

**(b) Complete Java Program (7 marks):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class SubmitForm extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JCheckBox reportCbx;
    JRadioButton hindiR, englishR;
    JButton submitBtn;

    public SubmitForm() {
        setTitle("User Form");
        setSize(350, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        // ---- Checkbox ----
        reportCbx = new JCheckBox("Send weekly report");
        add(reportCbx);

        // ---- Radio Buttons ----
        hindiR   = new JRadioButton("Hindi");
        englishR = new JRadioButton("English");
        ButtonGroup langGroup = new ButtonGroup();
        langGroup.add(hindiR);
        langGroup.add(englishR);
        add(hindiR);
        add(englishR);

        // ---- Submit Button ----
        submitBtn = new JButton("Submit");
        submitBtn.addActionListener(this);
        add(submitBtn);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String lang;
        if (hindiR.isSelected())      lang = "Hindi";
        else if (englishR.isSelected()) lang = "English";
        else                            lang = "(none selected)";

        String message = "Weekly Report: " + (reportCbx.isSelected() ? "Yes" : "No")
                       + "\nLanguage: " + lang;
        JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(this, message);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new SubmitForm();
    }
}
```

**Mark-Wise Breakdown:**

| Component | Marks |
|-----------|-------|
| Correct imports + class extending `JFrame` | 1 |
| Proper instantiation of `JCheckBox` | 0.5 |
| `JRadioButton` creation with `ButtonGroup` | 1.5 |
| `JButton` with `ActionListener` registration | 1 |
| Correct `actionPerformed` logic with `isSelected()` checks | 2 |
| `JOptionPane` summary message | 1 |
| **Total** | **7** |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** тАФ Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the role of the `ButtonGroup` class in Swing. What happens if multiple `JRadioButton`s are created without grouping? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program to construct a `JMenuBar` with a *File* menu containing *New*, *Open*, and *Exit* items, and a *View* submenu containing a `JCheckBoxMenuItem` for *"Show Toolbar"* and two `JRadioButtonMenuItem`s тАФ *"List View"* and *"Grid View"* тАФ grouped appropriately. Use `ActionListener` and `ItemListener` where applicable. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Role of `ButtonGroup` (7 marks):**

`javax.swing.ButtonGroup` is a logical container that enforces **mutual exclusion** among a set of `AbstractButton`s (typically `JRadioButton`s and `JRadioButtonMenuItem`s). It is **not a visual container** тАФ components are still laid out by the parent container; `ButtonGroup` only controls their selection state.

**Key Properties:**

- At most **one** button in the group can be selected at any time. Selecting a new button automatically deselects the previously selected one. (2 marks)
- It is essential for `JRadioButton`s because, by default, Swing treats every `JRadioButton` as an **independent toggle**, identical to a `JCheckBox`. (2 marks)
- `ButtonGroup` does not appear on the screen; it does not extend `Container`. Buttons must be added to both a `ButtonGroup` and a visible container (e.g., `JPanel`, `JMenu`). (1 mark)
- Calling `group.setSelected(ButtonModel m)` programmatically selects a specific button. (1 mark)
- Methods: `add(AbstractButton)`, `remove(AbstractButton)`, `getElements()`, `getSelection()`. (1 mark)

---

**(b) Menu Bar Program (7 marks):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class MenuDemo extends JFrame {

    public MenuDemo() {
        setTitle("Menu Demo");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        JMenuBar menuBar = new JMenuBar();

        // ---- File Menu ----
        JMenu fileMenu = new JMenu("File");
        fileMenu.setMnemonic(KeyEvent.VK_F);

        JMenuItem newItem   = new JMenuItem("New");
        JMenuItem openItem  = new JMenuItem("Open");
        JMenuItem exitItem  = new JMenuItem("Exit");
        exitItem.addActionListener(e -> System.exit(0));

        fileMenu.add(newItem);
        fileMenu.add(openItem);
        fileMenu.addSeparator();
        fileMenu.add(exitItem);

        // ---- View Submenu ----
        JMenu viewMenu = new JMenu("View");

        JCheckBoxMenuItem toolbarItem = new JCheckBoxMenuItem("Show Toolbar");
        toolbarItem.addItemListener(e ->
            System.out.println("Toolbar = " +
                    (e.getStateChange() == ItemEvent.SELECTED))
        );

        JRadioButtonMenuItem listView  = new JRadioButtonMenuItem("List View", true);
        JRadioButtonMenuItem gridView  = new JRadioButtonMenuItem("Grid View");

        ButtonGroup viewGroup = new ButtonGroup();
        viewGroup.add(listView);
        viewGroup.add(gridView);

        listView.addItemListener(e -> {
            if (listView.isSelected()) System.out.println("List View selected");
        });
        gridView.addItemListener(e -> {
            if (gridView.isSelected()) System.out.println("Grid View selected");
        });

        viewMenu.add(toolbarItem);
        viewMenu.addSeparator();
        viewMenu.add(listView);
        viewMenu.add(gridView);

        fileMenu.add(viewMenu);   // attach submenu to File
        menuBar.add(fileMenu);
        setJMenuBar(menuBar);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new MenuDemo();
    }
}
```

**Mark-Wise Breakdown:**

| Component | Marks |
|-----------|-------|
| `JMenuBar` + `setJMenuBar` | 1 |
| File menu with three `JMenuItem`s + Exit listener | 2 |
| View submenu correctly added to File | 1 |
| `JCheckBoxMenuItem` with `ItemListener` | 1 |
| `JRadioButtonMenuItem`s grouped in `ButtonGroup` | 1 |
| Listeners wired correctly | 1 |
| **Total** | **7** |

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Root of all Swing buttons:** `javax.swing.AbstractButton` (extends `JComponent`). *(High-frequency theory question.)*
- **Hierarchy (must memorise):** `AbstractButton тЖТ {JButton, JToggleButton, JMenuItem}`; `JToggleButton тЖТ {JCheckBox, JRadioButton}`; `JMenuItem тЖТ {JCheckBoxMenuItem, JRadioButtonMenuItem}`.
- **Three listeners, three triggers:**
  - `ActionListener.actionPerformed` тЖТ fired on **click** of any button.
  - `ItemListener.itemStateChanged` тЖТ fired on **state change** of toggleable buttons.
  - `ChangeListener.stateChanged` тЖТ fired on low-level model change (rarely used in KTU).
- **`ButtonGroup` is mandatory** for `JRadioButton` and `JRadioButtonMenuItem` mutual exclusion тАФ without it, they behave like checkboxes.
- **`JMenuItem` cannot be added to a `JFrame` directly**; it must be added to a `JMenu`, which is added to a `JMenuBar`, which is attached to a `JFrame` via `setJMenuBar()`.
- **Mnemonic shortcut:** `setMnemonic(KeyEvent.VK_X)` binds **Alt+X** to a button/menu.
- **Action command:** `setActionCommand("SAVE")` + `e.getActionCommand()` lets one listener handle multiple buttons.
- **Pre-selection:** `JToggleButton`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, and `JMenuItem` variants all have a constructor that accepts a `boolean selected` argument.
- **Common pitfalls:** Forgetting to call `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`, not importing `java.awt.event.*`, forgetting `setVisible(true)`, adding `JMenuItem` to a `JFrame` directly.
- **Engineering relevance:** Swing buttons are the foundation of every HMI/SCADA-style desktop app in industrial automation labs тАФ be ready to answer *"Where would you use a `JToggleButton` vs a `JCheckBox` in an engineering application?"*

> [!WARNING]
> **Last-Mark KTU Trap:** In 14-mark coding questions, an examiner will check `import` statements, class declaration, listener interface implementation (or lambda), `ButtonGroup` usage (for radio buttons), and a working `JOptionPane`/`System.out.println` output. **Never skip these тАФ they account for 30тАУ40% of the marks.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: JTextField
Event Handling - Event Handling Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Event Handling - Event Handling Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview
