## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Event Handling** is the mechanism that controls the flow of a program when a user interacts with GUI (Graphical User Interface) components. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PBCST304, event handling is defined as the process by which the Java AWT/Swing framework detects user actions (such as clicking a button, pressing a key, or moving the mouse) and dispatches a corresponding response to registered listener objects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Board-Exact):**
> *"Event Handling is a mechanism that enables a GUI component (event source) to react to user interactions by delegating the event to a registered listener object, which executes the appropriate handler method."*

The Java event handling model is built upon three principal actors:

| Terminology | Formal Role | Plain-English Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Event Source** | The GUI component that generates an event | The "doorbell" being pressed |
| **Event Object** | The encapsulated information about the occurrence | The "package" describing what happened |
| **Event Listener** | The object that receives and processes the event | The "person" inside who answers the door |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The Restaurant Model)

Imagine a fine-dining **restaurant**:

- The **Customer** is the user. They perform an action (clapping hands to call the waiter).
- The **Bell on the table** is the **Event Source**. It generates a "ding" when triggered.
- The **Waiter** is the **Event Listener**. The waiter is *registered* to that specific table's bell.
- The **Order slip** carried back to the kitchen is the **Event Object** тАФ it contains information (which table, what kind of attention is needed).
- The **Manager (JVM/Event Dispatch Thread)** routes the bell's signal to the correct waiter.

Just as a bell without a registered waiter produces no service, a GUI component without a registered listener simply ignores user actions.

## 1.3 The Delegation Event Model (DEM)

The JDK 1.1+ implementation is officially termed the **Delegation Event Model**. Instead of a component handling its own events (the old "inheritance model" of JDK 1.0), it *delegates* the work to an external listener object.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 module explicitly emphasizes the **Delegation Event Model** as the canonical mechanism. Examiners frequently ask students to *list its components* or *explain the event delegation flow* for **3 to 7 marks**.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Quick-Reference

- **Event Source Object** тАФ A component (e.g., `JButton`, `JTextField`) capable of generating events.
- **Event Listener Object** тАФ An object that implements a `Listener` interface (e.g., `ActionListener`).
- **Registration** тАФ Calling `source.addXXXListener(listener)` to bind a listener to a source.
- **Event Dispatching** тАФ The JDK internally routes the event from source to listener via the `EventQueue`.
- **Event Handler** тАФ The method inside the listener (e.g., `actionPerformed()`) that executes the response logic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Pillar Architecture of Event Handling

The event handling mechanism in Java is fundamentally a **publish-subscribe** pattern decomposed into three logical stages:

### Pillar 1: The Event Source
- A GUI component (must extend `java.awt.Component` directly or indirectly).
- Maintains an internal list (often implemented as an `EventListener` vector array) of all registered listeners.
- Provides public methods of the form `addXXXListener(XXXListener l)` and `removeXXXListener(XXXListener l)`.
- **Why it matters:** Without a source, there is nothing to trigger the event.

### Pillar 2: The Event Object
- An instance of a subclass of `java.util.EventObject` (the AWT base class).
- Encapsulates the **event source** reference and event-specific data (e.g., coordinates, key code, modifier masks).
- **Immutable by convention** тАФ once created, its data is not modified.

### Pillar 3: The Event Listener
- Implements an interface that extends `java.util.EventListener` (a marker interface with no methods).
- Contains one or more event-handler methods (e.g., `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`).
- The handler is invoked **synchronously on the Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)** when the event is fired.

## 2.2 The Lifecycle of an Event (Operational Steps)

1. **User Action** тАФ User clicks a button.
2. **Event Generation** тАФ The AWT/Swing toolkit constructs an `ActionEvent` object.
3. **Event Queuing** тАФ The event is placed onto the system event queue.
4. **Dispatch** тАФ The Event Dispatch Thread dequeues the event and calls the source's `processEvent()`.
5. **Delegation** тАФ `processEvent()` invokes the corresponding registered listener's handler method.
6. **Handler Execution** тАФ The user-defined logic runs and the event is consumed.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Component | Base Class / Interface | Purpose | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Event Object (Base)** | `java.util.EventObject` | Root class of all AWT/Swing events | `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent` |
| **Listener (Base)** | `java.util.EventListener` | Marker interface for all listeners | `ActionListener`, `MouseListener` |
| **Source (Base)** | `java.awt.Component` | Any object that can fire events | `JButton`, `JTextField` |
| **Adapter Helper** | `xxxAdapter` (abstract class) | Provides empty implementations of listener methods | `MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`, `WindowAdapter` |
| **Event ID Constant** | Defined inside each event class | Identifies the event type | `MouseEvent.MOUSE_CLICKED` |
| **EDT Identifier** | `SwingUtilities.isEventDispatchThread()` | Verifies execution on the dispatch thread | Used in Swing threading logic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - `Component` class defines the method `processEvent(AWTEvent e)`. This method is called for **every** event.
> - Subclasses like `Button` override `processEvent` and dispatch to type-specific handlers like `processActionEvent`.
> - When `enableEvents(AWTEvent.xxx)` is called, event handling is overridden тАФ but this is rarely used in modern Swing.

## 2.4 Categories of Events (Examiner-Weighted)

| Category | Triggered By | Listener Interface | Key Handler Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ActionEvent** | Clicking buttons, menu items | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` |
| **MouseEvent** | Mouse press, release, click, enter, exit | `MouseListener` | `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, etc. (5 methods) |
| **KeyEvent** | Keyboard press, release, type | `KeyListener` | `keyTyped`, `keyPressed`, `keyReleased` (3 methods) |
| **WindowEvent** | Opening, closing, iconifying a window | `WindowListener` | `windowClosing`, `windowOpened`, etc. (7 methods) |
| **ItemEvent** | Selecting/deselecting checkboxes, list items | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` |
| **FocusEvent** | Gaining/losing input focus | `FocusListener` | `focusGained`, `focusLost` |
| **TextEvent** | Text content modification | `TextListener` | `textValueChanged(TextEvent e)` |

## 2.5 Adapter Classes тАФ The "Don't Force Me to Code" Pattern

Since interfaces like `MouseListener` declare **multiple** methods, a developer must implement *all* of them even if only one is needed. **Adapter classes** solve this by providing empty default implementations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Adapter classes are widely used in production Swing applications. They reduce boilerplate code by 80% and are the **de facto** way to handle multi-method listener interfaces in enterprise GUI development.

## 2.6 The `this` Keyword as a Listener (The Common Pattern)

In OOP, the host class (often the same class that builds the GUI) frequently implements the listener interface itself. This allows the class to register via:

```java
button.addActionListener(this);
```

This is a textbook approach covered in every KTU module-4 question paper.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Mechanism Derivation

## 3.1 Deriving the Event Dispatch Flow (Step-by-Step)

Let us **derive** the complete operational flow when a user clicks a `JButton` named `submitBtn`. We will trace every transition from user action to handler execution.

### Step 1: The User Performs the Physical Action

The user clicks the mouse on the `submitBtn` component. The operating system reports this hardware-level interrupt to the Java AWT subsystem.

### Step 2: JDK Constructs the Event Object

The AWT constructs an `ActionEvent` object. The conceptual structure of the object is:

$$
\text{ActionEvent} = \{ \text{source}, \text{id}, \text{command}, \text{modifiers}, \text{when} \}
$$

Where:
- $\text{source}$ = reference to `submitBtn`
- $\text{id} = \text{ACTION\_PERFORMED} = 1001$
- $\text{command}$ = the button's action command string (defaults to label)
- $\text{modifiers}$ = shift, ctrl, alt, meta bitmask
- $\text{when}$ = timestamp of the event

### Step 3: Posting to the System Event Queue

The event is posted via `Toolkit.getDefaultToolkit().getSystemEventQueue().postEvent(e)`. It enters the FIFO queue awaiting dispatch.

### Step 4: Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) Polling

A single dedicated thread named `AWT-EventQueue-0` continuously polls the queue. The dispatch loop conceptually executes:

$$
\text{while}(\text{queue not empty}) \rightarrow \text{event} \leftarrow \text{queue.dequeue()} \rightarrow \text{source.dispatchEvent(event)}
$$

### Step 5: Component's `dispatchEvent` Method

The button's `dispatchEvent(AWTEvent e)` is invoked. This is the master routing function inherited from `Component`. It determines the event type and forwards it.

### Step 6: Delegation to the Registered Listener

The button internally maintains a listener registry. The conceptual data structure is:

$$
\text{ListenerRegistry}_{\text{button}} = \left[ \text{listener}_{1}, \text{listener}_{2}, \ldots, \text{listener}_{n} \right]
$$

The component iterates this list and invokes the appropriate handler on each listener. For an `ActionEvent`, the call becomes:

$$
\forall \, l \in \text{ListenerRegistry} : \, l.\text{actionPerformed}(e)
$$

### Step 7: Handler Execution

The user-defined `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` method executes the response logic (e.g., printing a message, validating input, opening a new window).

### Step 8: Return Path

After all listeners have been notified, the call stack unwinds back to the EDT loop, which polls for the next event.

## 3.2 Full Algebraic Representation of the Mechanism

The complete mechanism can be expressed as the composition of five transformations:

$$
M = \text{Handler} \circ \text{Dispatch} \circ \text{Delegate} \circ \text{Enqueue} \circ \text{Generate}
$$

Where each function is defined as:

$$
\text{Generate}: \text{UserAction} \rightarrow \text{EventObject}
$$

$$
\text{Enqueue}: \text{EventObject} \rightarrow \text{Queue}
$$

$$
\text{Delegate}: \text{Queue} \times \text{Source} \rightarrow \text{Listener}
$$

$$
\text{Dispatch}: \text{Listener} \times \text{EventObject} \rightarrow \text{HandlerCall}
$$

$$
\text{Handler}: \text{HandlerCall} \rightarrow \text{Response}
$$

The inverse mapping (event тЖТ traceback) is intentionally not implemented because the event lifecycle is one-way and ephemeral.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Multi-Listener Registration

Suppose three listeners $L_1, L_2, L_3$ are registered to a single `JButton` source $S$. When a click event $E$ is generated, the dispatch order is:

$$
S.\text{dispatch}(E) \rightarrow L_1.\text{actionPerformed}(E) \rightarrow L_2.\text{actionPerformed}(E) \rightarrow L_3.\text{actionPerformed}(E)
$$

Listener invocation order matches **registration order** (FIFO from the internal vector). This is a board-favorite viva question.

## 3.4 Handling Multiple Events With One Listener

A single class can implement multiple listener interfaces simultaneously. For example, a class can implement `ActionListener` *and* `WindowListener`. The declaration syntax is:

```java
public class MyFrame extends JFrame implements ActionListener, WindowListener {
    ...
}
```

This is algebraically analogous to a function $f$ being a member of multiple type categories. The class must provide concrete implementations for **every abstract method** declared in *all* implemented interfaces, unless an adapter is used.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Event Handling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Action] --> B[Operating System Hardware Event]
    B --> C[Java AWT Toolkit Layer]
    C --> D[Event Object Constructed]
    D --> E[PostEvent to System Event Queue]
    E --> F[EDT Event Dispatch Thread Polls]
    F --> G[Source dispatchEvent Method]
    G --> H[Source processEvent Method]
    H --> I[Registered Listener List Lookup]
    I --> J[Listener Handler Method Invoked]
    J --> K[Application Response Logic]
    K --> L[Return to EDT Loop]

    M[Event Source e.g. JButton] -.addXXXListener.-> I
    N[Event Listener Object] -.Implements.-> I
    O[Event Object Encapsulates Data] -.Carried in.-> J

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style C fill:#3a8fb7,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style D fill:#4ea3a8,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style E fill:#62b09a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style F fill:#76bd8b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style G fill:#8aca7d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style H fill:#9ed76e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style I fill:#b3e35f,stroke:#fff,color:#000
    style J fill:#c8ef50,stroke:#fff,color:#000
    style K fill:#ddfb41,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L fill:#9ed76e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Class Hierarchy of the Delegation Event Model

```mermaid
graph TD
    UtilEventObj["java.util.EventObject"] --> AwtEvent["java.awt.AWTEvent"]
    AwtEvent --> ActionEvt["ActionEvent"]
    AwtEvent --> MouseEvt["MouseEvent"]
    AwtEvent --> KeyEvt["KeyEvent"]
    AwtEvent --> WindowEvt["WindowEvent"]
    AwtEvent --> ItemEvt["ItemEvent"]
    AwtEvent --> TextEvt["TextEvent"]
    AwtEvent --> FocusEvt["FocusEvent"]

    UtilListener["java.util.EventListener"] --> ActList["ActionListener"]
    UtilListener --> MouseList["MouseListener"]
    UtilListener --> KeyList["KeyListener"]
    UtilListener --> WinList["WindowListener"]
    UtilListener --> ItemList["ItemListener"]

    MouseList -.Extended by.-> MouseMotList["MouseMotionListener"]
    MouseList -.Default impl.-> MouseAdp["MouseAdapter"]
    KeyList -.Default impl.-> KeyAdp["KeyAdapter"]
    WinList -.Default impl.-> WinAdp["WindowAdapter"]

    style UtilEventObj fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style AwtEvent fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style UtilListener fill:#7e3f8c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style MouseAdp fill:#b85a8c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style KeyAdp fill:#b85a8c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style WinAdp fill:#b85a8c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Event Dispatch Sequence Diagram (Time-Ordered)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant OS as Operating System
    participant T as AWT Toolkit
    participant Q as System Event Queue
    participant EDT as Event Dispatch Thread
    participant S as Event Source JButton
    participant L as Event Listener
    participant H as Handler Method

    U->>OS: Mouse Click Hardware Signal
    OS->>T: Forward Raw Event
    T->>T: Construct ActionEvent Object
    T->>Q: postEvent e
    EDT->>Q: dequeue Event
    EDT->>S: dispatchEvent e
    S->>L: lookup registered listener
    L->>H: actionPerformed e
    H->>L: Return
    L->>S: Return
    S->>EDT: Return to Loop
    EDT->>Q: Poll Next Event
```

## 4.4 Registration vs. Firing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Application Startup] --> B[Create GUI Components]
    B --> C[Instantiate Listener Object]
    C --> D["source.addActionListener(L)"]
    D --> E[Listener Registered in Internal Vector]
    E --> F[Application Enters Event Loop]
    F --> G{User Interaction?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Event Fired and Handled]
    G -- No --> I[Wait for Next Event]
    H --> F
    I --> F

    style D fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style E fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style H fill:#76bd8b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam тАФ May 2024]**
*Explain the three primary components of the Delegation Event Model in Java.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Delegation Event Model in Java consists of three primary components:

1. **Event Source** (1 mark): A GUI component such as a `JButton` or `JTextField` that generates an event when a user interacts with it. Examples: `Button`, `TextField`, `Choice`.

2. **Event Object** (1 mark): An object that encapsulates the information about the event, including the source of the event and event-specific data. All event classes extend `java.util.EventObject`. Example: `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`.

3. **Event Listener** (1 mark): An object that implements a `Listener` interface and contains the event handler method(s) to be executed when the event occurs. The source "delegates" the event to this listener.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between an Event Listener and an Event Adapter in Java AWT.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Event Listener | Event Adapter |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Type** | Interface (e.g., `MouseListener`) | Abstract class (e.g., `MouseAdapter`) |
| **Methods** | Declares all abstract methods | Provides empty default implementations |
| **Usage** | Must implement *all* methods | Override only the methods you need |
| **Example** | `class A implements MouseListener` | `class B extends MouseAdapter` |

(2 marks for the table, 1 mark for the concluding statement that **adapters are convenience classes that simplify coding when only one or two methods of a multi-method listener are required**.)

---

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Mandate:** As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Both options are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) тАФ [KTU University Exam тАФ May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Delegation Event Model with a neat diagram. List the various event classes and listener interfaces available in Java AWT.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Write a Java program using AWT/Swing to create a frame with a text field and a button. When the button is clicked, the text typed in the text field should be displayed in a label.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) тАФ 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):** The Delegation Event Model is the event-handling mechanism introduced in JDK 1.1, where a GUI component (event source) delegates the handling of an event to a separate listener object, rather than handling it itself.

**Components (2 marks):**
- Event Source
- Event Listener
- Event Object (instance of an Event Class)

**Event Classes in AWT (2 marks):**

| Event Class | Triggered By |
| :--- | :--- |
| `ActionEvent` | Button click, menu selection |
| `MouseEvent` | Mouse actions |
| `KeyEvent` | Keyboard actions |
| `WindowEvent` | Window operations |
| `ItemEvent` | Checkbox / list selection |
| `TextEvent` | Text modification |
| `FocusEvent` | Focus changes |

**Listener Interfaces (1 mark):** `ActionListener`, `MouseListener`, `KeyListener`, `WindowListener`, `ItemListener`, `TextListener`, `FocusListener`, plus their `Motion` variants.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram of this note for the required flow architecture.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) тАФ 7 Marks

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class TextDisplayDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    Label resultLabel;
    TextField inputField;
    Button showBtn;

    public TextDisplayDemo() {
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setTitle("KTU Event Handling Demo");
        setSize(400, 200);

        inputField = new TextField(20);
        showBtn = new Button("Show Text");
        resultLabel = new Label("Result will appear here");

        add(inputField);
        add(showBtn);
        add(resultLabel);

        // Register listener
        showBtn.addActionListener(this);

        // Close window properly
        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                dispose();
            }
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String text = inputField.getText();
        resultLabel.setText("You typed: " + text);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new TextDisplayDemo();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Code (3 marks for full program):**
- The class `TextDisplayDemo` extends `Frame` and implements `ActionListener` (1 mark).
- The `addActionListener(this)` line registers the current object as the listener for the button click (1 mark).
- The `actionPerformed` method receives the event, retrieves the text from the text field, and updates the label (1 mark).
- A `WindowAdapter` is used to gracefully close the window (anonymous inner class тАФ important concept) (1 mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget to register the listener using `addActionListener(this)`. Without this single line, **no event will be handled** and the button will appear "dead" at runtime. Marks are typically deducted even if the rest of the code is perfect. Also, do not confuse `addActionListener` (correct) with `setActionListener` (does not exist in AWT).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) тАФ [KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are Adapter classes in Java? Explain with suitable examples. How do they simplify event handling?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program to handle keyboard events. When the user types in a text field, the program should display whether the key pressed was a vowel, consonant, digit, or special character in a label.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) тАФ 7 Marks

**Definition (2 marks):** Adapter classes are abstract classes defined in `java.awt.event` package that provide empty (no-op) implementations of all the methods declared in the corresponding listener interface. They exist as convenience classes so programmers can override only the methods they actually need.

**Why They Exist (1 mark):** Listener interfaces such as `MouseListener`, `KeyListener`, and `WindowListener` declare multiple methods. Implementing the interface requires providing code for *every* method, even unused ones. Adapters eliminate this overhead.

**Examples (3 marks):**

| Adapter Class | Implements | Methods Overridden If Needed |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `MouseAdapter` | `MouseListener`, `MouseMotionListener` | `mouseClicked`, `mouseEntered`, etc. |
| `KeyAdapter` | `KeyListener` | `keyTyped`, `keyPressed`, `keyReleased` |
| `WindowAdapter` | `WindowListener`, `WindowStateListener` | `windowClosing`, `windowOpened`, etc. |
| `FocusAdapter` | `FocusListener` | `focusGained`, `focusLost` |

**Code Example (1 mark):**

```java
frame.addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
    public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
        System.exit(0);
    }
});
```

Here, only `windowClosing` is overridden; the other six methods inherit empty implementations from `WindowAdapter`.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) тАФ 7 Marks

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class KeyAnalyzer extends Frame implements KeyListener {
    Label prompt, result;
    TextField input;

    public KeyAnalyzer() {
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setTitle("Key Event Analyzer");
        setSize(450, 200);

        prompt = new Label("Type a character:");
        input = new TextField(20);
        result = new Label("Analysis will appear here");

        add(prompt);
        add(input);
        add(result);

        input.addKeyListener(this);

        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                dispose();
            }
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void keyTyped(KeyEvent e) {
        char ch = e.getKeyChar();
        String classification;

        if (Character.isDigit(ch)) {
            classification = "Digit";
        } else if (Character.isLetter(ch)) {
            char lower = Character.toLowerCase(ch);
            if (lower == 'a' || lower == 'e' || lower == 'i' ||
                lower == 'o' || lower == 'u') {
                classification = "Vowel";
            } else {
                classification = "Consonant";
            }
        } else {
            classification = "Special Character";
        }

        result.setText("You typed: " + ch + " тЖТ " + classification);
    }

    public void keyPressed(KeyEvent e) { }
    public void keyReleased(KeyEvent e) { }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new KeyAnalyzer();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Code (3 marks):**
- The class implements `KeyListener` and registers itself via `input.addKeyListener(this)` (1 mark).
- The `keyTyped` method extracts the character using `e.getKeyChar()` (1 mark).
- The character is classified using `Character` class utility methods and the result is displayed (1 mark).
- Empty implementations of `keyPressed` and `keyReleased` are mandatory since the interface declares them. (Alternatively, `KeyAdapter` could be extended to avoid this boilerplate тАФ important point to mention in viva.) (1 mark bonus remark)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students mistakenly write `addKeyListener` on the `Frame` instead of the `TextField`. Remember: **the listener must be registered on the component that generates the event**. The text field is the source, not the frame. Also, `e.getKeyChar()` returns a `char`, not a `String` тАФ using `e.getKeyText()` is a different (and incorrect for this problem) function that returns the localized key name.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three Pillars:** Every event handling question hinges on the trio of **Source**, **Event Object**, and **Listener**. Memorize this trio first.
- **Delegation Event Model:** Replaced the old JDK 1.0 inheritance-based model; the source *delegates* the event to a listener.
- **Registration is Mandatory:** A source has zero listeners by default. Always call `addXXXListener(...)` or no event will ever be handled.
- **Method Count Matters:** `ActionListener` has 1 method, `MouseListener` has 5, `KeyListener` has 3, `WindowListener` has 7. Board questions often ask students to *list* these counts.
- **Adapter vs Listener:** Use an **adapter (extends)** when you only need 1-2 of many methods. Use a **listener (implements)** when you need *all* methods or are coding concisely.
- **The `this` Trick:** Implementing the listener interface in the same class that builds the GUI allows `source.addActionListener(this)`. This is the most common pattern in KTU lab exams.
- **EDT Awareness:** All event handlers run on the AWT Event Dispatch Thread. Long-running tasks inside handlers will freeze the GUI тАФ a common viva question.
- **Anonymous Inner Classes:** Often used inline (e.g., `new ActionListener() { ... }`) to avoid creating a separate named class. Frequently tested in KTU Module 4.
- **No `|` in math tables:** When writing your own exam answers, remember that listener methods are separated by commas, not pipes.
- **Final Viva Question to Expect:** *"What happens if no listener is registered and a button is clicked?"* тАФ Answer: The AWT still generates and queues the event, but the component's listener vector is empty, so the event is silently discarded. The GUI remains unresponsive.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Delegation Event Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Delegation Event Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Delegation Event Model (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> The **Delegation Event Model** is the event-handling mechanism used in Java's AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit) and Swing frameworks, in which an **Event Source** delegates the responsibility of handling an event to a separate object called an **Event Listener** (or Event Handler). The source object generates the event and "delegates" (passes on) the event object to the registered listener, which contains the actual event-handling logic.

## 1.1 The Four Pillars of the Delegation Event Model

| Pillar | Role | Analogy |
|--------|------|---------|
| **Event** | A state change or user action (click, keypress, mouse move) | A fire alarm ringing |
| **Event Source** | The GUI component that generates the event (Button, TextField) | The smoke detector that sensed fire |
| **Event Listener / Handler** | The object that receives and processes the event | The fire brigade that responds |
| **Event Object** | Encapsulates details of the event (coordinates, modifiers, etc.) | The incident report handed to the brigade |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Newspaper Subscription Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy тАФ "Newspaper Delivery":**
> Imagine a newspaper company (the **Event Source**) that has thousands of customers. Instead of the company personally finding out which customer wants a paper and delivering it manually, the company maintains a **subscription register (the listener list)**. Whenever the newspaper is printed (an **Event** occurs), the company simply looks at its register and **delegates** the delivery job to all subscribed customers (the **Event Listeners**). The customer (listener) decides what to do with the paper тАФ read it, ignore it, or pass it on. The key insight: **the source does NOT know what the listener will do; it just hands over the responsibility.**

### 1.3 Why "Delegation"?

The word **"delegation"** is critical in KTU exams. The source component **does not handle the event itself**. It delegates (transfers) this responsibility to a registered listener object. This produces a clean **separation of concerns** тАФ GUI design (front-end) is decoupled from event logic (back-end behavior).

### 1.4 Hierarchy of Key Java AWT Event Classes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All event classes in Java inherit from `java.util.EventObject` (root), and AWT-specific events inherit from `java.awt.AWTEvent`.

$$
\text{java.util.EventObject} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.AWTEvent} \rightarrow \text{Specific Event Classes (ActionEvent, MouseEvent, ...)}
$$

### 1.5 Standard KTU Keywords to Memorize

- **Event Source** тАФ the generator (e.g., a `JButton`)
- **Event Listener** тАФ the receiver (an object implementing `ActionListener`)
- **Event Object** тАФ the data carrier (e.g., `ActionEvent ae`)
- **Registration** тАФ `source.addActionListener(listenerObj);`
- **Unregistration** тАФ `source.removeActionListener(listenerObj);`
- **Multicasting** тАФ one source can register *multiple* listeners for the same event.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Flow тАФ Step-by-Step Breakdown

1. **User Interaction:** The user performs an action (e.g., clicks a button).
2. **Event Generation:** The JVM detects the action and the **Event Source** (the button) constructs an **Event Object** containing contextual data (e.g., source, timestamp, modifiers).
3. **Listener Invocation:** The source iterates through its internal `listenerList` (a `Vector`) and invokes the appropriate callback method (`actionPerformed`, `mouseClicked`, etc.) on each registered listener.
4. **Event Handling:** The listener's callback method executes the business logic.
5. **Return:** Control returns to the JVM event-dispatch thread (EDT).

## 2.2 The Two Phase Logic тАФ *Why* It Works

| Phase | Why It Matters |
|-------|----------------|
| **Registration Phase** | Decouples source from handler. A button has *no* event-handling code of its own. |
| **Notification Phase** | The source doesn't decide *what* to do тАФ it just *notifies* interested parties. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This is the textbook definition of the **Observer Design Pattern** in OOP. The Delegation Event Model is a direct Java AWT implementation of the GoF Observer Pattern. A high-yield answer to write in exams: *"Delegation Event Model follows the Observer design pattern, where the event source acts as the Subject and listeners act as Observers."*

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Methods Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Event Registration Syntax (Universal Formula):**
> $$ \texttt{eventSource.addXxxListener(eventListenerObject);} $$
> Where `Xxx` is replaced by the specific event type (e.g., `addActionListener`, `addMouseListener`).

### 2.3.1 Core Listener Interfaces and Their Callback Methods

| Listener Interface | Event Type | Key Callback Methods (from `java.awt.event`) |
|--------------------|------------|------------------------------------------------|
| `ActionListener` | ActionEvent | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` |
| `MouseListener` | MouseEvent | `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited` |
| `MouseMotionListener` | MouseEvent | `mouseDragged`, `mouseMoved` |
| `KeyListener` | KeyEvent | `keyTyped`, `keyPressed`, `keyReleased` |
| `FocusListener` | FocusEvent | `focusGained`, `focusLost` |
| `WindowListener` | WindowEvent | `windowOpened`, `windowClosing`, `windowClosed`, `windowIconified`, `windowDeiconified`, `windowActivated`, `windowDeactivated` |
| `ItemListener` | ItemEvent | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` |
| `TextListener` | TextEvent | `textValueChanged(TextEvent e)` |
| `AdjustmentListener` | AdjustmentEvent | `adjustmentValueChanged` |
| `ComponentListener` | ComponentEvent | `componentResized`, `componentMoved`, `componentShown`, `componentHidden` |
| `ContainerListener` | ContainerEvent | `componentAdded`, `componentRemoved` |

### 2.3.2 Event Object Common Methods

| Method | Returns | Purpose |
|--------|---------|---------|
| `getSource()` | `Object` | Returns the source component |
| `getID()` | `int` | Returns the event type ID |
| `getWhen()` | `long` | Returns event timestamp |
| `getModifiers()` | `int` | Returns modifier keys held (Shift, Ctrl) |
| `getKeyChar()` / `getKeyCode()` | `char` / `int` | For KeyEvent |
| `getPoint()` / `getX()` / `getY()` | `Point` / `int` | For MouseEvent |

### 2.3.3 Adapter Classes тАФ The "Shortcuts"

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Adapters Exist:** Listener interfaces often contain many abstract methods. To avoid implementing *all* of them just to handle *one*, Java provides **Adapter classes** (e.g., `MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`, `WindowAdapter`) which provide empty default implementations. You subclass the adapter and override only what you need.

| Adapter Class | Implements | Useful For |
|---------------|------------|------------|
| `MouseAdapter` | `MouseListener`, `MouseMotionListener` | Handling only one mouse event |
| `KeyAdapter` | `KeyListener` | Capturing a specific key |
| `WindowAdapter` | `WindowListener`, `WindowStateListener`, `WindowFocusListener` | Closing a frame cleanly |
| `FocusAdapter` | `FocusListener` | Single focus event |
| `ComponentAdapter` | `ComponentListener` | Single component event |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GUI Applications:** Every desktop Java application (Eclipse IDE, NetBeans) uses this model.
- **Embedded Systems:** Java ME for mobile phones uses AWT-derived event handling.
- **Cross-platform GUIs:** Swing and JavaFX still inherit this conceptual model.
- **Reactive Programming Foundation:** Modern frameworks (RxJava, Reactor) generalize this publish-subscribe idea.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Complete Event Handling Workflow тАФ Exhaustive Derivation

Below is the **end-to-end logical derivation** of how an event travels from a user action to handler execution. This is a high-frequency 14-mark question pattern in KTU exams.

### Step 1: Source Component Creation

A GUI component is instantiated, e.g., a `JButton`. Internally, AWT creates an `EventListenerList` (a thread-safe vector).

$$
\text{button = new JButton("Click Me");} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{listenerList} = \emptyset
$$

### Step 2: Listener Class Declaration

A class is declared that **implements** the appropriate listener interface.

$$
\text{class MyHandler implements ActionListener} \;\{\; \text{...} \;\}
$$

### Step 3: Listener Object Instantiation

An instance of the handler is created.

$$
\text{ActionListener handler = new MyHandler();}
$$

### Step 4: Event Source Registration (The "Delegation" Step)

The listener is registered with the source via the `addXxxListener` method. Internally, the source appends the listener object to its `listenerList`.

$$
\text{button.addActionListener(handler);} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{listenerList} = \{ \text{handler} \}
$$

### Step 5: User Action Triggers Event

User clicks the button. The AWT event-dispatch thread detects this via the OS.

### Step 6: Event Object Construction

The source creates a new `ActionEvent` object with details like source, ID (`ACTION_PERFORMED = 1001`), timestamp, and modifier keys.

$$
\text{ActionEvent ae = new ActionEvent(button, ACTION\_PERFORMED, "Click Me");}
$$

### Step 7: Delegation / Notification

The source iterates over `listenerList` and invokes `actionPerformed(ae)` on each listener.

$$
\forall \; L \in \text{listenerList}: \quad L.\texttt{actionPerformed}(ae)
$$

### Step 8: Handler Executes Logic

Inside the listener's `actionPerformed` method, the developer-written code runs (e.g., display a message, update a label).

### Step 9: Return to Event Dispatch Thread

After all listeners are notified, control returns to the EDT, and the UI redraws if needed.

## 3.2 Worked Java Code Model (For CS Students)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the canonical KTU-style Java program illustrating the Delegation Event Model. **Not** a Python or algorithm-style block тАФ this is legitimate Java for the OOP course.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{import java.awt.*;} \\
&\texttt{import java.awt.event.*;} \\
&\texttt{class MyFrame extends Frame implements ActionListener \{ } \\
&\quad \texttt{Label l; Button b;} \\
&\quad \texttt{MyFrame() \{ } \\
&\quad\quad \texttt{l = new Label("Hello"); b = new Button("Click");} \\
&\quad\quad \texttt{b.addActionListener(this); \quad // <-- REGISTRATION} \\
&\quad\quad \texttt{add(l); add(b); setSize(300,200); setVisible(true);} \\
&\quad \texttt{\}} \\
&\quad \texttt{public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) \{ } \\
&\quad\quad \texttt{l.setText("Button Clicked! Event Source: " + e.getSource());} \\
&\quad \texttt{\}} \\
&\texttt{\}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Source тЖТ Listener тЖТ Method Mapping

| User Action | Source Component | Event Class | Listener Interface | Callback Method | Adapter Class |
|-------------|------------------|-------------|---------------------|------------------|----------------|
| Click button | `JButton`, `Button` | `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed` | тАФ |
| Mouse hover | Any `Component` | `MouseEvent` | `MouseListener` | `mouseEntered` | `MouseAdapter` |
| Drag mouse | Any `Component` | `MouseEvent` | `MouseMotionListener` | `mouseDragged` | `MouseAdapter` |
| Press key | Component with focus | `KeyEvent` | `KeyListener` | `keyPressed` | `KeyAdapter` |
| Close window | `Frame`, `JFrame` | `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | `windowClosing` | `WindowAdapter` |
| Select item | `Checkbox`, `Choice` | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged` | тАФ |
| Gain/Lose focus | Any `Component` | `FocusEvent` | `FocusListener` | `focusGained` | `FocusAdapter` |
| Move/Resize comp. | Any `Component` | `ComponentEvent` | `ComponentListener` | `componentResized` | `ComponentAdapter` |

## 3.4 Key Logical Conclusions

1. **Decoupling:** The source and listener can be developed, compiled, and maintained independently.
2. **Broadcasting:** A single source can register multiple listeners (multicasting).
3. **Dynamic Binding:** Listeners can be added or removed at runtime.
4. **Reusability:** A single listener object can be registered to multiple sources.

$$
\text{Broadcast} = \{ \text{List}(L_1), L_2, \ldots, L_n \} \quad \text{where each } L_i \text{ is notified upon event}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Delegation Event Model Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Performs Action] --> B[JVM Detects Action on OS]
    B --> C[Event Source Component]
    C --> D{Is Listener Registered?}
    D -- No --> E[No Action Taken]
    D -- Yes --> F[Construct Event Object]
    F --> G[Iterate Through ListenerList Vector]
    G --> H[Invoke Callback Method on Listener 1]
    G --> I[Invoke Callback Method on Listener 2]
    G --> J[Invoke Callback Method on Listener N]
    H --> K[Listener Executes Business Logic]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Return Control to Event Dispatch Thread]
    L --> M[Repaint UI if Needed]
```

## 4.2 Component / Class Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[java util EventObject] --> B[java awt AWTEvent]
    B --> C1[ActionEvent]
    B --> C2[MouseEvent]
    B --> C3[KeyEvent]
    B --> C4[WindowEvent]
    B --> C5[ItemEvent]
    B --> C6[FocusEvent]
    B --> C7[ComponentEvent]
    B --> C8[TextEvent]
    B --> C9[AdjustmentEvent]
    B --> C10[ContainerEvent]

    I1[ActionListener] -.implements.-> C1
    I2[MouseListener] -.implements.-> C2
    I3[KeyListener] -.implements.-> C3
    I4[WindowListener] -.implements.-> C4
    I5[ItemListener] -.implements.-> C5
    I6[FocusListener] -.implements.-> C6
    I7[ComponentListener] -.implements.-> C7
    I8[TextListener] -.implements.-> C8
    I9[AdjustmentListener] -.implements.-> C9
    I10[ContainerListener] -.implements.-> C10

    AD1[MouseAdapter] -.default impl.-> I2
    AD2[KeyAdapter] -.default impl.-> I3
    AD3[WindowAdapter] -.default impl.-> I4
    AD4[FocusAdapter] -.default impl.-> I6
    AD5[ComponentAdapter] -.default impl.-> I7
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture тАФ Registration & Notification

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SRC[Event Source Component]
        S1[Button Object]
        S2[Internal ListenerList Vector]
        S3[addActionListener method]
        S4[removeActionListener method]
        S1 --> S2
        S3 --> S2
        S4 --> S2
    end

    subgraph LST[Event Listeners]
        L1[Listener A implements ActionListener]
        L2[Listener B implements ActionListener]
        L3[Listener C implements ActionListener]
    end

    EVT[ActionEvent Object] --> S2
    S2 --> L1
    S2 --> L2
    S2 --> L3
    L1 --> CB1[actionPerformed Method]
    L2 --> CB2[actionPerformed Method]
    L3 --> CB3[actionPerformed Method]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The `EventSource` maintains a `ListenerList` (a `Vector` of registered listeners). When the user triggers the event, the source creates an `ActionEvent` and iterates through this list, invoking the callback method on each registered listener тАФ this is the essence of **delegation**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam тАФ May 2024]
**Define the Delegation Event Model. List its key participants.**

**Model Answer:**

The Delegation Event Model is an event-handling approach in Java AWT/Swing where the responsibility of processing an event generated by a GUI component (the source) is **delegated** to one or more separate objects called **listeners**. The source does not handle the event itself; it merely notifies registered listeners by invoking their callback methods.

**Key Participants:**
1. **Event** тАФ an action or state change (e.g., button click).
2. **Event Source** тАФ the component that generates the event.
3. **Event Listener** тАФ the object that handles the event via callback methods.
4. **Event Object** тАФ encapsulates event data and is passed to listeners.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023]
**What is the role of an Adapter class in Java AWT? Give an example.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Adapter class** in `java.awt.event` provides **default empty implementations** for all methods of a multi-method listener interface. This allows programmers to subclass the adapter and override **only the methods they need**, avoiding the burden of implementing every abstract method of the interface.

**Example:** `MouseAdapter` implements `MouseListener` and `MouseMotionListener` with empty bodies. If we want to handle only `mouseClicked`, we extend `MouseAdapter` and override only `mouseClicked(MouseEvent e)`. Similarly, `WindowAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`, and `FocusAdapter` exist for the same purpose.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall тАФ Part A:**
> Many students write *"Adapter is used to adapt events."* This is **wrong**. The correct phrasing is *"Adapter provides default (empty) implementations of a listener interface so that subclasses override only required methods."* Marks are lost for vague, imprecise language.

---

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### тЬЕ Question A (Choice 1) тАФ 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam тАФ Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **Delegation Event Model** in detail with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **Event Source**, **Event Listener**, and **Event Object** with examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a complete Java AWT program to create a frame containing a `TextField` and a `Button`. When the button is clicked, the text entered in the text field should be displayed in a `Label`. Use the Delegation Event Model. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

The **Delegation Event Model** is the event-handling mechanism defined in `java.awt.event` package. In this model:

- **Event Source** is the GUI component (e.g., `Button`, `TextField`) that detects a user action and creates an **Event Object**.
- The source maintains a list of registered listeners in a thread-safe `EventListenerList`.
- **Event Listener** is an object that implements a listener interface (e.g., `ActionListener`) and contains the event-handling logic in a callback method (e.g., `actionPerformed`).
- The source **delegates** event processing by invoking the listener's callback method, passing the event object as an argument.
- This model follows the **Observer design pattern** and provides **decoupling** between UI components and business logic.

**Example:** A `Button` is the event source. A class `MyHandler` implements `ActionListener`. Registration: `button.addActionListener(handler)`. On click, the button creates an `ActionEvent` and calls `handler.actionPerformed(ae)`.

*(Include the block diagram from Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

class DelegationDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    Label l;
    TextField tf;
    Button b;

    DelegationDemo() {
        l = new Label("Result appears here");
        tf = new TextField(20);
        b = new Button("Show Text");

        // Registration тАФ the core of Delegation Event Model
        b.addActionListener(this);

        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        add(tf);
        add(b);
        add(l);

        setSize(350, 200);
        setTitle("Delegation Event Model Demo");
        setVisible(true);
    }

    // Callback method тАФ invoked when the button is clicked
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        l.setText("You typed: " + tf.getText());
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new DelegationDemo();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Key Lines:**
- `implements ActionListener` тАФ declares that this class will handle `ActionEvent`s.
- `b.addActionListener(this);` тАФ registers the current frame as a listener for the button's action events. **This is the registration step.**
- `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` тАФ the callback method automatically called by AWT when the button is clicked.
- `e.getSource()` could be used to identify the source if multiple sources were registered.

---

### тЬЕ Question B (Choice 2) тАФ 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam тАФ Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Event Listeners** and **Adapter Classes** in Java AWT. Explain with a suitable example how `WindowAdapter` can be used to handle window-closing events. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain **Event Classes** in `java.awt.event` package. List any **five event classes** along with their corresponding listener interfaces and the methods they trigger. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

| Aspect | Event Listener Interface | Adapter Class |
|--------|--------------------------|---------------|
| **Nature** | Abstract interface with all methods abstract | Concrete class with empty method bodies |
| **Implementation** | Must implement *all* methods | Override *only* required methods |
| **Usage** | `class H implements MouseListener` | `class H extends MouseAdapter` |
| **Example Interfaces** | `MouseListener`, `KeyListener`, `WindowListener` | `MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`, `WindowAdapter` |
| **Purpose** | Define the contract | Provide convenience default implementations |

**Example using `WindowAdapter`:**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

class CloseDemo extends Frame {
    CloseDemo() {
        setSize(300, 300);
        setTitle("WindowAdapter Demo");
        setVisible(true);

        // Register a WindowAdapter (anonymous inner class)
        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                System.exit(0);  // Close the application
            }
        });
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new CloseDemo();
    }
}
```

Here, instead of implementing all 7 methods of `WindowListener`, we extend `WindowAdapter` and override only `windowClosing`. This is the primary benefit of adapters.

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

Event classes in `java.awt.event` represent various user interactions. They all extend `java.awt.AWTEvent`, which in turn extends `java.util.EventObject`.

| # | Event Class | Triggered By | Listener Interface | Callback Method(s) |
|---|--------------|---------------|--------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | `ActionEvent` | Clicking a button, selecting a menu item, pressing Enter in a text field | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` |
| 2 | `MouseEvent` | Mouse click, press, release, enter, exit | `MouseListener` | `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited` |
| 3 | `KeyEvent` | Pressing, releasing, or typing a key | `KeyListener` | `keyPressed`, `keyReleased`, `keyTyped` |
| 4 | `WindowEvent` | Opening, closing, iconifying, activating a window | `WindowListener` | `windowOpened`, `windowClosing`, `windowClosed`, `windowIconified`, etc. |
| 5 | `ItemEvent` | Selecting/deselecting an item in a `Checkbox` or `Choice` | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Part B:**
> 1. **Forgetting `setVisible(true)`** in AWT programs тАФ your program will compile but show nothing. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing `ActionListener` with `ActionEvent`** тАФ these are different. The **event** is the data object; the **listener** is the handler interface. Mixing them up loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to register the listener** (`addActionListener(...)`) is the most common error. The button does nothing because the source has no one to delegate to.
> 4. **Implementing the interface but not overriding the callback method** causes a compile error or a silent no-op (if an empty body is given). Examiners look specifically for the `actionPerformed` body.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ Delegation Event Model**

- **Definition:** A model where the event **source delegates** event-handling to a registered **listener** object.
- **Four Key Components:** Event, Event Source, Event Listener, Event Object.
- **Root Class:** `java.util.EventObject` тЖТ `java.awt.AWTEvent` тЖТ Specific events.
- **Registration Formula:** `source.addXxxListener(listenerObj);`
- **Multicasting:** One source can have **multiple listeners**; one listener can be registered to **multiple sources**.
- **Observer Pattern:** Delegation Event Model **is** the Observer design pattern in Java.
- **Adapter Classes:** Provide empty default implementations; subclass to override only what you need.
- **Callback Method Names:** Always start with the event name (e.g., `actionPerformed`, `mouseClicked`, `keyPressed`).
- **Event Object Key Methods:** `getSource()`, `getID()`, `getWhen()`, `getModifiers()`.
- **Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
  - Forgetting `addXxxListener` registration.
  - Confusing adapter classes with listener interfaces.
  - Forgetting to set layout/visibility in AWT programs.
  - Not overriding the correct callback method.
- **Exam Tip:** Always draw the **flow diagram** (User тЖТ Source тЖТ ListenerList тЖТ Listener тЖТ Callback) for full marks in 7-mark theory questions.
- **Packages to Remember:** `java.awt`, `java.awt.event`, `javax.swing`, `javax.swing.event`.
- **Single-line Definition (for 1-mark MCQs):** *"In the Delegation Event Model, the source component generates an event and delegates its handling to one or more registered listener objects via callback methods."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Event Classes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Event Classes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Object Oriented Programming (Java)** as prescribed by the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, an **Event Class** is a specialized class that encapsulates the state and behavior of an occurrence (an *event*) happening within a graphical user interface (GUI) component. Under the **Delegation Event Model (DEM)**, every user interaction (such as a mouse click, key press, window closing, or button activation) is represented as an **object** instantiated from a predefined event class residing primarily in the `java.awt.event` and `javax.swing.event` packages.

The root of all Java event classes is `java.util.EventObject`, which is extended by `java.awt.AWTEvent`. From `AWTEvent`, the JDK derives a precise hierarchy of **Semantic Events** (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `ItemEvent`, `TextEvent`) and **Low-Level Events** (e.g., `KeyEvent`, `MouseEvent`, `WindowEvent`, `FocusEvent`, `ComponentEvent`, `ContainerEvent`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Event Classes are a core Module 4 topic. You must know the class hierarchy, the package names (`java.awt.event`), and the purpose of each event class. The examiner expects you to map a real-world GUI action (like "user typed 'A'") to the correct Event class object (`KeyEvent`).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **modern smart home security system**:

- A **motion sensor** at the door *detects* something (the **Event Source** тАФ e.g., a `JButton`).
- The sensor creates a **signal packet** containing the timestamp, sensor ID, and type of motion (the **Event Object** тАФ e.g., an `ActionEvent`).
- This packet is handed off to the **security control room** (the **Event Listener / Handler**).
- The control room decides whether to ring an alarm, turn on lights, or call the police (the **Event Handler Method**).

In Java, the **sensor does not decide what to do**. It merely **creates and dispatches** an `Event` object. The handler receives the object and decides the response. The **Event Class** is the blueprint for that "signal packet."

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary:**
> - **Event Source:** The object that *generates* the event (e.g., `JButton`, `JTextField`).
> - **Event Object:** The instance of an Event Class that *carries* the event details.
> - **Event Listener:** The interface implemented by the receiver of the event.
> - **Event Handler:** The method inside the listener that executes when the event occurs.

## 1.3 Fundamental Metrics

- **Package:** `java.awt.event` (primary), `javax.swing.event` (Swing-specific).
- **Root Class:** `java.util.EventObject` (since JDK 1.1).
- **Direct Subclass for AWT:** `java.awt.AWTEvent`.
- **Semantic vs. Low-Level:** Semantic events represent high-level actions (e.g., clicking a button); low-level events represent raw input (e.g., every pixel of mouse movement).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Class Hierarchy тАФ Why It Matters

Java organizes event classes into an inheritance hierarchy for three engineering reasons:

1. **Polymorphism:** A single method signature like `public void handle(AWTEvent e)` can accept *any* AWT event because of upcasting.
2. **Common API:** All events inherit the `getSource()` and `getID()` methods from `EventObject` and `AWTEvent`.
3. **Categorization:** Low-level events (`InputEvent`) provide raw detail, while semantic events (`ActionEvent`) provide user-friendly abstraction.

The inheritance tree is structured as:

$$
\text{java.util.EventObject} \xrightarrow{\text{extends}} \text{java.awt.AWTEvent} \xrightarrow{\text{extends}} \{ \text{Semantic and Low-Level Events} \}
$$

$$
\text{InputEvent} \xrightarrow{\text{extends}} \{ \text{KeyEvent}, \text{MouseEvent} \}
$$

$$
\text{ComponentEvent} \xrightarrow{\text{extends}} \{ \text{FocusEvent}, \text{WindowEvent} \} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{ContainerEvent extends ComponentEvent}
$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**How an Event Object is Created and Dispatched:**

- **Step 1 тАФ Detection:** The AWT/Swing component (e.g., `JButton`) detects user interaction via the underlying OS/JDK event queue.
- **Step 2 тАФ Instantiation:** The component instantiates an `Event` subclass object, passing the source, ID, and event-specific data to its constructor.
- **Step 3 тАФ Source Registration:** The component maintains a list of registered `EventListener` objects.
- **Step 4 тАФ Dispatch:** The component invokes the appropriate listener method, passing the Event object as the argument.
- **Step 5 тАФ Consumption:** The listener's handler method uses the Event object's getter methods (`getSource()`, `getKeyCode()`, `getX()`, etc.) to react.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is a high-density reference table for every Event Class KTU examiners typically test. Memorize the **purpose**, **constructor signature**, and **key accessor methods**.

| Event Class | Type | Package | Key Constructor | Critical Getter Methods |
|-------------|------|---------|-----------------|------------------------|
| `EventObject` | Root | `java.util` | `EventObject(Object source)` | `getSource()`, `toString()` |
| `AWTEvent` | Root AWT | `java.awt` | `AWTEvent(Object source, int id)` | `getID()`, `paramString()` |
| `ActionEvent` | Semantic | `java.awt.event` | `ActionEvent(Object src, int id, String cmd)` | `getActionCommand()`, `getModifiers()` |
| `ItemEvent` | Semantic | `java.awt.event` | `ItemEvent(ItemSelectable src, int id, Object item, int stateChange)` | `getItem()`, `getStateChange()` |
| `TextEvent` | Semantic | `java.awt.event` | `TextEvent(Object src, int id)` | *(inherits only base methods)* |
| `AdjustmentEvent` | Semantic | `java.awt.event` | `AdjustmentEvent(Adjustable src, int id, int type, int value)` | `getValue()`, `getAdjustmentType()` |
| `ComponentEvent` | Low-Level | `java.awt.event` | `ComponentEvent(Component src, int id)` | `getComponent()` |
| `ContainerEvent` | Low-Level | `java.awt.event` | `ContainerEvent(Component src, int id, Component child)` | `getChild()`, `getContainer()` |
| `FocusEvent` | Low-Level | `java.awt.event` | `FocusEvent(Component src, int id, boolean temporary)` | `isTemporary()`, `getOppositeComponent()` |
| `WindowEvent` | Low-Level | `java.awt.event` | `WindowEvent(Window src, int id, Window other, int fromState, int toState)` | `getWindow()`, `getOppositeWindow()` |
| `InputEvent` | Low-Level (Abstract) | `java.awt.event` | *(abstract)* | `getModifiers()`, `getModifiersEx()`, `getWhen()`, `isConsumed()` |
| `KeyEvent` | Low-Level | `java.awt.event` | `KeyEvent(Component src, int id, long when, int modifiers, int keyCode, char keyChar)` | `getKeyCode()`, `getKeyChar()`, `getKeyText()` |
| `MouseEvent` | Low-Level | `java.awt.event` | `MouseEvent(Component src, int id, long when, int modifiers, int x, int y, int clickCount, boolean popupTrigger)` | `getX()`, `getY()`, `getPoint()`, `getClickCount()`, `isPopupTrigger()` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Constants (Memorize for KTU):**
> - `ActionEvent.ACTION_PERFORMED` (id = 1001)
> - `ItemEvent.SELECTED` / `ItemEvent.DESELECTED`
> - `KeyEvent.KEY_PRESSED` / `KEY_RELEASED` / `KEY_TYPED`
> - `MouseEvent.MOUSE_CLICKED` / `MOUSE_PRESSED` / `MOUSE_RELEASED` / `MOUSE_ENTERED` / `MOUSE_EXITED` / `MOUSE_MOVED` / `MOUSE_DRAGGED`
> - `WindowEvent.WINDOW_OPENING` / `WINDOW_CLOSING` / `WINDOW_CLOSED` / `WINDOW_ICONIFIED` / `WINDOW_DEICONIFIED` / `WINDOW_ACTIVATED` / `WINDOW_DEACTIVATED`

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Event Classes are not academic fiction тАФ they power every Java desktop application, Android UI callback, and JavaFX controller. They enable:

- **Decoupling:** The view (button) and the logic (handler) are completely independent.
- **Reusability:** One listener can be attached to multiple components.
- **GUI Testing:** Automated testing tools inspect Event objects without rendering UI.
- **Industrial Applications:** Banking software uses `FocusEvent` to validate field input; CAD software uses `MouseEvent` for drawing precision; embedded touchscreens use a derivative of `InputEvent`.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Event Class Hierarchy

The Java event framework was designed top-down. We will derive each class, its purpose, and its key methods in academic detail.

---

### 3.1.1 Derivation of `EventObject` (Root Class)

**Purpose:** Provide a universal abstraction for *any* kind of event in any domain, not just AWT.

$$
\text{EventObject} : \text{java.lang.Object}
$$

$$
\text{Constructor: } \text{EventObject}(\text{Object source})
$$

$$
\text{Key Method: } \texttt{public Object getSource()} \rightarrow \text{returns the object on which the event initially occurred}
$$

The `getSource()` method is the single most important method in the entire hierarchy тАФ it is the only way the listener knows *which* component fired the event when one listener handles many components.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation of `AWTEvent`

$$
\text{AWTEvent} : \text{EventObject}
$$

$$
\text{Constructor: } \text{AWTEvent}(\text{Object source}, \text{int id})
$$

$$
\text{Key Method: } \texttt{public int getID()} \rightarrow \text{returns the event type identifier}
$$

The `id` field distinguishes event subtypes (e.g., `MOUSE_PRESSED` vs `MOUSE_RELEASED`). Each subclass defines a unique set of `static final int` constants for these IDs.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation of `ActionEvent` (Semantic Event)

**Trigger Source:** `Button`, `MenuItem`, `JButton`, `JMenuItem`, `TextField` (when Enter is pressed).

$$
\text{ActionEvent} : \text{AWTEvent}
$$

$$
\text{Constructor: } \text{ActionEvent}(\text{Object src}, \text{int id}, \text{String command})
$$

$$
\text{Key Method: } \texttt{public String getActionCommand()} \rightarrow \text{returns the button label or command string}
$$

The `getActionCommand()` method is the standard mechanism for identifying which button was clicked when a single listener handles multiple buttons.

**Analytical Logic:**

$$
\text{listener.actionPerformed}(\text{ActionEvent e}) \rightarrow \text{String cmd} = \texttt{e.getActionCommand()}
$$

$$
\text{cmd.equals("Submit")} \Rightarrow \text{execute Submit logic}
$$

---

### 3.1.4 Derivation of `ItemEvent` (Semantic Event)

**Trigger Source:** `Checkbox`, `CheckboxMenuItem`, `Choice`, `List`.

$$
\text{ItemEvent} : \text{AWTEvent}
$$

$$
\text{Constructor: } \text{ItemEvent}(\text{ItemSelectable src}, \text{int id}, \text{Object item}, \text{int stateChange})
$$

$$
\text{Key Methods: } \texttt{getItem()}, \texttt{getStateChange()}
$$

$$
\text{State Change Constants: } \text{SELECTED} = 1, \quad \text{DESELECTED} = 2
$$

The `getItem()` returns the object that changed state (e.g., a `String` from a dropdown), while `getStateChange()` returns whether it was selected or deselected.

---

### 3.1.5 Derivation of `InputEvent` (Abstract Low-Level Event)

This is an **abstract class** тАФ it cannot be instantiated directly. It is the parent of `KeyEvent` and `MouseEvent`.

$$
\text{InputEvent} : \text{AWTEvent}
$$

$$
\text{Key Methods: } \texttt{getModifiers()}, \texttt{getModifiersEx()}, \texttt{getWhen()}, \texttt{isConsumed()}
$$

The `getWhen()` method returns the timestamp of the event (useful for measuring user response time in UX research).

---

### 3.1.6 Derivation of `KeyEvent`

**Trigger Source:** Any focus-holding component when a keyboard key is involved.

$$
\text{KeyEvent} : \text{InputEvent}
$$

$$
\text{Constructor: } \text{KeyEvent}(\text{Component src}, \text{int id}, \text{long when}, \text{int modifiers}, \text{int keyCode}, \text{char keyChar})
$$

$$
\text{Key Methods: } \texttt{getKeyCode()}, \texttt{getKeyChar()}, \texttt{getKeyText(int)}
$$

**Three event types:**

$$
\text{id} = \texttt{KEY_PRESSED} \rightarrow \text{physical key pushed down}
$$

$$
\text{id} = \texttt{KEY_RELEASED} \rightarrow \text{physical key released}
$$

$$
\text{id} = \texttt{KEY_TYPED} \rightarrow \text{character actually entered (after modifier combination)}
$$

**Critical Distinction:** `getKeyCode()` returns the physical key (e.g., `VK_SHIFT = 16`), while `getKeyChar()` returns the resulting character (e.g., `'A'` after Shift is applied). This distinction is a **favourite KTU question**.

---

### 3.1.7 Derivation of `MouseEvent`

**Trigger Source:** Any component over which the mouse cursor interacts.

$$
\text{MouseEvent} : \text{InputEvent}
$$

$$
\text{Constructor: } \text{MouseEvent}(\text{Component src}, \text{int id}, \text{long when}, \text{int modifiers}, \text{int x}, \text{int y}, \text{int clickCount}, \text{boolean popupTrigger})
$$

$$
\text{Key Methods: } \texttt{getX()}, \texttt{getY()}, \texttt{getPoint()}, \texttt{getClickCount()}, \texttt{isPopupTrigger()}
$$

The `getClickCount()` returns whether it is a single, double, or triple click тАФ essential for distinguishing double-click events.

---

### 3.1.8 Derivation of `WindowEvent`

**Trigger Source:** A `Window` or `Frame` or `JFrame`.

$$
\text{WindowEvent} : \text{ComponentEvent}
$$

$$
\text{Key Methods: } \texttt{getWindow()}, \texttt{getOppositeWindow()}
$$

$$
\text{Seven Event Types: } \{\text{WINDOW_OPENING, WINDOW_CLOSING, WINDOW_CLOSED, WINDOW_ICONIFIED, WINDOW_DEICONIFIED, WINDOW_ACTIVATED, WINDOW_DEACTIVATED}\}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** `WINDOW_CLOSING` fires *before* the window is closed (you can cancel it). `WINDOW_CLOSED` fires *after* it is gone. Do not confuse these in code.

---

### 3.1.9 Derivation of Remaining Event Classes

$$
\text{ComponentEvent} \rightarrow \text{generic component state change (moved, resized, shown, hidden)}
$$

$$
\text{ContainerEvent} \rightarrow \text{component added or removed from container}
$$

$$
\text{FocusEvent} \rightarrow \text{FOCUS_GAINED or FOCUS_LOST}
$$

$$
\text{AdjustmentEvent} \rightarrow \text{scrollbar or adjustable value changed}
$$

$$
\text{TextEvent} \rightarrow \text{text content changed}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Event-to-Listener Mapping

This matrix is **exam gold** тАФ KTU frequently asks you to match an event class to the correct listener interface and handler method.

| Event Class | Listener Interface | Handler Method Signature |
|-------------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | `void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` |
| `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | `void itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` |
| `TextEvent` | `TextListener` | `void textValueChanged(TextEvent e)` |
| `AdjustmentEvent` | `AdjustmentListener` | `void adjustmentValueChanged(AdjustmentEvent e)` |
| `ComponentEvent` | `ComponentListener` | `void componentResized/Moved/Shown/Hidden(ComponentEvent e)` |
| `ContainerEvent` | `ContainerListener` | `void componentAdded/Removed(ContainerEvent e)` |
| `FocusEvent` | `FocusListener` | `void focusGained/Lost(FocusEvent e)` |
| `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | `void windowOpening/Closing/Closed/Iconified/Deiconified/Activated/Deactivated(WindowEvent e)` |
| `KeyEvent` | `KeyListener` | `void keyPressed/Released/Typed(KeyEvent e)` |
| `MouseEvent` | `MouseListener` | `void mouseClicked/Pressed/Released/Entered/Exited(MouseEvent e)` |
| `MouseEvent` (motion/wheel) | `MouseMotionListener` | `void mouseDragged/Moved(MouseEvent e)` |
| `MouseWheelEvent` | `MouseWheelListener` | `void mouseWheelMoved(MouseWheelEvent e)` |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how `MouseEvent` is handled by **two** different listeners тАФ `MouseListener` for clicks/entries and `MouseMotionListener` for movement. This is a classic KTU trick question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Event Class Hierarchy тАФ Mermaid Class Diagram

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction TB
    classObj0["java lang Object"]
    classObj1["java util EventObject"]
    classObj2["java awt AWTEvent"]
    classObj3["java awt event ComponentEvent"]
    classObj4["java awt event InputEvent"]
    classObj5["java awt event ItemEvent"]
    classObj6["java awt event ActionEvent"]
    classObj7["java awt event AdjustmentEvent"]
    classObj8["java awt event TextEvent"]
    classObj9["java awt event ContainerEvent"]
    classObj10["java awt event FocusEvent"]
    classObj11["java awt event WindowEvent"]
    classObj12["java awt event PaintEvent"]
    classObj13["java awt event KeyEvent"]
    classObj14["java awt event MouseEvent"]
    classObj15["java awt event MouseWheelEvent"]
    classObj0 <|-- classObj1
    classObj1 <|-- classObj2
    classObj2 <|-- classObj3
    classObj2 <|-- classObj4

    classObj2 <|-- classObj7

    classObj3 <|-- classObj12

    classObj4 <|-- classObj14
    classObj14 <|-- classObj15
```

## 4.2 Delegation Event Model тАФ Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC[Event Source e g JButton]
        srcA["User Action e g Click"]
        srcB["Instantiate ActionEvent"]
        srcC["Maintain Listener List"]
    end
    subgraph DSP[Dispatcher Mechanism]
        dspA["Lookup registered listeners"]
        dspB["Invoke handler method"]
    end
    subgraph LST[Event Listener Object]
        lstA["Implements ActionListener"]
        lstB["actionPerformed ActionEvent e"]
        lstC["Extract data via e getSource e getActionCommand"]
    end
    srcA --> srcB
    srcB --> srcC
    srcC --> dspA
    dspA --> dspB
    dspB --> lstA
    lstA --> lstB
    lstB --> lstC
```

## 4.3 Event Lifecycle Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant SRC as Event Source Button
    participant EV as Event Object ActionEvent
    participant LIS as Listener Handler
    U->>SRC: Click mouse on button
    SRC->>EV: new ActionEvent this ACTION_PERFORMED "Submit"
    SRC->>LIS: call actionPerformed ev
    LIS->>EV: ev getSource returns button
    LIS->>EV: ev getActionCommand returns "Submit"
    LIS-->>U: Execute business logic and respond
```
