# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix тАФ Mapping SQL Clauses to Relational Algebra

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 examiners test whether students can **convert a SQL query into equivalent relational algebra** and vice versa. The following table is your master reference.

| SQL Construct | Relational Algebra Notation | Plain English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `SELECT col FROM T` | $\pi_{\text{col}}(T)$ | Project column from table |
| `SELECT * FROM T WHERE cond` | $\sigma_{\text{cond}}(T)$ | Select rows satisfying condition |
| `SELECT * FROM T ORDER BY c` | $\tau_{c}(T)$ | Sort by column `c` |
| `SELECT * FROM T1, T2 WHERE cond` | $\sigma_{\text{cond}}(T_1 \times T_2)$ | Filtered Cartesian product |
| `SELECT * FROM T1 JOIN T2 ON c` | $T_1 \bowtie_c T_2$ | Natural / theta join on `c` |
| `SELECT col, AGG FROM T GROUP BY col` | $\text{agg}\;\mathcal{G}_{\text{col}}(T)$ | Group-then-aggregate |

## 3.2 Worked Example тАФ Deriving a Query Step-by-Step

**Problem:** From a table `Employee(emp_id, name, dept, salary)`, find the **highest salary in each department, but only for departments having more than 2 employees**.

### Step 1 тАФ Identify the source

The data lives in `Employee`. So we begin with:

$$\text{Base} = \text{Employee}(emp\_id, name, dept, salary)$$

### Step 2 тАФ Group by department

We must aggregate salaries per department, so we use `GROUP BY dept`. The aggregate function `MAX(salary)` gives the maximum.

$$\text{Step 2 Output} = \mathcal{G}_{\text{dept}}\big(\text{MAX(salary)} \rightarrow \text{max\_sal}\big)(\text{Employee})$$

In SQL so far:

```sql
SELECT dept, MAX(salary) AS max_sal
FROM Employee
GROUP BY dept;
```

### Step 3 тАФ Apply the "more than 2 employees" filter

Because this is a *group-level* filter (operates on groups, not rows), it belongs in **`HAVING`**, not `WHERE`. To express "more than 2", we use `COUNT(*) > 2`.

$$\text{Step 3} = \sigma_{\text{COUNT}(*) > 2}\big(\text{Step 2 Output}\big)$$

### Step 4 тАФ Final SQL Assembly

```sql
SELECT dept, MAX(salary) AS max_sal
FROM Employee
GROUP BY dept
HAVING COUNT(*) > 2;
```

### Step 5 тАФ Logical Execution Walkthrough (Trace)

1. **FROM** тЖТ read all rows of `Employee`.
2. **GROUP BY dept** тЖТ partition rows into buckets per department.
3. **COUNT(*)** within each bucket тЖТ counts rows per department.
4. **HAVING COUNT(*) > 2** тЖТ discards groups with 2 or fewer employees.
5. **SELECT** тЖТ project only `dept` and `MAX(salary)`.
6. **Result** тЖТ one row per qualifying department showing its highest salary.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** "**Where filters Wares, Having filters Hands**" тАФ `WHERE` filters individual rows, `HAVING` filters groups (hands-clasped together).

## 3.3 Full Join-Derivation Worked Example

**Problem:** Display each student's name and their department name.

**Schema:**

```
Student(roll_no, name, dept_id)
Dept(dept_id, dept_name)
```

### Step 1 тАФ Identify the join column

`Student.dept_id` тЖФ `Dept.dept_id` is the foreign-key link.

### Step 2 тАФ Express as a theta-join

$$\text{Result} = \pi_{\text{Student.name},\; \text{Dept.dept\_name}} \Big( \sigma_{\text{Student.dept\_id} = \text{Dept.dept\_id}} (\text{Student} \times \text{Dept}) \Big)$$

### Step 3 тАФ Convert to SQL (modern explicit join syntax)

```sql
SELECT s.name, d.dept_name
FROM Student s
INNER JOIN Dept d ON s.dept_id = d.dept_id;
```

### Step 4 тАФ Execution Trace

1. `FROM Student s` тЖТ load 50 student rows.
2. `INNER JOIN Dept d ON ...` тЖТ for each student, find matching dept row; if no match, drop the student.
3. `SELECT s.name, d.dept_name` тЖТ project just two columns.
4. **Output:** one row per (student, matching department).

> [!WARNING]
> **`INNER JOIN` discards unmatched rows.** If a student has `dept_id = NULL` or a non-existent department, they vanish from results. Use **`LEFT JOIN`** to keep all students and show `NULL` for the missing department.

## 3.4 Decision Matrix тАФ Which Join to Use?

| Scenario | Recommended Join |
|---|---|
| Need only rows that match in **both** tables | `INNER JOIN` |
| Need **all** rows from left + matches from right | `LEFT [OUTER] JOIN` |
| Need **all** rows from right + matches from left | `RIGHT [OUTER] JOIN` |
| Need **all** rows from both, NULL where no match | `FULL OUTER JOIN` |
| Need every combination (rare, e.g., schedules) | `CROSS JOIN` |
| Self-comparison (e.g., employees & managers) | `SELF JOIN` |

## 3.5 Subquery Derivation Example

**Problem:** Find the name of the student(s) with the maximum marks.

```sql
SELECT name 
FROM Student 
WHERE marks = (SELECT MAX(marks) FROM Student);
```

**Trace:**

1. Inner query тЖТ `SELECT MAX(marks) FROM Student` returns a single value, e.g., `95`.
2. Outer query тЖТ `SELECT name FROM Student WHERE marks = 95` returns the student(s) with 95.
3. Result тЖТ list of toppers.

> [!NOTE]
> A subquery returning **one value** is a **scalar subquery** (can use `=`, `>`, `<`). A subquery returning **a list** is an **IN subquery** (`WHERE id IN (SELECT тАж)`).

## 4.2 Logical Execution Pipeline of a SELECT Statement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: User writes SELECT statement"] --> B["Step 1: FROM - Identify source tables"]
    B --> C["Step 2: JOIN - Combine related tables"]
    C --> D["Step 3: WHERE - Filter individual rows"]
    D --> E{"Step 4: GROUP BY present?"}
    E -- Yes --> F["Step 5: GROUP BY - Form groups"]
    F --> G["Step 6: HAVING - Filter groups"]
    G --> H["Step 7: SELECT - Choose output columns"]
    E -- No --> H
    H --> I["Step 8: DISTINCT - Remove duplicates"]
    I --> J["Step 9: ORDER BY - Sort result set"]
    J --> K["Step 10: LIMIT - Trim output size"]
    K --> L["Final Result Set returned to caller"]

    style A fill:#2c3e50,color:#fff
    style L fill:#27ae60,color:#fff
    style E fill:#e67e22,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Constraint Enforcement Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Incoming DML Statement: INSERT or UPDATE"] --> B["Parser validates syntax"]
    B --> C["Constraint Checker activated"]
    C --> D{"NOT NULL violated?"}
    D -- Yes --> E1["Reject with error: cannot insert NULL"]
    D -- No --> F{"UNIQUE violated?"}
    F -- Yes --> E2["Reject with error: duplicate value"]
    F -- No --> G{"CHECK violated?"}
    G -- Yes --> E3["Reject with error: check constraint failed"]
    G -- No --> H{"PRIMARY KEY violated?"}
    H -- Yes --> E4["Reject: duplicate or NULL primary key"]
    H -- No --> I{"FOREIGN KEY violated?"}
    I -- Yes --> E5["Reject: referential integrity error"]
    I -- No --> J["Accept and write to table"]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> - Forgetting `WHERE` in `UPDATE`/`DELETE` тЗТ **all rows modified** (loss of 2тАУ3 marks).
> - Using a `SELECT` alias inside `WHERE` тЗТ **runtime error** because aliases are resolved at step 5, after `WHERE` (step 2).
> - Confusing `DROP`, `TRUNCATE`, `DELETE` semantics in 3-mark questions.
> - Mixing up `WHERE` and `HAVING` тАФ `WHERE` is for **rows**, `HAVING` is for **groups**.
> - Not specifying `GROUP BY` while using aggregate functions alongside non-aggregated columns тЗТ query rejected.
> - Using single quotes for column names (must use double quotes or no quotes) тАФ though MySQL is lenient, **ANSI SQL is strict**.

---

## Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between the `DROP`, `TRUNCATE`, and `DELETE` commands in SQL. State one key property of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Command | Category | Effect | Rollback? | WHERE clause? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `DROP TABLE` | DDL | Removes **table structure + data + indexes** permanently | **No** (auto-commit) | Not allowed |
| `TRUNCATE TABLE` | DDL | Removes **all rows** but keeps structure | **No** (auto-commit) | Not allowed |
| `DELETE FROM` | DML | Removes **specific rows** based on condition | **Yes** (transactional) | Allowed |

> *Examiner's Note (1 mark):* Mentioning that `DROP` and `TRUNCATE` are **DDL** (auto-commit) while `DELETE` is **DML** earns the differentiating mark.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024]**
**Explain the difference between the `WHERE` and `HAVING` clauses in SQL with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- `WHERE` filters **individual rows** *before* grouping and is applied to **non-aggregated** columns (e.g., `WHERE dept = 'CSE'`).
- `HAVING` filters **groups of rows** *after* `GROUP BY` and is applied to **aggregated** values (e.g., `HAVING COUNT(*) > 5`).

**Example:**

```sql
-- WHERE: only CSE students
SELECT dept, COUNT(*) FROM Student WHERE dept = 'CSE' GROUP BY dept;

-- HAVING: only depts with more than 5 students
SELECT dept, COUNT(*) FROM Student GROUP BY dept HAVING COUNT(*) > 5;
```

> *Examiner's Note:* State clearly the **execution order**: WHERE тЖТ GROUP BY тЖТ HAVING.

---

## Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *KTU Rule:* Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** тАФ DDL & Constraints
Consider the following schema for a library management system:

```
BOOK(BookID, Title, ISBN, Price, PublisherID, Year)
PUBLISHER(PublisherID, Name, Country)
```

**Write the SQL `CREATE TABLE` statements for both tables with all appropriate constraints** (Primary Key, Foreign Key, NOT NULL, UNIQUE on ISBN, and a CHECK that `Price > 0`).

#### Model Solution (7 marks):

```sql
-- (1 mark) Publisher table with PK
CREATE TABLE PUBLISHER (
    PublisherID   INT          PRIMARY KEY,
    Name          VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    Country       VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL
);

-- (1 mark) Book table with PK and FK
CREATE TABLE BOOK (
    BookID        INT           PRIMARY KEY,
    Title         VARCHAR(200)  NOT NULL,
    ISBN          VARCHAR(20)   NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    Price         DECIMAL(8,2)  NOT NULL CHECK (Price > 0),
    PublisherID   INT           NOT NULL,
    Year          INT,
    -- (1 mark) Foreign Key with referential action
    FOREIGN KEY (PublisherID) REFERENCES PUBLISHER(PublisherID)
        ON DELETE CASCADE
        ON UPDATE CASCADE
);
```

**Mark Distribution:**
- 1 mark тАФ `PRIMARY KEY` declared correctly.
- 1 mark тАФ `FOREIGN KEY` with `REFERENCES`.
- 1 mark тАФ `UNIQUE` on ISBN.
- 1 mark тАФ `CHECK (Price > 0)`.
- 1 mark тАФ `NOT NULL` on mandatory fields.
- 1 mark тАФ Correct data types & syntax.
- 1 mark тАФ `ON DELETE/UPDATE CASCADE` for referential integrity (bonus explanation).

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** тАФ DML & Joins
**Write SQL queries for the following:**

1. Insert a new book with `BookID = 501, Title = 'Database Systems', ISBN = 'ISBN-001', Price = 599.50, PublisherID = 10, Year = 2023`. **(2 marks)**

2. Display the `Title` and `Price` of all books published after 2020, sorted by `Price` descending. **(2 marks)**

3. Display the `Name` of each publisher and the **total number of books** they have published тАФ but only publishers having published **more than 3 books**. **(3 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**1. Insert statement (2 marks):**
```sql
INSERT INTO BOOK (BookID, Title, ISBN, Price, PublisherID, Year)
VALUES (501, 'Database Systems', 'ISBN-001', 599.50, 10, 2023);
```

**2. Sorted selective query (2 marks):**
```sql
SELECT Title, Price
FROM BOOK
WHERE Year > 2020
ORDER BY Price DESC;
```

**3. Grouped query with join (3 marks):**
```sql
SELECT p.Name, COUNT(b.BookID) AS total_books
FROM PUBLISHER p
INNER JOIN BOOK b ON p.PublisherID = b.PublisherID
GROUP BY p.PublisherID, p.Name
HAVING COUNT(b.BookID) > 3;
```

> *Examiner's Note:* Common error тАФ students write `WHERE COUNT(*) > 3` which is invalid because `WHERE` is evaluated *before* grouping. The aggregate filter **must** use `HAVING`.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** тАФ *Alternative Choice*

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** тАФ SQL Sublanguages & DCL
**1. Classify the following SQL commands into DDL, DML, DCL, TCL:** `CREATE`, `INSERT`, `GRANT`, `COMMIT`, `UPDATE`, `ROLLBACK`, `DROP`, `DELETE`, `REVOKE`, `TRUNCATE`, `SELECT`, `SAVEPOINT`, `ALTER`. **(3 marks)**

**2. Explain the ACID properties of a database transaction. How does `COMMIT` and `ROLLBACK` enforce them?** **(4 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**1. Classification (3 marks):**
- **DDL:** `CREATE`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE`, `ALTER`
- **DML:** `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`, `SELECT`
- **DCL:** `GRANT`, `REVOKE`
- **TCL:** `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`, `SAVEPOINT`

**2. ACID Properties (4 marks):**

| Property | Meaning | How COMMIT/ROLLBACK Enforces It |
|---|---|---|
| **Atomicity** | All-or-nothing execution of a transaction | `ROLLBACK` undoes partial changes; `COMMIT` finalises all |
| **Consistency** | DB moves from one valid state to another | Constraints checked at `COMMIT`; failed тЗТ `ROLLBACK` |
| **Isolation** | Concurrent transactions don't interfere | Locks held until `COMMIT`/`ROLLBACK` |
| **Durability** | Once committed, changes survive crashes | `COMMIT` flushes log to disk; survives system failure |

**Example TCL usage:**
```sql
START TRANSACTION;
    UPDATE ACCOUNT SET balance = balance - 500 WHERE acc_id = 1;
    UPDATE ACCOUNT SET balance = balance + 500 WHERE acc_id = 2;
COMMIT;     -- Both succeed atomically
-- If error: ROLLBACK; -- both undo
```

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** тАФ Subqueries & Set Operations
**Given `EMPLOYEE(Eid, Ename, Dept, Salary, JoinDate)` and `DEPARTMENT(Did, Dname, Location)`, write SQL queries for:**

1. Find the name and salary of the **second highest paid** employee. **(2 marks)**
2. Find department names having **more than 2 employees** using a subquery. **(2 marks)**
3. List all employee names from the **'Sales'** department using `IN` subquery тАФ show the equivalent `JOIN` version as well. **(3 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**1. Second highest salary (2 marks):**
```sql
SELECT Ename, Salary
FROM EMPLOYEE
WHERE Salary = (
    SELECT MAX(Salary) FROM EMPLOYEE
    WHERE Salary < (SELECT MAX(Salary) FROM EMPLOYEE)
);
```

**2. Subquery-based department count (2 marks):**
```sql
SELECT Dname FROM DEPARTMENT
WHERE Did IN (
    SELECT Dept
    FROM EMPLOYEE
    GROUP BY Dept
    HAVING COUNT(*) > 2
);
```

**3. Subquery vs Join equivalence (3 marks):**

**Subquery form (1.5 marks):**
```sql
SELECT Ename FROM EMPLOYEE
WHERE Dept IN (SELECT Did FROM DEPARTMENT WHERE Dname = 'Sales');
```

**Equivalent JOIN form (1.5 marks):**
```sql
SELECT e.Ename
FROM EMPLOYEE e
INNER JOIN DEPARTMENT d ON e.Dept = d.Did
WHERE d.Dname = 'Sales';
```

> *Examiner's Note:* Either subquery or JOIN is acceptable. Bonus appreciation if student notes JOINs generally **outperform** subqueries in modern optimisers.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and stick it on your wall before the exam.**

- **SQL = Structured Query Language**, standardised by **ISO/IEC 9075**, declarative (you say *what*, not *how*).
- **Five sub-languages:** DDL, DML, DCL, TCL, DQL (DQL is essentially just `SELECT`).
- **`DROP`** removes structure; **`TRUNCATE`** removes all rows; **`DELETE`** removes selected rows. Only `DELETE` is rollback-able.
- **`WHERE` vs `HAVING`:** WHERE = row-level pre-group filter; HAVING = group-level post-group filter. Use `HAVING` with aggregate functions.
- **Logical SELECT order:** `FROM тЖТ WHERE тЖТ GROUP BY тЖТ HAVING тЖТ SELECT тЖТ DISTINCT тЖТ ORDER BY тЖТ LIMIT`. **Typed order тЙа Executed order.**
- **Constraints:** `PRIMARY KEY` (unique + NOT NULL), `FOREIGN KEY` (referential integrity), `UNIQUE` (no duplicates, NULL allowed), `NOT NULL`, `CHECK`, `DEFAULT`.
- **Joins:**
  - `INNER JOIN` тЖТ matching rows only.
  - `LEFT JOIN` тЖТ all left rows + matching right (NULL if no match).
  - `RIGHT JOIN` тЖТ all right rows + matching left.
  - `FULL OUTER JOIN` тЖТ everything from both.
  - `CROSS JOIN` тЖТ Cartesian product.
- **Aggregate functions:** `COUNT()`, `SUM()`, `AVG()`, `MIN()`, `MAX()` тАФ operate on groups formed by `GROUP BY`.
- **Set operations:** `UNION` (combine, remove dupes), `UNION ALL` (keep dupes), `INTERSECT`, `EXCEPT` (or `MINUS` in Oracle).
- **Relational Algebra mapping (viva favourite):**
  - `SELECT` col тЖТ $\pi$ (PROJECT)
  - `WHERE` cond тЖТ $\sigma$ (SELECT / restriction)
  - `JOIN` тЖТ $\bowtie$ (theta-join)
  - `ORDER BY` тЖТ $\tau$ (sort)
- **ACID properties** of transactions: **Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability** тАФ enforced by `COMMIT` and `ROLLBACK`.
- **In OOP context:** SQL is the *data-access language*; Java issues it via `Statement` / `PreparedStatement` objects in JDBC; the RDBMS engine parses, optimises, and executes it; results return as a `ResultSet`.
- **Performance tip:** Always use `PreparedStatement` (parameterised) over `Statement` тАФ it precompiles and prevents SQL injection.
- **Common 2-mark viva questions:**
  1. *Differences between SQL and NoSQL?*
  2. *What is the difference between `CHAR` and `VARCHAR`?*
  3. *Why is `HAVING` needed when we have `WHERE`?*
  4. *What is referential integrity?*
  5. *Explain `TRUNCATE` vs `DELETE`.*
- **Golden rule:** Always write the `WHERE` clause **before** the `SET` clause in an `UPDATE` statement to avoid mass-update disasters.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working with Result Set
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST304 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Working with Result Set

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Java Database Connectivity (JDBC)**, a **`ResultSet`** is an interface declared in the `java.sql` package that represents a **tabular representation of the data returned from an executed SQL query** (typically a `SELECT` statement). The `ResultSet` object maintains a **cursor** that points to the current row of data; initially, the cursor is positioned **before the first row**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 - JDBC):**
> A `ResultSet` is *not* a permanently stored table in the database. It is a **client-side, in-memory, scrollable or forward-only cursor object** that allows Java applications to **iterate, read, and update** rows resulting from a query.

The signature of the interface in the Java standard library is:
```java
public interface ResultSet extends Wrapper, AutoCloseable
```

It is obtained by invoking the `executeQuery(String sql)` method on a `java.sql.Statement`, `PreparedStatement`, or `CallableStatement` object.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ The Spreadsheet Cursor

Imagine you are working inside **Microsoft Excel**:

1. You run a query, and the database returns **1,000 rows**.
2. The database cannot dump all 1,000 rows to your Java program at once тАФ that would consume enormous memory.
3. Instead, the database opens a **virtual spreadsheet** (the `ResultSet`) and hands you a **single pointer (the cursor)** that currently sits *above* Row 1.
4. Every time you call `next()`, the pointer moves **down by one row**, and you can read the cell values using methods like `getString("name")` or `getInt(2)`.
5. If the `ResultSet` is **scrollable**, you can also move **up**, **jump to a specific row**, or **check if you are on the last row**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Cursor Mental Model (KTU Favorite Question):**
> Think of `ResultSet` as a **bookmark sliding through the pages of a query result**. The bookmark does not hold the entire book; it only tells you which page you are currently reading.

---

## 1.3 Why Do We Need a `ResultSet`?

The need for a `ResultSet` arises from a fundamental engineering constraint:

| Constraint | Reality | Solution Offered by `ResultSet` |
|------------|---------|----------------------------------|
| **Memory** | A 10-million-row table cannot fit in RAM | Streaming, row-by-row access via cursor |
| **Network bandwidth** | Sending millions of rows over JDBC-ODBC bridge is slow | Lazy fetch, only requested row is transmitted |
| **DB locking** | Holding a DB lock for the entire dataset is dangerous | Cursor-based, transactional row access |
| **Data integrity** | Two users editing the same data simultaneously | Concurrency modes (`CONCUR_READ_ONLY` vs `CONCUR_UPDATABLE`) |

> [!TIP]
> **Default Configuration in JDBC:**
> When you use `Statement.executeQuery(sql)` without explicitly setting parameters, the resulting `ResultSet` is **`TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY`** and **`CONCUR_READ_ONLY`**. This is the **minimum feature set** guaranteed to work on *every* JDBC-compliant database (a crucial point for KTU database portability questions).

---

## 1.4 Position of `ResultSet` in the JDBC Architecture

The `ResultSet` is the **third tier** of the standard JDBC execution pipeline:

$$
\text{Connection} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Statement} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{ResultSet}
$$

This forms the famous **"C-S-R triad"** (ConnectionтАУStatementтАУResultSet) that is a recurring theme in KTU 2-mark and 7-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a `ResultSet` тАФ Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The lifecycle of a `ResultSet` can be broken down into **six precise stages**:

1. **Creation Stage** тАФ A `ResultSet` object is produced by calling `stmt.executeQuery(sql)`. At this moment, the JDBC driver translates the SQL into the database's native protocol and prepares a server-side cursor.
2. **Initial Cursor Position** тАФ The cursor is logically placed *before row 1*. Calling any getter method *before* `next()` throws a `SQLException` with message *"Before start of result set"*.
3. **Navigation Stage** тАФ The application calls navigation methods (`next()`, `previous()`, `absolute(n)`, `relative(n)`, `first()`, `last()`, `beforeFirst()`, `afterLast()`). Each call updates the internal cursor pointer.
4. **Data Retrieval Stage** тАФ Once positioned on a valid row, **getter methods** (`getString`, `getInt`, `getDouble`, `getDate`, etc.) extract column values either by **column index (1-based)** or by **column label**.
5. **Update Stage (Optional)** тАФ If the `ResultSet` is updatable (`CONCUR_UPDATABLE`), the programmer can call **updater methods** (`updateString`, `updateInt`) and then `updateRow()` / `insertRow()` / `deleteRow()` to mutate the underlying database.
6. **Closing Stage** тАФ `rs.close()` releases the cursor. If unclosed, the `Statement` and `Connection` may also remain open, leading to **resource leaks** (a frequent KTU viva question).

---

## 2.2 Types of `ResultSet` тАФ The Three Official Cursor Types

JDBC defines **three scrollability types**, set when the `Statement` is created:

| Constant | Cursor Behavior | Practical Use Case |
|----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| `ResultSet.TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY` | Cursor moves **only forward** (`next()` only) | Memory-efficient streaming, default for all DBs |
| `ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE` | Bidirectional scroll, **does not see** external DB changes | Generating reports from a snapshot |
| `ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE` | Bidirectional scroll, **sees** external DB changes | Real-time dashboards, live trading apps |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insensitive vs Sensitive тАФ The KTU One-Liner:**
> *"INSENSITIVE = the cursor is blind to other users' commits; SENSITIVE = the cursor sees other users' commits in real-time."*

---

## 2.3 Concurrency Modes тАФ Who Can Edit?

| Constant | Edit Permission | Notes |
|----------|----------------|-------|
| `ResultSet.CONCUR_READ_ONLY` | **No updates** allowed (default) | Safe, portable, works on every driver |
| `ResultSet.CONCUR_UPDATABLE` | Allows `updateXxx()`, `insertRow()`, `deleteRow()` | Requires the query to reference **only one table** and include **all primary-key columns** (KTU board favorite) |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & API Cheat Sheet

| # | Component | Syntax / Formula | Returns | Default Behavior |
|---|-----------|------------------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Execute SELECT | `ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery(sql);` | `ResultSet` | Cursor before row 1 |
| 2 | Move Forward | `boolean b = rs.next();` | `true` if row exists | Returns `false` after last row |
| 3 | Move Backward | `boolean b = rs.previous();` | `true` if row exists | Needs scrollable type |
| 4 | Jump Absolute | `boolean b = rs.absolute(5);` | `true` if valid position | 1-based indexing |
| 5 | Jump Relative | `boolean b = rs.relative(-2);` | `true` if valid | Negative = backward |
| 6 | Get by Index | `String n = rs.getString(2);` | Column value | Index starts at **1** |
| 7 | Get by Label | `int id = rs.getInt("emp_id");` | Column value | Case-insensitive |
| 8 | Get Row Count | `int n = rs.getRow();` | Current row number | 0 if no current row |
| 9 | Check Empty | `if (rs.next() == false)` | тАФ | Use to detect zero rows |
| 10 | Update Cell | `rs.updateDouble("salary", 55000.0);` | тАФ | Needs updatable + `updateRow()` |
| 11 | Insert Row | `rs.moveToInsertRow(); rs.updateString(...); rs.insertRow();` | тАФ | Requires PK fields |
| 12 | Delete Row | `rs.deleteRow();` | тАФ | Cursor must be on a valid row |
| 13 | Refresh Row | `rs.refreshRow();` | тАФ | Re-fetches from DB |
| 14 | Close | `rs.close();` | `void` | Always wrap in `finally` |
| 15 | Get Metadata | `ResultSetMetaData rsmd = rs.getMetaData();` | `ResultSetMetaData` | Gives column count, types, names |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The `ResultSet` model is the **foundation of every data-driven Java application**:

- **Banking systems** тАФ Transactional queries that scroll through millions of customer records.
- **E-commerce** тАФ Product catalog pagination via `absolute()`.
- **Healthcare** тАФ Patient records where `TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE` ensures doctors see live updates from labs.
- **Educational ERP** тАФ KTU's own internal portal processes exam results through scrollable, read-only `ResultSet`s.
- **IoT dashboards** тАФ Sensor data streamed row-by-row using `TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY` for memory efficiency.

---

## 2.6 `ResultSetMetaData` тАФ The "About-Data" Object

A frequently paired topic in KTU Module 4:

$$
\text{ResultSetMetaData } = \text{ schema/structure of the ResultSet }
$$

It exposes:
- `getColumnCount()` тЖТ number of columns
- `getColumnName(i)` тЖТ name of $i$-th column
- `getColumnTypeName(i)` тЖТ SQL type (e.g., `VARCHAR`, `INT`)
- `getTableName(i)` тЖТ source table

This is the engine behind generic "report builders" that introspect any query dynamically.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 тАФ Creating a Default `ResultSet` and Reading All Rows

**Problem Statement:** Connect to a MySQL database, retrieve all employees from the `employees` table, and print their names and salaries.

**Step 1 тАФ Load Driver and Establish Connection**

```java
Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver");
Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(
    "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/ktu_db", "root", "password");
```

*Logic:* The driver registers itself with `DriverManager`. The `Connection` is a heavyweight, network-socket-backed object representing a live session.

**Step 2 тАФ Create a Statement**

```java
Statement stmt = con.createStatement();
```

*Logic:* A `Statement` acts as a **carrier** that ships your SQL string to the DB and brings back a `ResultSet`. Since no `ResultSet` type or concurrency is specified, the default is `TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY` + `CONCUR_READ_ONLY`.

**Step 3 тАФ Execute the Query**

```java
String sql = "SELECT emp_id, name, salary FROM employees";
ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery(sql);
```

*Logic:* The driver opens a server-side cursor and returns a *client-side handle* `rs`. The cursor is logically positioned **before row 1** (i.e., at row index 0).

**Step 4 тАФ Iterate Using `next()`**

```java
while (rs.next()) {
    int id       = rs.getInt("emp_id");
    String name  = rs.getString("name");
    double sal   = rs.getDouble("salary");
    System.out.println(id + " | " + name + " | " + sal);
}
```

*Logic:* Each call to `next()` advances the cursor by one row. The first call moves from "before row 1" to "row 1". When the cursor moves past the last row, `next()` returns `false` and the loop terminates.

**Step 5 тАФ Close Resources (Reverse Order of Creation)**

```java
rs.close();
stmt.close();
con.close();
```

*Logic:* Resources are released in **LIFO** (Last-In, First-Out) order to avoid leaks. In production code, this is wrapped in a `try-with-resources` block.

> [!NOTE]
> **The cursor position equation** can be expressed as:
>
> $$P_{\text{cursor}}(k) = P_{\text{cursor}}(k-1) + 1 \quad \text{after each } next()$$
>
> where $P_{\text{cursor}}(0) = 0$ (before row 1) and $P_{\text{cursor}}(N+1) = N+1$ (after last row, causing `next()` to return `false`).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 тАФ Scrollable & Updatable `ResultSet` with Insertion

**Problem Statement:** Insert a new employee using a scrollable, updatable `ResultSet`.

**Step 1 тАФ Create a Scrollable, Updatable Statement**

```java
Statement stmt = con.createStatement(
    ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE,
    ResultSet.CONCUR_UPDATABLE);
```

*Logic:* We override the defaults. The cursor is now bidirectional **and** we have permission to mutate rows. This combination requires JDBC 2.0+ and a driver that supports scrollability (e.g., MySQL Connector/J, PostgreSQL).

**Step 2 тАФ Define the Query Carefully**

```java
String sql = "SELECT emp_id, name, salary FROM employees";
ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery(sql);
```

*Logic:* For a `ResultSet` to be updatable, the SQL must:
- Reference **exactly one** table.
- Include **all primary-key columns** in the `SELECT` list.
- Not use joins, aggregations (`SUM`, `AVG`), or `DISTINCT`.

**Step 3 тАФ Move to the Insert Buffer**

```java
rs.moveToInsertRow();
```

*Logic:* This creates a special **"insert row"** тАФ a staging area disconnected from the original cursor position. The original cursor is remembered internally.

**Step 4 тАФ Populate the New Row**

```java
rs.updateInt(1, 104);
rs.updateString(2, "Anjali Krishnan");
rs.updateDouble(3, 62500.50);
```

*Logic:* Updater methods **do not** write to the database yet. They only fill the staging row buffer.

**Step 5 тАФ Commit the Insert**

```java
rs.insertRow();
```

*Logic:* The driver sends an `INSERT` SQL command to the DB. The new row appears in the underlying table.

**Step 6 тАФ Return to the Original Cursor**

```java
rs.moveToCurrentRow();
```

*Logic:* Restores the cursor to where it was *before* `moveToInsertRow()`. The original iteration is unaffected.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 тАФ Using `absolute()` for Random Pagination

**Problem Statement:** Display rows 11 to 20 (a typical "Page 2" scenario in a 10-row-per-page UI).

**Step 1 тАФ Create a Scrollable Statement**

```java
Statement stmt = con.createStatement(
    ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE,
    ResultSet.CONCUR_READ_ONLY);
```

*Step 2 тАФ Position at Row 11**

```java
ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT name FROM employees");
if (rs.absolute(11)) {
    do {
        System.out.println(rs.getString("name"));
    } while (rs.next() && rs.getRow() <= 20);
}
```

*Logic:* The `absolute(n)` method places the cursor on row $n$ (1-based). If $n$ is positive, the cursor is counted from the start; if negative, from the end. A return value of `false` means the requested position is invalid.

**Mathematical Formulation for the Page Boundary:**

$$
\text{Page Start Row} = (\text{Page Number} - 1) \times \text{Page Size} + 1
$$

$$
\text{Page End Row} = \text{Page Number} \times \text{Page Size}
$$

For Page 2, Page Size 10:

$$
\text{Start} = (2 - 1) \times 10 + 1 = 11
$$

$$
\text{End} = 2 \times 10 = 20
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix тАФ Choosing the Right `ResultSet` Type

| Application Scenario | Scroll Type | Concurrency | Justification |
|----------------------|-------------|-------------|---------------|
| Bulk CSV export (millions of rows) | `TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY` | `CONCUR_READ_ONLY` | Minimal memory, fastest stream |
| Print a static report | `TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE` | `CONCUR_READ_ONLY` | Bidirectional navigation, consistent snapshot |
| Live trading dashboard | `TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE` | `CONCUR_READ_ONLY` | Reflects real-time price changes |
| In-app CRUD editor | `TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE` | `CONCUR_UPDATABLE` | Edit rows directly through the cursor |
| Two-factor authentication lookup | `TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY` | `CONCUR_READ_ONLY` | Only one row needed; no scrolling |

---

## 3.5 Exhaustive `ResultSet` Method Reference (With Signatures)

### Navigation Methods

```java
boolean next()                  throws SQLException
boolean previous()              throws SQLException
boolean first()                 throws SQLException
boolean last()                  throws SQLException
boolean absolute(int row)       throws SQLException
boolean relative(int rows)      throws SQLException
void    beforeFirst()           throws SQLException
void    afterLast()             throws SQLException
```

### Getter Methods (per SQL Type)

```java
String  getString(int colIndex)   throws SQLException
String  getString(String colName) throws SQLException
int     getInt(int colIndex)
int     getInt(String colName)
long    getLong(int/String)
double  getDouble(int/String)
float   getFloat(int/String)
boolean getBoolean(int/String)
Date    getDate(int/String)
Time    getTime(int/String)
Timestamp getTimestamp(int/String)
Object  getObject(int/String)
```

### Updater Methods (mirror getters with an extra `value` parameter)

```java
void updateString(int/String, String)
void updateInt(int/String, int)
void updateDouble(int/String, double)
void updateRow()
void insertRow()
void deleteRow()
void refreshRow()
void cancelRowUpdates()
void moveToInsertRow()
void moveToCurrentRow()
```

### Information Methods

```java
int     getRow()
boolean isFirst()
boolean isLast()
boolean isBeforeFirst()
boolean isAfterLast()
ResultSetMetaData getMetaData()
int     getFetchSize()
void    setFetchSize(int)
int     getConcurrency()
int     getType()
boolean wasNull()
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hack:** A common 3-mark question is *"List any 5 methods of `ResultSet` interface."* Memorize at least one from each category above (Navigation, Getter, Updater, Information) to score full marks consistently.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram тАФ The C-S-R Triad and `ResultSet` Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Java Application] --> B[DriverManager.getConnection]
    B --> C[Connection Object]
    C --> D[Statement = con.createStatement]
    D --> E[SQL String: SELECT ...]
    E --> F[ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery]
    F --> G[rs Cursor Before Row 1]
    G --> H{rs.next}
    H -- true --> I[Read Data via getString / getInt]
    I --> J{More Rows}
    J -- yes --> H
    J -- no --> K[rs.next returns false]
    K --> L[rs.close]
    L --> M[stmt.close]
    M --> N[con.close]
    H -- false at start --> O[ResultSet is Empty]
    O --> L
    I --> P[Optional: updateXxx plus updateRow]
    P --> Q[Database Commits Change]
    Q --> I
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram тАФ `ResultSet` Type and Concurrency Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need a ResultSet] --> Q1{Need Bidirectional Scroll}
    Q1 -- no --> A1[TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY]
    Q1 -- yes --> Q2{Need to See Live DB Changes}
    Q2 -- no --> A2[TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE]
    Q2 -- yes --> A3[TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE]
    A1 --> Q3{Need to Update Rows}
    A2 --> Q3
    A3 --> Q3
    Q3 -- no --> B1[CONCUR_READ_ONLY]
    Q3 -- yes --> B2[CONCUR_UPDATABLE]
    B1 --> End1[Use stmt.executeQuery for read only]
    B2 --> End2[Use updater methods then updateRow]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram тАФ Block-Level Architecture: `ResultSet` Internals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CLIENT_SIDE[Client Side Java Application]
        direction TB
        App[Java Code]
        App --> RS[ResultSet Object rs]
        RS --> Cursor[Internal Cursor Pointer]
        RS --> Fetcher[Row Fetcher Cache]
        RS --> Meta[ResultSetMetaData]
    end
    subgraph DRIVER[JDBC Driver Layer]
        direction TB
        Proto[Protocol Translator]
        Proto --> Packets[Network Packets]
    end
    subgraph SERVER_SIDE[Database Server]
        direction TB
        ServerCursor[Server Side Cursor]
        Table[(Actual Table Data)]
        ServerCursor --> Table
    end
    Cursor -.maps to.-> ServerCursor
    Fetcher -.fetches rows from.-> Packets
    Packets -.translated by.-> Proto
    Proto -.talks to.-> ServerCursor
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram тАФ Insert Row via Updatable `ResultSet`

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Java App
    participant RS as ResultSet
    participant Stmt as Statement
    participant DB as Database

    App->>Stmt: createStatement(TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE, CONCUR_UPDATABLE)
    Stmt-->>App: Statement object
    App->>Stmt: executeQuery(SELECT emp_id, name, salary FROM employees)
    Stmt->>DB: Send SELECT
    DB-->>Stmt: Server cursor opened
    Stmt-->>App: ResultSet rs
    App->>RS: moveToInsertRow
    RS-->>App: Insert buffer ready
    App->>RS: updateInt(1, 104)
    App->>RS: updateString(2, Anjali)
    App->>RS: updateDouble(3, 62500.50)
    App->>RS: insertRow
    RS->>DB: INSERT INTO employees VALUES ...
    DB-->>RS: 1 row inserted
    App->>RS: moveToCurrentRow
    RS-->>App: Original cursor restored
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam тАФ Model Question 1] [3 Marks]**
> **Q: Define a `ResultSet` in JDBC. Mention any two methods of the `ResultSet` interface.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> A `ResultSet` is an interface in the `java.sql` package that represents the **tabular result of an SQL `SELECT` query**. It maintains a **cursor** that is initially positioned before the first row and can be advanced using navigation methods.
>
> Two important methods are:
>
> 1. `boolean next()` тАФ Moves the cursor to the next row; returns `false` when there are no more rows.
> 2. `String getString(int columnIndex)` тАФ Retrieves the value of the specified column in the current row as a `String`.

---

> **[KTU University Exam тАФ Model Question 2] [3 Marks]**
> **Q: Differentiate between `TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY` and `TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE` `ResultSet`s.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Feature | `TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY` | `TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE` |
> |---------|---------------------|---------------------------|
> | Cursor Movement | Only forward via `next()` | Bidirectional: `next()`, `previous()`, `absolute()` |
> | View of Data | Not relevant (one-pass) | Static snapshot тАФ does **not** see external updates |
> | Memory | Lower overhead | Higher overhead (server holds entire result) |
> | Portability | Supported by all JDBC drivers | Requires JDBC 2.0+ and DB support |

---

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam тАФ Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern]**
> **Module Learning Outcome:** Apply JDBC concepts to retrieve and manipulate database records using Java.

---

### тЬж Question A (14 Marks)

> **(a)** Explain the different **types of `ResultSet`** in JDBC with suitable examples. *[7 Marks]*
>
> **(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate **inserting, updating, and deleting** records through a scrollable, updatable `ResultSet`. *[7 Marks]*

---

### тЬН Model Solution тАФ Question A

#### Part (a) тАФ Types of `ResultSet`

JDBC supports **three** scrollability types and **two** concurrency modes, defined as constants on the `ResultSet` interface.

**1. `ResultSet.TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY`**
This is the default type. The cursor can move **only in the forward direction** (using `next()`). It is the most memory-efficient option and is supported by every JDBC-compliant database.

```java
Statement stmt = con.createStatement();    // default = forward only
ResultSet rs   = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM students");
while (rs.next()) {
    System.out.println(rs.getString("name"));
}
```

**2. `ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE`**
The cursor is **bidirectional**. You can call `previous()`, `first()`, `last()`, `absolute(n)`, etc. The "insensitive" qualifier means that once the `ResultSet` is populated, it behaves like a **frozen snapshot** тАФ any subsequent commit by another user is **not visible** to this cursor.

```java
Statement stmt = con.createStatement(
    ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE,
    ResultSet.CONCUR_READ_ONLY);
ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM students");
rs.last();                      // jump to last row
rs.previous();                  // step backward
```

**3. `ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE`**
The cursor is bidirectional **and** reflects changes made by other transactions while the `ResultSet` is open. This is required for real-time collaborative tools (e.g., stock-ticker apps). It is the **least portable** option.

```java
Statement stmt = con.createStatement(
    ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE,
    ResultSet.CONCUR_READ_ONLY);
```

**Concurrency Modes:**

- `CONCUR_READ_ONLY` тАФ Cannot update rows. Default.
- `CONCUR_UPDATABLE` тАФ Allows calling `updateXxx()`, `insertRow()`, `deleteRow()`.

**Summary Table:**

| Type Constant | Bidirectional | Sees Live Changes | Use Case |
|---------------|---------------|-------------------|----------|
| `TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY` | No | No (streaming) | Bulk exports |
| `TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE` | Yes | No | Reports, snapshots |
| `TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE` | Yes | Yes | Real-time dashboards |

---

#### Part (b) тАФ Insert/Update/Delete via Updatable `ResultSet`

```java
import java.sql.*;

public class UpdatableResultSetDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String url  = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/ktu_db";
        String user = "root";
        String pass = "password";

        try (Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass)) {

            // 1. Create a scroll-sensitive, updatable Statement
            Statement stmt = con.createStatement(
                ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE,
                ResultSet.CONCUR_UPDATABLE);

            ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery(
                "SELECT emp_id, name, salary FROM employees");

            // ---------- INSERT ----------
            rs.moveToInsertRow();
            rs.updateInt(1, 201);
            rs.updateString(2, "Rahul Menon");
            rs.updateDouble(3, 50000.00);
            rs.insertRow();
            rs.moveToCurrentRow();
            System.out.println("Inserted successfully.");

            // ---------- UPDATE ----------
            rs.beforeFirst();
            while (rs.next()) {
                if (rs.getString("name").equals("Rahul Menon")) {
                    rs.updateDouble("salary", 55000.00);
                    rs.updateRow();
                    System.out.println("Updated salary for Rahul Menon.");
                    break;
                }
            }

            // ---------- DELETE ----------
            rs.beforeFirst();
            while (rs.next()) {
                if (rs.getInt("emp_id") == 201) {
                    rs.deleteRow();
                    System.out.println("Deleted Rahul Menon's record.");
                    break;
                }
            }

            rs.close();
            stmt.close();

        } catch (SQLException e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Key Steps:**

1. `createStatement(int, int)` тАФ enables scrollability and updatability.
2. `moveToInsertRow()` тАФ opens a temporary staging buffer; original cursor is saved internally.
3. `updateInt`, `updateString`, `updateDouble` тАФ fill the staging buffer.
4. `insertRow()` тАФ flushes the buffer to the database.
5. `moveToCurrentRow()` тАФ restores the cursor to its prior position.
6. `updateRow()` тАФ commits changes made to an existing row.
7. `deleteRow()` тАФ removes the row under the cursor.

---

### тЬж Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Alternative Choice

> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of a `ResultSet`** and the **lifecycle of a cursor**. *[7 Marks]*
>
> **(b)** Write a Java program to implement **pagination** (e.g., show rows 21 to 30) using a scrollable `ResultSet`. *[7 Marks]*

---

### тЬН Model Solution тАФ Question B

#### Part (a) тАФ Architecture and Cursor Lifecycle

**Architecture Diagram (Verbal Description):**

A `ResultSet` is a **client-side handle** that maintains a **logical pointer (cursor)** mapping to a **server-side cursor** opened by the database. The flow is:

$$
\text{Java App} \;\to\; \text{ResultSet} \;\xleftrightarrow{\text{JDBC Driver}}\; \text{Server-Side Cursor} \;\to\; \text{Actual Table}
$$

The `ResultSet` does not hold the data permanently; it fetches rows **on demand** (controlled by the **fetch size**). The internal architecture has three components:

1. **Cursor Pointer** тАФ A logical row position. Initial value = 0 (before first row).
2. **Row Cache** тАФ A small buffer (default fetch size = 10) of prefetched rows.
3. **Metadata Cache** тАФ A `ResultSetMetaData` object describing column names, types, and counts.

**Cursor Lifecycle:**

| Stage | State | Method to Call |
|-------|-------|----------------|
| Initial | Before Row 1 | (automatic) |
| Move Forward | Row $k \to k+1$ | `next()` |
| Move Backward | Row $k \to k-1$ | `previous()` (needs scrollable) |
| Check Start | тАФ | `isBeforeFirst()` |
| Check End | тАФ | `isAfterLast()` |
| Final | Closed | `close()` |

---

#### Part (b) тАФ Pagination Program (Rows 21 to 30)

```java
import java.sql.*;

public class PaginationDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int pageSize   = 10;
        int pageNumber = 3;     // we want page 3
        int startRow   = (pageNumber - 1) * pageSize + 1;   // = 21
        int endRow     = pageNumber * pageSize;             // = 30

        try (Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(
                "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/ktu_db", "root", "password")) {

            Statement stmt = con.createStatement(
                ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE,
                ResultSet.CONCUR_READ_ONLY);

            ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT emp_id, name FROM employees");

            if (rs.absolute(startRow)) {
                do {
                    System.out.println(rs.getInt("emp_id") + " - "
                                       + rs.getString("name"));
                } while (rs.next() && rs.getRow() <= endRow);
            } else {
                System.out.println("Page out of range.");
            }

            rs.close();
            stmt.close();

        } catch (SQLException e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```

**Logic:**

- `absolute(21)` jumps the cursor to row 21.
- The `do-while` loop prints the current row and advances **only if** the new row number is still $\le 30$.
- This ensures exactly **10 rows** are displayed (rows 21 through 30).

---

## 5.3 тЪа KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing column indexing with array indexing** тАФ In JDBC, columns are **1-based**, NOT 0-based. Writing `rs.getString(0)` throws `SQLException`.
> 2. **Forgetting to call `updateRow()` after `updateXxx()`** тАФ The change is buffered, not committed. Examiners specifically check for this.
> 3. **Using `rs.getString("name")` after closing the `ResultSet`** тАФ Always close resources **last** (reverse order of creation: `rs` тЖТ `stmt` тЖТ `con`).
> 4. **Assuming `next()` is called automatically** тАФ It is not. You must invoke it explicitly; otherwise, the cursor remains before row 1.
> 5. **Using an updatable `ResultSet` on a multi-table JOIN** тАФ JDBC rejects this and throws an error mentioning that the query must reference a single table with primary keys.
> 6. **Mixing up `CONCUR_READ_ONLY` and `CONCUR_UPDATABLE`** тАФ Default is read-only. Many students lose 2 marks for not specifying it.
> 7. **Not handling `SQLException`** тАФ A bare `rs.next()` without try-catch results in compile-time error. Examiners deduct marks for sloppy exception handling.

---

## 5.4 ЁЯУМ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **`ResultSet`** = a `java.sql` interface representing the **rows returned by `executeQuery()`**.
- Cursor starts **before row 1**; the first call to `next()` moves it to row 1.
- **Three scroll types**: `TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY`, `TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE`, `TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE`.
- **Two concurrency modes**: `CONCUR_READ_ONLY` (default), `CONCUR_UPDATABLE`.
- **Column indexing is 1-based**, not 0-based.
- **Getter methods** can be called by **column index OR column name**.
- **Updater methods** (`updateString`, `updateInt`, тАж) require an explicit **`updateRow()`** to commit.
- **`insertRow()`** must be preceded by **`moveToInsertRow()`**; remember to call **`moveToCurrentRow()`** afterward.
- For an **updatable** `ResultSet`, the SELECT must reference **one table** and include **all primary-key columns**.
- **`getMetaData()`** returns a `ResultSetMetaData` describing the structure (column count, names, types).
- **`getRow()`** returns the **current** row number (0 if no current row).
- **Close order**: `ResultSet` тЖТ `Statement` тЖТ `Connection` (LIFO).
- For **pagination**, prefer `absolute(startRow)` with `TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE`.
- **Default `fetchSize`** is 10 тАФ increase it for batch processing to reduce network round-trips.
- Use **`try-with-resources`** (Java 7+) to auto-close `Connection`, `Statement`, and `ResultSet`.
- **`wasNull()`** reports whether the last-read column had SQL `NULL` тАФ useful for nullable columns.
- **Insensitive = frozen snapshot**; **Sensitive = live view** тАФ this distinction is a KTU favorite 3-mark question.

Class Design: Book (title, author, ISBN, genre, status), User (user ID, name, contact information, borrowed books list), Library (lists of books and users), Librarian (inherits from User, adds/removes books, manages users), BorrowTransaction (transaction ID, book, user, borrow date, return date). Deliverables: Design Document, Source Code, User Manual, Test Cases.

Class Design: Payment (abstract base class), CreditCardPayment, PayPalPayment, BankTransferPayment (inherit from Payment), PaymentProcessor (manages and processes different payment types).
